topics.title: Introduction to Petroleum Industry: Overview of the Petroleum Industry - Origin of Petroleum
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET861 - INSTRUMENTATION IN PETROCHEMICAL INDUSTRIES > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Introduction to Petroleum Industry: Overview of the Petroleum Industry - Origin of Petroleum # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of Petroleum > [!IMPORTANT] > **Petroleum** (from Latin *petra* = rock + *oleum* = oil) is a naturally occurring, flammable, complex mixture of hydrocarbons (primarily alkanes, cycloalkanes, and aromatics) with small amounts of sulfur, nitrogen, oxygen, and trace metals, found in sedimentary rock formations beneath the Earth's surface. In strict KTU 2024 terminology, petroleum is classified as a **fossil fuel** of biogenic origin, occurring in liquid (crude oil), gaseous (natural gas), and semi-solid (bitumen/tar) phases. It is the principal feedstock for the global petrochemical industry and is the source of every commercial hydrocarbon product, from LPG to plastics to pharmaceutical precursors. The American Petroleum Institute (API) defines crude oil as a mixture of hydrocarbons that existed in liquid phase in natural underground reservoirs and remains liquid at atmospheric pressure after passing through surface separating facilities. The chemical composition typically lies in the following range: | Element | Composition (% by weight) | |---|---| | Carbon (C) | 83 – 87 % | | Hydrogen (H) | 11 – 14 % | | Sulfur (S) | 0.1 – 3 % (up to 7 % in heavy sour crudes) | | Nitrogen (N) | 0.1 – 1.5 % | | Oxygen (O) | 0.1 – 1.5 % | | Metals (Ni, V, Fe) | < 1000 ppm | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic sits at the foundation of Module 1. Every downstream topic — drilling instrumentation, wellhead sensors, refining process control, and distillation column automation — ultimately depends on understanding *what* crude oil is chemically and *where* it came from geologically. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Slow Cooking Burial Imagine a vast ocean teeming with microscopic algae, plankton, and bacteria. When these organisms die, their remains rain down like autumn leaves onto a quiet, oxygen-starved seabed. Over millions of years — imagine compressing 100 million years of slow-cooking into a single sentence — layer after layer of mineral sediment buries this organic sludge. Heat from the Earth's interior (the geothermal gradient, ~25–30 °C per km of depth) acts like a slow oven, gently baking the organic matter under immense pressure. The result: a thick, dark, energy-dense liquid we call crude oil. The analogy is a **pressure cooker buried for 100 million years** — and this captures the three essential ingredients perfectly: 1. **Organic source material** (the food in the pot), 2. **Heat** (the burner underneath), and 3. **Pressure** (the heavy lid on top). Without any one of these, no petroleum forms. ## 1.3 The Two Scientific Theories of Origin Historically, the origin of petroleum has been debated through two competing hypotheses. The **biogenic (organic) theory** dominates modern petroleum geology and is universally accepted by the upstream industry; the **abiogenic (inorganic) theory** remains a fringe but historically important alternative. > [!NOTE] > **Biogenic (Organic) Theory** — Petroleum is derived from the buried remains of once-living organisms (mainly marine plankton, algae, and bacteria) transformed over geological time by heat and pressure. > [!NOTE] > **Abiogenic (Inorganic) Theory** — Petroleum originates from deep-Earth primordial carbon (methane and other hydrocarbons synthesized in the mantle) that has migrated upward into the crust. The 2024 KTU module accepts the **biogenic theory as the standard model**, but students are expected to know the abiogenic theory for context and contrast. ## 1.4 Standard Metrics Every KTU Student Must Memorise - **Geothermal gradient:** **25 – 30 °C per km** of depth below the surface. - **Oil window temperature range:** **60 °C – 120 °C** (the depth-temperature window where kerogen thermally cracks into liquid hydrocarbons). - **Gas window temperature range:** **120 °C – 225 °C** (where kerogen cracks into natural gas). - **Source rock age range:** primarily **Mesozoic and Paleozoic era** formations (250 – 65 million years ago). - **Typical API gravity of light crude:** **> 31.1°**, medium **22.3°–31.1°**, heavy **< 22.3°**. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Step-by-Step Origin Process (Biogenic Theory) The formation of petroleum is not a single event but a **five-stage geological continuum** spanning tens of millions of years. Each stage is essential; failure at any stage results in no producible reservoir. ### Stage 1: Production of Organic Matter (Biogenesis) - Marine and lacustrine environments host prolific blooms of **phytoplankton, zooplankton, cyanobacteria, and algae**. - Higher plant debris (spores, pollen, leaf cuticles) can contribute in deltaic settings, but the bulk of oil-prone organic matter is **aquatic**. - The key index is **Total Organic Carbon (TOC)**: a source rock must contain **> 0.5 wt% TOC** to be considered prospective, and high-quality source rocks exceed **2 – 5 wt% TOC**. ### Stage 2: Deposition and Preservation (Accumulation) - Organic matter settles onto an **anoxic (oxygen-deficient) seabed** — anoxic conditions are non-negotiable; aerobic bacteria would otherwise consume the organic matter and oxidise it back to CO₂. - Fine-grained clastic sediments (clay, mud, silt) bury the organic ooze rapidly, sealing it from further oxidation. - This buried mixture is called **sapropel**, the precursor of petroleum. ### Stage 3: Diagenesis and Kerogen Formation - As burial depth increases to **~ 0.5 – 1 km** and temperatures stay below ~ 50 °C, the sapropel undergoes **diagenesis**. - Microbial activity, compaction, and low-temperature chemical reactions transform the soft sapropel into a waxy, insoluble solid polymer called **kerogen**. - Kerogen is the **immediate parent of all petroleum**; it is not yet oil, but it is the fuel from which oil will be cooked. ### Stage 4: Catagenesis and Hydrocarbon Generation (The "Oil Window") - At depths of **2 – 4 km** and temperatures of **60 °C – 120 °C**, kerogen undergoes **thermal cracking** — long organic polymer chains break into shorter liquid hydrocarbon molecules. - This is the **catagenesis stage** and corresponds to the **oil window**. - The reaction is broadly endothermic and time-temperature dependent; the empirical **Lopatin Time-Temperature Index (TTI)** model relates burial history to maturation. - Above ~ 120 °C, the kerogen begins cracking into natural gas — the **gas window**. ### Stage 5: Migration, Accumulation, and Trapping - The newly formed hydrocarbons are expelled from the source rock (called **primary migration**) and move upward through porous, permeable carrier beds (called **secondary migration**). - Migration is driven by **buoyancy** (oil and gas are less dense than formation water), **capillary pressure**, and **formation pressure gradients**. - Hydrocarbons accumulate where an impermeable barrier — a **cap rock** (typically shale, salt, or tight limestone) — prevents further upward escape. This geometry is called a **petroleum trap**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Key Point:** A working petroleum system requires **four geological elements** in the right order: > 1. **Source rock** (contains kerogen) > 2. **Reservoir rock** (porous, permeable — typically sandstone or carbonate) > 3. **Cap rock** (impermeable seal) > 4. **Trap** (structural or stratigraphic geometry that focuses hydrocarbons) > > Plus two processes: **generation** (kerogen → oil) and **migration** (oil → trap). ## 2.2 Types of Kerogen — The Four Classes Kerogen is classified into four types (I, II, III, and IV) based on the **H/C and O/C atomic ratios** determined by elemental analysis (Van Krevelen diagram). This is one of the most exam-relevant sub-topics in KTU Module 1. | Type | Origin | H/C Ratio | O/C Ratio | Hydrocarbon Yield | KTU Keyword | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Type I** | Algal lacustrine sapropel | > 1.5 | < 0.1 | **Very high** — excellent oil source | "Algal, oil-prone" | | **Type II** | Marine planktonic | 1.2 – 1.5 | 0.1 – 0.2 | **High** — major oil and gas source | "Marine, oil + gas" | | **Type III** | Terrestrial higher plants | < 1.0 | > 0.2 | **Gas-prone**, some light oil | "Humic, gas-prone" | | **Type IV** | Oxidised/recycled inert carbon | < 0.5 | High | **Negligible** — no commercial HC | "Dead carbon" | > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's Tip:** KTU questions frequently ask students to plot H/C vs O/C or to identify kerogen type from elemental analysis. Memorise the Van Krevelen diagram coordinates for each type. ## 2.3 Petroleum Migration Mechanisms > [!IMPORTANT] > **Primary Migration** = expulsion of hydrocarbons from the source rock into the carrier bed. > **Secondary Migration** = movement of hydrocarbons through the carrier bed into the trap. The driving forces for secondary migration are captured by the following **engineering-grade relationships** (high-yield for KTU 2024): The **buoyancy pressure** that pushes oil upward through water-saturated pore space is given by: $$ \Delta P_b = (\rho_w - \rho_o) \cdot g \cdot h $$ where: - $\Delta P_b$ = buoyancy pressure differential (Pa) - $\rho_w$ = formation water density ($\approx 1.05 \times 10^3 \text{ kg/m}^3$) - $\rho_o$ = oil density ($\approx 700 - 900 \text{ kg/m}^3$) - $g$ = gravitational acceleration ($9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$) - $h$ = vertical height of the hydrocarbon column (m) The **capillary entry pressure** that the cap rock must overcome (and which ultimately traps the oil) is given by the **Laplace-Young equation** for a cylindrical pore throat: $$ P_c = \frac{2 \cdot \gamma \cdot \cos\theta}{r} $$ where: - $P_c$ = capillary entry pressure (Pa) - $\gamma$ = interfacial tension between oil and water ($\approx 20 - 30 \text{ mN/m}$ for oil-water) - $\theta$ = contact angle (degrees) - $r$ = pore throat radius (m) > A trap holds oil only if $P_c \ge \Delta P_b$ — i.e., the seal's capillary pressure must exceed the buoyancy pressure of the hydrocarbon column. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Quick Revision Table) | # | Concept / Formula | Expression | Variables | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Buoyancy pressure (migration) | $\Delta P_b = (\rho_w - \rho_o) g h$ | $\rho_w, \rho_o, h$ | Pa, kg/m³, m | | 2 | Capillary pressure (trap integrity) | $P_c = \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r}$ | $\gamma, \theta, r$ | Pa, N/m, °, m | | 3 | API gravity | $°\text{API} = \dfrac{141.5}{\rho_{60}} - 131.5$ | $\rho_{60}$ (relative density at 60 °F) | dimensionless | | 4 | Geothermal gradient (typical) | $dT/dz \approx 25 - 30$ | depth $z$ | °C/km | | 5 | Oil window lower bound (depth) | $z \approx 2 - 4$ km | temperature 60–120 °C | km, °C | | 6 | Source rock TOC threshold | $\text{TOC} \ge 0.5$ wt% | — | wt% | | 7 | Hydrocarbon elemental (C + H) | $94 - 99$ % of mass | C, H | wt% | | 8 | Van Krevelen H/C (Type I) | $\vert H/C \vert > 1.5$ | atomic ratio | dimensionless | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Reminder:** All numbers above are *empirical industry averages* — they are useful for back-of-envelope exam calculations, not for design of production facilities. Always state the assumed values explicitly in your KTU answers. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Relevance Understanding the origin of petroleum is **not merely academic** for an instrumentation engineer. The chemical composition set by the origin process (paraffinic vs naphthenic vs aromatic) directly determines: - The **refinery distillation curve** (which dictates the number of trays and reflux ratios in a CDU/VDU column — a key instrumentation topic in later modules). - The **sulphur and nitrogen content**, which fixes the sizing and control philosophy of hydrodesulphurisation (HDS) reactors. - The **wax and asphaltene content**, which dictates pipeline flow assurance, viscosity instrumentation, and pumping power. - The **API gravity**, which sets offshore loading and storage instrumentation ranges. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Buoyancy Pressure Driving Secondary Migration This derivation is one of the most commonly asked analytical questions in KTU Module 1 examinations, especially when the question is framed as a "numerical problem" worth 7 marks. ### Statement Derive an expression for the buoyancy pressure that drives oil upward through water-saturated porous rock during secondary migration. State clearly the assumption of hydrostatic equilibrium. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Define the system.** Consider a vertical column of oil-saturated porous rock of height $h$, sandwiched between a higher water-saturated zone (above the oil) and a lower water-saturated zone (below the oil-water contact). The pore space is interconnected and filled with formation water of density $\rho_w$ outside the oil column and oil of density $\rho_o$ inside the column. **Step 2 — Hydrostatic pressure assumption.** Assume the formation water obeys the **hydrostatic pressure relation**: $$ P(z) = P_0 + \rho_w \cdot g \cdot z $$ where $P_0$ is a reference pressure at the datum and $z$ is the vertical depth measured positive downward. **Step 3 — Pressure above the oil column (just at the top of the oil, in the water-saturated zone).** $$ P_{\text{top, water}} = P_0 + \rho_w \cdot g \cdot (z_{\text{top}}) $$ **Step 4 — Pressure at the top of the oil column (just inside the oil-saturated zone).** Inside the oil column, hydrostatic pressure is governed by the oil density: $$ P_{\text{top, oil}} = P_{\text{top, water}} $$ (Continuity of pressure at the interface.) **Step 5 — Pressure at the bottom of the oil column, evaluated in the water-saturated zone (outside the oil column).** $$ P_{\text{bot, water}} = P_0 + \rho_w \cdot g \cdot (z_{\text{top}} + h) $$ **Step 6 — Pressure at the bottom of the oil column, evaluated inside the oil column.** $$ P_{\text{bot, oil}} = P_{\text{top, oil}} + \rho_o \cdot g \cdot h $$ **Step 7 — The buoyancy pressure differential.** The pressure difference between the water at the bottom of the oil column and the oil at the same depth is the **buoyancy pressure** $\Delta P_b$ that pushes the oil upward: $$ \Delta P_b = P_{\text{bot, water}} - P_{\text{bot, oil}} $$ $$ \Delta P_b = \bigl[P_0 + \rho_w g (z_{\text{top}} + h)\bigr] - \bigl[P_{\text{top, oil}} + \rho_o g h\bigr] $$ **Step 8 — Substitute the continuity relation $P_{\text{top, oil}} = P_0 + \rho_w g z_{\text{top}}$.** $$ \Delta P_b = P_0 + \rho_w g z_{\text{top}} + \rho_w g h - P_0 - \rho_w g z_{\text{top}} - \rho_o g h $$ **Step 9 — Simplify.** $$ \Delta P_b = \rho_w g h - \rho_o g h $$ $$ \boxed{\Delta P_b = (\rho_w - \rho_o) \cdot g \cdot h} $$ ### Physical Interpretation - The buoyancy pressure scales **linearly with the height of the oil column** — taller oil columns produce larger driving forces. - It scales with the **density difference** between water and oil — light crude (low $\rho_o$) gives larger buoyancy, which is why light crudes migrate farther and accumulate in more traps. - For a typical oil column of $h = 50$ m with $\rho_w = 1050$ kg/m³ and $\rho_o = 850$ kg/m³, $\Delta P_b \approx 9.81$ kPa — small, but sufficient to drive oil through a permeable carrier bed over geological time. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style) > **Problem (KTU Style):** A crude oil of relative density 0.85 accumulates in a reservoir trap with a vertical oil column height of 40 m. The formation water density is 1.05 g/cm³. Calculate the buoyancy pressure differential driving secondary migration. If the cap rock's effective pore-throat radius is 2 μm and the oil-water interfacial tension is 25 mN/m with a contact angle of 30°, will the cap rock hold this column? (Assume $\cos 30° \approx 0.866$.) **Step 1 — Convert units.** $\rho_w = 1.05$ g/cm³ = 1050 kg/m³; $\rho_o = 0.85$ g/cm³ = 850 kg/m³; $h = 40$ m; $g = 9.81$ m/s². **Step 2 — Apply the buoyancy equation.** $$ \Delta P_b = (1050 - 850) \times 9.81 \times 40 $$ $$ \Delta P_b = 200 \times 9.81 \times 40 $$ $$ \Delta P_b = 78{,}480 \text{ Pa} \approx 78.48 \text{ kPa} $$ **Step 3 — Apply the Laplace capillary entry pressure.** $$ P_c = \frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r} = \frac{2 \times 0.025 \times 0.866}{2 \times 10^{-6}} $$ $$ P_c = \frac{0.0433}{2 \times 10^{-6}} = 21{,}650 \text{ Pa} \approx 21.65 \text{ kPa} $$ **Step 4 — Compare.** $$ \Delta P_b = 78.48 \text{ kPa} > P_c = 21.65 \text{ kPa} $$ **Conclusion:** The buoyancy pressure **exceeds** the capillary entry pressure of the cap rock. Therefore, the cap rock **will NOT hold** this 40 m oil column — the oil will leak through the cap rock and the trap will fail. The reservoir would either need a tighter cap rock (smaller $r$) or a shorter oil column. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget to **convert units consistently** before substituting. Mixing g/cm³ with m and N/m with μm is the most common calculation error. Always write the unit conversion as a separate step. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Source vs Reservoir vs Cap Rock | Property | Source Rock | Reservoir Rock | Cap Rock | |---|---|---|---| | **Primary function** | Generates hydrocarbons | Stores hydrocarbons | Seals hydrocarbons | | **Porosity** | Low–moderate (5–15%) | High (10–35%) | Very low (< 5%) | | **Permeability** | Very low (< 0.01 mD) | High (10 mD – 1 D) | Practically zero (< 0.001 mD) | | **Typical lithology** | Organic-rich shale, marl | Sandstone, carbonate, fractured chalk | Shale, salt, tight limestone, anhydrite | | **Critical petrophysical property** | TOC > 0.5 wt% | Porosity $\phi$ and permeability $k$ | Capillary entry pressure $P_c$ | | **Example formation** | Kimmeridge Clay (North Sea) | Brent Sandstone (North Sea) | Heather Formation shale | # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Petroleum Formation Process (End-to-End Block Flow) The following Mermaid flowchart traces the complete biogenic origin process from organic source material to a producible reservoir, with intermediate states and key parameters annotated. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Organic Source Material
Marine plankton, algae, bacteria] --> B[Deposition on Anoxic Seabed
TOC must exceed 0.5 wt%] B --> C[Sapropel Formation
Burial under fine clastic sediment] C --> D[Diagenesis Stage
Depth 0.5 to 1 km, T below 50 C] D --> E[Kerogen Formation
Type I II III or IV based on H/C and O/C] E --> F{Catagenesis Stage
Oil Window 60 to 120 C} F -->|Type I II kerogen| G[Liquid Crude Oil Generation
C8 to C40+ hydrocarbons] F -->|Type III kerogen| H[Natural Gas Generation
Methane dominated] G --> I[Primary Migration
Expulsion from source rock] H --> I I --> J[Secondary Migration
Buoyancy driven upward through carrier bed] J --> K{Trap Geometry Exists
Structural or stratigraphic} K -->|Yes| L[Accumulation in Reservoir Rock
Porous and permeable sandstone or carbonate] K -->|No| M[Hydrocarbons leak to surface
Seeps and tar sands] L --> N[Cap Rock Seal
Impermeable shale or salt] N --> O[Producible Petroleum Reservoir
Ready for exploration and drilling] M --> P[Surface expression
Pitch lake or methane seep] ``` ## 4.2 Petroleum System Elements and Processes ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Essential_Elements E1[Source Rock
Organic rich shale] E2[Reservoir Rock
Porous permeable] E3[Cap Rock
Impermeable seal] E4[Trap Geometry
Structural or stratigraphic] end subgraph Critical_Processes P1[Generation
Kerogen to hydrocarbons] P2[Migration
Buoyancy driven] end E1 --> P1 --> P2 P2 --> E2 E2 --> E3 E3 --> E4 E4 --> O[Working Petroleum System
Ready for production] ``` ## 4.3 Van Krevelen Diagram — Kerogen Classification (Block Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart TB Start[Van Krevelen Diagram
H/C atomic ratio vs O/C atomic ratio] Start --> T1[Type I Kerogen
H/C greater than 1.5
O/C less than 0.1
Algal lacustrine
Excellent oil source] Start --> T2[Type II Kerogen
H/C 1.2 to 1.5
O/C 0.1 to 0.2
Marine planktonic
Major oil and gas source] Start --> T3[Type III Kerogen
H/C less than 1.0
O/C greater than 0.2
Terrestrial humic
Gas prone] Start --> T4[Type IV Kerogen
H/C less than 0.5
Inert recycled carbon
No commercial yield] ``` ## 4.4 Migration and Trapping — Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD SR[Source Rock
Generates oil and gas] -->|Primary Migration
Expulsion under pressure| CB[Carrier Bed
Permeable sandstone] CB -->|Secondary Migration
Buoyancy upward| TR[Trap
Anticline fault or stratigraphic] TR --> RES[Reservoir Rock
Stores hydrocarbons] RES --> CAP[Cap Rock
Impermeable seal] CAP -->|If seal fails| LEAK[Leakage to surface] CAP -->|If seal holds| ACC[Accumulation
Commercial reservoir] ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!NOTE] > **Mark Distribution Reference (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):** > - Part A: 3 marks each (short answer/definition) > - Part B: 14 marks each (full descriptive, internal choice between two questions) > - Part C / Numerical: typically integrated into Part B sub-parts > - Bloom's Levels tested: Remember (L1), Understand (L2), Apply (L3), Analyse (L4) --- ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]** **Q: Define petroleum. List the two principal scientific theories explaining its origin.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Petroleum is a naturally occurring, complex mixture of hydrocarbons — predominantly alkanes, cycloalkanes, and aromatics — along with minor amounts of sulphur, nitrogen, oxygen, and trace metals, found in sedimentary rock formations beneath the Earth's surface. It is recovered as crude oil, natural gas, and bitumen. The two principal scientific theories of origin are: 1. **Biogenic (Organic) Theory:** Petroleum is derived from the buried remains of once-living organisms, mainly marine plankton, algae, and bacteria, which accumulated on anoxic seabeds and were transformed by heat and pressure over millions of years. This is the universally accepted modern theory. 2. **Abiogenic (Inorganic) Theory:** Petroleum originates from deep-Earth primordial hydrocarbons — primarily methane — synthesised in the upper mantle from inorganic carbon and hydrogen, which then migrated upward into the crust. (1 mark for each theory + 1 mark for the definition.) --- ### Question A2 **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]** **Q: What is kerogen? Classify the four types of kerogen based on their hydrocarbon-generating potential.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Kerogen is the insoluble, waxy, solid organic polymer found in source rocks; it is the immediate precursor of all petroleum hydrocarbons. It is formed during the diagenesis of buried organic matter and is thermally cracked into oil and gas during catagenesis. The four types of kerogen are: 1. **Type I:** Algal lacustrine origin; H/C > 1.5; very high oil yield. 2. **Type II:** Marine planktonic origin; H/C ≈ 1.2 – 1.5; high oil and gas yield. 3. **Type III:** Terrestrial higher plant (humic) origin; H/C < 1.0; primarily gas-prone. 4. **Type IV:** Inert, oxidised, or recycled carbon; negligible commercial yield. (1 mark for definition; 1 mark for naming the four types; 1 mark for the correct classification criterion.) --- ## Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > **KTU Internal Choice Pattern:** Two questions are offered, and the student answers ONE. Each question carries two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Sub-parts test progressively higher Bloom's levels (L2 → L3 → L4). ### Question B-A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]** **Q (a) [7 marks]:** With the aid of a neat block flow diagram, describe the biogenic theory of petroleum formation. Clearly identify the five stages from source material to producible reservoir. **Q (b) [7 marks]:** Explain the differences between primary migration, secondary migration, and accumulation of hydrocarbons. What are the four geological elements that must coexist for a working petroleum system? --- #### Model Answer to Q (a): **Introduction (1 mark):** The biogenic (organic) theory states that petroleum originates from the buried remains of once-living marine and lacustrine organisms, transformed by heat and pressure over geological time scales of 10 – 100 million years. **Stage 1 — Production of Organic Matter (1 mark):** Marine environments host prolific phytoplankton, zooplankton, cyanobacteria, and algae. Their organic remains accumulate on the seafloor. Higher plant debris may contribute in deltaic settings. **Stage 2 — Deposition and Preservation (1 mark):** Organic matter settles onto an anoxic (oxygen-deficient) seabed. Fine clastic sediments (clay, mud) rapidly bury the ooze, sealing it from oxidation. The mixture is called sapropel. Source rocks must exceed 0.5 wt% TOC. **Stage 3 — Diagenesis and Kerogen Formation (1 mark):** At depths of 0.5 – 1 km and temperatures below ~ 50 °C, the sapropel undergoes diagenesis to form kerogen — an insoluble solid organic polymer. Kerogen is classified into Types I–IV by H/C and O/C ratios. **Stage 4 — Catagenesis and Oil/Gas Generation (1.5 marks):** At depths of 2 – 4 km and temperatures of 60 – 120 °C (the "oil window"), kerogen undergoes thermal cracking to yield liquid hydrocarbons (Type I and II kerogen) and natural gas (Type III kerogen, 120 – 225 °C). **Stage 5 — Migration, Accumulation, and Trapping (1.5 marks):** Hydrocarbons are expelled from the source rock (primary migration) and migrate upward through porous carrier beds (secondary migration) under buoyancy, until they are trapped beneath an impermeable cap rock in a reservoir. **Conclusion (1 mark):** The five stages must occur in the correct order and within suitable time-temperature-pressure windows for a commercial petroleum reservoir to form. **Block Flow Diagram (included in the answer):** A simple five-block flow diagram showing: Organic Source → Anoxic Deposition → Diagenesis → Kerogen → Catagenesis → Migration → Trap → Reservoir. --- #### Model Answer to Q (b): **Primary Migration (2 marks):** Primary migration is the **expulsion** of hydrocarbons from the source rock into the adjacent carrier bed. It occurs once the hydrocarbon saturation in the source rock exceeds a critical threshold, after which the hydrocarbons are squeezed out by compaction and micro-fracturing driven by continued burial and overpressure. **Secondary Migration (2 marks):** Secondary migration is the **upward movement** of hydrocarbons through a porous, permeable carrier bed (typically sandstone or carbonate) toward a trap. The driving force is **buoyancy**: oil and gas are less dense than formation water, so they rise. The path is generally along the carrier bed's top, beneath an impermeable seal, until a structural or stratigraphic trap is encountered. **Accumulation (1 mark):** Accumulation is the **concentration** of hydrocarbons in the pore space of a reservoir rock at the trap, where the seal's capillary entry pressure exceeds the buoyancy pressure of the column. Above this critical column height, the seal fails and oil leaks. **Four Geological Elements of a Working Petroleum System (2 marks):** A working petroleum system requires: 1. **Source rock** — organic-rich, has generated hydrocarbons. 2. **Reservoir rock** — porous and permeable (sandstone, carbonate). 3. **Cap rock** — impermeable seal (shale, salt, anhydrite). 4. **Trap** — structural (anticline, fault) or stratigraphic (pinch-out, reef) geometry. Plus two processes: **generation** and **migration**. --- ### Question B-B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]** **Q (a) [7 marks]:** A crude oil of relative density 0.88 has accumulated in a reservoir with a vertical oil column height of 60 m. The formation water has a density of 1.04 g/cm³, and the cap rock has an effective pore-throat radius of 1.5 μm with an oil-water interfacial tension of 28 mN/m and a contact angle of 25°. Determine (i) the buoyancy pressure driving secondary migration, and (ii) the capillary entry pressure of the cap rock. Will the seal hold? Justify. **Q (b) [7 marks]:** Compare the biogenic and abiogenic theories of petroleum origin. Which is universally accepted, and what evidence supports it? --- #### Model Answer to Q (a): **Given data:** - $\rho_o = 0.88$ g/cm³ = **880 kg/m³** - $h = 60$ m - $\rho_w = 1.04$ g/cm³ = **1040 kg/m³** - $r = 1.5$ μm = $1.5 \times 10^{-6}$ m - $\gamma = 28$ mN/m = 0.028 N/m - $\theta = 25°$, so $\cos 25° \approx 0.9063$ - $g = 9.81$ m/s² **Part (i) — Buoyancy pressure (3 marks):** $$ \Delta P_b = (\rho_w - \rho_o) \cdot g \cdot h $$ $$ \Delta P_b = (1040 - 880) \times 9.81 \times 60 $$ $$ \Delta P_b = 160 \times 9.81 \times 60 $$ $$ \Delta P_b = 94{,}176 \text{ Pa} \approx 94.18 \text{ kPa} $$ **Part (ii) — Capillary entry pressure (3 marks):** $$ P_c = \frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r} $$ $$ P_c = \frac{2 \times 0.028 \times 0.9063}{1.5 \times 10^{-6}} $$ $$ P_c = \frac{0.0508}{1.5 \times 10^{-6}} = 33{,}853 \text{ Pa} \approx 33.85 \text{ kPa} $$ **Part (iii) — Seal integrity (1 mark):** $$ \Delta P_b = 94.18 \text{ kPa} > P_c = 33.85 \text{ kPa} $$ The buoyancy pressure **exceeds** the capillary entry pressure. Therefore, the cap rock will **NOT hold** the 60 m oil column. The oil will leak through the cap rock, and the trap will fail. The reservoir would require a tighter cap (smaller $r$) or a shorter oil column to be commercially viable. --- #### Model Answer to Q (b): **Biogenic Theory (3 marks):** - Petroleum originates from the buried remains of once-living organisms, primarily marine plankton, algae, and bacteria. - Organic matter accumulates on anoxic seabeds, is buried under fine clastic sediments, and transformed by heat (60 – 120 °C) and pressure over 10 – 100 million years into kerogen and then into liquid/gaseous hydrocarbons. - The organic origin is supported by the presence of **biomarkers** (e.g., porphyrins, isoprenoids like pristane and phytane, hopanes) that can be traced directly to chlorophyll and bacterial cell walls. - The carbon isotope ratio (δ¹³C) of crude oils is consistent with photosynthetic carbon fixation, not mantle-derived carbon. **Abiogenic Theory (2.5 marks):** - Petroleum is synthesised in the Earth's upper mantle from inorganic carbon (CO₂, carbonates) and hydrogen (H₂) under high temperature and pressure, yielding methane and higher hydrocarbons. - These primordial hydrocarbons migrate upward through deep faults and fractures into the crust. - Supporting evidence is sparse but includes the presence of hydrocarbons in some igneous and metamorphic rocks and in meteorites. **Conclusion (1.5 marks):** The **biogenic theory is universally accepted** in modern petroleum geology. The biomarker evidence, carbon isotope signatures, and the consistent association of commercial oil deposits with sedimentary basins (not crystalline basement rocks) collectively disprove a purely abiogenic origin. The abiogenic theory remains historically important and is occasionally invoked to explain trace hydrocarbons in non-sedimentary environments, but it does not account for the bulk of commercial petroleum. --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Board Exams (Origin of Petroleum)** > > 1. **Confusing diagenesis with catagenesis.** Diagenesis is the *low-temperature* formation of kerogen (below ~ 50 °C); catagenesis is the *higher-temperature* cracking of kerogen into oil (60 – 120 °C). Many students use the terms interchangeably — this is a guaranteed 2-mark loss. > > 2. **Forgetting unit conversions in numericals.** A classic KTU 7-mark question on buoyancy vs capillary pressure requires careful conversion: g/cm³ → kg/m³, μm → m, mN/m → N/m. Mixing units loses 1–2 marks. > > 3. **Listing kerogen types without the H/C and O/C criteria.** Simply naming "Type I, II, III, IV" without the elemental ratio criterion is incomplete. Always pair the type with the H/C and O/C numbers and the source environment. > > 4. **Confusing source rock with reservoir rock.** A source rock *generates* hydrocarbons but typically has very low permeability and cannot *produce* them at commercial rates. A reservoir rock *stores and produces* hydrocarbons and must have high porosity and permeability. Examiners love this distinction. > > 5. **Not identifying the four petroleum system elements explicitly.** KTU's official answer key for 7-mark questions on petroleum systems expects the student to write the **source rock, reservoir rock, cap rock, and trap** as a numbered list. A vague paragraph loses marks. > > 6. **Skipping the diagram.** A 7-mark question on the biogenic theory *requires* a labelled block flow diagram. Without it, expect a 2-mark deduction even if the prose is excellent. > > 7. **Writing "petroleum comes from dinosaurs."** This is a popular-science oversimplification and is **factually misleading**. Petroleum is overwhelmingly derived from microscopic marine plankton and algae, not from large vertebrates. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Origin of Petroleum)** > > ✅ **Definition:** Petroleum = naturally occurring complex mixture of hydrocarbons (C + H = 94 – 99 % by mass) with minor S, N, O, and trace metals, found in sedimentary formations. > > ✅ **Theories:** Biogenic (organic, accepted) vs Abiogenic (inorganic, fringe). KTU expects both, with the biogenic theory as the standard model. > > ✅ **Five stages of biogenic origin:** (1) Production of organic matter, (2) Anoxic deposition, (3) Diagenesis → kerogen, (4) Catagenesis → oil window (60 – 120 °C) / gas window (120 – 225 °C), (5) Migration and trapping. > > ✅ **Kerogen types:** Type I (algal, H/C > 1.5, oil-prone), Type II (marine, oil + gas), Type III (humic, gas-prone), Type IV (inert, no yield). > > ✅ **Four petroleum system elements:** Source rock, Reservoir rock, Cap rock, Trap. Two processes: Generation, Migration. > > ✅ **Key formulas:** $\Delta P_b = (\rho_w - \rho_o) g h$ and $P_c = 2 \gamma \cos\theta / r$. A trap holds oil if $P_c \ge \Delta P_b$. > > ✅ **Source rock threshold:** TOC ≥ 0.5 wt% (high-quality > 2 wt%). > > ✅ **Geothermal gradient:** ~ 25 – 30 °C/km. > > ✅ **Oil window:** 2 – 4 km depth, 60 – 120 °C. > > ✅ **Biomarker evidence:** Porphyrins, pristane, phytane, hopanes, δ¹³C isotope signature — these are the definitive proof of biogenic origin. > > ✅ **Exam hot-spots:** (a) Five-stage biogenic diagram; (b) Kerogen classification with H/C and O/C; (c) Buoyancy vs capillary pressure numericals with unit conversions; (d) Differences between source, reservoir, and cap rock. > > ✅ **Avoid these errors:** Confusing diagenesis/catagenesis, mixing units in numericals, omitting the diagram, stating the "dinosaur origin" myth.

topics.title: Source Rock and Maturation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET861 - INSTRUMENTATION IN PETROCHEMICAL INDUSTRIES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Source Rock and Maturation # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Source Rock — Formal Academic Definition A **Source Rock** is defined as a fine-grained sedimentary rock (typically shale, mudstone, or carbonate) that contains sufficient quantities of organic matter (kerogen) capable of generating and expelling hydrocarbons (oil and/or natural gas) when subjected to appropriate thermal stress (burial and heating) over geological time. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for Petroleum Engineering, a rock must satisfy **three essential conditions** to be classified as a source rock: 1. **Quantity of Organic Matter**: Total Organic Carbon (TOC) $\geq$ **0.5 wt%** (minimum) and preferably $\geq$ **1.0–2.0 wt%** for a good source rock. 2. **Quality of Organic Matter**: Presence of hydrogen-rich kerogen (Type I or Type II) capable of yielding hydrocarbons. 3. **Thermal Maturity**: The rock must have been buried deep enough to reach the **"oil window"** (typically 60–120 °C). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight**: A source rock is not just an organic-rich rock — it must have **generated**, **expelled**, and **migrated** hydrocarbons to a reservoir rock. A rock rich in organic carbon that has never generated oil is technically an **immature source rock**, not a true (effective) source rock. ## 1.2 Maturation — Formal Academic Definition **Maturation** is the irreversible, temperature- and time-dependent process by which **kerogen** (insoluble organic matter in sedimentary rocks) is progressively transformed into liquid and gaseous hydrocarbons through **thermal cracking** of carbon–carbon bonds. It is essentially the **"cooking" of organic matter** inside the Earth. The maturation process is governed by **three primary factors**: - **Temperature** (the dominant factor — governed by the geothermal gradient, typically **25–30 °C/km**). - **Time** (duration of burial). - **Pressure** (lithostatic and pore-fluid pressure). The relationship is often described by the **T-T (Time-Temperature) Index** of Lopatin (1971) and Waples (1980), but the simplest formulation is **Arrhenius-type kinetics**, where the reaction rate doubles for every **10 °C** rise in temperature. ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy — The "Pressure Cooker" Model** Imagine a **kitchen pressure cooker** as the geological basin, **raw rice and lentils** as the organic matter (kerogen) deposited in a sedimentary environment, **water** as the pore fluids, and **heat from the burner** as the geothermal gradient of the Earth. - When the cooker is **cold**, the rice and lentils remain uncooked (analogous to **immature kerogen** in a young basin). - As you slowly increase the heat and let the cooker run for a long time, the rice and lentils begin to soften and release steam (analogous to **early diagenesis** and the start of **bitumen formation**). - At the right temperature and after sufficient time, the food is perfectly cooked — moist and aromatic (analogous to **peak oil generation** in the **catagenesis stage**). - If you overcook it, the food burns to dry charcoal and only smoke is released (analogous to **metagenesis**, where only dry gas and eventually graphite remain). **Source rock = the uncooked ingredients sealed in the cooker. Maturation = the slow, sustained cooking process.** Without the right combination of ingredients (organic richness), temperature (heat), and time (burial duration), you will never produce a meal (hydrocarbon reservoir). ## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics in Source Rock Evaluation The following **standardized parameters** (per **KTU 2024 Scheme** and industry practice) are universally used: | Metric | Symbol | Typical Range | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Total Organic Carbon | TOC | 0.5 – 20 | wt% | | Vitrinite Reflectance | Ro | 0.2 – 4.0 | % | | Hydrogen Index | HI | 50 – 900 | mg HC/g TOC | | Oxygen Index | OI | 10 – 200 | mg CO₂/g TOC | | Production Index | PI | 0.0 – 0.4 | dimensionless | | Tmax | Tmax | 380 – 550 | °C | --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Maturation Pathway — Structured Logic Breakdown The transformation of buried organic matter into hydrocarbons follows a well-defined **three-stage thermal evolution pathway**. Each stage is characterized by a distinct **temperature window**, **Ro range**, and **dominant product**. ### Stage 1 — Diagenesis (Immature Stage) - **Temperature range**: 0 – 60 °C - **Vitrinite Reflectance (Ro)**: $\lt$ **0.5%** - **Burial depth**: 0 – 1000 m (typical) - **Dominant process**: Microbial activity, dewatering, loss of oxygen functional groups - **Product**: Biogenic methane (in some cases), kerogen formation from biopolymers - **State of kerogen**: Solid, immature, incapable of significant hydrocarbon generation ### Stage 2 — Catagenesis (Mature Stage — Hydrocarbon Generation Window) - **Temperature range**: 60 – 175 °C - **Vitrinite Reflectance (Ro)**: **0.5% – 2.0%** - **Burial depth**: 1000 – 4500 m - **Dominant process**: Thermal cracking of kerogen (C–C bond breakage) - **Sub-stages**: - **Early catagenesis** (Ro = 0.5–0.7%): Wet gas and condensate begin forming - **Peak oil window** (Ro = 0.7–1.0%): Maximum liquid hydrocarbon generation - **Late catagenesis** (Ro = 1.0–1.3%): Wet gas and light oil - **Condensate/wet gas window** (Ro = 1.3–2.0%) ### Stage 3 — Metagenesis (Post-Mature Stage) - **Temperature range**: $\gt$ 175 °C - **Vitrinite Reflectance (Ro)**: $\gt$ **2.0%** - **Burial depth**: $\gt$ 4500 m - **Dominant process**: Cracking of residual kerogen and previously formed oil into dry gas - **Product**: Dry methane gas, eventually graphite (carbon residue) > [!IMPORTANT] > **"The Why" Behind the Stages**: At low temperatures, the thermal energy is insufficient to break the strong C–C bonds in kerogen (bond dissociation energy $\approx$ **350 kJ/mol**). As temperature increases, molecules acquire enough kinetic energy to overcome this activation barrier, causing progressive bond scission and hydrocarbon release. ## 2.2 Kerogen Types and Hydrocarbon Potential Kerogen is classified into **four types** based on its **H/C and O/C atomic ratios** (Van Krevelen Diagram): | Kerogen Type | Origin | H/C Ratio | O/C Ratio | Hydrocarbon Potential | Depositional Environment | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Type I** | Algal, lacustrine | $\gt$ 1.5 | $\lt$ 0.1 | **Excellent — oil-prone** | Freshwater lakes, algal mats | | **Type II** | Marine planktonic | 1.0 – 1.5 | 0.1 – 0.2 | **Good — oil and gas prone** | Marine shales, carbonates | | **Type III** | Terrestrial (woody) | $\lt$ 1.0 | 0.2 – 0.3 | **Gas-prone only** | Deltaic, fluvial, coal seams | | **Type IV** | Recycled/inert | $\lt$ 0.5 | variable | **None (inert)** | Oxidized, terrigenous reworked | > [!NOTE] > **Instrumentation Tie-in**: The H/C and O/C ratios are measured using **elemental analyzers** (CHN-O analyzers), and the kerogen type is plotted on the **Van Krevelen Diagram** — a fundamental KTU exam concept. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table summarizes **all critical formulas** for solving numerical problems on Source Rock and Maturation in the KTU 2024 Scheme examination. | # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables and Units | Boundary / Validity Condition | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Total Organic Carbon | $\text{TOC} = \dfrac{\text{Organic Carbon Mass}}{\text{Rock Mass}} \times 100$ | wt% | Good source: TOC $\geq$ 1.0 wt% | | 2 | Hydrogen Index | $\text{HI} = \dfrac{S_2}{\text{TOC}} \times 100$ | mg HC / g TOC | Type I: HI $\gt$ 600; Type III: HI $\lt$ 150 | | 3 | Oxygen Index | $\text{OI} = \dfrac{S_3}{\text{TOC}} \times 100$ | mg CO₂ / g TOC | Decreases with maturation | | 4 | Production Index | $\text{PI} = \dfrac{S_1}{S_1 + S_2}$ | dimensionless | PI $\lt$ 0.1: immature; PI $\gt$ 0.4: mature | | 5 | Transformation Ratio | $\text{TR} = \dfrac{S_1}{S_1 + S_2 + S_{2\text{residual}}} \times 100$ | % | 0% $\leq$ TR $\leq$ 100% | | 6 | Lopatin TTI | $\text{TTI} = \sum\limits_{i} 2^{n_i} \cdot \Delta t_i$ | dimensionless | TTI $\lt$ 15: immature; 15–75: oil window | | 7 | Geothermal Gradient | $G = \dfrac{\Delta T}{\Delta Z}$ | °C / 100 m | Normal: 2.5 – 3.0 °C / 100 m | | 8 | Arrhenius Rate | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ | s$^{-1}$ | $E_a \approx 150$ – 200 kJ/mol for kerogen cracking | | 9 | Vitrinite Reflectance | $R_o = \dfrac{\text{Incident Light Intensity} - \text{Reflected Light Intensity}}{\text{Incident Light Intensity}} \times 100$ | % | Measured in oil immersion, $\lambda$ = 546 nm | | 10 | Equivalent Vitrinite (Barker Eq.) | $\text{Rc} = 0.018 \cdot \text{Tmax} - 7.16$ | % | Valid for 435 °C $\lt$ Tmax $\lt$ 470 °C | **Where**: - $S_1$ = Free hydrocarbons already generated (mg HC / g rock) - $S_2$ = Hydrocarbons generated by thermal cracking of kerogen (mg HC / g rock) - $S_3$ = CO₂ released from kerogen (mg CO₂ / g rock) - $T_{\max}$ = Temperature at peak $S_2$ generation (°C) - $A$ = Frequency factor (pre-exponential constant) - $E_a$ = Activation energy (J/mol) - $R$ = Universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The understanding of source rock and maturation is the **foundation of petroleum exploration**. In modern petrochemical industries: - **Exploration geologists** use maturation data to identify "sweet spots" for drilling. - **Reservoir engineers** use kerogen typing to predict fluid type (oil vs. gas) before drilling. - **Refinery planning teams** correlate source rock composition with crude oil quality (API gravity, sulfur content). - **Instrumentation systems** such as **Rock-Eval Pyrolyzers**, **Vitrinite Reflectance Microscopes**, and **GC-MS** units are deployed in laboratories to quantify these parameters within minutes. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Calculation of Total Organic Carbon (TOC) from Rock-Eval Pyrolysis The **Rock-Eval Pyrolysis** technique (developed by **IFP Energies Nouvelles, France**) is the industry-standard instrumental method for source rock evaluation. The steps below derive the TOC from Rock-Eval outputs. ### Given Data (Typical KTU Numerical Problem Setup): - Mass of crushed rock sample: $m_{\text{rock}} = 100$ mg - $S_1$ (free hydrocarbons): $S_1 = 2.0$ mg HC / g rock - $S_2$ (pyrolyzed hydrocarbons): $S_2 = 8.0$ mg HC / g rock - $S_3$ (CO₂ released): $S_3 = 1.5$ mg CO₂ / g rock - Residual carbon (from oxidation at 850 °C): $S_4 = 3.0$ mg C / g rock ### Step 1 — Express the Pyrolyzed Carbon (PC) The carbon contained in the $S_2$ peak (hydrocarbons generated from kerogen cracking) is converted to elemental carbon equivalent. Approximately **83% of $S_2$ is carbon** (standard empirical conversion for hydrocarbons). $$ \text{PC} = 0.083 \times S_2 $$ Substituting $S_2 = 8.0$ mg HC / g rock: $$ \text{PC} = 0.083 \times 8.0 = 0.664 \text{ mg C / g rock} $$ ### Step 2 — Express the Residual Carbon (RC) The residual carbon $S_4$ is directly obtained from the oxidation phase at 850 °C: $$ \text{RC} = S_4 = 3.0 \text{ mg C / g rock} $$ ### Step 3 — Compute Total Carbon (TC) Total Carbon is the sum of Pyrolyzed Carbon and Residual Carbon: $$ \text{TC} = \text{PC} + \text{RC} $$ $$ \text{TC} = 0.664 + 3.0 = 3.664 \text{ mg C / g rock} $$ ### Step 4 — Compute Total Organic Carbon (TOC) TOC is Total Carbon minus the Mineral Carbon (if any). In a shale sample free of carbonates, Mineral Carbon $\approx 0$, so: $$ \text{TOC} = \text{TC} - \text{Mineral Carbon} $$ $$ \text{TOC} = 3.664 - 0 = 3.664 \text{ mg C / g rock} $$ ### Step 5 — Convert to Weight Percent (wt%) To express TOC as a weight percentage (the standard reporting unit): $$ \text{TOC (wt\%)} = \dfrac{\text{TOC (mg C / g rock)}}{10} $$ $$ \text{TOC (wt\%)} = \dfrac{3.664}{10} = 0.3664 \text{ wt\%} $$ ### Step 6 — Interpret the Result A TOC of **0.3664 wt%** falls **below the 0.5 wt% threshold**, indicating this rock is a **poor source rock** according to industry and KTU classification standards. > [!NOTE] > **Conversion Logic Recap**: The factor **0.083** in Step 1 is derived from the empirical observation that the average mass fraction of carbon in $C_nH_{2n+2}$-type hydrocarbons generated from kerogen is approximately **83%** (e.g., octane C₈H₁₈ has a C mass fraction of 84%). ## 3.2 Derivation: Vitrinite Reflectance and Equivalent Ro from Tmax In the absence of measurable vitrinite (e.g., in pre-Devonian or carbonate rocks), the **Barker & Pawlewicz (1986) empirical equation** is used to estimate equivalent vitrinite reflectance from $T_{\max}$: ### Given: - $T_{\max} = 445$ °C (typical for a marginally mature source rock) ### Derivation Steps: **Step 1** — Recall the Barker empirical correlation: $$ R_c = 0.018 \times T_{\max} - 7.16 $$ **Step 2** — Substitute $T_{\max} = 445$ °C: $$ R_c = 0.018 \times 445 - 7.16 $$ **Step 3** — Perform the multiplication: $$ R_c = 8.01 - 7.16 $$ **Step 4** — Final result: $$ R_c = 0.85 \text{ \%} $$ **Step 5** — Interpret using the KTU maturity classification: - $R_c = 0.85\%$ falls in the **0.7% – 1.0%** range $\rightarrow$ **Peak oil window** (early mature, excellent liquid hydrocarbon generation). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Validity Boundary**: This equation is only valid for $435$ °C $\lt T_{\max} \lt 470$ °C. Outside this range, errors become significant. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Source Rock Quality vs. Maturation Level The following **2D classification matrix** is a high-frequency KTU question type. It cross-references **source rock quality** (based on TOC) with **maturation level** (based on Ro) to determine the **petroleum potential**. | | **Immature** (Ro $\lt$ 0.5%) | **Mature — Oil Window** (Ro = 0.5 – 1.3%) | **Overmature — Gas Window** (Ro $\gt$ 1.3%) | |---|---|---|---| | **Poor Source** (TOC $\lt$ 0.5%) | No potential | Marginal gas | No potential | | **Fair Source** (TOC = 0.5 – 1.0%) | Immature oil-prone | Modest oil potential | Dry gas potential | | **Good Source** (TOC = 1.0 – 2.0%) | Immature — needs more burial | **Commercial oil reservoir potential** | Wet gas / condensate potential | | **Excellent Source** (TOC $\gt$ 2.0%) | Immature — needs more burial | **High-quality commercial oil field** | Large dry gas field potential | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Source Rock Maturation Pathway (Thermal Evolution) The following diagram illustrates the **complete thermal maturation pathway** of kerogen from deposition through metamorphism, including all three stages and the hydrocarbon products generated at each stage. ```mermaid graph TD A[Organic Matter Deposition
in Sediment] --> B[Diagenesis Stage
Temperature 0 to 60 C
Ro less than 0.5 percent] B --> C[Catagenesis Stage
Temperature 60 to 175 C
Ro 0.5 to 2.0 percent] C --> D[Metagenesis Stage
Temperature greater than 175 C
Ro greater than 2.0 percent] B --> E[Kerogen Formation
Type I II III IV] E --> F[Type I Kerogen
Algal Lacustrine
Oil Prone] E --> G[Type II Kerogen
Marine Planktonic
Oil and Gas Prone] E --> H[Type III Kerogen
Terrestrial Woody
Gas Prone] C --> I[Early Catagenesis
Ro 0.5 to 0.7 percent
Wet Gas Formation] C --> J[Peak Oil Window
Ro 0.7 to 1.0 percent
Liquid Oil Generation] C --> K[Late Catagenesis
Ro 1.0 to 1.3 percent
Light Oil and Condensate] C --> L[Wet Gas Window
Ro 1.3 to 2.0 percent

Primary Migration] G --> O H --> O I --> O J --> O K --> O L --> O M --> O O --> P[Secondary Migration
to Reservoir Rock] P --> Q[Accumulation in
Porous Reservoir Trap] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Source Rock Evaluation Workflow Using Instrumentation This block diagram maps the **instrumentation workflow** used in modern petrochemical laboratories to evaluate source rock quality and maturation level — a core KTU 2024 Scheme learning outcome. ```mermaid graph LR A1[Rock Sample Collection
Drill Cuttings or Core] --> A2[Sample Preparation
Crushing and Pulverizing
to 100 mesh] A2 --> B1[CHNSO Elemental Analyzer

Mass Spectrometer

H by C Ratio

and Tmax Maturity] B3 --> C3[Ro Maturity

via Biomarkers] C1 --> D1[Van Krevelen Diagram
Kerogen Classification] C2 --> D2[Modified Van Krevelen
Plot for Kerogen Type] C3 --> D3[Maturity Window
Determination] C4 --> D4[Depositional Environment

Assessment Report] D2 --> E1 D3 --> E1 D4 --> E1 E1 --> F1[Petroleum Potential
Classification] F1 --> F2[Good for Oil Generation] F1 --> F3[Good for Gas Generation] F1 --> F4[Non Source Rock Reject] ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Petroleum System from Source to Trap This diagram provides a **functional architecture view** of how a source rock fits into the larger petroleum system, emphasizing the migration pathway and the role of maturation. ```mermaid graph TD SRC[Source Rock
Organic Rich Shale
TOC greater than 1 wt percent] --> MAT[Maturation Process
Thermal Cracking
of Kerogen] MAT --> HC[Generated Hydrocarbons
Oil and Gas Pools

Pressure Driven
