Buoyancy Driven
Upward through Carrier Bed] MIG --> RES[Reservoir Rock
Porous Sandstone
or Carbonate] RES --> SEAL[Seal Rock
Impermeable Shale
or Evaporite Cap] SEAL --> TRAP[Structural Trap
Anticline Fault
Stratigraphic Trap] TRAP --> ACC[Hydrocarbon Accumulation
Commercial Petroleum
Deposit] ACC --> PROD[Production Well
Extraction and
Surface Processing] MAT -.Instrumentation.- INST[Rock Eval Pyrolyzer
Vitrinite Microscope
GC MS System] INST -.Provides.- MAT ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation Tip for KTU Exam**: In Part B (14-mark) questions on this topic, the examiner expects you to label each block with its **function**, the **instrument used** for measurement, and the **typical parameter range**. The three key instruments are: **(1) Rock-Eval Pyrolyzer** (for S1, S2, S3, Tmax), **(2) Vitrinite Reflectance Microscope** (for Ro%), and **(3) CHNSO Analyzer** (for TOC and elemental ratios). --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Model Question Bank] **Define source rock. List the three essential conditions that a rock must satisfy to be classified as a source rock.** **Model Answer (3 Marks)**: A source rock is a fine-grained sedimentary rock containing sufficient organic matter (kerogen) that can generate and expel hydrocarbons upon thermal maturation. The three essential conditions are: 1. **Quantity of organic matter**: TOC $\geq$ **0.5 wt%** (preferably $\geq$ 1.0 wt% for a good source rock). 2. **Quality of organic matter**: Presence of hydrogen-rich kerogen (Type I or Type II) with adequate H/C atomic ratio. 3. **Thermal maturity**: The rock must have reached the oil window (Ro $\geq$ 0.5%, T $\geq$ 60 °C) for hydrocarbon generation. --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Model Question Bank] **What is maturation? Name the three stages of thermal maturation of kerogen.** **Model Answer (3 Marks)**: Maturation is the irreversible thermal transformation of kerogen into liquid and gaseous hydrocarbons as a function of temperature and time. The three stages are: 1. **Diagenesis** (Temperature: 0 – 60 °C, Ro $\lt$ 0.5%) — formation of kerogen and biogenic methane. 2. **Catagenesis** (Temperature: 60 – 175 °C, Ro = 0.5 – 2.0%) — main hydrocarbon generation window (oil and wet gas). 3. **Metagenesis** (Temperature $\gt$ 175 °C, Ro $\gt$ 2.0%) — cracking to dry gas and eventual graphite residue. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A — Option 1: [KTU University Exam – Model Question Bank] **(a) Explain in detail the different types of kerogen and their hydrocarbon potential. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer (7 Marks)**: Kerogen is the insoluble organic matter in sedimentary rocks, classified by **Van Krevelen (1961)** into four types based on atomic H/C and O/C ratios: 1. **Type I Kerogen**: - **Origin**: Algal deposits, lacustrine (lake) environments, botryococcus algae. - **H/C ratio** $\gt$ 1.5, **O/C ratio** $\lt$ 0.1 - **Hydrocarbon potential**: Excellent — **oil-prone**, can generate up to **80%** of its weight as hydrocarbons. - **Example**: Green River Shale (USA), torbanite. 2. **Type II Kerogen**: - **Origin**: Marine planktonic organisms, deposited in reducing marine environments. - **H/C ratio** = 1.0 – 1.5, **O/C ratio** = 0.1 – 0.2 - **Hydrocarbon potential**: Good — **oil and gas prone**, generates ~50–60% hydrocarbons. - **Example**: Kimmeridge Clay (North Sea), Paris Basin Toarcian Shale. 3. **Type III Kerogen**: - **Origin**: Terrestrial higher plant material (wood, pollen, spores), deposited in deltaic/fluvial settings. - **H/C ratio** $\lt$ 1.0, **O/C ratio** = 0.2 – 0.3 - **Hydrocarbon potential**: Gas-prone only, generates **methane-rich dry gas**. - **Example**: Coal seams, Carboniferous coal-bearing strata. 4. **Type IV Kerogen**: - **Origin**: Highly oxidized, reworked terrestrial organic matter. - **H/C ratio** $\lt$ 0.5 - **Hydrocarbon potential**: **None — inert**, considered as dead carbon. - **Example**: Soot, fusinite, inertinite-rich sediments. The classification is visualized on the **Van Krevelen Diagram** (H/C vs. O/C plot), which tracks maturation paths as both ratios decrease with progressive thermal evolution. --- **(b) Describe the Rock-Eval Pyrolysis technique for source rock evaluation with a neat sketch. List the key parameters measured. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer (7 Marks)**: **Rock-Eval Pyrolysis** (developed by IFP, France) is the standard laboratory instrumental technique for rapid source rock evaluation. Approximately **100 mg** of crushed rock sample is heated in an inert atmosphere (helium) following a programmed temperature ramp. **Procedure and Key Parameters Measured**: 1. **Stage 1 (300 °C isothermal, ~3 min)** — Volatilization of free hydrocarbons: - **S₁** = Free hydrocarbons already present in the rock (mg HC / g rock) - Detected by **Flame Ionization Detector (FID)** 2. **Stage 2 (300 °C → 600 °C at 25 °C/min ramp)** — Pyrolysis of kerogen: - **S₂** = Hydrocarbons generated by thermal cracking of kerogen (mg HC / g rock) - **Tmax** = Temperature at peak S₂ release (°C) — indicates maturity - Detected by **FID** 3. **Stage 3 (300 °C → 600 °C)** — CO₂ release from kerogen oxygen groups: - **S₃** = CO₂ released (mg CO₂ / g rock) - Detected by **Thermal Conductivity Detector (TCD)** 4. **Stage 4 (600 °C → 850 °C in air)** — Oxidation of residual carbon: - **S₄** = Residual carbon (mg C / g rock) - Detected by **FID** or **infrared detector** **Key Derived Parameters**: - **Hydrogen Index (HI)** = (S₂ / TOC) × 100 - **Oxygen Index (OI)** = (S₃ / TOC) × 100 - **Production Index (PI)** = S₁ / (S₁ + S₂) - **Tmax** indicates thermal maturity **Interpretation**: - PI $\lt$ 0.1 → immature; PI = 0.1 – 0.4 → mature; PI $\gt$ 0.4 → overmature - Tmax $\lt$ 435 °C → immature; Tmax = 435 – 465 °C → oil window; Tmax $\gt$ 465 °C → gas window - High HI + low OI → oil-prone kerogen (Type I/II) - Low HI + high OI → gas-prone kerogen (Type III) **Neat Sketch (Block Diagram) — See SECTION 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2** for the instrumentation workflow. --- ### Question B — Option 2: [KTU University Exam – Model Question Bank] **(a) Discuss the stages of thermal maturation of organic matter with reference to vitrinite reflectance (Ro%). (7 Marks)** **Model Answer (7 Marks)**: Vitrinite reflectance (Ro%) is the most widely used **paleothermometer** for determining the thermal maturity of sedimentary organic matter. Vitrinite is a maceral derived from terrestrial plant cell walls (lignin + cellulose) and is present in most sedimentary rocks. As maturation progresses, the aromatic ring condensation in vitrinite increases, causing higher reflectance of incident light. Ro is measured under oil immersion ($\lambda$ = 546 nm) using a **reflected-light microscope** with a photomultiplier detector. **Stages of Thermal Maturation vs. Ro%**: | Stage | Ro (%) | Temperature (°C) | Product | |---|---|---|---| | **Diagenesis (Immature)** | $\lt$ 0.5 | $\lt$ 60 | Biogenic methane, immature kerogen | | **Early Catagenesis** | 0.5 – 0.7 | 60 – 90 | Wet gas, condensate, early oil | | **Peak Oil Window** | 0.7 – 1.0 | 90 – 120 | **Maximum liquid oil generation** | | **Late Catagenesis** | 1.0 – 1.3 | 120 – 150 | Light oil, condensate | | **Wet Gas Window** | 1.3 – 2.0 | 150 – 180 | Wet gas, gas-condensate | | **Metagenesis (Overmature)** | $\gt$ 2.0 | $\gt$ 180 | Dry methane gas, graphite | **Why Ro% Works**: Vitrinite reflectance is **irreversible** — once the kerogen is heated, the reflectance increase is permanent. Therefore, Ro records the **maximum paleotemperature** experienced by the rock, making it a reliable thermal maturity indicator even if the rock is later uplifted and cooled. **Limitations**: - Vitrinite is absent in **pre-Devonian rocks** (no terrestrial plants before ~400 Ma). - In **type I and type II kerogens** (marine), vitrinite is sparse or absent — equivalent Ro (Rc) is calculated from Tmax using the Barker equation: $$ R_c = 0.018 \times T_{\max} - 7.16 $$ - **Suppression effects**: Hydrogen-rich vitrinite can show anomalously low Ro readings. --- **(b) Explain the various geochemical parameters used to assess source rock quality and maturation level. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer (7 Marks)**: The following **seven geochemical parameters** form the standard KTU evaluation matrix for source rocks: 1. **Total Organic Carbon (TOC) — Quantity Indicator**: - Measured via **Rock-Eval Pyrolysis** or **CHN analyzer**. - **Classification**: - TOC $\lt$ 0.5 wt% → Poor - 0.5 – 1.0 wt% → Fair - 1.0 – 2.0 wt% → Good - $\gt$ 2.0 wt% → Excellent 2. **Hydrogen Index (HI) — Quality Indicator**: - $\text{HI} = (S_2 / \text{TOC}) \times 100$ (mg HC / g TOC) - HI $\gt$ 600 → Type I (oil-prone); HI = 300 – 600 → Type II; HI $\lt$ 150 → Type III (gas-prone) 3. **Oxygen Index (OI) — Quality Indicator**: - $\text{OI} = (S_3 / \text{TOC}) \times 100$ (mg CO₂ / g TOC) - Decreases with increasing maturity. 4. **Production Index (PI) — Maturity Indicator**: - $\text{PI} = S_1 / (S_1 + S_2)$ - PI $\lt$ 0.1 → immature; PI = 0.1 – 0.4 → mature oil window; PI $\gt$ 0.4 → overmature gas window 5. **Tmax — Maturity Indicator**: - Temperature of peak S₂ generation. - Tmax $\lt$ 435 °C → immature; 435–465 °C → oil window; $\gt$ 465 °C → gas window 6. **Vitrinite Reflectance (Ro) — Maturity Indicator**: - The gold standard maturity parameter. - Ro = 0.5–0.7% → early mature; 0.7–1.0% → peak oil; $\gt$ 1.3% → gas window 7. **Biomarker Ratios — Source and Maturity Indicators**: - Measured by **GC-MS** analysis. - **CPI (Carbon Preference Index)** $\approx$ 1 → mature - **C₂₉/C₃₀ hopane ratio** → indicates source rock type - **Sterane isomerization ratios** — increases with maturity **Summary Quality-Maturity Workflow**: First, run **Rock-Eval** to get TOC, S1, S2, S3, Tmax, HI, OI, PI. Second, perform **vitrinite reflectance microscopy** for Ro. Third, perform **GC-MS biomarker analysis** for source confirmation. The integrated result is plotted on a **Modified Van Krevelen Diagram** (HI vs. Tmax or HI vs. OI) for final kerogen typing and maturity classification. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Examinations (Source Rock and Maturation Topic)** > > 1. **Confusing TOC with Total Carbon (TC)**: Many students forget to subtract mineral carbon (from carbonates) when computing TOC. If the rock contains carbonate minerals, you MUST use: $\text{TOC} = \text{TC} - \text{Mineral Carbon}$ (acidify sample with HCl first to remove carbonates). > > 2. **Mixing up the units of TOC and HI**: TOC is in **wt%**, HI is in **mg HC / g TOC** (not wt%). Using inconsistent units leads to numerical errors worth 1–2 marks. > > 3. **Forgetting the boundaries of maturity windows**: A common error is stating "Ro = 1.5% is the oil window". It is actually the **wet gas/condensate window**. The peak oil window is Ro = 0.7–1.0%. > > 4. **Inverting the Production Index formula**: PI = S₁ / (S₁ + S₂), NOT S₂ / (S₁ + S₂). Easy to lose 1 mark. > > 5. **Omitting the instrument name**: Since this is an **Instrumentation course**, KTU examiners award extra marks if you correctly name the instrument (e.g., "**Rock-Eval Pyrolyzer**" for S1/S2/S3, "**Vitrinite Reflectance Microscope**" for Ro). Do NOT write "we measure TOC" without specifying the instrument. > > 6. **Confusing Type I, II, III, IV kerogens with maturity stages**: Kerogen types relate to **organic matter origin** (lacustrine/marine/terrestrial), NOT to maturity. The maturity stages are diagenesis/catagenesis/metagenesis. > > 7. **Not drawing a diagram in the 14-mark question**: KTU valuation key mandates at least **one neat sketch/diagram** in any 14-mark question. A Rock-Eval block diagram or a maturation pathway diagram earns 1–2 additional marks. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Source Rock and Maturation** - [ ] **Source rock** = organic-rich sedimentary rock (shale/mudstone/carbonate) with TOC $\geq$ 0.5 wt%, hydrogen-rich kerogen, and reached thermal maturity. - [ ] **Three conditions** for source rock: Quantity (TOC), Quality (Kerogen type), Maturity (Ro/Tmax). - [ ] **Maturation** = thermal transformation of kerogen → oil/gas with increasing temperature and time. - [ ] **Three maturation stages**: Diagenesis ($\lt$ 60 °C), Catagenesis (60–175 °C, oil window), Metagenesis ($\gt$ 175 °C, dry gas). - [ ] **Four kerogen types**: Type I (algal, oil), Type II (marine, oil+gas), Type III (woody, gas), Type IV (inert, none). - [ ] **Vitrinite Reflectance (Ro)** is the gold-standard maturity parameter. Ro = 0.5–1.3% = oil window; Ro $\gt$ 1.3% = gas window. - [ ] **Rock-Eval Pyrolysis** measures S₁, S₂, S₃, Tmax; computes HI, OI, PI, and TOC. - [ ] **HI = (S₂ / TOC) × 100** — high HI = oil-prone; low HI = gas-prone. - [ ] **PI = S₁ / (S₁ + S₂)** — PI $\lt$ 0.1 = immature; PI $\gt$ 0.4 = overmature. - [ ] **Barker equation** (for equivalent Ro when vitrinite is absent): $R_c = 0.018 \times T_{\max} - 7.16$. - [ ] **Van Krevelen Diagram** = H/C vs. O/C plot; classifies kerogen types and tracks maturation paths. - [ ] **Tmax maturity windows**: $\lt$ 435 °C immature; 435–465 °C oil; $\gt$ 465 °C gas. - [ ] **Time-Temperature Index (Lopatin TTI)** $\lt$ 15 = immature; 15–75 = oil window. - [ ] **Geothermal gradient** normal = 2.5–3.0 °C per 100 m of burial depth. - [ ] **Arrhenius kinetics**: $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$; activation energy for kerogen cracking $\approx$ 150–200 kJ/mol. - [ ] **Key instruments** in source rock analysis: Rock-Eval Pyrolyzer, Vitrinite Reflectance Microscope, CHNSO Analyzer, GC-MS. - [ ] **H/C ratio benchmarks**: Type I $\gt$ 1.5; Type II = 1.0–1.5; Type III $\lt$ 1.0. - [ ] **Source rock quality threshold**: TOC $\geq$ 1.0 wt% + HI $\geq$ 150 mg HC/g TOC + Type I/II kerogen = commercially viable. ---

topics.title: Oil and Gas Traps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET861 - INSTRUMENTATION IN PETROCHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Oil and Gas Traps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)
An **Oil and Gas Trap** is a subsurface geological configuration of rocks that permits the accumulation and concentration of hydrocarbons (crude oil and natural gas) in a manner conducive to their preservation and subsequent extraction. A trap must physically prevent the upward escape (migration) of buoyant hydrocarbons over geological timescales.

According to the **Petroleum Geology** framework prescribed in the KTU 2024 Scheme for PEAET861, a valid trap consists of three essential geological elements:

1. **Source Rock** — A fine-grained, organic-rich sedimentary rock (typically shale or marl) that has generated hydrocarbons through thermal maturation (kerogen cracking between 60 °C–150 °C).
2. **Reservoir Rock** — A porous, permeable rock (e.g., sandstone, carbonate) capable of storing hydrocarbons in its pore space.
3. **Cap Rock (Seal)** — An impermeable layer (e.g., shale, salt, anhydrite) that prevents further upward migration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A trap is the *geometric arrangement* of reservoir and seal rocks. Without a proper trap geometry, even a high-quality reservoir rock cannot hold hydrocarbons — they will simply escape to the surface as **seeps**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Units Used in KTU Board Exams:**
> - Porosity: **% (dimensionless fraction)**
> - Permeability: **Darcy (D)** or **milliDarcy (mD)**
> - Formation Pressure: **psi** or **MPa**
> - Temperature: **°C** or **°F**

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a **shallow plastic bowl with a sponge inside**, covered by a thin sheet of cling film, all placed underwater. The sponge is the **reservoir rock** (porous and absorbent). The cling film is the **cap rock** (impermeable). The water is the **formation water**. The bowl shape is the **trap geometry** that holds everything in place. Now, drop a drop of oil into the sponge — because oil is lighter than water, it tries to float up, gets trapped under the cling film, and stays inside the sponge within the bowl. That, in essence, is an **oil and gas trap**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** Hydrocarbons are **buoyant** (lower density than formation water). Without a structural or stratigraphic barrier above, they would rise to the surface and be lost. The trap is the geological "container" that captures them.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Essential Elements of a Petroleum System
A complete hydrocarbon accumulation requires all five elements to coexist in space and time:

| # | Element | Function | Typical Rock Type |
|---|---------|----------|------------------|
| 1 | **Source Rock** | Generates hydrocarbons | Shale, marl, coal |
| 2 | **Migration Path** | Conduit for hydrocarbon flow | Faults, carrier beds |
| 3 | **Reservoir Rock** | Stores hydrocarbons | Sandstone, limestone, dolomite |
| 4 | **Cap Rock (Seal)** | Prevents escape | Shale, salt, anhydrite |
| 5 | **Trap Geometry** | Localizes accumulation | Anticline, fault, reef, etc. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for instrumentation:** Detection and characterization of each element requires specific downhole tools — **gamma ray logs** (source rock identification), **neutron-density logs** (porosity), **resistivity logs** (hydrocarbon vs. water saturation), and **seismic surveys** (trap geometry mapping).

### 2.2 Classification of Traps (KTU 2024 Board Focus)

#### A. Structural Traps
Formed by **tectonic deformation** of rock layers after deposition. The geometry is created by folding, faulting, or intrusion of salt/igneous material.

- **Anticline Trap:** An upward-arching fold. Hydrocarbons migrate to the **crest** of the anticline. Most prolific trap type globally (e.g., Ghawar Field, Saudi Arabia).
- **Fault Trap:** Created by displacement along a fault plane. Hydrocarbons are sealed against the impermeable fault gouge or juxtaposed shale.
- **Salt Dome Trap:** Buoyant salt diapir pierces overlying strata, deforming them into a dome. Hydrocarbons accumulate in the **flanks** and **cap** of the dome.
- **Dome Trap:** A localized upwarp; similar to anticline but more circular.

#### B. Stratigraphic Traps
Formed by **lateral variations in rock properties** (lithology, porosity, permeability) — not by structural deformation. These are harder to detect with seismic.

- **Unconformity Trap:** A buried erosional surface truncates older reservoir rocks, overlain by impermeable younger sediments.
- **Reef Trap:** A bioherm (carbonate reef build-up) acts as a reservoir, sealed by surrounding shale.
- **Lenticular (Sand Lens) Trap:** A porous sand body is completely enclosed within impermeable shale.
- **Pinched-Out Sand Trap:** A sandstone layer thins to zero (pinches out) against an impermeable boundary.

#### C. Combination (Composite) Traps
Result from a combination of structural and stratigraphic influences — e.g., a faulted reef, or a truncated anticline.

### 2.3 Migration of Hydrocarbons (Critical Process)

**Primary Migration:** Expulsion of hydrocarbons from the source rock into adjacent permeable carrier beds. Driven by compaction and hydrocarbon saturation exceeding capillary entry pressure.

**Secondary Migration:** Movement of hydrocarbons through carrier beds (typically sandstones) toward a trap. Governed by **buoyancy force** (upward) vs. **capillary force** (downward/retarding).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\phi = \dfrac{V_p}{V_b} \times 100\%$ | **Porosity** = Pore volume / Bulk volume | Dimensionless (%) |
| 2 | $k = \dfrac{Q \cdot \mu \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta P}$ | **Darcy's Law** for permeability | Darcy (D) |
| 3 | $F = \phi^{-m}$ | **Archie's Formation Resistivity Factor** | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $S_w = \left(\dfrac{F \cdot R_w}{R_t}\right)^{1/n}$ | **Archie's Water Saturation Equation** | Fraction (0–1) |
| 5 | $P_c = \dfrac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{r}$ | **Capillary Pressure** (Laplace–Young equation) | Pa or psi |
| 6 | $F_b = (\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g \cdot h$ | **Buoyancy Force** driving secondary migration | N/m³ |
| 7 | $T = \dfrac{GR - GR_{min}}{GR_{max} - GR_{min}}$ | **Gamma Ray Index** (shale volume indicator) | Fraction |

Where:
- $\phi$ = porosity, $V_p$ = pore volume, $V_b$ = bulk volume
- $k$ = permeability, $Q$ = flow rate, $\mu$ = fluid viscosity, $L$ = length, $A$ = cross-sectional area, $\Delta P$ = pressure drop
- $R_w$ = formation water resistivity, $R_t$ = true resistivity, $m$ = cementation exponent (~2), $n$ = saturation exponent (~2)
- $\gamma$ = interfacial tension, $\theta$ = contact angle, $r$ = pore throat radius
- $\rho_w, \rho_{hc}$ = densities of water and hydrocarbon, $h$ = column height

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** These formulas are used by **Petrophysicists** in KTU-aligned industries (ONGC, Schlumberger, Halliburton, Reliance E&P) to compute **Hydrocarbon Pore Volume (HCPV)** reserves and to design **completion strategies** for production wells.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Porosity from Log Data (Density Log Method)

The density log measures the **bulk density** ($\rho_b$) of the formation. Pore space is filled with fluid (water or hydrocarbon) of density $\rho_f$. The matrix (rock grain) density is $\rho_{ma}$.

**Step 1:** Write the volumetric density model.

The bulk density is a volumetric average of the matrix density and the fluid density:

$$\rho_b = (1 - \phi)\rho_{ma} + \phi \cdot \rho_f$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange to solve for $\phi$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_b &= \rho_{ma} - \phi \cdot \rho_{ma} + \phi \cdot \rho_f \\
\rho_b - \rho_{ma} &= \phi \cdot (\rho_f - \rho_{ma}) \\
\phi &= \dfrac{\rho_{ma} - \rho_b}{\rho_{ma} - \rho_f}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply realistic KTU board values.

Given: $\rho_{ma} = 2.65 \text{ g/cc}$ (sandstone), $\rho_b = 2.35 \text{ g/cc}$ (from log), $\rho_f = 1.00 \text{ g/cc}$ (water filtrate).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi &= \dfrac{2.65 - 2.35}{2.65 - 1.00} \\
\phi &= \dfrac{0.30}{1.65} \\
\phi &= 0.1818 \\
\phi &\approx 18.18\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The formation has approximately **18% porosity**, which is a *fair reservoir quality* in KTU evaluation terms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Explained:** Equation 1 says the bulk density is the mass-weighted average of the rock grains and the fluid filling the pores. By isolating $\phi$, we directly read porosity from the density log. This is a **direct measurement** (no calibration needed) — a KTU favorite for numerical problems.

### 3.2 Derivation of Capillary Pressure (Laplace–Young Equation)

A hydrocarbon-water interface in a pore throat experiences a pressure jump due to surface tension.

**Step 1:** Consider two fluid phases (wetting = water, non-wetting = oil) in a cylindrical pore throat of radius $r$.

**Step 2:** The interfacial tension $\gamma$ acts along the circumference of the pore throat, pulling the interface together.

**Step 3:** Equate the force due to surface tension to the force due to pressure difference across the interface.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{tension}} &= 2\pi r \gamma \cos\theta \\
F_{\text{pressure}} &= \pi r^2 (P_c) \\
\text{Equating: } \pi r^2 P_c &= 2\pi r \gamma \cos\theta \\
\therefore P_c &= \dfrac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{r}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Physical interpretation.

- **Smaller pore radius** → **Higher capillary pressure** → Stronger seal against hydrocarbon entry.
- **Larger pore radius** → **Lower capillary pressure** → Easier for hydrocarbons to displace water.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** This is why **shale** (small pore throats, ~nm scale) is an excellent cap rock, and **sandstone** (larger pore throats, ~μm scale) is a good reservoir rock. The size contrast alone creates a strong capillary seal.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Trap Type vs. Detection Tool

| Trap Type | Best Detection Method (Seismic) | Best Logging Tool | Difficulty to Find |
|-----------|--------------------------------|-------------------|--------------------|
| Anticline | 2D/3D seismic (closure mapping) | Resistivity + Neutron-Density | Easy |
| Fault Trap | 3D seismic (coherence, fault slices) | Image logs (FMI) for fault zone | Moderate |
| Salt Dome | 3D seismic (salt imaging) | Density log (salt = 2.03 g/cc) | Moderate–Hard |
| Reef | Seismic attribute analysis | Sonic log (high velocity contrast) | Hard |
| Unconformity | Seismic interpretation | Gamma Ray + Resistivity | Hard |
| Sand Lens | 3D seismic (amplitude anomalies) | Gamma Ray (clean sand signature) | Very Hard |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matrix is KTU-relevant:** Module 1 of PEAET861 bridges **geological concepts** with **instrumentation** — the table maps each trap type to the specific logging/seismic tool a KTU engineer would deploy to detect it.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Petroleum System & Trap Formation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Organic Matter Burial] --> B[Source Rock Maturation]
    B --> C[Primary Migration]
    C --> D[Carrier Bed Transport]
    D --> E[Secondary Migration]
    E --> F{Does Trap Exist?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Reservoir Rock Accumulation]
    F -- No --> H[Surface Seep Loss]
    G --> I[Cap Rock Seal Confirmed]
    I --> J[Hydrocarbon Accumulation Validated]
    J --> K[Discovery Well Drilling]
    K --> L[Reserves Estimation]
    L --> M[Production Phase]

    subgraph subsurface["Subsurface Petroleum System"]
        A
        B
        C
        D
        E
    end

    subgraph trapcheck["Trap Geometry Validation"]
        F
        G
        H
    end

    subgraph deliverability["Commercial Deliverability"]
        I
        J
        K
        L
        M
    end

    style A fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#8a6d00
    style B fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#8a6d00
    style C fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style D fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style E fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style F fill:#ffd6cc,stroke:#800000
    style G fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#006600
    style H fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#555555
    style I fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#006600
    style J fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#006600
    style K fill:#f0d6ff,stroke:#4a0070
    style L fill:#f0d6ff,stroke:#4a0070
    style M fill:#f0d6ff,stroke:#4a0070
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Classification of Traps

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Oil and Gas Traps] --> B[Structural Traps]
    A --> C[Stratigraphic Traps]
    A --> D[Combination Traps]

    B --> B1[Anticline Trap]
    B --> B2[Fault Trap]
    B --> B3[Salt Dome Trap]
    B --> B4[Dome Trap]

    C --> C1[Unconformity Trap]
    C --> C2[Reef Trap]
    C --> C3[Sand Lens Trap]
    C --> C4[Pinched Out Sand Trap]

    D --> D1[Faulted Reef]
    D --> D2[Truncated Anticline]

    style A fill:#ffe680,stroke:#665500,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#003366
    style C fill:#b3ffb3,stroke:#006600
    style D fill:#ffb3d9,stroke:#800040
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Anticline Trap Cross Section (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph surface["Surface"]
        SURF[Ground Level]
    end

    subgraph cap_layer["Impermeable Cap Rock Layer"]
        CAP1[Shale Seal]
        CAP2[Shale Seal]
        CAP3[Shale Seal]
    end

    subgraph res_layer["Reservoir Rock Layer"]
        RES1[Sandstone Reservoir]
        RES2[Sandstone Reservoir with Oil and Gas]
        RES3[Sandstone Reservoir with Water]
    end

    subgraph basement["Basement Rock"]
        BASE[Impermeable Basement]
    end

    SURF --> CAP1
    SURF --> CAP2
    SURF --> CAP3
    CAP1 --> RES1
    CAP2 --> RES2
    CAP3 --> RES3
    RES1 --> BASE
    RES2 --> BASE
    RES3 --> BASE

    GAS[Natural Gas at Crest] -.-> RES2
    OIL[Oil Column Below Gas] -.-> RES2
    WATER[Formation Water Below Oil] -.-> RES3

    style GAS fill:#ff9999,stroke:#660000
    style OIL fill:#8b4513,stroke:#3d1f00
    style WATER fill:#6699ff,stroke:#003366
    style CAP1 fill:#555555,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff
    style CAP2 fill:#555555,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff
    style CAP3 fill:#555555,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff
    style RES1 fill:#f4e4bc,stroke:#665500
    style RES2 fill:#f4e4bc,stroke:#665500
    style RES3 fill:#f4e4bc,stroke:#665500
    style BASE fill:#333333,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Anticline Schematic:** Hydrocarbons accumulate in the **upward-arching reservoir** because of buoyancy. Gas (lowest density) sits at the **crest**, oil (medium density) forms the **middle column**, and formation water (highest density) lies **below** — this is a perfect density-stacked accumulation known as **gravitational segregation**.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define an oil and gas trap. List the four essential elements of a complete petroleum system.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
An **oil and gas trap** is a geometric arrangement of reservoir rock and impermeable cap rock that localizes the accumulation of hydrocarbons and prevents their escape to the surface.

The four essential elements are:
1. **Source rock** — generates hydrocarbons from organic matter.
2. **Reservoir rock** — porous, permeable rock that stores hydrocarbons.
3. **Cap rock (seal)** — impermeable layer preventing upward migration.
4. **Trap geometry** — the structural or stratigraphic configuration that holds hydrocarbons in place.

*(A fifth element, migration pathway, may also be credited as a valid answer.)*

#### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Distinguish between structural and stratigraphic traps with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
| Aspect | Structural Trap | Stratigraphic Trap |
|--------|-----------------|---------------------|
| Formation cause | Tectonic deformation (folding, faulting, salt intrusion) | Lateral variation in lithology/porosity |
| Detection ease | Easier with seismic | Harder; requires integrated log analysis |
| Example | **Anticline trap** (e.g., Ghawar Field) | **Reef trap** (e.g., Golden Lane, Mexico) |

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

#### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 — Modified]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **classification of oil and gas traps** with neat diagrams. Highlight the differences between structural and stratigraphic traps. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Traps are classified into three broad categories:

**1. Structural Traps** (formed by tectonic deformation)
- **Anticline Trap:** Upward-arching fold. Hydrocarbons accumulate at the crest. Most prolific trap type — about 80% of the world's known reserves.
- **Fault Trap:** A fault displaces permeable rock against impermeable rock. Hydrocarbons are sealed along the fault plane.
- **Salt Dome Trap:** Buoyant salt pierces overlying strata. Hydrocarbons accumulate in dome flanks and the cap.
- **Dome Trap:** Localized upwarp of strata.

**2. Stratigraphic Traps** (formed by depositional/lithological variation)
- **Unconformity Trap:** Erosional surface truncates reservoir, overlain by seal.
- **Reef Trap:** Carbonate build-up in shale sea.
- **Lenticular (Sand Lens) Trap:** Sand body enclosed in shale.
- **Pinched-Out Sand Trap:** Sandstone thins to zero against shale.

**3. Combination (Composite) Traps**
- Formed by combined structural and stratigraphic influences (e.g., faulted reef).

**Key Differences (Table):**
| Feature | Structural | Stratigraphic |
|---------|-----------|--------------|
| Origin | Tectonic | Depositional/Erosional |
| Seismic detectability | High | Lower |
| Reserve size | Often large | Often smaller |
| Exploration risk | Lower | Higher |

**(b)** Derive the **capillary pressure equation** for a hydrocarbon-water interface in a cylindrical pore throat. Explain its significance in the context of cap rock sealing. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (as shown in Section 3.2):**

For a cylindrical pore throat of radius $r$, with interfacial tension $\gamma$ and contact angle $\theta$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{tension}} &= 2\pi r \gamma \cos\theta \\
F_{\text{pressure}} &= \pi r^2 P_c \\
\text{At equilibrium: } \pi r^2 P_c &= 2\pi r \gamma \cos\theta \\
\therefore P_c &= \dfrac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{r}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Significance in Cap Rock Sealing:**

1. **Inverse relation to pore radius:** Cap rocks (shale) have pore throat radii on the order of nanometers — yielding extremely high capillary pressures (often >1000 psi).
2. **Buoyancy must be overcome:** Hydrocarbon column height generates buoyancy. If buoyancy pressure $F_b$ exceeds $P_c$ of the seal, hydrocarbons leak.
3. **Maximum column height** of trapped hydrocarbons:

$$
h_{\max} = \dfrac{P_{c,\text{seal}}}{(\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g}
$$

4. **Engineering implication:** This equation directly informs **seal capacity analysis** in KTU 2024 petroleum engineering practice and is the basis for **column height prediction** in field development plans.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Modified]**

**(a)** Discuss the **process of hydrocarbon migration** (primary and secondary) with reference to buoyancy force and capillary pressure. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Primary Migration:**
- Expulsion of hydrocarbons from the source rock into adjacent permeable carrier beds.
- Occurs when **kerogen-to-hydrocarbon conversion** saturates the source rock.
- Overpressure from compaction and hydrocarbon saturation exceeding capillary entry pressure drives the expulsion.
- Typical depth window: 2000 m–4500 m (oil window: 60–120 °C).

**Secondary Migration:**
- Movement of hydrocarbons through carrier beds (sandstones, carbonates) toward a trap.
- Governed by **two opposing forces**:

  **Upward — Buoyancy Force:**
  $$F_b = (\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g \cdot h$$
  Hydrocarbons are buoyant because $\rho_{hc} < \rho_w$.

  **Downward — Capillary Pressure:**
  $$P_c = \dfrac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{r}$$
  The seal's capillary pressure opposes the entry of hydrocarbons.

- Migration **ceases** when the trap geometry provides a structural closure, allowing hydrocarbons to accumulate against the seal.

**Migration Pathway Map:**

$$\text{Source Rock} \xrightarrow{\text{Primary}} \text{Carrier Bed} \xrightarrow{\text{Secondary (buoyancy driven)}} \text{Trap Closure}$$

**(b)** A sandstone reservoir has a matrix density of 2.65 g/cc, bulk density (from log) of 2.40 g/cc, and fluid density of 1.05 g/cc. **Calculate the porosity**. If the formation resistivity is 25 Ω·m, water resistivity is 0.05 Ω·m, and saturation exponent is 2, find the **water saturation** ($S_w$) using Archie's equation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Porosity from Density Log**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi &= \dfrac{\rho_{ma} - \rho_b}{\rho_{ma} - \rho_f} \\
\phi &= \dfrac{2.65 - 2.40}{2.65 - 1.05} \\
\phi &= \dfrac{0.25}{1.60} \\
\phi &= 0.15625 \approx 15.6\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Formation Resistivity Factor (assuming $m = 2$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= \phi^{-m} = (0.15625)^{-2} \\
F &= \dfrac{1}{(0.15625)^2} = \dfrac{1}{0.02441} \approx 40.96
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Water Saturation (Archie's Equation)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_w &= \left(\dfrac{F \cdot R_w}{R_t}\right)^{1/n} \\
S_w &= \left(\dfrac{40.96 \times 0.05}{25}\right)^{1/2} \\
S_w &= \left(\dfrac{2.048}{25}\right)^{1/2} \\
S_w &= (0.0819)^{1/2} \\
S_w &= 0.2862 \approx 28.6\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:**
- Porosity is **15.6%** — fair reservoir.
- Water saturation is **28.6%**, meaning hydrocarbon saturation $S_{hc} = 1 - S_w = 71.4\%$.
- This is a **good hydrocarbon-bearing zone** (below 50% $S_w$ is generally considered producible).

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes — Avoid These in the KTU 2024 Board Exam:**
> 1. **Confusing "trap" with "reservoir":** A trap is the *geometry*; a reservoir is the *rock*. Do NOT interchange these terms.
> 2. **Forgetting units in numerical answers:** Always write $\phi = 15.6\%$, $S_w = 28.6\%$, and not just the decimal.
> 3. **In Archie's equation, missing the exponent:** Many students write $S_w = (F \cdot R_w) / R_t$ — this is wrong. The $1/n$ power is critical.
> 4. **Not labeling Mermaid/diagram parts:** When asked to "draw" or "explain with diagram," a clear label for each layer (cap rock, reservoir, source rock, gas, oil, water) is mandatory.
> 5. **Skipping steps in derivations:** Each algebraic step must be shown. KTU examiners allocate **partial marks per step**, not just the final answer.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Trap = Geometry + Seal + Reservoir.** No trap geometry → no hydrocarbon accumulation.
- **Three trap classes:** Structural (tectonic), Stratigraphic (depositional), Combination.
- **Structural traps include:** Anticline, Fault, Salt Dome, Dome.
- **Stratigraphic traps include:** Unconformity, Reef, Sand Lens, Pinched-Out Sand.
- **Primary migration** = source rock → carrier bed. **Secondary migration** = carrier bed → trap.
- **Buoyancy force** $F_b = (\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g \cdot h$ is the driver of secondary migration.
- **Capillary pressure** $P_c = \dfrac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{r}$ governs the seal capacity of cap rock.
- **Porosity from density log:** $\phi = \dfrac{\rho_{ma} - \rho_b}{\rho_{ma} - \rho_f}$.
- **Archie's water saturation:** $S_w = \left(\dfrac{F \cdot R_w}{R_t}\right)^{1/n}$ with $F = \phi^{-m}$.
- **Anticline trap** is the **most prolific** trap type worldwide.
- **Salt dome trap** traps hydrocarbons in **flanks and cap** of the salt structure.
- **Density-stacked accumulation order** (top to bottom): **Gas → Oil → Water**.
- **Instrumentation link:** Gamma Ray for source/shale identification; Neutron-Density for porosity; Resistivity for hydrocarbon saturation; Seismic for trap geometry.
- **Typical porosity range for a good reservoir:** 15%–25%.
- **Typical water saturation cutoff for a producible hydrocarbon zone:** $S_w < 50\%$.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physical and Chemical Characteristics of Crude Oil
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET861 - INSTRUMENTATION IN PETROCHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Physical and Chemical Characteristics of Crude Oil

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🛢️ Physical and Chemical Characteristics of Crude Oil

## 1.1 What is Crude Oil? — The Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> Crude oil (petroleum) is a naturally occurring, **fossil-derived, dark-coloured, oily, and flammable liquid mixture** found in underground geological reservoirs. It is a **complex multicomponent hydrocarbon system** composed predominantly of **carbon (83–87%)** and **hydrogen (10–14%)**, with minor but technologically critical amounts of **sulfur (0.1–6 wt%)**, **nitrogen (0.1–1.5 wt%)**, **oxygen (0.05–1.5 wt%)**, and **trace metals** (Ni, V, Fe, Cu, etc.).

In petrochemical engineering, crude oil is treated as a **non-ideal, multi-phase, multi-component fluid** whose behaviour cannot be described by a single chemical formula. Instead, it is characterised by a set of **bulk physical properties** (density, viscosity, flash point) and **compositional/chemical properties** (paraffin/naphthene/aromatic content, sulfur type, acid number).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "Crude Oil is a Natural Smoothie"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> Imagine crude oil as a **natural fruit smoothie made deep inside the Earth millions of years ago**. Just as a smoothie contains water, sugar, fibre, pulp, and vitamins in varying proportions, crude oil contains thousands of different hydrocarbon "molecules" (paraffins, naphthenes, aromatics, resins) all mixed together. **You cannot write a single chemical formula for a smoothie** — instead, you describe it by its **bulk physical properties** (viscosity = thickness, density = heaviness, colour) and its **chemical composition** (sweet vs sour, paraffinic vs naphthenic).
>
> The **refinery's job** is to act like a **centrifuge + sieve** that separates this smoothie into pure products: gasoline, diesel, kerosene, naphtha, lubricating oil, and petrochemical feedstocks.

## 1.3 Why Are These Characteristics Critical for Instrumentation?

The entire downstream instrumentation chain — **level transmitters in storage tanks, viscosity metres in pipelines, distillation column temperature/pressure control, and online sulfur analysers** — is fundamentally driven by the **physical and chemical nature of the crude oil** being processed. Different crudes (light Arabian vs heavy Mexican vs waxy North Sea) demand **different instrument ranges, materials of construction, and control strategies**.

| Property Class | Examples | Why It Matters to the Instrumentation Engineer |
|---|---|---|
| **Physical** | Density, Viscosity, Pour Point | Flow meter sizing, pump selection, tank gauging accuracy |
| **Thermal** | Flash Point, Fire Point, Auto-ignition | Safety instrumentation, fire & gas detector placement |
| **Compositional** | Sulfur content, Wax, Asphaltene | Corrosion-resistant materials (SS316, Monel), heat tracing design |
| **Chemical** | TAN (Total Acid Number), Salt content | Neutraliser dosing control, desalter performance |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis — Physical Characteristics

## 2.1 Density and Specific Gravity

**Density ($\rho$)** is defined as mass per unit volume, typically measured in **kg/m³** at **standard conditions of 15 °C and 1 atm (101.325 kPa)**. For petrochemicals, we also use **relative density (specific gravity, SG)**, which is the **ratio of the density of the oil to the density of water** at the same temperature.

$$SG_{15.6/15.6} = \frac{\rho_{\text{oil}} \text{ at } 15.6\,^\circ\text{C}}{\rho_{\text{water}} \text{ at } 15.6\,^\circ\text{C}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 15.6 °C (60 °F)?** This is the historical ASTM/IP standard reference temperature for petroleum measurements, ensuring global trade and contract consistency.

## 2.2 API Gravity — The Universal Crude Oil "Weight" Scale

**API (American Petroleum Institute) gravity** is the **inverse density scale** universally used in the petroleum industry. A **higher API gravity = lighter crude**, a **lower API gravity = heavier crude**.

The defining equation is:

$$^\circ API = \frac{141.5}{SG_{60/60}} - 131.5$$

### Classification by API Gravity (Industry Standard)

| Crude Classification | API Gravity (°API) | Typical Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Light Crude** | > 31.1 | Brent (38.3), WTI (39.6), Arabian Light (33) |
| **Medium Crude** | 22.3 – 31.1 | Arabian Medium (31), Iranian Heavy (30) |
| **Heavy Crude** | 10.0 – 22.3 | Maya (22), Venezuelan Bachaquero (16) |
| **Extra-Heavy Crude** | < 10.0 | Canadian Oil Sands (8–10), Cerro Negro |

## 2.3 Viscosity — Resistance to Flow

Viscosity defines how **"thick" or "runny"** the crude oil is. It is the single most important property for **pumping, pipeline transportation, and flow metering**.

- **Dynamic (Absolute) Viscosity, $\mu$**: Force per unit area required to move one fluid layer over another. Unit: **Pa·s** (or cP, where 1 cP = 1 mPa·s).
- **Kinematic Viscosity, $\nu$**: $\nu = \mu / \rho$. Unit: **m²/s** (or cSt, where 1 cSt = 1 mm²/s).

Crude oils show **Newtonian** behaviour at moderate temperatures, but **waxy crudes** exhibit **non-Newtonian** (shear-thinning) behaviour below their **pour point** — a critical concern for offshore pipeline instrumentation.

## 2.4 Pour Point and Cloud Point

- **Pour Point**: The **lowest temperature** at which the crude oil will still **flow** under standard test conditions (ASTM D97). Below this, wax crystals form a gel and the oil **stops flowing** — directly impacting pipeline shutdown procedures.
- **Cloud Point**: The temperature at which **wax crystals first become visible** ("cloudy appearance") when the oil is cooled. Always slightly **higher** than the pour point.

## 2.5 Flash Point, Fire Point, and Auto-Ignition Temperature

These are **safety-critical** thermal properties:

| Property | Definition (ASTM D93 / D92) | Typical Range for Crude |
|---|---|---|
| **Flash Point** | Lowest temp at which vapours ignite **momentarily** with a flame | -20 to +100 °C |
| **Fire Point** | Temp at which vapours sustain **continuous combustion** | 5–30 °C above flash point |
| **Auto-Ignition Temp** | Temp at which ignition occurs **spontaneously without a spark** | 250–450 °C |

## 2.6 Chemical Composition — The Molecular Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Elemental Composition of Crude Oil (Average Range):**
> - **Carbon (C)**: 83 – 87 wt%
> - **Hydrogen (H)**: 10 – 14 wt%
> - **Sulfur (S)**: 0.1 – 6.0 wt%
> - **Nitrogen (N)**: 0.1 – 1.5 wt%
> - **Oxygen (O)**: 0.05 – 1.5 wt%
> - **Metals** (Ni, V, Fe, Cu): < 1000 ppm

### Hydrocarbon Families (The Four Pillars)

| Family | Structure | Boiling Behaviour | Industrial Importance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Paraffins (Alkanes)** | Straight/branched C–C chains, C$_n$H$_{2n+2}$ | Low BP, clean burning | Gasoline, diesel, jet fuel |
| **Naphthenes (Cycloalkanes)** | Saturated rings, C$_n$H$_{2n}$ | Moderate BP | Lubricating oils, kerosene |
| **Aromatics** | Benzene-ring structure, C$_n$H$_{2n-6}$ | Higher density, smoke | Petrochemical feedstocks (BTX) |
| **Asphaltenes & Resins** | Large polycyclic, high-MW polar molecules | Non-volatile, residue | Road asphalt, coke |

### Crude Oil Classification by Base (NIRA / Tanimura Method)

| Base Type | Dominant Hydrocarbon | Composition Rule |
|---|---|---|
| **Paraffinic Base** | > 50% paraffins | Wax-rich, low asphalt |
| **Naphthenic Base** | > 50% naphthenes | Low wax, more aromatics |
| **Aromatic Base** | > 50% aromatics | High asphalt, high S |
| **Mixed Base** | Balanced ratio | Most common in practice |

## 2.7 Sulfur Classification — Sweet vs Sour

| Class | Sulfur Content | Corrosion Implications for Instrumentation |
|---|---|---|
| **Sweet Crude** | < 0.5 wt% S | Mild steel acceptable for storage |
| **Sour Crude** | > 0.5 wt% S (some > 3%) | Requires SS316L, Monel, Inconel; H₂S detectors mandatory |

## 2.8 Other Industrial Properties

- **Total Acid Number (TAN)**: mg KOH required to neutralise 1 g of oil. High TAN ⇒ naphthenic acid corrosion.
- **Salt Content**: Typically 10–1500 mg/L (as NaCl). Removed in **desalter** upstream of atmospheric distillation.
- **Water & Sediment (BS&W)**: Basic Sediment & Water, measured by **centrifuge** (ASTM D96).
- **Reid Vapour Pressure (RVP)**: Vapour pressure at 37.8 °C — determines volatility class.

## 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| Formula | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **API Gravity** | $^\circ API = \dfrac{141.5}{SG_{60/60}} - 131.5$ | dimensionless | Crude classification |
| **Specific Gravity** | $SG = \rho_{\text{oil}}/\rho_{\text{water}}$ at 15.6 °C | dimensionless | Density reference |
| **API ↔ SG** | $SG = \dfrac{141.5}{^\circ API + 131.5}$ | dimensionless | Two-way conversion |
| **Kinematic Viscosity** | $\nu = \mu/\rho$ | m²/s or cSt | Flow design |
| **Dynamic Viscosity** | $\mu$ (measured) | Pa·s or cP | Pump power: $P = \mu \cdot Q \cdot \nabla v$ |
| **H/C Atomic Ratio** | $n_H/n_C$ (typically 1.5–1.9) | mol/mol | Refining yield indicator |
| **TAN** | mg KOH per g oil | mg KOH/g | Corrosion prediction |
| **Reid Vapour Pressure** | $P_{37.8\,^\circ\text{C}}$ | kPa or psi | Storage tank design |
| **Sulfur Classification** | S wt% | % | Sweet (<0.5), Sour (>0.5) |
| **Watson K-Factor (UOP K)** | $K = \sqrt[3]{T_B / SG}$ | T$_B$ in °R | Paraffinicity index |
| **API Density** | $\rho \approx \dfrac{141.5 \times 999\,kg/m^3}{^\circ API + 131.5}$ | kg/m³ | Tank volume conversion |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📐 Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Problems

## 3.1 Derivation: From Density to API Gravity

The API scale was designed so that **water (SG = 1.0) corresponds to exactly 10 °API**. We will derive the API formula from this anchor point.

**Step 1 — Anchor Point:**
For water at 60 °F, $SG_{60/60} = 1.000$ and $^\circ API = 10$.

**Step 2 — Empirical Linear Form:**
The API scale is an **inverse, approximately linear** function of SG. Engineers define:

$$^\circ API = \frac{a}{SG} - b$$

**Step 3 — Apply Anchor (SG = 1, °API = 10):**
$$10 = \frac{a}{1.000} - b \quad \Rightarrow \quad a - b = 10$$

**Step 4 — Reference Anchor (SG = 0.611, °API = 100):**
Historically, the scale was also anchored such that an oil with SG ≈ 0.611 (very light) corresponds to 100 °API.

$$100 = \frac{a}{0.611} - b \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = 1.6366 \times (100 + b)$$

**Step 5 — Solve System of Equations:**
$$a - b = 10 \quad \text{and} \quad a = 1.6366b + 163.66$$

Substituting:

$$(1.6366b + 163.66) - b = 10$$
$$0.6366b = -153.66 \quad \Rightarrow \quad b = 131.5$$
$$a = 141.5$$

**Step 6 — Final Canonical Equation:**

$$\boxed{^\circ API = \frac{141.5}{SG_{60/60}} - 131.5}$$

This equation is the **backbone of every petroleum density calculation** and is universally used in refinery LIMS, custody transfer, and online densitometer calibration.

## 3.2 Derivation: Watson K-Factor (UOP K) — Paraffinicity Index

The **Watson K-Factor** quantifies the **paraffinicity** of a crude fraction. Higher K = more paraffinic; lower K = more aromatic.

**Step 1 — Empirical Definition:**
Watson defined the K-factor as the cube root of the **normal boiling point (in °Rankine)** divided by the **specific gravity**:

$$K = \frac{\sqrt[3]{T_B}}{SG}$$

**Step 2 — Conversion to °Rankine:**
$$T_B\,[°R] = (T_B\,[°C] + 273.15) \times \frac{9}{5}$$

**Step 3 — Final Form:**

$$\boxed{K = \frac{\sqrt[3]{T_B\,[°R]}}{SG}}$$

| K-Factor Range | Crude Type |
|---|---|
| > 12.0 | Highly Paraffinic |
| 11.5 – 12.0 | Paraffinic |
| 11.0 – 11.5 | Naphthenic |
| < 11.0 | Highly Aromatic |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem #1 — API Gravity Calculation

**Problem:** A crude oil sample has a density of 920 kg/m³ at 15.6 °C. Density of water at 15.6 °C is 999 kg/m³. Calculate:
(a) Specific gravity, (b) API gravity, (c) Classify the crude.

**Step 1 — Calculate Specific Gravity:**

$$SG = \frac{\rho_{\text{oil}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}} = \frac{920}{999}$$

$$SG = 0.9210$$

**Step 2 — Calculate API Gravity:**

$$^\circ API = \frac{141.5}{0.9210} - 131.5$$

$$^\circ API = \frac{141.5}{0.9210} - 131.5$$

$$^\circ API = 153.64 - 131.5$$

$$\boxed{^\circ API \approx 22.14\,^\circ API}$$

**Step 3 — Classification:**

Since $10.0 \le 22.14 < 22.3$? Let us re-examine the table. API 22.14 lies in the **Heavy Crude** range (10.0 – 22.3) by the most common industry table; however, marginal crudes near 22 are often commercially blended and called "medium-heavy."

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Note:** The boundary between heavy and medium is often quoted as **22.3 °API** (SG = 0.92) in the standard classification. Hence this crude is on the **heavy side of medium / light side of heavy**.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem #2 — Reverse API Calculation

**Problem:** An online densitometer sends a 4–20 mA signal proportional to specific gravity. The current is **12 mA** and the range is **SG = 0.80 to 1.00**. Find the API gravity.

**Step 1 — Convert mA to SG (Linear Interpolation):**

$$SG = SG_{\min} + \frac{(I - I_{\min})}{(I_{\max} - I_{\min})} \times (SG_{\max} - SG_{\min})$$

$$SG = 0.80 + \frac{(12 - 4)}{(20 - 4)} \times (1.00 - 0.80)$$

$$SG = 0.80 + \frac{8}{16} \times 0.20$$

$$SG = 0.80 + 0.5 \times 0.20$$

$$SG = 0.80 + 0.10$$

$$\boxed{SG = 0.90}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate API Gravity:**

$$^\circ API = \frac{141.5}{0.90} - 131.5$$

$$^\circ API = 157.222 - 131.5$$

$$\boxed{^\circ API \approx 25.72\,^\circ API}$$

This is a **medium crude** — typical of certain Iranian, Mexican, and Russian export blends.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Problem #3 — Kinematic to Dynamic Viscosity Conversion

**Problem:** A crude oil at 40 °C has kinematic viscosity 35 cSt and density 880 kg/m³. Calculate the dynamic viscosity in cP and Pa·s.

**Step 1 — Relation:**
$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mu = \nu \cdot \rho$$

**Step 2 — Convert Units (cSt → m²/s, kg/m³ → g/cm³):**

$$\nu = 35\,\text{cSt} = 35 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$$

$$\rho = 880\,\text{kg/m}^3 = 0.88\,\text{g/cm}^3$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Dynamic Viscosity:**

$$\mu = 35 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s} \times 880\,\text{kg/m}^3$$

$$\mu = 30800 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{kg/(m·s)}$$

$$\mu = 0.0308\,\text{Pa·s}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to cP (1 cP = 0.001 Pa·s):**

$$\mu = \frac{0.0308}{0.001} = 30.8\,\text{cP}$$

$$\boxed{\mu \approx 30.8\,\text{cP} \;\; (0.0308\,\text{Pa·s})}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Insight:** This is a moderately viscous medium-heavy crude. Pipeline designers use a **maximum allowable viscosity of 400 cSt at pumping temperature**; above this, diluents (light condensate) must be blended or heated.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Property → Instrument Mapping

| Property | Standard Test Method | Typical On-line Instrument | Accuracy Class |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Density / API** | ASTM D1298, D5002 | Nuclear densitometer, Coriolis meter, vibrating tube | ± 0.0005 g/cm³ |
| **Viscosity** | ASTM D445, D2196 | Capillary viscometer, torsional oscillation | ± 1% of reading |
| **Flash Point** | ASTM D93 (PMCC), D92 (COC) | Online flash point analyser (Grabner) | ± 2 °C |
| **Pour Point** | ASTM D97, D5950 | Rotational viscometer with cooling | ± 3 °C |
| **Sulfur Content** | ASTM D4294 (XRF), D2622 (WDXRF) | Online XRF / SCD analyser | ± 0.005 wt% |
| **Water Content** | ASTM D4377, D4928 | Capacitance probe, Karl Fischer | ± 0.02 vol% |
| **Salt Content** | ASTM D3230 | Conductivity meter (in-line) | ± 1 mg/L |
| **TAN** | ASTM D664 | Potentiometric titrator (lab) | ± 0.05 mg KOH/g |
| **RVP** | ASTM D323, D5191 | Online PVT analyser | ± 1 kPa |
| **BS&W** | ASTM D96, D4007 | Capacitance / microwave BS&W probe | ± 0.05 vol% |
