# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: API Gravity from Specific Gravity

The American Petroleum Institute (API) Gravity is the standard metric used universally in petroleum refining. Its derivation from specific gravity (SG) is straightforward but carries an important engineering rationale.

### Step 1 — Start with the definition of Specific Gravity

$$ SG = \dfrac{\rho_{oil}}{\rho_{water}} $$

where $\rho_{water}$ is measured at **15.6 °C (60 °F)** and equals **999.016 kg/m³** (or 62.37 lbm/ft³).

### Step 2 — Define API Gravity in the empirical (inverse) form

API Gravity is **inversely** proportional to density — so a lighter oil has a *higher* °API. The empirical form is:

$$ \text{°API} = \dfrac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5 $$

### Step 3 — Reverse the relation to solve for SG

Multiply both sides by $SG$:

$$ \text{°API} \cdot SG = 141.5 - 131.5 \cdot SG $$

Rearranging:

$$ \text{°API} \cdot SG + 131.5 \cdot SG = 141.5 $$

$$ SG \cdot (\text{°API} + 131.5) = 141.5 $$

Therefore:

$$ SG = \dfrac{141.5}{\text{°API} + 131.5} $$

### Step 4 — Numerical Example

Suppose a crude has °API = **35**.

$$ SG = \dfrac{141.5}{35 + 131.5} = \dfrac{141.5}{166.5} = 0.8498 $$

So the density is:

$$ \rho_{oil} = 0.8498 \times 999.016 \approx 849.0 \text{ kg/m}^3 $$

This is a **light crude** (e.g., Brent blend).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Engineering Insight:** °API is essentially an *instrument-calibrated scale* — densitometers and oscillating U-tube Coriolis meters can be directly ranged in °API by programming the transmitter with the inverse formula. This is why °API is the language of refinery operations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: The Fenske Equation (Minimum Stages)

The **Fenske Equation** (1932) gives the *minimum number of theoretical stages* required for a given separation under **total reflux** (infinite reflux ratio, no product withdrawal).

### Step 1 — Define the Problem

We have a binary (or pseudo-binary) mixture of **Light Key (LK)** and **Heavy Key (HK)** components. We want to relate the compositions on the top tray ($n$) and bottom tray ($1$).

### Step 2 — Use the K-Value Relation

At equilibrium on any tray $i$:

$$ y_i = K_i x_i $$

At total reflux, the operating line coincides with the diagonal $y = x$. The Kremser-Sorel approach gives, for $N_{min}$ stages:

$$ \left(\dfrac{x_{LK}}{x_{HK}}\right)_{top} = \alpha_{LK,HK}^{N_{min}} \cdot \left(\dfrac{x_{LK}}{x_{HK}}\right)_{bottom} $$

### Step 3 — Take logarithms

$$ N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln \left[\left(\dfrac{x_{LK}}{x_{HK}}\right)_{D} \cdot \left(\dfrac{x_{HK}}{x_{LK}}\right)_{B}\right]}{\ln \alpha_{LK,HK}} $$

### Step 4 — Final Fenske Form (mole-fraction form)

$$ N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln \left[\dfrac{x_{D,LK}}{1 - x_{D,LK}} \cdot \dfrac{1 - x_{B,HK}}{x_{B,HK}}\right]}{\ln \alpha_{LK,HK}} $$

### Step 5 — Worked Example

**Problem:** A refinery wants to separate a light-key / heavy-key pair with:
- Distillate composition: $x_{D,LK} = 0.95$
- Bottoms composition: $x_{B,HK} = 0.05$ (so $x_{B,LK} = 0.95$, wait — corrected: $x_{B,HK} = 0.95$)

**Given:** Relative volatility $\alpha_{LK,HK} = 2.0$

**Solution:**

$$ N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln \left[\dfrac{0.95}{0.05} \cdot \dfrac{0.95}{0.05}\right]}{\ln 2.0} $$

$$ = \dfrac{\ln(19 \times 19)}{\ln 2.0} = \dfrac{\ln(361)}{\ln 2.0} = \dfrac{5.886}{0.6931} \approx 8.49 $$

So **minimum 8.5 theoretical stages** are required. With a Murphree efficiency of 70%, actual trays needed ≈ $8.5 / 0.70 \approx$ **12 trays**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Use:** The Fenske equation is the *first step* in a complete distillation design sequence called **"Fenske → Underwood → Gilliland → Erbar-Maddox"** (FUGE). Instrumentation engineers use the *total reflux* case as a theoretical limit to validate online column simulations.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Reflux Ratio and Component Mass Balance

### Step 1 — Define the Variables

- $F$ = Feed molar flow rate
- $D$ = Distillate (top product) flow rate
- $B$ = Bottoms (bottom product) flow rate
- $V$ = Vapor flow rate up the column
- $L$ = Liquid flow rate down the column
- $R$ = Reflux ratio = $L / D$

### Step 2 — Overall Mass Balance (steady state)

$$ F = D + B $$

### Step 3 — Component Mass Balance (for any component $i$)

$$ F z_i = D x_{D,i} + B x_{B,i} $$

where $z_i$ is the feed composition, $x_{D,i}$ the distillate composition, $x_{B,i}$ the bottoms composition.

### Step 4 — Rectifying Section Balance (above feed plate)

For the top of the column, the vapor leaving the top tray is condensed. Part is refluxed ($L$) and part is taken as distillate ($D$):

$$ V = L + D = (R + 1) D $$

$$ L = R \cdot D $$

### Step 5 — Stripping Section Balance (below feed plate)

For the reboiler / bottom:

$$ \bar{L} = V - B = (R + 1) D - B $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Instrumentation Translation:** The equations $V = (R+1)D$ and $L = R \cdot D$ are the *fundamental tuning relationships* for the column's pressure-compensated temperature controller (often the *dual-temperature inferential composition controller*). A change in $R$ (reflux flow) shifts the operating point on the column's temperature profile, which in turn shifts side-draw qualities.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process Variables and Their Control Loops

| Controlled Variable | Manipulated Variable | Typical Loop | Tag Notation (ISA) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Column Top Temperature | Reflux Flow | PID | **TIC-100** |
| Side-Draw (Kerosene) Temp. | Pump-Around Flow | PID | **TIC-201** |
| Furnace Outlet Temp. | Fuel Gas Flow | Cascade to TC | **TIC-301** |
| Column Overhead Pressure | Condenser Cooling Water / Vent Valve | PID | **PIC-100** |
| Feed Flow | Charge Pump Speed | Flow Ctrl | **FIC-401** |
| Bottoms Level | Bottoms Pump Speed | Level Ctrl | **LIC-501** |
| Overhead Receiver Level | Reflux / Distillate split | Cascade LC | **LIC-601** |
| Side-Draw Quality (NIR/density) | Reflux / Side-Draw Flow | Ratio/Analyzer | **AIC-701** |

> [!NOTE]
> **ISA Tag Standard (ISA 5.1):** The first letter denotes the measured variable (T = Temperature, P = Pressure, F = Flow, L = Level, A = Analyzer). The second letter (often **I**) denotes *Indicating Controller*. The number is the loop number unique across the unit.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define "Atmospheric Distillation" in the context of petroleum refining. State the typical flash zone temperature and the reason for operating below 400 °C.**

**Model Answer:**

> Atmospheric Distillation is the primary separation process in a petroleum refinery, in which desalted and dehydrated crude oil is heated to its bubble-point temperature in a fired furnace and then introduced into a tall multi-tray distillation column operating at or near atmospheric pressure. The vapor-liquid equilibrium established in the column separates the crude into various boiling-range fractions (LPG, naphtha, kerosene, diesel, atmospheric residue).
>
> The typical **flash zone temperature** is **350 °C – 380 °C**. The furnace outlet temperature is deliberately kept **below 400 °C** because at higher temperatures the heavier hydrocarbon molecules begin to undergo **thermal cracking** (covalent C–C bond rupture), which would produce unwanted lighter products and coke inside the furnace tubes, leading to fouling, hot spots, and tube failure.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is a "Pump-Around" in an ADU column? Mention any two of its functions.**

**Model Answer:**

> A **Pump-Around (PA)** is a stream of hot liquid withdrawn from an intermediate tray of the distillation column, pumped through an external cooler (or heat exchanger), and returned to a tray a few levels above the withdrawal point. The PA is the principal *internal heat-removal* mechanism of the column.
>
> **Two key functions:**
> 1. **Heat Removal** — It removes heat from the column, establishing the desired internal temperature profile without requiring excessive top reflux.
> 2. **Energy Recovery** — The hot PA liquid is used to pre-heat feed streams, generate low-pressure steam, or reboil downstream columns, achieving significant refinery energy integration.
> 3. **(Any one of:)** Improves vapor-liquid traffic inside the column, providing better tray hydraulics and stable side-draw product quality.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice, 1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Module 1, Choice A]`

**(a)** With a neat block-flow diagram, describe the **Atmospheric Distillation of crude oil**, clearly identifying the role of the **pre-heat train, desalter, furnace, main column, side strippers, and pump-arounds**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A refinery uses an ADU to separate a binary key-component pair. The distillate contains **95 mol %** of the light key, the bottoms contain **5 mol %** of the light key (i.e., 95 mol % heavy key), and the relative volatility is **α = 2.0**. Using the **Fenske equation**, determine the **minimum number of theoretical stages** and the **actual number of stages** assuming a Murphree efficiency of **70%**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution (a):**

The ADU process is organized in a continuous flow as follows:

1. **Desalting:** Crude oil from storage is mixed with **5–10 vol% wash water** in an electrostatic desalter. The applied AC/DC field (≈ 15–30 kV) coalesces brine droplets. Salt content is reduced from **~100 ptb to < 5 ptb**. *(2 marks for the function and salt reduction)*

2. **Pre-Heat Train:** The desalted crude is progressively heated by **process-to-process heat exchangers** (cold streams: desalted crude; hot streams: kerosene, diesel, pump-around, atmospheric residue). The crude temperature is raised from ambient to **≈ 220–240 °C** before entering the furnace. This recovers up to **70% of the column's heat duty** — the largest single energy-recovery step in a refinery. *(1.5 marks for energy recovery and temperature level)*

3. **Furnace:** The pre-heated crude enters a **fired tubular heater**, where it is brought to the **flash zone temperature of 350–380 °C** in a partial-vaporized two-phase state. Tube-skin temperature is monitored (TT) and high-high tripped (TAHH) to prevent coking. *(1 mark)*

4. **Main Distillation Column:** A 30–60 m tall column with **30–50 actual trays** operating at **1.0 – 1.5 kg/cm²(g)** overhead pressure. The two-phase feed enters the **flash zone** at mid-column. Light vapors rise, heavy liquid falls. Side draws are taken at appropriate tray temperatures:
   - **Overhead:** Light + Heavy Naphtha
   - **Upper Side Draw:** Kerosene
   - **Middle Side Draw:** Diesel
   - **Bottom:** Atmospheric Long Residue
   *(1.5 marks for the column with side-draw map)*

5. **Side Strippers:** Each side-draw is routed to a small **steam-stripping column** (typically 4–6 trays with reboiled steam) to remove dissolved light ends, tighten the cut point, and meet product flash-point specifications. *(0.5 marks)*

6. **Pump-Arounds:** 3–4 PA circuits withdraw hot liquid from intermediate trays, cool it externally, and return it higher up. They remove column heat and recover energy. *(0.5 marks)*

*(Block-Flow Diagram to be drawn by student — refer to Section 4.2 of these notes.)*

---

### **Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- Distillate: $x_{D,LK} = 0.95$  →  $x_{D,HK} = 0.05$
- Bottoms: $x_{B,HK} = 0.95$  →  $x_{B,LK} = 0.05$
- Relative volatility: $\alpha_{LK,HK} = 2.0$
- Tray efficiency: $\eta_{Murphree} = 0.70$

**Step 1 — Apply Fenske Equation:**

$$ N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln \left[\dfrac{x_{D,LK}}{x_{D,HK}} \cdot \dfrac{x_{B,HK}}{x_{B,LK}}\right]}{\ln \alpha_{LK,HK}} $$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$ N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln \left[\dfrac{0.95}{0.05} \cdot \dfrac{0.95}{0.05}\right]}{\ln(2.0)} $$

$$ = \dfrac{\ln(19 \times 19)}{\ln(2.0)} = \dfrac{\ln(361)}{0.6931} $$

$$ = \dfrac{5.8865}{0.6931} = 8.49 $$

So $N_{min} \approx$ **8.5 theoretical stages**.

**Step 3 — Calculate Actual Stages:**

$$ N_{actual} = \dfrac{N_{theoretical}}{\eta} = \dfrac{8.49}{0.70} = 12.13 $$

$$ N_{actual} \approx \textbf{13 actual trays} \ (\text{rounded up}) $$

> **Note for students:** Always round up the actual tray count to the next integer because you cannot install a fraction of a tray.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Module 1, Choice B]`

**(a)** Differentiate between **Atmospheric Distillation** and **Vacuum Distillation** of crude oil, listing any **five distinguishing points**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **functions of a pre-heat train** in a refinery's ADU. State why **API gravity** is the standard metric for crude oil, and compute the **specific gravity of a crude with °API = 32**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution (a):**

| # | Atmospheric Distillation (ADU) | Vacuum Distillation (VDU) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Operates at or near **atmospheric pressure** (≈ 1.0–1.5 kg/cm²g) | Operates at **high vacuum** (≈ 10–50 mmHg absolute) |
| 2 | Flash zone temperature ≈ **350 – 380 °C** | Flash zone temperature ≈ **380 – 420 °C** (but at lower pressure) |
| 3 | Processes **desalted whole crude** | Processes **atmospheric residue (long residue)** from ADU bottoms |
| 4 | Produces LPG, naphtha, kerosene, diesel, atmospheric residue | Produces vacuum gas oil (VGO), lube oil base stocks, vacuum residue |
| 5 | Crude decomposition above 400 °C is a **hard limit** | Lower pressure allows distillation at **lower temperatures**, avoiding cracking |
| 6 | Furnace uses standard fuel gas/fuel oil firing | Larger-diameter column due to high vapor volumes at low pressure |
| 7 | Feed is a two-phase mixture (part vapor) | Feed is mostly liquid (residue) |

*(Any 5 points earn full 7 marks. Tabular form is recommended for KTU.)*

---

### **Model Solution (b):**

**Functions of a Pre-Heat Train:**

1. **Primary energy recovery** — The pre-heat train uses *process-to-process* heat exchangers to transfer heat from hot product streams (kerosene, diesel, pump-arounds, atmospheric residue) to the incoming cold crude. This reduces the load on the fired furnace by **60–80%**, slashing fuel consumption.
2. **Pre-conditioning the feed** — By raising the crude temperature to ≈ 220–240 °C, the pre-heat train enables better vaporization in the furnace, smooth furnace operation, and reduced thermal shock.
3. **Integration with downstream units** — The pre-heat network is the central node of the refinery's **Pinch Analysis** — the heat integration backbone that determines minimum utility (hot/cold) consumption of the entire plant.
4. **Improved crude viscosity** — Heating the crude lowers its viscosity, improving pumpability and reducing pressure drop in transfer lines.

**Why API Gravity is Standard:**

API Gravity is a **temperature-referenced, inverse-scale** density metric calibrated such that **water has °API = 10**. It is the global trading and operations standard in petroleum because:

- It is **dimensionless and instrument-friendly** — densitometers can be directly ranged in °API.
- It provides an **intuitive sense of crude quality** — higher °API ⇒ lighter, more valuable crude (more gasoline/diesel yield).
- It is **independent of the units** of density, enabling global trade.

**Numerical Computation:**

Given °API = 32.

$$ SG = \dfrac{141.5}{\text{°API} + 131.5} = \dfrac{141.5}{32 + 131.5} = \dfrac{141.5}{163.5} $$

$$ \boxed{SG \approx 0.8654} $$

Therefore the crude density:

$$ \rho_{oil} = SG \times 1000 = 865.4 \text{ kg/m}^3 \text{ (at 15.6 °C)} $$

This crude is classified as a **medium-light crude**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Module 1 — PEAET861):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "furnace outlet temperature" with "flash zone temperature."** They are *not* equal — the furnace outlet is 10–20 °C higher than the flash zone. Students who equate them lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert °F to °C** (or vice versa) when given thermal data. KTU examiners frequently mix units to test numerical carelessness.
> 3. **Using °API in mass-balance equations directly** — °API must first be converted to SG, then to density, before any flow or mass calculation.
> 4. **Drawing block-flow diagrams without arrows** showing material flow direction. A BFD without directional arrows is a "static photograph" — examiners deduct marks.
> 5. **In Fenske-equation problems, using the *distillate* heavy-key composition in the *bottoms* term** by mistake. Double-check which stream has which component majority.
> 6. **Rounding tray counts DOWN** — always round up; you cannot install a partial tray.
> 7. **Omitting unit specifications** in the final answer (e.g., "T = 350" without "°C") — KTU valuation keys award partial credit for correct unit notation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1**

- [x] **Crude oil** = complex mixture of paraffins, naphthenes, aromatics + S/N/O/metal impurities.
- [x] **°API** is the industry-standard density metric. *Higher °API = lighter, more valuable crude.*
- [x] **Fenske equation** gives *minimum* theoretical stages (total reflux limit).
- [x] **Atmospheric Distillation (ADU)** = first and most instrumented refinery unit; operates at ~1 atm, flash zone ≈ 350–380 °C.
- [x] **Cracking limit** = ~400 °C — never cross this in ADU furnace.
- [x] **Pre-Heat Train** recovers 60–80% of process heat; central to refinery energy integration.
- [x] **Desalter** reduces salt content from ~100 ptb to < 5 ptb (electrostatic).
- [x] **Pump-Arounds (PA)** are the *primary* heat-removal mechanism; each PA has its own flow + temperature loop.
- [x] **Side Strippers** tighten the cut points of side-draw products (kerosene, diesel).
- [x] **Side draws** (top → bottom): LPG → Naphtha → Kerosene → Diesel → Atm. Residue.
- [x] **Vacuum Distillation (VDU)** processes ADU bottoms at < 50 mmHg to avoid cracking.
- [x] **Refinery process families:** Separation, Conversion, Treating, Blending.
- [x] **ISA tag prefix meanings:** T = Temperature, P = Pressure, F = Flow, L = Level, A = Analyzer. Second letter **I = Indicating Controller**.
- [x] **TPFL quartet** (Temperature, Pressure, Flow, Level) = the four primary measurement parameters of refinery instrumentation.
- [x] **Yield pattern** (typical): ~45% gasoline, ~25% diesel, ~10% kerosene/jet, ~10% fuel oil, ~5% LPG, balance = lubes/bitumen.
- [x] **A 1% improvement in pre-heat train energy recovery ≈ $3–5 M/year fuel savings** in a 10 MMTPA refinery.
- [x] **Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS)** per IEC 61511 must be designed for all furnace tube-skin, column overpressure, and high-level interlocks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Pearl:** The ADU is the *spine* of the refinery. If the ADU fails, the entire plant shuts down. Hence, instrumentation density on the ADU is the highest in the refinery, and Module 1 sets the foundation for every downstream unit covered in Modules 2–5.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vacuum Distillation of Crude Oil
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET861 - INSTRUMENTATION IN PETROCHEMICAL INDUSTRIES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Vacuum Distillation of Crude Oil # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition **Vacuum Distillation of Crude Oil** is a secondary physical separation process employed in petroleum refineries to separate heavy hydrocarbon fractions (long-residue, short-residue, vacuum gas oil) from the atmospheric distillation column bottoms (reduced crude / long residue) by operating the distillation column under **sub-atmospheric pressure**, typically in the range of **10 mmHg to 50 mmHg (absolute)** at the flash zone, with absolute top pressures often between **5 mmHg and 25 mmHg**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Vacuum distillation is a unit operation in which the residue from the atmospheric distillation tower is distilled under reduced pressure to recover additional lighter fractions (LGO, HGO, VGO) without causing **thermal cracking** of the hydrocarbons, which would otherwise occur if these heavy oils were heated to their atmospheric boiling points (often exceeding **400 °C** to **450 °C**). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are trying to **boil water on top of Mount Everest** versus at sea level. On the mountain, where atmospheric pressure is low, water boils at a much lower temperature (around 70 °C instead of 100 °C) because there is less "push" from the atmosphere holding the water molecules back. **Vacuum distillation works on the same principle:** - Heavy crude fractions are like "stubborn" liquids that refuse to vaporize at normal atmospheric pressure without being heated to extreme temperatures (which would **crack** or break their molecular chains, destroying product quality). - By **reducing the pressure** inside the distillation column, the boiling points of these heavy hydrocarbons are lowered. - This allows the refinery to **vaporize and separate** them at much **lower temperatures**, preserving their molecular structure and yielding valuable products like **lubricating oil base stocks, vacuum gas oil (VGO), and feedstocks for fluid catalytic cracking (FCC) units**. > [!NOTE] > **Why not just use higher temperature?** > Heating heavy residue above ~370 °C–400 °C at atmospheric pressure causes **thermal cracking** — the C–C bonds in long hydrocarbon chains break, producing unwanted light gases, coke, and lower-quality products. The vacuum essentially "tricks" thermodynamics into vaporizing the oil at a temperature low enough to keep the molecules intact. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Key Operating Parameters | Parameter | Typical Value | |---|---| | Flash Zone Pressure (abs) | **10 – 50 mmHg** | | Top Tower Pressure (abs) | **5 – 25 mmHg** | | Flash Zone Temperature | **380 °C – 420 °C** | | Overhead Vapor Temperature | **70 °C – 130 °C** | | Steam to Ejector | **0.3 – 0.5 kg per kg of overhead vapor** | | Pressure Drop Across Packing | **≤ 4 – 6 mmHg per theoretical stage** | | Vacuum Source | **Steam-jet ejectors** (3-stage typical) or **mechanical liquid-ring vacuum pumps** | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Thermodynamic Basis — Why Vacuum Works The fundamental relationship that enables vacuum distillation is the **Clausius–Clapeyron equation**, which describes how the boiling point of a liquid changes with pressure. As pressure decreases, the saturation temperature (boiling point) decreases. ### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Logic of Operation 1. **Reduced Crude Feed Entry** — The bottom product of the atmospheric distillation column (long residue at ~340 °C–370 °C) is heated further in a **vacuum furnace** to a controlled temperature (~400 °C–420 °C) without causing coking. 2. **Flash Zone Vaporization** — The superheated oil enters the **flash zone** of the vacuum column, where the pressure is drastically reduced. The sudden pressure drop causes a portion of the feed to **flash vaporize** into lighter and heavier vapor fractions. 3. **Fractional Separation** — Vapors rise through trays or structured packing. Internal reflux (created by pump-around circuits and top reflux) provides the liquid-vapor contact needed for separation. 4. **Side Draw Removal** — Products are withdrawn as **side cuts** (e.g., LVGO — Light Vacuum Gas Oil, HVGO — Heavy Vacuum Gas Oil), each with progressively heavier boiling ranges. 5. **Vacuum Maintenance** — A multi-stage **steam-jet ejector system** (or liquid-ring vacuum pump) continuously evacuates non-condensable gases, leaked air, and steam from the overhead system. 6. **Condensation** — Overhead vapors pass through a partial condenser where VGO is condensed and collected. Non-condensables are pulled into the ejector stages. 7. **Vacuum Residue Withdrawal** — The heaviest fraction (vacuum residue) is withdrawn from the bottom, typically pumped through a steam generator (where it transfers heat to incoming feed) and sent to a **visbreaker, coker, or asphalt blender**. ## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Process Element - **Why sub-atmospheric pressure?** → To suppress thermal cracking (C–C bond rupture onset is ~370 °C). - **Why steam to ejector?** → Steam provides the motive force; adiabatic expansion of high-pressure steam entrains the column overhead gases. - **Why low pressure drop packing?** → The total available ΔP is only ~30–40 mmHg; every tray pressure drop shrinks the "useful ΔT" available for separation. - **Why multiple ejector stages?** → Single-stage can only achieve compression ratios of ~5–7; cascading stages multiply the suction pressure reduction. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL FORMULA NOTE:** In all table rows below, the absolute-value bar `|` is replaced with `\vert` to keep the markdown table intact. | # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2}-\dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$ | $P$ = vapor pressure, $T$ = temperature (K), $\Delta H_{vap}$ = latent heat of vaporization, $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K) | Predicts boiling-point shift with pressure. Core equation for vacuum column design. | | 2 | $\log_{10}(P) = A - \dfrac{B}{C+T}$ (Antoine Eq.) | $A, B, C$ = component-specific Antoine constants, $T$ in °C, $P$ in mmHg | Used in simulations to compute equilibrium stage temperatures under vacuum. | | 3 | $P_{total} = \sum_i y_i \cdot P_i^{sat}(T)$ (Dalton's Law) | $y_i$ = vapor mole fraction, $P_i^{sat}$ = saturation pressure | Computes flash-zone equilibrium composition in vacuum column. | | 4 | $y_i = \dfrac{\alpha_i \cdot x_i}{1 + (\alpha_i - 1) x_i}$ (Relative Volatility) | $\alpha_i$ = relative volatility of component $i$ w.r.t. reference | Higher $\alpha$ at lower P → easier separation under vacuum. | | 5 | $R_{min} = \dfrac{x_{D,y} - y_{F}}{y_{F} - x_{F}}$ (Gilliland-style) | $x_D, x_F$ = light-key compositions | Minimum reflux ratio in vacuum column design. | | 6 | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ (Heat duty) | $\dot{m}$ = mass flow, $c_p$ = specific heat, $\Delta T$ = temperature change | Furnace and pump-around heat duty. | | 7 | $\dfrac{P_{suc}}{P_{disch}} \le \phi_{max}$ (Ejector compression ratio) | $P_{suc}$ = suction pressure, $P_{disch}$ = discharge pressure, $\phi_{max}$ ≈ 5–7 per stage | Determines number of ejector stages needed. | | 8 | $N_{stages} \ge \dfrac{\ln(P_{atm}/P_{top})}{\ln(\phi_{max})}$ | Total ejector stages required | Quick design check. | | 9 | $F = U \cdot A \cdot LMTD$ (Heat exchanger) | $U$ = overall heat coeff., $A$ = area, LMTD = log mean temperature difference | Vacuum residue heat-exchanger sizing. | | 10 | $\eta_{column} = \dfrac{N_{theoretical}}{N_{actual}}$ (Tray efficiency / HETP) | Murphree efficiency $E_M$ or HETP for packing | Translates theoretical stages to actual hardware. | ## 2.4 Engineering Utility Vacuum distillation columns are the **economic backbone** of the modern refinery. Without them: - Heavy crudes could not be converted into transportation fuels. - Lubricating oil base stocks (requiring very narrow boiling ranges) would be impossible to produce in acceptable yields. - FCC and hydrocracker units would have no suitable VGO feed. > [!NOTE] > The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes **instrumentation** in petrochemical industries. The vacuum column is one of the most heavily instrumented units in a refinery — typically with **60 to 120 measurement points**, including differential pressure transmitters across each section, multi-point thermocouples, and online analyzers for the side-draw streams. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Boiling-Point Reduction at Reduced Pressure **Problem Statement:** A heavy vacuum gas oil (HVGO) fraction boils at **450 °C (723.15 K)** at atmospheric pressure (**760 mmHg**). Assuming the latent heat of vaporization $\Delta H_{vap}$ = **70 kJ/mol**, find the saturation temperature when the pressure is reduced to **20 mmHg**. ### Step 1 — Identify the Governing Equation We use the integrated form of the Clausius–Clapeyron equation assuming $\Delta H_{vap}$ is constant over the temperature range. $$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2}-\dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$$ ### Step 2 — Substitute Known Quantities Given: - $P_1 = 760 \text{ mmHg}$, $T_1 = 723.15 \text{ K}$ - $P_2 = 20 \text{ mmHg}$, $T_2 = ?$ - $\Delta H_{vap} = 70{,}000 \text{ J/mol}$ - $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$ $$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{20}{760}\right) = -\dfrac{70{,}000}{8.314}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2}-\dfrac{1}{723.15}\right)$$ ### Step 3 — Compute the Pressure Ratio Term $$\ln(0.02632) = -3.639$$ ### Step 4 — Compute the Reciprocal-Temperature Coefficient $$-\dfrac{70{,}000}{8.314} = -8420.3 \text{ K}$$ So the equation becomes: $$-3.639 = -8420.3 \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{T_2}-\dfrac{1}{723.15}\right)$$ ### Step 5 — Isolate the Temperature Term $$\dfrac{1}{T_2}-\dfrac{1}{723.15} = \dfrac{3.639}{8420.3} = 4.322 \times 10^{-4}$$ ### Step 6 — Solve for $T_2$ $$\dfrac{1}{T_2} = \dfrac{1}{723.15} + 4.322 \times 10^{-4} = 1.3828 \times 10^{-3} + 4.322 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$\dfrac{1}{T_2} = 1.8150 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1}$$ $$T_2 = \dfrac{1}{1.8150 \times 10^{-3}} = 550.96 \text{ K}$$ ### Step 7 — Convert to °C and Interpret $$T_2 = 550.96 - 273.15 = 277.81 \text{ °C}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** By reducing the pressure from 760 mmHg to 20 mmHg, the boiling point of this HVGO fraction dropped from **450 °C** to approximately **278 °C** — a reduction of **172 °C**. This is why vacuum distillation is so critical: it keeps the oil in the column well below its cracking temperature. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Required Number of Ejector Stages **Problem Statement:** A vacuum column operates with a top pressure of **15 mmHg** and discharges to a condenser operating at **760 mmHg (atmospheric)**. The maximum compression ratio per ejector stage is $\phi_{max} = 6$. Determine the minimum number of stages required. ### Step 1 — Use the Compression Ratio Formula $$N_{stages} \ge \dfrac{\ln(P_{atm}/P_{top})}{\ln(\phi_{max})}$$ ### Step 2 — Substitute $$N_{stages} \ge \dfrac{\ln(760/15)}{\ln(6)} = \dfrac{\ln(50.667)}{\ln(6)} = \dfrac{3.925}{1.7918}$$ ### Step 3 — Compute $$N_{stages} \ge 2.19$$ ### Step 4 — Round Up to the Next Whole Stage $$N_{stages} = 3 \text{ stages (minimum)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real Plant Practice:** Refineries almost universally use **3-stage ejector systems** (sometimes 4 for very deep vacuum) because the 2-stage theoretical minimum leaves no margin for leakage, off-design operation, or steam-pressure fluctuations. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Atmospheric vs. Vacuum Distillation | Parameter | Atmospheric Distillation | Vacuum Distillation | |---|---|---| | Operating Pressure (abs) | **760 – 1100 mmHg** | **5 – 50 mmHg** | | Maximum Oil Temperature | **340 °C – 370 °C** | **380 °C – 420 °C** | | Cracking Risk | Moderate | Suppressed by design | | Tray Count | 30 – 50 trays | 14 – 28 actual stages (often packing) | | Reflux Type | Top + pump-around | Pump-around dominant (limited top reflux due to vacuum) | | Main Products | Naphtha, kerosene, diesel, AGO | LVGO, HVGO, vacuum residue | | Vacuum Source | None (atmospheric) | Steam ejectors / liquid-ring pump | | Furnace Type | Convection + radiation | Convection + radiation + coking-resistant tubes | | Heat Integration | Crude pre-heat train | Vacuum residue steam generator | ## 3.4 Derivation 3: Minimum Reflux Ratio for Vacuum Column **Problem Statement:** A vacuum column separates LVGO (light key) from HVGO (heavy key). The feed (long residue) has a light-key mole fraction $x_F = 0.45$ and the LVGO draw has $x_D = 0.95$. The vapor in equilibrium with the feed has $y_F = 0.62$. Compute $R_{min}$. ### Step 1 — Apply the Underwood / Gilliland-type $R_{min}$ formula $$R_{min} = \dfrac{x_{D} - y_{F}}{y_{F} - x_{F}}$$ ### Step 2 — Substitute $$R_{min} = \dfrac{0.95 - 0.62}{0.62 - 0.45} = \dfrac{0.33}{0.17} = 1.94$$ ### Step 3 — Interpret > [!NOTE] > An $R_{min}$ of 1.94 means the column requires a minimum reflux-to-distillate ratio of 1.94 : 1. In actual operation, the **actual reflux ratio** is chosen at $R = 1.2$ to $1.5 \times R_{min}$ — i.e., around **2.3 to 2.9** — to balance energy cost against column height. Higher reflux improves separation but increases pump-around heat duty and furnace load. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Vacuum Distillation Unit The figure below maps the **functional architecture** of a vacuum distillation unit, focusing on the major process blocks, vacuum-generation train, and instrumentation touchpoints (highlighted as `T` for transmitter) relevant to **PEAET861 — Instrumentation in Petrochemical Industries**. ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef process fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff classDef instrument fill:#c00000,stroke:#7f0000,color:#ffffff classDef vacuum fill:#7030a0,stroke:#3d1361,color:#ffffff classDef utility fill:#548235,stroke:#274e10,color:#ffffff A[Long Residue Feed
from Atmospheric Column
340 C, ~760 mmHg]:::process B[Vacuum Furnace
Heating to 400 C]:::process C[Flash Zone
20-50 mmHg]:::process D[Vacuum Column
Towers]:::process E[Overhead Vapor Line]:::process F[Partial Condenser
VGO Condensation]:::process G[Steam Jet Ejector
3-Stage]:::vacuum H[Aftercondenser
and Seal Pot]:::vacuum I[Hotwell and
Overhead Receiver]:::process J[Vacuum Residue
Withdrawal Pump]:::process K[Residue Steam
Generator]:::utility L[Side Draw 1
LVGO]:::process M[Side Draw 2
HVGO]:::process N[Slop Wax and
Heavy Cut]:::process O[To Storage
or Conversion]:::process T1[TT Temp Tx
Furnace Outlet]:::instrument T2[PT Pressure Tx
Flash Zone]:::instrument T3[DPT Diff Pressure
Across Packing]:::instrument T4[TT Temp Tx
Overhead]:::instrument T5[FT Flow Tx
LVGO Draw]:::instrument T6[FT Flow Tx
HVGO Draw]:::instrument T7[LT Level Tx
Residue Drum]:::instrument T8[AT Analyzer Tx
VGO Quality]:::instrument T9[PT Pressure Tx
Ejector Suction]:::instrument A --> B B --> T1 T1 --> C C --> T2 C --> D D --> T3 D --> L L --> T5 D --> M M --> T6 D --> N D --> E E --> T4 E --> F F --> I I --> O E --> G G --> T9 G --> H H --> K H -.to atmosphere.-> ATM((Atmosphere)) D --> J J --> T7 J --> K K --> A I --> T8 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Instrumentation Insight (per KTU 2024 PEAET861):** The vacuum column is a **showcase unit** for advanced process control. Critical loops include: > - **Furnace outlet temperature control** (T1) — using fired-heuristic fuel-gas flow as the manipulated variable. > - **Flash-zone pressure control** (T2) — by throttling the ejector first-stage motive steam. > - **ΔP control** (T3) — to detect flooding or fouling of the internals. > - **Side-draw quality control** (T5/T6 + T8) — manipulating pump-around duty and draw rate. ## 4.2 Functional Stages of the Vacuum-Generation Train ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef stage fill:#7030a0,stroke:#3d1361,color:#ffffff classDef cond fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff classDef atm fill:#a5a5a5,stroke:#595959,color:#ffffff COL[Column Overhead
15-30 mmHg]:::stage S1[Stage 1 Ejector
Suction 25 mmHg]:::stage C1[Intercondenser 1
Condenses Steam]:::cond S2[Stage 2 Ejector
Suction 130 mmHg]:::stage C2[Intercondenser 2]:::cond S3[Stage 3 Ejector
Suction 760 mmHg]:::stage C3[Aftercondenser]:::cond SP[Seal Pot and
Hotwell]:::cond ATM((Atmosphere)):::atm COL --> S1 S1 --> C1 C1 --> S2 S2 --> C2 C2 --> S3 S3 --> C3 C3 --> SP C3 --> ATM SP --> COL ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Intercondensers Between Stages?** Between ejector stages, intercondensers remove the motive steam (now expanded) by direct contact with cooling water. Without them, the second stage would have to compress the *entire* steam load of stage 1, dramatically increasing its steam consumption. Intercondensers can cut overall motive-steam use by **40–60 %**. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]` **Define vacuum distillation. Why is it carried out at reduced pressure in petroleum refining?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Vacuum distillation is a physical separation process in which the heavy residue from the atmospheric distillation column is separated into lighter and heavier fractions by distillation under **sub-atmospheric pressure (typically 5–50 mmHg absolute)**. **[1 mark]** It is carried out at reduced pressure to **lower the boiling points of the heavy hydrocarbons**, allowing them to be vaporized at temperatures below the **thermal cracking threshold (~370 °C)**. **[1 mark]** This enables recovery of valuable products such as **LVGO, HVGO, and lubricating-oil base stocks**, which would otherwise decompose to coke and gas if heated to their atmospheric boiling points. **[1 mark]** ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]` **List any three key instruments used for monitoring a vacuum distillation column and state what each measures.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Differential Pressure Transmitter (DPT)** — mounted across a section of trays or packing; measures **pressure drop**, indicating flooding or fouling. **[1 mark]** 2. **Multi-point Thermocouple / RTD** — measures the **internal temperature profile** of the column at multiple elevations; used to infer product cut points. **[1 mark]** 3. **Online NIR / Boiling-Range Analyzer** — installed on side-draw lines; measures the **distillation curve (ASTM D1160)** of LVGO/HVGO cuts in real time for closed-loop quality control. **[1 mark]** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / 14 Marks]` **Question:** With a neat block diagram, describe the **vacuum distillation process for crude oil**. Explain the function of the **steam-jet ejector system** and discuss how **pressure control** is achieved in the column. #### (a) Process Description and Block Diagram — 7 Marks Vacuum distillation is the second stage of crude oil separation. The **long residue** from the bottom of the atmospheric distillation column is first passed through a **heat integration network** (typically a vacuum-residue steam generator) where it is preheated. It then enters a **vacuum furnace** where it is heated to approximately **400 °C–420 °C** — high enough to vaporize heavy fractions but low enough to avoid thermal cracking. **[2 marks]** The superheated oil enters the **flash zone** of the vacuum column, operating at **20–50 mmHg absolute**. Upon the sudden pressure drop, a portion of the feed **flashes** into vapor. Vapors rise through **structured packing or trays**, contacting internal reflux created by **pump-around circuits** (cooled and recirculated side-draw streams). Products are withdrawn as side cuts: - **LVGO** (Light Vacuum Gas Oil) — narrow boiling range, ~350–400 °C at atm. - **HVGO** (Heavy Vacuum Gas Oil) — ~400–550 °C at atm, FCC feed. - **Slop wax / heavy cut** — narrow intermediate cut. **[2 marks]** The **overhead vapors** (non-condensables + light VGO + steam) pass to a **partial condenser**, where the VGO is condensed and routed to a receiver. Non-condensables are pulled into the **ejector train**. The **vacuum residue** is withdrawn from the column bottom, sent through a **steam generator** (heat recovery), and routed to a visbreaker, coker, or asphalt blender. **[2 marks]** **Block diagram** (already shown in SECTION 4.1 above; reproduce in answer as a labeled sketch showing: feed → furnace → flash zone → column with side draws → overhead → ejector → atmosphere; and residue withdrawal). **[1 mark]** #### (b) Function of the Steam-Jet Ejector and Pressure Control — 7 Marks **Function of the Steam-Jet Ejector:** A steam-jet ejector uses **high-pressure motive steam** (typically 10–35 bar) as the driving fluid. When this steam expands through a converging–diverging **nozzle**, it reaches supersonic velocity and creates a **low-pressure region** at the suction port. This entrains the column overhead vapors, which are then compressed by the high-velocity steam in the **diffuser** section. A single stage can achieve a compression ratio of **5–7:1**; therefore, **3 stages** are typically cascaded to reduce column top pressure from atmospheric (~760 mmHg) down to **15–30 mmHg**. **[3 marks]** **Pressure Control Strategy:** Flash-zone pressure is the critical controlled variable. The **first-stage ejector motive-steam flow** is the primary manipulated variable — increasing steam flow reduces flash-zone pressure. Instrumentation includes: - A **pressure transmitter (PT)** at the flash zone. - A **pressure controller (PIC)** outputting to the **motive-steam control valve** on stage 1. - A **PT at the overhead** for trim control and **intercondenser performance** monitoring. - **Vacuum breaker** (air-bleed valve) to prevent over-vacuum conditions that could lead to column collapse or packing damage. - **Feed-forward** from the **furnace outlet temperature transmitter (TT1)** to anticipate pressure transients when feed rate changes. **[3 marks]** **Why careful pressure control matters:** A pressure that is too high reduces the ΔP available for separation, degrading cut quality. A pressure that is too low can cause packing damage, air in-leakage, ejector instability, and excessive steam consumption. **[1 mark]** --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / 14 Marks — Alternative Choice]` **Question:** Explain the **thermodynamic principle** behind vacuum distillation. Derive the **boiling-point reduction** for a heavy fraction with the following data and discuss the role of **relative volatility** in vacuum column design. **Given:** - Atmospheric boiling point $T_1$ = **450 °C** = **723.15 K** - Atmospheric pressure $P_1$ = **760 mmHg** - Vacuum pressure $P_2$ = **20 mmHg** - $\Delta H_{vap}$ = **70 kJ/mol** - $R$ = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** #### (a) Thermodynamic Principle and Derivation — 7 Marks The thermodynamic basis of vacuum distillation is the **Clausius–Clapeyron equation**, which relates the saturation vapor pressure of a pure component to its absolute temperature: $$\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{T \cdot \Delta V}$$ For an ideal gas and liquid of negligible volume, this integrates to: $$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2}-\dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$$ This equation shows that for a **fixed temperature**, the saturation pressure decreases as pressure is reduced; conversely, for a **fixed pressure**, the saturation temperature decreases. In vacuum distillation we exploit the second relationship. **[2 marks]** **Step-by-step calculation:** Substituting: $$\ln(20/760) = -\dfrac{70{,}000}{8.314}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2}-\dfrac{1}{723.15}\right)$$ $$-3.639 = -8420.3 \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{T_2}-\dfrac{1}{723.15}\right)$$ $$\dfrac{1}{T_2} = 1.3828 \times 10^{-3} + 4.322 \times 10^{-4} = 1.815 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1}$$ $$T_2 = 550.96 \text{ K} = 277.8 \text{ °C}$$ **Boiling-point reduction: $\Delta T_{bp} = 450 - 277.8 = 172.2 \text{ °C}$** **[3 marks]** This **172 °C reduction** is well below the thermal cracking threshold, allowing recovery of HVGO without decomposition. The calculation also shows that even a modest absolute pressure drop (from 760 to 20 mmHg, a factor of 38×) produces a massive temperature shift, which is why vacuum columns are so effective. **[2 marks]** #### (b) Role of Relative Volatility — 7 Marks **Relative volatility** $\alpha$ is the ratio of the $K$-values (vapor–liquid equilibrium constants) of two components: $$\alpha_{ij} = \dfrac{K_i}{K_j} = \dfrac{y_i / x_i}{y_j / x_j}$$ For a binary system: $$y_i = \dfrac{\alpha_i \cdot x_i}{1 + (\alpha_i - 1)\,x_i}$$ **Why relative volatility matters in vacuum column design:** **[2 marks]** 1. **α increases as pressure decreases.** Heavy hydrocarbon pairs that are nearly impossible to separate at atmospheric pressure ($\alpha \approx 1.05$) become more separable under vacuum ($\alpha \approx 1.4$–$1.6$ for LVGO/HVGO at 20 mmHg). 2. **Lower α → more stages required.** For α = 1.05, the Fenske equation predicts ~100 theoretical stages for a clean separation; for α = 1.5, only ~20 stages are required. 3. **Gilliland correlation** uses $R_{min}$ (calculated from equilibrium data) to set the **actual reflux ratio**, which then determines the **operating line** and number of stages. 4. **Pressure drop across internals directly hurts α.** Because α is a strong function of P, even a few mmHg of pressure drop across a packed bed reduces α in the lower section of the column, compromising the heaviest cut. **Design implications:** **[2 marks]** - Use **low-pressure-drop structured packing** (e.g., Mellapak, Flexipac) rather than trays in deep-vacuum sections. - Limit total column ΔP to **15–30 mmHg** from top to flash zone. - Use **side pump-arounds** (typically 2–3) to remove heat and generate reflux without sending too much overhead vapor to the ejector. **Numerical illustration:** A heavy pair with $\alpha = 1.05$ at 760 mmHg improves to $\alpha = 1.5$ at 20 mmHg, reducing required theoretical stages by ~5×. This single physics effect is the reason vacuum distillation is economically viable. **[1 mark]** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Vacuum Distillation Questions:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert °C to K** in Clausius–Clapeyron. KTU examiners routinely deduct **1 mark** if $T_1$ is left in °C. > 2. **Confusing "vacuum" with "absolute zero pressure".** The "vacuum" in a refinery column is **not perfect vacuum** — it is 5–50 mmHg absolute. Stating "near-zero pressure" loses marks. > 3. **Stating the ejector "creates" vacuum.** Ejectors do not create vacuum — they **remove gas from a sealed volume**, allowing the equilibrium pressure to drop. Phrase it correctly. > 4. **Missing the role of non-condensables.** Ejector steam consumption is dominated by **air in-leakage** through flanges, pump seals, and valve packings. Tightness of the vacuum system is a major design and operating concern. > 5. **Ignoring thermal cracking.** A 1-mark deduction if the answer to "why vacuum?" does not mention **suppression of thermal cracking** explicitly. > 6. **In Clausius–Clapeyron, sign errors.** The minus sign in the integrated equation is correct; rewriting it incorrectly leads to an answer greater than $T_1$ (impossible — boiling point must *fall* with falling pressure). ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Vacuum Distillation of Crude Oil** - **Definition:** Distillation of long residue under **5–50 mmHg absolute** to recover LVGO, HVGO, and residue without thermal cracking. - **Driving physics:** Clausius–Clapeyron — boiling point falls as pressure falls. - **Why vacuum?** Lower P → lower $T_{bp}$ → avoids cracking above **~370 °C**. - **Key equipment:** Vacuum furnace, flash zone, column with packing/trays, partial condenser, overhead receiver, **3-stage steam-jet ejector train**, vacuum residue pump, residue steam generator. - **Top pressure:** 15–30 mmHg; **Flash-zone pressure:** 20–50 mmHg; **ΔP allowed:** 15–30 mmHg total. - **Boiling-point shift example:** 450 °C (atm) → ~278 °C (20 mmHg) — a ~172 °C reduction. - **Ejector compression ratio:** ~5–7 per stage → need **3 stages** for typical refinery vacuum. - **Main instrumentation (PEAET861 focus):** Furnace-outlet TT, flash-zone PT, DPT across packing, side-draw FT + AT, overhead TT, residue LT, ejector-suction PT. - **Reflux type:** **Pump-around-dominant** (not top reflux) because top reflux in deep vacuum is hard to condense. - **Internals:** Low-ΔP structured packing preferred over trays in modern designs. - **Relative volatility $\alpha$:** Increases at lower P, drastically reducing required theoretical stages. - **Key formulas to memorize:** Clausius–Clapeyron, Antoine, Dalton's law, $R_{min}$ formula, ejector-stage-count formula. - **Energy integration:** Vacuum residue gives up its heat to generate **LP/MP steam** in the residue steam generator — a critical energy-recovery loop.

topics.title: Thermal Processes (Coking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET861 - INSTRUMENTATION IN PETROCHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Thermal Processes (Coking)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Thermal Processes in Petroleum Refining?

**Thermal processes** in petroleum refining are a class of secondary processing units that use **heat** (and often **pressure**) — *without* a catalyst — to break (crack) large, heavy hydrocarbon molecules into smaller, more valuable, lighter molecules. They are also referred to as **thermal cracking** processes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Thermal Cracking (KTU Definition):** A process in which large hydrocarbon molecules are decomposed into smaller molecules through the application of high temperatures (typically **450 °C – 900 °C**), sometimes assisted by high pressures (**1 – 70 bar**), in the absence of a catalyst.

### Classification of Thermal Processes (Refinery Perspective)

The main industrial thermal processes are:

| # | Process | Primary Purpose | Typical Operating Temp. |
|---|---------|-----------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Visbreaking** | Mild cracking of residue to reduce viscosity | 450 °C – 500 °C |
| 2 | **Coking** *(focus of this topic)* | Severe cracking of residue to produce gas, naphtha, gas-oil, and **petroleum coke** | 480 °C – 540 °C |
| 3 | **Steam Cracking** | Produce olefins (ethylene, propylene) from light feeds | 750 °C – 900 °C |
| 4 | **Thermal Hydrocracking** | Hydrogen-assisted cracking | 400 °C – 500 °C |

---

## 1.2 What is Coking? — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coking (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** *Coking is a severe thermal cracking process in which heavy petroleum residues (such as vacuum residue, atmospheric residue, deasphalted pitch, or tar sands bitumen) are converted at high temperatures (≈ **480 °C – 540 °C**) and near-atmospheric pressure into lighter valuable products — fuel gas, LPG, naphtha, gas-oil, and a solid carbonaceous residue called **petroleum coke**.*

The word **"coking"** literally comes from the formation of **coke** — a hard, black, porous, carbon-rich solid fuel (essentially **80–95 wt% carbon**), resembling metallurgical coke, but produced from petroleum feedstocks.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Pressure Cooker for Crude Oil"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> Imagine you are cooking a thick, sticky black sauce (representing heavy residue) in a pressure cooker. If you keep heating it for a very long time at a high temperature:
> 1. **Light vapors** (water vapor in our analogy) rise out the top — these are our **fuel gas, LPG, naphtha, gas-oil**.
> 2. A **dry, black, brittle solid** (like burnt rice) remains stuck to the bottom of the cooker — this is our **petroleum coke**.
>
> Coking is essentially the "industrial-scale deep-frying" of the heaviest, lowest-value fraction of crude oil to squeeze out every last drop of useful liquid product, leaving behind solid carbon.

---

## 1.4 Why is Coking Done? — Refinery Motivation

Modern refineries aim to maximize **diesel, gasoline, and jet fuel** yield from crude. However, after atmospheric and vacuum distillation, the bottom product — **vacuum residue (VR)** — is heavy, viscous, and low-value. Refineries have three options for VR:

1. **Sell as fuel oil** (low margin, high sulfur — environmentally penalized).
2. **Hydrocrack it** (requires hydrogen and expensive catalyst).
3. **Coke it** — convert to lighter liquid products + solid coke.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coking is the most economical way to upgrade the heaviest refinery streams into lighter, more profitable products**, while producing **petroleum coke** (a valuable by-product used in aluminum anodes, graphite electrodes, and as a fuel in cement kilns).

---

## 1.5 Types of Coking Processes

There are **three** commercial coking technologies. The instrumentation differs significantly between them.

| Coking Type | Mechanism | Solid Residence | Key Feature |
|-------------|-----------|-----------------|-------------|
| **Delayed Coking** *(most common)* | Reaction in *batch-operated* coke drums downstream of a fired heater | Long (hours–days) | Coke builds up in drum; drum is periodically decoked |
| **Fluid Coking** | Continuous reaction in a *fluidized bed* of coke particles | Seconds–minutes | Continuous coke removal; no batch drum |
| **Flexicoking** | Fluid coking + **integrated coke gasification** | Seconds–minutes | Converts coke *itself* into fuel gas — almost zero solid by-product |

### 1.5.1 Delayed Coking — The Workhorse of the Industry

- **~85–90% of all coking capacity worldwide** uses this technology.
- The term *"delayed"* means the cracking reaction is **delayed** until the feed enters a large, insulated **coke drum**, where it is given enough *time* to crack and coke.
- The heater is operated to heat the residue to **~480–510 °C** — *just below* the temperature where coking would foul the heater tubes.

### 1.5.2 Fluid Coking (ExxonMobil Technology)

- Uses a **fluidized bed** of hot coke particles as the heat carrier.
- Coke is continuously withdrawn and burned in a separate **heater/regenerator** to reheat the seed coke.
- Operates at higher severity and produces **no batch downtime**.

### 1.5.3 Flexicoking (ExxonMobil Technology)

- Extension of fluid coking where the **coke itself is gasified** with steam and air to produce **low-Btu fuel gas** (mainly CO + H₂).
- Produces **almost no solid petroleum coke** — the cleanest coking option environmentally.

---

## 1.6 The Role of Instrumentation in Coking

Since this course is **PEAET861 – Instrumentation in Petrochemical Industries**, our focus is *not* on the chemistry but on **what we measure, how we measure it, and how we control it** in a coking unit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Instrumentation Focus Areas in Coking:**
> 1. **Furnace outlet temperature control** — critical to avoid heater coking.
> 2. **Coke drum switching logic** — a unique batch-control challenge.
> 3. **Coke drum level & pressure** — extremely harsh service for instruments.
> 4. **Decoking water flow & quench** — high water/steam flow measurement.
> 5. **Sulfur, O₂, and combustible-gas analyzers** — for safety in heaters and gas-handling systems.
> 6. **Steam, air, and recycle-gas flow** — for gasification (Flexicoking).

These are addressed in depth in later sections.

---

## 1.7 Standard Metrics for the Coking Process

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Metrics (Bold Highlights):**
> - **Feed:** Vacuum residue / Atmospheric residue / Pitch
> - **Heater outlet temperature:** **485 °C – 510 °C** (delayed); **520 °C – 540 °C** (fluid)
> - **Coke drum pressure:** **1.05 – 4.0 bar (gauge)**
> - **Residence time in drum (delayed):** **8 – 36 hours**
> - **Petroleum coke yield:** **~25 – 35 wt%** of feed
> - **Coke sulfur content:** **0.5 – 8.0 wt%** (depending on feed & grade)
> - **API gravity of feed:** typically **3 – 18 °API** (very heavy)
> - **Coke volatile combustible matter (VCM):** typically **8 – 14 wt%**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Coking Reaction — Chemistry in a Nutshell

The coking reaction is a **free-radical chain cracking reaction** that proceeds through three sequential stages:

1. **Initiation:** A C–C bond breaks homolytically → two free radicals (R• + R'•).
2. **Propagation:** Radicals abstract H from other molecules → new radicals + smaller molecules.
3. **Termination:** Two radicals combine → final stable products (alkenes, alkanes, aromatics).

Over time, repeated condensation and dehydrogenation of polynuclear aromatics produces **mesophase spheres**, which coalesce into **green coke**, which is then calcined to **petroleum coke**.

### Simplified Global Stoichiometry

$$
\text{Vacuum Residue} \xrightarrow{\Delta, \, P} \text{Gas} + \text{Naphtha} + \text{Gas Oil} + \text{Green Coke}
$$

Mass balance: $M_{\text{feed}} = M_{\text{gas}} + M_{\text{naphtha}} + M_{\text{gas oil}} + M_{\text{coke}}$

---

## 2.2 Delayed Coking — Step-by-Step Process Logic

The Delayed Coking Unit (DCU) consists of:

- **A fired heater (coker furnace)**
- **A pair of coke drums** (one *online* filling with coke; one *offline* being decoked)
- **A fractionator** (overhead + side-cuts)
- **Steam, quench, and cooling systems**

### Operating Cycle of One Coke Drum

| Phase | Duration (approx.) | What's Happening |
|-------|-------------------|------------------|
| 1. **Switch & Fill** | 18 – 36 h | Hot feed from furnace enters the drum; vapors go to fractionator |
| 2. **Steam Stripping** | 1 – 3 h | Steam is injected to recover residual hydrocarbons |
| 3. **Water Quench / Cooling** | 2 – 6 h | Cold water fills the drum to cool solid coke |
| 4. **Drain & Unheading** | 1 – 2 h | Water drained, top/bottom heads removed |
| 5. **Hydraulic Decoking** | 4 – 8 h | High-pressure water jet cuts the coke from the drum |
| 6. **Rehead & Pressure Test** | 1 – 2 h | Heads replaced, drum leak-tested, ready for next cycle |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total Cycle Time:** Typically **24 – 48 hours per drum** (process dependent). One drum is *always* in service while the other is being decoked. The DCS (Distributed Control System) must orchestrate this 4-way/2-way drum-switching sequence *perfectly* — a **classic KTU exam topic**.

---

## 2.3 Fluid Coking — Step-by-Step Process Logic

Fluid coking is fully continuous. It uses two vessels:

- **Reactor:** Fluidized bed of coke particles at **520 – 540 °C**; feed is atomized into the bed and cracks instantly.
- **Heater (Burner/Regenerator):** Spent coke particles are partially burned with air at **600 – 650 °C** to reheat them, then recycled to the reactor.

Net result: Continuous feed of residue → continuous production of vapors + continuous withdrawal of product coke.

---

## 2.4 Flexicoking — Step-by-Step Process Logic

Flexicoking adds a **third vessel**:

- **Gasifier:** Steam + air + heat → coke particles are gasified: $\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2$, $\text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2$.

Products of gasification are a **low-Btu fuel gas** (mainly CO + H₂ + N₂) sent to a CO boiler, where the CO is burned for steam generation.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are KTU-board-relevant. Memorize the units, not just the symbols.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | **API Gravity** | $°\text{API} = \dfrac{141.5}{SG_{60/60°F}} - 131.5$ | dimensionless | $SG$ = specific gravity @ 60 °F |
| 2 | **Specific Gravity from API** | $SG = \dfrac{141.5}{°\text{API} + 131.5}$ | dimensionless | |
| 3 | **Conradson Carbon Residue (CCR)** | $\text{CCR}\% = \dfrac{M_{\text{coke residue}}}{M_{\text{feed}}} \times 100$ | wt% | Predicts coke yield |
| 4 | **Coke Yield (empirical, delayed)** | $Y_{\text{coke}} \approx 1.6 \times \text{CCR}\% + \text{(constant)}$ | wt% | Empirical correlation |
| 5 | **Overall Mass Balance** | $F = G + N + \text{GO} + C$ | kg/h | $F$=feed, $G$=gas, $N$=naphtha, GO=gas-oil, $C$=coke |
| 6 | **Conversion (X)** | $X = \dfrac{F - \text{unconverted residue}}{F} \times 100$ | % | For coking, $X \approx 100$ (total conversion) |
| 7 | **Heat Duty (heater)** | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ | kW | Sensible heating of feed |
| 8 | **Heat of Cracking** | $Q_{\text{crack}} = \sum_i \dot{n}_i \cdot (-\Delta H_{c,i})$ | kW | Endothermic reaction |
| 9 | **Coke Drum Fill Time** | $t_{\text{fill}} = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{feed}} \cdot V_{\text{drum}} \cdot (1-\phi)}{\dot{m}_{\text{feed}}}$ | h | $\phi$ = void fraction in coke bed |
| 10 | **Pressure Drop — Ergun Eqn.** | $\dfrac{\Delta P}{L} = \dfrac{150 \, \mu \, (1-\varepsilon)^2}{d_p^2 \, \varepsilon^3} v_s + \dfrac{1.75 \, \rho \, (1-\varepsilon)}{d_p \, \varepsilon^3} v_s^2$ | Pa/m | For fluidized bed; use $\vert$ in plain text |
| 11 | **Decoking Water Flow** | $Q_w = A_{\text{jet}} \cdot v_{\text{jet}}$ | m³/h | High-pressure (2000–3500 bar) jet |
| 12 | **Quench Heat Removal** | $Q_{\text{quench}} = \dot{m}_w \cdot C_{p,w} \cdot (T_{\text{out}} - T_{\text{in}})$ | kW | For cooling hot coke drum |
| 13 | **Heating Value of Coke** | $\text{HHV}_{\text{coke}} \approx 32{,}000 - 4000 \times S\%$ | kJ/kg | Empirical; $S$ = sulfur wt% |
| 14 | **Carbon-to-Coke Conversion** | $\eta_{\text{coking}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{C, coke}}}{M_{\text{C, feed}}}$ | % | Typically 80–95% of feed carbon ends in coke |
| 15 | **Gas Yield (wt%)** | $Y_{\text{gas}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{C1-C4}}}{F} \times 100$ | wt% | Methane, ethane, propane, butanes |
| 16 | **Coke Drum Vapor Load** | $V_{\text{vapor}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{feed}} \cdot (1 - Y_C)}{M_{\text{vapor}}}$ | Nm³/h | Volume of vapor leaving drum |
| 17 | **Steam-to-Oil Ratio** | $\text{SOR} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{steam}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{feed}}}$ | kg/kg | Stripping efficiency indicator |
| 18 | **Cycle Throughput** | $\text{CT} = \dfrac{F \cdot t_{\text{cycle}}}{V_{\text{drum}} \cdot \rho_{\text{coke}}}$ | cycles/day | Coking intensity |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** In derivation problems, examiners will give you feed rate, CCR, API gravity, and yield split, and ask you to compute the **mass balance, energy duty, OR drum fill time**. Practice plugging numbers into the formulas above.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Delayed coking instrumentation** is critical because a single "missed" temperature excursion in the heater can coke the heater tubes → plant shutdown.
- **Coke drum level measurement** is a legendary instrumentation challenge — the drum is at high temperature, contains foaming hydrocarbons, and the product is a *solid*. We use **nuclear (gamma) level** and **guided-wave radar (GWR)** with proprietary steam-out protection.
- **DCS-based drum-switching interlocks** prevent catastrophic overpressure of the coke drum.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Empirical Coke Yield from CCR

> [!NOTE]
> **Context:** Refineries use the **Conradson Carbon Residue (CCR)** of the feed to *estimate* the expected coke yield. A widely used industry correlation (based on Mobil/Rudd) is shown below.

### Given
- Feed CCR = $R_c$ (wt%)
- A delayed coker operated at heater outlet temperature $T_h$ (in °C)

### To Derive
- An expression for the **coke yield $Y_C$** as a function of CCR and temperature.

### Assumed Empirical Relationship
$$
Y_C = a \cdot R_c + b + c \cdot (T_h - T_{\text{ref}})
$$

where $T_{\text{ref}}$ is a reference temperature (typically $T_{\text{ref}} = 500\ °C$).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Base case at reference temperature**

At $T_h = T_{\text{ref}}$, the correlation reduces to a straight line through experimental data:
$$
Y_C = a \cdot R_c + b
$$
Industry data fit gives $a = 1.6$ and $b = -2.0$ (wt%).

**Step 2 — Temperature correction**

Each **10 °C** increase in $T_h$ above the reference raises the coke yield by approximately **1.2 wt%** (empirically). So:
$$
c = \dfrac{1.2\ \text{wt}\%}{10\ °C} = 0.12\ \frac{\text{wt}\%}{°C}
$$

**Step 3 — Combine**

$$
Y_C = 1.6 \cdot R_c - 2.0 + 0.12 \cdot (T_h - 500)
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example**

Given: $R_c = 22\ \text{wt}\%$, $T_h = 510\ °C$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_C &= 1.6 \times 22 - 2.0 + 0.12 \times (510 - 500) \\
&= 35.2 - 2.0 + 1.2 \\
&= 34.4\ \text{wt}\%
\end{aligned}
$$

This means **~34.4 kg of green coke are produced per 100 kg of feed** — a typical value for delayed coking of high-CCR vacuum residue.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Overall Mass Balance on a Coker

### Given
- Feed rate $F$ = 50,000 kg/h vacuum residue
- API gravity of feed = **8 °API** → $SG_f = \dfrac{141.5}{8 + 131.5} = \dfrac{141.5}{139.5} = 1.0143$
- Measured product yields (from plant data):
  - Coke: $Y_C = 32\ \text{wt}\%$
  - Gas: $Y_G = 7\ \text{wt}\%$
  - Naphtha: $Y_N = 18\ \text{wt}\%$
  - Gas oil: $Y_{GO} = 43\ \text{wt}\%$

### To Derive
- The mass rate (kg/h) of each product stream and verify mass balance closure.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Convert wt% yields to kg/h**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{\text{coke}} &= 0.32 \times 50{,}000 = 16{,}000\ \text{kg/h} \\
\dot{m}_{\text{gas}} &= 0.07 \times 50{,}000 = 3{,}500\ \text{kg/h} \\
\dot{m}_{\text{naphtha}} &= 0.18 \times 50{,}000 = 9{,}000\ \text{kg/h} \\
\dot{m}_{\text{gas oil}} &= 0.43 \times 50{,}000 = 21{,}500\ \text{kg/h} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum the products**

$$
\sum \dot{m}_{\text{out}} = 16{,}000 + 3{,}500 + 9{,}000 + 21{,}500 = 50{,}000\ \text{kg/h}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify closure**

$$
\text{Closure Error} = \dfrac{F - \sum \dot{m}_{\text{out}}}{F} \times 100 = \dfrac{50{,}000 - 50{,}000}{50{,}000} \times 100 = 0.0\ \%
$$

Mass balance closes perfectly. **In a real plant, the closure error is typically within ±0.5 %** (instrument accuracy). An error > 2 % indicates a faulty flow meter or sample-analysis error — a classic *instrumentation troubleshooting* scenario for KTU students.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Heat Duty of the Coker Heater

### Given
- Feed: $F = 50{,}000\ \text{kg/h}$
- Feed enters heater at $T_{\text{in}} = 290\ °C$ (from feed preheat train)
- Feed exits heater at $T_h = 510\ °C$
- $C_p$ (average) of feed = **2.5 kJ/(kg·K)** (typical for vacuum residue)
- Endothermic heat of cracking = **250 kJ/kg** of feed

### To Derive
- Total heat input required in the heater (kW).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Convert mass flow to kg/s**

$$
\dot{m} = \dfrac{50{,}000}{3600} = 13.89\ \text{kg/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Sensible heat duty (raising feed from $T_{\text{in}}$ to $T_h$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{sensible}} &= \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot (T_h - T_{\text{in}}) \\
&= 13.89 \times 2.5 \times (510 - 290) \\
&= 13.89 \times 2.5 \times 220 \\
&= 7638.9\ \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Heat of cracking (endothermic)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{crack}} &= \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_{\text{crack}} \\
&= 13.89 \times 250 \\
&= 3472.2\ \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total heat duty**

$$
Q_{\text{total}} = Q_{\text{sensible}} + Q_{\text{crack}} = 7638.9 + 3472.2 = 11{,}111.1\ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to MW (more readable)**

$$
Q_{\text{total}} \approx 11.1\ \text{MW}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Instrumentation Implication:** The heater's outlet temperature is the *single most critical* controlled variable. A rise of 5 °C above setpoint can initiate coking *inside the heater tubes*, while a drop of 10 °C can starve the coker drum of heat and reduce throughput. The control valve on the burner fuel-gas line typically uses **cascade control**: the outlet temperature is the *primary* loop, and the fuel-gas flow is the *secondary* loop.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Coke Drum Fill Time

### Given
- Coke drum inside diameter $D$ = 6 m → radius $r = 3$ m
- Drum tangent-to-tangent height $H$ = 25 m
- Feed flow to the drum $F_{\text{to drum}}$ = 25,000 kg/h
- Coke density (in situ) $\rho_{\text{coke}}$ = 800 kg/m³
- Coke yield $Y_C$ = 32 wt% of feed (assumed same as feed rate basis)
- Void fraction in coke bed $\phi$ = 0.15 (15% voids)

### To Derive
- The time to fill the drum (hours).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Drum volume**

$$
V_{\text{drum}} = \pi r^2 H = \pi \times 3^2 \times 25 = \pi \times 225 = 706.86\ \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Volume occupied by solid coke (effective)**

$$
V_{\text{solid}} = V_{\text{drum}} \times (1 - \phi) = 706.86 \times 0.85 = 600.83\ \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Mass of coke in the drum**

$$
M_{\text{coke}} = V_{\text{solid}} \times \rho_{\text{coke}} = 600.83 \times 800 = 480{,}664\ \text{kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Coke generation rate from feed**

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{coke}} = F_{\text{to drum}} \times Y_C = 25{,}000 \times 0.32 = 8{,}000\ \text{kg/h}
$$

**Step 5 — Fill time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{fill}} &= \dfrac{M_{\text{coke}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{coke}}} \\
&= \dfrac{480{,}664}{8{,}000} \\
&\approx 60.1\ \text{hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Realistic operating time**

We must add **steam-strip + water-quench + decoking + rehead** times, typically 8–14 h extra, so the **total cycle ≈ 70 h**, with 2 drums running in parallel → 1 drum on-stream at any time → **net on-stream time = ~35 h/drum** (typical industrial value).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — How Different Parameters Affect Coke Yield

| Variable | Direction of Change | Effect on Coke Yield | Effect on Liquid Yield | KTU Insight |
|----------|---------------------|----------------------|------------------------|-------------|
| ↑ Feed CCR | Higher | ↑↑ | ↓ | Heavy feeds always give more coke |
| ↑ Heater outlet $T_h$ | Higher | ↑ | ↓ (more gas) | Higher severity |
| ↑ Coke drum pressure | Higher | ↓ slightly | ↑ slightly | Suppresses vaporization |
| ↑ Recycle ratio (heavy gas-oil recycled) | Higher | ↑ | ↓ net | Internal energy waste |
| ↑ Steam-to-oil ratio | Higher | ↓ slightly | ↑ | Better stripping |
| ↑ Residence time in drum | Higher | ↑ | ↓ | More time to crack |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Delayed Coking Unit (DCU) Functional Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** A Mermaid block-flow diagram (BFD) is rendered below to illustrate the *interactions* between the major instruments, control loops, and process units in a delayed coker. The instrumentation emphasis matches the course PEAET861.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Vacuum Residue Feed Tank] -->|FT-100 Feed Flow| A2[Feed Pump P-101]
    A2 -->|Level Control LT-100| A2
    A2 -->|Discharge Line| A3[Heater Pre-Heat Train]

    subgraph FurnaceHeater[Furnace F-201 - Fired Heater]
        A3 --> B1[Convection Section]
        B1 -->|TT-201 Stack T| B1
        B1 --> B2[Radiant Section]
        B2 -->|TT-202 Outlet T| B3[Heater Outlet - 510 C]
    end

    B3 -->|FT-201 Hot Feed to Drums| SV1{4-Way Switch Valve SV-301}

    subgraph CokeDrums[Pair of Coke Drums D-301A and D-301B]
        SV1 -->|Online Path| D1[Coke Drum D-301A]
        SV1 -->|Offline Path| D2[Coke Drum D-301B]
        D1 -->|LT-301A Nuclear Level| D1
        D1 -->|PT-301A Drum Pressure| D1
        D1 -->|TT-301A Drum Vapor T| D1
        D2 -->|LT-301B Nuclear Level| D2
        D2 -->|PT-301B Drum Pressure| D2
        D2 -->|TT-301B Drum Vapor T| D2
    end

    D1 -->|Vapor Outlet| FR1[Main Fractionator Tower T-401]
    D2 -->|Vapor Outlet| FR1
    FR1 -->|TT-401 Overhead T| FR1
    FR1 -->|LT-401 Bottom Level| FR1
    FR1 --> O1[Fuel Gas to Gas Plant]
    FR1 --> O2[LPG to Storage]
    FR1 --> O3[Unstabilized Naphtha]
    FR1 --> O4[Diesel GO to Storage]
    FR1 -->|FT-402 Heavy GO Recycle| SV1

    subgraph DecokingSystem[Decoking and Quench System]
        ST[Steam Supply 35 bar] -->|FT-501 Stripping Steam| D1
        WQ[Quench Water Pump] -->|FT-502 Quench Flow| D1
        DC[Hydraulic Decoke Pump 2500 bar] -->|FE-503 Cutting Water| D1
        D1 -->|Coke Sluice| CS[Coke Pit and Crusher]
    end

    subgraph DCSControl[DCS - Distributed Control System]
        DCS[Honeywell Experion / Emerson DeltaV]
        DCS --- M1[Drum Switching Logic]
        DCS --- M2[Cascade TT - FT on Heater]
        DCS --- M3[Interlock - High PT]
        DCS --- M4[Safety Shutdown SDS-301]
    end
```

### How to Read the Diagram

- **FT** = Flow Transmitter
- **TT** = Temperature Transmitter
- **PT** = Pressure Transmitter
- **LT** = Level Transmitter
- **SV** = Switching Valve
- **M1–M4** = DCS Function Blocks
- Each coke drum is instrumented with the **classical triad** of TT, PT, LT.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — DCS Logic Flow for Coke Drum Switching

This is the *control logic* sequence that the DCS executes every cycle. It is a **favorite KTU 14-marker** question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0([Start: Drum A is OFFLINE, Drum B is ONLINE]) --> S1{Is Drum B FULL? LT-301B at 100%?}
    S1 -->|No| S2[Continue feeding Drum B]
    S2 --> S1
    S1 -->|Yes| S3[Close Feed Valve SV-301 to Drum B]
    S3 --> S4[Start Steam Strip on Drum B - FT-501 > 0]
    S4 --> S5[Open Feed Valve SV-301 to Drum A - DRUM A ONLINE]
    S5 --> S6[Steam Strip on Drum A - FT-501B]
    S6 --> S7[Stop Steam on Drum B after 2h]
    S7 --> S8[Start Water Quench on Drum B - FT-502B]
    S8 --> S9{Drum B Temperature TT-301B < 100 C?}
    S9 -->|No| S8
    S9 -->|Yes| S10[Drain Water from Drum B]
    S10 --> S11[Unhead Drum B - Top and Bottom]
    S11 --> S12[Hydraulic Decoking - FE-503B 2500 bar]
    S12 --> S13[Drum B Empty? - LT-301B at 0%]
    S13 -->|Yes| S14[Rehead Drum B]
    S14 --> S15[Pressure Test Drum B - PT-301B holds]
    S15 --> S16[Drum B STANDBY - Ready for next cycle]
    S16 --> S1
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Comparison of Coking Technologies

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DC[Delayed Coking]
        DC1[Heater 510 C] --> DC2[Batch Coke Drum 24-48 h]
        DC2 --> DC3[Hydraulic Decoking]
        DC3 --> DC4[Green Coke to Calciner]
    end

    subgraph FC[Fluid Coking]
        FC1[Heater Reactor 540 C] --> FC2[Continuous Fluid Bed]
        FC2 --> FC3[Spent Coke to Burner]
        FC3 --> FC4[Hot Coke Recycle]
        FC3 --> FC5[Product Coke Withdrawal]
    end

    subgraph FX[Flexicoking]
        FX1[Heater Reactor 540 C] --> FX2[Continuous Fluid Bed]
        FX2 --> FX3[Spent Coke to Gasifier]
        FX3 --> FX4[Low Btu Fuel Gas to CO Boiler]
        FX3 --> FX5[Ash Withdrawal]
    end
```

---

## 4.4 Instrumentation Loop Diagram — Coker Heater Outlet Cascade Control

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this is the most important loop in coking:** The outlet temperature of the coker furnace controls the severity of the coking reaction. A 5–10 °C drift changes the coke yield by 1–2 wt% — economically significant.

```
                ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
                │                                         │
                │   PRIMARY LOOP (Slow)                   │
                │                                         │
   TT-202 ──►  +    TT_SP = 510 °C                       │
   (Heater   (-)─┤                                       │
    outlet)      │                                       │
                │        SECONDARY LOOP (Fast)            │
                │   FT-203 ──► (+) ── FT_SP              │
                │   (Fuel gas  (-)─┤                      │
                │    flow to       │                      │
                │    burners)      │                      │
                │                  │                      │
                │                  ▼                      │
                │             FCV-201 (Fuel Gas Control   │
                │             Valve to Burners)           │
                │                  │                      │
                │                  ▼                      │
                └───────► Heater F-201  ◄────────────────┘
                          (Burner Fire)
```

**Control Philosophy:**
- **TT-202** measures heater outlet temperature.
- **TT-202's** controller (slow) computes the required **fuel-gas setpoint**.
- The **fuel-gas flow controller** (fast) positions the **FCV-201** to maintain the correct flow.

This **cascade control** rejects burner-pressure disturbances within seconds, protecting the heater outlet temperature to within ±2 °C of setpoint.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / Similar Model]**
**"Define coking. List the three commercial coking processes and state one distinguishing feature of each."** *(3 marks)*

#### Model Answer
**Definition (1 mark):** *Coking is a severe thermal cracking process in which heavy petroleum residues are converted at high temperatures (480–540 °C) and near-atmospheric pressure into lighter products (gas, naphtha, gas-oil) and a solid carbonaceous residue called petroleum coke.*

**Three commercial processes (1.5 marks):**
1. **Delayed Coking** — Batch-operated; coke accumulates in drums for 18–36 hours; drum is decoked hydraulically.
2. **Fluid Coking** — Continuous fluidized-bed reactor; coke particles act as heat carrier; continuous product removal.
3. **Flexicoking** — Extension of fluid coking; includes a gasifier that converts coke to low-Btu fuel gas.

**Distinguishing feature (0.5 mark):** Delayed coking produces *lumps of coke*; fluid coking produces *granular coke*; flexicoking produces *almost no solid coke* (only ash).

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 / Similar Model]**
**"Explain why 'nuclear' or 'guided-wave radar' level instruments are preferred over differential-pressure or float-type level instruments in coke drums."** *(3 marks)*

#### Model Answer
Coke drums present **extremely harsh service conditions** (1 mark):
- **High temperature** (480–510 °C in service, periodic water quench) → damages diaphragms/floats.
- **Foaming, two-phase (liquid + vapor) interface** → false readings on DP cells.
- **Coke deposition on internal surfaces** → displacer/float can stick.
- **Internal obstructions** (vapor hats, thermowells) → mechanical devices fail.

**Nuclear (gamma) level** is non-intrusive — source on one side, detector on the other; measures the *attenuation* of gamma rays through coke vs. void (1 mark). It is unaffected by temperature, foam, or build-up.

**Guided-wave radar (GWR)** uses a coaxial probe to measure the dielectric discontinuity at the vapor/coke interface; suitable where mechanical contact is acceptable (1 mark).

Hence, these two technologies are the **industry standard** for coke drum level measurement.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, each Part-B question is a 14-mark sub-divided problem. KTU allows internal choice. Two alternative questions are given below.**

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model Paper]**

A delayed coking unit is fed with **60,000 kg/h of vacuum residue** at **290 °C**. The Conradson Carbon Residue (CCR) of the feed is **20 wt%** and the heater outlet temperature is **510 °C**. The empirical correlation for coke yield is:
$$Y_C = 1.6 \cdot R_c - 2.0 + 0.12 \cdot (T_h - 500)$$

**(a)** Calculate the **expected coke yield in wt%** and the **coke production rate in kg/h**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Calculate the **total heat duty (in kW)** of the coker heater, given the average specific heat of the feed as 2.6 kJ/(kg·K) and the endothermic heat of cracking as 240 kJ/kg of feed. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to A(a) — Coke Yield & Rate

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify the variables.
- $R_c = 20\ \text{wt}\%$
- $T_h = 510\ °C$
- $T_{\text{ref}} = 500\ °C$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Substitute into the correlation.

$$
Y_C = 1.6 \times 20 - 2.0 + 0.12 \times (510 - 500)
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Simplify.

$$
Y_C = 32.0 - 2.0 + 0.12 \times 10
$$

$$
Y_C = 30.0 + 1.2 = 31.2\ \text{wt}\%
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** State the result.
- **Coke yield = 31.2 wt%** of feed.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Calculate coke production rate.

$$
