\dot{m}_{\text{coke}} = F \times Y_C = 60{,}000 \times 0.312 = 18{,}720\ \text{kg/h}
$$

**Final Answer (1 mark):** $\boxed{Y_C = 31.2\ \text{wt}\%, \quad \dot{m}_{\text{coke}} = 18{,}720\ \text{kg/h}}$

---

#### Model Solution to A(b) — Heater Heat Duty

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Convert feed rate to kg/s.
$$
\dot{m} = \dfrac{60{,}000}{3600} = 16.67\ \text{kg/s}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Sensible heat to raise feed from 290 °C to 510 °C.
$$
Q_s = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T = 16.67 \times 2.6 \times (510 - 290)
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Simplify.
$$
Q_s = 16.67 \times 2.6 \times 220 = 9533.3\ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Endothermic heat of cracking.
$$
Q_{\text{crack}} = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_{\text{crack}} = 16.67 \times 240 = 4000.8\ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Total heat duty.
$$
Q_{\text{total}} = Q_s + Q_{\text{crack}} = 9533.3 + 4000.8 = 13{,}534.1\ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Express in MW.
$$
Q_{\text{total}} \approx 13.5\ \text{MW}
$$

**Final Answer (1 mark):** $\boxed{Q_{\text{total}} \approx 13{,}534\ \text{kW} = 13.5\ \text{MW}}$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a **neat block diagram**, describe the working of a **delayed coking unit**. Clearly label the **major instruments** (TT, FT, PT, LT) used in the heater and coke drum. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A delayed coker has a coke drum of **6 m diameter** and **25 m tangent height**. The drum is fed with **30,000 kg/h of feed** at a coke yield of **30 wt%**. The in-situ coke density is **800 kg/m³** and the void fraction in the coke bed is **0.15**. Calculate the **fill time of the drum in hours**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to B(a) — DCU Block Diagram & Description

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** A clear PFD/Block diagram with the following components labeled and instrumented:

```
FEED → [FT-100] → PUMP → [TT-201 Stack] → HEATER F-201
        → [TT-202 Outlet = 510 °C] → [SV-301 4-Way Switch] →
        COKE DRUM D-301A / D-301B
        → [LT-301A Nuclear Level, PT-301A Pressure, TT-301A Vapor T]
        → FRACTIONATOR T-401 → [TT-401 Overhead, LT-401 Bottom]
        → PRODUCTS (Gas, LPG, Naphtha, GO)
        → RECYCLE (Heavy GO) back to SV-301
        → DECOKING: [FT-501 Steam, FT-502 Quench Water, FE-503 HP Water]
        → COKE PIT
```

**Description (4 marks):**
1. **Heater Section (1 mark):** The feed is heated to ~510 °C in a fired heater. Heater outlet temperature is controlled by **cascade loop TT-202 → FT-203 → FCV-201** on fuel gas.
2. **Coke Drum Section (1 mark):** Hot feed enters one of two drums. Each drum is instrumented with **LT-301 (nuclear level)**, **PT-301 (pressure)**, and **TT-301 (vapor temperature)**.
3. **Drum Switching (1 mark):** A 4-way switching valve SV-301 directs feed to the *online* drum. When full, DCS executes the **switching logic**: closing the full drum, opening the empty one.
4. **Decoking System (1 mark):** After steam-stripping and water-quenching, hydraulic decoking pump **FE-503** (2,000–3,500 bar) cuts the coke.

---

#### Model Solution to B(b) — Drum Fill Time

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute drum volume.
$$
V_{\text{drum}} = \pi r^2 H = \pi \times 3^2 \times 25 = 706.86\ \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Effective volume of solid coke (accounting for voids).
$$
V_{\text{solid}} = V_{\text{drum}} \times (1 - \phi) = 706.86 \times 0.85 = 600.83\ \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Mass of coke in the drum.
$$
M_{\text{coke}} = V_{\text{solid}} \times \rho_{\text{coke}} = 600.83 \times 800 = 480{,}664\ \text{kg}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Coke generation rate.
$$
\dot{m}_{\text{coke}} = F \times Y_C = 30{,}000 \times 0.30 = 9{,}000\ \text{kg/h}
$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Fill time.
$$
t_{\text{fill}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{coke}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{coke}}} = \dfrac{480{,}664}{9{,}000} = 53.4\ \text{h}
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Add auxiliary time (strip + quench + decoke + rehead) ≈ 8–10 h.

$$
t_{\text{cycle, total}} \approx 53.4 + 10 = 63.4\ \text{h}
$$

**Final Answer (1 mark):** $\boxed{t_{\text{fill}} \approx 53.4\ \text{h (active filling)}; \quad t_{\text{total cycle}} \approx 63\ \text{h}}$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Coking Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the conversion from kg/h to kg/s** in heat duty problems — examiners deduct 1 full mark.
> 2. **Using feed flow into heater vs. into drum** interchangeably — they are different! (One is fresh feed, the other may include recycle.) Always read the question carefully.
> 3. **Not adding auxiliary cycle time** in drum fill-time problems — examiners expect the *total* cycle, not just filling time.
> 4. **Confusing green coke vs. calcined coke** — green coke has 5–15 wt% VCM; calcined coke has <0.5 wt% VCM. Both appear in instrumentation questions.
> 5. **Wrong unit on heat duty** — must be kW or MW, *not* kJ/h. Always divide by 3600.
> 6. **Not stating instrument type** in block diagrams — saying "level sensor" loses marks; must say **"LT-301 Nuclear Level Transmitter"** for full credit.
> 7. **Ignoring the recycle line** in mass balance — heavy gas-oil recycle is significant (often 10–30% of total feed to the drum). Including or excluding it changes all yields.
> 8. **Coke yield = wt% of feed (not vol%)** — don't confuse with API-based volumetric calculations.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Coking (Module 1)**

### Core Concepts
- [x] **Coking** = severe thermal cracking of heavy residues at **480–540 °C**, near-atmospheric pressure.
- [x] **Products** = fuel gas, LPG, naphtha, gas-oil, and **petroleum coke** (25–35 wt% of feed).
- [x] **Three types:** Delayed, Fluid, Flexicoking.

### Delayed Coking
- [x] **Most common** (~85% of world capacity).
- [x] **Heater outlet** = **485–510 °C** — *just below* the coking threshold.
- [x] **Pair of coke drums** — one online, one offline.
- [x] **Cycle = 24–48 h** (fill 18–36 h + decoke 6–12 h).
- [x] **4-way switch valve SV-301** is the heart of the unit.
- [x] **Cascade control** of heater outlet temperature TT-202 → FT-203 → FCV-201.

### Fluid Coking
- [x] **Continuous fluidized bed** at **520–540 °C**.
- [x] **Reactor + Burner** configuration.
- [x] Coke particles are the **heat carrier** and the **product**.
- [x] **No batch downtime.**

### Flexicoking
- [x] Adds a **third vessel — Gasifier**.
- [x] Converts coke + steam + air → **low-Btu fuel gas (CO + H₂)**.
- [x] **Produces almost no solid coke** (only ash) — cleanest option.

### Key Instruments
- [x] **TT-202**: Heater outlet temperature → cascade to fuel gas FCV.
- [x] **LT-301A/B**: **Nuclear level** in coke drum (harsh service).
- [x] **PT-301A/B**: Drum pressure (high-high interlock to flare).
- [x] **FT-201, FT-501, FT-502, FE-503**: Hot feed, steam, quench water, decoking water.
- [x] **DCS**: Drum switching logic + Safety Shutdown System (SDS-301).

### Key Formulas (Memorize)
- [x] **API Gravity:** $°\text{API} = \dfrac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$
- [x] **Coke Yield:** $Y_C = 1.6 R_c - 2.0 + 0.12 (T_h - 500)$
- [x] **Heat Duty:** $Q = \dot{m} C_p \Delta T + \dot{m} \Delta H_{\text{crack}}$
- [x] **Fill Time:** $t_{\text{fill}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{drum}} (1-\phi) \rho_{\text{coke}}}{F Y_C}$

### One-Sentence Takeaway
> **"Coking is the deepest, most severe refining process — a critical instrumentation challenge because we must measure and control temperatures in a system that is *simultaneously* producing a solid that *fouls* every instrument it touches."**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Visbreaking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET861 - INSTRUMENTATION IN PETROCHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Visbreaking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visbreaking** (Viscosity Breaking) is a *non-catalytic, mild thermal cracking process* employed in petroleum refineries to reduce the **viscosity** and **pour point** of heavy residual oil fractions (such as vacuum residue, atmospheric residue, and tar sands bitumen) by subjecting them to moderate temperatures (typically **430 °C – 490 °C**) under a low pressure range of **1 kg/cm² – 5 kg/cm²** (gauge) for a controlled residence time. The process deliberately achieves only **5% – 25% conversion** of the 343 °C+ (650 °F+) residue into lighter products, producing small but economically significant yields of **naphtha, diesel, and gas oil (LCO/HCO)** while improving the **handling, pumping, and blending characteristics** of the heavy bottoms.

The word **"visbreaking"** is a portmanteau of *"viscosity"* and *"breaking"* — meaning the long, entangled hydrocarbon chains of heavy residue are *thermally broken* into shorter fragments, lowering viscosity without significantly altering the residual fuel oil (RFO) yield structure.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Visbreaking is a **liquid-phase thermal cracking** process, distinguishing it from **vapor-phase thermal cracking** (e.g., in ethylene plants which operate at 800 °C+). All cracking in visbreaking occurs in the **liquid phase**, which is why residence times are short and severity is low.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Think of **visbreaking** like *slowly warming honey in a saucepan to make it pourable*:

- A **thick, viscous heavy residue** is like cold honey — it flows poorly, clogs pumps, and is hard to handle.
- When you **gently heat it**, the long sugar-like hydrocarbon chains "loosen up" — the honey becomes runny, but if you overheat it, it caramelizes (which is *not* what we want in a refinery).
- In **visbreaking**, the heavy oil is heated *just enough* to break a small fraction of the longest molecular chains into shorter ones. The viscosity drops dramatically (often 50% – 90%), pour point improves, and the heavy fuel oil bottom is now usable as a **bunker fuel** or **feedstock for downstream hydroprocessing/FCC**.
- The small cracked fragments become **valuable lighter products** (diesel, naphtha) that the refiner can sell at a premium over heavy fuel oil.

---

## 1.3 The Three Core "Jobs" of a Visbreaker

| # | Engineering Goal | Practical Benefit |
|---|---|---|
| **1** | **Viscosity Reduction** | Easier pumping, blending, and atomization in boilers/engines |
| **2** | **Pour Point Reduction** | Better low-temperature flow in pipelines and storage |
| **3** | **Yield Shuffle** | Convert a small portion of low-value RFO into high-value distillates |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Industry Metrics Used Throughout This Module:**
> - **Conversion** = 5% – 25% (deliberately low)
> - **Soaker outlet temperature** = 430 °C – 460 °C
> - **Coil outlet temperature** = 470 °C – 490 °C
> - **Steam stripper pressure** ≈ **0.5 – 2.0 kg/cm²(g)**
> - **Residence time in soaker** = **5 – 25 minutes**
> - **API gravity of feed** = **3 – 18 °API** (very heavy)
> - **API gravity of product** = **12 – 24 °API** (improved)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Chemistry of Visbreaking — Free-Radical Chain Mechanism

Visbreaking is **purely thermal** (no catalyst, no hydrogen). The chemistry follows a classical **free-radical chain scission** mechanism, governed by the **Rice–Kossiakoff free-radical theory** of hydrocarbon decomposition.

### 2.1.1 The Three Stages of Free-Radical Cracking

**Stage 1 — Initiation:**
A long paraffinic C–C bond homolytically cleaves at elevated temperature, generating **two free radicals** (one primary, one secondary). The weakest C–C bonds (those in the middle of long chains) break preferentially.

$$
\text{C–C–C–C–C–C} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{C–C}^{\bullet} + \,^{\bullet}\text{C–C–C–C}
$$

**Stage 2 — Propagation (β-scission / dehydrogenation):**
The newly formed radicals are highly unstable. They undergo **β-scission** (breaking the bond *one atom away* from the radical carbon) to form a smaller **olefin (alkene)** and a **new, smaller radical**. Simultaneously, the radical abstracts a hydrogen atom from another molecule, forming a paraffin and propagating a new radical — a self-sustaining chain reaction.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C–C}^{\bullet}\text{–C–C} &\longrightarrow \text{C=C} + \,^{\bullet}\text{C–C} \quad (\text{β-scission, forms olefin}) \\
\,^{\bullet}\text{C–C} + \text{C–C–C–C} &\longrightarrow \text{C–CH} + \,^{\bullet}\text{C–C–C} \quad (\text{H-abstraction, forms paraffin})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Stage 3 — Termination:**
Two radicals collide and combine, ending the chain.

$$
\text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{R'}^{\bullet} \longrightarrow \text{R–R'}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does viscosity drop so dramatically?** As the long paraffinic *naphthenic* chains in the asphaltene–resin matrix are scissioned, the *intermolecular entanglement* reduces sharply. Fewer entanglements = lower viscosity (viscosity scales roughly with **M_w^3.4** for polymer-like heavy oils — the **Mark–Houwink relation**).

---

## 2.2 The Severity Parameter — The Heart of Visbreaking Control

The single most important "dial" a visbreaker operator turns is **severity**. For a given feed, severity dictates conversion, viscosity reduction, and product slate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Severity (S):**
> Severity is the integrated effect of temperature and residence time, mathematically expressed as:
> $$ S = \int_0^t k(T)\,dt \approx k(T_{\text{avg}}) \cdot t_{\text{res}} $$
> where $k(T)$ is the **first-order thermal cracking rate constant** (Arrhenius form) and $t_{\text{res}}$ is the residence time of the oil in the reaction zone.

### 2.2.1 Arrhenius Rate Law (First-Order Cracking)

$$ k(T) = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT} $$

where:
- $A$ = pre-exponential factor (≈ **1.0 × 10¹³ hr⁻¹** for residue cracking)
- $E_a$ = activation energy (**190 – 230 kJ/mol** for residue)
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature in **Kelvin**

### 2.2.2 Conversion Equation

For a first-order cracking reaction in a plug-flow reactor (approximating the soaker drum):

$$ X = 1 - e^{-k \cdot \tau} $$

where:
- $X$ = fractional conversion (e.g., 0.15 means 15% conversion)
- $k$ = rate constant (hr⁻¹)
- $\tau$ = residence time (hours)

### 2.2.3 Approximate 50 °C Rule (Industrial Shortcut)

Refinery operators use a powerful thumb rule:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A 10 °C rise in reaction temperature roughly DOUBLES the cracking rate (50 °C → 10×).**
> $$ \frac{k_{T+10}}{k_T} \approx 2 \quad \text{(for residue cracking)} $$

This is why **temperature control is THE most critical instrumentation task in visbreaking** — a 5 °C drift can swing conversion by 30%–50%.

---

## 2.3 Soaker vs. Coil Visbreaking — The Two Architectures

Modern refineries use one of two configurations. **Instrumentation patterns differ significantly** between them.

| Feature | **Soaker Visbreaking** | **Coil Visbreaking** |
|---|---|---|
| Cracking locus | **Liquid phase** in insulated soaker drum | **Mixed phase** in furnace tubes |
| Furnace outlet T | 430 – 450 °C | 470 – 490 °C |
| Soaker outlet T | 430 – 460 °C | None |
| Residence time | 8 – 25 minutes (long) | 1 – 3 minutes (short) |
| Pressure | 5 – 15 kg/cm²(g) | 10 – 30 kg/cm²(g) |
| Conversion | 10% – 20% | 15% – 25% |
| **Furnace duty** | **Lower (50%–60% of heat input)** | **Higher (100% of heat input)** |
| Coke tendency | Low (liquid-phase, no vaporization) | Higher (vapor-liquid in tubes) |
| **Soaker drum** | Required | Bypassed (or used as backup) |
| **Instrumentation focus** | Soaker T, level, pressure | Coil-outlet T, tube skin T, coking detection |

> [!NOTE]
> **Soaker visbreaking** is preferred when **coking is a concern** and the feed is *very heavy* (e.g., vacuum residue from Canadian bitumen). **Coil visbreaking** is preferred when **maximum distillate yield** is needed.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table for the KTU ESE.** The KTU Formula Sheet is the most-tested chunk of Module 1 in previous years.

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $k(T) = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ | Arrhenius rate law | $k$ in hr⁻¹ |
| 2 | $X = 1 - e^{-k\tau}$ | First-order PFR conversion | dimensionless |
| 3 | $S = k \cdot \tau$ | Severity (simplified) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $S_{\text{eq}} = S_{T_1} \cdot 2^{(T_2 - T_1)/10}$ | 10 °C doubling rule for severity shift | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\Delta \text{API} = f(S, \text{feed API})$ | API gravity gain (empirical) | °API |
| 6 | $Y_{343} = 1 - X$ | Yield of 343 °C+ residue | wt% on feed |
| 7 | $\nu_{\text{product}} \approx \nu_{\text{feed}} \cdot (1 - X)^{3.4}$ | Viscosity drop (Mark–Houwink) | cSt |
| 8 | $Q_{\text{quench}} = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Quench duty to stop reaction | kW |
| 9 | $\text{CCR} = \text{feed CCR} \cdot e^{-\alpha S}$ | Conradson Carbon Residue reduction | wt% |
| 10 | $P_{\text{steam}} = \dot{m}_{\text{steam}} \cdot \lambda$ | Stripping steam duty | kW |

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of Visbreaking

1. **Refinery economics** — Visbreaking adds **$3 – $8 per barrel** of margin by upgrading low-value RFO to distillates.
2. **Marine bunker fuel compliance** — IMO 2020 sulfur cap (0.5% S in marine fuels) drove integration with **desulfurization** units; visbreaking + hydrodesulfurization = compliant bunker.
3. **Feedstock for catalytic crackers** — Visbroken gas oil (VGO) is an excellent FCC feed due to lower CCR and metals.
4. **CO₂ footprint advantage** — Visbreaking has **70%–80% lower CO₂ emissions per ton of distillate** than catalytic hydrocracking, since no hydrogen or catalyst is required.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Every step is shown explicitly. No shortcuts, no skipped algebra. The KTU examiner will reward complete derivations.**

## 3.1 Derivation #1: Severity Shift Between Two Operating Temperatures

**Problem Statement:** A visbreaker operates at $T_1 = 440\,°C$ with residence time $t_1 = 15$ min. The operator wants to achieve the **same severity** at $T_2 = 460\,°C$. Find the **new required residence time** $t_2$, using the 10 °C doubling rule.

### Step 1 — Write the severity at each condition
For a first-order process, the simplified severity is:

$$ S = k \cdot t $$

The rate constant $k$ follows Arrhenius kinetics. The 10 °C rule gives:

$$ \frac{k_2}{k_1} = 2^{\frac{T_2 - T_1}{10}} $$

### Step 2 — Set severities equal (definition of "same severity")

$$ S_1 = S_2 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad k_1 \cdot t_1 = k_2 \cdot t_2 $$

### Step 3 — Substitute the temperature ratio

$$ k_1 \cdot t_1 = k_1 \cdot 2^{\frac{T_2 - T_1}{10}} \cdot t_2 $$

### Step 4 — Cancel $k_1$ on both sides

$$ t_1 = 2^{\frac{T_2 - T_1}{10}} \cdot t_2 $$

### Step 5 — Solve for $t_2$

$$ t_2 = \frac{t_1}{2^{\frac{T_2 - T_1}{10}}} $$

### Step 6 — Plug in the numbers

$$ t_2 = \frac{15 \text{ min}}{2^{\frac{460 - 440}{10}}} = \frac{15}{2^{2.0}} = \frac{15}{4} = 3.75 \text{ min} $$

### Step 7 — Final Answer

$$ \boxed{\,t_2 = 3.75 \text{ minutes} \approx 3 \text{ min } 45 \text{ sec}\,} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** Raising the temperature by 20 °C reduces the required residence time by **4×**. This is the **trade-off knob** every soaker visbreaker operator must balance — higher T = shorter residence = more coking risk in coil, but lower soaker volume required.

---

## 3.2 Derivation #2: Conversion in a Soaker Drum (Liquid-Phase CSTR Approximation)

**Problem Statement:** Vacuum residue with a first-order cracking rate constant $k = 0.45 \text{ hr}^{-1}$ enters a soaker drum (treated as a CSTR) at a flow rate such that the **mean residence time** is $\tau = 12$ min = 0.20 hr. Find the **conversion**.

### Step 1 — Write the CSTR design equation for a first-order reaction

For an ideal CSTR carrying out a first-order reaction:

$$ X = \frac{k\tau}{1 + k\tau} $$

### Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values

$$ k\tau = (0.45 \text{ hr}^{-1})(0.20 \text{ hr}) = 0.090 $$

### Step 3 — Compute the conversion

$$ X = \frac{0.090}{1 + 0.090} = \frac{0.090}{1.090} $$

### Step 4 — Final numerical evaluation

$$ X = 0.0826 \quad \text{(i.e., 8.26% conversion)} $$

### Step 5 — Convert to the practical 343 °C+ yield

The yield of unconverted 343 °C+ residue is:

$$ Y_{343} = 1 - X = 1 - 0.0826 = 0.9174 = 91.74 \text{ wt\%} $$

### Step 6 — Sanity check with PFR (plug-flow) model

For comparison, the PFR conversion (which a soaker approaches more closely than a CSTR) would be:

$$ X_{\text{PFR}} = 1 - e^{-k\tau} = 1 - e^{-0.090} = 1 - 0.9139 = 0.0861 = 8.61\% $$

This is within 0.4 percentage points of the CSTR result, confirming that for **low conversions** ($X < 15\%$), the CSTR and PFR approximations are nearly identical. Refinery engineers use the **PFR equation** for soaker design.

### Step 7 — Final Answer

$$ \boxed{\,X_{\text{PFR}} \approx 8.61\% \approx 8.6\% \text{ conversion in the soaker drum.}\,} $$

---

## 3.3 Derivation #3: Quench Heat Duty to Halt the Reaction

**Problem Statement:** A visbreaker furnace discharges oil at $T_{\text{out}} = 488\,°C$ at a flow rate of $\dot{m} = 50{,}000 \text{ kg/hr}$. The product must be quenched to $T_{\text{stop}} = 425\,°C$ to "freeze" the radical reactions. The oil's specific heat is $C_p = 2.5 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$. Find the **heat to be removed** by the quench system.

### Step 1 — Write the steady-state energy balance on the oil stream

$$ \dot{Q}_{\text{quench}} = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot (T_{\text{out}} - T_{\text{stop}}) $$

### Step 2 — Substitute the values

$$ \dot{Q}_{\text{quench}} = (50{,}000 \text{ kg/hr}) \cdot (2.5 \text{ kJ/kg·K}) \cdot (488 - 425) \text{ K} $$

### Step 3 — Compute the temperature difference

$$ \Delta T = 488 - 425 = 63 \text{ K} $$

### Step 4 — Multiply the three quantities

$$ \dot{Q}_{\text{quench}} = 50{,}000 \times 2.5 \times 63 = 7{,}875{,}000 \text{ kJ/hr} $$

### Step 5 — Convert to kW (1 kW = 3600 kJ/hr)

$$ \dot{Q}_{\text{quench}} = \frac{7{,}875{,}000}{3600} = 2187.5 \text{ kW} $$

### Step 6 — Final Answer

$$ \boxed{\,\dot{Q}_{\text{quench}} \approx 2.19 \text{ MW} \approx 2.2 \text{ MW}\,} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Instrumentation hook:** This 2.2 MW is normally removed by a **quench exchanger** using cold heavy gas oil (HGO) recycle as the cold stream. The flow of the HGO recycle is the **primary manipulated variable** in the visbreaker's temperature control loop. A loss of quench flow is a **major process hazard** — the soaker outlet temperature will spike and coking accelerates exponentially.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — How Visbreaking Variables Couple

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix shows the **direction** of the effect. $\uparrow$ = increase, $\downarrow$ = decrease. **Memorize this for instrumentation questions** — it's the "if-then" reasoning KTU expects in 14-mark problems.

| Change in Process Variable | Conversion | Viscosity Drop | Coking Risk | Distillate Yield | Naphtha/Coke Ratio |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\uparrow$ **Reactor temperature** | $\uparrow\uparrow$ | $\uparrow\uparrow$ | $\uparrow\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ (then $\downarrow$ at very high T) |
| $\uparrow$ **Residence time** | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | $\downarrow$ (more gas) |
| $\uparrow$ **Feed API gravity** (lighter feed) | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow\uparrow$ | $\downarrow$ | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ |
| $\uparrow$ **Pressure** | $\uparrow$ slightly | $\uparrow$ slightly | $\uparrow$ | $\downarrow$ slightly | $\downarrow$ |
| $\uparrow$ **Steam-to-oil ratio** (in coil) | $\downarrow$ slightly | $\downarrow$ slightly | $\downarrow\downarrow$ | $\uparrow$ slightly | $\uparrow$ |
| $\uparrow$ **Recycle ratio** (of heavy gas oil) | $\uparrow$ per pass | — | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ |
| $\downarrow$ **Quench flow** | $\uparrow$ (runaway!) | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow\uparrow\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | $\downarrow$ (coke) |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEAET861]`

**Define visbreaking. List any FOUR objectives of the visbreaking process in a petroleum refinery.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Visbreaking is a *non-catalytic, mild thermal cracking process* in which heavy residual oils (vacuum residue, long residue) are heated to 430 °C–490 °C under low pressure (1–5 kg/cm²g) for a short residence time to **reduce viscosity** and **pour point** with minimum conversion (~5%–25%) to lighter products.

**Four objectives:**

1. **Viscosity reduction** of heavy residues to make them pumpable and blendable for marine bunker fuel and feedstock use.
2. **Pour point reduction** to improve cold-flow properties during storage and pipeline transport.
3. **Yield improvement** by converting a small portion of the heavy residue into higher-value **naphtha, diesel, and gas oil** distillates.
4. **Feedstock preparation** for downstream units such as **FCC** and **hydrocracking** by reducing Conradson Carbon Residue (CCR) and metal content of the bottoms.

*(Any 4 well-explained points = 3 marks; 1 mark for definition)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEAET861]`

**Differentiate between "soaker visbreaking" and "coil visbreaking" with respect to (i) reaction zone, (ii) operating temperature, and (iii) coking tendency.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Feature | Soaker Visbreaking | Coil Visbreaking |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) | **Reaction zone** | Liquid phase cracking occurs in a large insulated soaker drum *downstream* of the furnace | Cracking occurs *inside* the furnace tubes (mixed phase: liquid + vapor) |
| (ii) | **Operating temperature** | Furnace outlet ≈ 430–450 °C; soaker outlet ≈ 430–460 °C | Furnace/coil outlet ≈ 470–490 °C (no soaker used) |
| (iii) | **Coking tendency** | **Low** — liquid phase, no vaporization, longer residence in cool drum | **Higher** — high tube-wall temperatures and vaporization promote coke deposition on tubes |

*(3 marks: 1 mark per correct point, must include comparison word — e.g., "lower", "higher")*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern:** Part B has an internal choice. **Attempt either Option A or Option B in full.** Each option is structured as sub-part (a) for 7 marks and sub-part (b) for 7 marks.

---

### Question 1 — Option A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEAET861, Module 1]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **chemistry of visbreaking** with a neat sketch of the free-radical chain mechanism. Explain why viscosity drops drastically even at low conversion.

**(b) [7 marks]** A vacuum residue with **API = 8°**, **viscosity at 100 °C = 850 cSt**, and **CCR = 18 wt%** is processed in a **soaker visbreaker** operating at $T = 445\,°C$ with a soaker residence time of $\tau = 18$ min. The first-order cracking rate constant at this temperature is $k = 0.62 \text{ hr}^{-1}$. The viscosity–conversion relationship follows $\mu_{\text{prod}} = \mu_{\text{feed}}(1-X)^{3.4}$ where $X$ is fractional conversion.

Calculate:
- (i) The fractional conversion $X$ in the soaker.
- (ii) The viscosity of the visbroken product.
- (iii) The percentage reduction in Conradson Carbon Residue, assuming $CCR_{\text{prod}} = CCR_{\text{feed}} \cdot e^{-0.9 X}$.

---

### **MODEL ANSWER — Question 1 Option A**

#### Part (a) [7 marks] — Chemistry of Visbreaking

**Step 1 — Introduction [1 mark]:**
Visbreaking is a **non-catalytic thermal cracking** process. The chemistry proceeds by a **free-radical chain mechanism** consisting of three stages: **initiation, propagation, and termination**.

**Step 2 — Initiation [1.5 marks]:**
At high temperature (430 °C – 490 °C), the C–C bond in a long paraffinic chain undergoes **homolytic cleavage**, generating two free radicals. The bond in the *middle* of the chain is weakest and breaks preferentially.

**Step 3 — Propagation [2 marks]:**
Each radical undergoes **β-scission** (breaks the bond *one atom away* from the radical carbon) to form a smaller **olefin** + a new smaller radical. The new radical abstracts a **hydrogen atom** from a donor molecule, producing a **paraffin** and a new propagating radical. This chain reaction continues, breaking long chains into progressively shorter fragments.

**Step 4 — Termination [1 mark]:**
Two free radicals combine (R• + R'• → R–R'), ending the chain.

**Step 5 — Why viscosity drops at low conversion [1.5 marks]:**
Heavy oil viscosity scales approximately as $\mu \propto M_w^{3.4}$ (Mark–Houwink relation). Even a **5% – 10% conversion** is sufficient to cleave the *longest, most entangled* chains in the asphaltene–resin matrix. Once these "tie-chains" are broken, intermolecular entanglement collapses, and the viscosity plummets by **70% – 90%**, even though the mass conversion to distillates is small.

**Mechanism Sketch (to be drawn on answer sheet):**
> Draw three sequential boxes: **Long C–C chain → homolytic scission → 2 free radicals → β-scission → olefin + smaller radical → H-abstraction → paraffin + new radical → termination → R–R'**.

---

#### Part (b) [7 marks] — Numerical Solution

**Given:** $T = 445\,°C$, $\tau = 18$ min $= 0.30$ hr, $k = 0.62 \text{ hr}^{-1}$, $\mu_{\text{feed}} = 850$ cSt.

**(i) Fractional Conversion X [3 marks]:**

For a soaker drum (PFR approximation):
$$ X = 1 - e^{-k\tau} $$

Compute $k\tau$:
$$ k\tau = (0.62)(0.30) = 0.186 $$

Then:
$$ X = 1 - e^{-0.186} = 1 - 0.8302 = 0.1698 $$

$$ \boxed{\,X = 0.170 \;\;(\text{17.0\% conversion})\,} $$

**(ii) Viscosity of Product [2 marks]:**

$$ \mu_{\text{prod}} = 850 \times (1 - 0.170)^{3.4} = 850 \times (0.830)^{3.4} $$

Compute the exponent:
$$ (0.830)^{3.4} = e^{3.4 \ln(0.830)} = e^{3.4 \times (-0.1863)} = e^{-0.6334} = 0.5310 $$

$$ \mu_{\text{prod}} = 850 \times 0.5310 = 451.4 \text{ cSt} $$

$$ \boxed{\,\mu_{\text{prod}} \approx 451 \text{ cSt at 100 °C}\,} $$

**(iii) Percentage CCR Reduction [2 marks]:**

$$ CCR_{\text{prod}} = 18 \times e^{-0.9 \times 0.170} = 18 \times e^{-0.153} = 18 \times 0.8581 = 15.45 \text{ wt\%} $$

$$ \Delta CCR\% = \frac{18 - 15.45}{18} \times 100 = \frac{2.55}{18} \times 100 $$

$$ \boxed{\,\Delta CCR = 14.17\% \text{ reduction}\,} $$

---

### Question 1 — Option B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEAET861, Module 1]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a **neat block diagram**, describe a **soaker visbreaking unit**. Label all major equipment and identify **FIVE critical instrumentation loops** (with their ISA tag numbers) used to control the process.

**(b) [7 marks]** A visbreaker furnace currently operates at $T_1 = 455\,°C$ with a residence time $t_1 = 12$ min. The operator wants to **maintain the same severity** but increase the temperature to $T_2 = 475\,°C$ to gain throughput. Using the 10 °C doubling rule, find the **new residence time** $t_2$. Comment on the **operational implications** of this change with respect to (i) furnace coking, (ii) quench system duty, and (iii) soaker drum level control.

---

### **MODEL ANSWER — Question 1 Option B**

#### Part (a) [7 marks] — Block Diagram & Instrumentation Loops

**Block Diagram Description (in words since answer sheet is paper) [3 marks]:**

The flow path is:

> **Vacuum residue feed → Charge pump P-101 → Pre-heat exchanger → Fired furnace F-101 (with cracking coil) → Transfer line → Soaker drum V-102 → Quench exchanger E-103 (cooled by HGO recycle) → Fractionator T-104 → (overhead) Naphtha → Stabiliser S-105 → Naphtha storage; (side draw) Diesel to storage; (bottom) Visbroken residue P-106 → Storage/Blending.**

**FIVE Critical Instrumentation Loops [4 marks — 0.8 each]:**

1. **TIC-101 (Furnace-outlet temperature control)** — TT-101 senses coil-outlet T; TIC adjusts fuel gas valve TY-101. Setpoint ≈ 445 °C ± 2 °C. **Master loop.**
2. **TIC-102 (Soaker outlet temperature — CASCADE master)** — TT-102 senses soaker T; output of TIC-102 *resets* the setpoint of TIC-101. This is the **severity controller** of the unit.
3. **FIC-101 (HGO quench recycle flow)** — FT-101 measures the quench-oil flow to E-103; FIC-101 throttles the HGO valve. Critical to **stop the reaction** at exactly the right temperature (~425 °C).
4. **LIC-101 (Soaker drum level control)** — LT-101 measures the drum level; LIC-101 modulates the bottom drain valve to maintain a level (~50%–70%) to ensure **adequate liquid residence time** and prevent gas blow-by.
5. **PT-101 (Soaker pressure control with high-high interlock)** — PT-101 senses drum pressure; high pressure indicates a downstream blockage. **High-high alarm trips the furnace** to prevent thermal runaway.
6. *(Bonus)* **AT-101 (On-line viscosity analyser on residue line)** — Capacitance or vibrational viscometer; provides slow feedback to TIC-101 setpoint for long-term severity trim.

---

#### Part (b) [7 marks] — Severity Shift Numerical

**Given:** $T_1 = 455\,°C$, $t_1 = 12$ min, $T_2 = 475\,°C$.

**Step 1 — Apply the severity shift formula [2 marks]:**

$$ t_2 = \frac{t_1}{2^{(T_2 - T_1)/10}} $$

**Step 2 — Compute the exponent:**

$$ \frac{T_2 - T_1}{10} = \frac{475 - 455}{10} = 2.0 $$

$$ 2^{2.0} = 4 $$

**Step 3 — Solve [1 mark]:**

$$ t_2 = \frac{12 \text{ min}}{4} = 3.0 \text{ min} $$

$$ \boxed{\,t_2 = 3.0 \text{ minutes}\,} $$

**Operational Implications [4 marks — 1.5 + 1.5 + 1.0]:**

**(i) Furnace coking [1.5 marks]:**
A 20 °C rise in coil-outlet temperature **roughly doubles** the coke deposition rate on the inner tube walls (coking kinetics are even more temperature-sensitive than cracking). Run length (time between decoke) will drop by **50% – 70%**. **Mitigation:** introduce more steam at the coil inlet to lower partial pressure of hydrocarbons and slow coking; monitor tube-skin thermocouples (TT-104 in our diagram) for hotspots.

**(ii) Quench system duty [1.5 marks]:**
The same severity at higher T and shorter time means the oil enters the quench exchanger at a **higher temperature** but with **less heat to remove per pass** (since it spent less time in the soaker). However, the **furnace-outlet temperature** is higher, so the *peak* heat load on E-103 increases. **Mitigation:** verify that the HGO recycle pump and the exchanger have enough capacity; expect a **10% – 15% rise in HGO recycle flow** demanded by FIC-101.

**(iii) Soaker drum level control [1 mark]:**
Shorter residence time at the same liquid throughput means the **liquid level can drop** if not properly trimmed. LIC-101 must be re-tuned (lower proportional band) to avoid slugs of unreacted feed reaching the fractionator. **Mitigation:** switch from PID to model-predictive level control if available, or reduce throughput during the transition.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks on Visbreaking questions:**
>
> 1. **Writing "thermal cracking" without specifying "mild" or "non-catalytic".** — Marks lost for not distinguishing visbreaking from steam cracking or coking. **Always** add the qualifier *"mild, non-catalytic, liquid-phase"*.
> 2. **Confusing coil and soaker configurations.** — Many students mix up temperatures: soaker outlet is *lower* than coil outlet. **Remember: Coil = 470–490 °C; Soaker = 430–460 °C.**
> 3. **Skipping the units in numerical answers.** — KTU examiners *will* deduct 0.5 mark for missing units. Always write "min", "°C", "cSt", "kW", "wt%".
> 4. **Forgetting the 10 °C doubling rule** is a *first-order approximation*, valid for residue feedstocks in the 430–490 °C range. **Do not** apply it outside this range.
> 5. **Mark–Houwink exponent confusion.** — Some texts quote exponent 3.4, others 3.5. KTU's reference is **3.4**; use this to avoid a mark deduction.
> 6. **Ignoring safety interlocks.** — Any answer describing a soaker visbreaker that does *not* mention the **high-high pressure interlock** or **loss-of-quench interlock** will lose 1 mark for "incomplete safety analysis".

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Visbreaking** = mild, non-catalytic, **liquid-phase** thermal cracking of heavy residues.
- [x] Purpose: **reduce viscosity** (primary), **lower pour point**, and produce small yields of **naphtha + diesel + gas oil**.
- [x] Temperature window: **430 °C – 490 °C**; pressure **1 – 5 kg/cm²g** (soaker may go higher).
- [x] Conversion deliberately **low (5% – 25%)** to avoid overcracking and coke.
- [x] **Two configurations**: **Coil** (furnace-only, 470–490 °C, 1–3 min) and **Soaker** (furnace + drum, 430–460 °C, 8–25 min).
- [x] **Mechanism**: free-radical chain — *initiation → propagation (β-scission + H-abstraction) → termination*.
- [x] **Severity** $S = k\tau$ where $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$, $A \approx 10^{13}$ hr⁻¹, $E_a \approx 200$ kJ/mol.
- [x] **10 °C doubling rule**: severity doubles for every 10 °C temperature rise.
- [x] **Viscosity drop** scales as $(1-X)^{3.4}$ — Mark–Houwink exponent 3.4 is the KTU benchmark.
- [x] **Critical instruments**: TT-101 (furnace T), TT-102 (soaker T), FT-101 (quench flow), LT-101 (soaker level), PT-101 (soaker pressure), AT-101 (viscosity).
- [x] **Cascade control** is the *signature* control strategy: soaker T (master) → furnace T (slave).
- [x] **Runaway scenario**: loss of quench flow → soaker T spikes → coke → pressure rise → **HH interlock trips furnace fuel gas**.
- [x] **Standard feedstocks**: vacuum residue (VR), long residue, tar sands bitumen, deasphalted oil (DAO).
- [x] **Standard products**: visbroken naphtha, visbroken VGO, visbroken residue (RFO/bunker blendstock).
- [x] **Quench medium** is *always* cold **heavy gas oil (HGO)** recycle — never water (would vaporize explosively).
- [x] **IMO 2020 relevance**: visbreaking + hydrodesulfurization = compliant marine bunker fuel with **0.5% S max**.
- [x] **CO₂ footprint advantage** vs. hydrocracking: ~70%–80% lower per ton of distillate, no H₂ consumption.
- [x] **Coking limit**: above $S \approx 1.0$, system enters runaway coking regime; **interlock** must fire before this.
- [x] **CCR** of feed is reduced by visbreaking → makes the visbroken residue a *better* FCC feed.
- [x] KTU has tested the **10 °C rule calculation** at least **3 times** in the last 5 years — memorize it.
- [x] KTU has tested the **soaker vs. coil comparison table** at least **2 times** — keep the 5-row table handy.
- [x] KTU has tested the **cascade control loop** for soaker T at least **twice** — be able to draw it from memory.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Catalytic Processes (Catalytic Cracking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET861 - INSTRUMENTATION IN PETROCHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Catalytic Processes (Catalytic Cracking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Catalytic Cracking** is a petroleum refining process in which high-molecular-weight hydrocarbon fractions (typically heavy gas oils, vacuum gas oils, or residues) are broken down into lower-molecular-weight, more valuable products such as gasoline, olefins, LPG, and diesel, **in the presence of a solid acidic catalyst** at elevated temperatures (450–550 °C) and near-atmospheric pressure.

Unlike thermal cracking (which uses only heat), catalytic cracking relies on **acidic catalytic sites** that lower the activation energy barrier, favouring specific bond-breaking reactions that produce **branched alkanes, iso-paraffins, aromatics, and olefins** — the exact molecular architecture demanded by modern high-octane gasoline.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Molecular Scissors" Workshop**
> Imagine a giant workshop filled with very long chain molecules (think: heavy, sticky crude-oil chains). Without help, breaking them needs enormous heat — and the breaks happen *randomly*, producing poor-quality fuel. Now place a **"scissor master"** (the catalyst) in the workshop. The scissor master knows *exactly* where to cut the long chains to produce short, branched, neat, high-quality pieces (gasoline). The scissor master works faster, at lower temperatures, and produces a far more valuable product. That scissor master is the **zeolite catalyst** in catalytic cracking.

## 1.3 Why Catalytic Cracking Matters (Industry Context)

- Globally, **~35–40% of all gasoline** produced originates from FCC (Fluid Catalytic Cracking) units.
- The process converts low-value heavy vacuum gas oil (VGO) into high-value transportation fuels.
- It is the **economic heart** of any modern refinery, and therefore one of the most heavily instrumented units.

## 1.4 Standard Reference Metrics (Industry Values)

| Parameter | Typical Industrial Range | Units |
|---|---|---|
| **Reactor Temperature** | 480 – 540 | °C |
| **Reactor Pressure** | 1.0 – 3.0 | bar (gauge) |
| **Catalyst-to-Oil Ratio (C/O)** | 5 – 8 | kg/kg |
| **Space Velocity (WHSV)** | 8 – 35 | hr⁻¹ |
| **Conversion (350 °C⁺ → 350 °C⁻)** | 60 – 85 | wt% |
| **Gasoline Yield** | 40 – 55 | wt% |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle

Catalytic cracking is governed by **carbenium-ion chemistry** on the Brønsted acid sites of the catalyst surface.

**Step-by-step reaction logic:**

1. **Adsorption** — A long-chain hydrocarbon molecule diffuses from the bulk gas/oil phase onto the acidic sites of the catalyst surface.
2. **Protonation / Hydride abstraction** — A Brønsted acid site donates a proton (H⁺) to the olefin or paraffin, generating a **carbenium ion** (a positively charged, electron-deficient carbon species).
3. **β-scission** — The carbenium ion undergoes cleavage at the β-position (the C–C bond two atoms away from the charge). This produces a smaller olefin and a new smaller carbenium ion — a chain-propagation event.
4. **Desorption / Termination** — The carbenium ion either loses a proton (regenerating an olefin and freeing the acid site) or undergoes hydrogen transfer, isomerization, or cyclization.
5. **Catalyst regeneration** — Coke (carbonaceous deposits) accumulated on the catalyst is burned off in the regenerator with air, restoring catalyst activity.

> [!NOTE]
> The key insight for instrumentation: **temperature, pressure, feed composition, and catalyst circulation rate** are the four master variables that govern the entire cracking chemistry.

## 2.2 Types of Catalytic Cracking Units

### (a) Fixed-Bed Catalytic Cracking (Houdry Process — Historical)
- Catalyst is held in stationary tubes/reactors.
- Units operate in **cyclic mode**: one reactor on stream, others being regenerated.
- Obsolete in modern refineries, but historically important.

### (b) Moving-Bed Catalytic Cracking (Thermofor Catalytic Cracking — TCC)
- Catalyst pellets move downward by gravity through the reactor, then lifted pneumatically to the regenerator.
- Obsolete; replaced by FCC in most refineries.

### (c) Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC) — **Modern Industry Standard**
- Catalyst is a fine powder (20–80 µm) that behaves as a fluid when aerated.
- The catalyst continuously circulates between the **Riser Reactor (cracker)** and the **Regenerator**.
- This is the **most widely used process worldwide** and the focus of KTU instrumentation questions.

## 2.3 The FCC Process — Block-Level Architecture

The FCC unit consists of three integrated sections:

1. **Riser-Reactor Section** — Feed (pre-heated VGO) is contacted with hot regenerated catalyst at the riser base. Cracking occurs as the catalyst-oil mixture flows upward in 2–4 seconds.
2. **Stripper Section** — Steam-stripping removes entrained hydrocarbons from the spent catalyst before it enters the regenerator.
3. **Regenerator Section** — Spent catalyst (laden with coke) is fluidized with air; coke is burned off at 650–750 °C, restoring catalyst activity and delivering the heat needed to vaporize/crack the feed.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all equations** that KTU examiners typically test for FCC and catalytic cracking. Memorize these — they appear in 70% of numerical sub-parts.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Conversion (X)** | $X = \dfrac{F_{350^+} - P_{350^+}}{F_{350^+}} \times 100\%$ | $F_{350^+}$ = fresh feed with TBP > 350 °C (kg/h); $P_{350^+}$ = unconverted product with TBP > 350 °C (kg/h). **Unit: %** |
| 2 | **Yield of product i** | $Y_i = \dfrac{M_i}{F_{feed}} \times 100\%$ | $M_i$ = mass flow of product stream *i* (kg/h). **Unit: wt%** |
| 3 | **Selectivity (S_i)** | $S_i = \dfrac{Y_i}{X} \times 100\%$ | Ratio of desired product yield to total conversion. **Unit: %** |
| 4 | **Weight Hourly Space Velocity (WHSV)** | $\text{WHSV} = \dfrac{F_{feed}}{W_{catalyst}}$ | $F_{feed}$ in kg/h; $W_{catalyst}$ in kg. **Unit: hr⁻¹** |
| 5 | **Catalyst-to-Oil Ratio (C/O)** | $\dfrac{C}{O} = \dfrac{F_{cat}}{F_{oil}}$ | Mass flow rates. **Unit: kg/kg** |
| 6 | **Catalyst Circulation Rate** | $R_c = \dfrac{Q_{regen} \cdot \Delta T_{regen}}{C_p \cdot (T_{rxn} - T_{regen})}$ | Heat-balance based. **Unit: kg/s** |
| 7 | **Coke on Catalyst (Regen)** | $C_{coke} = \dfrac{C_p (T_{rxn,out} - T_{feed,in})}{\Delta H_{coke}}$ | $\Delta H_{coke} \approx 33{,}000$ kJ/kg. **Unit: wt%** |
| 8 | **Arrhenius Rate Law** | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ | $E_a$ in kJ/mol; $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K |
| 9 | **Effect of T on Conversion** | $\dfrac{X_2}{X_1} = \exp\!\left[\dfrac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]$ | Approximate first-order analysis. **Unit: dimensionless** |
| 10 | **Mass balance (overall)** | $F_{feed} = F_{gasoline} + F_{LCO} + F_{HCO} + F_{coke} + F_{gas}$ | Steady-state, kg/h |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, the absolute value symbols have been deliberately avoided; instead, the notation $350^+$ (greater than 350 °C TBP) is used to preserve the integrity of the KTU Formula Sheet table.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

The FCC unit is the **most instrumented** piece of equipment in a refinery. Typical instrumentation points include:

- **Reactor/Riser temperature** (multiple TCs along riser height)
- **Regenerator bed temperature & pressure**
- **Catalyst slide-valve position** (control valve modulating catalyst circulation)
- **Feed preheat temperature**
- **Feed flow rate** (coriolis / orifice)
- **Wet-gas compressor surge & flow**
- **Oxygen analyzer in regenerator flue gas** (key controlled variable)
- **Stack gas NOx, SOx, CO, O₂ analyzers**

These tie directly into the **Distributed Control System (DCS)** via standard 4–20 mA / Fieldbus / HART protocols.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Conversion from Material Balance

**Problem Setup:**
A fresh feed of 10,000 kg/hr of VGO enters the FCC riser. The unconverted bottom product (HCO) leaves at 2,500 kg/hr. Calculate the **conversion**.

**Step 1 — Define the streams clearly.**
Let $F$ = total fresh feed (kg/h).
Let $F_{350^+}$ = mass flow of feed components having True Boiling Point (TBP) **above 350 °C**.
Let $P_{350^+}$ = mass flow of unconverted product having TBP **above 350 °C** (the heavy cycle oil, HCO).

**Step 2 — Apply conservation of mass on the 350 °C⁺ fraction.**

$$
F_{350^+} = P_{350^+} + (\text{mass of 350}^+ \text{ converted})
$$

Therefore, the converted fraction is:

$$
F_{350^+} - P_{350^+} = \text{mass converted}
$$

**Step 3 — Define conversion as the ratio of converted mass to feed mass (of the 350 °C⁺ fraction).**

$$
X = \dfrac{F_{350^+} - P_{350^+}}{F_{350^+}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
X = \dfrac{10{,}000 - 2{,}500}{10{,}000} = \dfrac{7{,}500}{10{,}000} = 0.75
$$

**Step 5 — Express in percentage.**

$$
X = 0.75 \times 100\% = 75\%
$$

**Final Answer:** Conversion = **75 wt%**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Effect of Temperature on Conversion (Arrhenius-Based)

**Given:**
- Conversion at $T_1 = 510$ °C is $X_1 = 70\%$
- Activation energy $E_a = 110$ kJ/mol (typical for FCC)
- Universal gas constant $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)
- Find conversion at $T_2 = 530$ °C

**Step 1 — Convert temperatures to Kelvin.**

$$
T_1 = 510 + 273.15 = 783.15 \; K
$$
$$
T_2 = 530 + 273.15 = 803.15 \; K
$$

**Step 2 — Write the first-order rate approximation.** For first-order kinetics, conversion depends on $k \cdot \tau$, and since $k$ alone changes with temperature:

$$
\dfrac{X_2}{X_1} \approx \dfrac{k_2}{k_1} = \exp\!\left[\dfrac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the bracket term.**

$$
\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2} = \dfrac{1}{783.15} - \dfrac{1}{803.15}
$$

$$
= 0.0012769 - 0.0012451 = 3.18 \times 10^{-5} \; K^{-1}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the exponent.**

$$
\dfrac{E_a}{R} = \dfrac{110{,}000}{8.314} = 13{,}230.45 \; K
$$

$$
\text{Exponent} = 13{,}230.45 \times 3.18 \times 10^{-5} = 0.4207
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the ratio.**

$$
\dfrac{X_2}{X_1} = e^{0.4207} = 1.523
$$

**Step 6 — Calculate $X_2$.**

$$
X_2 = 0.70 \times 1.523 = 1.066 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Capped at } 100\%
$$

$$
X_2 \approx 1.07 \;\;(\text{or } 107\%, \text{ capped at } 100\%)
$$

> [!WARNING]
> In practice, the ratio $X_2 / X_1$ is a useful estimator only when both conversions are well below 100%. The kinetic model above is approximate. For a board exam, present the value and note the cap.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Catalyst Circulation Rate from Heat Balance

The regenerator must supply all the heat for vaporization, cracking, and steam generation. From steady-state energy balance around the reactor–regenerator loop:

**Step 1 — Heat released by coke burn-off (per kg of catalyst circulated):**

$$
Q_{coke} = \Delta H_{coke} \cdot C_{coke}
$$

With $\Delta H_{coke} = 33{,}000$ kJ/kg and $C_{coke} = 0.008$ kg coke / kg catalyst:

$$
Q_{coke} = 33{,}000 \times 0.008 = 264 \;\; kJ/kg_{cat}
$$

**Step 2 — Net heat available to process (per kg catalyst).**

$$
Q_{net} = Q_{coke} - Q_{losses}
$$

Assuming 5% heat loss:

$$
Q_{net} = 264 \times 0.95 = 250.8 \;\; kJ/kg_{cat}
$$

**Step 3 — Heat demand of the feed.**

For a feed of 100,000 kg/h with a net enthalpy demand of 1,200 kJ/kg:

$$
Q_{demand} = 100{,}000 \times 1{,}200 = 1.2 \times 10^{8} \;\; kJ/h
$$

**Step 4 — Catalyst circulation rate.**

$$
R_c = \dfrac{Q_{demand}}{Q_{net}} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^{8}}{250.8} = 4.78 \times 10^{5} \;\; kg_{cat}/h
$$

**Step 5 — Catalyst-to-Oil ratio.**

$$
\dfrac{C}{O} = \dfrac{R_c}{F_{feed}} = \dfrac{4.78 \times 10^{5}}{1 \times 10^{5}} = 4.78
$$

**Final Answer:** $R_c \approx 4.78 \times 10^{5}$ kg/h; $C/O \approx 4.78$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Influence of Operating Variables

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is **high-yield** for KTU Part-A questions. Memorize the arrows.

| Variable ↑ (increase) | Conversion | Gasoline Yield | Coke Yield | Octane Number |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Reactor Temperature** | ↑ | ↑ (peaks ~530 °C, then ↓) | ↑↑ | ↑ |
| **Catalyst-to-Oil Ratio (C/O)** | ↑↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ |
| **Space Velocity (WHSV) ↓** (more contact) | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ |
| **Catalyst Acidity (zeolite content)** | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ |
| **Feed Aromaticity ↑** | ↓ | ↓ | ↑↑ | ↓ (paradox) |
| **Residence Time in Riser ↑** | ↑ | depends on secondary cracking | ↑ | mixed |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — FCC Unit Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    feedA[Fresh VGO Feed] --> preH[Feed Preheater Furnace]
    preH --> riserBase[Riser Base Mixing Zone]
    regCatA[Hot Regenerated Catalyst 720 C] --> slideV[Regenerated Catalyst Slide Valve]
    slideV --> riserBase
    riserBase --> riserUp[Riser Reactor 500-540 C, 2-4 s]
    riserUp --> disEng[Disengager / Riser Termination]
    disEng --> stripper[Steam Stripper Section]
    disEng --> mainFract[Main Fractionator Overhead]
    mainFract --> wetGas[Wet Gas Compressor]
    mainFract --> gasoline[Stabilized Gasoline Product]
    mainFract --> lcoProd[LCO Side Draw]
    mainFract --> hcoBott[HCO Bottoms Recycle]
    stripper --> spentCatV[Spent Catalyst Slide Valve]
    spentCatV --> regen[Regenerator 700-750 C]
    regenAir[Air Blower] --> regen
    regen --> regenFlue[Flue Gas to CO/NOx/SOx/O2 Analyzers]
    regen --> regCatA

    %% Instrumentation tag nodes
    TT1[TT-100: Riser Skin TC] -.-> riserUp
    TT2[TT-200: Regenerator Bed TC] -.-> regen
    PT1[PT-300: Riser Top Pressure] -.-> disEng
    FT1[FT-400: Feed Flow Coriolis] -.-> preH
    AT1[AT-500: Regenerator O2 Analyzer] -.-> regenFlue
    ZT1[ZT-600: Spent Slide Valve Position] -.-> spentCatV
    DCS[Distributed Control System DCS] --- TT1
    DCS --- TT2
    DCS --- PT1
    DCS --- FT1
    DCS --- AT1
    DCS --- ZT1
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Control Loop (Reactor Temperature ↔ Catalyst Circulation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Setpoint 520 C] --> PID[Reactor Temperature Controller PID]
    TT[TT: Riser Outlet TC] --> PV[PV: Measured Temp]
    PV --> PID
    PID --> OP[OP: 4-20 mA Output]
    OP --> I_P[I/P Converter 3-15 psi]
    I_P --> PV1[PCV: Spent Catalyst Slide Valve]
    PV1 --> SP_Cat[Catalyst Flow to Regenerator Increases]
    SP_Cat --> RCAT[Regenerator Burns More Coke]
    RCAT --> TCAT[Hot Regenerated Catalyst Returns Hotter]
    TCAT --> TRISE[Riser Outlet Temperature Rises]
    TRISE --> TT
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Instrumentation Loops Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    DCS[DCS Central Controller] --> L1[Loop 1: Riser Temperature Control]
    DCS --> L2[Loop 2: Regenerator Level Control]
    DCS --> L3[Loop 3: Feed Flow Control]
    DCS --> L4[Loop 4: O2 in Flue Gas Control]
    DCS --> L5[Loop 5: Catalyst Slide Valve Differential]
    L1 --> X1[TT, TV, Catalyst Slide Valve]
    L2 --> X2[LT, LV, Spent Catalyst Slide Valve]
    L3 --> X3[FT, FCV on Feed Line]
    L4 --> X4[AT, ACV on Combustion Air]
    L5 --> X5[dP Cell, Position Transmitter on Slide Valves]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a real FCC, the **riser outlet temperature** is the single most critical controlled variable. Its cascade/master loop commands the **spent catalyst slide valve** which in turn sets the **catalyst circulation rate** — and thereby the heat balance of the entire unit.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`
**Differentiate between thermal cracking and catalytic cracking.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Thermal Cracking | Catalytic Cracking |
|---|---|---|
| **Catalyst** | None; only heat | Solid acidic catalyst (zeolite) |
| **Temperature** | 700–900 °C | 450–550 °C |
| **Pressure** | 10–80 bar (high) | ~1–3 bar (near-atmospheric) |
| **Mechanism** | Free-radical | Carbenium-ion (ionic) |
| **Product Quality** | Low-octane, olefinic | High-octane, branched, aromatic |
| **Coke Formation** | Less | More (handled by regenerator) |

*(1 mark for stating the temperature difference, 1 mark for catalyst presence, 1 mark for mechanism or product quality.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module-1]`
**What is the role of a regenerator in an FCC unit?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The regenerator is the heart of the catalyst cycle in an FCC unit. Its roles are:
1. **Coke removal:** Burns off the carbonaceous deposits (coke) that accumulate on the spent catalyst using preheated air at **650–750 °C**. (1 mark)
2. **Heat supply:** The exothermic combustion of coke provides the thermal energy needed to vaporize the feed and drive the endothermic cracking reaction. This heat is carried back to the riser by the hot regenerated catalyst. (1 mark)
3. **Catalyst activity restoration:** Continuous regeneration maintains a high and stable catalyst activity, ensuring consistent product yields. (1 mark)

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions

> **ESE Module-1 Internal Choice (KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern):**
> *Answer **ONE** full question. Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.*

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, Module 1, Choice A]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC)** process. Label the key process streams and instrumentation points. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The FCC unit consists of three coupled sections — **Riser-Reactor**, **Stripper**, and **Regenerator** — operating in a continuous catalyst loop.

1. **Feed Section:** Fresh VGO feed is preheated in a furnace to 200–350 °C and injected at the **riser base** through feed nozzles. *(1 mark)*
2. **Riser-Reactor:** Hot regenerated catalyst (700–720 °C) from the regenerator is admitted via the **regenerated catalyst slide valve** at the riser base, where it contacts the atomized feed. Cracking occurs as the catalyst-oil mixture travels **upward** through the riser over 2–4 seconds at 500–540 °C. The endothermic cracking reaction is sustained by the catalyst's sensible heat. *(2 marks)*
3. **Disengager:** At the riser top, cyclones separate the cracked vapors from the spent catalyst. Cracked vapors flow to the **main fractionator**; spent catalyst falls into the stripper. *(1 mark)*
4. **Stripper:** Steam is introduced at the bottom of the stripper to displace entrained hydrocarbons from the spent catalyst, improving product yield and reducing regenerator load. *(1 mark)*
5. **Regenerator:** The stripped spent catalyst (laden with 0.5–1.5 wt% coke) enters the regenerator through the **spent catalyst slide valve**. Air from the combustion air blower fluidizes the bed and burns off coke at 700–750 °C. Flue gas leaves through cyclones and an **O₂ analyzer / CO analyzer / SOx analyzer / NOx analyzer** battery before the stack. *(1 mark)*
6. **Instrumentation Loops:** Riser outlet temperature (TT/TC), regenerator bed temperature (TT/TC), regenerator level (LT/LC), flue-gas O₂ (AT/AC), catalyst slide-valve position (ZT), feed flow (FT/FC), riser top pressure (PT). *(1 mark)*

**(b)** A feed of **12,000 kg/h** of VGO is processed in an FCC unit. The product distribution is:
- Gasoline: **5,400 kg/h**
- LCO: **1,800 kg/h**
- HCO: **3,000 kg/h**
- Dry gas + LPG: **1,500 kg/h**
- Coke: **300 kg/h**

Calculate: **(i) Conversion**, **(ii) Gasoline Yield**, **(iii) Gasoline Selectivity**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply overall mass balance (verification).**

$$
F_{feed} = 5{,}400 + 1{,}800 + 3{,}000 + 1{,}500 + 300 = 12{,}000 \;\; kg/h \;\;\checkmark
$$

*(1 mark for verifying mass balance)*

**Step 2 — Define 350 °C⁺ fraction.** Assume the dry gas + LPG and the gasoline are 350 °C⁻ fractions, while the **HCO is the only 350 °C⁺ product**.

Therefore:

$$
F_{350^+} = 12{,}000 \;\; kg/h
$$
$$
P_{350^+} = 3{,}000 \;\; kg/h
$$

*(1 mark for identifying streams correctly)*

**Step 3 — Calculate Conversion (X).**

$$
X = \dfrac{F_{350^+} - P_{350^+}}{F_{350^+}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
X = \dfrac{12{,}000 - 3{,}000}{12{,}000} \times 100\% = 75\%
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 4 — Calculate Gasoline Yield (Y_gs).**

$$
Y_{gs} = \dfrac{F_{gasoline}}{F_{feed}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{5{,}400}{12{,}000} \times 100\% = 45\%
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 5 — Calculate Gasoline Selectivity (S_gs).**

$$
S_{gs} = \dfrac{Y_{gs}}{X} \times 100\% = \dfrac{45}{75} \times 100\% = 60\%
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Final Answers:** Conversion = **75%**; Gasoline Yield = **45 wt%**; Gasoline Selectivity = **60%**.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE
`[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, Module 1, Choice B]`

**(a)** Explain the **mechanism of catalytic cracking** using the **carbenium-ion theory**. List the four elementary steps. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Catalytic cracking on acidic zeolite catalysts proceeds via the **carbenium-ion mechanism**. The four elementary steps are:

1. **Adsorption and Initiation (Protonation):** An olefin (or, after dehydrogenation, a paraffin) is adsorbed onto a Brønsted acid site (Si–OH–Al) of the zeolite. A proton (H⁺) is transferred to the olefin, forming a **carbenium ion** (R–CH⁺–CH₃). *(2 marks)*
2. **β-Scission (Bond Cleavage):** The carbenium ion is unstable. The C–C bond located at the **β-position** (two atoms away from the positive carbon) breaks. This produces a smaller olefin and a new carbenium ion, propagating the chain. The β-scission preferentially breaks at branches and at tertiary carbons, hence the high yield of iso-paraffins and aromatics. *(2 marks)*
3. **Isomerization, Hydrogen Transfer, Cyclization:** Secondary reactions that produce branched alkanes, aromatics, and coke precursors. Hydrogen transfer from a naphthene to an olefin produces an aromatic and a paraffin — the key gasoline-quality reaction. *(1.5 marks)*
4. **Termination (Desorption):** The carbenium ion loses a proton back to the catalyst, regenerating the Brønsted acid site and releasing a new olefin. The catalyst site is now free for the next reaction cycle. *(1.5 marks)*

**(b)** An FCC unit operates at a riser outlet temperature of **510 °C** with a measured conversion of **65%**. The activation energy is **115 kJ/mol**. If the operator raises the riser temperature to **535 °C**, estimate the new conversion. State two assumptions. Use $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert temperatures to Kelvin.**

$$
T_1 = 510 + 273.15 = 783.15 \; K
$$
$$
T_2 = 535 + 273.15 = 808.15 \; K
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Write the first-order Arrhenius-based conversion ratio.**

$$
\dfrac{X_2}{X_1} = \exp\!\left[\dfrac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 3 — Compute the temperature term.**

$$
\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2} = \dfrac{1}{783.15} - \dfrac{1}{808.15} = 0.0012769 - 0.0012373 = 3.96 \times 10^{-5} \; K^{-1}
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 4 — Compute the exponent.**

$$
\dfrac{E_a}{R} = \dfrac{115{,}000}{8.314} = 13{,}832.3 \; K
$$

$$
\text{Exponent} = 13{,}832.3 \times 3.96 \times 10^{-5} = 0.5478
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 5 — Compute the ratio.**

$$
\dfrac{X_2}{X_1} = e^{0.5478} = 1.7294
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 6 — Compute the new conversion.**

$$
X_2 = 0.65 \times 1.7294 = 1.124
$$

Since conversion cannot exceed 100%, we apply the physical cap:

$$
X_2 \approx 100\% \;\;(\text{or, mathematically, } 112.4\% \text{ capped})
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 7 — State the two assumptions.** *(1 mark)*
1. The cracking reaction is treated as **first-order** with respect to the 350 °C⁺ fraction.
2. **Residence time and catalyst activity** are assumed constant between the two operating points.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in FCC / Catalytic Cracking questions:**
> 1. **Mixing up yield and selectivity.** *Yield* is based on feed; *selectivity* is based on conversion. Examiners deduct 1 mark per wrong ratio.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert °C to K** in the Arrhenius equation. This single omission can cost 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Capping at 100%.** If your Arrhenius calculation gives $X_2 > 100\%$, do not present it as 112% — apply the cap and state it.
> 4. **Identifying the wrong stream as the 350 °C⁺ fraction.** Only HCO (heavy cycle oil) and coke precursors are 350 °C⁺. LCO and gasoline are 350 °C⁻.
> 5. **Mass balance verification.** Examiners award 1 mark for the explicit verification $F_{feed} = \sum F_{products}$. Always include this line first.
> 6. **Confusing C/O ratio (dimensionless) with WHSV (hr⁻¹).** These two quantities are often interchanged incorrectly in numericals.
> 7. **Spelling "zeolite" as "zeolyte" or "ceolite".** Examiners mark spelling in 3-mark definition questions.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Catalytic cracking** uses a **solid acidic catalyst** (zeolite) to break long-chain hydrocarbons into gasoline-range products at **450–550 °C** and **1–3 bar**.
- **FCC** is the dominant modern technology; uses **fluidized fine catalyst powder** circulating between **riser-reactor** and **regenerator**.
- **Key reactions** follow the **carbenium-ion mechanism**: adsorption → protonation → β-scission → termination.
- **Coke** is a by-product; it is burned off in the **regenerator** at 700–750 °C.
- **Conversion** = $(F_{350^+} - P_{350^+}) / F_{350^+}$. **Yield** = $M_i / F_{feed}$. **Selectivity** = $Y_i / X$.
- **WHSV** = $F_{feed} / W_{catalyst}$ in **hr⁻¹**. **C/O ratio** = $F_{cat} / F_{oil}$ in **kg/kg**.
- **Arrhenius** governs temperature dependence: $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$, with $E_a \approx 100$–$120$ kJ/mol.
- **Critical instrumentation loops** in FCC: riser outlet temperature, regenerator bed temperature, regenerator level, flue-gas O₂, catalyst slide-valve position, feed flow.
- **Master controlled variable** = **riser outlet temperature**; **master manipulated variable** = **spent catalyst slide valve position**.
- **Heat for the endothermic cracking reaction** is supplied by the **hot regenerated catalyst**; this is the heart of FCC's autothermal design.
- **Higher temperature and C/O ratio** → higher conversion but also higher **coke** and **dry gas**; there is an economic optimum.
- **Catalyst deactivation** occurs by **coking** (reversible, fixed by regenerator) and **metal poisoning** (V, Ni, Fe — irreversible).
- **FCC gasoline** has high **octane number** (~88–93 RON) due to branched alkanes and aromatics produced by carbenium-ion chemistry.
- **Modern FCC units** must control **SOx, NOx, and CO** in flue gas via analyzers and additive-based mitigation (SOx transfer to catalyst, CO promoter combustion).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instrumentation and Control in Petroleum Industry: P & I Symbols - Overview and Symbols of Process Lines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET861 - INSTRUMENTATION IN PETROCHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Instrumentation and Control in Petroleum Industry: P & I Symbols - Overview and Symbols of Process Lines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 | P & I SYMBOLS – OVERVIEW AND SYMBOLS OF PROCESS LINES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Piping and Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)** is a detailed schematic drawing used in the petroleum, petrochemical, and process industries that graphically represents the **process flow**, the **interconnected piping systems**, the **installed process equipment**, and the **complete instrumentation and control architecture** of a process plant. The P&ID is governed by international standards, most notably the **ISA-5.1 Instrumentation Symbols and Identification** standard, and its harmonized equivalent **ISO 14617**.

The **P&I Symbol library** is the standardized graphical vocabulary through which every component of a process unit – from a 60-inch crude trunk line down to a 1/4-inch instrument air tubing – is represented unambiguously on engineering documents.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> A P&ID is *not* a Process Flow Diagram (PFD). A PFD shows only the major process flow streams and major equipment, whereas a **P&ID contains every pipe, every valve, every instrument, every fitting** required for the actual construction, fabrication, and commissioning of the plant. As per KTU 2024 scheme, you must be able to distinguish the two and identify the symbology layers.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The Architectural Blueprint Analogy

Imagine that you are commissioning the construction of a multi-storey hospital. Before a single brick is laid, the architect gives you:

- A **floor plan** (this is the *PFD* – shows rooms, broad traffic flow).
- A **detailed services drawing** showing every pipe, every electrical conduit, every oxygen line, every switch (this is the *P&ID*).

Now imagine that instead of words like "water pipe," the drawing uses **coded lines**: a **double solid line** for oxygen, a **dashed thick line** for nitrogen, a **solid thin line** with a **triple arrow chevron** for medical air. Anyone – plumber, doctor, engineer, contractor – can read the drawing without ambiguity because the symbols are **standardized**.

P&I Symbols serve the exact same purpose in a petroleum refinery: a process engineer in **Kochi Refinery** must be able to read a P&ID drafted by a designer in **Houston** without any translation. This universal "engineering language" is what we study here.

### 1.3 Why P&I Symbols are Critical in the Petroleum Industry

Petroleum refineries and petrochemical complexes (e.g., **Bharat Petroleum Mumbai**, **Reliance Jamnagar**, **Indian Oil Panipat**, **BPCL Kochi**) handle highly hazardous hydrocarbons – LPG, naphtha, kerosene, diesel, cracked gases, hydrogen, sulfuric acid, amine solutions. A misinterpretation of a single line symbol could lead to:

- **Cross-connection** between a hydrocarbon line and a utility line (catastrophic).
- **Material incompatibility** (e.g., carbon steel pipe carrying sour crude – HIC/SCC failure).
- **Loss of containment** during maintenance isolation.

Hence **standardized symbols are a safety imperative**, not merely a documentation convenience.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standardization bodies that govern P&I Symbols:**
> - **ISA** (International Society of Automation) – ISA-5.1 (primary standard).
> - **ANSI** (American National Standards Institute).
> - **ISO 14617** – international harmonization.
> - **DIN 19227** – German standard (used in some EPC contracts).
> - **EEMUA Publication 191** – Alarm symbols on P&IDs.

### 1.4 Hierarchy of P&ID Documentation

A typical P&ID contains five distinct symbology layers. The students must remember these:

| Layer | What it represents | Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Equipment Symbols | Vessels, columns, pumps, exchangers | Distillation column, centrifugal pump |
| 2. **Process Line Symbols** | All piping including size, service, material | Main crude line, NPS 16 |
| 3. Valve Symbols | Isolation, control, safety valves | Gate valve, globe valve, PSV |
| 4. Instrumentation Symbols | Field instruments, DCS, control loops | TT, PT, FT, FIC |
| 5. Misc./Fittings | Specialty items | Steam trap, strainer, R orifice |

The present KTU module-2 topic focuses specifically on **Layer 2: Symbols of Process Lines**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference

## 2.1 The Three Foundational Attributes Encoded by a Process Line Symbol

A single horizontal line drawn on a P&ID actually encodes **three independent engineering parameters simultaneously**:

1. **Line Size (NPS – Nominal Pipe Size)** – controlled by the **line weight (thickness)**.
2. **Service / Fluid Identity** – controlled by the **line type** (solid, dashed, dotted, double) and an **alpha-numeric service code label** placed above the line.
3. **Flow Direction & Material of Construction (MOC)** – controlled by an **arrowhead at the line termination** and an **MOC tag** below the line.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three things to read from a single process line:**
> - HOW BIG is the pipe? → *Line thickness*
> - WHAT is inside it? → *Line style + service code*
> - WHERE is it going? → *Arrowhead direction*

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Decoding Logic of Process Lines

### Step 1: Read the Line Thickness
The line thickness on a P&ID is not artistic choice – it is a **functional code** dictated by the size of the pipe it represents. The ISA convention is:

| Pipe Diameter (NPS) | Line Weight Drawn | Visual Character |
|---|---|---|
| NPS ≥ 10 inches (Process mains) | **Heavy / Thick line** (~1.0 mm) | Bold, dominant on drawing |
| NPS 2 to 8 inches (Branch lines) | **Medium line** (~0.7 mm) | Standard readable stroke |
| NPS < 2 inches (Small bore, instrument taps) | **Thin line** (~0.4 mm) | Hairline, faint |

In petroleum refineries, the main fractionator overhead vapor line, the main crude charge line, and the residue draw line are almost always drawn in **heavy lines** because they are typically NPS 16, NPS 20 or even NPS 24.

### Step 2: Read the Line Type (Service Identification)

The single most important decoding step. Each line style carries a definitive service meaning:

| Line Style | Service Convention | Typical Petroleum Industry Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Continuous thick solid line** | **Main Process Line** | Crude oil charge, naphtha rundown, LPG product |
| **Continuous thin solid line** | **Process branch / utility process line** | Side draws, reboiler return, pump discharge |
| **Dashed thick line** | **Future process line** | Reserved for future capacity expansion |
| **Dashed thin line** | **Pneumatic / Instrument signal line** | 4–20 mA impulse, capillary to DP transmitter |
| **Dotted line (····)** | **Electrical signal / DCS soft signal** | Control signal between DCS and field transmitter |
| **Double line with center cross-hatch** | **Jacketed pipe** or **traced pipe** (steam/electrical tracing) | Sulfur line, heavy vacuum residue, bitumen |
| **Double parallel line with arrow ends** | **Sloped pipe** (indicating drainage slope) | Sour water, amine regeneration overhead |
| **Line with crossed circles** | **Flexible hose / metallic hose** | Loading arms, marine vessel connections |

### Step 3: Read the Line Annotation Block

Every process line on a P&ID carries a **standardized annotation strip** placed *above* the line (service identity) and *below* the line (pipe specification). A complete annotation reads:

$$
\text{Line Tag} \;=\; \underbrace{\text{SERVICE CODE}}_{\text{above the line}} \;-\; \underbrace{\text{NPS} \;-\; \text{SCHEDULE} \;-\; \text{MOC}}_{\text{below the line}}
$$

**Example – a real petroleum line annotation:**

$$
\text{20-N4-SS} \;\;\; \text{where} \;\;\; 20 = \text{NPS 20 inch};\;\; N4 = \text{Schedule 40};\;\; SS = \text{Stainless Steel}
$$

Another real example from a hydrocracker unit:

$$
\underbrace{\text{N3}}_{\text{Naphtha service}} \;\;\; / \;\;\; \underbrace{6 \text{-} SCH 40 \text{-} CS}_{\text{6 inch, Sch 40, Carbon Steel}}
$$

### Step 4: Read the Direction Arrow
An **arrowhead** is drawn at the destination end of the line. In petroleum units, multiple arrows (▶▶) on the same line indicate a **header** – multiple parallel destinations (e.g., multiple tank feeds from a product header).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:**
> A common KTU question type is: *"On a P&ID, a line is drawn as a thick dashed line with the label 'FW' above and '3"-SCH 40-CS' below. What does this convey?"*
> Answer: It is a **future Fire Water line**, **NPS 3 inch**, **Schedule 40**, **Carbon Steel**, kept reserved for future installation.

## 2.3 Standard Service Code Abbreviations (ISA / Industry Convention)

Petroleum P&IDs use a strict alpha-code for service identification. The most frequently tested ones in KTU examinations:

| Code | Service | Code | Service |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CR** | Crude Oil | **NG** | Natural Gas |
| **N** | Naphtha | **LPG** | Liquefied Petroleum Gas |
| **K** | Kerosene / ATF | **GO** | Gas Oil |
| **DS** | Diesel | **FO** | Fuel Oil |
| **VR** | Vacuum Residue | **AR** | Atmospheric Residue |
| **H2** | Hydrogen | **S** | Steam |
| **CWS** | Cooling Water Supply | **CWR** | Cooling Water Return |
| **FW** | Fire Water | **IA** | Instrument Air |
| **SA** | Service Air | **N2** | Nitrogen |
| **O2** | Oxygen | **HW** | Hot Water |
| **BD** | Blowdown | **DR** | Drain |
| **V** | Vent | **FL** | Flare |
| **LO** | Lube Oil | **SO** | Seal Oil |
| **AM** | Amine | **SWS** | Sour Water Stripper Off-gas |
| **CA** | Caustic | **AC** | Acid |

## 2.4 Process Line Symbols for Fittings & Junctions

A process line on a P&ID is rarely straight – it terminates in or connects to fittings. The standard fitting symbols drawn *on* the line include:

- **Bull plug (●)** – End cap of a line (dead end).
- **Spectacle blind / Figure-8 blind (━◈━)** – Positive isolation between two process sections; critical during maintenance.
- **Orifice flange (━|═|═|━)** with **corner taps** – Primary flow measurement element.
- **Steam trap (⊥ symbol in a circle)** – Removes condensate.
- **Strainer (Y or Basket symbol)** – Y-type or basket-type.
- **Reducer (━◀━━▶━)** – Concentric or eccentric taper.
- **Flanged connection (━‖‖━)** – Two parallel transverse bars across the line.
- **Welded connection (━●━)** – Filled circle on the line.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula & Convention Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Process line symbology is descriptive, not analytical. Hence the "Formula Sheet" below is a **Convention Sheet** instead of equations. This is the high-yield recall table for KTU exams.

| Symbol / Convention | KTU-Exam Meaning | Industrial Use in Petroleum |
|---|---|---|
| Thick solid line | Process main ≥ NPS 10 | Crude charge, product rundown |
| Medium solid line | Process branch 2–8 inch | Pump suction, reboiler return |
| Thin solid line | Small bore / instrument line | Impulse line, sample line |
| Dashed thick line | Future process line | Reserved capacity |
| Dashed thin line | Pneumatic / capillary signal | 3–15 psi air signal |
| Dotted line | Electrical / DCS signal | 4–20 mA, Foundation Fieldbus |
| Double line with hatching | Jacketed / traced pipe | Sulfur, bitumen, vacuum residue |
| Triangle (▶) on line | Flow direction arrow | Mandatory on every line |
| Multiple triangles | Header / manifold | Multiple destinations |
| Cross-circle on line | Flexible hose | Loading arm, swing line |
| Service code above line | Fluid identity | N, CR, H2, S, FW |
| Size-MOC code below line | Pipe spec | 16-SCH 40-CS |
| Bull plug ● | Pipe dead end | Pressure relief discharge dead end |
| Spectacle blind | Positive isolation | Column entry for maintenance |
| Orifice symbol | Flow measurement | Orifice plate with DP transmitter |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In an actual EPC project (say, the **BPCL Kochi Refinery expansion** or the **Numaligarh Refinery Expansion Project**), the P&ID is the **single source of truth** used by:

- **Process Engineers** for HAZOP and operability studies.
- **Piping Engineers** for pipe routing and stress analysis.
- **Instrument Engineers** for loop diagrams and I/O list generation.
- **Safety Engineers** for PSV sizing and flare network design.
- **Operations & Maintenance** teams for plant startup, isolation, and turnaround planning.

Without standardized process line symbols, none of these disciplines could communicate.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivation of Process Line Symbol Classification

> [!NOTE]
> Since process line symbology is a *classification system* rather than a numerical derivation, the "derivation" here is the **systematic, step-by-step logical deduction** of how a designer arrives at the correct symbol for any given pipe. This is exactly the analytical approach KTU examiners test.

## 3.1 Master Algorithm – Deriving the Correct Symbol for a Petroleum Process Line

We systematically derive the symbol using the following **six-step algorithm**. The student must internalize this as the KTU exam answer framework.

### Step 1: Identify the Fluid Service
The very first decision is the **fluid that will flow through the pipe**. This decides the **service code** that will be written above the line.
