For example, if the designer is documenting the line carrying overhead vapor from the **CDU (Crude Distillation Unit) atmospheric column** to the receiver, the service is **Overhead Naphtha Vapor** and the service code is **OV** (or **N-V**).

### Step 2: Determine the Pipe Size (NPS)
The pipe size is determined by the **hydraulic and process calculation** (mass balance + pressure drop). For a typical atmospheric column overhead vapor line carrying ~250,000 kg/hr of naphtha vapor, the calculated NPS could be **20 inch**.

### Step 3: Determine Material of Construction (MOC)
For hydrocarbon services, the default is **Carbon Steel (CS)**. For sour service (H₂S > 50 ppm) or chloride service, it becomes **Stainless Steel (SS)** or **Alloy 825/625**. For sulfuric acid alkylation units, it is **Carbon Steel with internal lining**.

### Step 4: Select Line Weight (Thickness on Drawing)
Using the rule:

$$
\text{Line Weight} = 
\begin{cases}
\text{Heavy (Thick)} & \text{if NPS} \geq 10'' \\
\text{Medium} & \text{if } 2'' \leq \text{NPS} < 10'' \\
\text{Thin (Hairline)} & \text{if NPS} < 2''
\end{cases}
$$

For the 20-inch naphtha vapor line, the line is drawn as **heavy/thick**.

### Step 5: Select Line Type (Style)
The line is a continuous, currently active, hydrocarbon service. Hence:

$$
\text{Line Style} = \text{Continuous Solid}
$$

If the same naphtha line were planned for a **future debottlenecking**, it would be drawn as a **dashed thick line** instead.

### Step 6: Add Annotation Strip and Flow Arrow
The complete symbol set on the P&ID becomes:

$$
\underbrace{\text{OV}}_{\text{above}} \;\;\; \longleftrightarrow \;\;\; \underbrace{20''\text{-}SCH 40\text{-}CS}_{\text{below}}
$$

with a **single arrowhead** pointing toward the overhead receiver, drawn in **heavy continuous solid line**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Consolidated KTU Answer:**
> A 20-inch overhead naphtha vapor line from the CDU column, in carbon steel, scheduled for current operation, is represented as a **heavy continuous solid line**, labelled **"OV" above** and **"20-SCH 40-CS" below**, with a **flow-direction arrow** pointing toward the receiver drum.

## 3.2 Derivative Case Derivations – KTU-Frequent Variations

### Case A: Instrument Air Line to a Control Valve

$$
\text{Service} = \text{IA} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Code above} = \text{IA}
$$

$$
\text{Pipe size} = 1/2'' \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Line Weight} = \text{Thin}
$$

$$
\text{Purpose} = \text{Supply to pneumatic actuator} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Line Type} = \text{Continuous Solid}
$$

Therefore: **thin continuous solid line, labelled "IA" above, "1/2-SCH 80-CS" below**.

### Case B: 4–20 mA Signal from a Pressure Transmitter to DCS

$$
\text{Signal type} = \text{Electrical} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Line Type} = \text{Dotted}
$$

$$
\text{Purpose} = \text{DCS soft signal} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{No physical pipe MOC code}
$$

Therefore: **dotted line with arrowhead**, with the instrument bubble "PT" at one end and DCS terminal at the other.

### Case C: Pneumatic 3–15 psi Signal from Controller to Control Valve

$$
\text{Signal type} = \text{Pneumatic} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Line Type} = \text{Dashed Thin}
$$

### Case D: Heavy Vacuum Residue Draw from Column Bottom (Hot Service)

$$
\text{Temperature} \approx 380^\circ C \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Needs steam tracing}
$$

$$
\text{Therefore} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Double-line with cross-hatching (jacketed/traced symbol)}
$$

### Case E: Future LPG Spare Line to Loading Gantry

$$
\text{Status} = \text{Future} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Dashed Thick Line}
$$

$$
\text{Annotation: 'LPG'} \;\;\text{above}, \;\;\;'6-SCH 40-CS'} \;\;\text{below}
$$

## 3.3 Boolean Decision Matrix – Master Classification Logic

The student can use this logical truth-table to derive the correct symbol for any combination:

| Is Future? | Service Type | NPS ≥ 10? | Resulting Line Symbol |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| No | Hydrocarbon / Process | Yes | **Heavy Solid Line** |
| No | Hydrocarbon / Process | No | **Medium Solid Line** |
| No | Utility (Water, Steam, Air) | Yes | **Heavy Solid Line** |
| No | Utility | No | **Medium Solid Line** |
| **Yes** | Any | Any | **Dashed Line** (weight follows size) |
| No | Instrument signal (pneumatic) | N/A | **Dashed Thin Line** |
| No | Instrument signal (electrical) | N/A | **Dotted Line** |
| No | Hot service needing tracing | Any | **Double-line with cross-hatch** |
| No | Sloped pipe | Any | **Double parallel line with arrows** |

This boolean matrix is the **analytical backbone** of any KTU question on process line symbology.

## 3.4 Numerical Convention – Line Numbering (ISA-5.1 Compliant)

Although not strictly a "symbol," every process line on a P&ID has a **unique line number** that ties it to the **Line List** and **Piping Material Specification (PMS)**.

The standard ISA convention is:

$$
\text{Line Number} \;=\; \underbrace{\text{XX}}_{\text{Area No.}} \;\; \underbrace{\text{Y}}_{\text{Service Group Code}} \;\; \underbrace{\text{NNN}}_{\text{Sequential No.}}
$$

- **XX** = Two-digit area number (e.g., 12 = Hydrocracker area, 14 = Catalytic Reformer).
- **Y** = Service group (e.g., P = Process, U = Utility, S = Steam, I = Instrument).
- **NNN** = Three-digit sequential line number.

**Example derivation:**
Line **12-P-145** belongs to **Area 12 (Hydrocracker)**, is a **Process line**, and is the **145th process line** documented in that area.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> P&ID symbology is fundamentally a visual language. Below are the **block-level functional architecture maps** showing how the symbology hierarchy is organized. These follow ISA-5.1 standards and are the *exact* patterns KTU examiners expect students to recall.

## 4.1 Master Hierarchy of P&I Symbol Families

```mermaid
graph TD
    ROOTNODE[P AND I SYMBOL SYSTEM] --> EQ[LAYER 1 EQUIPMENT SYMBOLS]
    ROOTNODE --> PL[LAYER 2 PROCESS LINE SYMBOLS]
    ROOTNODE --> VS[LAYER 3 VALVE SYMBOLS]
    ROOTNODE --> IS[LAYER 4 INSTRUMENTATION SYMBOLS]
    ROOTNODE --> FIT[LAYER 5 FITTINGS AND SPECIALTIES]

    PL --> PLMAIN[MAIN PROCESS LINE]
    PL --> PLBR[BRANCH AND UTILITY PROCESS LINE]
    PL --> PLINST[INSTRUMENT AND SIGNAL LINE]
    PL --> PLFUT[FUTURE PROCESS LINE]
    PL --> PLJACK[JACKETED OR TRACED PIPE]

    PLMAIN --> PLM1[HEAVY SOLID LINE]
    PLMAIN --> PLM2[SERVICE CODE ABOVE]
    PLMAIN --> PLM3[SIZE MOC BELOW]
    PLMAIN --> PLM4[ARROWHEAD DIRECTION]

    PLBR --> PLB1[MEDIUM SOLID LINE]
    PLBR --> PLB2[TYPICALLY NPS 2 TO 8]

    PLINST --> PLI1[DASHED THIN PNEUMATIC SIGNAL]
    PLINST --> PLI2[DOTTED ELECTRICAL SIGNAL]
    PLINST --> PLI3[CAPILLARY FOR DP TX]

    PLFUT --> PLF1[DASHED THICK LINE]
    PLFUT --> PLF2[USED FOR FUTURE EXPANSION]

    PLJACK --> PLJ1[DOUBLE LINE WITH CROSS HATCH]
    PLJACK --> PLJ2[USED FOR BITUMEN AND SULFUR LINES]
```

## 4.2 Line Style Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    STARTNODE[START IDENTIFY THE LINE] --> Q1{IS THE LINE FUTURE RESERVED}

    Q1 -->|YES| FUTOUT[DASHED LINE HEAVY OR MEDIUM]
    Q1 -->|NO| Q2{WHAT IS THE FLUID SERVICE}

    Q2 -->|PROCESS HYDROCARBON| Q3{WHAT IS THE NPS}
    Q3 -->|NPS AT LEAST 10| SOLIDHVY[HEAVY SOLID CONTINUOUS]
    Q3 -->|NPS 2 TO 8| SOLIDMED[MEDIUM SOLID CONTINUOUS]
    Q3 -->|NPS BELOW 2| SOLIDTHN[THIN SOLID CONTINUOUS]

    Q2 -->|UTILITY STEAM WATER AIR| SOLIDU[MEDIUM OR HEAVY SOLID]
    Q2 -->|INSTRUMENT SIGNAL| Q4{SIGNAL TYPE}

    Q4 -->|PNEUMATIC 3 TO 15 PSI| PNEOUT[DASHED THIN LINE]
    Q4 -->|ELECTRICAL 4 TO 20 MA OR FIELDBUS| ELECOUT[DOTTED LINE]
    Q4 -->|CAPILLARY TO DP TX| CAPOUT[DASHED THIN DOUBLE LINE]

    Q2 -->|HOT OR SOLIDIFYING FLUID| JACKOUT[DOUBLE LINE WITH HATCH JACKETED OR TRACED]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture – Annotation of a Single Process Line

```mermaid
graph TD
    LINENODE[REPRESENTATION OF A PROCESS LINE ON PID] --> ANNOV[ANNOTATION ABOVE THE LINE]
    LINENODE --> ANNOB[ANNOTATION BELOW THE LINE]
    LINENODE --> LINEDRAW[THE LINE ITSELF]
    LINENODE --> ARROWND[DIRECTION ARROW AT TERMINATION]

    ANNOV --> ANNOV1[SERVICE CODE E G OV FOR OVERHEAD VAPOR]
    ANNOV --> ANNOV2[FLUID IDENTITY]

    ANNOB --> ANNOB1[NPS NOMINAL PIPE SIZE]
    ANNOB --> ANNOB2[SCHEDULE NUMBER]
    ANNOB --> ANNOB3[MOC MATERIAL OF CONSTRUCTION]

    LINEDRAW --> LD1[LINE THICKNESS ENCODES PIPE SIZE]
    LINEDRAW --> LD2[LINE STYLE ENCODES SERVICE CLASS]
    LINEDRAW --> LD3[CONTINUOUS DASHED DOTTED DOUBLE]

    ARROWND --> AR1[SINGLE ARROW FOR ONE DESTINATION]
    ARROWND --> AR2[MULTIPLE ARROWS FOR HEADER]
```

## 4.4 Service Code Logic Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    SERVICEROOT[PETROLEUM PROCESS FLUID] --> SERVHYD[HYDROCARBON STREAMS]
    SERVICEROOT --> SERVUTIL[UTILITY STREAMS]
    SERVICEROOT --> SERVELEC[ELECTRICAL AND SIGNAL STREAMS]

    SERVHYD --> H1[CR CRUDE]
    SERVHYD --> H2[N NAPHTHA]
    SERVHYD --> H3[K KEROSENE]
    SERVHYD --> H4[DS DIESEL]
    SERVHYD --> H5[FO FUEL OIL]
    SERVHYD --> H6[LPG LIQUEFIED PETROLEUM GAS]
    SERVHYD --> H7[H2 HYDROGEN]

    SERVUTIL --> U1[S STEAM]
    SERVUTIL --> U2[CWS COOLING WATER SUPPLY]
    SERVUTIL --> U3[CWR COOLING WATER RETURN]
    SERVUTIL --> U4[FW FIRE WATER]
    SERVUTIL --> U5[IA INSTRUMENT AIR]
    SERVUTIL --> U6[N2 NITROGEN]

    SERVELEC --> E1[PNS PNEUMATIC SIGNAL DASHED THIN]
    SERVELEC --> E2[ELS ELECTRICAL SIGNAL DOTTED]
    SERVELEC --> E3[CAP CAPILLARY DOUBLE DASH]
```

## 4.5 Legend Block – The Universal Process Line Symbol Key (Typical Petroleum Refinery)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[HEAVY SOLID] --> AA[MAIN PROCESS NPS 10 AND ABOVE]
    B[MEDIUM SOLID] --> BB[BRANCH AND UTILITY NPS 2 TO 8]
    C[THIN SOLID] --> CC[SMALL BORE INSTRUMENT TAP]
    D[DASHED THICK] --> DD[FUTURE PROCESS]
    E[DASHED THIN] --> EE[PNEUMATIC 3 TO 15 PSI SIGNAL]
    F[DOTTED] --> FF[ELECTRICAL 4 TO 20 MA OR FIELDBUS]
    G[DOUBLE WITH HATCH] --> GG[JACKETED OR TRACED]
    H[DOUBLE PARALLEL] --> HH[SLOPED PIPE FOR DRAINAGE]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Every refinery's P&ID must contain a **legend block** at the bottom or top-right corner containing the above key. Without this legend, the P&ID is non-compliant with ISA-5.1. KTU examiners specifically look for the student's familiarity with the legend block as part of professional documentation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between a Process Flow Diagram (PFD) and a Piping and Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID). Why is standardization of P&I symbols essential in a petroleum refinery?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **PFD** is a high-level process schematic that shows only the major equipment (columns, reactors, exchangers, pumps) and the major process flow streams between them. It does *not* contain pipe sizes, valve details, instrumentation, or utility connections.

A **P&ID** is a much more detailed engineering drawing that contains **every pipe** (with NPS, schedule, MOC), **every valve** (manual, control, safety), **every instrument** (sensors, controllers, DCS), and **every fitting** (flanges, blinds, traps) needed for actual plant construction.

**Standardization is essential** because petroleum refineries handle highly hazardous hydrocarbons (LPG, naphtha, H₂, sour crude). A misinterpretation of a single symbol could cause:
- Cross-connection of incompatible services (hydrocarbon in a utility line).
- Wrong material selection during fabrication.
- Catastrophic loss of containment.

International standards like **ISA-5.1** and **ISO 14617** ensure that engineers across India, the Middle East, Europe, and the USA can read each other's drawings unambiguously, ensuring **safety, operability, and EPC contract compliance**. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**On a refinery P&ID, a process line is drawn as a thick continuous solid line, labelled "N-3" above and "16-SCH 40-CS" below, with an arrowhead pointing from the CDU column overhead to the reflux drum. Decode every piece of information conveyed by this symbol.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Position | Symbol | Decoded Information |
|---|---|---|
| Line type | Thick continuous solid | Main process line, currently active |
| Above line | "N-3" | Service: **Naphtha**, Line Number **3** in the area |
| Below line | "16" | Nominal Pipe Size = **NPS 16 inch** |
| Below line | "SCH 40" | Pipe schedule = **Schedule 40** |
| Below line | "CS" | Material of Construction = **Carbon Steel** |
| Arrowhead | ▶ | Flow from **CDU column overhead** → **Reflux Drum** |

Hence the line carries **16-inch carbon steel Schedule 40 naphtha**, currently in service, flowing from the crude distillation unit overhead to the reflux receiver. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) – 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the **different process line symbols** used on a P&ID with reference to **ISA-5.1** standards. Discuss the significance of **line weight, line type, service code, and direction arrow** with at least **five petroleum-industry examples**.

**(b) [7 marks]** With the help of a **decision flowchart**, derive the **correct process line symbol** for the following petroleum services:
1. A 24-inch crude oil charge line from the desalter to the fired heater.
2. A 3-inch instrument air line to a pneumatic control valve.
3. A 4–20 mA electrical signal from a flow transmitter to the DCS.
4. A planned future 6-inch LPG loading line (not yet installed).
5. A jacketed 8-inch vacuum residue line requiring steam tracing.

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) [7 marks] – Detailed Explanation of Process Line Symbols (ISA-5.1)**

The ISA-5.1 standard classifies process line symbols using **three orthogonal parameters** simultaneously on the same line drawing:

**1. Line Weight (Pipe Size Encoding):**
- **Heavy line** → NPS ≥ 10 inches (mains).
- **Medium line** → NPS 2 to 8 inches (branches).
- **Thin line** → NPS < 2 inches (small bore, instrument taps).

**2. Line Type (Service Encoding):**
- **Continuous solid** → Active process or utility line.
- **Dashed thick** → Future process line.
- **Dashed thin** → Pneumatic signal (3–15 psi).
- **Dotted** → Electrical signal (4–20 mA / Fieldbus).
- **Double line with cross-hatch** → Jacketed / traced pipe.
- **Double parallel line with arrows** → Sloped pipe.

**3. Annotation Block:**
- **Above the line:** Service code (e.g., N = Naphtha, H₂ = Hydrogen, FW = Fire Water).
- **Below the line:** Pipe specification `NPS-SCHEDULE-MOC` (e.g., `16-SCH 40-CS`).

**4. Direction Arrow:** Mandatory at the destination end. Multiple arrows = header.

**Five Petroleum Examples:**
- **Example 1:** The crude charge line from desalter to heater is a 24-inch main → **Heavy continuous solid line**, annotated **CR** above and **24-SCH 40-CS** below.
- **Example 2:** The naphtha rundown from the CDU overhead receiver to the stabilizer is a 6-inch branch → **Medium continuous solid line**, annotated **N** above and **6-SCH 40-CS** below.
- **Example 3:** The 3-15 psi pneumatic signal from a controller to the control valve is a **Dashed thin line**.
- **Example 4:** The 4-20 mA signal from a pressure transmitter on the reactor is a **Dotted line**.
- **Example 5:** The hydrogen line to the hydrocracker reactor is drawn as a **Heavy continuous solid line** with **H₂** above and **8-SCH 80-CS** below (high-pressure hydrogen requires heavier schedule).

**(7 marks awarded for complete coverage and correct petroleum examples.)**

---

**(b) [7 marks] – Flowchart Derivation:**

The systematic decision logic for each service is:

**1. 24-inch crude charge line (desalter → heater):**
- Service: Hydrocarbon (Process) → Continuous Solid
- Size: 24 inch (NPS ≥ 10) → Heavy Line
- Status: Current (not future) → Solid (not dashed)
- **Symbol: Heavy continuous solid line**, label `CR` above, `24-SCH 40-CS` below, arrow from desalter → heater.

**2. 3-inch instrument air line to pneumatic control valve:**
- Service: Utility (Instrument Air) → Solid line
- Size: 3 inch → Medium line
- **Symbol: Medium continuous solid line**, label `IA` above, `3-SCH 80-CS` below, arrow toward the valve actuator.

**3. 4–20 mA electrical signal (flow transmitter → DCS):**
- Signal type: Electrical / DCS soft signal → **Dotted line**
- **Symbol: Dotted line** from the FT bubble to the DCS terminal, no physical MOC code (only signal tag).

**4. Future 6-inch LPG loading line:**
- Service: Hydrocarbon → but future → **Dashed**
- Size: 6 inch → Medium → Dashed Medium Line
- **Symbol: Dashed medium line**, label `LPG` above, `6-SCH 40-CS` below, drawn alongside the active loading header.

**5. Jacketed 8-inch vacuum residue line with steam tracing:**
- Service: Hot, solidifying residue → requires tracing → **Double line with cross-hatch (jacketed/traced)**
- Size: 8 inch → represented as **Medium double line with hatch**.
- **Symbol: Double parallel lines with diagonal cross-hatch between them**, label `VR` above, `8-SCH 40-CS` below, with a separate tracing line marked `S` (Steam Tracing).

**(7 marks awarded for correct symbol choice for each service with annotation logic.)**

---

### Question B (Choice 2) – 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss in detail the **standards governing P&ID symbology** (ISA, ISO, ANSI, DIN) and explain why harmonization across international EPC contractors is critical for the Indian petroleum sector. Mention at least **three specific consequences** of using non-standard symbols.

**(b) [7 marks]** Draw (using a clearly labelled block schematic) the **complete annotation and symbol set** for the following process line on a typical hydrocracker unit P&ID:

> *"A 10-inch, Schedule 40, Carbon Steel line carrying high-pressure hydrogen-rich recycle gas from the make-up compressor discharge to the hydrocracker reactor inlet. The hydrogen service is currently active. The flow direction is from the compressor to the reactor."*

Identify **every element** of the symbol: line weight, line type, service code annotation, pipe specification annotation, flow arrow, and the appropriate position on the P&ID.

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) [7 marks] – Standards Governing P&ID Symbology**

**1. ISA-5.1 (Primary International Standard):**
The Instrumentation Society of Automation (now International Society of Automation) publishes ISA-5.1 – *Instrumentation Symbols and Identification*. It is the **de facto global standard** for P&ID symbology, adopted by virtually every refinery EPC contractor worldwide. It defines line symbols, instrument bubbles (circles), valve symbols, and identification letters (e.g., T = Temperature, P = Pressure, F = Flow, L = Level).

**2. ISO 14617 (International Harmonization):**
ISO 14617 is the international equivalent, harmonizing P&ID symbols across European, Asian, and American projects. EPC contracts in India (e.g., for **IOCL Panipat Refinery expansion** or **HMEL Bathinda**) typically require compliance with **both ISA-5.1 and ISO 14617** to enable multi-vendor engineering.

**3. ANSI Standards:**
The American National Standards Institute provides supporting standards – ANSI/ISA-S5.1 (same as ISA-5.1), ANSI/ASME B31.3 (Process Piping), ANSI/ISA-S5.4 (Instrument Loop Diagrams).

**4. DIN 19227 (German Standard):**
Older European (especially German and Italian) EPC companies still reference DIN for valve and instrumentation symbols. Many Indian refineries built with **Linde, Lurgi, or Uhde** technology retain DIN-based P&IDs.

**5. EEMUA Publication 191:**
Used specifically for **alarm symbols** on P&IDs (Level 1, Level 2, Level 3 alarms), increasingly important in modern HAZOP/LOPA studies.

**Why Harmonization is Critical for the Indian Petroleum Sector:**

- Indian refineries engage **multiple EPC contractors** (Indian: EIL, L&T; International: Bechtel, Technip, Toyo Engineering). Without a common symbol standard, the same diagram could be misread.
- Operations and maintenance staff trained on one standard (say ISA) must safely operate a plant designed using a different standard (say DIN).
- Cross-border projects (e.g., **India-Middle East pipeline**, **India-Russia CPC blend**) demand a universally readable drawing language.

**Three Consequences of Non-Standard Symbols:**

1. **Cross-connection hazard:** A contractor unfamiliar with a non-standard symbol may connect a hydrocarbon line to a utility line, causing catastrophic failure.
2. **Wrong material procurement:** Misreading a symbol could lead to ordering the wrong pipe schedule or material (e.g., CS instead of SS for sour service), resulting in SCC failures.
3. **Maintenance-induced accidents:** During turnaround maintenance, a wrong isolation due to misreading could lead to personnel exposure to live hydrocarbons.

**(7 marks awarded.)**

---

**(b) [7 marks] – Hydrocracker Recycle Hydrogen Line Schematic**

The complete symbol set is:

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1[REPRESENTATION OF THE HYDROGEN LINE ON PID]
    L1 --> L2[ABOVE THE LINE]
    L1 --> L3[THE LINE STROKE]
    L1 --> L4[THE FLOW ARROW]
    L1 --> L5[BELOW THE LINE]

    L2 --> L21[SERVICE CODE H2 FOR HYDROGEN]
    L2 --> L22[LINE NUMBER 12 R 008 MEANING AREA 12 REFINING GROUP SERIAL 8]

    L3 --> L31[HEAVY CONTINUOUS SOLID LINE BECAUSE NPS IS 10 INCH WHICH IS AT OR ABOVE THRESHOLD]
    L3 --> L32[NOT DASHED BECAUSE THE LINE IS CURRENTLY ACTIVE NOT FUTURE]

    L4 --> L41[ARROWHEAD POINTING FROM COMPRESSOR DISCHARGE TO REACTOR INLET]

    L5 --> L51[NPS 10 INCH]
    L5 --> L52[SCHEDULE 40]
    L5 --> L53[MATERIAL CARBON STEEL]
    L5 --> L54[HIGH PRESSURE H2 SERVICE NOTE OPERATOR TO CHECK MATERIAL SUITABILITY]
```

**Step-by-Step Decoding of the Final Symbol:**

| Element | Choice Made | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Line Weight** | Heavy (thick) | NPS = 10 inch, which is at the threshold of ≥ 10 inch → heavy line as per ISA convention |
| **Line Type** | Continuous Solid | Line is currently active, not future, not a signal |
| **Above-line Annotation** | `H2` (hydrogen) and Line Number | Service code identifies the fluid |
| **Below-line Annotation** | `10-SCH 40-CS` | 10 inch, Schedule 40, Carbon Steel |
| **Flow Arrow** | Single arrowhead at reactor end | Indicates flow from compressor discharge → reactor inlet |
| **Special Note** | High-pressure H₂ → Schedule 40 must be hydrotested to high pressure (per ASME B31.3) | Designer must add a note about pressure rating |

**Final Reconstructed Symbol (textual representation):**

$$
\underbrace{H2 \text{ (above)}}_{\text{service}} \quad \longleftrightarrow \quad \underbrace{10\text{-}SCH 40\text{-}CS \text{ (below)}}_{\text{pipe spec}}
$$

Drawn as a **heavy continuous solid line** with a **single arrowhead** pointing from the make-up compressor discharge to the hydrocracker reactor inlet, on a typical hydrocracker unit P&ID in **Area 12**.

**(7 marks awarded for correct identification of all six symbol elements.)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Conflating "dashed thick" with "dashed thin":** Many students write any dashed line regardless of size. Always apply the size rule: **NPS ≥ 10 → thick**, even if dashed.
>
> 2. **Confusing pneumatic and electrical signal lines:** Pneumatic is **dashed thin**, electrical is **dotted**. This is the most-asked distinction in KTU Module 2 questions.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the flow arrow:** A line without an arrow is **non-compliant** with ISA-5.1. Examiners deduct 1 mark if the arrow is missing.
>
> 4. **Wrong position of annotation:** Service code is **above**, pipe spec is **below**. Reversing these loses marks.
>
> 5. **Ignoring line numbering:** A complete P&ID answer must mention the line number `XX-Y-NNN` (Area-Service-Serial). KTU frequently awards 1–2 marks specifically for correct line numbering.
>
> 6. **Not specifying material of construction:** Writing only "16 inch" without "SCH 40-CS" is incomplete.
>
> 7. **Treating PFD and P&ID as interchangeable:** They are NOT the same. Always state that P&ID contains valves, instruments, fittings, while PFD does not.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density, exam-day recall points for this topic:**

- [ ] A **P&ID** is the *detailed* engineering document; a **PFD** is the *high-level* process schematic.
- [ ] Process line symbols are governed by **ISA-5.1** (primary), with harmonization to **ISO 14617**.
- [ ] **Three parameters are encoded simultaneously** in a process line symbol: pipe size (weight), service (type), and direction (arrow).
- [ ] **Line weight rule:** Heavy = NPS ≥ 10", Medium = 2–8", Thin = < 2".
- [ ] **Continuous solid line** = active process/utility.
- [ ] **Dashed thick line** = future process.
- [ ] **Dashed thin line** = pneumatic 3–15 psi signal.
- [ ] **Dotted line** = electrical 4–20 mA or Fieldbus signal.
- [ ] **Double line with cross-hatch** = jacketed or steam-traced pipe (used for bitumen, sulfur, vacuum residue).
- [ ] **Double parallel line with arrows** = sloped pipe (used for drainage, sour water).
- [ ] **Annotation above line** = service code (e.g., CR, N, H2, LPG, FW, IA, S, CWS).
- [ ] **Annotation below line** = `NPS-SCHEDULE-MOC` (e.g., `16-SCH 40-CS`).
- [ ] **Line numbering convention:** `XX-Y-NNN` = Area-Service-Serial.
- [ ] **Bull plug (●)** = dead-end pipe cap.
- [ ] **Spectacle blind** = positive isolation for maintenance.
- [ ] **Orifice symbol** = flow measurement primary element.
- [ ] **Service codes to remember:** CR, N, K, DS, FO, VR, H2, LPG, S, CWS, FW, IA, N2, AM, BD, DR, FL.
- [ ] **Mandatory legend block** on every P&ID for ISA-5.1 compliance.
- [ ] A line **without a flow arrow** is non-compliant.
- [ ] In hydrocarbon services, default MOC is **Carbon Steel (CS)**; upgrade to **SS** for sour or chloride service.
- [ ] High-pressure hydrogen lines (e.g., hydrocracker) require heavier schedules (e.g., SCH 80 or SCH 160).
- [ ] Cross-country pipelines and loading arms use **flexible hose symbols** (cross-circle on line).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Equipment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET861 - INSTRUMENTATION IN PETROCHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Process Equipment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Process Equipment** in a petrochemical industry refers to the engineered, pressure-rated, and code-compliant mechanical hardware and vessels designed to perform the physical, thermal, chemical, and separation operations required to convert crude hydrocarbon feedstocks into refined products, intermediates, and petrochemical end-products. In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme instrumentation, process equipment constitutes the *controlled plant* (the "process") upon which measurement, control, and safeguarding instrumentation is mounted to ensure safe, stable, and optimal operation.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, process equipment typically encompasses the following hierarchical classes:
- **Vessels and Reactors** (Distillation columns, Fixed-Bed Reactors, CSTRs, Cracker units)
- **Heat Transfer Equipment** (Shell-and-Tube exchangers, Fired Heaters, Air-Cooled exchangers)
- **Fluid Handling Equipment** (Pumps, Compressors, Expanders, Ejectors)
- **Solids Handling Equipment** (Filters, Dryers, Conveyors, Crushers)
- **Storage and Containment** (Atmospheric & Pressure Storage Tanks, Spheres, Bullet tanks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Block:** "Process Equipment" is the umbrella term for all unit-operation hardware within the *Battery Limits (BL)* of a petrochemical complex. From an instrumentation viewpoint, every piece of process equipment is a *multi-input, multi-output (MIMO) controlled system* characterized by states such as temperature ($T$), pressure ($P$), level ($L$), flow ($F$), and composition ($x$).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **giant, highly orchestrated kitchen** where a master chef (the **Plant Operator**) is preparing a complex multi-course meal from raw ingredients (crude oil). The chef requires:
- **Pots and pans** that hold liquids at different temperatures → These are the **Vessels and Reactors**
- **Stoves and ovens** that supply heat → These are the **Fired Heaters and Heat Exchangers**
- **Faucets and water pumps** that move ingredients → These are the **Pumps and Compressors**
- **Strainers and colanders** that separate solids and liquids → These are the **Filters and Separators**
- **Thermometers, pressure gauges, and weighing scales** that tell the chef what is happening → These are the **Instruments (Sensors/Transmitters)**

Just as a chef cannot cook without the right utensils and instruments, a petrochemical plant cannot operate without **process equipment** (the utensils) and **instrumentation** (the senses). The instrumentation system is the *nervous system* of the plant, while the process equipment is the *musculoskeletal system* — they are inseparable.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Key Physical Quantities

The following metrics are universally used to characterize process equipment performance:

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | Significance |
|----------|--------|---------|--------------|
| Operating Pressure | $P$ | $\text{Pa}$ (or bar) | Determines vessel thickness & class |
| Operating Temperature | $T$ | $\text{K}$ (or °C) | Affects material selection & reactions |
| Volumetric Flow Rate | $Q$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | Throughput capacity |
| Mass Flow Rate | $\dot{m}$ | $\text{kg}/\text{s}$ | Used in mass/energy balance |
| Liquid Level | $L$ | $\text{m}$ (or %) | Inventory in a vessel |
| Heat Duty | $Q_H$ | $\text{W}$ (or kW) | Heat exchanger capacity |
| Composition | $x_i$ | mol fraction or ppm | Purity of product |
| Viscosity | $\mu$ | $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ | Affects flow & mixing |
| Density | $\rho$ | $\text{kg}/\text{m}^3$ | Affects level & flow measurement |
| **Design Pressure** | $P_{\text{des}}$ | $\text{Pa}$ | Maximum allowable (1.1× max working) |
| **Design Temperature** | $T_{\text{des}}$ | $\text{K}$ | Maximum allowable metal temperature |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why bold?**: **Design Pressure** and **Design Temperature** are *code-mandated* (ASME Sec VIII, API 650) parameters used to size the vessel wall. Operating conditions must NEVER exceed design limits — this is a recurring KTU question on safety instrumentation.

## 1.4 Classification of Process Equipment — Overview

Process equipment is classified based on the **unit operation** it performs:

1. **Reaction Equipment** — Where chemical transformation occurs (e.g., Catalytic Cracker, Hydrocracker, Reforming Reactors)
2. **Separation Equipment** — Where components are separated without chemical change (e.g., Distillation Column, Absorber, Extractor)
3. **Heat Transfer Equipment** — Where energy in transit is added or removed (e.g., Shell-and-Tube, Plate Exchanger, Air-Cooler, Fired Heater)
4. **Fluid Transport Equipment** — Where momentum is added to fluids (e.g., Centrifugal Pump, Reciprocating Compressor)
5. **Storage Equipment** — Where feedstocks and products are held (e.g., Floating Roof Tank, Horton Sphere, Bullet)
6. **Solids Handling** — Where solid-fluid separation or size reduction occurs (e.g., Centrifuge, Filter, Dryer, Cyclone)

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** For Module 2, the emphasis is on the *instrumentation aspect* — i.e., *what variables are measured and controlled on each equipment type*, and *which sensors and final control elements are deployed*. Memorize at least three (3) measurement points and two (2) control loops for each major equipment category.

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Detailed Equipment-by-Equipment Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Distillation Column (Fractionating Tower)

The **distillation column** is the single most important separation device in a refinery. It separates a liquid mixture into fractions of differing volatility based on differences in **boiling points**.

**Internal Hardware:**
- **Trays (Sieve, Valve, Bubble-Cap)** — Provide vapor-liquid contact
- **Packing (Random: Pall rings, Raschig; Structured: Mellapak, Sulzer)** — Used in packed columns
- **Reboiler** — Supplies vapor at the bottom
- **Condenser** — Condenses overhead vapor
- **Reflux Drum** — Holds condensed distillate
- **Pumparound (PA) Trays** — Side draws for heat removal

**Key Instrumentation Points (7 typical control loops):**
1. **TC-101**: Top Temperature (controls Reflux Flow)
2. **FC-102**: Feed Flow (controls Feed Preheater)
3. **LC-103**: Reflux Drum Level (controls Distillate Product)
4. **LC-104**: Bottoms Level (controls Bottoms Draw)
5. **PC-105**: Column Pressure (controls Condenser Duty / Overhead Receiver)
6. **TC-106**: Pumparound Temperature (controls PA Draw)
7. **AC-107**: Top Product Composition / Boiling Point (Analyzers)

**Why these?** → Mass balance, energy balance, and component balance must simultaneously hold (3 degrees of freedom for a binary column).

### 2.1.2 Shell-and-Tube Heat Exchanger (STHE)

The **STHE** is the workhorse of refinery heat recovery. It consists of a *shell* (outer vessel) and a *tube bundle* (inner pipes), with one fluid flowing inside the tubes and the other across them.

**Process Variables & Instrumentation:**
- **Hot Inlet/Outlet Temperature** (TI, TT, TIC)
- **Cold Inlet/Outlet Temperature** (TI, TT, TIC)
- **Shell-side Pressure** (PI, PT)
- **Tube-side Pressure** (PI, PT)
- **Bypass Valve** (3-way or split-range) for capacity control

**Control Strategies:**
- **Single-loop bypass control** (simple)
- **Cascade control**: Temperature → Flow of bypass (better disturbance rejection)
- **Feedforward control**: Hot flow rate → Cold bypass (best for fouling services)

### 2.1.3 Centrifugal Pump

Converts mechanical energy from a driver (electric motor, steam turbine) into kinetic energy of the fluid.

**Instrumentation:**
- **Suction Pressure (PI)** — Avoid cavitation; NPSH monitoring
- **Discharge Pressure (PI, PIC)**
- **Flow (FI, FT, FIC)** — Often in a flow recirculation loop
- **Bearing Temperature (TI)** — Critical for predictive maintenance
- **Vibration Sensor (VI)** — Mechanical health
- **Seal Pot Level** — For mechanical seal integrity

**Pump Performance Equation (Affinity Laws):**
$$\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}, \quad \frac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2, \quad \frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3$$

### 2.1.4 Reciprocating Compressor

Compresses gases using pistons. Used for high-pressure service (e.g., Hydrocracker recycle gas, Ethylene compression).

**Key Instrumentation:**
- **Suction Pressure / Temperature** (PI/TI)
- **Discharge Pressure** (PI/PT/PIC) — Critical anti-surge control
- **Inter-stage Pressure** (PI)
- **Inter-stage Temperature** (TI/TIC) — Anti-overheat
- **Cylinder Jacket Temperature** (TI)
- **Anti-Surge Flow** (FI/FIC) — Most important control loop

### 2.1.5 Fixed-Bed Catalytic Reactor

A vertical vessel packed with catalyst pellets where heterogeneous reactions occur (e.g., Hydrodesulfurization, Reforming, Cracking).

**Instrumentation:**
- **Inlet Temperature** (TI/TT) — Often redundant
- **Multiple Bed Temperature** (TI) — *Hot-spot* detection
- **Outlet Temperature** (TI)
- **Inlet/Outlet Pressure** (ΔP measurement indicates plugging)
- **Quench Flow Control** — Between catalyst beds

### 2.1.6 Storage Tanks

**Atmospheric & Low-Pressure Tanks** (Cone-roof, Floating-roof, Dome-roof) and **Pressure Spheres/Bullets** for LPG.

**Key Instrumentation:**
- **Level (LT, LG, LSH, LSL)** — Servo, Radar, Hydrostatic, Float
- **Temperature (TT)** — Important for heavy crude
- **Pressure (PT)** — For vapor space
- **API 2350 Overfill Protection** — Multi-level switch + alarm
- **Free Water Detection** — Capacitance probe

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the *core equations* that are repeatedly tested in KTU Module 2 questions. **Memorize the meaning, units, and typical numerical range of each.**

| # | Equation | LaTeX Form | Variables / Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|----------|------------|---------------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Bernoulli (Energy Balance)** | $P + \rho g h + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^2 = \text{const}$ | $P$ = pressure, $v$ = velocity, $h$ = height | $\text{Pa}, \text{m/s}, \text{m}$ |
| 2 | **Reynolds Number** | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | $D$ = pipe diameter | dimensionless |
| 3 | **Heat Duty** | $Q_H = \dot{m} \, c_p \, \Delta T$ | $\dot{m}$ = mass flow, $c_p$ = specific heat | $\text{W}$ |
| 4 | **Overall Heat Transfer** | $Q = U A \, \Delta T_{LMTD}$ | $U$ = overall coefficient, $A$ = area | $\text{W}$ |
| 5 | **LMTD** | $\Delta T_{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$ | Counter/cocurrent correction applied | $\text{K}$ |
| 6 | **Pump Power** | $P_{\text{pump}} = \rho g Q H / \eta$ | $H$ = head, $\eta$ = efficiency | $\text{W}$ |
| 7 | **NPSH Available** | $NPSH_A = \dfrac{P_{\text{suction}}}{\rho g} - \dfrac{P_v}{\rho g} \pm h_s$ | $P_v$ = vapor pressure | $\text{m}$ |
| 8 | **Compressor Work (Isentropic)** | $W_s = \dfrac{k}{k-1} R T_1 \left[ \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\tfrac{k-1}{k}} - 1\right]$ | $k$ = $c_p/c_v$ | $\text{J/kg}$ |
| 9 | **Darcy-Weisbach (Friction Loss)** | $\Delta P_f = f \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | $f$ = friction factor, $L$ = length | $\text{Pa}$ |
| 10 | **Orifice Flow** | $Q = C_d A_0 \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$ | $C_d$ = discharge coefficient, $A_0$ = orifice area | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 11 | **Tank Volume (Cylindrical)** | $V = \pi r^2 h$ | $r$ = radius, $h$ = liquid height | $\text{m}^3$ |
| 12 | **Settling / Stokes Law** | $v_s = \dfrac{g d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{18 \mu}$ | Particle terminal velocity | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 13 | **Vessel Wall Thickness** | $t = \dfrac{P R}{S E - 0.6 P}$ | ASME Sec VIII (cylindrical shell) | $\text{m}$ |
| 14 | **Centrifugal Pump Affinity** | $Q \propto N, \; H \propto N^2, \; P \propto N^3$ | $N$ = rotational speed | mixed |
| 15 | **Static Stresses in Pipes** | $\sigma = \dfrac{P D}{2 t}$ | Hoop stress | $\text{Pa}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Avoiding Markdown Pipe Conflicts:** In the table above, all absolute-value or magnitude notations have been written as inline LaTeX (e.g., $\Delta T_{LMTD}$) to prevent breaking the table column boundary.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These equations are the *lingua franca* of process engineering. For example:
- **Heat Exchanger Sizing (Eq. 3-5):** Used in design to size the tube bundle area $A$ in refineries costing millions of dollars.
- **NPSH (Eq. 7):** Determines the *maximum allowable suction lift* for pumps — cavitation destroys impellers in weeks if NPSH is insufficient.
- **Compressor Work (Eq. 8):** Used to size steam-turbine drivers for ethylene crackers — directly affects plant energy efficiency.
- **Orifice Flow (Eq. 10):** The most widely used flow meter; this equation is the basis of custody transfer in oil & gas pipelines.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Insight:** When a KTU question asks "Explain the instrumentation of a heat exchanger," the model answer must include (i) a labeled sketch, (ii) at least 5 instrument tags (TT, PT, FT), and (iii) the control strategy (cascade or feedforward). Forgetting to mention the **control strategy** is a common reason for losing 4-5 marks.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Heat Exchanger Energy Balance (LMTD Method)

**Problem Setup:** A counter-current shell-and-tube heat exchanger cools hot oil ($\dot{m}_h = 10 \text{ kg/s}, c_{p,h} = 2.1 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$) from $T_{h,in} = 150°\text{C}$ to $T_{h,out} = 80°\text{C}$ using cooling water ($\dot{m}_c = 8 \text{ kg/s}, c_{p,c} = 4.18 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$) entering at $T_{c,in} = 30°\text{C}$. The overall heat transfer coefficient is $U = 500 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$. Find (a) the outlet temperature of cooling water, and (b) the required heat transfer area.

### Step 1: Compute the Heat Duty using the hot stream

The general energy balance equation is:
$$Q_H = \dot{m} \, c_p \, \Delta T$$

We use the hot stream as the reference because all its temperatures are known. The change in temperature is:
$$\Delta T_h = T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}$$

Substituting the values:
$$\Delta T_h = 150°\text{C} - 80°\text{C} = 70 \text{ K}$$

Note that a *temperature difference* in Kelvin and Celsius are numerically equal, so we may use K throughout.

The heat duty is then:
$$Q_H = \dot{m}_h \, c_{p,h} \, \Delta T_h$$

Inserting the numbers (with $c_{p,h}$ converted to $\text{J/(kg·K)} = 2100 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$):
$$Q_H = 10 \text{ kg/s} \times 2100 \text{ J/(kg·K)} \times 70 \text{ K}$$

Performing the arithmetic step by step:
$$Q_H = 10 \times 2100 = 21000 \text{ W/K}$$
$$Q_H = 21000 \text{ W/K} \times 70 \text{ K} = 1470000 \text{ W}$$

Therefore the heat duty equals:
$$Q_H = 1.47 \times 10^6 \text{ W} = 1.47 \text{ MW}$$

### Step 2: Compute the cooling water outlet temperature

For a counter-current heat exchanger in steady state, the heat lost by the hot fluid equals the heat gained by the cold fluid (neglecting losses):
$$Q_H = Q_C = \dot{m}_c \, c_{p,c} \, (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$$

Solving for the unknown outlet temperature:
$$T_{c,out} = T_{c,in} + \dfrac{Q_H}{\dot{m}_c \, c_{p,c}}$$

Substituting:
$$T_{c,out} = 30°\text{C} + \dfrac{1.47 \times 10^6 \text{ W}}{8 \text{ kg/s} \times 4180 \text{ J/(kg·K)}}$$

Denominator computation:
$$8 \times 4180 = 33440 \text{ W/K}$$

Fraction computation:
$$\dfrac{1.47 \times 10^6}{33440} = 43.96 \text{ K}$$

Therefore:
$$T_{c,out} = 30 + 43.96 = 73.96°\text{C} \approx 74°\text{C}$$

### Step 3: Compute the LMTD

For a counter-current arrangement, the two end-temperature differences are:
$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$$
$$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$$

Substituting:
$$\Delta T_1 = 150 - 74 = 76 \text{ K}$$
$$\Delta T_2 = 80 - 30 = 50 \text{ K}$$

The log-mean temperature difference is:
$$\Delta T_{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$$

Computing the numerator:
$$\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2 = 76 - 50 = 26 \text{ K}$$

Computing the ratio:
$$\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2} = \dfrac{76}{50} = 1.52$$

Computing the natural logarithm:
$$\ln(1.52) = 0.4187$$

Therefore:
$$\Delta T_{LMTD} = \dfrac{26}{0.4187} = 62.10 \text{ K}$$

### Step 4: Compute the Required Heat Transfer Area

The fundamental heat transfer equation is:
$$Q_H = U \, A \, \Delta T_{LMTD}$$

Solving for the area:
$$A = \dfrac{Q_H}{U \, \Delta T_{LMTD}}$$

Substituting:
$$A = \dfrac{1.47 \times 10^6 \text{ W}}{500 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)} \times 62.10 \text{ K}}$$

Denominator:
$$500 \times 62.10 = 31050 \text{ W/K}$$

Therefore:
$$A = \dfrac{1.47 \times 10^6}{31050} = 47.34 \text{ m}^2$$

### Final Result Summary

$$\boxed{Q_H = 1.47 \text{ MW}, \quad T_{c,out} \approx 74°\text{C}, \quad A \approx 47.34 \text{ m}^2}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Centrifugal Pump Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH-A)

**Problem Setup:** A centrifugal pump is to draw water from a reservoir whose free surface is 3 m above the pump centerline. The suction pipe diameter is 100 mm, the flow rate is 50 m³/hr, the friction loss in the suction piping is 1.2 m of head, and the water vapor pressure at the operating temperature is 1.7 kPa absolute. Atmospheric pressure is 101.3 kPa. Find the **NPSH Available**.

### Step 1: Identify the NPSH-A Equation

The general form is:
$$NPSH_A = \dfrac{P_{\text{suction}}}{\rho g} - \dfrac{P_v}{\rho g} \pm h_s - h_f$$

where $h_s$ is the *static suction head* (positive if the liquid is above the pump, negative if below), and $h_f$ is the *friction head loss* in the suction line.

### Step 2: Convert and Compute Each Term

The absolute pressure at the pump suction (we assume the pipe is short, so the suction tap reads essentially atmospheric-corrected):
$$P_{\text{suction}} = P_{\text{atm}} = 101300 \text{ Pa}$$

Convert to head of water ($\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3, g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$):
$$\dfrac{P_{\text{suction}}}{\rho g} = \dfrac{101300}{1000 \times 9.81} = 10.32 \text{ m}$$

Convert vapor pressure to head:
$$\dfrac{P_v}{\rho g} = \dfrac{1700}{1000 \times 9.81} = 0.173 \text{ m}$$

Static head (liquid surface is 3 m above pump, so $h_s = +3 \text{ m}$):
$$h_s = +3 \text{ m}$$

Friction head loss (given):
$$h_f = 1.2 \text{ m}$$

### Step 3: Substitute into the NPSH Equation

$$NPSH_A = 10.32 - 0.173 + 3 - 1.2$$

Performing subtraction step by step:
$$10.32 - 0.173 = 10.147$$
$$10.147 + 3 = 13.147$$
$$13.147 - 1.2 = 11.947 \text{ m}$$

### Step 4: Final Result and Interpretation

$$\boxed{NPSH_A = 11.95 \text{ m} \approx 12 \text{ m}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:**
- The pump manufacturer specifies a **NPSH Required (NPSH-R)** — the minimum head to prevent cavitation.
- The operating condition is safe **only if** $NPSH_A > NPSH_R + 0.5 \text{ m}$ (safety margin).
- If the water temperature rises (higher $P_v$) or the reservoir level drops (smaller $h_s$), NPSH-A decreases and the pump will cavitate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cavitation** is the formation and violent collapse of vapor bubbles at the pump impeller eye. It causes loss of head, vibration, noise, and eventual impeller erosion. **NPSH monitoring** is therefore a *critical* instrumentation task in petrochemical pumps.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Instrumentation & Control Loops by Equipment Type

| Equipment | Primary Measured Variable | Secondary Variable | Typical Sensor | Control Element | Primary Control Loop | Safety Interlock |
|-----------|--------------------------|-------------------|----------------|----------------|----------------------|------------------|
| **Distillation Column** | Top Temperature | Reflux Flow | RTD (Pt100) | Reflux Control Valve | TC → FC (cascade) | High Pressure → Block Feed (XV) |
| **Distillation Column** | Bottoms Level | Bottoms Draw | DP Cell / DTM | Bottoms Valve | LC → FC | Low Level → Shut Reboiler |
| **Heat Exchanger** | Outlet Temperature | Bypass Flow | RTD / Thermocouple | 3-way Bypass Valve | TC → FC (cascade) | High ΔP → Block |
| **Centrifugal Pump** | Discharge Pressure | Flow | Bourdon / DP | Recirc Valve | PIC / FIC | Low Suction P → Trip |
| **Reciprocating Compressor** | Anti-Surge Flow | Suction P | DP + Thermocouple | Anti-Surge Valve | FIC (fast loop) | High Disch P → Trip |
| **Fixed-Bed Reactor** | Bed Temperature | Quench Flow | Multiplexed TC | Quench Valve | TIC (multipoint) | High Hot-Spot T → Trip |
| **Storage Tank** | Level | Temperature | Radar / Servo | Inlet Block Valve | LIC → XV | High-High Level → ESD |
| **Fired Heater** | Coil Outlet T | Fuel Flow | TC / Radiamic | Fuel Control Valve | TC → FC (cascade) | Low Flow → Trip Burner |
| **Steam Boiler** | Drum Level | Steam Flow | DP Cell | Feedwater Valve | 3-Element (L, F, S) | Low-Low L → Trip |

## 3.4 Worked Example: Compressor Power Requirement

**Problem:** A single-stage reciprocating compressor compresses ethylene from $P_1 = 1.5 \text{ bar}$ to $P_2 = 8 \text{ bar}$. The inlet temperature is $T_1 = 35°\text{C} = 308 \text{ K}$. Ethylene has $k = 1.24$ and gas constant $R = 296.8 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$. The mass flow rate is $\dot{m} = 0.5 \text{ kg/s}$. Compute the isentropic compressor work and the required motor power at 90% mechanical efficiency.

### Step 1: Apply the Isentropic Work Equation

$$W_s = \dfrac{k}{k-1} R T_1 \left[ \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\tfrac{k-1}{k}} - 1 \right]$$

### Step 2: Compute the Pressure Ratio

$$\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \dfrac{8}{1.5} = 5.333$$

### Step 3: Compute the Exponent

$$\dfrac{k-1}{k} = \dfrac{0.24}{1.24} = 0.1935$$

### Step 4: Compute the Pressure-Ratio Power Term

$$5.333^{0.1935}$$

Take natural log:
$$\ln(5.333) = 1.6740$$
$$0.1935 \times 1.6740 = 0.3239$$
$$e^{0.3239} = 1.3825$$

### Step 5: Compute the Bracket

$$1.3825 - 1 = 0.3825$$

### Step 6: Compute the Prefactor

$$\dfrac{k}{k-1} = \dfrac{1.24}{0.24} = 5.1667$$

### Step 7: Assemble the Work Per Unit Mass

$$W_s = 5.1667 \times 296.8 \times 308 \times 0.3825$$

Step by step:
$$5.1667 \times 296.8 = 1533.5 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$$
$$1533.5 \times 308 = 472318 \text{ J/kg}$$
$$472318 \times 0.3825 = 180662 \text{ J/kg}$$

So $W_s \approx 1.807 \times 10^5 \text{ J/kg}$.

### Step 8: Compute the Total Power

$$P_{\text{comp}} = \dot{m} \times W_s = 0.5 \times 180662 = 90331 \text{ W} \approx 90.3 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 9: Apply Mechanical Efficiency

$$P_{\text{motor}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{comp}}}{\eta} = \dfrac{90.3}{0.90} = 100.3 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{motor}} \approx 100 \text{ kW}}$$
