```mermaid
    TOV --> TC1
    TC1 --> RFLX
    BTM --> LC1
    LC1 --> BDL
    RDR --> LC2
    LC2 --> PRD
    COLP --> PC1
    PC1 --> CDV
    OVRD --> AC1
    AC1 --> CPV
```

## 4.4 Safety Instrumented System (SIS) Architecture for a Pump

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Suction Pressure PT401]
    DP[Discharge Pressure PT402]
    BT[Bearing Temperature TT403]
    VB[Vibration Sensor VT404]

    SIS1{SIS Logic Solver: Safety PLC}
    ESD[Emergency Shutdown Output]

    XV1[Solenoid Valve to Block XV401]
    XV2[Solenoid Valve to Vent XV402]
    ALM[Safety Alarm Horn Beacon]

    SP --> SIS1
    DP --> SIS1
    BT --> SIS1
    VB --> SIS1

    SIS1 --> ESD
    ESD --> XV1
    ESD --> XV2
    ESD --> ALM
```

## 4.5 Reading the Diagrams (Schematic Interpretation Notes)

> [!TIP]
> **P&ID vs Block Diagram:** The Mermaid diagrams above are *block-level functional flow diagrams*. A real engineering **P&ID (Piping and Instrumentation Diagram)** uses ISA-5.1 symbology:
> - **Circles** = Instruments (e.g., $\bigcirc$ = field-mounted, $\bigoplus$ = DCS-mounted, slashed $\oslash$ = DCS-shared display)
> - **Letters inside circle**: First letter = *measured variable*, Subsequent letters = *function* (e.g., **TIC** = Temperature Indicating Controller; **FT** = Flow Transmitter; **LIC** = Level Indicating Controller)
> - **Bubbled valve symbol** = Control Valve; **Crossed valve** = Shutdown Valve
> - **Dashed line** = Pneumatic / electronic signal; **Solid line with crosshatch** = capillary or impulse line

**Decoding a Typical Instrument Tag:** `FT-2031`
- **F** = Functional designation (Flow)
- **T** = Transmitter
- **2031** = Loop number 2031 (usually also indicates area)

**Standard ISA Tag Letters (Quick Reference):**
- A = Analysis, B = Burner, C = Conductivity, D = Density, E = Voltage, F = Flow, G = Gauge, H = Hand, I = Current, J = Power, K = Time, L = Level, M = Moisture, N = Speed, O = Orifice, P = Pressure, Q = Quantity, R = Radiation, S = Speed/Switch, T = Temperature, U = Multivariable, V = Viscosity, W = Weight, X = Unclassified, Y = Event/State, Z = Position.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**Question:** List the major process equipment used in a typical Crude Distillation Unit (CDU) and state the primary instrument on each.

**Model Answer:**

The major process equipment in a CDU and their primary instruments are:

| Equipment | Primary Instrument |
|-----------|-------------------|
| **Atmospheric Distillation Column** | Top Temperature (TT) for reflux control |
| **Desalter (Pretreatment Vessel)** | Density/Conductivity Meter for salt removal control |
| **Fired Heater (Furnace)** | Coil Outlet Temperature (TC) for fuel valve |
| **Heat Exchanger Network (Crude Preheat Train)** | Outlet Temperature (TT) — for bypass control |
| **Reflux Drum** | Level (LT) for distillate draw |
| **Overhead Condenser** | Pressure (PT) — for condenser duty |
| **Product Strippers (Side Draw)** | Level (LT) for bottom draw |
| **Charge Pump** | Suction & Discharge Pressure (PT) |
| **Vacuum Distillation Column** | Absolute Pressure (PT) for ejector control |

**Key Learning Point:** A refinery has *interconnected* equipment where one instrument on a downstream item can be used to control an upstream item (e.g., Column Top T → Heater Outlet T cascade).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Question:** What is NPSH and why is it important for a centrifugal pump in a refinery?

**Model Answer:**

**NPSH (Net Positive Suction Head)** is the total head available at the pump suction, measured above the vapor pressure of the liquid at the pumping temperature. It has two forms:

- **NPSH-A (Available):** Determined by the system — function of atmospheric pressure, static suction head, friction losses, and vapor pressure.
- **NPSH-R (Required):** Determined by the pump manufacturer — minimum head needed to prevent cavitation.

**Importance in Refineries:**
1. **Prevents cavitation** — the formation and collapse of vapor bubbles at the impeller eye.
2. **Avoids mechanical damage** — cavitation causes impeller pitting, vibration, and bearing failure.
3. **Maintains head and flow** — cavitating pumps cannot deliver rated head/flow.
4. **Safety & reliability** — In hot hydrocarbon service (e.g., hot naphtha at 150°C), vapor pressure is high; low NPSH can lead to flashing at the suction, causing loss of prime and pump damage.

**Operational Rule:** Always maintain $NPSH_A > NPSH_R + 0.5 \text{ m safety margin}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q. (a)** Describe the major process equipment in a Petrochemical Fluidized Catalytic Cracking (FCC) Unit with a labeled block diagram. Explain the function of each equipment. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** With a neat sketch, explain the instrumentation of a Shell-and-Tube Heat Exchanger used for naphtha cooling. List at least 5 control loops and explain the cascade control strategy. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to A(a)

A **Fluidized Catalytic Cracking (FCC) Unit** converts heavy gas oil into lighter, more valuable products (gasoline, LPG, diesel) by contacting the feed with a hot fluidized catalyst.

| # | Equipment | Function |
|---|-----------|----------|
| 1 | **Reactor (Riser)** | Cracking reaction at 500-550°C, vapor and catalyst contact |
| 2 | **Regenerator** | Burns off coke from catalyst with air at 700°C |
| 3 | **Main Fractionator** | Separates cracked products into gas, gasoline, LCO, HCO |
| 4 | **Catalyst Standpipe** | Returns regenerated catalyst to riser |
| 5 | **Slurry Settler** | Removes catalyst fines from heavy product |
| 6 | **Wet Gas Compressor** | Compresses overhead gas from main fractionator |
| 7 | **Air Blower** | Supplies air to regenerator |
| 8 | **Spent Catalyst Stripper** | Strips hydrocarbons from catalyst before regeneration |

**Key Reaction:** $\text{Gas Oil} \rightarrow \text{Gasoline} + \text{LPG} + \text{LCO} + \text{Coke (on catalyst)}$

**Energy Cycle:** Coke burning in regenerator provides heat → catalyst is the heat carrier → riser reactor.

---

### Model Solution to A(b)

A **Shell-and-Tube Heat Exchanger (STHE)** used for naphtha cooling has the following instruments:

**Process Description:** Hot naphtha flows through the tube side and is cooled by cold water on the shell side. The control objective is to maintain the naphtha outlet temperature constant (e.g., 60°C) regardless of disturbances in inlet flow or inlet temperature.

**Instrumentation List (at least 5 control loops):**

| Tag | Measurement | Sensor | Function | Control Element |
|-----|-------------|--------|----------|-----------------|
| **TT-101** | Naphtha Inlet T | RTD Pt100 | Master variable | (feeds TIC-103) |
| **TT-102** | Naphtha Outlet T | RTD Pt100 | Final controlled variable | (feeds TIC-103) |
| **TT-103** | Cooling Water Inlet T | RTD | Disturbance feedforward | — |
| **FT-101** | Cooling Water Flow | Orifice + DP | Inner loop | — |
| **PT-101** | Shell-side Pressure | Bourdon | Safety high ΔP | — |
| **PT-102** | Tube-side Pressure | Bourdon | Safety high ΔP | — |
| **TIC-103** | Naphtha Outlet T | Controller | Master | Outputs to FIC-104 |
| **FIC-104** | Cooling Water Flow | Controller | Slave | Outputs to TCV-105 |
| **TCV-105** | 3-Way Bypass Valve | — | Manipulated | — |

**Cascade Control Strategy (Key Concept):**

A cascade controller has TWO loops: a **primary (master, slow)** and a **secondary (slave, fast)**.

- **Primary Loop (TC-103):** Measures naphtha outlet temperature, compares to setpoint (60°C), outputs a *flow setpoint* (not a valve signal directly) to the secondary controller.
- **Secondary Loop (FC-104):** Receives the flow setpoint, measures actual cooling water flow, and adjusts the bypass valve TCV-105.

**Why cascade is used:**
- Disturbances in cooling water pressure, temperature, or pump surges are corrected by the FAST flow loop BEFORE they affect the slower temperature loop.
- Better disturbance rejection, better stability, and faster response than single-loop temperature control.

**Block Diagram (in words):**
$$\text{Setpoint} \rightarrow \text{TC} \rightarrow \text{FC (setpoint)} \rightarrow \text{Valve} \rightarrow \text{Process}$$

**Safety Interlocks:**
- High outlet T → close cooling water isolation valve
- Low cooling water flow → open naphtha bypass to flare
- High shell-side P → open rupture disk and alarm

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q. (a)** Describe the construction and working of a centrifugal compressor used in a petrochemical plant. List its major instrumentation points. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Explain the different types of control valves used in petrochemical process equipment. Compare globe, ball, butterfly, and control valve characteristics. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to B(a)

**Centrifugal Compressor — Construction & Working:**

**Construction:**
- **Impeller**: The rotating component with curved blades that imparts kinetic energy to the gas. Cast in stainless steel or alloy.
- **Casing (Volute)**: Spiral-shaped stationary housing that converts kinetic energy into pressure.
- **Diffuser**: Stationary vanes (between impeller stages) that slow down the gas to convert velocity head to pressure head.
- **Shaft & Bearings**: Rotor supported on hydrodynamic journal bearings; thrust bearing handles axial load.
- **Seals**: Labyrinth seals, dry gas seals, or mechanical seals to prevent gas leakage along the shaft.
- **Coupling & Driver**: Connected to a steam turbine or electric motor via flexible coupling or gearbox.
- **Inlet Guide Vanes (IGV)**: Variable-pangle vanes at the suction that control gas flow rate and head.

**Working Principle:**
1. Gas enters the **eye** of the impeller axially.
2. Rotating impeller accelerates the gas radially outward (centrifugal force).
3. High-velocity gas enters the **diffuser**, where kinetic energy is converted into pressure.
4. For multi-stage machines, the gas re-enters the next impeller for further compression.
5. Pressurized gas exits the **volute** discharge.

**Major Instrumentation Points:**

| Tag | Variable | Sensor | Purpose |
|-----|----------|--------|---------|
| PT-101 | Suction Pressure | Bourdon/DP | Surge prevention |
| PT-102 | Discharge Pressure | Bourdon/DP | Anti-surge, control |
| TI-101 | Suction Temperature | RTD | Density calculation |
| TI-102 | Discharge Temperature | RTD | Overheat protection |
| FI-101 | Inlet Flow | Orifice | Surge limit calculation |
| TT-103 | Bearing Temperature | RTD | Predictive maintenance |
| VT-101 | Vibration | Piezo-accelerometer | Mechanical health |
| SI-101 | Speed | Tachometer | Affinity law input |
| IGV-POS | Inlet Guide Vane Position | LVDT | Flow modulation |

**Anti-Surge Control (Most Critical Loop):**
- Surge is a violent oscillation that occurs when flow drops below a minimum threshold.
- The surge controller (FIC) opens a **recycle valve** to maintain flow above the surge line.
- This is the *fastest* and *most important* control loop in a compressor station.

---

### Model Solution to B(b)

**Types of Control Valves in Petrochemical Equipment:**

| # | Valve Type | Construction | Flow Characteristic | Typical Use | Advantages | Disadvantages |
|---|-----------|--------------|---------------------|-----------|-----------|---------------|
| 1 | **Globe Valve** | Plug & seat; linear stem motion | Linear or Equal % | General throttling, flow control | Best throttling accuracy, tight shutoff | Tall, heavy, expensive |
| 2 | **Ball Valve** | Spherical ball with bore | Modified equal % | On/Off & some throttling | Tight shutoff, low cost, fast | Cavitation at low openings |
| 3 | **Butterfly Valve** | Disc rotates 90° in flow | Approximately equal % | Large lines, low ΔP | Cheap, lightweight, fast | Poor low-flow control |
| 4 | **Diaphragm Valve** | Flexible diaphragm pressed against weir | Linear | Corrosive, sanitary service | No leakage past stem | Limited pressure/temperature |
| 5 | **Plug Valve** | Tapered or cylindrical plug | Linear | Slurries, paper stock | Good for dirty fluids | Heavy, expensive |
| 6 | **Gate Valve** | Wedge-gate sliding perpendicular | On/Off only | Isolation | Minimal pressure drop | NOT for throttling |

**Flow Characteristics — The Core Concept:**

The **inherent flow characteristic** is the relationship between valve travel ($h$) and flow coefficient ($C_v$) at constant pressure drop.

- **Linear**: $C_v$ is proportional to travel. Best when ΔP is constant across the valve (e.g., system with mostly pipe friction).
- **Equal Percentage (Eq% / EqPct)**: Small flow at low opening, large flow at high opening. Best when ΔP varies widely (e.g., heat exchangers with varying ΔP).
- **Modified Parabolic / Quick Opening**: Sharp opening, used for on/off or two-position service.

**Selection Logic in Petrochemical Plants:**

1. **Liquid flow, large ΔP variation, throttling needed** → *Equal Percentage globe valve*
2. **Gas flow, dirty service** → *Ball valve with characterized ball*
3. **Large diameter, low pressure** → *Butterfly valve with high-performance disc*
4. **Corrosive chemicals** → *Diaphragm valve or lined globe valve*
5. **Slurry / viscous fluids** → *Plug valve or V-ball*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls in This Type of Question:**
> 1. **Forgetting to mention SAFETY INTERLOCKS** — A description of process equipment without ESD/safety loops loses 2-3 marks. Always include at least one interlock (e.g., High-High level → block feed, or low flow → trip compressor).
> 2. **Confusing P&ID symbols with descriptive text** — Don't just say "thermometer"; write "RTD (Pt100) → Transmitter (TT) → DCS Controller (TIC) → Control Valve (TCV)" with a clear signal flow.
> 3. **No ISA tag convention** — Use proper ISA-5.1 letters (TT, PT, FT, LT, AT) and loop numbers. A freehand circle with "T" inside loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting units in numerical answers** — Always include units (K, Pa, m, kg/s, m², kW). Numerical answer *without* unit = half-mark deduction.
> 5. **Cascade control confusion** — Many students draw a single-loop control and call it cascade. Cascade REQUIRES two controllers, with the master TC outputting a SETPOINT to the slave FC, NOT a valve signal.
> 6. **Ignoring installation details** — For thermocouples, mention *thermowell*; for pressure taps, mention *impulse line and seal pot*; for flow, mention *orifice flange and D/P transmitter*. These installation details carry 1-2 marks each.
> 7. **No mention of materials of construction** — For refinery heat exchangers, mention *carbon steel shell, stainless steel tubes, Monel baffles* etc. as applicable.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 2: Process Equipment**

**1. Distillation Column**
- [ ] Seven standard control loops (TC, FC, LC×2, PC, AC, plus reboiler duty)
- [ ] 3 degrees of freedom: mass, energy, and component balance
- [ ] Reflux ratio determines separation sharpness
- [ ] Pumparound used for heat removal at intermediate trays

**2. Heat Exchanger (STHE)**
- [ ] LMTD method: $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$
- [ ] Need correction factor $F$ when $P$ and $R$ deviate from unity
- [ ] **Cascade control** = Temperature master + Flow slave
- [ ] **Feedforward control** = Inlet flow disturbance
- [ ] Three-way bypass valve for capacity control
- [ ] Always install P&V safety relief on both sides

**3. Centrifugal Pump**
- [ ] **Affinity Laws**: $Q \propto N, \; H \propto N^2, \; P \propto N^3$
- [ ] **NPSH-A > NPSH-R** at all times (cavitation prevention)
- [ ] Suction pressure transmitter for low-suction trip
- [ ] Bearing temperature and vibration monitoring
- [ ] Mechanical seal vs. packed gland (modern prefers seal)

**4. Reciprocating/Compressor**
- [ ] **Anti-surge control** is the #1 critical loop
- [ ] Surge line vs. stone-wall on compressor map
- [ ] Inter-stage cooling essential for polytropic efficiency
- [ ] Capacity control: IGV (centrifugal), clearance pockets (reciprocating)

**5. Fixed-Bed Reactor**
- [ ] **Hot-spot temperature** monitoring (multiple TCs along bed)
- [ ] Quench inter-bed cooling
- [ ] ΔP monitoring → catalyst plugging
- [ ] Inlet/outlet composition analysis (AT)

**6. Storage Tank**
- [ ] **API 2350** overfill protection (3-tier: Level + HH switch + Hardwired interlock)
- [ ] Floating roof tanks reduce evaporation loss
- [ ] Radar / Servo / Hydrostatic for level
- [ ] Vapor pressure monitoring for sealed tanks

**7. Fired Heater / Furnace**
- [ ] **3-element combustion control**: Fuel, Air, Flue gas O₂
- [ ] Coil outlet temperature master → Fuel flow slave (cascade)
- [ ] Low flow → trip burner (BMS: Burner Management System)
- [ ] Tube skin temperature monitoring

**8. Control Valves (ISA 75 / IEC 60534)**
- [ ] **Cv formula**: $Q = C_v \cdot N \cdot \sqrt{\Delta P / G}$
- [ ] Equal % for varying ΔP; Linear for constant ΔP
- [ ] Actuator: Pneumatic (3-15 psi), Hydraulic, Electric, Smart (HART/Fieldbus)
- [ ] Failure mode: Air-to-Open (FO) or Air-to-Close (FC) — depends on SAFETY

**9. ISA-5.1 Tag Convention**
- [ ] First letter = measured variable (T, P, F, L, A)
- [ ] Succeeding letters = function (I=Indicate, C=Control, T=Transmit, S=Switch, A=Alarm, V=Valve)
- [ ] Loop number at end (e.g., `TT-2031`)

**10. Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS)**
- [ ] **SIL 1, 2, 3** levels per IEC 61508 / 61511
- [ ] Independent from DCS
- [ ] High-High level/pressure/temperature → ESD valve
- [ ] Fire & gas detection as part of SIS

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When answering "Explain the instrumentation of X," the examiner is *specifically* looking for: **(i) a labeled diagram, (ii) list of instrument tags using ISA-5.1, (iii) at least one cascade/feedforward/advanced control, and (iv) safety interlocks.** A 7-mark question can be answered in 2 pages of neatly labeled material — but a 14-mark question needs 5-6 pages, including one full P&ID-style sketch. **Practice drawing the diagram FIRST, then writing the text.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instrument Bubbles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET861 - INSTRUMENTATION IN PETROCHEMICAL INDUSTRIES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Instrument Bubbles # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition An **Instrument Bubble (Bubbler System / Bubble Tube Level Measurement System)** is a hydrostatic-head-based, purge-type continuous level measurement technique used extensively in the **petrochemical, refinery, and chemical process industries**. In this method, a slender open-ended tube (called the *bubble tube* or *dip tube*) is inserted vertically into a process vessel (or tank) and submerged below the minimum expected liquid level. A regulated, continuous flow of a clean purge gas — typically **instrument air** or **nitrogen (N₂)** — is forced down the tube. The gas escapes at the bottom tip as a steady stream of bubbles into the liquid. The back-pressure developed inside the tube is a direct function of the hydrostatic head of the liquid column standing above the bubble-tube outlet, and therefore, this back-pressure is directly proportional to the liquid level in the vessel. This back-pressure is sensed by a **Differential Pressure (DP) transmitter** (often configured in a single-sided gauge-pressure mode with the low side vented to atmosphere), and the resulting signal — typically a **4–20 mA current loop** or a **digital HART / Foundation Fieldbus signal** — is sent to the DCS / PLC for indication, recording, and control. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The bubble system is classified under **Purge-Type Level Measurement** in Module 2 (Level Measurement Techniques). Students must clearly distinguish it from **DP-transmitter-based wet-leg/dry-leg** and **displacer-type** level systems. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are sitting at a table and drinking water through a thin plastic straw. The harder you have to suck (the more pressure you must apply at your mouth) the deeper the straw tip is below the water surface. If you push the straw tip deeper into the glass, you must apply more pressure; if you lift it out (less liquid above the tip), the pressure needed drops. Now reverse the action: instead of sucking, **blow** gently through the straw. The pressure in your lungs (and hence at the top of the straw) must overcome the column of liquid above the tip to push bubbles out. That pressure is exactly the **hydrostatic pressure** of the liquid — the very quantity we want to measure. > **Real-world connection:** In a refinery, an operator can stand on the catwalk, look at a pressure gauge connected to a bubble tube in a hot crude-oil drum, and know the level inside — **without ever opening the vessel, without any moving parts in contact with the process fluid, and without using expensive exotic wetted materials**. ## 1.3 Why Bubbler Systems Are Pivotal in Petrochemical Industries | Advantage | Engineering Significance | |---|---| | No moving parts in contact with process | Ideal for **slurry, viscous, corrosive, hot** services | | Only wetted part is the tube tip (cheap SS, Hastelloy, PTFE) | Drastically reduces **material-of-construction cost** | | The transmitter stays **outside** the vessel, away from heat | Protects electronics — easy maintenance | | Can be **purged continuously** to keep the impulse line open | Self-cleaning in services that tend to plug | | Uses the existing **instrument-air ring main** | No separate power, no separate utility | | Naturally **linear** output (P = ρgh) | Simple calibration and DCS scaling | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Operating Metric:** Typical purge (bubble) flow rate for petrochemical applications is in the range of **0.5 to 5 SCFH** (Standard Cubic Feet per Hour) — i.e., approximately **15 to 150 cm³/min** at standard conditions. Too low → bubbles stall; too high → dynamic pressure errors and wasted purge gas. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic The bubbler system operates on the **hydrostatic pressure balance**. Let us dissect the principle into the following ordered engineering steps: 1. **Insertion of the Dip Tube:** A ½″ or ¾″ stainless-steel tube is mechanically coupled to the side of the vessel via a process connection (flanged or threaded). The tube tip is anchored to a fixed depth — typically **a few centimetres below the lowest expected liquid level** so that the tip remains submerged throughout the measurement range. 2. **Supply of Purge Gas:** A precisely regulated supply of clean, dry instrument air (or N₂) is fed to the top of the bubble tube. The supply consists of a **pressure regulator** followed by a **flow-set rotameter (purge flow indicator)** and a **needle valve** to fix a constant, low flow rate. 3. **Bubble Discharge:** The purge gas exits the bottom of the tube as a continuous chain of bubbles. As long as gas is being supplied, the pressure inside the tube builds up until it just equals the **sum of (a) the hydrostatic head of the liquid above the tip and (b) the small friction/dynamic losses in the tube**. 4. **Pressure Measurement:** A **Differential Pressure (DP) transmitter** is connected to the gas line. Its **HIGH side** senses the bubble-tube back-pressure; the **LOW side** is vented to atmosphere (or to a reference leg in compensated arrangements). The transmitter outputs a **4–20 mA signal** proportional to the liquid level. 5. **Computation in DCS:** The DCS applies the relationship **Level = (Transmitter Pressure) / (ρ × g)** to convert pressure to a level reading in mm H₂O, inches WC, or percentage. 6. **Continuous Self-Purging:** Because gas is always flowing, the tube is naturally **self-cleaning** — solids cannot settle and polymer deposits cannot form. This is the single most important reason the system is preferred in **refinery crude, coker bottoms, asphalt, and slurry services**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Subtlety for KTU:** The "pressure at the tip" is **not** exactly ρgh. There is an additional term — the **frictional pressure drop (ΔP_fric)** of the purge gas flowing down the tube. At the very low flow rates used, this is small (typically **< 0.1 %** of the measured pressure) but is the answer to a classic KTU exam follow-up: *"Why is the bubble-tube pressure slightly higher than ρgh?"* ## 2.2 Components of a Complete Bubbler System A KTU examiner expects the following block-level enumeration: 1. **Filter-Regulator Assembly (FRA)** — cleans and pressure-reduces the plant instrument air (typically from **4–7 barg supply** down to a few mbar above process pressure). 2. **Rotameter / Purge Flow Indicator** — visual confirmation of bubble rate; usually with a low-flow alarm switch. 3. **Needle Valve (Flow-Set Valve)** — fine adjustment of purge flow. 4. **Reference/Equaliser Valve** — for zeroing the transmitter. 5. **Differential Pressure Transmitter** — the heart of the system; converts back-pressure to 4–20 mA. 6. **Bubble Tube (Dip Tube)** — typically ½″–¾″ OD, SS-316, Hastelloy-C, or PTFE-lined. 7. **Process Connection** — flanged (ANSI 150# / 300#) or threaded half-coupling. 8. **Three-Valve Manifold (3VM)** — for online isolation, zero, and calibration of the DP transmitter. 9. **Impulse Tubing & Air Header** — connects FRA → rotameter → transmitter → bubble tube. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination-Critical) The following table is the **single most useful summary** for any KTU question on this topic. Memorise it. | # | Quantity | Formula | Description of Each Symbol | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Hydrostatic head at the tip | $$P_{h} = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$ | $\rho$ = liquid density (kg/m³), $g$ = 9.81 m/s², $h$ = liquid height above tube tip (m), $P_h$ in Pa | | 2 | Practical bubble-tube pressure | $$P_{BT} = \rho \cdot g \cdot h + \Delta P_{fric}$$ | $\Delta P_{fric}$ = frictional pressure drop of purge gas (Pa) | | 3 | Frictional ΔP (Hagen–Poiseuille, laminar purge flow) | $$\Delta P_{fric} = \frac{128 \,\mu \, L \, Q}{\pi \, d^{4}}$$ | $\mu$ = gas dynamic viscosity (Pa·s), $L$ = tube length (m), $Q$ = volumetric flow (m³/s), $d$ = tube inner diameter (m) | | 4 | Purge flow rate (volume) | $$Q = \frac{\pi \, d^{2}}{4} \cdot v$$ | $v$ = average gas velocity in tube (m/s) | | 5 | Range of transmitter (4 mA ↔ 0 %) | $$P_{LRV} = \rho \cdot g \cdot h_{min}$$ | $h_{min}$ = level corresponding to 0 % (often tip depth itself) | | 6 | Range of transmitter (20 mA ↔ 100 %) | $$P_{URV} = \rho \cdot g \cdot h_{max}$$ | $h_{max}$ = level at 100 % of span | | 7 | Span of the transmitter | $$\Delta P_{span} = \rho \cdot g \cdot (h_{max} - h_{min})$$ | Difference in head between the two calibration points | | 8 | Calibration in mm H₂O | $$1 \text{ mm H}_2\text{O} = 9.80665 \text{ Pa}$$ | Engineering conversion, useful when the DP cell is ranged in mmWC | | 9 | Calibration in inches WC | $$1 \text{ in WC} = 248.84 \text{ Pa}$$ | US-units conversion (Western refinery practice) | | 10 | Approximate purge-flow rule-of-thumb | $$Q \approx 1 \text{ SCFH per inch of tube ID}$$ | A practical field rule used by instrument engineers | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall #1:** When a question says *"level is 60 % and the span is 0–3 m"*, students often confuse span with absolute head. The transmitter output pressure is **always** proportional to **span**, not to absolute height. Always use the **suppressed-zero** formula when the tube tip is **below** the 0 % level. ## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields - **Crude Desalters & Settling Drums:** Highly dirty, oily, and emulsified service — the bubble tube never gets plugged. - **Asphalt & Bitumen Tanks:** High temperature (> 200 °C); no electronic instrument can survive at the tip, so the transmitter is mounted remotely and connected by the bubble tube. - **Acid & Caustic Storage:** PTFE-lined tubes survive H₂SO₄, HCl, and NaOH service at low cost. - **Sour Crude & Amine Contactors:** Nitrogen purging eliminates fire/explosion risk and prevents condensation inside the impulse line. - **Boiler Drum Level (legacy plants):** Where an expensive level column cannot be retrofitted. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix This section expands every formula in the cheat sheet into a full, textbook-quality derivation, so that no step is skipped and the KTU answer sheet is filled with examiner-pleasing reasoning. ## 3.1 Derivation of the Fundamental Hydrostatic Equation P = ρgh We start with the elementary definition of pressure as force per unit area. **Step 1 — Force exerted by a column of liquid:** A vertical column of liquid of cross-sectional area $A$ and height $h$ exerts a downward force equal to the weight of the column. $$F = m \cdot g = (\rho \cdot V) \cdot g = \rho \cdot (A \cdot h) \cdot g$$ This means the gravitational force on the liquid column is directly proportional to the product of density, area, and height. **Step 2 — Pressure is force per unit area:** Pressure is force distributed over an area, so we divide the force by the cross-sectional area $A$. $$P = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{\rho \cdot A \cdot h \cdot g}{A}$$ The cross-sectional area $A$ appears in both the numerator and the denominator. **Step 3 — Cancellation and final result:** Cancelling $A$ yields the famous **hydrostatic-pressure equation**. $$P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$ This is the **back-pressure** that must be applied at the top of the bubble tube to push gas out of the submerged tip. > [!NOTE] > **Physical insight:** Density $\rho$ is in kg/m³, $g$ is in m/s², $h$ is in m, so the product has units (kg/m³)(m/s²)(m) = kg/(m·s²) = N/m² = **Pa**. Units check out. ## 3.2 Derivation of the Frictional Pressure Drop in the Bubble Tube (Hagen–Poiseuille) The purge gas flows down a long, slender tube. For typical SCFH flow rates and millimetric tube diameters, the Reynolds number is well below 2000, so the flow is **laminar** and Poiseuille's law applies. **Step 1 — Velocity profile assumption:** For a steady, incompressible, fully-developed laminar flow in a circular tube, the Navier–Stokes equations reduce to a parabolic velocity profile: $$v(r) = \frac{\Delta P_{fric}}{4 \mu L} \left( R^{2} - r^{2} \right)$$ where $R$ is the tube radius and $r$ is the radial coordinate. **Step 2 — Volumetric flow rate (integration):** Total volumetric flow rate $Q$ is the integral of the velocity profile over the cross-section. $$Q = \int_{0}^{R} v(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$ **Step 3 — Substituting the velocity profile:** $$Q = \int_{0}^{R} \frac{\Delta P_{fric}}{4 \mu L} \left( R^{2} - r^{2} \right) 2\pi r \, dr$$ **Step 4 — Pulling constants out of the integral:** $$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P_{fric}}{2 \mu L} \int_{0}^{R} \left( R^{2} r - r^{3} \right) dr$$ **Step 5 — Performing the integration:** $$\int_{0}^{R} R^{2} r \, dr = R^{2} \cdot \frac{r^{2}}{2} \Big|_{0}^{R} = \frac{R^{4}}{2}$$ $$\int_{0}^{R} r^{3} \, dr = \frac{r^{4}}{4} \Big|_{0}^{R} = \frac{R^{4}}{4}$$ So the bracketed integral becomes: $$\frac{R^{4}}{2} - \frac{R^{4}}{4} = \frac{R^{4}}{4}$$ **Step 6 — Final result, in terms of radius $R$:** $$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P_{fric}}{2 \mu L} \cdot \frac{R^{4}}{4} = \frac{\pi \, R^{4} \, \Delta P_{fric}}{8 \, \mu \, L}$$ **Step 7 — Converting radius to diameter** ($R = d/2$, so $R^{4} = d^{4}/16$): $$Q = \frac{\pi \, d^{4} \, \Delta P_{fric}}{8 \cdot 16 \, \mu \, L} = \frac{\pi \, d^{4} \, \Delta P_{fric}}{128 \, \mu \, L}$$ **Step 8 — Solving for $\Delta P_{fric}$ (the form used by KTU students):** $$\boxed{\;\Delta P_{fric} = \frac{128 \, \mu \, L \, Q}{\pi \, d^{4}}\;}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why is $d$ to the fourth power?** Because for laminar flow, halving the tube diameter increases the friction loss by **a factor of 16**. This is why instrument engineers prefer larger-diameter bubble tubes (½″ OD ≈ 8 mm ID) to keep friction losses negligible. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem (Full Solution — KTU Style) **Problem Statement:** A refinery gasoline tank is fitted with a bubbler system. The bubble tube has an inner diameter of **6 mm** and a total length of **8 m**. The minimum and maximum measurable levels are **0.5 m and 4.5 m** above the tube tip, respectively. The liquid density is **720 kg/m³**. The purge gas (instrument air) has a viscosity of **$1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s**, and the purge flow rate is maintained at **$2.5 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/s** (≈ 5 SCFH). Calculate: 1. The DP transmitter range (LRV and URV) in **mbar** and **mm H₂O**. 2. The frictional pressure drop in the tube at the maximum purge flow. 3. The total back-pressure the transmitter must withstand at the **maximum level** (i.e., with the 4 m water column, but for gasoline, replace with 4 m of gasoline). ### Solution **Part (a) — Lower Range Value (LRV):** This corresponds to the pressure at the minimum level $h_{min} = 0.5$ m. $$P_{LRV} = \rho \cdot g \cdot h_{min}$$ Substituting the values: $$P_{LRV} = 720 \times 9.81 \times 0.5$$ $$P_{LRV} = 3531.6 \text{ Pa}$$ Converting to mbar (1 mbar = 100 Pa): $$P_{LRV} = 35.316 \text{ mbar}$$ Converting to mm H₂O (1 mm H₂O = 9.80665 Pa): $$P_{LRV} = \frac{3531.6}{9.80665} \approx 360.1 \text{ mm H}_2\text{O}$$ **Part (a) — Upper Range Value (URV):** This corresponds to the pressure at the maximum level $h_{max} = 4.5$ m. $$P_{URV} = \rho \cdot g \cdot h_{max}$$ $$P_{URV} = 720 \times 9.81 \times 4.5$$ $$P_{URV} = 31784.4 \text{ Pa}$$ $$P_{URV} = 317.844 \text{ mbar}$$ $$P_{URV} = \frac{31784.4}{9.80665} \approx 3241.1 \text{ mm H}_2\text{O}$$ **Part (a) — Span of the transmitter:** $$\Delta P_{span} = P_{URV} - P_{LRV}$$ $$\Delta P_{span} = 317.844 - 35.316 = 282.528 \text{ mbar}$$ $$\Delta P_{span} \approx 282.5 \text{ mbar} \approx 2880.7 \text{ mm H}_2\text{O}$$ **Part (b) — Frictional pressure drop:** Using the Hagen–Poiseuille equation: $$\Delta P_{fric} = \frac{128 \, \mu \, L \, Q}{\pi \, d^{4}}$$ Note that $d = 6$ mm = 0.006 m, so $d^{4} = 1.296 \times 10^{-6}$ m⁴. $$\Delta P_{fric} = \frac{128 \times (1.8 \times 10^{-5}) \times 8 \times (2.5 \times 10^{-6})}{\pi \times 1.296 \times 10^{-6}}$$ **Step 1 — Numerator:** $$128 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-5} = 2.304 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$2.304 \times 10^{-3} \times 8 = 1.8432 \times 10^{-2}$$ $$1.8432 \times 10^{-2} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-6} = 4.608 \times 10^{-8}$$ **Step 2 — Denominator:** $$\pi \times 1.296 \times 10^{-6} = 4.0723 \times 10^{-6}$$ **Step 3 — Final ratio:** $$\Delta P_{fric} = \frac{4.608 \times 10^{-8}}{4.0723 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$\Delta P_{fric} = 0.01132 \text{ Pa}$$ This is an extremely small value (about 0.0001 mbar), confirming that frictional losses are negligible at typical purge flow rates — exactly as predicted in the theory section. **Part (c) — Total back-pressure at maximum level:** The total back-pressure the transmitter senses is the sum of the hydrostatic head and the friction loss. $$P_{BT,\,max} = P_{URV} + \Delta P_{fric}$$ $$P_{BT,\,max} = 31784.4 + 0.01132$$ $$P_{BT,\,max} \approx 31784.41 \text{ Pa} \approx 317.84 \text{ mbar}$$ The friction term is essentially invisible in practice. The hydrostatic component dominates by **more than six orders of magnitude**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion from this example:** At practical purge flow rates, the friction term is negligible. The bubbler system therefore behaves as a **virtually ideal hydrostatic transducer**, and the linear approximation $P = \rho g h$ is more than sufficient for instrumentation purposes. This is precisely why it remains an industry standard in petrochemical plants worldwide. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Complete Bubbler System The following Mermaid block diagram traces the **air path** (red, instrument-air supply) and the **signal path** (blue, electrical 4–20 mA loop) through a standard refinery-grade bubbler installation. It is drawn as a block-level functional architecture so that all the engineering components and their interactions are visible at a glance. ```mermaid flowchart LR PLANT["PLANT INSTRUMENT AIR HEADER
4 to 7 barg"]:::source FRA["FILTER REGULATOR ASSEMBLY
FRA / FRL"]:::util ROTAMETER["PURGE FLOW ROTAMETER
0 to 5 SCFH"]:::util NEEDLE["NEEDLE VALVE / FLOW SET"]:::util TVM["THREE VALVE MANIFOLD
3VM"]:::util DPT["DIFFERENTIAL PRESSURE
TRANSMITTER"]:::core DPTLOOP["4 TO 20 mA HART
SIGNAL"]:::signal DCS["DCS / PLC
LEVEL INDICATION"]:::signal BTUBE["BUBBLE TUBE DIP
TIP IN VESSEL"]:::core VESSEL["PROCESS VESSEL
PETROCHEM TANK"]:::source LIQUID["PROCESS LIQUID
LEVEL h ABOVE TIP"]:::source BUBBLES["BUBBLE DISCHARGE
AT TUBE TIP"]:::core PLANT --> FRA FRA --> ROTAMETER ROTAMETER --> NEEDLE NEEDLE --> TVM TVM --> DPT DPT --> DPTLOOP DPTLOOP --> DCS TVM --> BTUBE BTUBE --> BUBBLES BUBBLES --> VESSEL LIQUID --> DPT classDef source fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef util fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#274e13,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef core fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#0b3d91,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef signal fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 ``` **How to read the diagram:** - The **amber (orange)** nodes are external sources — the plant air header and the process vessel. - The **green** nodes are utility / conditioning elements — filter, regulator, rotameter, needle valve, 3-valve manifold. - The **blue** nodes are the heart of the measurement chain — the DP transmitter, the bubble tube, and the bubble discharge point. - The **red** nodes are the signal output and the destination — the 4–20 mA loop leading to the DCS. ## 4.2 Pressure-Profile Block Diagram (Hydrostatic Head Build-Up) This second diagram shows how the pressure evolves **along the air path** from the FRA all the way down the bubble tube to the bubble-discharge point. The height of each bar represents the pressure (in mbar) at that point in the system. ```mermaid flowchart TB P0["P AT FRA OUTLET
SET TO 50 mbar ABOVE
PROCESS PRESSURE"]:::util P1["P AT ROTAMETER
NEGLIGIBLE LOSS"]:::util P2["P AT NEEDLE VALVE
MAJOR FLOW-RESISTIVE DROP"]:::util P3["P AT TX HIGH SIDE
NEAR ZERO LOSS"]:::core P4["P AT TUBE TOP
EQUAL TO TX READING"]:::core P5["P AT TUBE TIP
EQUAL TO rho g h
HYDROSTATIC HEAD"]:::core P6["P BELOW TUBE TIP
GAS DISCHARGES INTO
LIQUID AS BUBBLES"]:::signal P0 --> P1 P1 --> P2 P2 --> P3 P3 --> P4 P4 --> P5 P5 --> P6 classDef util fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#274e13,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef core fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#0b3d91,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef signal fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 ``` **Pedagogical takeaway:** The **needle valve is the only place** where a large pressure drop occurs. Everything after the needle valve is essentially at the *same* pressure — the hydrostatic head of the liquid. This is the engineering elegance of the bubbler system: once flow is set, the pressure at the transmitter simply **reads the level**. ## 4.3 Typical Installation Sketch (Side View) — Block Form The following ASCII-style block drawing summarises a typical physical installation on a refinery horizontal vessel. ``` +--------------------------------------------------+ | INSTRUMENT AIR SUPPLY | | (4-7 barg) | +------------------------+-------------------------+ | v +-----------------------------+ | FILTER + REGULATOR + NEEDLE| | VALVE + ROTAMETER ASSEMBLY | | (mounted on instrument rack)| +-------------+---------------+ | | (1/4" SS impulse tubing) v +======================================================+ || DIFFERENTIAL PRESSURE TRANSMITTER (DP CELL) || || HIGH side <-- gas line || || LOW side --> vented to atmosphere || ++=====================+=============================++ | | (gas line) v +--------------------------+ +----------------+ | | | | | PROCESS VESSEL | | LIQUID | | (Refinery Crude Drum) |<------>| LEVEL h | | | | | | | | +----------------+ | | BUBBLE TUBE TIP | | v (submerged) | | . . . . . . . . . . | <-- BUBBLES RISING +--------------------------+ ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023] > **Q: Define an "instrument bubble" (bubbler) system used for level measurement. List any two of its advantages over a conventional float-type level instrument.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A bubbler system is a **purge-type hydrostatic level measurement technique** in which a tube is immersed in the process liquid and a regulated flow of clean gas (instrument air or nitrogen) is bubbled continuously through the liquid from the tube tip. The back-pressure developed inside the tube, which equals the hydrostatic head of the liquid above the tip ($\rho g h$), is measured by a differential pressure transmitter to obtain the level indication. **Two advantages (1.5 marks each):** 1. **No moving parts in contact with the process fluid** — hence suitable for **corrosive, hot, viscous, slurry, or dirty** petrochemical services where a float would quickly fail or jam. 2. **Continuous self-purging** of the impulse path keeps the tube clean, eliminating plugging in services such as crude oil, asphalt, and coker slurry, which is a common failure mode for float chambers. > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Note:** A third advantage students can legitimately add is the **remote mounting of the transmitter**, which protects electronics from high temperatures and vibration. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] > **Q: Why is nitrogen (N₂) sometimes preferred over instrument air as the purge gas in a bubbler system used in a refinery?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Instrument air, although convenient, contains **oxygen, moisture, and oil aerosols** that can degrade the process. Nitrogen is preferred in refineries for the following reasons (1 mark each): 1. **Inertness / Explosion Safety:** Refineries handle hydrocarbons. A leak of instrument air into a hydrocarbon vapour space creates an explosive mixture. Nitrogen is chemically inert and eliminates this risk. 2. **No Condensation / No Corrosion:** Dry nitrogen does not condense inside the impulse line in cold weather, whereas moist instrument air can freeze, plug the line, or cause corrosion at fittings. 3. **No Oxidation of Process Fluid:** In services such as hot bitumen, asphalt, or polymer melts, oxygen from instrument air can oxidise the process, degrading product quality. Nitrogen prevents this. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Module-Internal Choice) > The two questions below are independent alternatives. In a KTU ESE (End Semester Exam), the student is expected to answer **one** of them. Each is divided into two 7-mark sub-parts to mirror the actual question paper. ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024] > **Q (A):** A bubbler-type level measurement system is to be installed on a vertical cylindrical tank containing **kerosene** at the refinery. > The tank height is **6 m**, the bubble tube tip is fixed at **0.4 m above the tank bottom**, and the level measurement range is to be set from **0.4 m to 5.0 m above the bottom**. > The kerosene density is **780 kg/m³** at the operating temperature. > > **(a)** Compute the **LRV, URV, and span of the differential pressure transmitter** in units of **mbar** and **mm H₂O**. Show all derivations. (7 marks) > > **(b)** The bubble tube has an inner diameter of **8 mm** and a length of **6.5 m**. The purge gas (air) viscosity is **$1.85 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s** and the purge flow rate is **$5 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/s**. Calculate the **frictional pressure drop** in the bubble tube using the Hagen–Poiseuille equation, and comment on its significance relative to the calculated span. (7 marks) --- **Solution to Question A:** #### Part (a) — Transmitter Range Calculations **Step 1 — Identify the heights above the tip:** The tip is at 0.4 m above the tank bottom. The level range is from 0.4 m to 5.0 m above the tank bottom, i.e., from the tip level to 4.6 m above the tip. $$h_{min} = 0.4 - 0.4 = 0 \text{ m (effectively, level at the tip itself)}$$ $$h_{max} = 5.0 - 0.4 = 4.6 \text{ m above the tip}$$ **Step 2 — Compute LRV (Lower Range Value):** $$P_{LRV} = \rho \cdot g \cdot h_{min} = 780 \times 9.81 \times 0 = 0 \text{ Pa}$$ This means the transmitter zero is set at the tip — a **non-suppressed zero** configuration. **Step 3 — Compute URV (Upper Range Value):** $$P_{URV} = \rho \cdot g \cdot h_{max}$$ $$P_{URV} = 780 \times 9.81 \times 4.6$$ $$P_{URV} = 35{,}196.0 \text{ Pa}$$ Converting to mbar: $$P_{URV} = \frac{35{,}196.0}{100} = 351.96 \text{ mbar}$$ Converting to mm H₂O (1 mm H₂O = 9.80665 Pa): $$P_{URV} = \frac{35{,}196.0}{9.80665} \approx 3589.2 \text{ mm H}_2\text{O}$$ **Step 4 — Span of the transmitter:** $$\Delta P_{span} = P_{URV} - P_{LRV} = 351.96 - 0 = 351.96 \text{ mbar}$$ $$\Delta P_{span} \approx 3589.2 \text{ mm H}_2\text{O}$$ **Step 5 — Engineer selects the next-higher standard range:** A DP cell of **0–400 mbar** (or equivalent mm H₂O range) would be chosen in practice, with 351.96 mbar mapped to 100 % in the DCS. #### Part (b) — Frictional Pressure Drop **Step 1 — State the Hagen–Poiseuille equation:** $$\Delta P_{fric} = \frac{128 \, \mu \, L \, Q}{\pi \, d^{4}}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute values:** - $\mu = 1.85 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s - $L = 6.5$ m - $Q = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/s - $d = 8$ mm = 0.008 m, so $d^{4} = 4.096 \times 10^{-7}$ m⁴ **Step 3 — Numerator:** $$128 \times 1.85 \times 10^{-5} = 2.368 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$2.368 \times 10^{-3} \times 6.5 = 1.5392 \times 10^{-2}$$ $$1.5392 \times 10^{-2} \times 5 \times 10^{-6} = 7.696 \times 10^{-8}$$ **Step 4 — Denominator:** $$\pi \times 4.096 \times 10^{-7} = 1.2868 \times 10^{-6}$$ **Step 5 — Final result:** $$\Delta P_{fric} = \frac{7.696 \times 10^{-8}}{1.2868 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$\Delta P_{fric} \approx 0.05981 \text{ Pa} = 5.98 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mbar}$$ **Step 6 — Significance (1 mark):** $$\frac{\Delta P_{fric}}{\Delta P_{span}} = \frac{5.98 \times 10^{-4}}{351.96} \approx 1.7 \times 10^{-6} = 0.00017\,\%$$ The frictional pressure drop is **less than 0.0002 % of the span** — completely negligible. This confirms that for all practical bubbler installations, the linear approximation $P = \rho g h$ is valid, and the contribution of friction can be safely ignored. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — *Alternative Choice* > **Q (B):** A petrochemical storage tank contains **n-hexane** (density **660 kg/m³**). It is proposed to use a **bubbler system** with a dip tube and a DP transmitter. The level range required is **0 to 3.5 m** measured from the bottom of the tank. The tip of the bubble tube is located **0.2 m above the bottom** of the tank. > > **(a)** Explain, with a neat sketch, the **construction and working** of a bubbler-type level measurement system. Clearly state the role of each component. (7 marks) > > **(b)** Determine the **range (LRV and URV) of the DP transmitter** in **kPa** and **inches of water column (in WC)**, given the data above. Also compute the **current output** when the level is at **2.1 m** from the bottom. (7 marks) --- **Solution to Question B:** #### Part (a) — Construction and Working (7 marks) **Working Principle (3 marks):** A bubbler system operates on the principle of **hydrostatic pressure measurement** using a continuous purge of clean gas. When a tube is immersed in a liquid and gas is forced through it, the pressure required to maintain a continuous flow of bubbles equals the hydrostatic pressure of the liquid column above the tube tip. By measuring this back-pressure with a DP transmitter, the level of the liquid can be determined. **Component Roles (3 marks; identify at least 4):** | Component | Function | |---|---| | Filter-Regulator Assembly (FRA) | Removes dust, oil, and water from the plant air; reduces the pressure from ~7 barg to a value just above the expected hydrostatic head. | | Rotameter / Purge Flow Indicator | Visually indicates the bubble rate; allows the operator to set and monitor purge flow. | | Needle Valve | Sets the precise, small purge flow rate (typically 0.5–5 SCFH). | | Three-Valve Manifold (3VM) | Permits online isolation, zeroing, and calibration of the DP transmitter. | | Differential Pressure Transmitter | Converts the back-pressure to a 4–20 mA current signal. | | Bubble Tube (Dip Tube) | Submerged in the liquid; its tip is the pressure-sensing point. | | Process Connection | Mounts the tube to the vessel — flanged or threaded. | **Neat Sketch (1 mark):** A labelled block diagram such as the one in Section 4.1 of these notes should be redrawn on the answer sheet, showing: instrument air → FRA → rotameter → needle valve → 3VM → DP transmitter → bubble tube tip → vessel liquid. #### Part (b) — Range and Current Output **Step 1 — Determine heights above the tip:** The tip is at 0.2 m from the bottom. The level range is 0 m to 3.5 m from the bottom. $$h_{min} = 0 - 0.2 = -0.2 \text{ m (level is below the tip)}$$ $$h_{max} = 3.5 - 0.2 = 3.3 \text{ m above the tip}$$ **Step 2 — Compute LRV (Lower Range Value):** $$P_{LRV} = \rho \cdot g \cdot h_{min} = 660 \times 9.81 \times (-0.2)$$ $$P_{LRV} = -1294.92 \text{ Pa} = -1.295 \text{ kPa}$$ **Interpretation:** A **negative LRV** indicates a **suppressed-zero** configuration. The transmitter will be ranged from **−1.295 kPa to +21.355 kPa**, i.e., from −132.0 mm H₂O to +2177.4 mm H₂O. In practice, the engineer would choose a standard 0–30 kPa cell and apply electronic zero-suppression of 1.295 kPa in the DCS. **Step 3 — Compute URV (Upper Range Value):** $$P_{URV} = \rho \cdot g \cdot h_{max} = 660 \times 9.81 \times 3.3$$ $$P_{URV} = 21{,}355.18 \text{ Pa} = 21.355 \text{ kPa}$$ **Step 4 — Span:** $$\Delta P_{span} = P_{URV} - P_{LRV} = 21.355 - (-1.295) = 22.65 \text{ kPa}$$ **Step 5 — Conversion to inches WC** (1 in WC = 248.84 Pa): $$P_{URV,\,in\,WC} = \frac{21{,}355.18}{248.84} \approx 85.82 \text{ in WC}$$ $$P_{LRV,\,in\,WC} = \frac{-1294.92}{248.84} \approx -5.20 \text{ in WC}$$ $$\Delta P_{span,\,in\,WC} \approx 85.82 - (-5.20) = 91.02 \text{ in WC}$$ **Step 6 — Current output at level = 2.1 m from bottom:** Height above tip at this level: $$h = 2.1 - 0.2 = 1.9 \text{ m}$$ Pressure at this level: $$P = 660 \times 9.81 \times 1.9 = 12{,}304.74 \text{ Pa} = 12.305 \text{ kPa}$$ Current output (linear 4–20 mA over the span 22.65 kPa): $$I = 4 + \frac{P - P_{LRV}}{P_{URV} - P_{LRV}} \times (20 - 4)$$ $$I = 4 + \frac{12{,}304.74 - (-1294.92)}{21{,}355.18 - (-1294.92)} \times 16$$ $$I = 4 + \frac{13{,}599.66}{22{,}650.10} \times 16$$ $$I = 4 + 0.6004 \times 16$$ $$I = 4 + 9.607$$ $$\boxed{\,I \approx 13.61 \text{ mA}\,}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Suppress-vs-elevate confusion:** When the **tip is below the 0 % level** (as in Question B), the LRV becomes **negative** and the span is **larger** than the URV. Many students write the URV as the span and lose **2 to 3 marks**. > 2. **Unit conversion errors:** Mixing **Pa, mbar, mm H₂O, in WC** without showing the conversion factor invites partial-mark deduction. Always state the conversion factor explicitly: **1 in WC = 248.84 Pa**. > 3. **Friction term neglect without justification:** If a question provides viscosity, length, and flow rate, the **friction term must be calculated** and **then compared** to the span. Writing "friction is negligible" *without numbers* loses 1 mark. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Definition:** A bubbler (instrument bubble) system is a **purge-type hydrostatic level measurement** technique that infers liquid level from the back-pressure required to bubble gas out of a submerged dip tube. - **Core equation:** $P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ — the heart of every derivation. - **Friction correction (Hagen–Poiseuille):** $\Delta P_{fric} = \dfrac{128 \, \mu \, L \, Q}{\pi \, d^{4}}$ — typically **negligible** at standard purge flows. - **Purge flow rate:** Always maintained at a **constant, low value** (0.5–5 SCFH); set by a needle valve and indicated by a rotameter. - **Purge gas:** Instrument air for non-flammable service; **nitrogen (N₂)** for hydrocarbon / refinery service (inertness). - **Transmitter configuration:** DP cell with **HIGH side** on the bubble tube and **LOW side** vented to atmosphere. - **Span calculation:** $\Delta P_{span} = \rho \cdot g \cdot (h_{max} - h_{min})$; **LRV and URV** are obtained by multiplying by $h_{min}$ and $h_{max}$ individually. - **Suppressed-zero:** Tip **below** the 0 % level → negative LRV; **elevated-zero:** Tip **above** the 0 % level → positive LRV. - **Key components to enumerate:** Filter-Regulator, Rotameter, Needle Valve, 3-Valve Manifold, DP Transmitter, Bubble Tube, Process Connection. - **Petrochemical applications:** Crude desalters, asphalt tanks, slurry drums, acid/caustic storage, amine contactors, sour-service drums. - **Major advantage:** No moving wetted parts, self-purging impulse line, remote-mounted electronics — ideal for **hot, corrosive, slurry services**. - **Major limitation:** Requires a continuous, clean, dry gas supply; not suitable for **vacuum service** or for **very low density** liquids where DP becomes very small. - **Unit conversions to memorise:** 1 mbar = 100 Pa; 1 mm H₂O = 9.80665 Pa; 1 in WC = 248.84 Pa; 1 SCFH ≈ 7.866 × 10⁻⁶ m³/s at standard conditions. - **Examiner's favourite 14-mark question pattern:** "Compute LRV, URV, span, then compute the friction term, then comment on its significance." Always carry **at least 5 significant figures** in intermediate steps and round only the final answer.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET861 - INSTRUMENTATION IN PETROCHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Valve Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

A **valve** is a mechanical device installed in a piping system to control, regulate, isolate, throttle, or divert the flow of process fluids (liquids, gases, slurries, or vapours) by means of a movable element (plug, disc, ball, or diaphragm) that responds to an external actuating force. In petrochemical industries, valves are classified primarily into two functional categories:

- **Isolation / On-Off Valves**: Permit full-open or full-closed operation; operate in two discrete positions.
- **Control Valves (Final Control Elements)**: Modulate the flow rate continuously in response to an automatic controller signal (typically 3–15 psi pneumatic or 4–20 mA electronic).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The course emphasises **control valves, actuators, positioners, valve characteristics, and valve sizing (Cv)** as these directly affect loop dynamics and process safety in refineries, crackers, and polymer plants.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tap" Model

Imagine the water supply pipeline entering your home:

- The **main gate valve** at the street entry is the *isolation valve* — fully open or fully closed, no in-between.
- The **tap (faucet) in your kitchen** is the *control valve* — you rotate the handle to obtain a continuous, smooth variation in flow from a trickle to a full stream.
- The **handle** acts as the *actuator* (your hand supplies the torque).
- A **thermostatic mixing valve** in a geyser is the *self-actuated control valve* — it senses temperature and adjusts hot/cold water ratio automatically.

In the same way, petrochemical control valves use **pneumatic, hydraulic, or electric actuators** instead of a human hand, and **automatic controllers (PID)** instead of human judgement.

## 1.3 Primary Classification of Valves

| Functional Family | Typical Petrochemical Application | Operating Principle |
|---|---|---|
| **Globe Valve** | Flow control in reboiler / reflux lines | Linear plug motion vs. seat |
| **Gate Valve** | Mainline isolation in crude units | Wedge slides perpendicular to flow |
| **Ball Valve** | Emergency shut-down (ESD) systems | 90° rotation of bored sphere |
| **Butterfly Valve** | Large diameter (≥ 12") hydrocarbon lines | Disc rotates 90° on a central shaft |
| **Diaphragm Valve** | Corrosive / slurry service (e.g., spent acid) | Flexible diaphragm pressed onto weir |
| **Plug Valve** | Viscous fluid (bitumen, tars) | Tapered plug rotates in body |
| **Check Valve** | Pump discharge to prevent backflow | Self-actuated by flow direction |
| **Safety / Relief Valve** | Overpressure protection on reactors & columns | Spring-loaded disc opens at set pressure |
| **Needle Valve** | Instrumentation impulse lines, sample lines | Fine tapered needle for throttling |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standards Reference:** Petrochemical valve design and testing follow **API 6D** (pipeline), **API 600** (gate, globe, check), **API 609** (butterfly), and **ISA 75.01** (control valve sizing) — these are the bench-mark standards universally cited in KTU viva and design questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Control Valve — The Final Control Element

A **control valve** is the single most important component of any automatic control loop in a petrochemical plant. It physically executes the corrective action computed by the controller. Its three sub-systems are:

1. **Actuator** — converts the control signal (3–15 psi / 4–20 mA) into mechanical motion (force or torque).
2. **Positioner** — closed-loop feedback device that ensures stem position matches the controller's command, eliminating hysteresis, dead-band, and stem-friction errors.
3. **Valve Body (Trim)** — the internal plug, seat, and cage that actually throttles the process fluid.

## 2.2 Inherent Flow Characteristics (KTU Favourite Question)

The relationship between **valve stroke (L)** and **flow rate (Q)** at constant pressure drop defines the inherent characteristic. Three standard curves are used:

| Characteristic | Equation (L/Lmax = x) | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| **Linear** | $Q/Q_{max} = x$ | Equal Δx produces equal ΔQ |
| **Equal Percentage** | $Q/Q_{max} = R^{(x-1)}$ | Equal Δx produces equal % change in Q |
| **Quick Opening** | $Q/Q_{max} = \sqrt{x}$ | Most flow occurs in first 25% of stroke |

where **R = rangeability** = ratio of maximum controllable flow to minimum controllable flow (typically 50:1 for equal-percentage trim).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection Rule (KTU):** Use **equal-percentage** trim for processes with high turn-down and where equal-loop gain is required across the operating range (most common in petrochemical flow and temperature loops). Use **linear** for liquid level control where ΔP is nearly constant. Use **quick-opening** for on-off or safety dump service.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Symbol | Engineering Meaning | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_v = Q \sqrt{\dfrac{G_f}{\Delta P}}$ | Valve flow coefficient (liquid) | $Q$ = GPM, $\Delta P$ = psi, $G_f$ = sp.gr. |
| 2 | $C_v = \dfrac{W}{N \cdot F_p \cdot \sqrt{x \cdot P_1 \cdot \rho_1}}$ | Sizing for compressible fluids | $W$ = kg/h, $P_1$ = kPa abs |
| 3 | $Q = C_v \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P}{G_f}}$ | Flow rate from installed Cv | — |
| 4 | $R = \dfrac{Q_{max}}{Q_{min}} = 50$ (typical) | Rangeability | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $F = A_e \cdot (P_s - P_0) - k \cdot x$ | Diaphragm actuator force balance | N (Newtons) |
| 6 | $A_e = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D_o^2 - D_r^2)$ | Effective diaphragm area | m² |
| 7 | $T_{net} = T_{air} - T_{stem}$ | Net torque on rotary actuator | N·m |
| 8 | $\Delta P_{allow} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v^2}{2}$ | Avoid choked flow / cavitation | Pa |

> [!WARNING]
> **Notation Trap:** In the Cv formula, $G_f$ is the **specific gravity relative to water at 60 °F** — *not* the density in SI units. Converting SI flow (m³/h) to GPM requires $1 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} = 4.4029$ GPM. Examiners deduct marks for unit confusion.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Petrochemical Engineering

- **Crude Distillation Column**: Reflux flow control uses a **cage-guided globe valve with equal-percentage trim** and a **diaphragm actuator + I/P positioner**.
- **Ethylene Cracker Furnace**: Fuel gas modulation uses a **high-capacity butterfly valve** (temperature uniformity across multiple burner passes).
- **Hydrocracker HP Loop**: 250 bar hydrogen stream uses a **double-seated globe valve** to balance stem thrust.
- **FCC Slurry Circuit**: **Erosive slurry service** demands a **ceramic-lined ball valve or pinch valve**.
- **Compressor Anti-Surge**: A dedicated **quick-opening plug valve** (mandatory API 618 anti-surge requirement).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Pneumatic Diaphragm Actuator Force Balance

A spring-opposed diaphragm actuator is the workhorse of the petrochemical industry. We derive the static force equation, which the KTU 2024 paper frequently tests.

**Given:**
- Diaphragm effective area $A_e$ (m²)
- Supply signal pressure $P_s$ (bar)
- Spring rate $k$ (N/m)
- Spring pre-compression $x_0$ (m)
- Stem displacement from initial $x$ (m)

**Step 1 — Write the forces acting on the diaphragm plate.**

The pneumatic signal pushes the diaphragm **downwards**, generating thrust $F_p$.

$$F_p = A_e \cdot P_s$$

The spring opposes the motion, producing an upward restoring force.

$$F_{spring} = k \cdot (x_0 + x)$$

**Step 2 — Apply static equilibrium for a frictionless, massless diaphragm.**

At any steady position, the net force must be zero.

$$F_p - F_{spring} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and rearrange.**

$$A_e \cdot P_s - k \cdot (x_0 + x) = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the stem displacement x as a function of signal pressure.**

$$A_e \cdot P_s = k \cdot (x_0 + x)$$

$$x_0 + x = \dfrac{A_e \cdot P_s}{k}$$

$$x = \dfrac{A_e \cdot P_s}{k} - x_0$$

**Step 5 — Apply the standard 3–15 psi signal band (lower range 3 psi → upper range 15 psi).**

Stem travel $L$ corresponds to the pressure swing from 3 to 15 psi.

$$L = \dfrac{A_e}{k} \cdot (15 - 3) = \dfrac{12 \cdot A_e}{k}$$

**Resulting net thrust available to overcome packing friction and unbalance:**

$$F_{net} = A_e \cdot (P_s - P_0) - k \cdot x$$

where $P_0$ is the lower-range pressure and $x$ is the current stem travel. This is the **actuator sizing equation** used in ISA 75.13 / IEC 60534 actuator selection.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example — Valve Sizing Using Cv

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Style Numerical (Frequently Asked for 7 marks):**

**Problem:** A control valve is required to pass **$200$ m³/h** of water (specific gravity $G_f = 1.0$) when the pressure drop across the valve is **$1.4$ bar**. Compute the required valve coefficient $C_v$.

**Step 1 — Convert to the consistent unit set for the Cv formula.**

The Cv equation uses **GPM** for Q and **psi** for ΔP. We must convert first.

$$Q_{GPM} = 200 \, \text{m}^3/\text{h} \times 4.4029 = 881 \, \text{GPM}$$

$$\Delta P_{psi} = 1.4 \, \text{bar} \times 14.5038 = 20.3 \, \text{psi}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the standard liquid Cv equation.**

$$C_v = Q \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{G_f}{\Delta P}}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the square-root term.**

$$\sqrt{\dfrac{G_f}{\Delta P}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.0}{20.3}} = \sqrt{0.04926} = 0.2219$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by the GPM flow.**

$$C_v = 881 \times 0.2219 = 195.5$$

**Step 5 — Apply the 1.25 safety factor (engineering practice).**

$$C_{v,design} = 1.25 \times 195.5 \approx 244$$

> **Answer:** Select the next standard valve size — typically a **4-inch globe valve with $C_v = 256$** from the manufacturer's catalogue (ISA S75.03 rounding up). ✓

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selection Guide (KTU Viva Standard)

| Process Variable to be Controlled | Recommended Valve Type | Inherent Characteristic | Actuator | Positioner? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Reflux flow on distillation column | Cage-guided globe | Equal percentage | Diaphragm (spring-opposed) | Yes |
| Furnace fuel gas pressure drop | Globe, single-seat | Linear | Piston (cylinder) | Yes |
| Steam header pressure | Globe or angle | Linear | Diaphragm | Yes |
| Slurry / catalyst flow | Pinch or ceramic ball | Quick opening | Electric / manual | No |
| Compressor anti-surge | Quick-opening plug | Quick opening | Pneumatic piston | Yes |
| Crude charge line isolation | Gate or ball | On-Off | Manual / electric | No |
| Boiler feedwater pump discharge | Swing check | N/A (self-actuated) | None | No |
| Ethylene compressor surge line | Plug / butterfly | Quick opening | Piston | Yes |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define the valve flow coefficient (Cv) and state its significance in valve sizing.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The valve flow coefficient $C_v$ is defined as the **number of US gallons per minute of water at 60 °F that will flow through a fully open valve with a pressure drop of 1 psi across it.** Mathematically:

$$C_v = Q \sqrt{\dfrac{G_f}{\Delta P}}$$

where $Q$ is in GPM, $G_f$ is specific gravity and $\Delta P$ is the pressure drop in psi. **Significance (1 mark each):** (i) It is the standard parameter by which valve interchangeability is judged. (ii) It directly enables valve sizing for any given flow and ΔP condition. (iii) It is the universal comparison index used in all major catalogues and in the ISA 75.01 sizing standard.

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between equal-percentage and linear inherent flow characteristics of a control valve.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Linear | Equal Percentage |
|---|---|---|
| Equation | $Q/Q_{max} = L/L_{max}$ | $Q/Q_{max} = R^{(L/L_{max}-1)}$ |
| Incremental gain | Constant | Small at low stroke, large at high stroke |
| Best application | Constant ΔP, level control | Variable ΔP, high rangeability flow / temp control |
| Sensitivity to ΔP | High | Low — compensates for process non-linearity |

The equal-percentage characteristic is preferred in petrochemical service because process ΔP often falls off as flow rises, and the trim's geometric gain compensates for this.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **OPTION — Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q-A (a). With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of a pneumatically actuated globe valve. State its advantages and limitations.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Construction (3 marks):**
   - **Valve body**: Cast steel / stainless body with flanged ends (ANSI 150# to 600#) per ASME B16.34.
   - **Trim**: Hard-faced plug (typically 17-4 PH) and seat (Stellite).
   - **Stem**: Ground and polished, connected to actuator.
   - **Packing**: PTFE / graphite V-rings to seal stem against process pressure.
   - **Actuator**: Upper diaphragm case, elastomer diaphragm, range spring.
   - **Positioner**: Mounted on yoke, senses stem position via a feedback lever.

2. **Working (3 marks):**
   - A 3–15 psi pneumatic signal enters the upper diaphragm chamber.
   - The diaphragm deflects downward against the spring, pushing the stem and plug toward the seat.
   - Flow area reduces, throttling the process fluid.
   - The positioner continuously compares stem position with the input signal and adjusts relay air to maintain exact correspondence, eliminating hysteresis and packing-friction errors.
   - On loss of signal (3 psi), the spring forces the valve to fail-safe position — typically **air-to-close (push stem down to close plug)** with **spring to open** as the safe direction for hydrocarbons.

3. **Advantages (0.5):** Precise throttling, tight shut-off, wide rangeability (50:1), good serviceability.
4. **Limitations (0.5):** Higher ΔP loss, heavier, more expensive than ball/butterfly.

---

**Q-A (b). A globe valve has a Cv of 95. It is required to control the flow of kerosene (specific gravity 0.81) in a pipeline. If the maximum allowable pressure drop is 8.5 psi, determine the maximum flow of kerosene in GPM and m³/h.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Use the liquid Cv equation.**

$$C_v = Q \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{G_f}{\Delta P}}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for Q.**

$$Q = C_v \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P}{G_f}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values.**

$$Q = 95 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{8.5}{0.81}} = 95 \times \sqrt{10.4938} = 95 \times 3.2395$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the GPM value.**

$$Q = 307.75 \, \text{GPM}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to m³/h.**

$$Q = 307.75 \div 4.4029 = 69.90 \, \text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Final Answer:** $Q_{max} \approx 308 \, \text{GPM} \approx 69.9 \, \text{m}^3/\text{h}$ of kerosene. ✓

---

### **OPTION — Question B (14 Marks)**

**Q-B (a). Explain the constructional features of a butterfly valve and a diaphragm valve. Compare their typical applications in the petrochemical industry.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Butterfly Valve (3 marks):**
   - Construction: A circular disc rotates 90° on a central shaft inside a short body. Soft (PTFE/RPTFE) or metal (Stellite) seat.
   - Working: Disc parallel to flow = open; disc perpendicular = closed. Quarter-turn actuator (pneumatic scotch-yoke or rack-and-pinion).
   - **Application:** Large-diameter (24"+) hydrocarbon transfer lines, cooling-water headers, flare gas recovery. Low cost, low weight, but limited to moderate temperature/pressure service (≤ ANSI 300#).

2. **Diaphragm Valve (3 marks):**
   - Construction: Flexible elastomer (EPDM, Viton, PTFE) diaphragm separates the process from the actuator. A compressor (saddle) presses the diaphragm onto a raised weir.
   - Working: Linear stem pushes the diaphragm onto the weir, throttling flow. Body is normally lined (glass, rubber, PFA) for corrosion protection.
   - **Application:** Highly corrosive fluids (chlor-alkali, spent acid in alkylation units), slurries, sterile / hygienic service, pharmaceutical-grade solvents. Cannot handle very high pressure (≤ 16 bar typical) or very high temperature.

3. **Comparison (1 mark):**
   - Butterfly = large bore, low-pressure hydrocarbon lines. Diaphragm = small-to-medium bore, highly corrosive / slurry lines. Both are quick-acting but butterfly has a tighter shut-off at large sizes.

---

**Q-B (b). With neat sketches, explain the inherent flow characteristics of control valves. For an equal-percentage valve with R = 50, calculate the flow ratio $Q/Q_{max}$ when the valve is 25%, 50%, 75% and 100% open.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Definitions of the three characteristics (3 marks):**
   - **Linear**: Flow varies linearly with stroke. Equation: $Q/Q_{max} = L/L_{max}$.
   - **Equal Percentage**: Equal increments of stroke produce equal percentage change in flow. Equation: $Q/Q_{max} = R^{(L/L_{max} - 1)}$.
   - **Quick Opening**: A small stroke produces a large flow change. Equation: $Q/Q_{max} = \sqrt{L/L_{max}}$.
   - **Sketches**: A typical answer includes three curves on a graph of $Q/Q_{max}$ vs. $L/L_{max}$, with the quick-opening curve convex upward, the linear as a straight line through origin, and the equal-percentage curve concave upward (very low flow at low stroke, exponential rise later).

2. **Numerical Computation (4 marks):**

Given: $R = 50$, $x = L/L_{max}$.

$$Q/Q_{max} = 50^{(x - 1)}$$

| % Open (x) | Exponent (x − 1) | $Q/Q_{max} = 50^{(x-1)}$ |
|---|---|---|
| 25% | −0.75 | $50^{-0.75} = 0.0501$ ≈ **5.0%** |
| 50% | −0.50 | $50^{-0.50} = 0.1414$ ≈ **14.1%** |
| 75% | −0.25 | $50^{-0.25} = 0.3795$ ≈ **38.0%** |
| 100% | 0.00 | $50^{0} = 1.000$ = **100%** |

> **Observation:** At 25% stroke the valve delivers only 5% of the maximum flow — this confirms why equal-percentage trim is selected for **high turn-down services** such as furnace fuel-gas and reflux flow control.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Compiled from KTU Valuation Keys):**
> 1. **Unit mixing in Cv problems:** Students frequently insert $Q$ in m³/h and $\Delta P$ in bar into the Cv equation without conversion to GPM and psi. **Full 2-mark penalty** is routinely applied. Always convert first.
> 2. **Confusing inherent vs. installed characteristics:** Inherent is at constant ΔP; installed includes the effect of varying process ΔP. Examiners expect the distinction in any 14-mark question.
> 3. **Forgetting the safety factor of 1.25** in valve sizing numericals leads to a half-mark deduction.
> 4. **In equal-percentage problems, students often compute $R^x$** instead of $R^{(x-1)}$ — the (x−1) form is essential because at $x=1$ (fully open) the ratio must equal 1.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram** in a 7-mark descriptive question costs at least 1.5 marks — KTU mandates a *neat labelled sketch* for full credit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Valve = final control element** in any petrochemical control loop; converts controller signal into fluid-flow modulation.
- Two functional families: **On-Off (isolation)** and **Control (modulating)**.
- Globe, gate, ball, butterfly, diaphragm, plug, check, relief, needle are the nine standard types.
- **Control valve sub-systems**: Body (trim) + Actuator + Positioner.
- **Inherent characteristics**: Linear, Equal Percentage, Quick Opening — selectable per process need.
- **Equal-percentage trim is the petrochemical default** because it compensates for process non-linearity and gives wide rangeability.
- **Cv equation (liquid)**: $C_v = Q \sqrt{G_f / \Delta P}$ — always use GPM, psi, and water-specific gravity.
- **Cv equation (gas/vapour)**: involves $F_p$, $x_T$, $Y$ expansion factors, and $P_1$ absolute.
- **Rangeability** $R = Q_{max}/Q_{min} = 50$ typical for equal-percentage.
- **Pneumatic actuator force balance**: $F_{net} = A_e (P_s - P_0) - k \cdot x$.
- **Fail-safe modes**: Air-to-close / spring-open (most common for hydrocarbon fire safety).
- **Standards to remember**: API 6D, API 600, API 609, ISA 75.01 / 75.13, ASME B16.34, IEC 60534.
- **Selection rule of thumb**: Reflux & flow → equal-percentage cage globe; large bore → butterfly; corrosive / slurry → diaphragm; isolation → ball or gate; backflow prevention → swing check; overpressure → spring-loaded relief.
- **Always apply 1.25 safety factor** when sizing a control valve from a Cv calculation.
- **Globe valve = high ΔP loss but precise throttling**; ball/butterfly = low ΔP loss, better for isolation.
- **Diaphragm valve = corrosion champion** but limited to moderate pressure and temperature.
- **Positioner is mandatory** for tight control loops and for valves with high packing friction.
- **Stem position is fed back** to the positioner to close the inner loop and override hysteresis.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
