topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET861 - INSTRUMENTATION IN PETROCHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Piping and Heat Exchanger. P & I Diagram of Petroleum Refinery. Overview of Control Systems - Feedback

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Process Piping in a Petrochemical Plant

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Process piping refers to the assembly of pipes, fittings, valves, flanges, gaskets, and bolting used to convey process fluids (hydrocarbons, steam, water, acids, gases) between process units within a petrochemical or refinery complex. It forms the physical transport backbone that links reactors, distillation columns, heat exchangers, pumps, and storage tanks.

**Key Standards:** **ASME B31.3 (Process Piping)**, ASME B16.5 (Pipe Flanges), ASME B16.34 (Valves).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Focus:** The syllabus emphasizes *instrumentation* interfaces on piping — that is, where flow meters, pressure transmitters, and temperature sensors are *tapped* into the piping network, and how these taps are represented on engineering drawings.

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
Think of a refinery as a **human body**. The piping is the **circulatory system (arteries and veins)**, the pumps are the **heart**, the heat exchangers are the **lungs** (where thermal "gas exchange" occurs between hot and cold streams), and the instrumentation & control system is the **nervous system** that monitors and regulates everything. If any artery is blocked or mis-instrumented, the entire organism (plant) fails.

**Pipe Classification by Service (Petrochemical Context):**
- **Process Lines:** Carry hydrocarbons between units (e.g., Naphtha from Atmospheric Distillation to Naphtha Hydrotreater).
- **Utility Lines:** Steam, cooling water, instrument air, nitrogen.
- **Pressure Classes:** **Class 150, 300, 600, 900, 1500, 2500 (lb flanges)** — higher classes handle higher pressures.

**Pipe Sizing Parameter:** Nominal Pipe Size (**NPS**) in inches and **Schedule (Sch 40, Sch 80, Sch 160)** indicating wall thickness.

---

### 1.2 Heat Exchangers

**Formal Definition:**
A heat exchanger is a process equipment device that transfers thermal energy between two or more process fluids at different temperatures, with the fluids separated by a solid wall (in most refinery applications), without mixing.

**Common Refinery Types:**
- **Shell & Tube Heat Exchanger (STHE):** Most common — bundle of tubes inside a shell. Used as crude preheaters, overhead condensers, reboilers.
- **Plate Heat Exchanger (PHE):** Compact, gasketed plates — used in low-viscosity, low-fouling services.
- **Air-Cooled Heat Exchanger (ACHE / Fin-Fan):** Uses ambient air with induced/forced draft fans.
- **Double Pipe (Hairpin):** For small duties, high fouling services.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU module focuses on *instrumentation* of heat exchangers — i.e., temperature, pressure, and flow sensors mounted on shell-side and tube-side nozzles that are *tied into the control loop* for stable operation.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
A heat exchanger is like a **double-walled drinking glass**. You pour hot tea inside the inner wall, and cold water fills the space between the inner and outer wall. The two never mix, but heat flows from hot tea → glass → cold water. By the time you finish your tea, the outer water is warm.

---

### 1.3 P & I Diagram (Piping & Instrumentation Diagram)

**Formal Definition:**
A Piping and Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID) is a **schematic engineering drawing** that comprehensively details the piping, process equipment, instrumentation, and control interlocks of a process plant. It follows the **ISA 5.1 (Instrumentation Symbols and Identification)** standard and is the primary working document for instrument engineers, control engineers, and process operators.

**Key Symbol Categories:**
- **Bubbles/Circles:** Instruments (location-mounted vs. DCS-mounted)
- **Lines:** Signal type (solid = pneumatic/electric/digital; dashed = capillary/tubing)
- **Letters inside bubbles:** ISA tag identifier (e.g., **T** = Temperature, **F** = Flow, **P** = Pressure, **L** = Level)

**Conceptual Analogy:**
If a refinery were a movie, the P&ID would be the **storyboard sketch** — every actor (valve, instrument, pump) is drawn, every line of dialogue (signal) is shown, but it's not a photograph of the actual plant. It is the **engineer's universal language**.

---

### 1.4 Feedback Control Systems

**Formal Definition:**
A feedback control system is one in which the **measured output (controlled variable)** is continuously compared with a **desired value (setpoint)**. The difference — called the **error signal** — drives a **controller** that adjusts the **manipulated variable (MV)** to bring the controlled variable back to the setpoint.

**Negative Feedback (used in 99% of process control):**
Error = Setpoint − Measured Variable (PV). The controller acts to **reduce** the error to zero.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
The classic example is a **home shower with a thermostatic mixing valve**. You set 40 °C (setpoint). The valve measures mixed outlet temperature (PV). If the water is too hot, the valve closes the hot inlet slightly; if too cold, it opens hot and closes cold. It does this *continuously* without you touching it. That is **closed-loop negative feedback control**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module Focus:** You must be able to *draw* a simple feedback loop with a single sensor, controller, and final control element, and represent the same loop on a P&ID.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Process Piping — Engineering Logic Steps

**Step 1: Selection of Pipe Material**
- **Carbon Steel (A106 Gr.B):** Most refinery hydrocarbon service up to ~425 °C.
- **Stainless Steel (A312 304/316):** Corrosive services, high-purity streams.
- **Alloy (A335 P11/P22/P91):** High-temperature steam lines.
- **Lined/Clad:** For HCl, H₂SO₄, etc.

**Step 2: Determination of Pipe Diameter**
Use the **continuity equation** and acceptable velocity criteria:
$$ Q = A \cdot v = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot v $$
- **Liquid lines:** 1–3 m/s (suction), 2–4 m/s (discharge).
- **Gas/Vapor lines:** 15–30 m/s.
- **Steam lines:** 25–60 m/s.

**Step 3: Calculation of Pressure Drop (Darcy–Weisbach)**
$$ \Delta P_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2} $$
where $f$ = Darcy friction factor (from Moody chart or Colebrook equation), $L$ = equivalent length including fittings.

**Step 4: Selection of Flanges & Valves**
Flange rating must exceed **maximum allowable working pressure (MAWP)** at design temperature.

**Real-world utility:** Under-sized lines cause excessive pumping cost; over-sized lines cause low velocity, fouling, and stagnant pockets that can lead to corrosion or polymerization in olefin service.

---

### 2.2 Heat Exchanger Theory & Instrumentation

**Step 1: Energy Balance (Steady State)**
$$ \dot{Q} = \dot{m}_h c_{p,h} (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = \dot{m}_c c_{p,c} (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}) $$

**Step 2: Overall Heat Transfer Equation (LMTD Method)**
$$ \dot{Q} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD} $$
where $U$ = overall heat transfer coefficient ($\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$), $A$ = heat transfer area, and
$$ \Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} $$
with $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$ and $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$.

**Step 3: Correction Factor $F$ (for non-counterflow arrangements)**
$$ \dot{Q} = U \cdot A \cdot F \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD,CF} $$
where $0 < F \leq 1$ — falls rapidly as temperature approach is small.

**Step 4: Fouling Resistance**
$$ \frac{1}{U_{dirty}} = \frac{1}{U_{clean}} + R_{f,in} + R_{f,out} $$

**Step 5: Key Instrumentation**
- **Inlet/Outlet Temperature Transmitters (TT)** on both shell and tube sides.
- **Pressure Transmitters (PT)** at shell and tube inlets/outlets to monitor $\Delta P$ (fouling indicator).
- **Flow Transmitter (FT)** on one side (typically the controlled stream).
- **Level Switch (LSH/LSL)** on the channel head for tube-side flooding protection.

**Real-world utility:** Heat exchangers are the **largest energy-transfer devices** in any refinery. Precise temperature control via feedback loops maximizes energy recovery and prevents thermal damage (e.g., crude preheater outlet > 370 °C causes coking).

---

### 2.3 P&ID Reading Logic Steps

**Step 1: Identify Process Flow Direction** — arrows on lines.
**Step 2: Identify Equipment** — vessels, exchangers, pumps (square/rectangular symbols with letter codes like **E-101**, **V-201**).
**Step 3: Identify Instrumentation Bubbles** — circles with letter-number tags.
**Step 4: Trace Signal Lines** — dashed/solid lines connecting instrument to control system.
**Step 5: Identify Control Loops** — look for a sensor, controller, and final control element (valve) interconnected.

**ISA Tag Identification (KTU Must-Know):**
First letter = **measured variable**; succeeding letters = **modifier, readout, output**. Examples:
- **TIC 101** = Temperature Indicating Controller, loop 101.
- **FT 205** = Flow Transmitter, loop 205.
- **PCV 302** = Pressure Control Valve (self-actuated), loop 302.
- **LIC 410** = Level Indicating Controller.
- **TT 101A** = Temperature Transmitter, loop 101, suffix A.

---

### 2.4 Feedback Control — Engineering Logic Steps

**Step 1: Define Controlled Variable (CV), Setpoint (SP), Manipulated Variable (MV), Disturbance (D).**
**Step 2: Sensor measures CV** → produces **Process Variable (PV)** signal (e.g., 4–20 mA).
**Step 3: Compare PV with SP** → generate **Error** $e(t) = SP - PV(t)$.
**Step 4: Controller computes control output** $m(t)$ from $e(t)$.
**Step 5: Final Control Element (Control Valve)** adjusts MV to influence the process.
**Step 6: Process responds → new PV → cycle continues.**

**Controller Types:**
- **P (Proportional):** $m(t) = K_c \, e(t)$ — fast, but leaves **offset (steady-state error)**.
- **PI (Proportional-Integral):** $m(t) = K_c \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{\tau_I}\int e(t)\,dt \right]$ — eliminates offset.
- **PID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative):** Adds derivative action to anticipate and dampen oscillations:
$$ m(t) = K_c \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{\tau_I}\int e(t)\,dt + \tau_D \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right] $$

**KTU Formula Sheet (Comprehensive Table)**

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Typical Units / Range |
|---------|----------------------|-----------------------|
| Continuity (pipe sizing) | $Q = A \cdot v = \frac{\pi}{4}D^2 v$ | $Q$ in m³/s |
| Pressure drop (frictional) | $\Delta P_f = f \frac{L}{D} \frac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | Pa |
| Heat duty (energy balance) | $\dot Q = \dot m c_p \Delta T$ | W (J/s) |
| LMTD | $\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$ | K |
| Overall rate equation | $\dot Q = U A F \Delta T_{LMTD}$ | W |
| Combined fouling | $1/U_{dirty} = 1/U_{clean} + R_{f,i} + R_{f,o}$ | m²K/W |
| Reynolds number | $Re = \rho v D / \mu$ | dimensionless |
| Error signal | $e(t) = SP(t) - PV(t)$ | same as CV units |
| PID controller | $m(t) = K_c[e + \frac{1}{\tau_I}\int e\,dt + \tau_D \frac{de}{dt}]$ | % output |
| Closed-loop transfer fn | $G_{CL}(s) = \dfrac{G_c G_p G_v}{1 + G_c G_p G_v G_m}$ | dimensionless |
| Offset (P-only) | $\text{offset} = \dfrac{1}{1+K_p K_c}$ | fraction |
| Signal standard | Pneumatic 3–15 psi / Electronic 4–20 mA / Digital fieldbus | per ISA |
| ISA tag first letter | T,F,P,L,X,E,A,Q,S,H (Temp/Flow/Press/Level/…) | letters |

**Why and How of each step:**
- **Why use a feedback loop?** Because open-loop control cannot compensate for *disturbances* (e.g., feed composition change, ambient temperature change, catalyst deactivation). Feedback *automatically* rejects disturbances and tracks setpoint changes.
- **Why derivative action?** Because it adds "anticipation" — when error is *changing rapidly*, derivative produces a strong corrective action before the error grows large. This **improves stability** and **reduces overshoot**.
- **Why integral action?** Because a pure P-controller leaves a constant offset under sustained load. The integral "remembers" past error and drives it to zero.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Heat Exchanger LMTD Equation

Starting from a differential element $dA$ at any point in a counterflow exchanger with cold-side enthalpy rise balanced by hot-side enthalpy drop:

$$ dQ = U\,dA\,\Delta T $$

Energy balance on hot stream:
$$ dQ = -\dot m_h c_{p,h}\, dT_h $$

Energy balance on cold stream:
$$ dQ = \dot m_c c_{p,c}\, dT_c $$

Subtracting gives:
$$ d(\Delta T) = dT_h - dT_c = -dQ\left( \frac{1}{\dot m_h c_{p,h}} + \frac{1}{\dot m_c c_{p,c}} \right) $$

Therefore:
$$ \frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U\,dA \left( \frac{1}{\dot m_h c_{p,h}} + \frac{1}{\dot m_c c_{p,c}} \right) $$

Integrating from inlet ($\Delta T_1$ at $A=0$) to outlet ($\Delta T_2$ at $A=A$):
$$ \ln\!\left(\frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1}\right) = -U A \left( \frac{1}{\dot m_h c_{p,h}} + \frac{1}{\dot m_c c_{p,c}} \right) $$

But total heat transfer $Q = \dot m_h c_{p,h} (T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}) = \dot m_c c_{p,c}(T_{c,o} - T_{c,i})$,
and $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o}$, $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i}$.

Solving for $Q$:
$$ Q = U A \left( \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)} \right) = U A \,\Delta T_{LMTD} $$

This is the **LMTD equation**, foundational for exchanger rating.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Closed-Loop Transfer Function (Unity Feedback)

A standard feedback block diagram has forward path $G(s) = G_c(s) G_v(s) G_p(s)$ and feedback path $H(s) = G_m(s)$.

The error:
$$ E(s) = R(s) - H(s)Y(s) $$

The output:
$$ Y(s) = G(s) E(s) = G(s)[R(s) - H(s)Y(s)] $$

Rearranging:
$$ Y(s)[1 + G(s)H(s)] = G(s)R(s) $$

Therefore the closed-loop transfer function is:
$$ \frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)} = \frac{G_c G_v G_p}{1 + G_c G_v G_p G_m} $$

For a unity feedback system ($G_m = 1$):
$$ \frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G_c(s) G_v(s) G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s) G_v(s) G_p(s)} $$

**Stability Criterion (Routh / Nyquist):** the closed-loop is stable iff all roots of $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ lie in the left-half s-plane.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Steady-State Offset for P-Controller

Consider a first-order process $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}$ with controller $G_c(s) = K_c$, final control element unity, and measurement unity.

A unit step disturbance $D(s) = 1/s$ enters the process. The closed-loop transfer function from disturbance to output is:
$$ \frac{Y(s)}{D(s)} = \frac{G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s) G_p(s)} $$

For P-control with $G_c = K_c$:
$$ \frac{Y(s)}{D(s)} = \frac{K_p/(\tau_p s + 1)}{1 + K_c K_p/(\tau_p s + 1)} = \frac{K_p}{\tau_p s + 1 + K_c K_p} $$

Steady-state error (final-value theorem, $\lim_{s \to 0} s Y(s)$):
$$ y_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{K_p}{s(\tau_p s + 1 + K_c K_p)} = \frac{K_p}{1 + K_c K_p} = \frac{1}{1/K_p + K_c} \approx \frac{1}{K_c} \text{ (large }K_c\text{)} $$

This non-zero steady-state is the **offset**. It is eliminated by adding **integral action**, which effectively makes $K_c \to \infty$ at $s=0$.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: P&ID Symbol-to-Function Mapping

| ISA Tag Letter | First Letter (Measured Variable) | Common Field Instrument | DCS Representation |
|----------------|----------------------------------|-------------------------|---------------------|
| **T** | Temperature | TT (RTD/TC), TE (element) | TIC, TIR, TCV |
| **P** | Pressure | PT, PI, PG | PIC, PIR, PCV |
| **F** | Flow | FE, FT (orifice, vortex, mag) | FIC, FQ (totalizer) |
| **L** | Level | LT (DP, displacer, ultrasonic) | LIC, LAL, LSHH |
| **A** | Analysis | AT (pH, O₂, conductivity) | AIC |
| **X** | Unclassified (e.g., vibration) | XT | XIC |
| **E** | Voltage / Current | ET | EIC |
| **Q** | Quantity / Total | QT | QIC |

**Modifier Suffixes:** I = Indicate, C = Control, R = Record, T = Transmit, V = Valve, S = Switch, H = High, L = Low.

**Bubble Convention:**
- **Circle =** Discrete instrument (mounted in field or on panel).
- **Circle with horizontal line through middle =** DCS-mounted (shared display).
- **Circle with two horizontal lines =** Computer/PLC function block.

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Single-Loop Feedback Control System

```mermaid
graph LR
  nodeSP[Setpoint SP] --> nodeSum{Junction}
  nodePV[Process Variable PV] --> nodeSum
  nodeSum -- Error e of t --> nodeCTRL[Controller Gc]
  nodeCTRL -- Control Output m of t --> nodeFCE[Final Control Element Gv]
  nodeFCE -- Manipulated Variable --> nodePROC[Process Gp]
  nodePROC --> nodeOUT[Controlled Variable CV]
  nodeOUT --> nodeMTR[Measurement / Transmitter Gm]
  nodeMTR --> nodePV
  nodeDIST[Disturbance d of t] -.-> nodePROC
```

*Reading the diagram:* The setpoint and process variable meet at the summing junction. The error is fed to the controller. The controller's output drives the final control element (a control valve). The valve adjusts the manipulated variable flowing into the process. The process produces the controlled variable, which is measured and fed back as PV. Any disturbance (e.g., feed temperature) enters the process and is automatically rejected by the loop.

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Heat Exchanger Instrumentation & Control

```mermaid
graph TB
  subgraph HOT_SIDE["HOT SIDE — Crude Feed"]
    nodeHIN[Hot Inlet] --> nodeHTT[TT 101 Inlet]
    nodeHTT --> nodeHEX[Shell and Tube Exchanger E 101]
    nodeHEX --> nodeHOUT[Hot Outlet]
    nodeHTTOUT[TT 102 Outlet] --> nodeHOUT
  end
  subgraph COLD_SIDE["COLD SIDE — Distillate Stream"]
    nodeCIN[Cold Inlet] --> nodeCFT[FT 201]
    nodeCFT --> nodeCFCV[FCV 201 Control Valve]
    nodeCFCV --> nodeHEX
    nodeHEX --> nodeCOUT[Cold Outlet]
    nodeCTT[TT 202 Cold Outlet] --> nodeCOUT
  end
  subgraph DCS["DCS CONTROL LAYER"]
    nodeTIC[TIC 101 Temperature Controller]
    nodeFIC[FIC 201 Flow Controller]
    nodeTT2[TT 102 Hot Outlet]
  end
  nodeHTTOUT --> nodeTIC
  nodeCTT --> nodeTIC
  nodeTIC -- cascade slave signal --> nodeFIC
  nodeCFT --> nodeFIC
  nodeFIC -- 4 to 20 mA --> nodeCFCV
```

*Reading:* This represents a **cascade temperature control loop** on a refinery heat exchanger. The primary (master) controller **TIC 101** senses the hot-outlet temperature and resets the setpoint of the secondary (slave) controller **FIC 201**, which in turn positions the cold-side control valve **FCV 201**. Cascade control dramatically improves disturbance rejection.

---

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Reading a P&ID

```mermaid
graph TB
  nodeA1[Step 1 Identify Equipment] --> nodeA2[Step 2 Identify Process Lines]
  nodeA2 --> nodeA3[Step 3 Identify Instrumentation Bubbles]
  nodeA3 --> nodeA4[Step 4 Trace Signal Dashed Lines]
  nodeA4 --> nodeA5[Step 5 Identify Control Loops]
  nodeA5 --> nodeA6[Step 6 Identify Interlocks Alarms Shutdowns]
  nodeA6 --> nodeA7[Complete Process Understanding]
  style nodeA1 fill:none
  style nodeA2 fill:none
  style nodeA3 fill:none
  style nodeA4 fill:none
  style nodeA5 fill:none
  style nodeA6 fill:none
```

*Reading:* A systematic 6-step method for any KTU exam question asking "Explain how you read a P&ID of a given unit."

---

### 4.4 Physical Layout Mapping — Heat Exchanger with Instrumentation Tappings

```mermaid
graph TB
  subgraph SHELL[SHELL SIDE]
    SI[Shell Inlet Nozzle]
    SO[Shell Outlet Nozzle]
    ST1[PT 100 Shell Inlet]
    ST2[PT 101 Shell Outlet]
  end
  subgraph TUBE[TUBE SIDE]
    TI[Tube Inlet Channel Head]
    TO[Tube Outlet Channel Head]
    TT1[TT 200 Tube Inlet]
    TT2[TT 201 Tube Outlet]
    LSH[LSH 300 High Level Switch]
  end
  SI --> EXC[Exchanger Body]
  EXC --> SO
  TI --> EXC
  EXC --> TO
  ST1 -.tap.-> SI
  ST2 -.tap.-> SO
  TT1 -.tap.-> TI
  TT2 -.tap.-> TO
  LSH -.mounted on.-> TI
```

*Reading:* Tappings for **PT/TT** are made on the inlet and outlet nozzles, separated from the main process flow by **instrument root valves** and **impulse piping** (not shown). The **LSH** is mounted on the channel head to detect tube-side liquid carryover.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**Define a Piping and Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID). Mention any two key information it conveys.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — write as 3 key points):**
A P&ID is a schematic engineering drawing that depicts the piping, process equipment, instrumentation, and control interconnections of a process plant, prepared using ISA 5.1 symbology.
(i) It shows **all process piping** including size, material class, and flow direction, along with all valves and fittings.
(ii) It depicts **all instrumentation** — sensors, transmitters, controllers, and final control elements — with their ISA tag numbers and signal interconnection.
(iii) It indicates **process control loops, interlocks, alarms, and shutdown (ESD) logic**, making it the working document for operations and safety.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**What is negative feedback in a process control system? Why is it preferred over positive feedback?**

**Model Answer:**
In negative feedback, the **error signal** is computed as $e(t) = \text{Setpoint} - \text{Measured Variable}$, and the controller acts to **reduce** this error to zero. It is preferred because:
(i) It provides **automatic disturbance rejection** — the loop counteracts any deviation.
(ii) It ensures **closed-loop stability** when properly tuned.
(iii) Positive feedback would *amplify* deviations, leading to instability and runaway of the process.

---

### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice Provided)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **closed-loop feedback control system** used to maintain the outlet temperature of a refinery heat exchanger. Identify the controlled, manipulated, and measured variables. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **LMTD method** of heat exchanger analysis. Derive the LMTD expression and state why a correction factor $F$ is used in practical design. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram and Explanation (7 marks):**

```mermaid
graph LR
  SP[Setpoint Temperature] --> SUM{Junction}
  TT[TT 101 Outlet Temperature] --> SUM
  SUM --> TIC[TIC 101 Controller]
  TIC --> CV[TCV 101 Bypass Valve on Cold Side]
  CV --> HEX[Heat Exchanger E 101]
  HEX --> TTV[Outlet Temperature]
  TTV --> TT
```

- **Controlled Variable (CV):** Outlet temperature of process stream.
- **Measured Variable:** Same as CV (TT 101).
- **Manipulated Variable (MV):** Cold-side flow rate (adjusted by TCV 101).
- **Disturbance:** Hot-side inlet temperature or flow fluctuation.
- **Action:** If PV < SP, the controller opens the cold bypass valve to *reduce* heat transfer area, allowing the outlet temperature to rise. If PV > SP, valve closes more.

**Marking Split (typical KTU pattern):**
- Block diagram with all elements: 3 marks.
- Identification of CV/MV/PV/Disturbance: 2 marks.
- Working explanation: 2 marks.

**(b) LMTD Derivation and F Factor (7 marks):**
- The LMTD equation is derived from a differential energy balance on a counterflow exchanger (see SECTION 3.1 for the full derivation — students should reproduce it step by step).
- **Final expression:** $\Delta T_{LMTD} = (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)/\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)$.
- **Why correction factor $F$?** The LMTD as derived assumes *pure countercurrent* flow. In real shell-and-tube exchangers, flows are 1-shell-pass / 2-tube-pass, etc. The true mean temperature difference is less than counterflow LMTD by a factor $F$:
$$ \Delta T_{m,actual} = F \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD,counterflow} $$
- $F$ is read from standard charts as a function of $P = (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out})/(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})$ and $R = (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})/(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})$.
- $F < 1$ indicates the configuration is not a perfect counterflow. If $F$ falls below ~0.75, multiple shell passes are recommended.

**Marking Split:**
- Derivation: 4 marks.
- Explanation of $F$ factor: 3 marks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Draw the **P&ID of a shell-and-tube heat exchanger** used to preheat crude oil using a hot diesel stream. Show all instrumentation for temperature, pressure, and flow on both sides. Use standard ISA symbols. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **PID controller** algorithm. State the role of each of the three modes (P, I, D) and explain what **offset** is and how integral action eliminates it. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) P&ID Drawing (7 marks):**

The student must draw (and label) the following elements:
- **Heat Exchanger E-101** (square with internal tube/shell symbol or "1 pass shell / 2 pass tube" notation).
- **Hot side:** Hot Diesel In → E-101 → Hot Diesel Out, with **TT-101** (inlet) and **TT-102** (outlet), **PT-103** and **PT-104** (inlet/outlet pressure).
- **Cold side:** Crude In → E-101 → Crude Out, with **TT-201**, **TT-202**, **FT-203** (flow transmitter, with orifice plate **FE-203**), and a **control valve FCV-203** on the cold-side inlet line. **PT-205** and **PT-206** for pressure drop monitoring.
- **Temperature control loop:** TT-202 → TIC-202 → FCV-203 (single-loop temperature control on crude outlet).
- **Bubbles:** Circle = field-mounted; circle with horizontal line = DCS.

**Marking Split (typical KTU):**
- Correct heat exchanger symbol and piping: 2 marks.
- Instrumentation on hot side: 1.5 marks.
- Instrumentation on cold side with control loop: 2.5 marks.
- ISA tag format and bubble convention: 1 mark.

**(b) PID Algorithm (7 marks):**

The continuous-time PID controller output is:
$$ m(t) = K_c \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{\tau_I}\int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + \tau_D \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right] $$

- **Proportional (P):** Provides immediate corrective action proportional to current error. Higher $K_c$ = faster response but more oscillation. Leaves a **steady-state offset**.
- **Integral (I):** Accumulates past error. Ensures that the controller output keeps adjusting until the error is exactly zero. **Eliminates offset**. Setting too small $\tau_I$ causes slow response; too large causes instability.
- **Derivative (D):** Reacts to the *rate of change* of error. Provides anticipatory/damping action. Improves stability and reduces overshoot but is sensitive to noise.

**Offset and Integral Action:**
- In pure P-control, a sustained disturbance produces a permanent offset:
$$ \text{offset} = \frac{1}{1 + K_c K_p} $$
- Integral action makes the loop gain infinite at $s=0$ (steady state), forcing the long-term error to zero. The price is a possible **integrator windup**, mitigated by anti-windup algorithms.

**Marking Split:**
- Equation of PID: 2 marks.
- Explanation of P, I, D: 3 marks.
- Offset and its elimination: 2 marks.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in This Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing "Transmitter" (TT) with "Thermocouple/RTD" (TE/TT element):** The element is the sensor; the transmitter conditions the signal. Drawing the wrong one loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting to show "root valves" on instrument tappings:** Although not always marked, KTU examiners expect impulse piping & root valves for completeness in heat-exchanger P&ID questions.
> 3. **Using "P-only" controller in crude preheater questions:** The expected answer is **PI or PID**; pure P leaves an unacceptable temperature offset and is *not* used in refinery temperature loops.
> 4. **Drawing the LMTD equation without the $F$ factor:** If the configuration is 1-2 (one shell pass, two tube passes), you *must* apply the correction factor.
> 5. **Confusing "Feedback" with "Feedforward":** Feedback reacts *after* the error occurs. Feedforward acts *before* the disturbance affects the process. Examiners will deduct a mark if these are swapped.
> 6. **Writing "T" instead of "TT" in the bubble:** ISA convention demands **First Letter + Suffix**, e.g., T**T** 101, T**I**C 101, T**C**V 101. Writing just "T 101" loses a mark.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Piping:** Refinery arteries; sized using $Q = Av$; pressure drop via Darcy–Weisbach; ASME B31.3 governs.
- **Heat Exchanger:** Energy balance $\dot Q = \dot m c_p \Delta T$; rating by LMTD with $F$-factor; fouling resistance $R_f$ must be added.
- **Key HX instrumentation:** TT, PT, FT on inlet/outlet of each side; LSH on channel head; FCV/TCV on the controlled stream.
- **P&ID:** The instrument engineer's "language"; ISA 5.1 symbology is mandatory for KTU 2024.
- **Bubble convention:** Circle = field; circle-with-line = DCS; circle-with-two-lines = PLC/computer function.
- **ISA tag format:** First letter = measured variable (T, P, F, L, A, X, E, Q, S, H); succeeding letters = I (indicate), C (control), R (record), T (transmit), V (valve), S (switch), H/L (high/low).
- **Feedback control:** $e(t) = SP - PV$; controller computes $m(t)$; final control element adjusts MV; loop rejects disturbances.
- **PID equation:** $m(t) = K_c e + (K_c/\tau_I)\int e\,dt + K_c \tau_D\, de/dt$.
- **P-only leaves offset**; I-action eliminates it; D-action adds anticipation/damping.
- **Closed-loop TF:** $G_{CL}(s) = G_c G_v G_p / (1 + G_c G_v G_p G_m)$.
- **Negative vs positive feedback:** Negative = stabilizing (used in 99% of process control); positive = destabilizing (used only in oscillators, never in refinery loops).
- **Cascade control:** Master (TIC) resets slave (FIC) setpoint — used when secondary disturbance is faster than primary.
- **Signal standards:** 3–15 psi (pneumatic) and 4–20 mA (electronic) — zero in mA means 0% of range, not 4 mA.
- **KTU favourite exam questions:** "Draw P&ID of a heat exchanger"; "Explain LMTD method"; "Compare P, PI, PID with one example each"; "Explain offset and how I-action removes it."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feed-forward Control and Cascade. Importance of Instrumentation in the Petroleum Sector. Pressure Measurement - Types of Pressure (Gauge
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET861 - INSTRUMENTATION IN PETROCHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Feed-forward Control and Cascade. Importance of Instrumentation in the Petroleum Sector. Pressure Measurement - Types of Pressure (Gauge

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

## 1. Feed-forward Control

### Formal Academic Definition
**Feed-forward Control** is an advanced process control strategy in which the controller measures one or more *disturbance variables* (load variables) **before** they enter the process, calculates the required corrective action based on a process model, and applies the correction proactively — as opposed to waiting for the controlled variable to deviate from its setpoint.

Mathematically, the controller output is computed as:

$$u_{FF}(t) = -G_{FF}(s) \cdot D(s)$$

where $D(s)$ is the measured disturbance and $G_{FF}(s)$ is the feed-forward controller transfer function derived from the process model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (KTU Board Focus):** Feedback control acts **after** the error occurs (reactive), while feed-forward control acts **before** the disturbance affects the process (proactive). True feed-forward requires an **accurate process model** to be effective.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine you are walking with a cup of hot coffee. You **see** a bump coming on the road ahead. Before your foot even touches the bump, you tilt the cup slightly to anticipate the splash. This is **feed-forward** — you sensed the disturbance *upcoming* and acted in advance. Compare this to *feedback*: you spill coffee, see the spill, and then mop it up (too late!). In a distillation column, if you know the feed flow rate is about to increase, you can preemptively increase the steam flow to the reboiler — this is feed-forward control in action.

---

## 2. Cascade Control

### Formal Academic Definition
**Cascade Control** is a multiple-loop control strategy in which the output of one (primary/master) controller serves as the **setpoint** of another (secondary/slave) controller. The secondary loop is internal to the primary loop and is designed to be **faster** than the primary loop, thereby rejecting local disturbances before they propagate to the main process variable.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a captain steering a giant oil tanker. The captain (primary controller) sets the desired course (heading). The helmsman (secondary controller) actually turns the rudder to achieve that heading. If a sudden wave pushes the rudder, the helmsman corrects it instantly without the captain even noticing. The captain focuses on the *big picture* (longitude/latitude), while the helmsman handles *fast local disturbances*. This two-tier hierarchy is exactly cascade control in a petroleum refining process.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Cascade control is **single-input, single-output (SISO)** in each loop, but the *combined* system is **multi-loop (MISO/MIMO-like)**. The slave loop must be **at least 3–5 times faster** than the master loop for stability.

---

## 3. Importance of Instrumentation in the Petroleum Sector

### Formal Definition
Instrumentation in the petroleum sector refers to the deployment of sensors, transmitters, controllers, valves, and analytical devices for the **measurement, monitoring, and automatic control** of physical and chemical variables (pressure, temperature, flow, level, composition) throughout the entire hydrocarbon value chain — from exploration and drilling to refining, storage, and distribution.

### Key Reasons for Instrumentation
1. **Safety** — Prevention of explosions, fires, and toxic releases in hydrocarbon handling.
2. **Product Quality** — Ensuring gasoline octane, diesel cetane, and LNG purity meet specifications.
3. **Process Optimization** — Maximizing yield of valuable fractions (naphtha, kerosene, diesel).
4. **Regulatory Compliance** — Meeting EPA, OSHA, and pollution control norms.
5. **Energy Efficiency** — Minimizing fuel, steam, and electrical consumption in distillation columns.
6. **Asset Protection** — Preventing damage to expensive catalysts, furnace tubes, and compressors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Petrochemical plants contain thousands of instruments.** A single catalytic cracking unit (FCCU) may have **3,000–5,000 measurement points**, all networked via **Distributed Control Systems (DCS)** like Honeywell Experion, Emerson DeltaV, or Yokogawa CENTUM.

---

## 4. Pressure Measurement — Types of Pressure (Gauge Focus)

### Formal Definition
**Pressure** is defined as the normal force exerted per unit area by a fluid on a surface. In the SI system, the unit is **Pascal (Pa = N/m²)**, with **1 bar = 100 kPa = 10⁵ Pa**.

In industrial instrumentation, pressure is classified into **three fundamental types**:

| Type | Reference Point | Common Use |
|------|----------------|------------|
| **Absolute Pressure (P_abs)** | Perfect vacuum (zero reference) | Thermodynamic calculations |
| **Gauge Pressure (P_gauge)** | Local atmospheric pressure | Most industrial gauges |
| **Differential Pressure (DP)** | Difference between two points | Flow measurement, level measurement |

The relationship is:

$$P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** When a pressure gauge reads "0" at atmospheric conditions, it is a **gauge pressure** gauge. When it reads "1.013 bar" at the same conditions, it is an **absolute** pressure gauge. Always check whether vacuum is being measured: negative gauge pressure = **vacuum pressure**.

### Conceptual Analogy
Consider a sealed soda bottle. The pressure pushing outward on the bottle walls (above and beyond normal air) is the **gauge pressure**. If you also add the normal atmospheric air pressure pushing inward, you get the **absolute pressure**. The difference between inside and outside is what makes the bottle feel "hard" — that is the **differential pressure**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Gauge Pressure is the Industrial Standard:** Most process fluids are stored in vessels open to the atmosphere (or vented), so the operating pressure of interest is the **excess over atmospheric**. Hence, **Bourdon tubes, diaphragm gauges, and most transmitters are calibrated in gauge units** (bar(g), psig, kPa(g)).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 1. Feed-forward Control — Detailed Analysis

### Operating Principle (Step-by-Step)
1. A **disturbance sensor** continuously monitors the load variable $D(s)$ (e.g., feed flow rate, feed temperature, feed composition).
2. The signal is sent to a **feed-forward controller** $G_{FF}(s)$ (often a lead-lag compensator).
3. The controller computes a **manipulated variable correction** $u_{FF}(s)$ based on a process model.
4. The correction is added to the feedback controller output and applied to the final control element.
5. **Goal:** The correction should reach the process **at the same time and with the same magnitude** as the disturbance effect, achieving **perfect cancellation**.

### Block Diagram Transfer Function (Combined FF + FB)

$$Y(s) = \frac{G_p(s) \cdot G_v(s) \cdot G_c(s)}{1 + G_p(s) \cdot G_v(s) \cdot G_c(s) \cdot G_m(s)} \cdot R(s) + \frac{G_d(s) + G_{FF}(s) \cdot G_v(s) \cdot G_p(s)}{1 + G_p(s) \cdot G_v(s) \cdot G_c(s) \cdot G_m(s)} \cdot D(s)$$

For **ideal disturbance rejection**, the numerator of the disturbance term must be zero:

$$G_d(s) + G_{FF}(s) \cdot G_v(s) \cdot G_p(s) = 0$$

Solving for $G_{FF}(s)$:

$$G_{FF}(s) = -\frac{G_d(s)}{G_v(s) \cdot G_p(s)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **ideal feed-forward controller**. In practice, model mismatch causes the cancellation to be imperfect, so feed-forward is **always combined with feedback** to trim the residual error.

### Why "Lead-Lag" is Used in Practice
Because the disturbance dynamics and process dynamics have different time constants, the ideal controller is approximated as:

$$G_{FF}(s) = -K_{FF} \cdot \frac{\tau_1 s + 1}{\tau_2 s + 1}$$

where $K_{FF}$ is the **steady-state gain**, and $\tau_1, \tau_2$ are the **lead and lag** time constants that compensate for dynamic mismatch.

---

## 2. Cascade Control — Detailed Analysis

### Loop Hierarchy
- **Primary (Master/Slow) Loop:** Controls the main process variable (e.g., outlet temperature of a reactor).
- **Secondary (Slave/Fast) Loop:** Controls an intermediate variable (e.g., jacket coolant flow or fuel gas pressure).
- **Inner loop bandwidth must be ≥ 3× outer loop bandwidth** for proper cascade operation.

### Block Diagram Analysis

Let the secondary loop consist of process $G_{p2}(s)$, valve $G_{v2}(s)$, and controller $G_{c2}(s)$, with secondary measurement $H_2(s)$.

The **closed-loop transfer function of the inner loop** is:

$$G_{inner}(s) = \frac{G_{c2}(s) \cdot G_{v2}(s) \cdot G_{p2}(s)}{1 + G_{c2}(s) \cdot G_{v2}(s) \cdot G_{p2}(s) \cdot H_2(s)}$$

This $G_{inner}(s)$ becomes an **equivalent block** in the outer loop. The outer loop controller $G_{c1}(s)$ then controls the primary process $G_{p1}(s)$ through this inner block.

### Tuning Procedure
1. **Close** the secondary loop; put the primary controller in **manual**.
2. Tune the secondary loop using Ziegler-Nichols or SIMC rules.
3. Once secondary loop is stable and fast, **switch primary to auto**.
4. Tune the primary controller as if the inner loop is part of the process.
5. **Recheck** the secondary loop tuning for any interaction effects.

---

## 3. Pressure Measurement — Detailed Theory

### Fundamental Equation of Hydrostatics

$$P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

where $P$ is hydrostatic pressure, $\rho$ is fluid density, $g$ is gravitational acceleration (**9.81 m/s²**), and $h$ is the height of the fluid column.

### Conversion Between Pressure Types

$$P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$$

$$P_{vacuum} = P_{atm} - P_{abs} \quad \text{(when } P_{abs} < P_{atm}\text{)}$$

$$P_{differential} = P_{high} - P_{low}$$

### Standard Atmospheric Reference
**1 standard atmosphere = 101.325 kPa = 1.01325 bar = 14.696 psi = 760 mmHg = 760 torr**

---

## 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $u_{FF}(t) = -G_{FF}(s) \cdot D(s)$ | Feed-forward controller output | varies |
| 2 | $G_{FF}(s) = -\frac{G_d(s)}{G_v(s) \cdot G_p(s)}$ | Ideal FF compensator | dimensionless/s |
| 3 | $G_{FF}(s) = -K_{FF} \cdot \frac{\tau_1 s + 1}{\tau_2 s + 1}$ | Practical lead-lag FF | dimensionless |
| 4 | $G_{inner}(s) = \frac{G_{c2} G_{v2} G_{p2}}{1 + G_{c2} G_{v2} G_{p2} H_2}$ | Inner loop TF of cascade | dimensionless |
| 5 | $f_{slave} \geq 3 \cdot f_{master}$ | Bandwidth rule for cascade | Hz |
| 6 | $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$ | Gauge to absolute conversion | Pa, bar, psi |
| 7 | $P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ | Hydrostatic pressure | Pa |
| 8 | $P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$ | Vacuum calculation | Pa |
| 9 | $1 \text{ atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$ | Standard atmosphere | kPa |
| 10 | $1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 14.504 \text{ psi}$ | Bar conversion | Pa, psi |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Feed-forward + cascade combination is the **backbone of distillation column control** in refineries. Feed-forward handles feed rate swings; cascade on reflux drum level handles internal disturbances. Without these, modern refineries cannot meet product specifications economically.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: Ideal Feed-Forward Controller Transfer Function

### Given
A process with disturbance transfer function $G_d(s)$, process transfer function $G_p(s)$, valve transfer function $G_v(s)$, and feed-forward controller $G_{FF}(s)$. The disturbance enters additively into the process.

### Step 1: Write the process output equation
The total output of the process is the sum of the contributions from the controller action and the disturbance:

$$Y(s) = G_p(s) \cdot G_v(s) \cdot U_{FF}(s) + G_d(s) \cdot D(s)$$

Here, $U_{FF}(s)$ is the controller output and $D(s)$ is the disturbance. The controller has not yet been defined.

### Step 2: Express the controller output
The feed-forward controller is a transfer function acting on the disturbance:

$$U_{FF}(s) = G_{FF}(s) \cdot D(s)$$

### Step 3: Substitute into the output equation
Replace $U_{FF}(s)$ in the output equation:

$$Y(s) = G_p(s) \cdot G_v(s) \cdot G_{FF}(s) \cdot D(s) + G_d(s) \cdot D(s)$$

### Step 4: Factor out the disturbance term
Group the terms with $D(s)$ together:

$$Y(s) = \left[G_p(s) \cdot G_v(s) \cdot G_{FF}(s) + G_d(s)\right] \cdot D(s)$$

### Step 5: Apply the perfect cancellation condition
For the disturbance to have **zero effect** on the output, the term in brackets must be zero:

$$G_p(s) \cdot G_v(s) \cdot G_{FF}(s) + G_d(s) = 0$$

### Step 6: Solve for $G_{FF}(s)$
Rearrange the equation to isolate $G_{FF}(s)$:

$$G_p(s) \cdot G_v(s) \cdot G_{FF}(s) = -G_d(s)$$

Divide both sides by $G_p(s) \cdot G_v(s)$:

$$G_{FF}(s) = -\frac{G_d(s)}{G_p(s) \cdot G_v(s)}$$

### Step 7: Interpretation
The minus sign indicates that the controller action must be **opposite in sign** to the natural disturbance effect. The controller effectively **inverts the disturbance dynamics** and scales them by the inverse of the actuator and process gain.

> [!NOTE]
> This ideal $G_{FF}(s)$ is often **non-proper** (more zeros than poles) and hence unrealizable physically. A practical approximation uses a **lead-lag block** with a steady-state gain equal to $-G_d(0) / [G_p(0) \cdot G_v(0)]$ and dynamics tuned to match time constants.

---

## Derivation 2: Closed-Loop Transfer Function of a Cascade System

### Given
- Outer loop: controller $G_{c1}(s)$, process $G_{p1}(s)$, measurement $H_1(s)$
- Inner loop: controller $G_{c2}(s)$, process $G_{p2}(s)$, measurement $H_2(s)$
- The output of $G_{c1}$ becomes the setpoint of the inner loop.

### Step 1: Write the inner loop equation
The inner loop output $Y_2(s)$ responds to its setpoint $R_2(s)$:

$$Y_2(s) = \frac{G_{c2}(s) \cdot G_{p2}(s)}{1 + G_{c2}(s) \cdot G_{p2}(s) \cdot H_2(s)} \cdot R_2(s)$$

Define this ratio as $G_{inner}(s)$:

$$G_{inner}(s) = \frac{G_{c2}(s) \cdot G_{p2}(s)}{1 + G_{c2}(s) \cdot G_{p2}(s) \cdot H_2(s)}$$

### Step 2: Substitute into the outer loop
The outer loop sees $G_{inner}(s)$ as its actuator-process combination:

$$Y_1(s) = G_{p1}(s) \cdot G_{inner}(s) \cdot G_{c1}(s) \cdot E_1(s)$$

where $E_1(s) = R_1(s) - H_1(s) \cdot Y_1(s)$.

### Step 3: Substitute the error
Replace $E_1(s)$ in the output equation:

$$Y_1(s) = G_{p1}(s) \cdot G_{inner}(s) \cdot G_{c1}(s) \cdot \left[R_1(s) - H_1(s) \cdot Y_1(s)\right]$$

### Step 4: Expand the right-hand side
Distribute the multiplication:

$$Y_1(s) = G_{p1}(s) \cdot G_{inner}(s) \cdot G_{c1}(s) \cdot R_1(s) - G_{p1}(s) \cdot G_{inner}(s) \cdot G_{c1}(s) \cdot H_1(s) \cdot Y_1(s)$$

### Step 5: Collect $Y_1(s)$ terms
Move the $Y_1(s)$ term from the right to the left:

$$Y_1(s) + G_{p1}(s) \cdot G_{inner}(s) \cdot G_{c1}(s) \cdot H_1(s) \cdot Y_1(s) = G_{p1}(s) \cdot G_{inner}(s) \cdot G_{c1}(s) \cdot R_1(s)$$

Factor $Y_1(s)$:

$$Y_1(s) \cdot \left[1 + G_{p1}(s) \cdot G_{inner}(s) \cdot G_{c1}(s) \cdot H_1(s)\right] = G_{p1}(s) \cdot G_{inner}(s) \cdot G_{c1}(s) \cdot R_1(s)$$

### Step 6: Solve for the closed-loop transfer function
Divide both sides to isolate $Y_1(s)/R_1(s)$:

$$\frac{Y_1(s)}{R_1(s)} = \frac{G_{p1}(s) \cdot G_{inner}(s) \cdot G_{c1}(s)}{1 + G_{p1}(s) \cdot G_{inner}(s) \cdot G_{c1}(s) \cdot H_1(s)}$$

### Step 7: Substitute $G_{inner}(s)$

$$\frac{Y_1(s)}{R_1(s)} = \frac{G_{p1}(s) \cdot G_{c1}(s) \cdot \frac{G_{c2} G_{p2}}{1 + G_{c2} G_{p2} H_2}}{1 + G_{p1}(s) \cdot G_{c1}(s) \cdot H_1(s) \cdot \frac{G_{c2} G_{p2}}{1 + G_{c2} G_{p2} H_2}}$$

### Step 8: Final simplified form
Multiply numerator and denominator by $(1 + G_{c2} G_{p2} H_2)$:

$$\frac{Y_1(s)}{R_1(s)} = \frac{G_{p1} G_{c1} G_{c2} G_{p2}}{(1 + G_{c2} G_{p2} H_2) + G_{p1} G_{c1} G_{c2} G_{p2} H_1}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The characteristic equation now has **higher order** and the inner loop acts as a **low-pass filter**, attenuating high-frequency disturbances reaching the outer loop. This is the mathematical reason why cascade control is **superior to single-loop feedback** for disturbances inside the inner loop.

---

## Derivation 3: Pressure Type Conversions

### Problem
A pressure gauge on a LPG storage vessel reads **8.5 bar(g)**. The local barometer reads **1.013 bar**. Calculate the absolute pressure inside the vessel in **kPa, psi, and mmHg**.

### Step 1: Convert gauge to absolute
Use the relation $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$:

$$P_{abs} = 8.5 + 1.013 = 9.513 \text{ bar}$$

### Step 2: Convert bar to kPa
Since **1 bar = 100 kPa**:

$$P_{abs} = 9.513 \times 100 = 951.3 \text{ kPa}$$

### Step 3: Convert bar to psi
Since **1 bar = 14.504 psi**:

$$P_{abs} = 9.513 \times 14.504 = 137.99 \text{ psi}$$

### Step 4: Convert bar to mmHg
Since **1 bar = 750.06 mmHg**:

$$P_{abs} = 9.513 \times 750.06 = 7,135.5 \text{ mmHg}$$

### Step 5: Result summary

$$P_{abs} = 9.513 \text{ bar} = 951.3 \text{ kPa} = 137.99 \text{ psi} = 7{,}135.5 \text{ mmHg}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing "bar" with "bar(g)" or "bar(a)". A **bar(g) value of 0** does NOT mean zero absolute pressure — it means atmospheric. Always write the units in full to avoid KTU valuation marks being deducted.

---

## Derivation 4: Hydrostatic Pressure for Tank Level

### Problem
A vertical cylindrical oil storage tank contains crude oil of density **$\rho$ = 850 kg/m³**. The oil column height is **$h$ = 12 m**. Find the hydrostatic gauge pressure at the bottom of the tank. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

### Step 1: Apply the hydrostatic equation

$$P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

### Step 2: Substitute numerical values

$$P = 850 \times 9.81 \times 12$$

### Step 3: Perform the multiplication

$$P = 850 \times 9.81 = 8{,}338.5$$

$$P = 8{,}338.5 \times 12 = 100{,}062 \text{ Pa}$$

### Step 4: Convert to bar

$$P = \frac{100{,}062}{10^5} = 1.0006 \text{ bar(g)}$$

### Step 5: Final answer

$$P_{bottom} = 100.06 \text{ kPa(g)} = 1.0006 \text{ bar(g)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This principle is the **basis of hydrostatic tank gauging (HTG)**, a non-intrusive level measurement technique widely used in petroleum storage tanks where intrusive probes are avoided (especially for crude oil, asphalt, or sour crude).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1: Feed-forward Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1[Disturbance Source]:::dist --> DS[Disturbance Sensor]
    DS --> FFC[Feed-forward Controller Gff]
    FFC --> SUM[Summer Node]
    FBC[Feedback Controller Gc] --> SUM
    SUM --> FCV[Final Control Valve]
    FCV --> PROC[Process Gp]
    D1 --> PROC
    PROC --> PV[Process Variable Y]
    PV --> MS[Measurement Sensor Hm]
    MS --> COMP[Comparator]
    SP[Setpoint R] --> COMP
    COMP --> FBC
    PV2[Controlled Output]:::out --> PV

    classDef dist fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef out fill:#e5ffe5,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px
```

**Interpretation:** The disturbance is sensed in **parallel** with the process. The feed-forward path acts on the disturbance **before** it disturbs the process, while the feedback path trims the residual error. This **combined FF+FB architecture** is the **industry standard** because pure feed-forward is sensitive to model error.

---

## Diagram 2: Cascade Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SP[Setpoint R1] --> CMP1[Comparator 1]
    PV1[Primary Variable Y1] --> MS1[Primary Sensor H1]
    MS1 --> CMP1
    CMP1 --> PC[Primary Controller Gc1]
    PC --> SST[Secondary Setpoint R2]
    D2[Inner Loop Disturbance]:::dist --> SP2
    SST --> CMP2[Comparator 2]
    PV2[Secondary Variable Y2] --> MS2[Secondary Sensor H2]
    MS2 --> CMP2
    CMP2 --> SC[Secondary Controller Gc2]
    SC --> FCV2[Secondary Valve Gv2]
    FCV2 --> PP2[Secondary Process Gp2]
    SP2 --> PP2
    D2 --> PP2
    PP2 --> PV2
    PP2 --> PP1[Primary Process Gp1]
    PP1 --> PV1

    classDef dist fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#b38600,stroke-width:2px
```

**Interpretation:** The **inner (secondary) loop** is closed around an intermediate variable (e.g., fuel gas pressure) that responds quickly. The **outer (primary) loop** controls the main process variable (e.g., furnace tube temperature). Disturbances entering the inner loop are rejected **before** they reach the outer loop, dramatically improving control quality.

---

## Diagram 3: Block-Level Architecture of a Petroleum Refinery Instrumentation System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Field["Field Level - Hazardous Area Zone 1/2"]
        PT[Pressure Transmitter]:::field
        TT[Temperature Transmitter]:::field
        FT[Flow Transmitter]:::field
        LT[Level Transmitter]:::field
        AT[Analyzer AT - Octane, Sulfur]:::field
        XV[Control Valves + Solenoids]:::field
    end

    subgraph Control["Control Level - Safe Area Control Room"]
        DCS[DCS Controller - PID, Cascade, FF]:::control
        HIS[Operator HMI Workstations]:::control
        ENG[Engineering Station]:::control
    end

    subgraph Mgmt["Management Level"]
        MES[Manufacturing Execution System]:::mgmt
        ERP[Enterprise Resource Planning]:::mgmt
    end

    subgraph Safety["Safety Layer - SIS"]
        ESD[Emergency Shutdown System IEC 61508 SIL]:::safety
        FGS[Fire and Gas Detection System]:::safety
    end

    PT --> DCS
    TT --> DCS
    FT --> DCS
    LT --> DCS
    AT --> DCS
    DCS --> XV
    DCS --> HIS
    HIS --> ENG
    DCS --> MES
    MES --> ERP
    PT --> ESD
    TT --> ESD
    AT --> FGS

    classDef field fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#e5f0ff,stroke:#003399,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mgmt fill:#e5ffe5,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef safety fill:#fff0cc,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Interpretation:** This is the classic **ISA-95 / Purdue Reference Model** for petroleum plant automation. Field instruments (intrinsically safe, Ex-proof) feed the DCS, which executes advanced control strategies (cascade, feed-forward, MPC). The **Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** is **independent** from the DCS — it acts only in emergencies to bring the plant to a safe state.

---

## Diagram 4: Pressure Measurement System for a Distillation Column

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    COL[Distillation Column Overhead] --> IT1[Impulse Line SS316]
    IT1 --> DP[DP Cell / Pressure Tap]
    DP --> TR[Smart Pressure Transmitter 4-20 mA + HART]
    TR --> JB[Junction Box Ex-proof]
    JB --> DCS[DCS Controller - PID Loop]
    DCS --> CV[Control Valve on Reflux Line]
    CV --> COL
    TR -.HART.-> CM[Calibration/Asset Management]
    DCS --> OP[Operator Display P, T, Flow, Level]
```

**Interpretation:** Pressure is sensed at the column overhead, transmitted through an **impulse line** (filled with condensate to protect the diaphragm from hot vapors), converted to a 4–20 mA signal with HART digital protocol, and sent to the DCS. The DCS uses it in **cascade with temperature** (master: temperature, slave: pressure) and also in **feed-forward** for feed composition changes.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
**Differentiate between feed-forward and feedback control strategies. State one industrial application of each in a petroleum refinery.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Feedback Control | Feed-forward Control |
|--------|------------------|----------------------|
| Action basis | Acts on **error** between setpoint and measured variable | Acts on **measured disturbance** before it affects the process |
| Timing | **Reactive** (after error occurs) | **Proactive** (before error occurs) |
| Model dependence | Does **not** require a process model | **Requires** an accurate process model |
| Steady-state error | **Zero** (with integral action) | **Non-zero** (model mismatch) |
| Implementation | Single loop, simpler | Requires extra sensor + model |

**Petro-refinery applications:**
- **Feedback:** Level control in a reflux drum (PID on level → reflux valve).
- **Feed-forward:** Adjusting reboiler steam flow in response to a measured change in feed flow rate to a distillation column.

> [!TIP]
> For full 3 marks, students must include **at least 2 differences** and **one industrial example**. KTU examiners award 1 mark per valid difference and 1 mark for the example.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supplementary]**
**Define gauge pressure and absolute pressure. A pressure gauge on a crude oil pipeline reads 12.5 bar(g). If the local atmospheric pressure is 1.013 bar, calculate the absolute pressure in kPa and psi.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definitions (1 mark):**
- **Gauge pressure** is the pressure measured **relative to local atmospheric pressure**; zero on the gauge corresponds to atmospheric.
- **Absolute pressure** is the pressure measured **relative to a perfect vacuum**; zero corresponds to no pressure at all.

**Calculation (2 marks):**
- Step 1: $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm} = 12.5 + 1.013 = 13.513 \text{ bar}$
- Step 2: Convert to kPa: $P_{abs} = 13.513 \times 100 = 1{,}351.3 \text{ kPa}$
- Step 3: Convert to psi: $P_{abs} = 13.513 \times 14.504 = 196.0 \text{ psi}$

$$\boxed{P_{abs} = 13.513 \text{ bar} = 1{,}351.3 \text{ kPa} = 196.0 \text{ psi}}$$

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 2 Compulsory + Internal Choice Pattern]**

**a)** Explain the working principle of a **cascade control system** with a suitable block diagram. Clearly identify the primary and secondary loops. State the rule of thumb for selecting the secondary variable. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A catalytic cracking unit (FCCU) has a **feed-forward + feedback** control scheme for the riser outlet temperature. The feed flow rate is the measured disturbance. Given:
- Process gain from feed flow to riser temperature: $G_d = -0.8 \text{ °C/(kg/s)}$
- Process gain from steam flow to riser temperature: $G_p = +1.2 \text{ °C/(kg/s)}$
- Valve gain: $G_v = 1.0$
- Time constant of disturbance path: $\tau_d = 60 \text{ s}$
- Time constant of process path: $\tau_p = 30 \text{ s}$

Design a **steady-state feed-forward controller** and a **lead-lag dynamic compensator**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Cascade Control Explanation (7 marks)

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
Cascade control uses **two nested control loops**. The output of the primary (master) controller is **not** sent directly to the final control element; instead, it becomes the **setpoint** of the secondary (slave) controller. The secondary loop then controls an intermediate (secondary) variable that is closely related to the primary process variable. The secondary loop must be **significantly faster** (typically 3–5 times) than the primary loop so that it can reject local disturbances before they propagate to the primary loop.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**
*(See Diagram 2 in Section 4 of these notes — refer to the cascade control architecture with PC, SC, Gp1, Gp2, H1, H2.)*

**Selection Rule for Secondary Variable (2 marks):**
The secondary variable must satisfy three conditions:
1. It must be **measurable** reliably with a fast-responding sensor.
2. It must be **causally related** to the primary variable (i.e., changes in the secondary variable cause changes in the primary variable).
3. It must be located **closer to the disturbance** than the primary variable, so it can "see" the disturbance earlier.

**Example:** In a furnace, the primary variable is the tube outlet temperature; the secondary variable is the fuel gas pressure (fast, measurable, upstream of the temperature response).

---

#### Part (b) — Feed-Forward Controller Design (7 marks)

**Step 1: Compute the steady-state feed-forward gain**

$$K_{FF} = -\frac{G_d(0)}{G_v(0) \cdot G_p(0)}$$

Substitute the given values:

$$K_{FF} = -\frac{-0.8}{1.0 \times 1.2} = +0.667$$

This means a unit increase in feed flow rate requires a **0.667 unit increase in steam flow** to keep the riser temperature constant.

**Step 2: Determine the lead-lag time constants**

For a **first-order** process and disturbance model:

$$\frac{G_d(s)}{G_p(s)} = \frac{-0.8 / (60s + 1)}{1.2 / (30s + 1)} = -\frac{0.8}{1.2} \cdot \frac{30s + 1}{60s + 1}$$

The ideal $G_{FF}(s)$ from the equation in Section 2 is:

$$G_{FF}(s) = -\frac{G_d(s)}{G_v(s) \cdot G_p(s)} = \frac{0.8}{1.2} \cdot \frac{30s + 1}{60s + 1} = 0.667 \cdot \frac{30s + 1}{60s + 1}$$

**Step 3: Implement the lead-lag compensator**

$$G_{FF}(s) = K_{FF} \cdot \frac{\tau_{lead} s + 1}{\tau_{lag} s + 1} = 0.667 \cdot \frac{30s + 1}{60s + 1}$$

where:
- $\tau_{lead} = 30 \text{ s}$ (matches the process time constant for zero-lag compensation)
- $\tau_{lag} = 60 \text{ s}$ (matches the disturbance time constant)
- $K_{FF} = +0.667$ (steady-state ratio of process gains, sign-corrected)

**Step 4: Practical implementation note**
This compensator is **proper and realizable**. In a DCS function block, it is implemented using a standard LEAD-LAG block with gain = 0.667, lead time = 30 s, and lag time = 60 s.

**Step 5: Verification**
At steady state ($s = 0$): $G_{FF}(0) = 0.667 \cdot (1/1) = 0.667$ ✓
For a step change in feed flow of $\Delta D = 1$ kg/s, the FF controller output is:

$$\Delta u_{FF} = 0.667 \times 1 = 0.667 \text{ kg/s of steam}$$

This exactly compensates for the $\Delta T = -0.8$ °C drop, restoring the temperature.

$$\boxed{G_{FF}(s) = 0.667 \cdot \frac{30s + 1}{60s + 1}}$$

---

### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary Model]**

**a)** Discuss the **importance of instrumentation** in the petroleum sector. List **six major areas** where instrumentation plays a critical role. **(7 marks)**

**b)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of a **Bourdon tube pressure gauge** for measuring gauge pressure. A Bourdon tube is connected to a natural gas pipeline carrying gas at **35 bar(g)**. The local atmospheric pressure is **1.02 bar**. Determine the absolute pressure in **kPa, MPa, and mmH₂O** (density of mercury = 13,600 kg/m³, $g$ = 9.81 m/s²). **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Importance of Instrumentation (7 marks)

Instrumentation is the **nervous system** of any modern petroleum refinery or petrochemical plant. It performs six critical functions:

1. **Safety and Personnel Protection (1.5 marks):** Hydrocarbons are highly flammable; instrumentation provides early warning of leaks, overpressure, overtemperature, and explosive atmospheres. SIS (Safety Instrumented Systems) shut down equipment before catastrophic failure. Standards: **IEC 61508 / IEC 61511 (SIL ratings)**.

2. **Product Quality and Specification Compliance (1 mark):** Refined products (gasoline, diesel, jet fuel) must meet tight specifications (octane number, sulfur content, flash point). Online analyzers (NIR, GC, sulfur analyzers) provide real-time feedback for quality control.

3. **Process Optimization and Yield Maximization (1 mark):** Advanced control (MPC, cascade, feed-forward) increases yield of valuable products, reduces energy consumption, and minimizes giveaway of product specifications.

4. **Environmental Compliance (1 mark):** Continuous Emission Monitoring Systems (CEMS) measure SO₂, NOₓ, particulates, and CO₂ in flue gas stacks. Storage tank vapor recovery systems use level and pressure sensors.

5. **Asset Protection and Predictive Maintenance (1 mark):** Vibration, temperature, and acoustic emission sensors on rotating equipment (pumps, compressors) enable condition monitoring and prevent unscheduled downtime.

6. **Operational Visibility and Decision Support (1.5 marks):** DCS, SCADA, and alarm management systems give operators a clear real-time view of the entire plant, enabling informed decision-making and reducing operator error.

---

#### Part (b) — Bourdon Tube Gauge + Pressure Calculation (7 marks)

**Working of a Bourdon Tube Gauge (3.5 marks):**

A Bourdon tube is a **flattened, curved metal tube** (usually C-shaped, helical, or spiral) sealed at one end and connected to the process fluid at the other. When the internal pressure rises above atmospheric:

1. The flattened cross-section **tends to become circular** (because a circle has a higher moment of inertia than an ellipse).
2. This cross-section change causes the **curved tube to straighten slightly** (try to become less curved).
3. The **free (closed) end** of the tube moves by a small displacement, typically 2–5 mm.
4. This motion is amplified by a **link-and-lever mechanism** and transmitted to a **pointer** moving over a calibrated dial.
5. The dial is graduated directly in **gauge pressure units** (bar(g), psig, kPa(g)) because the **outside of the tube is at atmospheric pressure** — so the net force is the gauge pressure.

**Diagram (3.5 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Process Fluid Inlet] --> T1[Bourdon Tube C-shaped]
    T1 --> M[Link Mechanism]
    M --> G[Gear Train Amplifier]
    G --> PT[Pointer]
    PT --> D[Calibrated Dial in bar-g]
    A[Atmospheric Reference Outside Tube] -.-> T1
```

The Bourdon tube is the most widely used gauge pressure instrument in refineries — robust, simple, intrinsically safe, and inexpensive. Materials are typically **SS316, Monel, or Hastelloy** for corrosive service.

**Pressure Calculation (alternative use of remaining marks within the 7-mark allocation):**

**Step 1: Compute absolute pressure in bar**

$$P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm} = 35 + 1.02 = 36.02 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 2: Convert to kPa**

$$P_{abs} = 36.02 \times 100 = 3{,}602 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3: Convert to MPa**

$$P_{abs} = 36.02 \times 0.1 = 3.602 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Convert to mmHg first, then to mmH₂O**

Since $1 \text{ bar} = 750.06 \text{ mmHg}$:

$$P_{abs} = 36.02 \times 750.06 = 27{,}017.16 \text{ mmHg}$$

Convert mmHg to mmH₂O using the ratio of densities (1 mmHg = 13.6 mmH₂O):

$$P_{abs} = 27{,}017.16 \times 13.6 = 367{,}433.4 \text{ mmH}_2\text{O}$$

$$\boxed{P_{abs} = 36.02 \text{ bar} = 3{,}602 \text{ kPa} = 3.602 \text{ MPa} = 367{,}433 \text{ mmH}_2\text{O}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully:**
> 1. Marks are deducted if you do not show the **formula $P_{abs} = P_g + P_{atm}$** explicitly before substitution.
> 2. Marks are deducted for **unit confusion**: kPa vs kPa(g), bar vs bar(a), MPa vs N/m².
> 3. Marks are deducted if the Bourdon tube diagram **lacks labels** (process inlet, atmospheric reference, pointer direction). A diagram with **at least 5 labels** scores full marks.
> 4. For cascade control questions, students often forget to state the **3–5× bandwidth rule** — this is a direct 1-mark loss.

---

## 📝 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2**

### Feed-forward Control
- ☐ Acts on **measured disturbance** *before* it affects the process.
- ☐ Requires a **process model**: $G_{FF}(s) = -G_d(s) / [G_v(s) G_p(s)]$.
- ☐ Practical form is a **lead-lag block**: $K_{FF} \cdot (\tau_1 s + 1)/(\tau_2 s + 1)$.
- ☐ **Always combined with feedback** to handle model error.
- ☐ Industrial example: **Feed rate to distillation column → steam to reboiler**.

### Cascade Control
- ☐ **Two nested loops**: primary (slow) + secondary (fast).
- ☐ Secondary loop setpoint = primary controller output.
- ☐ **Bandwidth rule:** $f_{slave} \geq 3 f_{master}$.
- ☐ Tuning sequence: **tune inner loop first**, then outer loop.
- ☐ Industrial example: **Reactor temperature (master) + jacket water flow (slave)**.

### Importance of Instrumentation in Petroleum
- ☐ Six pillars: **Safety, Quality, Optimization, Environment, Asset Protection, Visibility**.
- ☐ Standards: **IEC 61508/61511 (SIL)**, **ISA-84**, **API 554 (MPC)**.
- ☐ Refinery has **thousands of I/O points** integrated via **DCS** (Emerson DeltaV, Honeywell Experion, Yokogawa CENTUM).
- ☐ **SIS is independent** from DCS (separate sensors, logic solvers, valves).

### Pressure Measurement
- ☐ **Three types:** Absolute, Gauge, Differential.
- ☐ Relationship: $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$.
- ☐ **1 atm = 101.325 kPa = 1.01325 bar = 760 mmHg = 14.696 psi**.
- ☐ **Gauge pressure** is the **default industrial unit** (bar(g), psig, kPa(g)).
- ☐ **Vacuum pressure** = $P_{atm} - P_{abs}$ (when below atmospheric).
- ☐ Hydrostatic equation: $P = \rho g h$ — used in tank level measurement.
- ☐ Common gauge devices: **Bourdon tube, diaphragm, capsule, bellows, manometer (U-tube, well-type)**.

### Conversion Factors to Memorize
- ☐ 1 bar = 100 kPa = 10⁵ Pa = 14.504 psi
- ☐ 1 atm = 1.01325 bar = 101.325 kPa
- ☐ 1 mmHg = 13.6 mmH₂O (at 0°C, standard gravity)
- ☐ 1 psi = 6.895 kPa

### Common KTU Mark-Losing Mistakes
- ☐ Confusing **bar(g)** with **bar(a)** — always write the full unit.
- ☐ Forgetting the **minus sign** in the FF controller formula.
- ☐ Drawing cascade block diagrams **without labels** on inner/outer loops.
- ☐ Not stating the **3× bandwidth rule** for cascade.
- ☐ Mixing up **absolute and gauge** in numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
