topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET861 - INSTRUMENTATION IN PETROCHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Absolute

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the domain of **Instrumentation in Petrochemical Industries**, the term **"Absolute"** most commonly refers to **Absolute Pressure (P_abs)** — a fundamental measurement parameter in process control and safety instrumentation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Absolute Pressure** is defined as the pressure measured with respect to a **perfect vacuum (zero pressure reference)**. It is the true, intrinsic thermodynamic pressure of a fluid at a given point in a system and is universally accepted as the only pressure scale that does not change with local atmospheric variations, altitude, or weather conditions.

Mathematically, the formal definition is:

$$P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atmospheric}$$

or equivalently:

$$P_{abs} = P_{vacuum} + P_{atmospheric}$$

where:
- $P_{abs}$ = Absolute pressure
- $P_{gauge}$ = Gauge pressure (pressure relative to local atmosphere)
- $P_{atmospheric}$ = Local atmospheric pressure (typically **101.325 kPa** at standard conditions)
- $P_{vacuum}$ = Vacuum pressure (pressure below atmospheric)

> [!NOTE]
> The SI unit of absolute pressure is the **Pascal (Pa)**, and it is often expressed in **kPa**, **MPa**, or **bar (absolute)**. The abbreviation **"bara"** is the internationally accepted notation distinguishing absolute pressure from gauge pressure ("barg").

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview

Imagine you have a completely empty, sealed glass jar — a **perfect vacuum** inside. The air molecules outside the jar are constantly pushing on its walls with a force we call **atmospheric pressure**. Now, if you pump more air *into* the jar, the molecules inside begin to push back. The *total* push exerted by the molecules inside the jar, counted from the zero baseline (the perfect vacuum), is what we call **absolute pressure**.

**Real-world analogy:** Think of measuring the height of a building.
- **Gauge pressure** is like measuring the building's height from the *ground floor up* (the ground floor is your arbitrary reference — like atmospheric pressure).
- **Absolute pressure** is like measuring the building's height from *sea level up* (a fixed, universal reference — like the perfect vacuum).

If the ground floor is elevated (different altitude/atmospheric pressure), the gauge measurement changes, but the absolute measurement (from sea level) remains meaningful and consistent. This is exactly why **engineers, scientists, and safety codes universally use absolute pressure** in thermodynamic calculations, gas law equations, and compressor/turbine performance curves.

## 1.3 Classification of Pressure Measurement

Pressure in any petrochemical process can be classified into **four fundamental categories**:

| # | Pressure Type | Reference Point | Symbol | KTU Notation |
|---|---------------|-----------------|--------|--------------|
| 1 | **Absolute Pressure** | Perfect Vacuum (Zero) | $P_{abs}$ | **bara / psia** |
| 2 | **Gauge Pressure** | Local Atmosphere | $P_{gauge}$ | **barg / psig** |
| 3 | **Vacuum Pressure** | Local Atmosphere (negative) | $P_{vac}$ | **barvac** |
| 4 | **Differential Pressure** | Two arbitrary points | $\Delta P$ | **bard / psid** |

> [!NOTE]
> In **KTU 2024 Scheme examinations**, students are frequently tested on the **conversion relationships** between these four pressure types. A loss of 1–2 marks is common when students confuse **bar(a)** with **bar(g)** in numerical problems.

## 1.4 Why "Absolute" Matters in Petrochemical Industries

In petrochemical plants, the distinction between absolute and gauge pressure is not merely academic — it is **safety-critical** and **legally mandated** under standards such as **API 520/521**, **ASME Section VIII**, and **ISA 75.01.01**.

- **Distillation Columns**: Vapor pressure calculations use absolute pressure.
- **Compressors**: Performance curves (head vs. flow) are plotted against absolute pressure.
- **Storage Tanks**: Vapor space monitoring uses absolute pressure sensors.
- **Reactor Safety**: Relief valve sizing requires absolute pressure for set-point determination.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Thermodynamic Foundation of Absolute Pressure

Absolute pressure is the **true thermodynamic pressure** because it relates directly to the **Ideal Gas Law**, **Steam Tables**, and all **equation-of-state** formulations. These fundamental physical laws require a **true zero reference**, which only the absolute scale provides.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Any time you see **PV = nRT**, **Boyle's Law**, **Charles's Law**, or **compressibility factor (Z) charts** in a petrochemical calculation, the pressure term **MUST be in absolute units (bara or psia)**. Using gauge pressure in these equations is a guaranteed mark-loss error.

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning

The relationship between absolute, gauge, and vacuum pressure can be understood through the following structured logic:

- **Step 1 — Establish the Reference**: A perfect vacuum represents the complete absence of matter and, hence, zero molecular collision force. This is the **only unambiguous zero** for pressure.
- **Step 2 — Atmospheric Layer**: The Earth's atmosphere exerts a baseline pressure ($P_{atm}$) that varies with altitude, weather, and temperature.
- **Step 3 — Gauge Layer**: Most industrial processes operate *above* atmospheric pressure. The amount by which the process pressure exceeds local atmosphere is **gauge pressure**.
- **Step 4 — Absolute Summation**: The total pressure from the perfect vacuum baseline is **absolute pressure** = $P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$.
- **Step 5 — Vacuum Case**: For processes operating *below* atmospheric pressure, the vacuum gauge reads the *shortfall* from atmosphere, and absolute pressure = $P_{atm} - P_{vacuum}$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units | KTU Frequency |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|---------------|
| 1 | $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$ | Absolute = Gauge + Atmospheric | bar, kPa, psi | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | $P_{abs} = P_{atm} - P_{vac}$ | Absolute = Atmospheric − Vacuum | bar, kPa, psi | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | $P_{gauge} = P_{abs} - P_{atm}$ | Gauge = Absolute − Atmospheric | bar, kPa, psi | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | $P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$ | Vacuum = Atmospheric − Absolute | bar, kPa, psi | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 5 | $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$ | Differential Pressure | bar, kPa, psi | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 6 | $PV = nRT$ | Ideal Gas Law (P must be absolute) | Various | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 7 | $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$ | Boyle's Law (P must be absolute) | Various | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 8 | $P_{std} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$ | Standard Atmospheric Pressure | kPa | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 9 | $1 \text{ bar} = 100 \text{ kPa} = 10^5 \text{ Pa}$ | Bar to Pascal Conversion | Pa | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 10 | $1 \text{ atm} = 14.696 \text{ psia} = 1.01325 \text{ bar}$ | Atm Conversion | psi / bar | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in Petrochemical Real-World Systems

| Application Area | Why Absolute Pressure is Mandatory |
|------------------|------------------------------------|
| **Distillation Column Operation** | Reboiler duty, tray hydraulics, and vapor-liquid equilibrium (VLE) all use $P_{abs}$ in Raoult's and Dalton's Laws. |
| **Gas Compression** | Compressor polytropic head $H = \frac{n}{n-1} \cdot ZRT_1 \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]$ requires $P_1, P_2$ in absolute units. |
| **Storage Tank Vapour Recovery** | VOC emission calculations and vapour return systems mandate absolute pressure monitoring. |
| **Vacuum Distillation (Crude Units)** | The top of the crude column operates at 25–50 mmHg absolute; instrumentation must read true absolute. |
| **Safety Relief Valve Sizing** | API 520 mandates set pressure in barg, but accumulation and orifice area require $P_{abs}$ for gas/vapour flow. |
| **Hydrocracker Reactors** | Hydrogen partial pressure calculations directly impact conversion and catalyst life. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Absolute Pressure Relationship

### Derivation 1: Absolute Pressure as the True Thermodynamic Pressure

**Statement:** Prove that absolute pressure is the sum of gauge pressure and atmospheric pressure.

**Starting Point — The Manometer Concept:**

Consider a U-tube manometer connected to a process vessel on one side and open to the atmosphere on the other side. The fluid in the manometer is mercury (Hg).

- The pressure at the bottom of the manometer from the **process side** is $P_{process} + \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h$, where $h$ is the height difference and $\rho_{Hg}$ is the density of mercury.
- The pressure at the bottom of the manometer from the **atmosphere side** is $P_{atm} + \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h$.

Since the manometer fluid is in static equilibrium, both pressures at the bottom are equal:

$$P_{process} + \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h = P_{atm} + \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Step 1 — Simplification:**

The hydrostatic terms $\rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h$ cancel from both sides (they are identical), giving:

$$P_{process} = P_{atm}$$

This is trivial when the manometer is balanced. Now, when the process pressure exceeds atmospheric, the mercury level shifts by a height $h$, and the manometer reads:

$$P_{process} = P_{atm} + \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Step 2 — Define Gauge Pressure:**

The height-difference term $\rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h$ is, by definition, the **gauge pressure** of the process:

$$P_{gauge} = \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Step 3 — Substitute to Obtain Absolute Pressure:**

Substituting $P_{gauge}$ into the equation:

$$P_{process} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$$

The process pressure measured from a true vacuum reference is:

$$\boxed{P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}}$$

**Conversion Logic:** The manometer physically measures the *difference* between process and atmospheric pressure. To recover the *true* pressure from the perfect-vacuum baseline, we add back the atmospheric component.

---

### Derivation 2: Boyle's Law — Why Absolute Pressure is Mandatory

**Statement:** Demonstrate that Boyle's Law fails if gauge pressure is substituted for absolute pressure.

**Starting Point — Boyle's Law (Valid for Ideal Gases at Constant Temperature):**

$$P_1 \cdot V_1 = P_2 \cdot V_2$$

**Step 1 — Assume (Incorrectly) That Gauge Pressure Can Be Used:**

Let $P_1 = 5 \text{ barg}$ and $P_2 = 10 \text{ barg}$, with $V_1 = 2 \text{ m}^3$. Find $V_2$ using gauge values:

$$V_2 = \frac{P_1 \cdot V_1}{P_2} = \frac{5 \times 2}{10} = 1.0 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Correct Calculation Using Absolute Pressure:**

Converting gauge to absolute by adding $P_{atm} = 1.01325 \text{ bar}$:

$$P_1^{abs} = 5 + 1.01325 = 6.01325 \text{ bara}$$
$$P_2^{abs} = 10 + 1.01325 = 11.01325 \text{ bara}$$

$$V_2 = \frac{6.01325 \times 2}{11.01325} = 1.0921 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Compare the Results:**

$$V_2^{\text{gauge}} = 1.000 \text{ m}^3 \quad \text{vs.} \quad V_2^{\text{abs}} = 1.092 \text{ m}^3$$

**Error Magnitude:**

$$\% \text{ Error} = \frac{1.0921 - 1.000}{1.0921} \times 100\% = 8.4\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The gauge-based calculation underestimates the volume by **8.4%**, which in a real petrochemical gas compression or storage system could lead to **compressor surge, undersized piping, or process upsets**. This is why KTU 2024 examiners emphasize absolute pressure in all gas-law derivations.

---

### Analytical Matrix: Worked Numerical Problems

**Numerical Problem 1 — Standard Conversion**

> A reactor operates at a gauge pressure of **7.5 barg**. Local atmospheric pressure is **1.013 bar**. Calculate the absolute pressure in **bar, kPa, and psia**.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Absolute Pressure in bar:**

$$P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm} = 7.5 + 1.013 = 8.513 \text{ bara}$$

**Step 2 — Convert bar to kPa** (using $1 \text{ bar} = 100 \text{ kPa}$):

$$P_{abs} = 8.513 \times 100 = 851.3 \text{ kPa (a)}$$

**Step 3 — Convert bar to psia** (using $1 \text{ bar} = 14.5038 \text{ psia}$):

$$P_{abs} = 8.513 \times 14.5038 = 123.46 \text{ psia}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{P_{abs} = 8.513 \text{ bara} = 851.3 \text{ kPa(a)} = 123.46 \text{ psia}}$$

---

**Numerical Problem 2 — Vacuum Application**

> A vacuum distillation column operates at a vacuum gauge reading of **620 mmHg vacuum**. Barometric pressure is **760 mmHg**. What is the absolute pressure in the column in **bar(a)** and **torr**?

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert Vacuum Gauge to Absolute Pressure in mmHg:**

$$P_{abs} = P_{atm} - P_{vac} = 760 - 620 = 140 \text{ mmHg (a)}$$

**Step 2 — Convert mmHg to torr** (1 mmHg = 1 torr):

$$P_{abs} = 140 \text{ torr}$$

**Step 3 — Convert mmHg to bar(a)** (using $1 \text{ bar} = 750.062 \text{ mmHg}$):

$$P_{abs} = \frac{140}{750.062} = 0.1867 \text{ bara}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{P_{abs} = 0.1867 \text{ bara} = 140 \text{ torr}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **typical vacuum distillation** operating condition in petroleum refining. Crude columns often operate at 25–50 torr for atmospheric residue processing.

---

**Numerical Problem 3 — Compressor Polytropic Head**

> A two-stage compressor compresses methane from $P_1 = 1.2 \text{ bara}$ to $P_2 = 12.5 \text{ bara}$. Inlet temperature is $T_1 = 305 \text{ K}$. Polytropic exponent $n = 1.30$. Gas constant $Z = 0.97$. $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$. Calculate the polytropic head.

**Solution (Conceptual, not required for derivation):**

$$H = \frac{n}{n-1} \cdot Z R T_1 \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]$$

Note: All pressures **MUST be in absolute units (bara)** for this equation. If gauge values were mistakenly used, the head calculation would be entirely wrong.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Absolute Pressure Reference Framework

The following diagram maps the **functional architecture** of pressure references used in petrochemical instrumentation, distinguishing absolute, gauge, and vacuum pressure systems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Perfect Vacuum Reference] --> B[Atmospheric Pressure Layer]
    B --> C{Gauge Type?}
    C -->|Process Above Atmosphere| D[Gauge Pressure P_gauge]
    C -->|Process Below Atmosphere| E[Vacuum Pressure P_vac]
    C -->|Process Equals Atmosphere| F[Zero Gauge Reading]
    D --> G[Absolute Pressure = P_atm + P_gauge]
    E --> H[Absolute Pressure = P_atm - P_vac]
    F --> I[Absolute Pressure = P_atm only]
    G --> J[Petrochemical Applications]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K1[Distillation Columns]
    J --> K2[Compressor Performance]
    J --> K3[Reactor Safety Relief]
    J --> K4[Storage Tank Vapour]
    J --> K5[Vacuum Distillation]
    B --> L[ISA Notation: bar_a vs bar_g]
    L --> M[bar_a = Absolute Scale]
    L --> N[bar_g = Gauge Scale]
    M --> O[Thermodynamic Equations PV = nRT]
    N --> P[Local Process Monitoring Only]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Absolute Pressure Sensor Architecture

This schematic shows the **internal functional architecture** of a typical absolute pressure transmitter (e.g., a piezoresistive or capacitive absolute pressure sensor).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Process Medium] --> S2[Diaphragm Sensor Element]
    S2 --> S3[Piezoresistive Bridge]
    S2 --> S4[Capacitive Plate Pair]
    S3 --> S5[Signal Conditioning Circuit]
    S4 --> S5
    S5 --> S6[ADC Conversion]
    S6 --> S7[Microprocessor with Vacuum Reference]
    S7 --> S8[4-20 mA Output / HART]
    S8 --> S9[DCS / PLC / SCADA]
    SE1[Sealed Vacuum Reference Chamber] -.->|True Zero Baseline| S2
    SE2[Atmospheric Vent] -.->|Reference Only| S5
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Functional Insight:** Notice the **"Sealed Vacuum Reference Chamber"** in the diagram. This is the defining physical component of an **absolute pressure transmitter**. Unlike a gauge transmitter (which vents one side of the diaphragm to atmosphere), an absolute transmitter has a *hermetically sealed vacuum* on the reference side. This sealed vacuum provides the **true zero baseline** against which the process-side pressure is measured.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Petrochemical Process Application Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Crude Distillation Unit] --> P1A[Top Pressure: 1.05 bara]
    P1 --> P1B[Bottom Pressure: 1.8 bara]
    P2[Vacuum Distillation Unit] --> P2A[Top: 0.03 bara / 25 torr]
    P3[Hydrocracker Reactor] --> P3A[H2 Partial Pressure: 140 bara]
    P4[Ethylene Cracker] --> P4A[Furnace Outlet: 1.8 bara]
    P5[LPG Storage Bullet] --> P5A[Vapour Space: 8.5 bara]
    P6[Compressor Stage 1 Suction] --> P6A[Suction: 1.2 bara]
    P6 --> P6B[Discharge: 4.5 bara]
    P1A --> APP[All in Absolute Pressure Units]
    P1B --> APP
    P2A --> APP
    P3A --> APP
    P4A --> APP
    P5A --> APP
    P6A --> APP
    P6B --> APP
    APP --> OUT[Engineering Calculations: PV=nRT, Compressor Curves, VLE, Relief Sizing]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**Q: Define absolute pressure. How is it different from gauge pressure? Why is absolute pressure used in thermodynamic calculations?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Absolute pressure** is the pressure measured with respect to a **perfect vacuum (zero reference)**. It represents the true, intrinsic thermodynamic pressure of a fluid.
>
> **Difference from gauge pressure:**
> - **Absolute pressure** references a perfect vacuum (universal, invariant).
> - **Gauge pressure** references the local atmosphere (varies with altitude, weather).
>
> **Why used in thermodynamics:**
> Thermodynamic equations such as the **Ideal Gas Law (PV = nRT)**, **Boyle's Law**, and **steam tables** require a true zero reference. Only the absolute scale provides this. Using gauge pressure in these equations leads to mathematically and physically incorrect results. In petrochemical processes (distillation, compression, reaction), absolute pressure is mandatory for correct VLE, compressor performance, and safety calculations.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Q: A pressure gauge reads 8.5 barg. Local atmospheric pressure is 1.013 bar. Convert this pressure to absolute units in bar(a), kPa(a), and psia.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Convert gauge to absolute in bar:**

$$P_{abs} = 8.5 + 1.013 = 9.513 \text{ bara}$$

**Step 2 — Convert bar to kPa:**

$$P_{abs} = 9.513 \times 100 = 951.3 \text{ kPa(a)}$$

**Step 3 — Convert bar to psia** (1 bar = 14.5038 psia):

$$P_{abs} = 9.513 \times 14.5038 = 138.0 \text{ psia}$$

$$\boxed{P_{abs} = 9.513 \text{ bara} = 951.3 \text{ kPa(a)} = 138.0 \text{ psia}}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between absolute pressure, gauge pressure, and atmospheric pressure, explaining the manometer principle. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A vacuum distillation column operates at a vacuum reading of **720 mmHg**. Barometric pressure is **760 mmHg**. Calculate the absolute pressure in the column in **(i)** mmHg, **(ii)** torr, and **(iii)** bar(a). **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Manometer Principle:**

Consider a U-tube manometer with one limb connected to the process and the other open to the atmosphere. Mercury is the manometric fluid. When the process pressure exceeds atmospheric, mercury is pushed down on the process side and rises on the atmosphere side by a height $h$.

**Pressure balance at the manometer bottom:**

$$P_{process} = P_{atm} + \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Define gauge pressure:**

The hydrostatic term $\rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h$ is the gauge pressure — the amount by which the process pressure exceeds local atmospheric:

$$P_{gauge} = \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Derive absolute pressure:**

Substituting the gauge pressure definition:

$$P_{process} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$$

Since $P_{process}$ is referenced to a true vacuum (the manometer physically measures the difference from atmosphere, and the atmosphere itself rests on the vacuum baseline):

$$\boxed{P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}}$$

This completes the derivation.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $P_{vacuum} = 720 \text{ mmHg}$, $P_{atm} = 760 \text{ mmHg}$

**(i) Absolute Pressure in mmHg:**

$$P_{abs} = P_{atm} - P_{vac} = 760 - 720 = 40 \text{ mmHg(a)}$$

**(ii) Absolute Pressure in torr:**

Since 1 mmHg = 1 torr:

$$P_{abs} = 40 \text{ torr}$$

**(iii) Absolute Pressure in bar(a):**

Using the conversion $1 \text{ bar} = 750.062 \text{ mmHg}$:

$$P_{abs} = \frac{40}{750.062} = 0.05333 \text{ bara}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{P_{abs} = 40 \text{ mmHg} = 40 \text{ torr} = 0.0533 \text{ bara}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of an **absolute pressure transmitter** with a neat block diagram. How is it different from a gauge pressure transmitter? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A gas in a refinery process occupies **3.5 m³** at **4.2 bara** and **350 K**. According to Boyle's Law (isothermal), what volume will it occupy when compressed to **8.5 bara**? Show the calculation using both absolute and gauge pressure and comment on the error introduced. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Construction and Working of an Absolute Pressure Transmitter:**

An absolute pressure transmitter is a smart field instrument that measures process pressure with reference to a **sealed vacuum chamber**.

**Key Components:**

1. **Process-side Diaphragm** — A stainless steel or Hastelloy diaphragm that flexes under process pressure.
2. **Sealed Vacuum Reference Chamber** — A hermetically sealed chamber on the *back* side of the diaphragm, evacuated to high vacuum ($\leq 10^{-5}$ torr). This is the **true zero reference**.
3. **Sensor Element** — Either a **piezoresistive bridge** (silicon strain gauges bonded to the diaphragm) or a **capacitive pair** (diaphragm acts as one plate of a capacitor against a fixed reference plate).
4. **Signal Conditioning Circuit** — Amplifies and linearizes the millivolt (mV) sensor output.
5. **ADC + Microprocessor** — Converts analog signal to digital, applies linearization and temperature compensation.
6. **Output Stage** — Generates a 4–20 mA current signal (with optional HART, Foundation Fieldbus, or Profibus PA digital overlay).

**Working Principle:**

The process pressure deflects the diaphragm. Since the back side is at sealed vacuum (true zero), the deflection is proportional to **absolute pressure**. The piezoresistive or capacitive sensor converts this deflection into an electrical signal, which is conditioned, digitized, and transmitted as a 4–20 mA output proportional to $P_{abs}$.

**Difference from a Gauge Pressure Transmitter:**

| Feature | Absolute Pressure TX | Gauge Pressure TX |
|---------|----------------------|-------------------|
| Reference Side | Sealed vacuum chamber | Vented to atmosphere |
| Zero Definition | True zero (perfect vacuum) | Local atmospheric pressure |
| Output at 0 Process | 4 mA (true zero) | 4 mA (atmospheric reference) |
| Use Case | Thermodynamic calcs, VLE, compressor | Local process monitoring |

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $V_1 = 3.5 \text{ m}^3$, $P_1 = 4.2 \text{ bara}$, $P_2 = 8.5 \text{ bara}$, $T = \text{constant}$

**Step 1 — Correct Calculation Using Absolute Pressure (Boyle's Law):**

$$P_1 \cdot V_1 = P_2 \cdot V_2$$

$$V_2 = \frac{P_1 \cdot V_1}{P_2} = \frac{4.2 \times 3.5}{8.5} = 1.729 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Incorrect Calculation Using Gauge Pressure:**

Convert absolute to gauge by subtracting $P_{atm} = 1.013 \text{ bar}$:

$$P_1^{gauge} = 4.2 - 1.013 = 3.187 \text{ barg}$$
$$P_2^{gauge} = 8.5 - 1.013 = 7.487 \text{ barg}$$

$$V_2^{gauge} = \frac{3.187 \times 3.5}{7.487} = 1.490 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Error:**

$$\% \text{ Error} = \frac{\vert 1.729 - 1.490 \vert}{1.729} \times 100\% = 13.8\%$$

**Comment:**

The gauge-pressure-based calculation underestimates the volume by **13.8%**. In a real refinery scenario, this error could lead to **undersized piping, incorrect compressor capacity selection, or even process safety incidents**. This demonstrates why absolute pressure is **mandatory** in all gas-law calculations.

$$\boxed{V_2^{abs} = 1.729 \text{ m}^3 \quad ; \quad V_2^{gauge} = 1.490 \text{ m}^3 \quad ; \quad \text{Error} = 13.8\%}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Loss Points:**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Forgetting to convert bar → kPa or bar → psia when the question specifies a particular unit. Always re-read the question's unit requirements.
> 2. **Forgetting to add atmospheric pressure:** In absolute pressure conversions from gauge, students often write $P_{abs} = P_{gauge}$ instead of $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$.
> 3. **Using gauge in gas laws:** Substituting $P_{gauge}$ into $PV = nRT$ or Boyle's Law is a **guaranteed 2–3 mark deduction** in KTU evaluations.
> 4. **Confusing mmHg and torr:** While numerically equal in many contexts, students should explicitly state the conversion when required.
> 5. **Mixing bara and barg notation:** Always use **bara** for absolute and **barg** for gauge. Omitting the suffix is a KTU common error.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Absolute pressure** = pressure measured from a **perfect vacuum (true zero)**.
- ✅ **Gauge pressure** = pressure measured from **local atmospheric pressure**.
- ✅ **Core formula:** $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$ (and $P_{abs} = P_{atm} - P_{vac}$ for vacuum systems).
- ✅ **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 1.01325 \text{ bar} = 14.696 \text{ psia}$.
- ✅ **ISA notation:** Always write **bar(a)** for absolute and **bar(g)** for gauge.
- ✅ **All thermodynamic equations** (Ideal Gas Law, Boyle's Law, steam tables, VLE, compressor head) **REQUIRE absolute pressure** — never use gauge.
- ✅ **Absolute pressure transmitters** have a **sealed vacuum reference chamber** on the back side of the diaphragm.
- ✅ **Vacuum distillation columns** in refineries operate at very low absolute pressures (25–50 torr) to avoid thermal cracking of residues.
- ✅ **Conversion factors to memorize:**
  - $1 \text{ bar} = 100 \text{ kPa} = 14.5038 \text{ psia} = 750.062 \text{ mmHg}$
  - $1 \text{ atm} = 1.01325 \text{ bar} = 14.696 \text{ psia} = 760 \text{ mmHg}$
  - $1 \text{ mmHg} = 1 \text{ torr}$
- ✅ **Petrochemical applications:** distillation, compression, reactor safety, storage tank vapour, vacuum distillation, hydrocracker hydrogen partial pressure.
- ✅ **Safety codes** (API 520, ASME VIII) mandate absolute pressure for relief valve sizing and accumulator calculations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Differential
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET861 - INSTRUMENTATION IN PETROCHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Differential

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Differential Pressure (DP) – Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Petrochemical Process Instrumentation**, the term **"Differential"** fundamentally refers to **Differential Pressure (DP) Measurement** — the measurement of the difference in pressure between two points in a process. Formally defined under the ISA-75 / IEC 60770 standards adopted by the petrochemical industry:

> **Differential Pressure (ΔP):** The difference between two pressure measurements, expressed as the higher pressure minus the lower pressure, where both pressures are referenced to a common datum (typically atmospheric or vacuum). Mathematically, $\Delta P = P_{\text{High}} - P_{\text{Low}}$, with the standard industrial unit being **kPa, bar, psi, or inches of water column (inWC)**.

A **Differential Pressure Transmitter (DPT)** is the field instrument that senses this ΔP, converts it into a standardized electrical signal (typically **4–20 mA** with **HART** protocol, or a digital **FOUNDATION Fieldbus** signal), and transmits it to the Distributed Control System (DCS) for monitoring and control.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme treats "Differential" as a *unifying measurement principle* — it is the heart of flow measurement (orifice plates, Venturi, Annubar), level measurement (bubblers, DP across vessels), and density measurement. Expect a question linking ΔP to its derived measurement application.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Two-Pan Balance" of Process Fluids

Imagine you are holding a **U-tube manometer** (the classical ancestor of the DP transmitter). The fluid inside is mercury. You connect the two ends of the U-tube to two different points in a pipe carrying oil. The mercury will tilt — one side rises, the other falls. That tilt (height difference) **IS** the differential pressure, expressed in **inches of mercury (inHg)** or **mmH₂O**.

Now picture a modern electronic equivalent:
- **High-pressure side (H)** → oil pushing on a diaphragm.
- **Low-pressure side (L)** → a reference (or another process point) pushing on the other side of the *same* diaphragm.
- The diaphragm **deflects microscopically** (a few micrometers).
- A strain gauge or capacitive plate **senses the deflection**.
- The electronics **convert** it to a 4–20 mA signal.

> **Real-world feel:** A differential pressure transmitter in a refinery Crude Distillation Unit (CDU) might measure ΔP across an orifice plate to determine the flow rate of naphtha — one of the most common field measurements in any petrochemical plant.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Differential Pressure Transmitters

| Classification Criterion | Type 1 | Type 2 | Type 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sensing Element** | Diaphragm-based (most common) | Bellows-based (older) | Piston / Capsule type |
| **Signal Output** | Pneumatic (3–15 psi) | Analog Electronic (4–20 mA) | Digital (HART / Fieldbus) |
| **Measurement Range** | Low DP (0–10 inH₂O) | Medium DP (0–100 psi) | High DP (0–2000 psi) |
| **Application** | Flow, Level, Filter ΔP | Reactor pressure drop | Compressor surge monitoring |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Standard:** The dominant transmitter in modern petrochemical plants is the **smart electronic 4–20 mA + HART DP transmitter** (e.g., Rosemount 3051CD, Yokogawa EJX110A, Honeywell STD980). The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly mentions smart transmitters in the syllabus.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Universal Gas Constant (R):** $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$
- **Standard Gravity:** $g = 9.80665 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Density of Water (4°C):** $\rho_{\text{water}} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Density of Mercury (0°C):** $\rho_{\text{Hg}} = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Standard Process Units for DP:** **kPa, mbar, inH₂O (inches of water column), psi, inHg**
- **Output Standard:** **4–20 mA** (with HART digital superimposed at $\pm 0.5 \text{ mA}$ peak-to-peak)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle – Force-Balance / Capacitive / Piezoresistive

The DP transmitter operates on one of three primary principles:

### Principle A: Force-Balance (Used in older pneumatic & some electronic DPTs)
1. The process differential pressure deflects a diaphragm.
2. The deflection moves a force bar.
3. A position detector senses the movement.
4. A feedback coil / nozzle-flapper generates a counter-force.
5. The current required to maintain null position is proportional to ΔP.

### Principle B: Capacitive Sensing (Most common in modern smart DPTs like Rosemount 3051)
1. ΔP deflects an isolating diaphragm.
2. Silicon oil transmits the force to a **capacitive sensor diaphragm**.
3. The sensor diaphragm moves between two fixed capacitor plates.
4. Capacitance changes: $C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$
5. Differential capacitance is measured and converted to a digital output.

### Principle C: Piezoresistive (Silicon Strain Gauge)
1. ΔP deflects a silicon diaphragm.
2. Four piezoresistors in a **Wheatstone bridge** change resistance.
3. Bridge output voltage is proportional to ΔP.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this matter in petrochemical plants?** The choice of sensing principle determines drift performance, overpressure tolerance, and temperature compensation. Modern refineries prefer **capacitive** sensors due to their **±0.075%** reference accuracy and **10-year stability**.

---

## 2.2 Static and Dynamic Characterization of DPTs

A DPT is mathematically modeled as a **second-order system** with transfer function:

$$G(s) = \frac{K}{\tau^2 s^2 + 2\zeta\tau s + 1}$$

Where:
- $K$ = Static sensitivity (mA per unit ΔP)
- $\tau$ = Time constant (seconds)
- $\zeta$ = Damping ratio (typically 0.7 to 1.0 in petrochemical service)

**Key static performance parameters (must memorize for KTU):**

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Definition |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | — | ±0.075% of span | Maximum deviation from true value |
| **Repeatability** | — | ±0.05% of span | Ability to reproduce the same reading |
| **Hysteresis** | — | ±0.05% of span | Difference between up-scale and down-scale readings |
| **Zero Shift** | — | ±0.1% URL/30°C | Drift at zero with temperature |
| **Span Shift** | — | ±0.1% URL/30°C | Drift of span with temperature |
| **Turndown Ratio** | TDR | 100:1 or 200:1 | Ratio of URL to calibrated span |
| **Static Pressure Effect** | — | ±0.1% of URL/1000 psi | Zero shift due to line pressure |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are **mandatory** for KTU 2024 ESE and internal assessments. Memorize the variables and units; numerical problems in petrochemical instrumentation almost always reduce to one of these.

| # | Formula | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta P = P_H - P_L$ | $P_H, P_L$ in Pa or kPa | Basic DP definition |
| 2 | $\Delta P = h \cdot (\rho_m - \rho_f) \cdot g$ | $h$ = manometer reading (m), $\rho_m$ = manometer fluid density (kg/m³), $\rho_f$ = process fluid density (kg/m³) | U-tube manometer with process fluid above mercury |
| 3 | $Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot \Delta P}{\rho}}$ | $Q$ = volumetric flow (m³/s), $C_d$ = discharge coefficient (≈ 0.6–0.65 for orifice), $A$ = orifice area (m²), $\rho$ = fluid density (kg/m³) | **Flow through orifice plate** — most important petrochemical formula |
| 4 | $h = \frac{\Delta P}{\rho_f \cdot g}$ | $h$ = level height (m), $\rho_f$ = process fluid density (kg/m³) | **DP Level Measurement** in a pressurized vessel |
| 5 | $P_{\text{static}} = \frac{P_H + P_L}{2}$ | Pa or kPa | Static (line) pressure — critical for DPT selection |
| 6 | $I_{\text{out}} = 4 + 16 \cdot \frac{\Delta P_{\text{meas}} - \Delta P_{\text{LRV}}}{\Delta P_{\text{URV}} - \Delta P_{\text{LRV}}}$ | $I$ in mA, $\Delta P$ in process units | **4–20 mA output linearization** |
| 7 | $C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | $C$ in Farads, $d$ = gap (m) | Capacitive sensor displacement |
| 8 | $\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon$ | $G$ = gauge factor, $\varepsilon$ = strain | Piezoresistive strain gauge |
| 9 | $\text{Turndown Ratio} = \frac{\text{URL}}{\text{Calibrated Span}}$ | Dimensionless | Smart transmitter flexibility |
| 10 | $\text{Reference Accuracy} (\%)= \pm\left[0.05 + 0.005 \cdot \frac{\text{URL}}{\text{Span}}\right]$ | % of span | Rosemount-style accuracy equation |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Flow Measurement (60% of DP transmitter applications):** Orifice plate, Venturi tube, Annubar, V-cone, and wedge meters all use DP as the primary sensing mechanism. In ethylene crackers, orifice flow meters with DPTs are the workhorse for feed and product accounting.
2. **Level Measurement (25% of DP applications):** In a pressurized LPG bullet, a DPT connected to the bottom and the top (via a reference leg) infers liquid level. Compensated for density and static pressure.
3. **Filter / Strainers (5%):** ΔP across a filter indicates fouling — a DPT triggers backwash when ΔP exceeds 1.5 bar.
4. **Density / Interface (5%):** Two DPTs at known vertical separation infer fluid density: $\rho_f = \frac{\Delta P}{g \cdot \Delta h}$.
5. **Boiler Drum Level (5%):** Critical in steam generation; compensated for density changes with steam pressure.

> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Tag:** Every numerical problem in this module is built on **Formula 3** (flow) or **Formula 4** (level). Practice converting ΔP into flow or level until it is second nature.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Manometer Equation for Differential Pressure

### Problem Setup
A U-tube manometer using mercury ($\rho_m = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$) is connected between two points A (high pressure) and B (low pressure) in a pipe carrying oil ($\rho_f = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$). The mercury column shows a height difference of $h$ metres. Derive the equation for the differential pressure $\Delta P$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Apply hydrostatic pressure balance. Start from point A, go down through the oil to the mercury interface on the left, across the mercury, up through the oil to point B.

**Step 2:** Sum pressure changes along the path. Pressure increases as we go down, decreases as we go up.

**Step 3:** Let $h_A$ = height of oil column above the left mercury meniscus, and $h_B$ = height of oil column above the right mercury meniscus.

**Step 4:** The pressure at the lower mercury meniscus (left side) equals the pressure at the lower mercury meniscus (right side) — this is the fundamental balance.

**Step 5:** Write the balance equation:

$$P_A + \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h_A = P_B + \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h_B + \rho_m \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Step 6:** Rearrange:

$$P_A - P_B = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot (h_B - h_A) + \rho_m \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Step 7:** Recognize that $h_B - h_A = -h$ (geometrically, if mercury on left is depressed by $h$ from its level on the right, then $h_B - h_A = h$). Substituting:

$$P_A - P_B = -\rho_f \cdot g \cdot h + \rho_m \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Step 8:** Factor out $g \cdot h$:

$$\boxed{\Delta P = P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) \cdot g \cdot h}$$

**Step 9:** Unit verification:
- $\rho_m - \rho_f$ has units of kg/m³
- $g$ has units of m/s²
- $h$ has units of m
- Product: $\frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^3} \cdot \frac{\text{m}}{\text{s}^2} \cdot \text{m} = \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m·s}^2} = \frac{\text{N}}{\text{m}^2} = \text{Pascal}$ ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Orifice Plate Flow Equation from Bernoulli + Continuity

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Apply Bernoulli's equation between upstream point (1) and the vena contracta (2), assuming **incompressible, steady, inviscid** flow:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho} + \frac{v_1^2}{2} + g z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho} + \frac{v_2^2}{2} + g z_2$$

**Step 2:** For a horizontal pipe ($z_1 = z_2$), this simplifies to:

$$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho} = \frac{v_2^2 - v_1^2}{2}$$

**Step 3:** From the **continuity equation** (conservation of mass), the volumetric flow is conserved:

$$Q = A_1 \cdot v_1 = A_2 \cdot v_2$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $v_1$ in terms of $v_2$:

$$v_1 = \frac{A_2}{A_1} \cdot v_2$$

**Step 5:** Substitute into the Bernoulli equation:

$$\frac{\Delta P}{\rho} = \frac{v_2^2}{2} \cdot \left[1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2\right]$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $v_2$:

$$v_2 = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot \Delta P}{\rho \cdot \left[1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2\right]}}$$

**Step 7:** Multiply by $A_2$ to get the theoretical volumetric flow:

$$Q_{\text{ideal}} = A_2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot \Delta P}{\rho \cdot \left[1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2\right]}}$$

**Step 8:** Introduce the **discharge coefficient $C_d$** to account for real-world losses (viscous effects, vena contracta, etc.) and the **velocity of approach factor** $\beta = d/D$:

$$\boxed{Q = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot \Delta P}{\rho \cdot (1 - \beta^4)}}}$$

**Step 9:** Often written in terms of orifice diameter $d$ where $A_2 = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$:

$$Q = C_d \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot \Delta P}{\rho \cdot (1 - \beta^4)}}$$

**Step 10:** Define the **flow coefficient $K$** as:

$$K = \frac{C_d}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^4}}$$

So the final compact form used in **all petrochemical flow calculations** is:

$$Q = K \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot \Delta P}{\rho}}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix – Worked Numerical Examples

### Example 1: Manometer Reading to ΔP Conversion

> **Problem:** A DPT measures a manometer reading of $h = 250 \text{ mm}$ in a pipe carrying crude oil ($\rho_f = 920 \text{ kg/m}^3$) using mercury ($\rho_m = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$). Calculate the differential pressure in **kPa** and **bar**.

**Given:** $h = 0.250 \text{ m}$, $\rho_f = 920 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_m = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Solution:**

$$\Delta P = (\rho_m - \rho_f) \cdot g \cdot h$$

$$\Delta P = (13600 - 920) \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.250$$

$$\Delta P = 12680 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.250$$

$$\Delta P = 31098.9 \text{ Pa} \approx 31.10 \text{ kPa}$$

**Converting to bar:** $1 \text{ bar} = 100 \text{ kPa}$

$$\Delta P = \frac{31.10}{100} = 0.311 \text{ bar}$$

**Answer:** $\Delta P = 31.10 \text{ kPa} = 0.311 \text{ bar}$

---

### Example 2: DP to Flow Conversion (Orifice Plate)

> **Problem:** An orifice plate ($d = 50 \text{ mm}$) in a 100 mm diameter pipe ($\beta = 0.5$, $C_d = 0.62$) measures $\Delta P = 10 \text{ kPa}$ for natural gas flow. The flowing density is $\rho = 12.5 \text{ kg/m}^3$. Calculate the volumetric flow in **Nm³/h** (assume standard conditions).

**Given:** $d = 0.050 \text{ m}$, $\beta = 0.5$, $C_d = 0.62$, $\Delta P = 10000 \text{ Pa}$, $\rho = 12.5 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Solution:**

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.050)^2}{4} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$1 - \beta^4 = 1 - (0.5)^4 = 1 - 0.0625 = 0.9375$$

$$Q = 0.62 \cdot 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 10000}{12.5 \cdot 0.9375}}$$

$$Q = 1.2174 \times 10^{-3} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{20000}{11.7188}}$$

$$Q = 1.2174 \times 10^{-3} \cdot \sqrt{1706.67}$$

$$Q = 1.2174 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 41.31$$

$$Q = 5.029 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Converting to Nm³/h:** $Q = 0.05029 \cdot 3600 = 181.05 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{h}$

**Answer:** Volumetric flow rate $Q \approx 181 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{h}$

---

### Example 3: DP Level Measurement in a Pressurized Vessel

> **Problem:** A DPT connected between the top and bottom of a condensate storage tank shows $\Delta P = 45 \text{ kPa}$. The condensate density is $\rho_f = 980 \text{ kg/m}^3$. Calculate the level in metres.

**Given:** $\Delta P = 45000 \text{ Pa}$, $\rho_f = 980 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Solution:**

$$h = \frac{\Delta P}{\rho_f \cdot g} = \frac{45000}{980 \cdot 9.81} = \frac{45000}{9613.8} = 4.681 \text{ m}$$

**Answer:** Level $h \approx 4.68 \text{ m}$

---

### Example 4: 4–20 mA Output Calculation

> **Problem:** A DPT is calibrated for $\Delta P_{\text{LRV}} = 0 \text{ inWC}$ and $\Delta P_{\text{URV}} = 100 \text{ inWC}$. What is the output current for a measured $\Delta P = 65 \text{ inWC}$?

**Solution:**

$$I_{\text{out}} = 4 + 16 \cdot \frac{65 - 0}{100 - 0} = 4 + 16 \cdot 0.65 = 4 + 10.4 = 14.4 \text{ mA}$$

**Answer:** Output current $I_{\text{out}} = 14.4 \text{ mA}$

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Smart Differential Pressure Transmitter (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Process High Pressure P_H] --> ISO1[Isolating Diaphragm H]
    P2[Process Low Pressure P_L] --> ISO2[Isolating Diaphragm L]
    ISO1 --> FILL1[Silicon Oil Fill Fluid]
    FILL1 --> CAP1[Capacitive Sensor Cell H]
    ISO2 --> FILL2[Silicon Oil Fill Fluid L]
    FILL2 --> CAP2[Capacitive Sensor Cell L]
    CAP1 --> SIG[Signal Conditioning ASIC]
    CAP2 --> SIG
    SIG --> ADC[24-bit Sigma Delta ADC]
    ADC --> DSP[Digital Signal Processor]
    DSP --> COMP[Temperature and Static Pressure Compensation]
    COMP --> DAC[Digital to Analog Converter]
    DAC --> OUT[4 to 20 mA Output with HART]
    DSP --> LCD[Local LCD Indicator]
    OUT --> DCS[Distributed Control System DCS]
    HART[Hand Held Communicator HART] -.-> OUT

```
