# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define a capsule as a pressure-sensing element. List any two advantages of using a capsule over a single diaphragm.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> A **capsule** is a pressure-sensing element formed by joining two circular diaphragms (usually corrugated) at their outer edges, creating a sealed cavity. The deflection of the capsule center is proportional to the differential pressure applied across its two faces.

**Two advantages over a single diaphragm:**

1. **Larger deflection for the same pressure** — Because the two diaphragms are mechanically in series, the total deflection is approximately *twice* that of a single diaphragm, improving measurement sensitivity.
2. **Independent reference pressure** — The sealed cavity can be evacuated or pressurized to a known reference, allowing measurement of *absolute* pressure, which is not possible with an open single diaphragm.
3. *(Bonus third point)* Higher overrange protection because the two diaphragms share the load.

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Q: A capsule consists of two flat circular diaphragms of radius 25 mm and thickness 0.25 mm. If the differential pressure applied is 10 kPa, calculate the center deflection. Assume $E = 200$ GPa.**

**Model Answer:**

Given: $R = 25$ mm $= 25 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $t = 0.25$ mm $= 0.25 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $\Delta P = 10 \times 10^{3}$ Pa, $E = 200 \times 10^{9}$ Pa.

$$w_{cap} = \dfrac{3\,\Delta P\,R^{4}}{8\,E\,t^{3}}$$

$$w_{cap} = \dfrac{3 \times 10 \times 10^{3} \times (25 \times 10^{-3})^{4}}{8 \times 200 \times 10^{9} \times (0.25 \times 10^{-3})^{3}}$$

Numerator: $3 \times 10^{4} \times 3.906 \times 10^{-6} = 0.1172$

Denominator: $8 \times 2 \times 10^{11} \times 1.5625 \times 10^{-11} = 25.0$

$$w_{cap} = \dfrac{0.1172}{25.0} = 4.6875 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{\,w_{cap} \approx 4.69 \text{ mm}\,}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working principle of a capsule-type pressure sensor. Discuss its use in petrochemical industry differential-pressure (DP) transmitters.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the center deflection and maximum bending stress of a flat circular diaphragm clamped at the edge and subjected to uniform pressure. Hence, write the corresponding expression for a capsule (two such diaphragms in series).

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

### Part (a) — Construction, Working, and Petrochemical Application

**Construction:** A capsule consists of **two circular diaphragms** of identical material, dimensions, and profile, **welded along their periphery**. The internal cavity is hermetically sealed. Depending on the application, the cavity is:

- **Evacuated** — for absolute-pressure measurement (acts as the absolute vacuum reference).
- **Sealed at atmospheric pressure** — for gauge-pressure measurement.
- **Sealed at a known reference** — for differential-pressure measurement between two process points.

The diaphragms are usually **corrugated** (concentric circular waves pressed into the metal) to increase the linear travel range and reduce stiffness. The capsule is mounted between two flanges, exposing each outer face to a different pressure. The **center deflection** is coupled mechanically to a transducer — most commonly a **capacitive plate** (in modern smart transmitters) — which converts displacement into an electrical signal.

**Working Principle:**

1. The differential pressure $\Delta P = P_H - P_L$ acts across the capsule.
2. The diaphragms deflect inward or outward depending on the sign of $\Delta P$.
3. The center deflection $w_{cap} = \dfrac{3\,\Delta P\,R^{4}}{8\,E\,t^{3}}$ is **linear** with $\Delta P$.
4. This deflection changes the gap in a parallel-plate capacitor, altering its capacitance $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d - w_{cap}}$.
5. The capacitance change is detected by an AC bridge, converted to 4–20 mA current, and transmitted to the DCS.

**Petrochemical Application — DP Transmitter:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a refinery or petrochemical plant, a **DP transmitter** with a capsule element is used to measure:
>
> - **Level** in distillation columns, reactors, and storage tanks (via hydrostatic head $\Delta P = \rho g h$).
> - **Flow** of hydrocarbons, steam, and cooling water through orifice plates (via $\Delta P \propto Q^{2}$).
> - **Filter / strainer health** (rising $\Delta P$ indicates clogging).
> - **Liquid-liquid interface level** using the density difference.

The capsule is filled with **silicone oil** that hydraulically transmits process pressure to the isolating diaphragm, protecting the sensor from corrosive hydrocarbons.

### Part (b) — Derivation (Already Done in Section 3.1; Summarized)

**Step 1 — Governing equation (Germain-Lagrange plate equation):**

$$D\,\nabla^{4}w = P, \quad D = \dfrac{E\,t^{3}}{12(1-\nu^{2})}$$

**Step 2 — Deflection profile (axisymmetric, regular at center, clamped at rim):**

$$w(r) = \dfrac{3P\,R^{4}}{16E\,t^{3}}\left(1-\dfrac{r^{2}}{R^{2}}\right)^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum deflection at center ($r=0$):**

$$w_{max} = \dfrac{3P\,R^{4}}{16E\,t^{3}}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum bending stress at clamped edge ($r=R$):**

$$\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{3PR^{2}}{4t^{2}}$$

**Step 5 — For a capsule (two diaphragms in series), using series spring combination $\frac{1}{K_c} = \frac{2}{K_s}$:**

$$w_{cap,max} = \dfrac{3\,\Delta P\,R^{4}}{8\,E\,t^{3}} \quad \text{(twice the single-diaphragm deflection)}$$

$$\sigma_{max,cap} = \dfrac{3\,\Delta P\,R^{2}}{4\,t^{2}} \quad \text{(same as a single diaphragm, because the pressure is shared)}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** A capsule pressure sensor uses two flat diaphragms of radius 30 mm and thickness 0.3 mm, made of stainless steel ($E = 200$ GPa, $\sigma_{allow} = 250$ MPa). Determine:
   (i) The maximum center deflection for a differential pressure of 8 kPa.
   (ii) The maximum bending stress at the clamped edge.
   (iii) The maximum allowable differential pressure before yielding.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the capsule, bellows, and Bourdon tube as pressure-sensing elements. State at least four points of comparison and identify which one is preferred for low-pressure (sub-1 bar) petrochemical applications.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

### Part (a) — Numerical Design Problem

**Given:** $R = 30 \text{ mm} = 0.030$ m, $t = 0.3 \text{ mm} = 0.0003$ m, $E = 200$ GPa $= 2 \times 10^{11}$ Pa, $\sigma_{allow} = 250$ MPa $= 2.5 \times 10^{8}$ Pa, $\Delta P = 8$ kPa $= 8 \times 10^{3}$ Pa.

**(i) Center deflection of the capsule:**

$$w_{cap} = \dfrac{3 \times 8 \times 10^{3} \times (0.030)^{4}}{8 \times 2 \times 10^{11} \times (0.0003)^{3}}$$

Numerator: $3 \times 8 \times 10^{3} \times 8.1 \times 10^{-7} = 1.944 \times 10^{-2}$

Denominator: $8 \times 2 \times 10^{11} \times 2.7 \times 10^{-11} = 43.2$

$$w_{cap} = \dfrac{1.944 \times 10^{-2}}{43.2} = 4.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.45 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{\,w_{cap} = 0.45 \text{ mm}\,}$$

**(ii) Maximum bending stress:**

$$\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{3 \times 8 \times 10^{3} \times (0.030)^{2}}{4 \times (0.0003)^{2}}$$

$$\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{3 \times 8 \times 10^{3} \times 9 \times 10^{-4}}{4 \times 9 \times 10^{-8}} = \dfrac{21.6}{3.6 \times 10^{-7}} = 6.0 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\,\sigma_{max} = 60 \text{ MPa}\,}$$

**(iii) Maximum allowable differential pressure:**

$$P_{max} = \dfrac{4 \times 2.5 \times 10^{8} \times (0.0003)^{2}}{3 \times (0.030)^{2}}$$

$$P_{max} = \dfrac{4 \times 2.5 \times 10^{8} \times 9 \times 10^{-8}}{3 \times 9 \times 10^{-4}} = \dfrac{90}{2.7 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.33 \times 10^{4} \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\,P_{max} \approx 33.3 \text{ kPa}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design insight:** The 8 kPa operating pressure is well below the 33.3 kPa yield limit, giving a **factor of safety** of ~4.16. This is consistent with industrial practice (FoS of 3–5 for pressure-sensing elements).

### Part (b) — Comparison Table

| Parameter | Capsule | Bellows | Bourdon Tube |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | Two welded diaphragms deflect | Corrugated tube with multiple convolutions deflects axially | Curved tube straightens under pressure |
| **Pressure Range** | **Very low** (0.05 mbar – 5 bar) ✅ Best for low-P | Low to medium (0.1 – 100 bar) | Medium to high (1 – 7000 bar) |
| **Deflection Magnitude** | Moderate (mm range) | **Largest** (cm range) | Small (degrees of arc) |
| **Sensitivity** | **Highest at low pressures** | High | Low at low pressures |
| **Hysteresis** | Low (if corrugated properly) | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Temperature Limit** | ~600 °C (metallic) | ~400 °C | ~600 °C |
| **Best Application** | **DP transmitters, draft, barometer** | Pressure gauges, DP transmitters at higher P | Industrial pressure gauges, high-P |

**Preferred for sub-1 bar petrochemical applications: CAPSULE** — because of highest sensitivity at low pressures, low hysteresis, ability to measure differential pressure, and compatibility with capacitive transduction.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
>
> **Where students commonly lose marks on Capsule questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 for capsule vs. single diaphragm.** The capsule deflection is *twice* the single-diaphragm deflection. Writing the single-diaphragm formula and stopping costs **3 marks** out of 7.
> 2. **Not stating the clamped-edge boundary condition explicitly.** Examiners *require* you to write: *"slope $dw/dr = 0$ at $r = R$"* and *"deflection $w = 0$ at $r = R$"*. Omitting these costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Unit errors.** Mixing mm with m. The deflection formula needs **all SI units** (m, Pa, N/m²) for the answer in metres. A common mistake is to leave the answer in mm without showing the conversion.
> 4. **Forgetting that a capsule is in mechanical series, not parallel.** Students sometimes write $K_c = 2K_s$, which is wrong. The correct relation is $1/K_c = 2/K_s$.
> 5. **Not mentioning the corrugated vs. flat distinction.** Examiners often award 1 mark for noting that real industrial capsules are corrugated, with the flat-diaphragm equation being a *simplified* analysis.
> 6. **No diagram in 7-mark questions.** A neat labelled sketch of a capsule (with reference and process sides, weld, cavity) is worth 1–2 marks by itself.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **Capsule definition:** Two diaphragms welded at the rim, forming a sealed cavity. **Primary element** for low-pressure and DP measurement.
- **Pressure range:** **0.05 mbar to 5 bar** (the lowest range in the elastic-element family).
- **Deflection formula (single flat diaphragm):** $w_{max} = \dfrac{3PR^{4}}{16Et^{3}}$ — at the **center** ($r=0$).
- **Deflection formula (capsule):** $w_{cap} = \dfrac{3\Delta P\,R^{4}}{8E\,t^{3}}$ — **double** the single-diaphragm value because two diaphragms in series.
- **Maximum stress location:** Clamped **rim** ($r = R$).
- **Maximum stress formula:** $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{3PR^{2}}{4t^{2}}$.
- **Maximum allowable pressure:** $P_{max} = \dfrac{4\sigma_{all}\,t^{2}}{3R^{2}}$.
- **Capsule stiffness (series):** $K_c = \dfrac{8Et^{3}}{3R^{4}}$, which is **half** of the single-diaphragm stiffness $K_s = \dfrac{16Et^{3}}{3R^{4}}$.
- **Deflection profile (axisymmetric):** $w(r) \propto \left(1 - r^{2}/R^{2}\right)^{2}$ — quadratic in the bracketed term.
- **Boundary conditions used in derivation:** (i) $w(R) = 0$, (ii) $w'(R) = 0$, (iii) $w'(0) = 0$ (regularity at center).
- **Sensitivity:** $S = \dfrac{w_{max}}{P} = \dfrac{3R^{4}}{8Et^{3}}$ — increases as $R^{4}$ and decreases as $t^{3}$. **Radius dominates.**
- **Corrugation effect:** Increases travel, reduces stiffness, improves linearity — *not* in the basic formula, but crucial in industrial design.
- **Transduction:** Capacitive (modern), strain gauge, LVDT, piezoresistive, or pneumatic.
- **Petrochemical uses:** Level, flow (orifice DP), filter DP monitoring, draft pressure, absolute pressure reference, interface level.
- **Fill fluid:** Silicone oil, hydraulically couples the isolating diaphragm to the sensing element.
- **Material of construction:** SS 316, Hastelloy C, Inconel — for corrosion resistance in hydrocarbon service.
- **Comparison point:** Capsule = best for **low** pressure, Bellows = best for **larger travel**, Bourdon = best for **high** pressure.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strain Gauge
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET861 - INSTRUMENTATION IN PETROCHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Strain Gauge

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Strain Gauge (Definition per KTU 2024 Instrumentation Syllabus):**
> A **strain gauge** is a passive resistive transducer that converts mechanical deformation (strain) into a measurable change in electrical resistance. It operates on the principle that the resistance of a conductor changes when it is subjected to mechanical stress, given by the relationship between its length, cross-sectional area, and resistivity.

The most common form is the **bonded metallic foil strain gauge**, which consists of a thin metallic resistive foil arranged in a grid pattern, bonded to a flexible backing (carrier) and firmly attached to the test specimen using a specialized adhesive. When the host specimen deforms under load, the gauge deforms identically, and the resulting change in resistance is measured using a **Wheatstone bridge** circuit.

The dimensionless ratio relating fractional resistance change to fractional length change is defined as the **Gauge Factor (GF)**:

$$GF = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\Delta L / L} = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$$

where $\varepsilon$ (epsilon) is the **mechanical strain** expressed in microstrain ($\mu\varepsilon = 10^{-6}$).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Build-Up

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of a strain gauge as a "stretchy electrical wire"** — Imagine a rubber band that has a thin copper wire glued along its length. When you stretch the rubber band, the copper wire also stretches (it becomes longer and thinner). Since electrical resistance depends on both length and thickness, stretching changes the resistance. By precisely measuring this tiny change in resistance, engineers can figure out exactly how much the rubber band (or any machine part) has been stretched or compressed.

### Real-World Engineering Intuition

In a petrochemical plant, a strain gauge bonded to a **reactor vessel wall** or **pipeline** behaves like a sensitive "skin" that feels mechanical stress. When internal pressure rises and tries to bulge the pipe, the strain gauge — being perfectly bonded to the metal — stretches by an almost imperceptible amount (often less than the width of a human hair). This microscopic stretch is amplified by electronics into a readable signal, telling operators how much stress the equipment is enduring — critical for **predictive maintenance** and **safety interlock systems**.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Numerical Benchmarks for KTU Board Examination:**
> - Gauge Factor of constantan (typical alloy) : **$GF \approx 2.0$ to $2.2$**
> - Gauge Factor of semiconductor (silicon) gauges : **$GF \approx 100$ to $200$**
> - Standard gauge resistance : **$R = 120\ \Omega$, $350\ \Omega$, or $1000\ \Omega$**
> - Poisson's ratio for steel : **$\nu \approx 0.30$**
> - Poisson's ratio for aluminum : **$\nu \approx 0.33$**
> - Poisson's ratio for constantan : **$\nu \approx 0.30$ to $0.33$**
> - Young's Modulus of steel : **$E \approx 200\ \text{GPa}$**
> - Allowable strain range (typical) : **$\varepsilon \leq 3000\ \mu\varepsilon$ (0.3%)**

### 1.4 Classification Snapshot of Strain Gauges (KTU 2024 Module 2)

| **Type** | **Operating Principle** | **Typical GF** | **Application in Petrochemical Industry** |
|----------|------------------------|----------------|-------------------------------------------|
| Bonded Metallic (Foil/Wire) | Piezoresistive (geometric + piezoresistive) | 2.0 – 2.2 | Pressure vessel stress, pipeline monitoring |
| Unbonded Metallic | Piezoresistive (mechanical) | 2.0 – 4.0 | Load cells, force measurement |
| Semiconductor (Piezoresistive) | Piezoresistive (strong) | 100 – 200 | High-sensitivity miniature sensors |
| Capacitive | Capacitance change | N/A | Specialized precision instruments |
| Piezoelectric | Charge generation | N/A | Dynamic strain (vibration analysis) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For the **KTU 2024 PEAET861 Module 2** examination, focus primarily on **bonded metallic strain gauges** and the **Wheatstone bridge** measurement technique, as these are the board's high-weightage topics.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Why Does Resistance Change Under Strain?

The electrical resistance of a uniform conductor is governed by:

$$R = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A}$$

where:
- $\rho$ = resistivity of the conductor material (Ω·m)
- $L$ = length of the conductor (m)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area of the conductor (m²)

When the conductor is subjected to an axial mechanical strain $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$, three simultaneous phenomena occur:

### Step-by-Step Logic Breakdown

1. **Geometric Effect (Length Change)**
   The conductor elongates, so $L$ increases by $\Delta L$. This directly increases $R$.

2. **Geometric Effect (Area Change — Poisson Effect)**
   As the conductor elongates axially, it contracts laterally by an amount governed by **Poisson's ratio** $\nu$. Therefore, the cross-sectional area $A$ decreases, which further increases $R$.

3. **Piezoresistive Effect (Resistivity Change)**
   The mechanical stress alters the microscopic lattice spacing of the conductor's atoms, slightly changing the resistivity $\rho$. For metals like constantan, this is small; for semiconductors, it dominates.

---

## 2.2 Derivation of Gauge Factor (GF)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Gauge Factor formula** is the single most important equation in this module. Memorize it for the KTU 2024 examination.

Starting with $R = \rho L / A$, taking the logarithmic differential:

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} - \frac{dA}{A}$$

The lateral strain in two perpendicular directions (assuming isotropic material and uniaxial loading) gives:

$$\frac{dA}{A} = -2\nu \cdot \frac{dL}{L} = -2\nu \cdot \varepsilon$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \varepsilon + 2\nu \cdot \varepsilon$$

For a metallic strain gauge, the piezoresistive term $d\rho/\rho$ is small and grouped with the geometric terms. Therefore:

$$\frac{dR}{R} = (1 + 2\nu) \cdot \varepsilon + \frac{d\rho}{\rho}$$

Defining the **Gauge Factor** as the ratio of fractional resistance change to applied strain:

$$GF = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = (1 + 2\nu) + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$$

For a **metallic strain gauge** where $\nu \approx 0.30$:

$$GF_{\text{metal}} \approx 1 + 2(0.30) = 1.60 \quad \text{(geometric contribution only)}$$

In practice, for **constantan**, the piezoresistive term adds $\approx 0.5$, giving $GF \approx 2.0$ to $2.2$.

For a **semiconductor strain gauge**, the piezoresistive term dominates:

$$GF_{\text{semi}} = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon} \approx 100 \text{ to } 200$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formula Table for KTU 2024 Board Examination — Strain Gauge Module**

| **Equation** | **Symbolic Form** | **Description / Use** |
|--------------|-------------------|------------------------|
| Basic Resistance | $R = \rho L / A$ | Defines conductor resistance |
| Mechanical Strain | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$ | Dimensionless strain (often in $\mu\varepsilon$) |
| Gauge Factor Definition | $GF = (\Delta R / R) / \varepsilon$ | Master equation for strain gauge |
| GF in Terms of Poisson's Ratio | $GF = 1 + 2\nu + (\Delta \rho / \rho) / \varepsilon$ | Theoretical GF breakdown |
| Stress-Strain Relation | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | Young's Modulus linkage (Hooke's Law) |
| Force-Strain Relation | $F = \sigma \cdot A = E \cdot A \cdot \varepsilon$ | Converts strain to force (load cell basis) |
| Wheatstone Bridge Output | $V_o = V_s \cdot (\Delta R / 4R)$ | Single active gauge, balanced bridge |
| Bridge Sensitivity | $S = V_s / 4$ | For one active gauge in quarter-bridge |
| Temperature Effect | $R_T = R_0 (1 + \alpha \Delta T)$ | Thermal drift in unstrained gauge |
| Apparent Strain | $\varepsilon_{apparent} = (\alpha_s - \alpha_g) \Delta T$ | Mismatch between specimen and gauge |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Pitfall:** When writing gauge factor values, students frequently confuse the geometric term $1 + 2\nu$ (which is always about 1.6 for metals) with the actual measured GF of constantan (≈ 2.0–2.2). Always distinguish between **theoretical** and **practical** gauge factor in your answer.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In petrochemical industries, strain gauges are deployed in:

- **Pressure Transmitter Diaphragm Sensing** — Bourdon tubes and diaphragms bonded with strain gauges convert process pressure into electrical signals (4–20 mA loop).
- **Load Cells on Reactor Weighing Systems** — Reactor catalyst loading monitored using strain-gauge-based load cells.
- **Pipeline Stress Monitoring** — Detecting thermal expansion stress and preventing rupture in high-temperature hydrocarbon lines.
- **Vibration Monitoring of Compressors and Pumps** — Dynamic strain measurement for condition-based maintenance.
- **Tank Level Measurement (Hydrostatic)** — Strain gauge load cells under storage tanks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — Wheatstone Bridge Output for Quarter-Bridge Configuration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is a **favourite KTU 2024 Part B question**. Practice writing every step.

**Setup:** A single active strain gauge $R_g$ is connected in Arm 1 of a Wheatstone bridge. The other three arms contain fixed resistors $R$. The bridge is excited by a source voltage $V_s$.

### Step 1 — Bridge Balance Condition (No Strain)

When no strain is applied, all four arms are equal: $R_g = R$. The bridge is balanced and the output voltage $V_o = 0$.

### Step 2 — Apply Strain to Active Gauge

The active gauge resistance changes by $\Delta R$:

$$R_g = R + \Delta R$$

### Step 3 — Output Voltage Formula (General Wheatstone Bridge)

The output voltage between the midpoints of the bridge is:

$$V_o = V_s \left[ \frac{R_3}{R_3 + R_4} - \frac{R_g}{R_g + R_2} \right]$$

For our configuration: $R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$ and $R_g = R + \Delta R$:

$$V_o = V_s \left[ \frac{R}{R + R} - \frac{R + \Delta R}{(R + \Delta R) + R} \right]$$

### Step 4 — Simplify the First Term

$$\frac{R}{R + R} = \frac{R}{2R} = \frac{1}{2}$$

### Step 5 — Simplify the Second Term

$$\frac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R} = \frac{R(1 + \Delta R / R)}{2R(1 + \Delta R / 2R)}$$

$$= \frac{1 + \Delta R / R}{2(1 + \Delta R / 2R)}$$

### Step 6 — Apply Binomial Approximation

Since $\Delta R \ll R$, we use the approximation $1 / (1 + x) \approx 1 - x$ for small $x$:

$$\frac{1 + \Delta R / R}{2(1 + \Delta R / 2R)} \approx \frac{1 + \Delta R / R}{2} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\Delta R}{2R}\right)$$

Multiplying out:

$$\approx \frac{1}{2}\left[1 + \frac{\Delta R}{R} - \frac{\Delta R}{2R} - \frac{(\Delta R)^2}{2R^2}\right]$$

Neglecting the second-order term $(\Delta R)^2 / (2R^2)$:

$$\approx \frac{1}{2}\left[1 + \frac{\Delta R}{2R}\right]$$

### Step 7 — Compute the Output Voltage

$$V_o = V_s \left[ \frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{2}\left(1 + \frac{\Delta R}{2R}\right) \right]$$

$$V_o = V_s \left[ \frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{2} - \frac{\Delta R}{4R} \right]$$

$$\boxed{V_o = V_s \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{4R}}$$

### Step 8 — Express in Terms of Strain and Gauge Factor

Using $\Delta R / R = GF \cdot \varepsilon$:

$$\boxed{V_o = \frac{V_s \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon}{4}}$$

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — Bridge Output for Full-Bridge Configuration (4 Active Gauges)

> [!NOTE]
> A **full-bridge with 4 active gauges** is the highest-sensitivity configuration. Used in precision load cells.

In a full-bridge, two gauges experience tension ($+ \Delta R$) and two experience compression ($- \Delta R$). With $R_1 = R + \Delta R$, $R_2 = R - \Delta R$, $R_3 = R + \Delta R$, $R_4 = R - \Delta R$:

$$V_o = V_s \left[ \frac{R_3}{R_3 + R_4} - \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} \right]$$

First fraction (R3 and R4 in series, opposite strains cancel):

$$\frac{R + \Delta R}{(R + \Delta R) + (R - \Delta R)} = \frac{R + \Delta R}{2R}$$

Second fraction (R1 and R2 in series, opposite strains cancel):

$$\frac{R + \Delta R}{2R}$$

Substituting:

$$V_o = V_s \left[ \frac{R + \Delta R}{2R} - \frac{R + \Delta R}{2R} \right]$$

Wait — for the differential output, we need opposite arms with opposite strain. The standard full-bridge output is:

$$\boxed{V_o = V_s \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}}$$

This is **4× more sensitive** than a quarter-bridge. The full derivation with proper sign conventions yields:

$$V_o = V_s \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Bridge Configurations Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparison Matrix** — High-yield for KTU Part A & Part B numerical problems

| **Configuration** | **Active Gauges** | **Output Voltage $V_o$** | **Sensitivity Factor** | **Temperature Compensation** |
|-------------------|-------------------|---------------------------|------------------------|------------------------------|
| Quarter-Bridge (1 active) | 1 | $V_s \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon / 4$ | 1× | Poor (needs dummy gauge) |
| Half-Bridge (2 active, adjacent arms) | 2 | $V_s \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon / 2$ | 2× | Good (automatic) |
| Half-Bridge (2 active, opposite arms) | 2 | $V_s \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon / 2$ | 2× | Good |
| Full-Bridge (4 active) | 4 | $V_s \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$ | 4× | Excellent |

---

## 3.4 Exhaustive Numerical Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU 2024 Numerical Type Problem — Practice this format**

**Problem:** A strain gauge with resistance $R = 350\ \Omega$ and gauge factor $GF = 2.1$ is bonded to a steel specimen subjected to a stress of $\sigma = 70\ \text{MPa}$. Young's modulus of steel is $E = 200\ \text{GPa}$. The gauge is connected in a quarter-bridge with excitation voltage $V_s = 10\ \text{V}$. Calculate:
(a) The strain $\varepsilon$
(b) The change in resistance $\Delta R$
(c) The output voltage $V_o$

### Solution

**(a) Strain Calculation:**

Using Hooke's Law: $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$, therefore:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{70 \times 10^6}{200 \times 10^9} = 3.5 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\varepsilon = 350\ \mu\varepsilon \quad \text{(microstrain)}$$

**(b) Change in Resistance:**

$$\Delta R = R \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon = 350 \times 2.1 \times 3.5 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\Delta R = 350 \times 2.1 \times 0.00035$$

$$\Delta R = 0.25725\ \Omega$$

**(c) Output Voltage (Quarter-Bridge):**

$$V_o = \frac{V_s \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon}{4} = \frac{10 \times 2.1 \times 3.5 \times 10^{-4}}{4}$$

$$V_o = \frac{7.35 \times 10^{-3}}{4} = 1.8375 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V}$$

$$\boxed{V_o \approx 1.84\ \text{mV}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students often forget that in a quarter-bridge, the formula has a **division by 4**. In a full-bridge, there is **no division by 4**. Mixing these up is the single most common cause of numerical errors in this module.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Strain Gauge Functional Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> The following block diagram maps the complete functional chain of a strain-gauge-based measurement system used in petrochemical instrumentation.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Mechanical Input: Force or Pressure on Specimen] --> nodeB[Strain Generation in Host Structure]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Strain Gauge Bonded on Surface]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Resistance Change Delta R in Gauge]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Wheatstone Bridge Circuit]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Differential Output Voltage Vo]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Signal Amplifier and Filter]
    nodeG --> nodeH[ADC and Microprocessor]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Display or 4-20 mA Transmitter Output]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Control Room DCS or PLC]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Wheatstone Bridge with Quarter Configuration

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeSrc[Excitation Source Vs] --> nodeA
    nodeA[Arm 1: Active Strain Gauge R plus Delta R] --> nodeB
    nodeB[Output Terminal Vo Positive] --> nodeAmp
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeC[Arm 2: Fixed Resistor R] --> nodeD[Output Terminal Vo Negative]
    nodeD --> nodeAmp
    nodeSrc --> nodeC
    nodeE[Arm 3: Fixed Resistor R] --> nodeB
    nodeF[Arm 4: Fixed Resistor R] --> nodeD
    nodeAmp[Differential Amplifier] --> nodeOut[Conditioned Output Signal]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Flow — Strain Gauge Working Principle Breakdown

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeStart[Mechanical Load Applied] --> nodeStep1[Specimen Elongates by Delta L]
    nodeStep1 --> nodeStep2[Axial Strain epsilon equals Delta L over L]
    nodeStep2 --> nodeStep3[Gauge Length Increases]
    nodeStep3 --> nodeStep4[Cross Section Contracts via Poisson Effect]
    nodeStep4 --> nodeStep5[Resistance R increases to R plus Delta R]
    nodeStep5 --> nodeStep6[Bridge Output Voltage Vo is Generated]
    nodeStep6 --> nodeStep7[GF times epsilon equals Delta R over R]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Flow — Temperature Compensation Logic

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeT[Temperature Change Delta T] --> nodeA1[Active Gauge Resistance Changes]
    nodeT --> nodeA2[Dummy Gauge Resistance Changes Equally]
    nodeA1 --> nodeB1[Bridge Arm 1 Voltage Divider]
    nodeA2 --> nodeB2[Adjacent Bridge Arm Voltage Divider]
    nodeB1 --> nodeC1[Output Vo Affected by Thermal Drift]
    nodeB2 --> nodeC2[Opposite Drift Cancels at Output]
    nodeC1 --> nodeOut[Net Output is Strain Only]
    nodeC2 --> nodeOut
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** These Mermaid block diagrams represent the functional architecture of a strain gauge measurement chain. The actual physical gauge is a tiny foil pattern (often 5 mm × 10 mm) with a serpentine grid that maximizes the active length in the direction of measurement while minimizing transverse sensitivity.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Gauge Factor. Explain the significance of its value for a strain gauge. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Gauge Factor (GF)** is defined as the ratio of the fractional change in resistance to the fractional change in length (i.e., mechanical strain) of a strain gauge:
>
> $$GF = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\Delta L / L} = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$$
>
> It is a **dimensionless quantity** that quantifies the sensitivity of a strain gauge. A higher GF means a larger change in resistance for a given applied strain, making the gauge more sensitive.
>
> - For **metallic gauges** (constantan), $GF \approx 2.0$ to $2.2$.
> - For **semiconductor gauges** (silicon), $GF \approx 100$ to $200$, making them highly sensitive but more temperature-dependent and nonlinear.

---

### Question 2: What is the function of a dummy strain gauge in a Wheatstone bridge? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> A **dummy strain gauge** is an unstrained gauge mounted on the same material as the active gauge but oriented perpendicular to the active direction, so that it does not experience the strain being measured. However, it experiences the **same temperature changes** as the active gauge.
>
> Its function is to provide **automatic temperature compensation**. Since both gauges change resistance identically due to temperature, the Wheatstone bridge treats this as a common-mode signal and rejects it, leaving only the strain-induced resistance change in the active gauge to produce an output voltage.
>
> $$V_o^{\text{temp-compensated}} = V_o^{\text{active}} - V_o^{\text{dummy}} = V_o^{\text{strain only}}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, Module 2 question choices will offer **two alternative questions** of equal marks. Students must answer ONE.

---

### Question A: (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the gauge factor of a strain gauge in terms of Poisson's ratio, explaining the physical significance of each term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A quarter-bridge strain gauge circuit uses a single active gauge with $R = 120\ \Omega$ and $GF = 2.0$. The bridge is excited with $5\ \text{V}$. If the measured output is $V_o = 2.5\ \text{mV}$, calculate:
- (i) The strain $\varepsilon$ experienced by the gauge
- (ii) The change in resistance $\Delta R$
- (iii) The applied stress if the gauge is bonded to a steel member with $E = 200\ \text{GPa}$ **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

Starting with the fundamental resistance equation:

$$R = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A}$$

Taking the logarithmic differential of both sides:

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} - \frac{dA}{A}$$

Under uniaxial strain, the change in cross-sectional area is related to axial strain by Poisson's ratio $\nu$:

$$\frac{dA}{A} = -2\nu \cdot \frac{dL}{L} = -2\nu \cdot \varepsilon$$

Substituting back:

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \varepsilon + 2\nu \cdot \varepsilon$$

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \left(1 + 2\nu\right) \cdot \varepsilon + \frac{d\rho}{\rho}$$

Dividing both sides by $\varepsilon$:

$$GF = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = \left(1 + 2\nu\right) + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$$

**Physical Significance of Each Term:**

1. **The "1" term** → Direct contribution of length increase: when the gauge stretches, it becomes longer, increasing resistance.
2. **The "$2\nu$" term** → Indirect contribution from area reduction: due to the Poisson effect, the cross-section shrinks, further increasing resistance.
3. **The "$\Delta \rho / \rho$" term** → Piezoresistive contribution: stress-induced change in the material's resistivity due to lattice distortion.

For metals, the first two terms dominate (≈ 1.6), and the piezoresistive term adds a small amount, giving $GF \approx 2$. For semiconductors, the third term dominates, giving $GF \approx 100$–$200$.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**(i) Strain calculation:**

Using the quarter-bridge formula:

$$V_o = \frac{V_s \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon}{4}$$

Solving for $\varepsilon$:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{4 V_o}{V_s \cdot GF} = \frac{4 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 2.0}$$

$$\varepsilon = \frac{10 \times 10^{-3}}{10} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon = 1000\ \mu\varepsilon}$$

**(ii) Change in resistance:**

$$\Delta R = R \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon = 120 \times 2.0 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta R = 0.24\ \Omega}$$

**(iii) Applied stress:**

Using Hooke's Law:

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon = 200 \times 10^9 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma = 200 \times 10^6\ \text{Pa} = 200\ \text{MPa}}$$

---

### Question B: (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Wheatstone bridge-based strain gauge measurement system. Compare quarter, half, and full-bridge configurations in terms of sensitivity and temperature compensation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A full-bridge strain gauge load cell uses four identical gauges, each with $R = 350\ \Omega$ and $GF = 2.2$. A 500 kg mass is placed on the load cell. The cross-sectional area of the load-bearing column is $A = 800\ \text{mm}^2$ and Young's modulus is $E = 210\ \text{GPa}$. With $V_s = 12\ \text{V}$, compute:
- (i) Stress and strain in the column
- (ii) Output voltage of the bridge
- (iii) Sensitivity in $\text{mV}/\text{V}$ **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Working of a Wheatstone Bridge Strain Gauge System** (refer to Section 4.2 diagram):

1. The strain gauge $R_g$ is bonded to the specimen and connected in one arm of the bridge.
2. The other three arms contain fixed precision resistors $R$.
3. A stable DC excitation voltage $V_s$ is applied across the bridge.
4. When no strain is present, the bridge is balanced and $V_o = 0$.
5. Under strain, $R_g$ changes by $\Delta R$, unbalancing the bridge and producing a differential output voltage.
6. This $V_o$ is amplified, filtered, digitized, and displayed.

**Configuration Comparison:**

| **Configuration** | **Sensitivity** | **Temperature Compensation** |
|-------------------|-----------------|------------------------------|
| Quarter-bridge (1 active) | $V_o = V_s \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon / 4$ | Requires dummy gauge in adjacent arm |
| Half-bridge (2 active) | $V_o = V_s \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon / 2$ | Built-in (one tension, one compression) |
| Full-bridge (4 active) | $V_o = V_s \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$ | Excellent (all gauges compensate) |

A full-bridge is **4 times more sensitive** than a quarter-bridge and provides the best linearity and temperature rejection — preferred for precision load cells.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**(i) Stress and strain:**

Force from mass: $F = m \cdot g = 500 \times 9.81 = 4905\ \text{N}$

Stress: $\sigma = F / A = 4905 / (800 \times 10^{-6}) = 6.131 \times 10^6\ \text{Pa} \approx 6.13\ \text{MPa}$

Strain: $\varepsilon = \sigma / E = 6.131 \times 10^6 / (210 \times 10^9) = 2.92 \times 10^{-5}$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon \approx 29.2\ \mu\varepsilon}$$

**(ii) Output voltage (Full-Bridge):**

$$V_o = V_s \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon = 12 \times 2.2 \times 2.92 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\boxed{V_o \approx 7.71 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{V} = 0.771\ \text{mV}}$$

**(iii) Sensitivity:**

$$\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{V_o}{V_s} = \frac{0.771 \times 10^{-3}}{12} = 64.3\ \mu\text{V}/\text{V}$$

In typical industry notation:

$$\boxed{\text{Sensitivity} \approx 0.0643\ \text{mV}/\text{V}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the bridge factor (1/4, 1/2, or 1):** Students mix up the quarter-bridge and full-bridge formulas, leading to a 4× error in the answer.
> 2. **Unit confusion:** Forgetting to convert area from $\text{mm}^2$ to $\text{m}^2$ when computing stress.
> 3. **Forgetting $g$ in load cell problems:** Using only mass instead of force ($F = mg$).
> 4. **Missing sign in Poisson's ratio:** Writing $A$ increasing when it actually decreases under tension.
> 5. **Skipping derivations:** The KTU 2024 valuation key demands step-by-step derivations for 14-mark questions, not just final answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 PEAET861 Module 2: Strain Gauge**

- **Definition:** A strain gauge is a passive resistive transducer that converts mechanical strain into a change in electrical resistance.

- **Master Equation:** $R = \rho L / A$ — resistance depends on resistivity, length, and area.

- **Gauge Factor:** $GF = (\Delta R / R) / \varepsilon = 1 + 2\nu + (\Delta \rho / \rho)/\varepsilon$.

- **Standard Values:** Metallic GF ≈ 2.0–2.2, Semiconductor GF ≈ 100–200.

- **Hooke's Law:** $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ — links stress, strain, and Young's modulus.

- **Quarter-Bridge Output:** $V_o = V_s \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon / 4$.

- **Half-Bridge Output:** $V_o = V_s \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon / 2$.

- **Full-Bridge Output:** $V_o = V_s \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$ (4× more sensitive).

- **Temperature Compensation:** Use dummy gauge in adjacent arm (quarter-bridge) or use half/full-bridge configurations where opposite-strain gauges cancel thermal drift.

- **Apparent Strain Equation:** $\varepsilon_{apparent} = (\alpha_s - \alpha_g) \cdot \Delta T$, where $\alpha_s$ and $\alpha_g$ are the thermal expansion coefficients of the specimen and gauge.

- **Poisson's Ratio:** Always remember the negative sign in the area-strain relationship: $dA/A = -2\nu \cdot dL/L$.

- **Applications in Petrochemical Industry:** Pressure transmitters, load cells on reactors, pipeline stress monitoring, vibration analysis on rotating equipment, hydrostatic tank gauging.

- **Material Choices:** Constantan (low temperature coefficient, stable) and Isoelastic (high output) are common foil alloys.

- **Bonding Adhesive:** Cyanoacrylate, epoxy, or ceramic cement — must transfer strain perfectly without slippage.

- **Practical Tip:** Strain values are typically very small (μstrain level), so Wheatstone bridges with high-resolution amplifiers are mandatory.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pressure Transmitters and Their Calibration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET861 - INSTRUMENTATION IN PETROCHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pressure Transmitters and Their Calibration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Pressure Transmitter** is a precision industrial instrument that senses the process pressure applied at its input and converts it into a proportional, standardized electrical output signal — conventionally a **4–20 mA DC current loop** — for transmission over long distances to Distributed Control Systems (DCS), Programmable Logic Controllers (PLC), or remote Indicators/Recorders.

In modern petrochemical practice, the transmitter is almost always a **"Smart Transmitter"** (per IEC 61518 / ISA 50.00.01), which contains an embedded microprocessor, non-volatile memory, and supports bidirectional digital communication over the **HART (Highway Addressable Remote Transducer) Protocol**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *"A pressure transmitter is a process-control instrument that continuously measures the pressure of a fluid (gas, liquid, or steam) and delivers a standardized output signal (4–20 mA with superimposed HART or fully digital FOUNDATION Fieldbus) whose magnitude is linearly proportional to the measured pressure."*

### Functional Symbol of a Pressure Transmitter (ISA 5.1 Standard)

$$
\text{Circle with letter “PT” (Pressure Transmitter) inside, top connection = process tap, bottom connection = signal output}
$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are squeezing a **balloon** in your hand. The harder you squeeze, the more you feel the internal pressure pushing back. Now imagine a tiny, ultra-sensitive "electronic tongue" embedded inside the balloon wall. Every time the internal pressure changes, this tongue whispers an electrical whisper (in *millivolts*). A built-in "translator" inside the device (called the **signal conditioner**) converts this whisper into a standardized, loud, robust shout (the **4–20 mA current signal**) that travels through kilometres of twisted-pair cable to the control room without losing accuracy.

- **Balloon pressure** → Process pressure
- **Tongue (sensor)** → Strain gauge / Capacitive / Piezoelectric sensing element
- **Translator (amplifier + linearizer)** → Signal conditioning electronics
- **Standardized shout (4–20 mA)** → Output signal reaching the DCS

This is exactly how a **Rosemount 3051**, **Yokogawa EJX**, **Honeywell ST 3000**, or **Endress+Hauser Cerabar** works in a refinery's distillation column overhead line.

## 1.3 Standard Units and Physical Constants Used

> [!NOTE]
> **Pressure Units a KTU Student MUST Know:**
>
> | Unit | Symbol | Equivalent in Pascal |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | Pascal (SI base) | Pa | $1\ \text{Pa} = 1\ \text{N/m}^2$ |
> | Kilopascal | kPa | $1\ \text{kPa} = 1000\ \text{Pa}$ |
> | Bar | bar | $1\ \text{bar} = 10^5\ \text{Pa}$ |
> | Pound per sq. inch | psi | $1\ \text{psi} \approx 6894.757\ \text{Pa}$ |
> | Inches of water | inH₂O | $1\ \text{inH}_2\text{O} \approx 248.84\ \text{Pa}$ |
> | Millimetres of mercury | mmHg / Torr | $1\ \text{mmHg} \approx 133.322\ \text{Pa}$ |
> | Standard atmosphere | atm | $1\ \text{atm} = 101325\ \text{Pa}$ |

### Universal Reference Constants

- **Standard gravity:** $g = 9.80665\ \text{m/s}^2$
- **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101325\ \text{Pa} = 1.01325\ \text{bar} = 14.696\ \text{psi}$
- **Reference temperature for density of water:** $4^\circ\text{C}$ ($\rho_{\text{water}} = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$)

## 1.4 Classification of Pressures Measured in Industry

1. **Gauge Pressure ($P_g$)** — Measured relative to local atmospheric pressure. $P_g = P_{abs} - P_{atm}$
2. **Absolute Pressure ($P_{abs}$)** — Measured relative to a perfect vacuum. $P_{abs} = P_g + P_{atm}$
3. **Differential Pressure ($\Delta P$)** — Difference between two pressure points. $\Delta P = P_H - P_L$
4. **Vacuum Pressure** — Negative gauge pressure, i.e., $P_{abs} < P_{atm}$.

## 1.5 KTU Must-Know Transducer Family

A pressure **transducer** (sensor only) vs. a pressure **transmitter** (sensor + signal conditioner + output stage):

| Feature | Transducer | Transmitter |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Output | mV, V (low level) | 4–20 mA / Digital |
| Distance capability | < 5 m (noise prone) | > 1 km |
| Built-in linearization | No | Yes |
| Smart / HART | No | Yes (modern units) |
| Used for | Lab / OEM | Plant floor |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — How a Smart Pressure Transmitter Actually Works

A modern electronic pressure transmitter has **four internal stages**, executed by the sensor and the microprocessor:

1. **Stage 1 — Primary Sensing (Mechanical-to-Electrical):**  
   Process pressure deflects a diaphragm (silicon, stainless steel, or Hastelloy). This deflection alters an electrical property:
   - Resistance (strain gauge / piezoresistive)
   - Capacitance (capacitive cell)
   - Resonant frequency (resonant-wire sensor)
   - Charge generation (piezoelectric)

2. **Stage 2 — Signal Conditioning:**  
   The microvolt-level signal is amplified, bridge-completed, and digitized by a **Σ-Δ ADC** with 16- to 24-bit resolution.

3. **Stage 3 — Linearization & Compensation:**  
   The microprocessor applies stored polynomials to correct for:
   - Static pressure (line pressure) effect on DP cells
   - Temperature drift of zero and span
   - Non-linearity of the sensor

4. **Stage 4 — Output Stage:**  
   Converts the digital value to a **4–20 mA current** using a **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)** with a precision V-to-I converter, with HART FSK signal superimposed.

## 2.2 Primary Sensing Technologies (High-Yield for KTU)

### A. Piezoresistive / Strain-Gauge Pressure Sensor

The Wheatstone bridge equation governs the output:

$$
V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_1 R_3 - R_2 R_4}{(R_1 + R_2)(R_3 + R_4)}
$$

For a balanced bridge at zero pressure, all four arms are equal ($R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$). Under pressure, two arms increase in resistance by $\Delta R$ (tension) and two decrease by $\Delta R$ (compression). Substituting:

$$
V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}
$$

The gauge factor $G_F$ links $\Delta R / R$ to strain $\varepsilon$:

$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G_F \cdot \varepsilon
$$

For silicon piezoresistors, $G_F \approx 100$ to $200$ — far higher than metallic gauges ($G_F \approx 2$), which is why silicon dominates.

### B. Capacitive Pressure Sensor

A pressure-deflected diaphragm forms one plate of a differential capacitor. The capacitance is:

$$
C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}
$$

Where $d$ changes with pressure. Two chambers (high and low side) form a differential capacitor $\Delta C = C_H - C_L$, eliminating common-mode errors.

### C. Piezoelectric Pressure Sensor

$$
q = d \cdot F = d \cdot P \cdot A
$$

Where $d$ is the charge-sensitivity coefficient (e.g., for quartz $d \approx 2.3 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{C/N}$). Best for dynamic (transient) pressure, not static.

### D. Resonant-Wire / Resonant-Force Sensor

Wire tension $T$ controls natural frequency $f$:

$$
f = \frac{1}{2L}\sqrt{\frac{T}{\rho A_w}}
$$

Tiny pressure change $\rightarrow$ tension change $\rightarrow$ frequency change — measured to **ppm** accuracy.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Print This!)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All KTU Board–Expected Pressure Transmitter Formulas**

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Output current for given pressure | $I_{out} = 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{P_{meas} - LRV}{URV - LRV}$ | The fundamental transfer relation (mA) |
| 2 | Equivalent pressure for given current | $P_{meas} = LRV + (URV - LRV) \cdot \dfrac{I_{out} - 4}{16}$ | Reverse transfer |
| 3 | Span / Range | $\text{Span} = URV - LRV$ | Engineering range (E.U.) |
| 4 | Turndown Ratio | $TD = \dfrac{URV_{max}}{URV_{set}}$ | Ratio of max to calibrated URL |
| 5 | Zero Elevation | $P_{static,elevated} = P_{process} + E$ | Lift zero above atmospheric |
| 6 | Zero Suppression | $P_{static,suppressed} = P_{process} - S$ | Push zero below atmospheric |
| 7 | Static Pressure Error (DP cell) | $\%Z = \dfrac{(Z_{SPL}-Z_{SPh})}{(Z_{ideal})} \times 100$ | Line-pressure zero shift |
| 8 | Hydrostatic head | $P = \rho g h$ | For seal/diaphragm systems |
| 9 | Bridge output (Wheatstone) | $V_{out} \approx V_{ex}\cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$ | Strain gauge sensitivity |
| 10 | Capacitance | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | Capacitive sensor |
| 11 | Frequency (resonant) | $f = \dfrac{1}{2L}\sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\rho A_w}}$ | Resonant wire |
| 12 | Accuracy in % Span | $\%\text{Span} = \dfrac{\text{Error}}{\text{Span}} \times 100$ | Industry standard |
| 13 | HART cable resistance | $R_{loop,max} = \dfrac{24 - 12}{0.020} = 600\ \Omega$ | At 24 V supply, 20 mA |
| 14 | DP flow formula | $\Delta P = K \cdot Q^2$ | Orifice flow basis |

> **Notation:** LRV = Lower Range Value, URV = Upper Range Value, E.U. = Engineering Units.

## 2.4 Smart Transmitter Architecture (HART Layer)

The **HART (Highway Addressable Remote Transducer)** Protocol superimposes a **Bell-202** FSK signal of $\pm 0.5\ \text{mA}$ at **1200 bps** on the 4–20 mA loop, using frequencies **1200 Hz** (binary 1) and **2200 Hz** (binary 0). This allows simultaneous analog and digital communication. Three command classes:

- **Universal Commands** (0–30): Read PV, tag, manufacturer.
- **Common Practice** (32–127): Re-range, damping, trim.
- **Device-Specific** (128–253): Manufacturer proprietary functions.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In petrochemical plants, pressure transmitters serve:

1. **Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS):** Pressure Safety Valves (PSV) activation, level-overflow protection.
2. **Flow Measurement:** Primary element (orifice) + DP transmitter for custody transfer.
3. **Distillation Control:** Column overhead pressure control via reflux/condenser.
4. **Compressor Anti-Surge:** Surge detection on centrifugal compressor recycle loops.
5. **Reactor Pressure Profiling:** Catalyst-bed pressure-drop monitoring.

## 2.6 Calibration — The Most Repeated KTU Question

**Calibration** is the procedure of comparing the transmitter output against a **traceable reference standard** (dead-weight tester, precision digital pressure calibrator) and adjusting zero and span so that the output matches the ideal 4 mA at LRV and 20 mA at URV.

### Two Standard Methods

1. **Five-Point Calibration (up-down-up, KTU favourite):**
   Apply 0%, 25%, 50%, 75%, 100% of span, both increasing and decreasing. Plot error vs. pressure. Calculate:
   - **Linearity:** Max deviation from best straight line (BSL).
   - **Hysteresis:** Max difference between up and down readings at the same input.
   - **Repeatability:** Standard deviation of repeated readings.
   - **Deadband:** Smallest input change that produces output change.

2. **Zero–Span Calibration (field-routine):**
   - Apply LRV pressure → adjust **ZERO** screw/key to read 4.000 mA.
   - Apply URV pressure → adjust **SPAN** screw/key to read 20.000 mA.
   - Iterate 2–3 times because zero and span interact.

> [!NOTE]
> **Smart Transmitters:** Traditional potentiometers are replaced by digital commands via HART handheld (e.g., Emerson 375/475) — using commands 144 (read PV) and 145 (trim DAC).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive derivation mandate:** Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. No skipping.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Transfer Equation of 4–20 mA Transmitter

We start from the defining engineering principle: **The output current must be a linear function of the input pressure.** Let the input be $P$ and the output be $I$. Linear form:

$$
I = a \cdot P + b
$$

**Step 1:** Apply boundary condition #1 — At $P = LRV$, output must be exactly $4\ \text{mA}$:

$$
4 = a \cdot LRV + b
$$

**Step 2:** Apply boundary condition #2 — At $P = URV$, output must be exactly $20\ \text{mA}$:

$$
20 = a \cdot URV + b
$$

**Step 3:** Subtract Eq. (1) from Eq. (2):

$$
20 - 4 = a \cdot URV + b - a \cdot LRV - b
$$

$$
16 = a \cdot (URV - LRV)
$$

**Step 4:** Solve for slope $a$:

$$
a = \frac{16}{URV - LRV}
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute back into Eq. (1) to solve for intercept $b$:

$$
b = 4 - a \cdot LRV = 4 - \frac{16 \cdot LRV}{URV - LRV}
$$

**Step 6:** Final combined equation:

$$
I = 4 + \frac{16 \cdot (P - LRV)}{URV - LRV}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,I_{out} = 4 + \dfrac{16 \cdot (P_{meas} - LRV)}{URV - LRV}\,}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Finding Output Current

**Problem:** A pressure transmitter has LRV = 0 bar(g), URV = 10 bar(g). Find output current when $P = 6.5\ \text{bar(g)}$.

**Solution:**

$$
\text{Span} = URV - LRV = 10 - 0 = 10\ \text{bar}
$$

$$
I_{out} = 4 + \frac{16 \cdot (6.5 - 0)}{10 - 0}
$$

$$
I_{out} = 4 + \frac{16 \cdot 6.5}{10}
$$

$$
I_{out} = 4 + \frac{104}{10} = 4 + 10.4
$$

$$
\boxed{\,I_{out} = 14.4\ \text{mA}\,}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Reverse Calculation (Find Pressure from Current)

**Problem:** The same transmitter reads $I = 12\ \text{mA}$. What is the measured pressure?

**Solution:**

$$
P_{meas} = LRV + (URV - LRV) \cdot \frac{I_{out} - 4}{16}
$$

$$
P_{meas} = 0 + 10 \cdot \frac{12 - 4}{16}
$$

$$
P_{meas} = 10 \cdot \frac{8}{16} = 10 \cdot 0.5
$$

$$
\boxed{\,P_{meas} = 5.0\ \text{bar(g)}\,}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 2 — Zero Elevation Calculation

**Problem Setup:** A vessel contains steam at 30 bar absolute. The transmitter is connected via a water-filled siphon (cold leg) with water column equivalent of **2 bar** above the tap. The transmitter zero must be elevated.

**Step 1:** The pressure at the transmitter diaphragm equals steam pressure plus hydrostatic head:

$$
P_{trans} = P_{steam} + P_{hydro}
$$

$$
P_{trans} = 30 + 2 = 32\ \text{bar}
$$

**Step 2:** The required span remains 0 to 30 bar. To handle the static offset, elevate the LRV:

$$
LRV_{new} = 2\ \text{bar(g)} \quad ; \quad URV = 32\ \text{bar(g)}
$$

**Step 3:** Verify output at the steam condition of 30 bar absolute (which is **29 bar(g)** at the tap if atmospheric is 1 bar — but we treat the head as gauge):

Output when steam at 30 bar(a) is present (i.e., 30 bar above atmospheric — already gauge-equivalent if local atm cancels):

$$
I_{out} = 4 + \frac{16 \cdot (30 - 2)}{32 - 2} = 4 + \frac{16 \cdot 28}{30}
$$

$$
I_{out} = 4 + 14.933 = 18.93\ \text{mA}
$$

**Step 4:** Reverse — what if we did **NOT** elevate? Using LRV = 0, URV = 32 bar:

At steam condition (assuming tap reads 32 bar):

$$
I_{out} = 4 + \frac{16 \cdot (32 - 0)}{32 - 0} = 20\ \text{mA}\ (\text{full scale})
$$

This is wrong because the transmitter would always operate in the upper 6% of range. **Elevation is therefore essential** for usable resolution.

## 3.5 Derivation 3 — Turndown Ratio Worked Example

**Problem:** A pressure transmitter has URL = 100 bar, and is calibrated URV = 25 bar. Calculate turndown.

**Solution:**

$$
TD = \frac{URL}{URV_{set}} = \frac{100}{25}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,TD = 4:1\,}
$$

A 4:1 turndown means we are only using 25% of the sensor's native capability, sacrificing some accuracy but gaining resolution in the lower 25% of the absolute range.

## 3.6 Derivation 4 — Hydrostatic Seal-Fluid Calculation (Important for Petrochemical)

**Problem:** A pressure transmitter is mounted below a tank. The tap line is filled with **glycerin** ($\rho = 1260\ \text{kg/m}^3$) with vertical height $h = 4\ \text{m}$. What static pressure does the transmitter sense at zero tank pressure?

**Step 1:** Apply hydrostatic relation:

$$
P_{hydro} = \rho \cdot g \cdot h
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values:

$$
P_{hydro} = 1260 \cdot 9.80665 \cdot 4
$$

$$
P_{hydro} = 1260 \cdot 39.2266
$$

$$
P_{hydro} = 49{,}425.5\ \text{Pa}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to bar:

$$
P_{hydro} = 49{,}425.5 / 100000 = 0.494\ \text{bar}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,P_{hydro} \approx 0.494\ \text{bar(g)}\,}
$$

This is the **zero suppression** value that must be applied to the transmitter calibration.

## 3.7 Derivation 5 — Loop Resistance Maximum for HART

A HART transmitter requires at least **250 Ω** loop resistance for proper FSK detection, and loop voltage must remain above **12 V** at the transmitter terminals at 20 mA.

**Step 1:** Apply Ohm's law on the loop:

$$
R_{loop,max} = \frac{V_{supply} - V_{min,transmitter}}{I_{max}}
$$

**Step 2:** Plug $V_{supply} = 24\ \text{V}$, $V_{min} = 12\ \text{V}$, $I_{max} = 0.020\ \text{A}$:

$$
R_{loop,max} = \frac{24 - 12}{0.020}
$$

$$
R_{loop,max} = \frac{12}{0.020}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,R_{loop,max} = 600\ \Omega\,}
$$

A typical field installation uses $250\ \Omega$ for HART and the remaining resistance in the indicator/DCS input.

## 3.8 Calibration Adjustment Order — Mathematical Justification

Because **ZERO** moves the entire curve up/down and **SPAN** changes the slope, they are NOT independent. Each adjustment affects the other:

$$
\text{After zero adjust (no pressure)}:\ I_{LRV} = 4.000\ \text{mA}\ (\text{ideal}),\ \text{but span endpoint shifts slightly}.
$$

$$
\text{After span adjust (at URV)}:\ I_{URV} = 20.000\ \text{mA},\ \text{but zero drifts slightly}.
$$

Hence the **iterative procedure**: alternate ZERO → SPAN → ZERO → SPAN until both endpoints settle within tolerance.

## 3.9 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix — Calibration Error Types

| Error Type | Cause | Mathematical Definition | Calibration Correctable? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Zero Error** | Static head offset, mounting tilt | $\Delta I_0 = I_{0,meas} - 4.000$ | Yes (ZERO adj) |
| **Span Error** | Sensor aging, excitation drift | $\Delta I_{span} = I_{URV,meas} - 20.000$ | Yes (SPAN adj) |
| **Linearity Error** | Non-linear sensor response | $\varepsilon_{lin} = \dfrac{\vert V_{out} - V_{BSL} \vert}{Span} \times 100$ | Only via smart trim (sensor trim) |
| **Hysteresis** | Mechanical backlash | $\varepsilon_H = \dfrac{I_{up} - I_{down}}{16}\times 100$ | Mechanical — cannot trim |
| **Repeatability** | Random noise, friction | $\sigma_n = \text{std. dev. of repeats}$ | Statistical, not trimmable |
| **Drift** | Thermal/aging | $\dfrac{\Delta P/P}{\Delta T}$ in ppm/°C | Compensated by firmware |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Smart Pressure Transmitter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Process Pressure Tap]:::in --> node2[Isolating Diaphragm and Fill Fluid]:::mech
    node2 --> node3[Sensing Element - Strain Gauge Bridge]:::sens
    node3 --> node4[Instrumentation Amplifier]:::amp
    node4 --> node5[24 bit Sigma Delta ADC]:::adc
    node5 --> node6[Miniature Microcontroller with NVM Memory]:::micro
    node6 --> node7[Linearization and Temperature Compensation Engine]:::firm
```
