<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between a pressure transducer and a pressure transmitter. List any three types of pressure-sensing elements used in industrial transmitters.**

**Model Answer (Memorize-Style for 3 Marks):**

A **pressure transducer** converts the applied pressure into a low-level electrical signal (mV or V), typically without built-in signal conditioning or transmission capability. It is suited only for short-distance, low-noise environments (laboratories, OEM modules). A **pressure transmitter** is a complete field instrument that combines a primary sensor with amplification, linearization, temperature compensation, and a standardized output stage (4–20 mA with HART or digital FOUNDATION Fieldbus). It can drive signals over kilometre-long field cables without loss of accuracy.

**Three types of pressure-sensing elements (any three, 1 mark each):**
1. **Piezoresistive (silicon strain gauge)** — silicon diaphragm with diffused Wheatstone bridge.
2. **Capacitive** — diaphragm forms one plate of a differential capacitor.
3. **Piezoelectric** — quartz crystal generates charge proportional to applied force (dynamic only).
4. **Resonant-wire** — wire tension change shifts natural frequency.
5. **Bonded / Foil strain gauge** — metal foil bonded to a stainless-steel diaphragm.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is meant by zero elevation and zero suppression in pressure transmitters? Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Zero Elevation:** A technique where the Lower Range Value (LRV) of the transmitter is set to a *positive* gauge pressure value rather than zero, in order to measure pressures that are always above atmospheric. The output 4 mA represents an elevated (non-zero) pressure.

*Example:* Measuring steam pressure of 30 bar with a water-filled siphon producing a 2 bar static head — the transmitter LRV is elevated to 2 bar(g) and URV set to 32 bar(g).

**Zero Suppression:** A technique where the LRV is set to a *negative* gauge pressure (vacuum) so that the transmitter can read pressures below atmospheric.

*Example:* Condenser vacuum of -0.85 bar(g) with a water leg of 1 bar — LRV is set to -1 bar(g) and URV to 0 bar(g).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**With a neat block diagram, explain the functional blocks of a smart pressure transmitter. Discuss the role of HART protocol in modern instrumentation.**

**Model Answer:**

A smart pressure transmitter consists of the following functional blocks (refer Section 4.1 diagram):

1. **Sensor / Primary Element:** Silicon piezoresistive or capacitive cell converts pressure to an electrical signal.
2. **Signal Conditioner:** Instrumentation amplifier boosts the millivolt signal to a usable level.
3. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC):** 16–24 bit Σ-Δ converter digitizes the conditioned signal.
4. **Microprocessor:** Performs linearization, engineering-unit conversion, damping, and diagnostics.
5. **Memory (EEPROM/NVM):** Stores calibration coefficients, tag, range, and device descriptor.
6. **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC) + V-to-I:** Generates the precise 4–20 mA output.
7. **HART Modem:** Bell-202 FSK modulator superimposing digital communication on the loop.
8. **Display:** Local LCD/LED for PV indication.

**Role of HART Protocol (3 sub-points for completeness):**

- **Bidirectional Communication:** Enables simultaneous transmission of the 4–20 mA analog variable AND digital process variables, calibration constants, and diagnostics on the **same pair of wires**.
- **Remote Configuration:** Using a HART handheld (Emerson 475) or Asset Management System (AMS), engineers can re-range, trim, change damping, change tag — all from the control room.
- **Diagnostics & Predictive Maintenance:** Smart transmitters report health status (NAMUR NE 107 categories), plugged impulse line detection, sensor drift — enabling condition-based maintenance.

(7 marks distributed: 4 for diagram + blocks, 3 for HART role.)

---

#### Part (b) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**A differential pressure transmitter is used to measure the flow of oil through an orifice plate in a refinery pipeline. The maximum flow rate is 250 m³/h and corresponds to a differential pressure of 16000 mmWC. The transmitter has an output range of 4–20 mA. Calculate:**
**(i) The differential pressure in kPa at a flow rate of 180 m³/h.**
**(ii) The output current in mA at this condition.**
**(iii) The percentage of span at this flow.**

**Given:** $\rho_{\text{oil}} = 850\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Establish the flow–ΔP relation. Since flow through an orifice obeys:

$$
Q = K\sqrt{\Delta P}
$$

**Step 2:** Find the proportionality constant $K$ from maximum flow condition:

$$
K = \frac{Q_{max}}{\sqrt{\Delta P_{max}}}
$$

$$
\Delta P_{max} = 16000\ \text{mmWC} = 16\ \text{mWC} = 16 \times 9.81 = 156.96\ \text{kPa (approx)}
$$

More precisely:

$$
\Delta P_{max} = \rho_{\text{water}} \cdot g \cdot h = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 16 = 156960\ \text{Pa} = 156.96\ \text{kPa}
$$

$$
K = \frac{250}{\sqrt{156.96}}
$$

$$
\sqrt{156.96} = 12.528
$$

$$
K = \frac{250}{12.528} = 19.957\ \text{m}^3\text{/h per kPa}^{0.5}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute ΔP at 180 m³/h:

$$
\Delta P_{180} = \left(\frac{Q_{180}}{K}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{180}{19.957}\right)^2
$$

$$
\frac{180}{19.957} = 9.019
$$

$$
\Delta P_{180} = (9.019)^2 = 81.34\ \text{kPa}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta P_{180} \approx 81.34\ \text{kPa}\,}
$$

**Step 4:** Output current calculation:

$$
I_{out} = 4 + 16 \cdot \frac{\Delta P - 0}{\Delta P_{max} - 0} = 4 + 16 \cdot \frac{81.34}{156.96}
$$

$$
I_{out} = 4 + 16 \cdot 0.5182 = 4 + 8.291
$$

$$
\boxed{\,I_{out} \approx 12.29\ \text{mA}\,}
$$

**Step 5:** Percentage of span:

$$
\% \text{Span} = \frac{\Delta P_{180}}{\Delta P_{max}} \times 100 = \frac{81.34}{156.96} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\%\text{Span} \approx 51.82\%\,}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 (Modified)]
**Explain the procedure for calibrating a smart pressure transmitter using a dead-weight tester. What are the advantages of using HART communicators for calibration?**

**Model Answer:**

**Calibration Procedure using Dead-Weight Tester (DWT):**

The Dead-Weight Tester is a primary pressure standard in which known weights loaded on a piston of known area generate a precisely known pressure:

$$
P_{DWT} = \frac{m \cdot g}{A_{piston}}
$$

**Steps:**

1. **Preparation:** Isolate the transmitter from the process. Bleed both taps. Connect the DWT high-pressure port to the transmitter HIGH side (and vice versa for DP). Connect a precision multimeter in series with the 24 V loop to read mA output.

2. **Leak Test:** Pressurize to 50% span and hold for 1 minute. Confirm zero pressure decay.

3. **Apply LRV Pressure (0%):** Generate LRV pressure (typically 0 bar). Read the transmitter's mA output. Use the HART command for **DAC Zero Trim (Cmd 145 variant or sensor trim)** to bring output to **4.000 mA exactly**.

4. **Apply URV Pressure (100%):** Generate URV pressure. Read output. Use HART command for **DAC Gain Trim** to set output to **20.000 mA exactly**.

5. **Iterate:** Because zero and span are mathematically coupled, repeat steps 3 and 4 alternately until both endpoints settle to within ±0.005 mA.

6. **Five-Point Check:** Apply 0, 25, 50, 75, 100 % in ascending and descending order. Record mA at each point. Compute linearity, hysteresis, and repeatability.

7. **Documentation:** Update calibration sticker with date, technician initials, traceability certificate number of the DWT, deviation recorded, and pass/fail status.

**Advantages of HART Communicator-Based Calibration (any 4):**

1. **No Physical Potentiometer Access:** Smart transmitters do not have external zero/span screws. All adjustments are via digital commands — safe in hazardous areas (intrinsically safe operation).
2. **Reduced Human Error:** Numeric entry is more accurate than mechanical trim potentiometers.
3. **Automatic Documentation:** HART stores calibration history, asset tag, time/date in non-volatile memory.
4. **Re-ranging Without Pressure Source:** For DP transmitters used as level/flow, the lower and upper range values can be **re-entered digitally** (e.g., 0–500 mmWC to 0–2500 mmWC) without physical pressure application — using **sensor trim (Cmd 137/138)**.
5. **Remote Calibration:** Calibration can be performed from the control room via Asset Management Software (AMS) — saves time and reduces exposure to hazardous areas.

---

#### Part (b) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 (Modified)]
**A pressure transmitter is connected to a steam header via a water-filled impulse line. The steam pressure varies from 25 to 35 bar(a). The vertical height of the water column is 3.5 m. The local atmospheric pressure is 1.013 bar. If the transmitter is calibrated to give 4 mA at LRV and 20 mA at URV:**
**(i) Determine the LRV and URV (in bar gauge) for the transmitter.**
**(ii) If the measured output current is 13.6 mA, what is the corresponding steam pressure in bar(a)?**

**Given:** $\rho_{\text{water}} = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Hydrostatic pressure head from water column:

$$
P_{head} = \rho \cdot g \cdot h = 1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 3.5
$$

$$
P_{head} = 34335\ \text{Pa} = 0.34335\ \text{bar}
$$

**Step 2:** Convert steam pressure to gauge:

$$
P_{steam,min}^{g} = P_{steam,min}^{a} - P_{atm} = 25 - 1.013 = 23.987\ \text{bar(g)}
$$

$$
P_{steam,max}^{g} = 35 - 1.013 = 33.987\ \text{bar(g)}
$$

**Step 3:** Pressure at the transmitter diaphragm includes hydrostatic head:

$$
P_{trans,min} = 23.987 + 0.34335 = 24.330\ \text{bar(g)}
$$

$$
P_{trans,max} = 33.987 + 0.34335 = 34.330\ \text{bar(g)}
$$

**Step 4:** Set LRV and URV:

$$
\boxed{\,LRV = 24.33\ \text{bar(g)} \quad;\quad URV = 34.33\ \text{bar(g)}\,}
$$

The zero has been **elevated** by ~0.343 bar.

**Step 5:** For $I_{out} = 13.6\ \text{mA}$:

$$
P_{meas} = LRV + (URV - LRV) \cdot \frac{I_{out} - 4}{16}
$$

$$
P_{meas} = 24.33 + (34.33 - 24.33) \cdot \frac{13.6 - 4}{16}
$$

$$
P_{meas} = 24.33 + 10 \cdot \frac{9.6}{16}
$$

$$
P_{meas} = 24.33 + 10 \cdot 0.6
$$

$$
P_{meas} = 24.33 + 6.0 = 30.33\ \text{bar(g)}
$$

**Step 6:** Convert back to absolute for steam:

$$
P_{steam}^{a} = P_{meas}^{g} + P_{atm} - P_{head}
$$

$$
P_{steam}^{a} = 30.33 + 1.013 - 0.343
$$

$$
\boxed{\,P_{steam}^{a} \approx 31.00\ \text{bar(a)}\,}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes (Valuation Pitfalls):**
> 1. Forgetting to convert between absolute and gauge pressure — examiners strictly check this.
> 2. Using gauge steam pressure directly without adding the hydrostatic head.
> 3. Not subtracting the hydrostatic head when back-calculating from mA to steam absolute pressure.
> 4. Confusing **zero elevation** (positive LRV) with **zero suppression** (negative LRV).
> 5. Units mismatch (mixing bar and kPa in the same calculation).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Exam Cheat Sheet**

- [ ] Pressure transmitter ≠ pressure transducer; transmitter has full signal conditioning & standard 4–20 mA output.
- [ ] **Standard output:** 4 mA = LRV, 20 mA = URV. Always linear relationship.
- [ ] **Master formula:** $I_{out} = 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{P - LRV}{URV - LRV}$
- [ ] **Reverse formula:** $P = LRV + (URV - LRV) \cdot \dfrac{I - 4}{16}$
- [ ] **Span** = URV - LRV (in engineering units).
- [ ] **Turndown Ratio (TD)** = URL ÷ Calibrated URV. Smart transmitters offer TD up to 100:1.
- [ ] **Zero elevation** → LRV set to positive gauge (e.g., steam with water column).
- [ ] **Zero suppression** → LRV set to negative gauge (vacuum measurements).
- [ ] **Static pressure effect** → applies to DP cells; affects zero shift due to line pressure.
- [ ] **Hydrostatic head formula:** $P = \rho g h$ — critical for seal/diaphragm calculations.
- [ ] **Bridge equation for strain gauge:** $V_{out} \approx V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$.
- [ ] **Capacitive sensor:** $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ — change in $d$ with pressure.
- [ ] **Piezoelectric:** only for dynamic, not static pressure measurements.
- [ ] **HART Protocol:** Bell-202 FSK at 1200 bps on 4–20 mA loop; 1200 Hz = "1", 2200 Hz = "0".
- [ ] **HART loop resistance minimum:** 250 Ω for FSK detection; **maximum 600 Ω** at 24 V supply.
- [ ] **Calibration standards:** DWT, digital pressure calibrator, precision bourdon gauge.
- [ ] **Five-point calibration:** 0/25/50/75/100 % up-down-up to determine linearity, hysteresis, repeatability.
- [ ] **Zero–Span Iteration:** they are mathematically coupled — always alternate adjustments.
- [ ] **Smart transmitter trim sequence:** Sensor Trim → DAC Zero Trim → DAC Gain Trim.
- [ ] **Accuracy stated in % of Span** (industry convention).
- [ ] **Reference standards traceability:** NIST (USA), NPL (UK), NABL (India).

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Examiner's Warning:**
> 1. Always show units in every step of derivation — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit.
> 2. Never confuse **absolute**, **gauge**, and **differential** pressures — a 1-mark “conceptual error” deduction is common.
> 3. In numerical problems, **state the assumption** (e.g., “assuming water column temperature is 25 °C, ρ = 997 kg/m³”) — this earns you a methodology mark even if the final number is slightly off.
> 4. Draw a **labelled block diagram** of a smart pressure transmitter — it is the most repeated 7-mark question in KTU ESE.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control Valves and Pressure Controllers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET861 - INSTRUMENTATION IN PETROCHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Control Valves and Pressure Controllers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Control Valve — Formal Definition

A **Control Valve** is a final control element (actuated by an external energy source, typically pneumatic, hydraulic, or electrical) that modulates the flow rate of a process fluid (liquid, gas, steam, or slurry) in response to a control signal received from a controller. In a petrochemical plant, it physically manipulates the manipulated variable (flow) to maintain the controlled variable (pressure, temperature, level, flow, composition) at its desired setpoint.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Control valves are explicitly classified as the "muscle" of any process control loop. They convert electrical/pneumatic controller output (typically a standardized 4–20 mA DC or 3–15 psig pneumatic signal) into a precise mechanical stem position, thereby regulating flow.

**Standard Control Signal Ranges (KTU Exam Favorite):**
- **Pneumatic:** 3–15 psig (20–100 kPa) → Live zero of 3 psig enables zero-flow detection.
- **Electronic:** 4–20 mA DC → Live zero of 4 mA enables fault detection (current < 4 mA = sensor/loop failure).
- **Digital/Fieldbus:** FOUNDATION Fieldbus, HART, Profibus PA.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Tap" (Water Tap Analogy)

Imagine an ordinary household tap (faucet). When you turn the handle:
- A small turn allows a trickle of water.
- A larger turn allows a gush.

Now imagine this tap is **robotic**, connected to your **smartphone**, and adjusts itself automatically based on a sensor measuring the water level in a tank upstream. If the level rises above setpoint, the tap automatically closes a bit; if the level drops, it opens more.

**That robotic tap is essentially a control valve.**
- The **handle** = Valve stem.
- The **internal rubber/plastic seal** = Valve plug and seat.
- The **smartphone app** = Controller (PID).
- The **level sensor** = Pressure transmitter.
- The **motor on the tap** = Actuator (pneumatic diaphragm or electric motor).

In a refinery's crude distillation column, for instance, a control valve on the reflux line modulates cooling liquid flow to keep the column's overhead temperature at 110 °C. Without the valve, the controller would have nowhere to "push" corrective action.

## 1.3 Control Valve — Major Sub-Assemblies

A complete control valve package consists of **two major sub-assemblies**:

| Sub-Assembly | Function | Petrochemical Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Actuator** | Provides the motive force to move the valve stem | Pneumatic diaphragm actuator on a refinery steam line |
| **Valve Body (with Trim)** | Actually throttles the flow via plug, seat, and stem | Globe valve on a hydrocracker recycle loop |

Additionally, auxiliary accessories include:
- **Positioner** — Ensures stem position exactly matches controller output (closed-loop on stem position).
- **I/P Transducer** — Converts 4–20 mA electronic signal to 3–15 psig pneumatic signal.
- **Air Filter Regulator (AFR)** — Supplies clean, stable instrument air.
- **Limit Switches & Solenoid Valves** — For on/off diagnostic feedback.
- **Handwheel** — Manual override during maintenance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valve Trim** refers to the *internal wetted parts* of the valve that contact the process fluid: the **plug**, **seat**, **stem**, and **cage**. Choosing the correct trim material (e.g., **Stellite**, **Inconel**, or **316 SS**) is critical in petrochemical service to resist corrosion from sour crude (H₂S) and erosion from catalyst fines.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Control Valve Terminology (The "Must-Know" Vocabulary)

For KTU 2024 Scheme, students must internalize the following engineering jargon, as it appears verbatim in textbook problems and previous university exams.

- **Stem Position (x):** Fractional travel of the stem, from 0 (fully closed) to 1 (fully open). Dimensionless.
- **Relative Flow (Q/Q_max):** Fractional flow capacity compared to maximum flow at full open.
- **Rangeability (R):** The ratio of maximum controllable flow to minimum controllable flow. A typical globe valve has R = 30:1 to 50:1.
- **Turndown:** Same concept as rangeability, often used interchangeably.
- **Valve Coefficient (C_v):** The flow rate of water in US gallons per minute (gpm) through a fully open valve at a pressure drop of 1 psi. **C_v is the most critical sizing parameter in KTU problems.**
- **Valve Coefficient (K_v):** Metric counterpart — flow rate of water in m³/h through a fully open valve at a pressure drop of 1 bar. **K_v ≈ 0.865 × C_v**.
- **Travel (L):** Total stem stroke in mm or inches.
- **Inherent Characteristic:** The relationship between stem travel (x) and flow (Q/Q_max) at **constant pressure drop** across the valve.
- **Installed Characteristic:** The actual relationship in the working piping system, where pressure drop varies with flow (because of pump curves and piping friction).
- **Hysteresis:** Dead band — the difference in stem position for the same controller output while increasing vs. decreasing (typically < 1% of full stroke for quality valves).
- **Dead Band:** Smallest range of input signal that produces no noticeable output change.
- **Seat Leakage Class:** ANSI/FCI 70-2 classification (Class I to Class VI). Petrochemical service typically demands **Class IV, V, or VI** (bubble-tight shutoff).

## 2.2 Inherent Flow Characteristics — The Three Sacred Curves

In every KTU 2024 paper, at least one of these characteristics is asked either as a definition, a graph-sketch, or a "select-the-right-valve" application.

### A. Quick-Opening Characteristic
- Produces a large change in flow for a small initial stem movement.
- Relationship: $\dfrac{Q}{Q_{max}} = \sqrt{x}$
- **Application:** On/Off service, safety interlocks, trip valves. NOT used for throttling.
- *Example: Quick-opening ball valve on a furnace burner fuel line.*

### B. Linear Characteristic
- Flow changes **linearly** with stem position.
- Relationship: $\dfrac{Q}{Q_{max}} = x$
- **Application:** When the system pressure drop is **constant** and the process gain is linear (e.g., level control, simple liquid flow loops).
- *Example: Linear globe valve on a small condensate receiver.*

### C. Equal Percentage Characteristic
- For a given equal increment of stem travel, the flow changes by a constant percentage of the **previous** flow.
- Relationship: $\dfrac{Q}{Q_{max}} = R^{(x-1)}$ , where R is the rangeability (typically 20 to 50).
- **Why "Equal Percentage"?** Because every 1% increase in stem travel produces, say, a 5% increase in flow — **regardless** of where the valve is currently operating (10% open or 80% open). This provides excellent **resolution at low flows** and **high capacity at high flows**.
- **Application:** The most common valve in petrochemical throttling service — temperature, pressure, and composition control loops. Roughly **70–80% of all control valves** in a refinery are equal percentage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Equal Percentage Dominates Petrochemicals:** In long pipelines with significant pipe friction, the available pressure drop across the valve decreases as flow increases (because more pressure is lost to pipe friction). The equal percentage curve compensates for this drop-off, yielding a near-linear *installed* characteristic. This is a classic KTU "why" question.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning / Variables | Typical Units | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_v = Q \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{G_f}{\Delta P}}$ | Liquid sizing equation (volumetric flow form) | Q in gpm, $\Delta P$ in psi, $G_f$ = specific gravity | Liquid service (non-cavitating, non-flashing) |
| 2 | $C_v = \dfrac{W}{N \cdot \sqrt{\Delta P \cdot \rho}}$ | Liquid sizing equation (mass flow form) | W in lb/hr, $\rho$ in lb/ft³, N = 63.3 (SI: 24.6 for K_v) | Heavy hydrocarbons, multi-phase |
| 3 | $Q = C_v \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P}{G_f}}$ | Flow from valve coefficient | gpm | Quick flow calculation |
| 4 | $\dfrac{Q}{Q_{max}} = x$ | Linear characteristic | dimensionless | Linear trim selection |
| 5 | $\dfrac{Q}{Q_{max}} = R^{(x-1)}$ | Equal percentage characteristic | dimensionless, R = 20–50 | Default trim in refining |
| 6 | $\dfrac{Q}{Q_{max}} = \sqrt{x}$ | Quick opening characteristic | dimensionless | On/off service |
| 7 | $K_v \approx 0.865 \cdot C_v$ | Unit conversion | m³/h vs gpm | Exam cross-questions |
| 8 | $\Delta P_{allowed} = P_1 - P_v$ | Avoid cavitation — pressure drop must not exceed (inlet − vapor pressure) | psi or bar | Pumped hydrocarbon service |
| 9 | $R = \dfrac{Q_{max}}{Q_{min}}$ | Rangeability | dimensionless | Sizing, controllability |
| 10 | $\sigma = \dfrac{P_1 - P_v}{P_1 - P_2}$ | Cavitation index; cavitation if $\sigma < 1$ | dimensionless | Cavitation check |
| 11 | $I = \dfrac{\Delta P_{valve}}{\Delta P_{system}}$ | Valve authority (ideally 0.5) | dimensionless | Sizing & dynamics |
| 12 | $W = C_v \cdot \sqrt{x \cdot \Delta P \cdot \rho}$ | Mass flow through valve with trim position | lb/hr | Energy balance in heat exchangers |

## 2.4 Pressure Controllers — The "Brain" for Pressure Loops

A **Pressure Controller** is a specialized PID controller whose process variable (PV) input is wired to a **pressure transmitter** and whose output is wired to a **control valve** on a pressure-relieving or pressure-modulating line. In petrochemical service, pressure control is the **most safety-critical** loop because over-pressurization can rupture vessels and cause catastrophic loss of containment.

### Types of Pressure Controllers (by Action)

**A. Direct-Acting Pressure Controller**
- Output **increases** when PV (pressure) **increases** above setpoint.
- Used for **cooling** service — e.g., valve on a coolant line: higher pressure → open coolant valve more.

**B. Reverse-Acting Pressure Controller**
- Output **decreases** when PV (pressure) **increases** above setpoint.
- Used for **venting/relief** service — e.g., valve on a flare line: higher pressure → close vent (or open relief).

### Types of Pressure Controllers (by Hardware)

1. **Self-Operated Pressure Regulator** — No external power. Uses process pressure acting on a diaphragm to directly position the valve. Cheap, simple, used in low-pressure gas regulation at wellheads.
2. **Pilot-Operated Pressure Controller** — A small pilot valve senses pressure and uses process fluid to amplify the force on the main valve diaphragm. Used for high-pressure natural gas distribution.
3. **Electronic/Microprocessor Pressure Controller** — A PID controller with a 4–20 mA input from a pressure transmitter, output to an I/P transducer and pneumatic actuator. Standard in modern DCS-controlled refineries.
4. **Differential Pressure Controller** — Senses $\Delta P$ across an orifice, filter, or heat exchanger. Used to detect fouling (rising $\Delta P$ at constant flow).

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Petrochemical Engineering

- **Crude Distillation Column:** Pressure controller on overhead receiver vents fixed gas pressure to flare at ~1.05 kg/cm². Failure → pressure surge → safety relief valve lifts → environmental release.
- **Catalytic Reformer:** Pressure controller on recycle hydrogen loop maintains H₂ partial pressure at 20–30 bar to prevent coke deposition.
- **Hydrotreater:** Differential pressure controller across the reactor alerts to plugging when $\Delta P$ exceeds 2.0 bar.
- **FCC Unit Main Fractionator:** Top pressure controlled by a back-pressure valve on the wet gas compressor suction; failure causes compressor surge.
- **Storage Sphere (LPG):** Pressure controller on vapor return line; opens to flare at 0.5 bar to prevent BLEVE (Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Liquid Valve Sizing Equation ($C_v$)

We begin from **Bernoulli's equation** for an incompressible fluid flowing through a restriction (the valve), combined with the continuity equation and a discharge coefficient.

**Step 1: Apply Bernoulli's Equation across the valve.**

Between the upstream point (1) and the vena contracta (vc) just downstream of the seat restriction:

$$
P_1 + \dfrac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 = P_{vc} + \dfrac{1}{2}\rho v_{vc}^2
$$

The downstream velocity $v_1$ is much smaller than the throat velocity $v_{vc}$ (since the valve is the restriction), so we can neglect $v_1^2$ with respect to $v_{vc}^2$ in the simplified orifice analysis. This gives:

$$
P_1 - P_{vc} = \dfrac{1}{2}\rho v_{vc}^2
$$

**Step 2: Define the pressure recovery factor $F_L$.**

Real valves do not fully recover the upstream pressure at the outlet; some kinetic energy is lost. The pressure at the actual valve outlet $P_2$ is related to $P_{vc}$ by:

$$
P_2 = P_{vc} + \dfrac{P_1 - P_{vc}}{F_L^2} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; P_1 - P_2 = F_L^2 (P_1 - P_{vc})
$$

Rearranging:

$$
P_1 - P_{vc} = \dfrac{P_1 - P_2}{F_L^2}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute into the velocity expression.**

$$
v_{vc} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_1 - P_{vc})}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho \cdot F_L^2}} = \dfrac{1}{F_L}\sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho}}
$$

**Step 4: Multiply by the discharge area $A$ to get volumetric flow.**

$$
Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot v_{vc} = \dfrac{C_d \cdot A}{F_L} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho}}
$$

**Step 5: Bundle all geometric constants into $C_v$.**

The flow coefficient $C_v$ is defined as the flow of **water** (density $\rho_w = 62.4$ lb/ft³) in **gpm** at a pressure drop of **1 psi** through the fully open valve. Plugging in these reference values yields:

$$
C_v = \dfrac{Q}{N \cdot \sqrt{\Delta P / G_f}} \;\;\text{where}\;\; N = 63.3 \text{ (in English units)}
$$

And the working equation is:

$$
\boxed{\,C_v = \dfrac{Q \sqrt{G_f}}{\sqrt{\Delta P}}\,}
$$

This is the single most important formula in Module 2 of KTU PEAET861. **Memorize it.**

## 3.2 Derivation: Equal Percentage Characteristic

The equal percentage trim is engineered so that a **constant percentage change in flow** results from a **constant increment in stem travel**.

**Step 1: Set up the differential condition.**

Let $x$ be the fractional stem travel. The condition for equal percentage behavior is:

$$
\dfrac{dQ}{Q} = k \cdot dx
$$

where $k$ is a constant (typically $k = \ln R$, where R is the rangeability).

**Step 2: Integrate both sides.**

$$
\int \dfrac{dQ}{Q} = \int k \cdot dx \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \ln Q = k x + C
$$

**Step 3: Apply boundary conditions.**

At $x = 0$ (valve fully closed), $Q = Q_{min}$. At $x = 1$ (valve fully open), $Q = Q_{max}$.

Substituting the lower limit:

$$
\ln Q_{min} = C \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; C = \ln Q_{min}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\ln Q - \ln Q_{min} = k x \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \ln \left(\dfrac{Q}{Q_{min}}\right) = k x
$$

**Step 4: Apply the upper limit to find $k$.**

At $x = 1$, $Q = Q_{max}$:

$$
k = \ln \left(\dfrac{Q_{max}}{Q_{min}}\right) = \ln R
$$

**Step 5: Solve for $Q/Q_{max}$.**

$$
\ln \left(\dfrac{Q}{Q_{min}}\right) = (\ln R) \cdot x
$$

$$
\dfrac{Q}{Q_{min}} = R^x
$$

Since $Q_{min} = Q_{max} / R$ (by definition of rangeability), we get the canonical KTU form:

$$
\boxed{\,\dfrac{Q}{Q_{max}} = R^{(x - 1)}\,}
$$

For typical R = 50, this curve is highly "bowed" — almost closed for the first 20% of travel, then exponential.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Valve Sizing for a KTU Exam Problem

**Problem (modeled on KTU ESE pattern):**
> A control valve is to be installed on a kerosene line. The flow rate is **200 gpm** of kerosene (specific gravity $G_f = 0.82$) at a pressure drop of **15 psi**. Calculate the required $C_v$ and recommend a valve size from the standard series: $C_v = 30,\ 60,\ 95,\ 160$.

**Step 1: Identify the applicable equation.**

Since the fluid is a non-cavitating, non-flashing liquid at moderate pressure, we use the basic liquid equation:

$$
C_v = Q \sqrt{\dfrac{G_f}{\Delta P}}
$$

**Step 2: Plug in the values.**

$$
C_v = 200 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{0.82}{15}}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the ratio inside the square root.**

$$
\dfrac{0.82}{15} = 0.05467
$$

**Step 4: Take the square root.**

$$
\sqrt{0.05467} = 0.2338
$$

**Step 5: Multiply by the flow rate.**

$$
C_v = 200 \cdot 0.2338 = 46.76
$$

**Step 6: Select the next standard size up (always round up for safety margin).**

The required $C_v$ is **46.76**. The next larger standard valve is **$C_v = 60$**.

**Step 7: Check the actual operating authority.**

Valve authority $I = \dfrac{\Delta P_{valve}}{\Delta P_{system}}$. Assuming system $\Delta P = 30$ psi (valve 15 psi + pipe 15 psi):

$$
I = \dfrac{15}{30} = 0.5
$$

This is in the **ideal range (0.3 to 0.7)**, so the sizing is correct.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Judgment:** If the calculated $C_v$ had been, say, 58, the engineer would still select the $C_v = 60$ valve but operate it at slightly less than full travel (~96%). If $C_v$ had been 62, the engineer might select $C_v = 95$ to leave room for future capacity increases. This is a typical KTU "design decision" question.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Control Valves vs. Pressure Controllers

| Criterion | Control Valve | Pressure Controller |
|---|---|---|
| **Role in loop** | Final control element (muscle) | Decision-making element (brain) |
| **Input** | Pneumatic 3–15 psig or electronic 4–20 mA | Process variable 4–20 mA from transmitter |
| **Output** | Mechanical stem position → flow | 4–20 mA to I/P or actuator |
| **Failure mode** | Air-to-close (fail closed) or air-to-open (fail open) | Depends on software configuration |
| **Time response** | Slow (1–10 s stroke time) | Fast (PID scan < 0.1 s) |
| **Sizing parameter** | $C_v$ or $K_v$ | Tuning constants $K_c$, $\tau_I$, $\tau_D$ |
| **Petrochemical example** | Reflux control valve on distillation column | Top pressure controller on overhead receiver |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete Control Valve Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Controller Output 4-20 mA] --> B[I/P Transducer]
    B --> C[Pneumatic Signal 3-15 psig]
    C --> D[Positioner]
    D --> E[Diaphragm Actuator]
    E --> F[Valve Stem Movement]
    F --> G[Valve Plug and Seat]
    G --> H[Process Flow Regulation]
    H --> I[Pressure Transmitter]
    I --> J[Controller PV Input]
    J --> A
    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333
    style B fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
    style G fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333
    style H fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333
    style I fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style J fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333
```

**Reading Guide:** The controller sends a 4–20 mA signal to the I/P (current-to-pressure) transducer, which converts it to a 3–15 psig pneumatic signal. The positioner compares this with the actual stem position and adjusts the pneumatic pressure to the diaphragm actuator until the stem is exactly where it should be. The stem moves the plug relative to the seat, modulating flow. The pressure transmitter measures the resulting process pressure and sends 4–20 mA back to the controller, closing the loop.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Pressure Control Loop in a Refinery Distillation Column

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SP[Setpoint Pressure 1.05 kg/cm2] --> SUM[ summing junction]
    PT[Pressure Transmitter on Overhead Receiver] --> SUM
    SUM --> PID[PID Pressure Controller]
    PID --> VALVE[Control Valve on Flare Line]
    VALVE --> VESSEL[Overhead Receiver Vessel]
    VESSEL --> PT
    style SP fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333
    style SUM fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333
    style PID fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333
    style VALVE fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333
    style VESSEL fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333
    style PT fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
```

**Reading Guide:** This is the classic "Pressure Control by Vent to Flare" loop. The setpoint is fixed (e.g., 1.05 kg/cm²). The pressure transmitter sends the measured pressure to the summing junction, where it is compared with the setpoint. The error is fed to the PID controller, which adjusts the flare-line control valve. If pressure rises above setpoint, the valve opens to vent gas to the flare header, lowering pressure.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Types of Control Valves

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CV[Control Valve Family] --> GLOBE[Globe Valve]
    CV --> BALL[Ball Valve]
    CV --> BF[Butterfly Valve]
    CV --> GATE[Gate Valve]
    CV --> PLUG[Plug Valve]
    GLOBE --> G1[Linear motion single seat]
    GLOBE --> G2[Linear motion double seat]
    GLOBE --> G3[Angle type]
    BALL --> B1[V port for throttling]
    BALL --> B2[Full port for on off]
    BF --> F1[Concentric disc]
    BF --> F2[Eccentric disc for high temp]
    GATE --> GT1[Slab gate for isolation only]
    PLUG --> P1[V notch for slurry service]
    style CV fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style GLOBE fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333
    style BALL fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style BF fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
    style GATE fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333
    style PLUG fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333
```

**Reading Guide:** This taxonomy shows the most common control valve families in petrochemical service. **Globe valves** dominate throttling service. **Ball valves with V-port** are used for slurry and viscous service. **Butterfly valves** are used on large-diameter (≥ 12 inch) lines such as flare and cooling-water headers. **Gate valves** are isolation-only — never used for throttling. **Plug valves** (V-notch) are workhorses in coker and slurry hydrocracker service.

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Pressure Controller Action (Direct vs. Reverse)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DR[Direct Acting Controller]
        D1[Pressure RISES] --> D2[Output RISES]
        D2 --> D3[Valve OPENS MORE]
        D3 --> D4[Coolant or Quench Flow INCREASES]
        D4 --> D5[Pressure FALLS back to SP]
    end
    subgraph RR[Reverse Acting Controller]
        R1[Pressure RISES] --> R2[Output FALLS]
        R2 --> R3[Valve CLOSES]
        R3 --> R4[Vent or Relief Flow DECREASES]
        R4 --> R5[Pressure FALLS back to SP]
    end
    style DR fill:#E0FFE0,stroke:#006400
    style RR fill:#FFE0E0,stroke:#8B0000
```

**Reading Guide:** In **direct action**, the controller output rises with the PV. This is used when the manipulated variable must **increase** to bring PV down (e.g., open a coolant valve). In **reverse action**, the output falls as PV rises — used when manipulated flow must **decrease** to bring PV down (e.g., close a vent valve that was venting excess gas). Choosing the wrong action causes positive feedback and process runaway — a classic KTU pitfall.
