<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Thermowells — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermowell:** A closed-end, pressure-tight metallic tubular protective device installed into a process vessel or pipeline that physically isolates and shields a temperature sensor (RTD, thermocouple, or bimetal) from the hostile process environment, while still allowing rapid heat transfer between the fluid and the sensing element.

In petrochemical plants, where line pressures routinely exceed **40 bar** and process temperatures may swing from cryogenic LNG service ($-162 \degree C$) to super-heated reformer outlets ($850 \degree C$), direct sensor immersion is unsafe and uneconomical. The thermowell is therefore the **mechanical interface** between the world of the process fluid and the world of the instrument.

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Bodyguard with a Stethoscope"

Imagine a doctor pressing a stethoscope against a patient's chest. The patient is the **process fluid**, the chest wall is the **thermowell**, and the stethoscope diaphragm is the **sensor**. The doctor never touches the heart directly, yet can "hear" everything happening inside. The thermowell is exactly that — a metal **shield that lets thermal energy pass but keeps corrosion, pressure, and velocity impact away from the fragile sensor**.

### Constituents of a Thermowell Assembly

| Component | Function | Petrochemical-Grade Material |
|---|---|---|
| Stem (shank) | Conducts heat to sensor | SS 316, SS 304, Inconel 600, Monel 400, Hastelloy C |
| Tip (closed end) | Houses sensor tip | Same as stem; may be tapered/stepped for vibration resistance |
| Process connection (flange / threaded / weld-in) | Mechanical mounting | ASME B16.5 flanges, NPT threads, socket-weld |
| Lagging extension | Keeps sensor out of insulation | Carbon steel or SS |
| Insertion length (U) | Immersion depth into pipe | Engineered per ASME PTC 19.3 TW |

> [!NOTE]
> **Wake-frequency rule (ASME PTC 19.3):** The natural frequency of the thermowell must always be at least **1.6 times** the vortex-shedding frequency to prevent destructive resonant fatigue failure — a critical petrochemical design constraint.

---

## 1.2 Temperature Controllers — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Temperature Controller:** A feedback instrument that continuously compares the **Process Variable (PV)** — measured temperature — with a pre-set **Set Point (SP)**, computes an **Error (e)**, and adjusts the final control element (heater, steam valve, cooling damper, etc.) to drive the error toward zero.

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Cruise Control of a Car"

A car's cruise control is the perfect mental image. The driver (operator) sets **100 km/h** (set point). The speedometer (RTD/TC) reports the current speed (PV). The controller decides whether to press the accelerator harder or back off (control output). The same three-term logic — *proportional, integral, derivative* — applies to a distillation column's reboiler steam valve as to a Toyota's throttle.

### Major Categories of Temperature Controllers

| Category | Working Principle | Petrochemical Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| ON–OFF (two-position) | Output switches fully ON or OFF at SP | Small reheat boilers, alarm trips |
| Proportional (P) | Output $\propto$ error | Jacketed reactor coolant valves |
| PI (Proportional + Integral) | Eliminates steady-state offset | Distillation column reflux control |
| PID (Proportional + Integral + Derivative) | Anticipates error trend | Cracker furnace outlet temperature |
| Cascade (master–slave) | Two loops nested | Reactor temp $\rightarrow$ coolant flow slave loop |

---

## 1.3 Electromagnetic Flow Meter (Magmeter) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electromagnetic Flow Meter:** A volumetric flow-measuring instrument that operates on **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**. A conductive liquid flowing through a magnetic field generated by external coils induces a voltage proportional to the mean velocity of the flow; this voltage, picked up by electrodes in contact with the fluid, is processed to yield a linear flow rate reading.

### Intuitive Analogy — "The River that Generates Electricity"

Picture a river flowing between two magnetic mountain ranges. As the water (conductive liquid) moves, the magnetic field pushes its ionic "charges" sideways, creating a tiny **voltage difference between the river banks** — exactly like a hydroelectric generator. The faster the river, the larger the voltage. The river banks are our **electrodes**, and the **mountains** are the **electromagnetic coils**.

> [!NOTE]
> The induced voltage is **independent** of the fluid's viscosity, density, or turbulence — making magmeters ideal for crude oils, slurries, acids, and brine that foul other meters.

---

## 1.4 Applications of Temperature Measurement in Petrochemical Processes — Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Temperature is the **single most-measured variable** in any refinery or petrochemical complex because virtually every chemical reaction rate, phase change, and separation efficiency is exponentially dependent on temperature (Arrhenius behaviour). A modern integrated refinery typically has between **3,000 and 5,000 temperature measurement points**.

### Key Process Units Where Temperature is Critical

| Process Unit | Typical Sensor | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| Atmospheric & Vacuum Distillation Columns | TC/RTD tray thermometers | $300 \degree C - 400 \degree C$ |
| Fluid Catalytic Cracker (FCCU) Riser/Reactor | Type K / Type N sheathed TC | $500 \degree C - 750 \degree C$ |
| Catalytic Reformer Heaters | Platinum RTD (Pt100) | $450 \degree C - 550 \degree C$ |
| Hydrocracker Reactors | High-pressure thermowell + TC | $300 \degree C - 450 \degree C$ |
| Ethylene Cracker Furnace (Coil Outlet) | Type K / Type S with thermowell | $750 \degree C - 900 \degree C$ |
| LNG Liquefaction Trains | Platinum RTD (low-temp) | $-162 \degree C - 50 \degree C$ |
| Delayed Coker Drums | Multi-point TC with thermowell | $400 \degree C - 500 \degree C$ |
| Storage Tank Skin | Averaging RTD | Ambient to $80 \degree C$ |
| Heat Exchanger Shell / Tube | Thermowell-mounted RTD | $-50 \degree C - 350 \degree C$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Thermowell — Engineering Analysis

A thermowell is a slender cantilever beam immersed in a moving fluid. Three failure modes must be designed against simultaneously:

1. **Static pressure stress** — bending + hoop stress
2. **Vortex-induced vibration** — alternating lift forces
3. **Galvanic / erosion / corrosion thinning** — long-term metallurgical decay

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Thermowell Design Logic

- **Step 1 — Identify the worst-case operating point:** maximum pressure, maximum temperature, maximum flow velocity, and **density at operating temperature** (not at ambient). For hot hydrocarbon vapours, density falls below **5 kg/m³**, but velocity rises; the dynamic pressure is what matters.

- **Step 2 — Compute the static stress** at the root of the well (where it enters the pipe wall). The von Mises equivalent stress must remain below the allowable stress of the material (per **ASME B31.3**).

- **Step 3 — Compute the Strouhal-based vortex shedding frequency.** The frequency at which alternating vortices peel off the wake is the **vortex-shedding frequency**, $f_v$.

- **Step 4 — Compute the thermowell's natural (resonant) frequency** treating it as a cantilever beam with tip mass.

- **Step 5 — Verify the natural frequency is at least 1.6 times $f_v$.** If not, switch to a **stepped-shank** or **tapered-shank** design to raise the natural frequency, or reduce the insertion length.

- **Step 6 — Check the ASME PTC 19.3 "frequency-ratio graph"** to ensure the design lies in the safe region.

### 2.1.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Thermowell Mechanics

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Vortex shedding frequency | $f_v = \dfrac{St \cdot V}{D}$ | Hz | $St \approx 0.22$ for cylinders, $V$ = fluid velocity, $D$ = thermowell OD |
| Strouhal number | $St = \dfrac{f_v \cdot D}{V}$ | dimensionless | $St \approx 0.2$ to $0.3$ for typical Reynolds range |
| Cantilever natural frequency | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{3 \cdot E \cdot I}{L^3 (m_{tip} + 0.236\,m_{stem})}}$ | Hz | $E$ = Young's modulus, $I$ = area moment, $L$ = insertion length |
| Dynamic pressure | $q = \dfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2$ | Pa | $\rho$ evaluated at process temperature |
| Drag force on well | $F_D = C_D \cdot q \cdot A$ | N | $C_D \approx 0.7$ for flow normal to cylinder |
| Frequency ratio criterion | $\dfrac{f_n}{f_v} \geq 1.6$ | dimensionless | ASME PTC 19.3 mandatory rule |
| Time response (1st-order lag) | $t_{63} = \tau$ | s | Lower for thinner wall, higher conductivity |

---

## 2.2 Temperature Controllers — Deep Theory

The classical feedback control loop has four parts: **Measurement → Comparison → Computation → Actuation**.

### 2.2.1 The PID Algorithm

The **parallel form** of the PID controller computes the control output $u(t)$ as:

$$u(t) = K_c \, e(t) + \dfrac{K_c}{\tau_I}\int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_c \tau_D \dfrac{mathrm{d}e(t)}{mathrm{d}t}$$

where:
- $K_c$ = proportional gain (dimensionless or %/%)
- $\tau_I$ = integral time (seconds, also called "reset time")
- $\tau_D$ = derivative time (seconds, also called "rate time")
- $e(t) = SP - PV$ = error signal

In the **ideal (non-interacting) form** commonly used in textbooks:

$$G_c(s) = K_c \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I s} + \tau_D s\right)$$

In the **Laplace domain**, this becomes the **transfer function**:

$$\dfrac{U(s)}{E(s)} = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$$

where the three terms correspond to the proportional, integral, and derivative contributions.

### 2.2.2 The Role of Each Term — Engineering Intuition

| Term | Effect on System | Risk of Over-tuning |
|---|---|---|
| **P** | Reduces rise time, increases speed of response | Large steady-state offset remains |
| **I** | Eliminates steady-state error (offset) | Introduces oscillation and overshoot |
| **D** | Damps oscillations, anticipates future error | Amplifies measurement noise |

### 2.2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — PID & Tuning

| Quantity | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Error signal | $e(t) = SP - PV$ | Sign convention may reverse depending on direct/indirect acting |
| Controller output | $u(t) = K_c e + \dfrac{K_c}{\tau_I}\int e\,dt + K_c \tau_D \dfrac{de}{dt}$ | Parallel / non-interacting form |
| Transfer function (ideal PID) | $G_c(s) = K_p\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s\right)$ | Used in Ziegler–Nichols tuning |
| P-only steady-state offset | $\text{offset} = \dfrac{1}{1+K_p K_v}$ | $K_v$ = process gain |
| Ziegler–Nichols ultimate gain | $K_u$ from sustained oscillation | $K_p = 0.6 K_u$ for PID |
| Ziegler–Nichols ultimate period | $P_u$ (seconds) | $T_i = 0.5 P_u$, $T_d = 0.125 P_u$ |
| Cohen–Coon PID (process-model) | $K_p = \dfrac{1}{K}\dfrac{\tau}{\theta}\left(1 + \dfrac{\theta}{3\tau}\right)$ | For self-regulating processes |
| Cohen–Coon integral time | $T_i = \theta \dfrac{30 + 3(\theta/\tau)}{9 + 20(\theta/\tau)}$ | seconds |
| Cohen–Coon derivative time | $T_d = \theta \dfrac{4}{11 + 2(\theta/\tau)}$ | seconds |
| ITAE criterion | $\text{ITAE} = \int_0^T t \, \vert e(t)\vert\,dt$ | Use `\vert e \vert` in LaTeX to avoid table conflict |

### 2.2.4 Real-World Utility in Petrochemical Plants

- **Cascade control** of an exothermic reactor: the *master* loop measures reactor temperature; the *slave* loop measures and controls coolant flow rate. The master updates the slave's set point — this filters disturbances in coolant supply pressure before they reach the reactor.
- **Feed-forward control** uses a flow transmitter on the feed to anticipate heat demand before the reactor temperature drifts, complementing the feedback PID.
- **Split-range control** routes the controller output to two valves: a heating steam valve (3–9 mA) and a cooling water valve (9–15 mA) on a single 4–20 mA loop.

---

## 2.3 Electromagnetic Flow Meter — Deep Theory

### 2.3.1 Faraday's Law Applied to Flow

When a conductor of length $L$ moves with velocity $v$ perpendicular to a magnetic field of flux density $B$, the induced EMF is:

$$E = B \cdot L \cdot v$$

For a fully developed axisymmetric flow through a circular pipe of internal diameter $D$ inside a uniform magnetic field perpendicular to the flow axis and to the diameter joining the two electrodes, the integrating effect of the flow profile yields:

$$E = B \cdot D \cdot \bar{v}$$

where $\bar{v}$ is the **mean fluid velocity** across the cross-section (this is one of the elegant properties of the magmeter — the integration over the velocity profile is *exact* for any axisymmetric profile, not only fully developed turbulent flow).

The volumetric flow rate follows as:

$$Q = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \, \bar{v} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \bar{v} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$$

Substituting:

$$E = B \cdot D \cdot \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2} = \dfrac{4B}{\pi D} \cdot Q$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\,Q = \dfrac{\pi D \cdot E}{4B}\,}$$

This shows a **linear** relationship between induced voltage $E$ and volumetric flow $Q$ — the source of the magmeter's excellent linearity.

### 2.3.2 Derivation of the Velocity Profile Independence

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A distinguishing feature of the electromagnetic flow meter is that the weight function of the induced EMF integrates the entire velocity profile — so any axisymmetric flow (laminar, turbulent, or transitional) gives the **same** relationship between $E$ and $\bar{v}$. This is why magmeters do **not** require Reynolds-number correction.

### 2.3.3 Step-by-Step Working Logic

- **Step 1 — Coil excitation:** External coils wrapped around the non-magnetic flow tube generate a strong, uniform, time-varying magnetic field (modern meters use **pulsed DC** to avoid polarization of electrodes in electrolytic fluids).
- **Step 2 — Ionic charge movement:** Charged species in the conductive fluid experience a Lorentz force $F = q\,v \times B$, deflecting positive ions to one electrode and negative ions to the other.
- **Step 3 — EMF detection:** Two electrodes, flush-mounted on the inner wall (diametrically opposite, perpendicular to both the flow axis and the field axis), pick up the resulting micro-volt-level potential.
- **Step 4 — Signal processing:** The microvolt signal is amplified, filtered, and the noise (from slurry abrasion, electrochemical potentials) is suppressed using synchronous detection locked to the coil's drive frequency.
- **Step 5 — Output:** A 4–20 mA / HART / Foundation Fieldbus signal proportional to $Q$ is delivered to the DCS.

### 2.3.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Electromagnetic Flow Meter

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Faraday's EMF | $E = B \cdot D \cdot \bar{v}$ | V | $B$ in T, $D$ in m, $\bar{v}$ in m/s |
| Mean velocity | $\bar{v} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$ | m/s | From continuity |
| Volumetric flow | $Q = \dfrac{\pi D \cdot E}{4B}$ | m³/s | Linear in $E$ |
| Mass flow | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q$ | kg/s | $\rho$ = fluid density at operating $T$ |
| Reynolds number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \bar{v} D}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | Magmeter works on any axisymmetric flow |
| Minimum conductivity required | $\sigma_{\min} \geq 5 \,\mu S/cm$ | siemens/cm | Tap water fails; sea water passes; oils fail |
| Coil excitation types | DC $\rightarrow$ AC $\rightarrow$ Pulsed DC | — | Pulsed DC standard since 1970s |
| Accuracy class | $\pm 0.5\%$ of rate typical | — | Calibrated against master meter or gravimetric rig |

### 2.3.5 Why Magmeters are Critical in Petrochemicals

- **No moving parts** — the only flow meter with zero obstruction in the flow path; ideal for **catalyst slurries** in FCCU and coker applications.
- **No pressure loss** — important in long transfer lines from tank farms to crude units.
- **Independence from viscosity, density, and turbulence** — a single calibration holds for hot, cold, light, or heavy hydrocarbons.
- **Bi-directional measurement** — the sign of $E$ reverses with flow direction.

### 2.3.6 Limitations

> [!CAUTION]
> - The fluid **must be electrically conductive** (≥ 5 µS/cm). Pure hydrocarbons, refined oils, and liquefied petroleum gases **cannot** be measured by magmeter.
> - **Gas entrainment** and **slurry abrasion** of electrode surfaces cause drift over time.
> - Requires **straight pipe runs** (typically 5D upstream, 3D downstream) to avoid asymmetric flow profile that introduces ±1–2 % error.
> - Liner materials (PTFE, ETFE, polyurethane, rubber) limit **temperature** (often $\leq 180\degree C$) and **pressure** ratings.

---

## 2.4 Temperature Measurement Applications — Deep Theory

In a petrochemical plant, the temperature is the **silent governor** of every chemical and physical process. Five temperature-measurement architectures dominate:

1. **Single-point TC/RTD** with thermowell — the workhorse of the industry.
2. **Multi-point averaging RTD** — multiple RTD elements along a 1–30 m sheath; used in **catalyst beds** and **reactor internals** where radial and axial temperature profiles must be known for safe operation.
3. **Infrared pyrometers** — non-contact; used in ethylene cracker furnace tubes where contact would burn out.
4. **Thermocouples with noble-metal sheaths** (Pt/Rh) — used in the *hot-end* of FCC regenerators ($> 700 \degree C$).
5. **Fiber-optic temperature sensors** — immune to EMI; used near high-voltage motors and lightning-prone tanks.

### 2.4.1 Why Every Reactor Needs Redundant Temperature

In hydrocrackers, a runaway exotherm can raise the catalyst bed temperature by **50 $\degree C$ per minute**, leading to a temperature excursion that damages catalyst and may rupture the vessel. API RP 520 and IEC 61511 require **triple-redundant** temperature measurement with a **2-out-of-3 voting** logic for safety instrumented functions (SIF) of SIL-2 or higher.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Volumetric Flow Rate from Faraday's Law

**Given:** A magnetic flux density $B = 0.05\ T$ is established across a flow tube of internal diameter $D = 0.2\ m$. The induced EMF at the electrodes is measured to be $E = 1.25\ mV$. The process fluid is a crude-oil/water emulsion at operating temperature.

**Find:** The volumetric flow rate $Q$ in m³/h and the mean velocity $\bar{v}$ in m/s.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Write Faraday's equation for a fully developed flow in a circular pipe:**

$$E = B \cdot D \cdot \bar{v}$$

This is the special-case integrated form valid for any axisymmetric velocity profile.

**Step 2 — Solve for the mean velocity $\bar{v}$:**

$$\bar{v} = \dfrac{E}{B \cdot D}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values.** Convert units carefully — $E = 1.25\ mV = 1.25 \times 10^{-3}\ V$.

$$\bar{v} = \dfrac{1.25 \times 10^{-3}\ V}{0.05\ T \times 0.2\ m}$$

$$\bar{v} = \dfrac{1.25 \times 10^{-3}}{0.01}$$

$$\bar{v} = 0.125\ m/s$$

**Step 4 — Compute the volumetric flow rate using the continuity equation:**

$$Q = A \cdot \bar{v} = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot \bar{v}$$

$$A = \dfrac{\pi (0.2)^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.04}{4} = 0.01\pi\ m^2 \approx 0.0314159\ m^2$$

**Step 5 — Multiply $A$ by $\bar{v}$:**

$$Q = 0.0314159\ m^2 \times 0.125\ m/s = 0.00392699\ m^3/s$$

**Step 6 — Convert to m³/h:**

$$Q = 0.00392699 \times 3600 = 14.137\ m^3/h$$

**Step 7 — Final boxed answer:**

$$\boxed{\,\bar{v} = 0.125\ m/s \quad ; \quad Q \approx 14.14\ m^3/h\,}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Velocity from Linear EMF–Flow Relation

Starting from the relationship derived in §3.1:

$$E = \dfrac{4B}{\pi D} \cdot Q$$

**Step 1 — Show the linear proportionality explicitly:**

For fixed $B$ and $D$, define the **meter constant** $K_m$:

$$K_m = \dfrac{4B}{\pi D} \quad [V\,s/m^3]$$

**Step 2 — Write the compact linear form:**

$$E = K_m \cdot Q$$

This linearity is the *practical reason* a magmeter's calibration curve is a straight line through the origin, allowing a single trim factor to convert volts to m³/h.

**Step 3 — Mass-flow rate derivation:**

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q = \rho \cdot \dfrac{\pi D \cdot E}{4B}$$

For the crude-oil/water emulsion with $\rho = 920\ kg/m^3$:

$$\dot{m} = 920 \times 0.00392699 = 3.6128\ kg/s$$

**Step 4 — Convert to tonnes per day:**

$$\dot{m}_{day} = 3.6128 \times 86400 = 312{,}144\ kg/day \approx 312.1\ tonnes/day$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Thermowell Vortex-Shedding Frequency Check

**Given:** A stainless-steel 316 thermowell of **outside diameter $D = 25\ mm$** and **insertion length $L = 0.4\ m$** is installed in a 12-inch crude line. The fluid velocity at the maximum pumping rate is $V = 4.5\ m/s$. Strouhal number $St = 0.22$. Material: SS316 with $E = 193\ GPa$, density $\rho_{ss} = 8000\ kg/m^3$. The thermowell tip mass is $m_{tip} = 0.08\ kg$.

**Step 1 — Compute vortex shedding frequency:**

$$f_v = \dfrac{St \cdot V}{D}$$

Convert $D$ to metres: $D = 0.025\ m$.

$$f_v = \dfrac{0.22 \times 4.5}{0.025} = \dfrac{0.99}{0.025} = 39.6\ Hz$$

**Step 2 — Compute the moment of inertia** of a solid cylindrical stem of length $L = 0.4\ m$, diameter $d = 0.025\ m$:

$$I = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{64} = \dfrac{\pi (0.025)^4}{64}$$

$$(0.025)^4 = 3.90625 \times 10^{-7}\ m^4$$

$$I = \dfrac{\pi \times 3.90625 \times 10^{-7}}{64} = 1.917 \times 10^{-8}\ m^4$$

**Step 3 — Compute the stem mass:**

$$m_{stem} = \rho_{ss} \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot L = 8000 \times \dfrac{\pi \times 6.25 \times 10^{-4}}{4} \times 0.4$$

$$m_{stem} = 8000 \times 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.4 = 1.571\ kg$$

**Step 4 — Compute the natural frequency** of the cantilever:

$$f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{3 E I}{L^3 (m_{tip} + 0.236\,m_{stem})}}$$

Numerator inside the square root:

$$3EI = 3 \times 193 \times 10^9 \times 1.917 \times 10^{-8} = 11{,}098\ N \cdot m$$

Denominator inside the square root:

$$L^3 (m_{tip} + 0.236\,m_{stem}) = (0.4)^3 \times (0.08 + 0.236 \times 1.571)$$

$$L^3 = 0.064\ m^3$$

$$0.236 \times 1.571 = 0.3708\ kg$$

$$m_{tip} + 0.236\,m_{stem} = 0.08 + 0.3708 = 0.4508\ kg$$

Denominator product:

$$0.064 \times 0.4508 = 0.02885\ kg \cdot m^3$$

**Step 5 — Take the ratio and square root:**

$$\dfrac{3EI}{L^3(\ldots)} = \dfrac{11{,}098}{0.02885} = 384{,}679\ s^{-2}$$

$$\sqrt{384{,}679} = 620.2\ rad/s$$

**Step 6 — Convert to Hz:**

$$f_n = \dfrac{620.2}{2\pi} = \dfrac{620.2}{6.2832} \approx 98.7\ Hz$$

**Step 7 — Check the ASME frequency ratio:**

$$\dfrac{f_n}{f_v} = \dfrac{98.7}{39.6} = 2.49$$

**Step 8 — Verdict:**

Since $f_n / f_v = 2.49 \geq 1.6$, the design **passes** the ASME PTC 19.3 criterion. The thermowell is safe against vortex-induced vibration.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — PID Output Response to Step Error

A temperature controller with $K_c = 5$, $\tau_I = 60\ s$, $\tau_D = 10\ s$ receives a step error $e(t) = 2\degree C$ at $t = 0$.

**Step 1 — Write the parallel-form PID equation:**

$$u(t) = K_c e(t) + \dfrac{K_c}{\tau_I}\int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_c \tau_D \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the step input** $e(t) = 2$ for $t \geq 0$:

- $K_c e = 5 \times 2 = 10$ (constant)
- $\dfrac{K_c}{\tau_I} \int e\,dt = \dfrac{5}{60} \times 2 t = 0.1667\,t$ (ramp)
- $K_c \tau_D \dfrac{de}{dt} = 5 \times 10 \times \delta(t)$ (impulse at $t=0$)

**Step 3 — Final expression:**

$$u(t) = 10 + 0.1667\,t + 50\,\delta(t)\quad (t \geq 0)$$

**Step 4 — Engineering interpretation:** The instant the error appears, the derivative term delivers an *impulse* (an immediate burst of corrective action) — this is why derivative action is called "anticipatory." The proportional term gives an immediate base correction. The integral term keeps adding output as long as the error persists, eventually eliminating the offset.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Temperature Sensor vs. Application

| Application | Sensor Choice | Thermowell | Mounting | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| FCCU Riser Outlet ($750\degree C$, slurry) | Type K TC, MgO insulated, SS310 sheath | Stepped SS310L thermowell | Flanged, welded | Vibration resistance, high-temp sheath |
| Hydrocracker Bed (high H₂) | Type K TC with Inconel 600 sheath | Stellite-tipped Inconel well | High-pressure autoclave flange | Hydrogen attack resistance |
| LNG Vaporiser Outlet | Pt100 RTD Class A | SS316L thermowell | Flanged, immersion 100 mm | Low-temperature accuracy |
| Ethylene Cracker Coil | Type S/R TC, Alumina sheath | Recirculating ceramic well | Welded to coil | Long life at $900\degree C$ |
| Catalytic Reformer Reactor | Multipoint Pt100 (3 points) | Large thermowell | Side-entry nozzle | Profile mapping |
| Storage Tank Roof (floating) | Averaging Pt100 with SS sheath | None (direct immersion in thermowell-free head) | Top nozzle | Wide-range averaging |

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Flow Meter Selection in Petrochemical Plants

| Fluid | Best Flow Meter | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Crude oil (conductive brine emulsion) | Electromagnetic | No moving parts, no pressure loss |
| Refined diesel (non-conductive) | Coriolis | Direct mass, density, T compensation |
| Natural gas | Ultrasonic / Vortex | No wetted moving parts |
| Steam (saturated) | Vortex shedding | Excellent rangeability |
| Coker slurry (catalyst fines) | Electromagnetic with rubber liner | Abrasion-resistant liner |
| LNG (cryogenic $-162\degree C$) | Coriolis (low-T) or ultrasonic | Cryogenic compatibility |
| Cooling water | Electromagnetic (low cost) | Conductive, abundant |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Temperature Measurement Loop with Thermowell

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    procA[Process Fluid in Pipe] --> twA[Thermowell Stem]
    twA --> sensA[RTD or Thermocouple Sensor]
    sensA --> transA[Field Transmitter Head]
    transA --> cableA[Shielded 4-20 mA Cable]
    cableA --> dcsA[DCS Temperature Card]
    dcsA --> pidA[PID Controller Block]
    pidA --> valveA[Control Valve Steam or Coolant]
    valveA --> procA
    dcsA --> hmiA[Operator HMI Display]
    dcsA --> silA[SIL Alarm and Trip Logic]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The fluid in the pipe transfers heat through the thermowell wall to the sensor. The sensor produces a low-level mV (TC) or ohmic (RTD) signal. The transmitter converts it to 4–20 mA HART. The DCS reads the PV, executes the PID algorithm, and outputs a 4–20 mA signal to the control valve. The same PV is mirrored to the operator HMI and to a safety-instrumented function for high-temperature trips.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — PID Controller Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    spA[Set Point SP] --> sumA[Summing Junction]
    pvA[Process Variable PV] --> sumA
    sumA --> errA[Error e equals SP minus PV]
    errA --> propA[Proportional Block Kc]
    errA --> intA[Integral Block Kc over tauI times s]
    errA --> derA[Derivative Block Kc tauD times s]
    propA --> sumOut[Output Summer]
    intA --> sumOut
    derA --> sumOut
    sumOut --> opA[Control Output u t to Final Element]
    opA --> procB[Process]
    procB --> pvA
```

**Reading the diagram:** The error is computed and fed **in parallel** to three blocks — proportional, integral, and derivative. Each block scales the error differently. The three scaled signals are summed to form the controller output $u(t)$, which drives the final element. The final element changes the process, which changes the PV, closing the loop.

---

## 4.3 Electromagnetic Flow Meter — Functional Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    pipeA[Conductive Fluid in Lined Flow Tube] --> flowDir[Flow Axis]
    coilA[Excitation Coils around Tube] -->|generate B field| pipeA
    pipeA -->|Lorentz Force| ionsA[Positive Ions Drift to Electrode 1]
    pipeA -->|Lorentz Force| ionsB[Negative Ions Drift to Electrode 2]
    ionsA --> elec1[Electrode 1]
    ionsB --> elec2[Electrode 2]
    elec1 --> ampA[Differential Amplifier]
    elec2 --> ampA
    ampA --> sigProcA[Synchronous Detector and Filter]
    coilA -->|reference freq| sigProcA
    sigProcA --> convA[Analog to Digital Converter]
    convA --> calA[Calibration Multiplier K Calibration]
    calA --> outA[4-20 mA plus HART plus Display]
```

**Reading the diagram:** A conductive fluid flows through a non-magnetic tube (lined with PTFE/ETFE/rubber). External coils create a transverse magnetic field $B$. The Lorentz force on ionic charges separates positive from negative charges, accumulating them on two flush-mounted electrodes. The microvolt-level differential EMF is amplified, synchronously detected (using the coil drive frequency as reference), digitised, scaled by the calibration factor, and output as a standard 4–20 mA HART signal.

---

## 4.4 Cascade Control Block Diagram — Reactor Temperature

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sp1A[Reactor Temp SP] --> sum1A[Summing Junction Master]
    pv1A[Reactor Temp PV] --> sum1A
    sum1A --> err1A[Master Error]
    err1A --> masterPID[Master PID Controller]
    masterPID --> sp2A[Slave Set Point for Coolant Flow]
    sp2A --> sum2A[Slave Summing Junction]
    pv2A[Coolant Flow PV] --> sum2A
    sum2A --> err2A[Slave Error]
    err2A --> slavePID[Slave Flow PID]
    slavePID --> valveOp[Coolant Control Valve]
    valveOp --> reactorProc[Reactor]
    reactorProc --> pv1A
    valveOp --> coolantSys[Coolant Supply System]
    coolantSys --> pv2A
```

**Reading the diagram:** The outer (master) loop measures the *reactor temperature* and outputs a *coolant flow set point*. The inner (slave) loop measures the *coolant flow rate* and drives the control valve. Disturbances in coolant pressure are corrected by the *inner* loop before they reach the *outer* loop — this is the key advantage of cascade control.

---

## 4.5 Thermowell Mechanical Design Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start1[Start ASME PTC 19.3 Design] --> collect1[Gather Process Data P T V density viscosity]
    collect1 --> v1[Compute Vortex Shedding Frequency fv]
    collect1 --> q1[Compute Dynamic Pressure q]
    q1 --> fmax1[Compute Max Drag Force FD]
    fmax1 --> stress1[Compute Static Stress at Root]
    stress1 --> allow1[Compare to Allowable ASME B31.3]
    allow1 -->|fail| redesign1[Increase Root Diameter or Wall Thickness]
    redesign1 --> collect1
    allow1 -->|pass| natFreq1[Compute Cantilever Natural Frequency fn]
    natFreq1 --> ratio1[Compute fn over fv]
    ratio1 --> crit1{Is fn over fv at least 1.6}
    crit1 -->|no| taper1[Use Stepped or Tapered Shank]
    taper1 --> natFreq1
    crit1 -->|yes| material1[Check Material Compatibility Corrosion Erosion Hydrogen]
    material1 --> comp1[Complete Design Package]
```

**Reading the diagram:** A thermowell design is a two-axis problem: *static* (mechanical stress) and *dynamic* (vibration). The flowchart shows the iterative engineering process — if any check fails, the design is modified and re-evaluated.
