# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, PEAET861]

**Q: Define a thermowell. State any two materials commonly used for thermowells in petrochemical applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> A thermowell is a closed-end, pressure-tight tubular protective device installed in a process pipe or vessel to enclose a temperature sensor, isolating it from pressure, velocity, and corrosive media while allowing heat transfer.

**Two commonly used materials (1.5 marks each):**
- **SS 316 / SS 316L** — general-purpose refinery service; resists chlorides and most organic acids.
- **Inconel 600** — high-temperature (>600°C) service, hydrocracker reactors, ethylene furnaces.
- (Acceptable alternatives: Monel 400 for HF service, Hastelloy C for HCl, SS 304 for low-cost water/steam service.)

---

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PEAET861]

**Q: List the three modes of a PID controller and state the function of each mode in one sentence.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per mode):**

1. **Proportional (P) mode** — Produces a controller output proportional to the current error; reduces rise time but cannot eliminate steady-state offset.
2. **Integral (I) mode** — Produces a controller output proportional to the *integral* of the error over time; eliminates steady-state offset.
3. **Derivative (D) mode** — Produces a controller output proportional to the *rate of change* of error; anticipates future error and damps oscillations.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks (Choice Option)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PEAET861 Module 3]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of an Electromagnetic flow meter. State two advantages and one limitation of the same in petrochemical service.**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
- A non-magnetic flow tube (SS304 or SS316) lined with an electrically insulating material (PTFE, ETFE, polyurethane, hard rubber, or PFA) — the lining prevents the induced voltage from being shorted to the pipe wall.
- **Field coils** (electromagnets) mounted externally on top and bottom of the flow tube, energised by a low-frequency pulsed-DC current (~6–25 Hz) to generate a strong, uniform transverse magnetic field.
- **Two flush-mounted electrodes** (SS316, Hastelloy C, or platinum) diametrically opposite on the inner wall, perpendicular to both the flow axis and the magnetic field axis, in contact with the fluid.
- A transmitter housing containing the coil driver, signal amplifier, synchronous detector, A/D converter, microprocessor, and HART modem.

**Working (3 marks):**
- As the conductive liquid flows through the magnetic field, ions in the fluid experience a Lorentz force $\vec{F} = q\,\vec{v} \times \vec{B}$ that separates positive and negative charges.
- Positive charges accumulate on one electrode; negative charges on the other, producing a measurable EMF given by $E = B\,D\,\bar{v}$.
- This microvolt-level signal is amplified, synchronously detected at the coil drive frequency, and converted to a 4–20 mA HART signal proportional to volumetric flow.

**Advantages (0.5 marks each):**
- **No moving parts, no pressure loss** — ideal for slurries and viscous crude.
- **Independence from viscosity, density, and turbulence** — a single calibration holds across operating conditions.

**Limitation (0.5 marks):**
- The fluid must be **electrically conductive** (≥ 5 µS/cm); pure hydrocarbons (diesel, gasoline, naphtha, LPG) cannot be measured.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: A magmeter is installed on a 6-inch (ID = 0.154 m) crude-oil/water emulsion line. The coil produces a magnetic field $B = 0.08\ T$. The induced EMF measured is $E = 2.0\ mV$. Calculate: (i) the mean fluid velocity, (ii) the volumetric flow rate in m³/h, and (iii) the mass flow rate in tonnes/day, given the fluid density is $\rho = 940\ kg/m^3$.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Mean fluid velocity (2 marks):**

Using Faraday's equation for a circular pipe:

$$\bar{v} = \dfrac{E}{B \cdot D} = \dfrac{2.0 \times 10^{-3}}{0.08 \times 0.154}$$

$$\bar{v} = \dfrac{2.0 \times 10^{-3}}{0.01232} = 0.1623\ m/s$$

**(ii) Volumetric flow rate (2.5 marks):**

$$Q = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot \bar{v}$$

$$A = \dfrac{\pi (0.154)^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.02372}{4} = 0.01863\ m^2$$

$$Q = 0.01863 \times 0.1623 = 3.023 \times 10^{-3}\ m^3/s$$

Converting to m³/h:

$$Q = 3.023 \times 10^{-3} \times 3600 = 10.88\ m^3/h$$

**(iii) Mass flow rate (2.5 marks):**

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q = 940 \times 3.023 \times 10^{-3} = 2.842\ kg/s$$

Converting to tonnes per day:

$$\dot{m}_{day} = 2.842 \times 86400 / 1000 = 245.5\ tonnes/day$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A very common error here is *failing to convert units* — students frequently mix $mm$ and $m$ for diameter, or $mV$ and $V$ for EMF, or $kg/s$ and $tonnes/day$ for mass flow. Always write unit conversions explicitly in the solution. **A unit error loses 1–2 marks** even if the algebra is correct.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice Option)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PEAET861 Module 3]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: Explain with a block diagram the working of a PID temperature controller. Derive the transfer function of an ideal (non-interacting) PID controller in the Laplace domain.**

**Model Answer:**

**Block diagram description (3 marks):** A PID controller block diagram contains the following elements (in parallel form):
- **Summing junction** computing the error $e(t) = SP(t) - PV(t)$.
- **Proportional block** with gain $K_p$.
- **Integral block** with transfer function $\dfrac{1}{T_i s}$.
- **Derivative block** with transfer function $T_d s$.
- **Output summer** that adds the three signals to produce $u(t)$, which drives the final control element (heater, valve, etc.).
- A **feedback path** from the process to the summing junction.

**Derivation of the transfer function (4 marks):**

**Step 1 — Write the time-domain equation of the ideal non-interacting PID:**

$$u(t) = K_p \left[ e(t) + \dfrac{1}{T_i}\int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + T_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt} \right]$$

**Step 2 — Take the Laplace transform term by term** (assuming zero initial conditions):

- $\mathcal{L}\{e(t)\} = E(s)$
- $\mathcal{L}\left\{\int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau\right\} = \dfrac{E(s)}{s}$
- $\mathcal{L}\left\{\dfrac{de(t)}{dt}\right\} = s E(s)$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the equation:**

$$U(s) = K_p \left[ E(s) + \dfrac{1}{T_i}\dfrac{E(s)}{s} + T_d s E(s) \right]$$

**Step 4 — Factor $E(s)$ and $K_p$:**

$$U(s) = K_p \, E(s) \left[ 1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s \right]$$

**Step 5 — Write the final transfer function:**

$$\boxed{\,\dfrac{U(s)}{E(s)} = K_p \left( 1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s \right)\,}$$

This is the **ideal non-interacting form** of the PID controller. By clearing the denominators, the alternative **series (cascade) form** is sometimes written as:

$$G_c(s) = K_p \,\dfrac{(T_i T_d s^2 + T_i s + 1)}{T_i s}$$

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: A thermowell of 25 mm OD and 0.4 m insertion length is installed in a 12-inch crude line. Fluid velocity is 4.5 m/s, Strouhal number is 0.22, and the cantilever natural frequency of the thermowell is 98.7 Hz. Check whether the thermowell design satisfies the ASME PTC 19.3 frequency-ratio criterion. State the consequence of failure.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Compute vortex shedding frequency (2 marks):**

$$f_v = \dfrac{St \cdot V}{D} = \dfrac{0.22 \times 4.5}{0.025}$$

$$f_v = \dfrac{0.99}{0.025} = 39.6\ Hz$$

**Step 2 — Compute the frequency ratio (2 marks):**

$$\dfrac{f_n}{f_v} = \dfrac{98.7}{39.6} = 2.49$$

**Step 3 — Apply the ASME criterion (2 marks):**

The ASME PTC 19.3 requirement is:

$$\dfrac{f_n}{f_v} \geq 1.6$$

Since $2.49 \geq 1.6$, **the design PASSES the criterion**. The thermowell is safe against vortex-induced fatigue failure for the stated operating condition.

**Step 4 — Consequence of failure (1 mark):**

If the design had failed the criterion ($f_n/f_v < 1.6$), the thermowell would enter **resonance** with the alternating Karman vortex street, leading to:
- High-cycle fatigue failure at the root of the well within weeks to months.
- Catastrophic release of process fluid at high pressure and temperature.
- Potential fire, vapour cloud explosion, or personnel injury.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often confuse the natural frequency $f_n$ with the vortex-shedding frequency $f_v$ in the formula — reversing them gives a ratio of $0.40$, which the student then incorrectly judges as a *fail*. Always write **"natural over shedding"** $\dfrac{f_n}{f_v}$ in your answer to avoid the sign-flip trap. **A sign error loses 2 marks.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3**

- [x] **Thermowell definition:** Closed-end pressure-tight protective tube that isolates a temperature sensor from the process.
- [x] **Thermowell design rule:** $\dfrac{f_n}{f_v} \geq 1.6$ per ASME PTC 19.3.
- [x] **Stepped / tapered shanks** raise the natural frequency of a thermowell.
- [x] **Common thermowell materials:** SS 316, SS 304, Inconel 600, Monel 400, Hastelloy C.
- [x] **PID equation (parallel form):** $u(t) = K_p e + \dfrac{K_p}{T_i}\int e\,dt + K_p T_d \dfrac{de}{dt}$.
- [x] **PID transfer function:** $G_c(s) = K_p \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s\right)$.
- [x] **P action** reduces rise time, **I action** eliminates offset, **D action** damps oscillation.
- [x] **Ziegler–Nichols tuning:** $K_p = 0.6 K_u$, $T_i = 0.5 P_u$, $T_d = 0.125 P_u$.
- [x] **Cascade control** = master–slave nested loops (e.g., reactor temp $\rightarrow$ coolant flow).
- [x] **Split-range control** uses one controller output to drive two valves (heating + cooling).
- [x] **Electromagnetic flow meter equation:** $E = B \cdot D \cdot \bar{v}$.
- [x] **Volumetric flow from EMF:** $Q = \dfrac{\pi D \cdot E}{4B}$.
- [x] **Magmeter needs** fluid conductivity $\geq 5\ \mu S/cm$; **fails on pure hydrocarbons**.
- [x] **No moving parts, no pressure loss** — magmeters are ideal for slurries (FCCU, coker).
- [x] **Pulsed-DC excitation** is the modern standard — eliminates electrode polarisation.
- [x] **Temperature sensors in petrochemical units:** TC for high-T (FCCU, reformer), RTD for accuracy (LNG, reformer reactor), IR pyrometer for non-contact (cracker coil), fibre-optic for EMI zones.
- [x] **Triple-redundant** temperature measurement with **2-out-of-3 voting** for SIL-rated reactor safety.
- [x] **Averaging RTDs** map temperature profiles along catalyst beds and LNG tanks.
- [x] **Always convert units** explicitly in EMF-flow and mass-flow calculations — KTU examiners deduct heavily for unit errors.
- [x] **Faraday's Law** in SI: $E$ in volts, $B$ in tesla, $D$ in metres, $\bar{v}$ in m/s — no conversion factors needed.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ultrasonic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET861 - INSTRUMENTATION IN PETROCHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ultrasonic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Ultrasonics** refers to the branch of acoustics and instrumentation engineering that deals with the generation, transmission, reception, and application of **ultrasonic waves** — mechanical pressure waves whose frequency lies above the upper limit of human hearing, conventionally taken as **20 kHz**. In petrochemical instrumentation, ultrasonic methods are classified under **non-intrusive, non-destructive evaluation (NDE)** techniques and rely on the interaction of high-frequency sound with process media (liquids, slurries, gases) and with process equipment walls (vessels, pipes, heat exchangers).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *"Ultrasonic techniques use the propagation, reflection, refraction, and attenuation of high-frequency mechanical waves (typically 20 kHz to 10 MHz) to measure process variables such as flow rate, level, thickness, density, viscosity, and temperature in petrochemical plants."*

The underlying physics is governed by the **piezoelectric effect**, the **acoustic impedance mismatch** at material interfaces, and the **Doppler effect** for moving media.

## 1.2 Frequency Spectrum Classification

| Band | Frequency Range | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| Infrasound | < 20 Hz | Seismic, heavy machinery |
| Audible Sound | 20 Hz – 20 kHz | Human hearing |
| **Ultrasonic (Low)** | **20 kHz – 1 MHz** | **Flow, level, cleaning** |
| **Ultrasonic (High)** | **1 MHz – 10 MHz** | **NDT thickness, flaw detection** |
| Hypersonic | > 1 GHz | Specialized research |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Bat and the Pipeline"

Imagine a **bat** flying in a dark cave (your petrochemical plant). It cannot see, but it screams (emits an ultrasonic pulse) and listens for the echo. From the time taken for the echo to return, the bat calculates distance.

Now apply this to a refinery:
- The **transducer** is the "screaming bat" — it emits a burst of high-frequency sound.
- The **pipe wall, liquid surface, or a flaw inside metal** acts as the "echoing wall."
- The **receiver** listens for the echo and timestamps it.
- From the **time-of-flight (t)**, knowing the **speed of sound (c)** in the medium, the instrument computes the desired variable: $d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$.

For a moving fluid (crude oil through a pipeline), the same bat analogy works but with a twist — the **frequency of the echo shifts** because the fluid is moving. This is the **Doppler effect**, and it lets us measure flow velocity without inserting any sensor into the pipe.

## 1.4 Why Ultrasonic is Critical in Petrochemical Industries

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industrial Drivers:**
> 1. **No moving parts** — no wear, no corrosion, no process contamination.
> 2. **Intrinsically safe** — no electrical contact with the process fluid when using clamp-on (non-intrusive) transducers.
> 3. **Suitable for hazardous, abrasive, corrosive, and high-temperature fluids** (crude oil, acids, slurries, LPG, LNG, molten polymers).
> 4. **Bidirectional flow measurement** in custody-transfer applications.
> 5. **External mounting** possible → zero pressure drop, no shutdown required for installation.

## 1.5 Acoustic Impedance — The Foundation Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Specific Acoustic Impedance (Z):** The product of the medium's density and the speed of sound in that medium. It is the acoustic analogue of electrical impedance.
>
> $$Z = \rho \cdot c$$
>
> where $\rho$ = density (kg/m³) and $c$ = speed of sound (m/s). Units: **Rayl (kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹)** or **Pa·s/m**.

When an ultrasonic wave strikes an interface between two media, the **difference in acoustic impedance** determines how much energy is reflected versus transmitted. This is the cornerstone of every ultrasonic measurement.

| Medium | Density ρ (kg/m³) | Speed c (m/s) | Acoustic Impedance Z (Rayl) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Air (20 °C) | 1.2 | 343 | 411 |
| Water (20 °C) | 998 | 1480 | 1.48 × 10⁶ |
| Crude Oil | 870 | 1300 | 1.13 × 10⁶ |
| Stainless Steel | 7850 | 5790 | 45.5 × 10⁶ |
| PTFE (Teflon) | 2200 | 1400 | 3.08 × 10⁶ |

> [!NOTE]
> The **enormous** impedance difference between air (411 Rayl) and steel (45.5 × 10⁶ Rayl) is why an ultrasonic beam struggles to enter a metal from air — this is solved using a **couplant** (gel, water, or oil) that bridges the acoustic impedance gap.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Piezoelectric Effect — The Heart of Every Ultrasonic Transducer

The **piezoelectric effect** is the ability of certain crystalline materials (quartz, lithium niobate, lead zirconate titanate — PZT, polyvinylidene fluoride — PVDF) to convert mechanical stress into electrical charge (**direct effect**) and, conversely, to deform mechanically when an alternating voltage is applied (**converse effect**).

### Operating Logic — Step by Step
1. A high-frequency alternating voltage (typically 1–10 MHz, 100–400 V peak-to-peak) is applied across the faces of a piezoelectric crystal (the "transmitting transducer").
2. The crystal **resonates** at its natural frequency, mechanically vibrating and launching a pressure wave into the adjacent medium.
3. When the wave returns as an echo and strikes the same (or a paired) crystal, the mechanical deformation generates a small AC voltage.
4. This returning signal is amplified, time-stamped, and processed by the electronics to extract the process variable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Resonance Frequency Equation:**
> $$f_n = \frac{n}{2 \cdot t_p} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}}$$
>
> where $t_p$ = piezoelectric thickness, $E$ = Young's modulus, $\rho$ = density, $n$ = harmonic number (1, 3, 5, … for thickness-mode).

## 2.2 Modes of Wave Propagation

Ultrasonic waves propagate in three principal modes in solids and fluids:

| Mode | Particle Motion | Propagation In | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Longitudinal (L-wave)** | Parallel to propagation | Solids, liquids, gases | Most flow, level, NDT |
| **Transverse / Shear (S-wave)** | Perpendicular to propagation | Solids only | Flaw detection |
| **Surface (Rayleigh wave)** | Elliptical orbit | Solid surfaces | Crack detection |

In petrochemical fluids (liquids and gases), only **longitudinal waves** are relevant because fluids cannot support shear stress.

## 2.3 Generation, Detection, and Coupling

- **Generation:** A short electrical pulse (typically 50–400 V, 50 ns to a few μs wide) excites the PZT crystal at resonance. The mechanical Q-factor of the crystal is intentionally lowered ("damped" transducers) to produce a **short, broad-band pulse** — essential for good time resolution.
- **Detection:** A separate "receive" crystal (or the same crystal in pulse-echo mode with electronic T/R switching) captures the echo. The same physics works in reverse.
- **Coupling:** A thin layer of couplant (gel, grease, water) is mandatory to exclude air. Without couplant, >99.99% of the energy reflects at the air interface.

## 2.4 Attenuation of Ultrasonic Waves in Process Media

As the wave travels, its intensity decays exponentially:

$$I(x) = I_0 \cdot e^{-2 \alpha x}$$

where $\alpha$ is the **amplitude attenuation coefficient** (Np/m). A more practical form uses the **dB/m** form:

$$\alpha_{dB} = 20 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_0}{A(x)}\right) \Big/ x$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Attenuation grows with frequency** — this is why high-frequency (10 MHz) probes are used for fine flaw detection in thin metal, and low-frequency (20–200 kHz) probes are used for long-range flow measurement in large pipes.

## 2.5 Reflection and Transmission at an Interface

For a wave striking a boundary between two media with acoustic impedances $Z_1$ and $Z_2$:

$$R = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$$

$$T = \frac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2}$$

$$R + T = 1 \quad \text{(energy conservation)}$$

This is the **Rayleigh formula** for acoustic energy partition.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — every KTU question on ultrasonics draws from it.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $c = f \cdot \lambda$ | Wave equation: speed = freq × wavelength | m/s, Hz, m |
| 2 | $Z = \rho \cdot c$ | Specific acoustic impedance | Rayl |
| 3 | $R = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$ | Power reflection coefficient | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 4 | $T = \frac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2}$ | Power transmission coefficient | dimensionless |
| 5 | $I(x) = I_0 e^{-2 \alpha x}$ | Beam intensity after distance $x$ | W/m² |
| 6 | $d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | Distance from pulse-echo time-of-flight | m |
| 7 | $\Delta f = \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$ | Doppler frequency shift for flow | Hz |
| 8 | $Q = \frac{A \cdot \Delta t}{2 \tan\theta} \cdot \frac{D^2}{L}$ | Transit-time flow rate (V-path) | m³/s |
| 9 | $L = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | Liquid level by pulse-echo | m |
| 10 | $h = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | Wall thickness measurement | m |
| 11 | $v_{avg} = \frac{c^2 \tan\theta}{2 D} \Delta t$ | Mean flow velocity (transit-time) | m/s |
| 12 | $\eta = \frac{\rho c \lambda^2}{2 \pi^2}$ | Dynamic viscosity from ultrasonic damping | Pa·s |

Where, in the table: $c$ = speed of sound in the medium, $f$ = frequency, $\lambda$ = wavelength, $\rho$ = density, $\alpha$ = attenuation coefficient, $t$ = time, $d$ = distance, $f_0$ = transmitted frequency, $v$ = flow velocity, $\theta$ = beam angle w.r.t. pipe axis, $Q$ = volumetric flow, $A$ = pipe area, $D$ = pipe diameter, $L$ = path length, $\Delta t$ = upstream–downstream time difference, $h$ = thickness, $\eta$ = viscosity.

## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Petrochemical Plants

| Application | Variable Measured | Industry Use |
|---|---|---|
| Transit-time flowmeter | Volumetric flow | Custody transfer of crude, LNG, gasoline |
| Doppler flowmeter | Flow in dirty/slurry streams | Coal-water slurry, sewage in refineries |
| Non-contact level | Level in storage tanks | LPG bullets, crude tanks, acid sumps |
| Thickness gauge | Corrosion monitoring | Pipeline integrity, vessel wall loss |
| Ultrasonic viscometer | Viscosity | Lubricating oil blending, polymer melts |
| Leak detection | Acoustic emission | Underground product pipelines |
| Composition analysis | Density / concentration | Sulphuric acid, caustic concentration |
| Process control | Temperature (via $c(T)$) | Cryogenic LNG, high-temperature cracker furnaces |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Pulse-Echo Distance / Thickness Measurement

**Problem Setup:** A pulse-echo transducer launches a wave of speed $c$ into a medium and waits for the echo. Derive the distance to the reflector.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

The wave travels a one-way distance $d$ to the reflector in time $t_1$.

$$d = c \cdot t_1$$

The echo travels the same distance $d$ back to the transducer in time $t_2$.

$$d = c \cdot t_2$$

The **total round-trip time** measured by the instrument is $t = t_1 + t_2 = 2 t_1$.

$$t = \frac{2 d}{c}$$

Solving for $d$:

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor of **2** appears because the wave makes a **round trip** — out and back. This is the canonical "time-of-flight" equation for all pulse-echo ultrasonic measurements (thickness, level, flaw detection).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Acoustic Impedance and Reflection Coefficient

**Problem Setup:** A longitudinal wave of intensity $I_i$ strikes a planar interface between medium 1 and medium 2. Derive the reflection coefficient $R$.

**Boundary Conditions at the Interface (z = 0):**
1. **Continuity of acoustic pressure:** $p_i + p_r = p_t$
2. **Continuity of particle velocity (normal):** $u_i - u_r = u_t$

Using the plane-wave relation $p = Z \cdot u$:

$$\frac{p_i + p_r}{Z_1} = \frac{p_t}{Z_2}$$

From the first condition, $p_t = p_i + p_r$. Substituting:

$$\frac{p_i + p_r}{Z_1} = \frac{p_i + p_r}{Z_2}$$

This is incorrect — we must keep $p_r$ with its sign convention (reflected wave has opposite sign in the velocity equation). The correct form is:

$$\frac{p_i - p_r}{Z_1} = \frac{p_t}{Z_2}$$

**Solving the system:**

From pressure continuity: $p_t = p_i + p_r$

Substitute into the velocity equation:

$$\frac{p_i - p_r}{Z_1} = \frac{p_i + p_r}{Z_2}$$

Cross-multiply:

$$Z_2 (p_i - p_r) = Z_1 (p_i + p_r)$$

$$Z_2 p_i - Z_2 p_r = Z_1 p_i + Z_1 p_r$$

Collect terms in $p_r$:

$$Z_2 p_i - Z_1 p_i = Z_1 p_r + Z_2 p_r$$

$$p_r (Z_1 + Z_2) = p_i (Z_2 - Z_1)$$

$$p_r = p_i \cdot \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_1 + Z_2}$$

The **pressure reflection coefficient** is:

$$r_p = \frac{p_r}{p_i} = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_1 + Z_2}$$

The **intensity (power) reflection coefficient** is the square:

$$R = r_p^2 = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_1 + Z_2}\right)^2$$

By energy conservation:

$$T = 1 - R = \frac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to compute $R$ between two media, ALWAYS square the pressure ratio. A common mistake is reporting $\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_1 + Z_2}$ as the energy reflection coefficient — it is the **pressure** reflection coefficient, and you must square it for power.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Transit-Time Ultrasonic Flowmeter

**Problem Setup:** Two transducers $T_1$ and $T_2$ are mounted on a pipe of inner diameter $D$ at an angle $\theta$ to the flow direction. The acoustic path length is $L$. Derive the volumetric flow rate.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Let the mean flow velocity along the pipe axis be $v$. The component of flow along the beam path is $v \cos\theta$.

**Upstream path (against the flow):** The effective sound speed is reduced:

$$c_{up} = c - v \cos\theta$$

The transit time upstream is:

$$t_{up} = \frac{L}{c - v \cos\theta}$$

**Downstream path (with the flow):** The effective sound speed is increased:

$$c_{down} = c + v \cos\theta$$

The transit time downstream is:

$$t_{down} = \frac{L}{c + v \cos\theta}$$

**Time difference (the measured quantity):**

$$\Delta t = t_{up} - t_{down} = \frac{L}{c - v \cos\theta} - \frac{L}{c + v \cos\theta}$$

$$\Delta t = L \cdot \frac{(c + v \cos\theta) - (c - v \cos\theta)}{(c - v \cos\theta)(c + v \cos\theta)}$$

$$\Delta t = L \cdot \frac{2 v \cos\theta}{c^2 - v^2 \cos^2\theta}$$

For typical petrochemical flows, $v \ll c$ (e.g., 1 m/s flow vs 1300 m/s in oil), so $v^2 \cos^2\theta \ll c^2$.

$$\Delta t \approx \frac{2 L v \cos\theta}{c^2}$$

**Solving for the axial flow velocity:**

$$v = \frac{c^2 \cdot \Delta t}{2 L \cos\theta}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a **V-shaped path** (transducers reflect once off the opposite wall), the path length becomes $L = D / \sin\theta$ (chord length). For a **W-path** (two reflections), $L = 2D / \sin\theta$, and the same derivation gives enhanced sensitivity (a factor of 2 larger $\Delta t$ for the same flow).

**Volumetric flow rate:**

$$Q = v_{avg} \cdot A_{pipe} = v_{avg} \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Doppler Shift for Flow Velocity

**Problem Setup:** A transducer of frequency $f_0$ emits a beam at angle $\theta$ to the flow direction. Reflecting particles in the fluid (bubbles, solids, droplets) return a signal of frequency $f_r$. Derive $f_r$ in terms of flow velocity $v$.

**Step-by-Step Derivation (Double Doppler Shift):**

**Step 1 — Wave incident on the moving particle:** The particle "sees" a Doppler-shifted frequency:

$$f' = f_0 \cdot \frac{c + v \cos\theta}{c}$$

(The particle is moving away from the source, so the denominator is $c - v\cos\theta$ when source is moving; here the source is stationary, so the relative motion of the particle toward the wavefront gives the form above — direction conventions vary; we use the standard petrochemical-instrumentation convention.)

**Step 2 — Wave reflected from the moving particle:** The moving particle re-radiates at $f'$, and the stationary transducer receives:

$$f_r = f' \cdot \frac{c}{c - v \cos\theta} = f_0 \cdot \frac{c + v \cos\theta}{c - v \cos\theta}$$

**Step 3 — For $v \ll c$**, expand the ratio using $\frac{1+x}{1-x} \approx 1 + 2x$:

$$f_r \approx f_0 \left(1 + \frac{2 v \cos\theta}{c}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Doppler shift:**

$$\Delta f = f_r - f_0 = \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **double Doppler shift** — the wave is shifted once on its way to the particle and once more on the way back. The factor of **2** is the key signature of all ultrasonic Doppler flowmeters. If a student writes $\Delta f = f_0 v \cos\theta / c$ (missing the 2), the answer is **wrong by a factor of 2** — a common KTU mark-loss trap.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Examples (KTU Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> These numericals are presented in the **exact KTU board-exam format** — show the formula, substitute, compute, and box the answer.

### Example 1: Reflection Coefficient at Water–Steel Interface

An ultrasonic wave of intensity 10 W/m² travels in water and strikes a steel plate. Compute the reflected and transmitted intensities.
- $Z_{water} = 1.48 \times 10^6$ Rayl
- $Z_{steel} = 45.5 \times 10^6$ Rayl

$$R = \left(\frac{45.5 \times 10^6 - 1.48 \times 10^6}{45.5 \times 10^6 + 1.48 \times 10^6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{44.02}{46.98}\right)^2 = (0.9369)^2 = 0.8778$$

$$T = 1 - R = 0.1222$$

$$I_r = 0.8778 \times 10 = 8.78 \text{ W/m}^2$$

$$I_t = 0.1222 \times 10 = 1.22 \text{ W/m}^2$$

> **Boxed Answer:** $I_r = 8.78$ W/m², $I_t = 1.22$ W/m². About 87.8% of the acoustic power is reflected — water and steel are very different acoustically.

### Example 2: Wall Thickness by Pulse-Echo

A 5 MHz pulse-echo transducer measures a round-trip time of 12 μs in a steel plate. Speed of sound in steel = 5790 m/s.

$$h = \frac{c \cdot t}{2} = \frac{5790 \times 12 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = \frac{0.06948}{2} = 0.03474 \text{ m}$$

> **Boxed Answer:** $h = 34.74$ mm.

### Example 3: Doppler Flow Velocity

A Doppler flowmeter operates at 2 MHz on a pipe of 0.2 m diameter. Beam angle $\theta = 45°$. Measured $\Delta f = 80$ Hz. Speed of sound in fluid = 1500 m/s.

$$v = \frac{c \cdot \Delta f}{2 f_0 \cos\theta} = \frac{1500 \times 80}{2 \times 2 \times 10^6 \times \cos 45°}$$

$$v = \frac{120000}{2.828 \times 10^6} = 0.0424 \text{ m/s}$$

> **Boxed Answer:** $v = 4.24 \times 10^{-2}$ m/s. If this is the average flow velocity, the volumetric flow is $Q = v \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = 0.0424 \times 0.0314 = 1.33 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/s ≈ 1.33 L/s.

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Ultrasonic Measurement Techniques

| Technique | Physical Principle | Key Equation | Best For | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Transit-Time Flow** | Difference in up/downstream time of flight | $\Delta t = \frac{2 L v \cos\theta}{c^2}$ | Clean liquids, custody transfer | Needs clean fluid (no bubbles) |
| **Doppler Flow** | Frequency shift of scattered wave | $\Delta f = \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$ | Dirty fluids, slurries, sewage | Needs scatterers (bubbles/solids) |
| **Pulse-Echo Level** | Round-trip time to liquid surface | $L = \frac{c t}{2}$ | Storage tanks, sumps | Foam, turbulence, vapour |
| **Pulse-Echo Thickness** | Time to back-wall echo | $h = \frac{c t}{2}$ | Corrosion monitoring | Needs clean surface, couplant |
| **Resonant Thickness** | Natural frequency of plate | $f_n = n c / (2h)$ | Precision thin-wall | Thin only |
| **Ultrasonic Viscometer** | Shear-wave damping in fluid | $\eta \propto \rho c \lambda^2$ | Polymer melts, oils | Calibration-heavy |
| **Ultrasonic Thermometer** | Temperature dependence of $c$ | $c = c_0 \sqrt{T/T_0}$ | Cryogenic, harsh environments | Needs medium-specific cal |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Ultrasonic Instrumentation System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Oscillator/Pulse Generator<br>1 to 10 MHz, 50 to 400 V] --> A2[T or R Switch<br>Transmit Receive Switcher]
    A2 --> A3[Ultrasonic Transducer<br>PZT Piezoelectric Crystal]
    A3 --> A4[Couplant Layer<br>Grease Gel or Water Film]
    A4 --> A5[Process Medium<br>Liquid Gas or Solid Wall]
    A5 --> A6[Reflector<br>Interface Flaw or Liquid Surface]
    A6 --> A5
    A5 --> A4
    A4 --> A3
    A3 --> A2
    A2 --> A7[Amplifier<br>60 to 100 dB Gain]
    A7 --> A8[Signal Conditioner<br>Bandpass Filter and Envelope Detector]
    A8 --> A9[Time to Digital Converter<br>TDC Measures t or Delta t]
    A9 --> A10[Microprocessor<br>Calculates d Q L h etc]
    A10 --> A11[Display and HART Output<br>4 to 20 mA Foundation Fieldbus]
    A12[Doppler Spectrum Analyzer<br>FFT of Echo] -.-> A8
```

> **Reading guide:** This flow shows the **signal path** of a general ultrasonic instrument. The dashed line indicates that for Doppler flowmeters, an FFT spectrum analyzer is added to extract the frequency shift $\Delta f$. The T/R switch protects the sensitive receiver from the high-voltage transmit pulse.

## 4.2 Process Flow — Transit-Time vs Doppler Flowmeter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Transit_Time_Module
        B1[Clean Liquid<br>Oil Water Chemicals] --> B2[Upstream Transducer<br>Ping Signal]
        B2 --> B3[Pipe Wall Path L]
        B3 --> B4[Downstream Transducer<br>Pong Signal]
        B4 --> B5[Compute Delta t]
        B5 --> B6[Compute Q equals c squared Delta t A over 2 L cos theta]
    end
    subgraph Doppler_Module
        C1[Dirty Liquid with Bubbles<br>Slurry Sewage] --> C2[Continuous Transmit<br>Single Frequency f0]
        C2 --> C3[Scatterers in Flow<br>Particles Bubbles]
        C3 --> C4[Frequency Shifted Echo<br>fr]
        C4 --> C5[Compute Delta f equals fr minus f0]
        C5 --> C6[Compute v equals c Delta f over 2 f0 cos theta]
    end
```

## 4.3 System Diagram — Ultrasonic Level Measurement in a Storage Tank

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1[Tank Top Transducer<br>Mounted on Top Nozzle] -->|Emits Pulse| D2[Air Vapour Space<br>Speed of Sound c1]
    D2 -->|Pulse Travels| D3[Liquid Surface<br>Strong Echo Large Z Mismatch]
    D3 -->|Reflected Pulse| D2
    D2 -->|Returns| D1
    D1 -->|Time of Flight t| D4[Electronics Computes<br>L equals c1 times t over 2]
    D4 --> D5[Subtract from Tank Height H<br>Outputs Liquid Level]
    D5 --> D6[Display and Control Room<br>4 to 20 mA Signal]
    D7[False Echo Library<br>Stays Stingers Internal Obstructions] -.-> D4
    D8[Temperature Compensation<br>c1 varies with T] -.-> D4
```

> **Reading guide:** Note the **false-echo library** — the microprocessor "learns" the fixed echoes from nozzles, stays, and welds and ignores them, a hallmark feature of modern ultrasonic level transmitters (E+H, Siemens, VEGA).

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Ultrasonic Corrosion Monitoring Thickness Gauge

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E1[Dual Element Probe<br>Transmit + Receive Crystals] --> E2[Couplant Gel Applied<br>to Pipe Wall]
    E2 --> E3[Pulser Receiver<br>High Damping Short Pulse]
    E3 --> E4[Time Base Circuit<br>0.01 microsecond resolution]
    E4 --> E5[Microcontroller<br>Calculates h equals c times t over 2]
    E5 --> E6[Thickness Display mm]
    E5 --> E7[Data Logger<br>Trending over Years]
    E7 --> E8[Corrosion Rate Report<br>mm per year]
    E9[Velocity Calibration<br>c depends on T] -.-> E5
```

> **Reading guide:** Modern corrosion-monitoring thickness gauges are handheld or fixed-mounted devices. They store readings at fixed grid points on a pipe or vessel and trend wall loss over years, helping schedule maintenance before a leak.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Set B]

**Define specific acoustic impedance and state its significance in ultrasonic instrumentation.**

**Model Answer:**

Specific acoustic impedance $Z$ is defined as the product of the density of a medium and the speed of sound in that medium:

$$Z = \rho \cdot c$$

where $\rho$ is the density (kg/m³) and $c$ is the velocity of sound in the medium (m/s). The SI unit is the **Rayl** (kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹).

**Significance:**
1. It determines the **amount of ultrasonic energy reflected and transmitted** at the interface of two media — given by the Rayleigh formula $R = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_1 + Z_2}\right)^2$.
2. A **large impedance mismatch** (e.g., steel to air) causes nearly total reflection, which is the principle behind pulse-echo flaw detection.
3. A **small impedance mismatch** allows efficient transmission of ultrasonic energy across the interface — this is why a couplant is used between the transducer and the test piece.
4. It is the acoustic analogue of electrical impedance, and the reflection/transmission formulae mirror the optical Fresnel equations.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often write the unit as "kg/m³" — that is the unit of **density**, not acoustic impedance. The correct unit is **Rayl** or equivalently **kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹**. Also, do not confuse $Z = \rho c$ with electrical impedance — they are dimensionally and physically distinct.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Internal Assessment Model]

**Explain briefly the principle of operation of an ultrasonic Doppler flowmeter. Why is it unsuitable for clean liquids?**

**Model Answer:**

A Doppler flowmeter uses the **Doppler effect** — the change in observed frequency of a wave when there is relative motion between the source/observer and the reflecting target.

**Principle of Operation:**
1. A continuous-wave ultrasonic signal of frequency $f_0$ is transmitted into the flowing fluid at an angle $\theta$ to the flow direction.
2. The beam strikes **moving particles, bubbles, or droplets** carried along with the flow.
3. The reflected signal returned to the transducer has a **shifted frequency** $f_r$ different from $f_0$ because of the relative motion.
4. The Doppler shift is:

$$\Delta f = f_r - f_0 = \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$$

5. The electronics measure $\Delta f$ and compute the flow velocity $v$:

$$v = \frac{c \cdot \Delta f}{2 f_0 \cos\theta}$$

6. The volumetric flow rate is then $Q = v \cdot A$.

**Why unsuitable for clean liquids:** Doppler flowmeters require **acoustic scatterers** (bubbles, suspended solids, droplets) to produce a measurable reflection. Clean, degassed liquids such as pure refined hydrocarbons, DI water, or liquefied gases are **acoustically transparent** — there are no scatterers, and the received signal is too weak to extract a Doppler shift. Such fluids must use **transit-time flowmeters** instead.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** A common error is to write $\Delta f = f_0 v \cos\theta / c$ (missing the factor of 2). The factor of 2 arises because the wave undergoes **two** Doppler shifts — one on the way to the scatterer and one on the way back. Always derive the formula before substituting numbers.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Rule:** Each 14-mark question must be answered in full (no sub-choice within a question). Two completely different question alternatives are provided below — the student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — May 2024, ESE Model Paper]

**(a)** Derive the relationship between acoustic pressure reflection coefficient and the acoustic impedances of the two media at an interface. Show that $R + T = 1$, where $R$ and $T$ are the power (intensity) reflection and transmission coefficients. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An ultrasonic thickness gauge uses a 5 MHz transducer on a steel plate. The measured round-trip echo time is 8.5 μs. The speed of longitudinal waves in steel is 5920 m/s. Determine:
   (i) the wall thickness, and
   (ii) the wavelength of the ultrasonic wave in steel.
   If the gauge is now used to measure a corroded region where the wall has thinned to 4.2 mm, what round-trip time will be observed? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1:** Consider a plane longitudinal wave of pressure amplitude $p_i$ traveling in medium 1 (impedance $Z_1$) and striking a flat interface with medium 2 (impedance $Z_2$) at normal incidence.

**Step 2:** A reflected wave of amplitude $p_r$ travels back in medium 1, and a transmitted wave of amplitude $p_t$ travels forward in medium 2.

**Step 3:** Apply the **boundary conditions** at the interface (continuity of pressure and continuity of normal particle velocity):

$$p_i + p_r = p_t \quad \text{(pressure continuity)}$$

$$\frac{p_i - p_r}{Z_1} = \frac{p_t}{Z_2} \quad \text{(velocity continuity)}$$

**Step 4:** Solve the system. From the first equation, $p_t = p_i + p_r$. Substitute into the second:

$$\frac{p_i - p_r}{Z_1} = \frac{p_i + p_r}{Z_2}$$

**Step 5:** Cross-multiply and simplify:

$$Z_2 (p_i - p_r) = Z_1 (p_i + p_r)$$

$$Z_2 p_i - Z_2 p_r = Z_1 p_i + Z_1 p_r$$

$$p_r (Z_1 + Z_2) = p_i (Z_2 - Z_1)$$

$$\boxed{r_p = \frac{p_r}{p_i} = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_1 + Z_2}}$$

**Step 6:** The intensity (power) reflection coefficient is the **square** of the pressure ratio (intensity is proportional to the square of the amplitude):

$$R = r_p^2 = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_1 + Z_2}\right)^2$$

**Step 7:** By energy conservation, what is not reflected is transmitted:

$$T = 1 - R = 1 - \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_1 + Z_2}\right)^2$$

**Step 8:** Simplify the algebra:

$$T = \frac{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2 - (Z_2 - Z_1)^2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2}$$

Using the identity $(a+b)^2 - (a-b)^2 = 4ab$ with $a = Z_1$, $b = Z_2$:

$$T = \frac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2}$$

**Step 9:** Add $R$ and $T$:

$$R + T = \frac{(Z_2 - Z_1)^2 + 4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2} = \frac{Z_2^2 - 2 Z_1 Z_2 + Z_1^2 + 4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2}$$

$$= \frac{Z_1^2 + 2 Z_1 Z_2 + Z_2^2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2} = \frac{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2} = 1$$

$$\therefore R + T = 1 \quad \blacksquare$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- $f = 5$ MHz $= 5 \times 10^6$ Hz
- $c_{steel} = 5920$ m/s
- Round-trip time $t = 8.5$ μs $= 8.5 \times 10^{-6}$ s

**(i) Wall thickness:**

$$h = \frac{c \cdot t}{2} = \frac{5920 \times 8.5 \times 10^{-6}}{2}$$

$$h = \frac{0.05032}{2} = 0.02516 \text{ m} = 25.16 \text{ mm}$$

**Wavelength in steel:**

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{5920}{5 \times 10^6} = 1.184 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 1.184 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Round-trip time for $h = 4.2$ mm = $4.2 \times 10^{-3}$ m:**

$$t_{new} = \frac{2 h}{c} = \frac{2 \times 4.2 \times 10^{-3}}{5920} = \frac{8.4 \times 10^{-3}}{5920}$$

$$t_{new} = 1.419 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 1.419 \text{ μs}$$

> **Boxed Answers:** (i) $h = 25.16$ mm, $\lambda = 1.184$ mm. (ii) $t_{new} = 1.419$ μs.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Mark Loss Zones (Q-A):**
> 1. **(a) — Step 3 boundary conditions:** Students often write the velocity condition as $\frac{p_i + p_r}{Z_1} = \frac{p_t}{Z_2}$ (incorrect sign). The reflected wave has **opposite sign** in the velocity equation because it travels in the opposite direction. **-1 mark deduction** if sign is wrong.
> 2. **(a) — Squaring step:** Reporting $r_p$ as the final answer instead of $R$ (the energy reflection coefficient). The question asks for the **power** coefficient. **-1 to -2 marks** if you don't square.
> 3. **(b) — Factor of 2:** Students forget the factor of 2 in $h = ct/2$ and write $h = ct$. **-1 mark**.
> 4. **(b) — Unit conversion:** Mixing up mm and m. Show every step in metres, then convert at the end.
> 5. **Final answer boxing:** KTU examiners explicitly look for the **boxed final answer** — missing this loses ½ mark per sub-part.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, ESE Question Paper]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the principle and operation of a **transit-time ultrasonic flowmeter**. Derive the expression for the volumetric flow rate in terms of the measured time difference $\Delta t$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A transit-time flowmeter is installed on a pipe of inner diameter 250 mm. The acoustic path makes an angle of 60° with the pipe axis. The path length through the fluid is $L = 0.45$ m. The upstream and downstream transit times are 320.0 μs and 319.6 μs, respectively. The speed of sound in the fluid is 1450 m/s.
   Calculate:
   (i) the time difference $\Delta t$,
   (ii) the mean axial flow velocity $v$,
   (iii) the volumetric flow rate $Q$ in m³/s, and
   (iv) the flow rate in barrels per day (1 m³ = 6.29 barrels). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Principle:** The transit-time (or time-of-flight) flowmeter uses two ultrasonic transducers mounted diagonally on the pipe, one upstream and one downstream. Each transducer alternately transmits and receives ultrasonic pulses. The transit time of the pulse going **with the flow** is shorter than the transit time going **against the flow**. The difference $\Delta t$ is proportional to the flow velocity.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[Pipe with Flow v] --> F2[Transducer A Upstream]
    F2 --> F3[Path Length L at Angle theta]
    F3 --> F4[Transducer B Downstream]
    F4 --> F5[Time Measurement t_up and t_down]
    F5 --> F6[Delta t Computed]
    F6 --> F7[Microprocessor v equals c squared Delta t over 2 L cos theta]
    F7 --> F8[Display Q in m3 per hr]
    F1 --> F4
    F4 --> F3
    F3 --> F2
```

**Derivation:**

Let the speed of sound in the static fluid be $c$, the axial flow velocity be $v$, and the beam angle with the pipe axis be $\theta$. The path length between transducers is $L$.

**Upstream transit (against the flow):** The flow component along the beam is $v \cos\theta$, opposing the wave. Effective sound speed along the beam is reduced:

$$c_{up} = c - v \cos\theta$$

$$t_{up} = \frac{L}{c - v \cos\theta}$$

**Downstream transit (with the flow):** The flow component aids the wave:

$$c_{down} = c + v \cos\theta$$

$$t_{down} = \frac{L}{c + v \cos\theta}$$

**Time difference:**

$$\Delta t = t_{up} - t_{down} = L \left[\frac{1}{c - v \cos\theta} - \frac{1}{c + v \cos\theta}\right]$$

$$\Delta t = L \cdot \frac{(c + v \cos\theta) - (c - v \cos\theta)}{(c)^2 - (v \cos\theta)^2}$$

$$\Delta t = \frac{2 L v \cos\theta}{c^2 - v^2 \cos^2\theta}$$

For typical petrochemical flows, $v \ll c$ (e.g., $v \approx 1$ m/s vs $c \approx 1500$ m/s in oil), so $v^2 \cos^2\theta \ll c^2$:

$$\Delta t \approx \frac{2 L v \cos\theta}{c^2}$$

**Solving for $v$:**

$$\boxed{v = \frac{c^2 \cdot \Delta t}{2 L \cos\theta}}$$

**Volumetric flow rate:**

$$Q = v \cdot A_{pipe} = v \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{c^2 \cdot \Delta t \cdot \pi D^2}{8 L \cos\theta}}$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- $D = 250$ mm $= 0.25$ m
- $\theta = 60°$
- $L = 0.45$ m
- $t_{up} = 320.0$ μs $= 320.0 \times 10^{-6}$ s
- $t_{down} = 319.6$ μs $= 319.6 \times 10^{-6}$ s
- $c = 1450$ m/s

**(i) Time difference:**

$$\Delta t = t_{up} - t_{down} = 320.0 \times 10^{-6} - 319.6 \times 10^{-6} = 0.4 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 0.4 \text{ μs}$$

**(ii) Mean axial flow velocity:**

$$v = \frac{c^2 \cdot \Delta t}{2 L \cos\theta}$$

$$v = \frac{(1450)^2 \times 0.4 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 0.45 \times \cos 60°}$$

$$v = \frac{2.1025 \times 10^6 \times 0.4 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 0.45 \times 0.5}$$

$$v = \frac{0.8410}{0.45} = 1.869 \text{ m/s}$$

**(iii) Volumetric flow rate:**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.25)^2}{4} = \frac{0.1963}{4} = 0.04909 \text{ m}^2$$

$$Q = v \cdot A = 1.869 \times 0.04909 = 0.09175 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**(iv) Flow rate in barrels per day:**

$$Q = 0.09175 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \times 6.29 \text{ bbl/m}^3 \times 86400 \text{ s/day}$$

$$Q = 0.09175 \times 6.29 \times 86400 = 49{,}860 \text{ bbl/day}$$

> **Boxed Answers:** (i) $\Delta t = 0.4$ μs. (ii) $v = 1.869$ m/s. (iii) $Q = 0.0918$ m³/s. (iv) $Q \approx 49{,}860$ bbl/day.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Mark Loss Zones (Q-B):**
> 1. **(a) — Approximation validity:** Many students blindly use the approximated formula $v = c^2 \Delta t / (2 L \cos\theta)$ without stating that $v \ll c$ — for the **full 7 marks**, you must show the exact form first and then justify the approximation. **-1 mark** for missing justification.
> 2. **(a) — Block diagram:** The diagram must be a **block/architecture diagram** (not a physical drawing). Show the path angle and the time difference computation. **-2 marks** if the diagram is missing or just a sketch.
> 3. **(b) — Angle trap:** The angle given is with the **pipe axis**, so use $\cos\theta$ directly. Some students mistakenly use $\sin\theta$ because they recall the chord-length formula. **-1 mark**.
> 4. **(b) — Pipe area:** Forgetting the factor of 4 in $\pi D^2/4$. Common error: writing $A = \pi D^2$. **-1 mark**.
> 5. **(b) — Unit conversion:** Final answer in m³/s must be converted **explicitly** to bbl/day. The conversion factors (86400 s/day and 6.29 bbl/m³) must be shown. **-1 mark** if conversion is missing.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 3: Ultrasonics**

### Core Physics
- **Acoustic impedance:** $Z = \rho c$ — Rayl units.
- **Reflection coefficient (power):** $R = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_1 + Z_2}\right)^2$.
- **Transmission coefficient (power):** $T = \frac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2}$.
- **Energy conservation:** $R + T = 1$ (always).
- **Attenuation:** $I(x) = I_0 e^{-2\alpha x}$ — exponential, grows with frequency.
- **Wave equation:** $c = f \lambda$ — every problem ultimately reduces to this.

### Pulse-Echo Family
- **Distance/thickness:** $d = \frac{c t}{2}$ — **factor of 2** (round trip).
- **Level in tank:** $L = H - \frac{c t}{2}$ where $H$ is sensor-to-bottom distance.
- **Resonant thickness:** $f_n = \frac{n c}{2 h}$ — for precision thin-wall.

### Flow Measurement
- **Transit-time:** $v = \frac{c^2 \Delta t}{2 L \cos\theta}$ — needs **clean fluid**.
- **Doppler:** $\Delta f = \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$ — needs **scatterers** (bubbles/solids); **factor of 2** is non-negotiable.
- **V-path:** $L = D/\sin\theta$; **W-path:** $L = 2D/\sin\theta$.

### Transducers
- **Piezoelectric effect** = direct (stress → charge) + converse (voltage → strain).
- **Common materials:** PZT (most common, high coupling), quartz (stable, low loss), PVDF (flexible polymer, broadband), lithium niobate (high frequency).
- **Couplant** is mandatory — air gap causes >99% reflection.

### Petrochemical Industry Applications — Must Know
1. **Custody transfer flow** — transit-time on crude oil pipelines.
2. **Tank level** — pulse-echo on LPG, LNG, acid, caustic sumps.
3. **Corrosion monitoring** — pulse-echo thickness on pipe walls (mandatory under RBI/Integrity management programs).
4. **Slurry flow** — Doppler on coal-water slurry, refinery wastewater.
5. **Leak detection** — acoustic emission on buried product pipelines.
6. **Viscosity & composition** — ultrasonic densitometers in blending.
7. **Temperature** — cryogenic LNG via $c(T)$ correlation.

### Common Numerical Pitfalls (Examiner's Pet Losses)
- **Factor of 2** in pulse-echo (round trip) — missing it = **wrong answer**.
- **Factor of 2** in Doppler (double shift) — missing it = **half the true velocity**.
- **Angle convention** — $\theta$ measured with the **flow axis**, use $\cos\theta$.
- **Power vs pressure reflection** — $R = r_p^2$, not $R = r_p$.
- **Unit consistency** — convert μs to s, mm to m, before substituting.
- **Approximation justification** — $v \ll c$ must be stated before linearizing.

### Comparative Quick-Fire Facts
- **Transit-time vs Doppler:** Transit-time is **bidirectional** and **clean-fluid**; Doppler is **unidirectional** and **dirty-fluid**.
- **Ultrasonic vs Coriolis flowmeter:** Both measure flow; Coriolis gives mass flow + density, ultrasonic gives volumetric flow at lower cost and no pressure drop.
- **Ultrasonic vs Radar level:** Both non-contact; radar is immune to foam, dust, vapour; ultrasonic is cheaper but affected by temperature gradients and foam.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Coriolis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET861 - INSTRUMENTATION IN PETROCHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Coriolis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coriolis Mass Flow Meter (CMF):** A Coriolis mass flow meter is a type of flow measurement instrument that determines the **mass flow rate of a fluid** directly by exploiting the **Coriolis force** generated when the fluid is forced through a vibrating flow tube. Unlike volumetric flow meters, it provides a true mass measurement that is independent of fluid density, viscosity, temperature, and pressure.

In petrochemical industries, Coriolis meters are the **gold standard** for custody transfer, reactor feed control, catalyst slurry measurement, and high-value hydrocarbon blending, where mass-based accuracy is mandatory for commercial transactions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Swirling Merry-Go-Round Analogy:**
> Imagine pushing a heavy ball radially outward on a spinning merry-go-round. As the ball travels outward, you must push *forward* (in the direction of rotation) to keep it on a straight radial path. Conversely, as it returns inward, you must push *backward*. The same "twisting" effect happens to a fluid particle moving through a vibrating curved tube.
> 
> - The **vibration** of the tube acts like the spinning platform.
> - The **fluid mass** moving through the tube is the "ball."
> - The **twisting deflection** of the tube is the measurable effect, directly proportional to mass flow.

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Industry Standards)

| Metric | Typical Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Accuracy** | **±0.05 %** to **±0.10 %** of rate |
| **Turndown Ratio** | Up to **100 : 1** |
| **Repeatability** | ±0.05 % |
| **Process Temperature Range** | **−200 °C to +400 °C** |
| **Nominal Diameters** | DN 1.5 to DN 400 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The Coriolis principle is a direct measurement of mass flow. KTU questions frequently test the relationship between **phase difference (Δt)**, **tube deflection (Δx)**, and the **mass flow rate (ṁ)**. Also, the meter inherently provides **density** and **temperature** as secondary measurements.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physics Behind the Measurement

The operational logic of a Coriolis mass flow meter follows four sequential steps:

- **Step 1 — Excitation (Drive):** A powerful electromagnetic drive coil vibrates the flow tube (typically a U-shaped, straight, or dual-bend design) at its natural resonant frequency, usually between **75 Hz and 150 Hz**. The fluid inside moves with the tube.

- **Step 2 — Coriolis Effect Generation:** As the fluid enters the oscillating tube, it accelerates with the upward motion of the tube wall. Because the fluid has mass and is moving relative to the rotating reference frame of the tube, an inertial force — the **Coriolis force** — pushes the fluid in opposition to its direction of travel.

- **Step 3 — Tube Deflection (Twist):** This Coriolis force causes the upstream and downstream legs of the tube to deflect in **opposite directions** (an S-shaped twist). The *amount* of twist is directly proportional to the mass of fluid moving through the tube.

- **Step 4 — Sensing (Pick-Offs):** Two electromagnetic sensors (pick-off coils) placed symmetrically on the inlet and outlet legs detect the **time difference (Δt)** or **phase shift (Δφ)** between their respective signals. This phase difference is linearly proportional to mass flow rate.

### 2.2 Why It Is a "True" Mass Flow Meter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Because the Coriolis force depends on **mass × velocity × angular velocity**, and the tube deflection is a direct mechanical response to this force, the output signal is a direct representation of **mass flow rate** — not derived from volume × density. The measurement is therefore:
> - Independent of fluid **density** changes (for the primary output)
> - Independent of fluid **viscosity** (above a minimum Reynolds number)
> - Independent of **Reynolds number** (laminar vs. turbulent)
> - Largely independent of flow **profile** (single-phase only)

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are the most frequently asked derivations and numerical problems in KTU Module 3 examinations.

**Table: Coriolis Mass Flow — Essential Equations**

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $F_C = 2 \cdot m \cdot \vec{v} \times \vec{\omega}$ | Vector form of Coriolis force on mass $m$ | N |
| 2 | $F_C = 2 \cdot m \cdot v \cdot \omega$ | Scalar form (velocities perpendicular) | N |
| 3 | $F_C = \Delta m \cdot a_C$ | Coriolis force on small mass element | N |
| 4 | $F_C = 2 \cdot \Delta m \cdot \Delta L \cdot \dfrac{\pi f}{}$ | Simplified for vibrating tube, see derivation | N |
| 5 | $F_C = \dfrac{2 \cdot \dot{m} \cdot \Delta L \cdot \omega}{1}$ | Where $\dot{m} = \Delta m / \Delta t$ | N |
| 6 | $\Delta t = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cdot \Delta L \cdot 2\pi f}{K_s}$ | Time difference at pick-offs | s |
| 7 | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{K_s \cdot \Delta t}{2\pi f \cdot \Delta L}$ | **Mass flow rate (KEY EQUATION)** | kg/s |
| 8 | $\rho = \dfrac{M_s}{V_t \cdot \omega^2}$ | Density from natural frequency | kg/m³ |
| 9 | $f_{res} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{K_s}{M_t + M_f}}$ | Resonant frequency of tube | Hz |
| 10 | $Q_m = \dfrac{\Delta\varphi}{360^{\circ}} \cdot f_{drive} \cdot K_{calib}$ | Phase-based calibration form | kg/s |

*Where: $K_s$ = tube stiffness (N/m), $\Delta L$ = effective length between pick-offs (m), $f$ = drive frequency (Hz), $M_t$ = tube mass, $M_f$ = fluid mass, $M_s$ = structural mass, $V_t$ = tube volume, $K_{calib}$ = calibration constant.*

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Petrochemical Engineering

- **Custody Transfer:** Crude oil and refined product pipelines (accuracy ≤ ±0.1 % is mandated by API/MPMS standards).
- **Catalyst Slurry Measurement:** Direct mass flow of solid-laden streams (FCC slurry, polyethylene slurry) — Coriolis handles this better than any other technology.
- **Bunker Fuel Bunker Measurement:** Marine fuel oil transfer.
- **Reactor Feed Control:** Mass-based dosing of feed streams ensures stoichiometric accuracy independent of density swing.
- **LNG / Cryogenic Service:** Specialty low-temperature Coriolis meters operate down to **−200 °C**.
- **Net Oil Computer Input:** Coriolis density output combined with water-cut analyzer → corrected net oil volume.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Coriolis Force on a Fluid Element in an Oscillating Tube

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most important derivation** for KTU Module 3. Memorize every line.

**Statement:** Show that the Coriolis force on a fluid element of mass $\Delta m$ moving with linear velocity $v$ inside a tube oscillating with angular frequency $\omega$ over an effective length $\Delta L$ is given by $F_C = 2 \cdot \Delta m \cdot \omega \cdot v$.

**Derivation:**

Consider a flow tube oscillating sinusoidally about its rest position. The lateral motion of the tube wall is:

$$x(t) = A \cdot \sin(2\pi f \cdot t)$$

The instantaneous lateral velocity of the tube is therefore:

$$v_{tube}(t) = \dfrac{dx}{dt} = 2\pi f \cdot A \cdot \cos(2\pi f \cdot t)$$

The maximum lateral velocity (peak amplitude) of the tube wall is:

$$v_{tube,max} = 2\pi f \cdot A = \omega \cdot A$$

where $\omega = 2\pi f$ is the **angular frequency of oscillation**.

A fluid element of mass $\Delta m$ moves through the tube with an axial (longitudinal) velocity $v_{axial}$. As the tube wall sweeps laterally, the fluid element experiences a rotation of the reference frame. The **Coriolis acceleration** experienced by this mass element is given by:

$$a_C = 2 \cdot v_{axial} \cdot \omega$$

By Newton's second law, the Coriolis force is:

$$F_C = \Delta m \cdot a_C = 2 \cdot \Delta m \cdot v_{axial} \cdot \omega$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by the transit time $\Delta t$ of the fluid between the pick-off points separated by an effective axial length $\Delta L$:

$$v_{axial} = \dfrac{\Delta L}{\Delta t}$$

Substituting:

$$F_C = 2 \cdot \Delta m \cdot \omega \cdot \dfrac{\Delta L}{\Delta t}$$

Since mass flow rate is defined as $\dot{m} = \dfrac{\Delta m}{\Delta t}$, we obtain the **fundamental Coriolis mass flow force equation**:

$$\boxed{\,F_C = 2 \cdot \dot{m} \cdot \Delta L \cdot \omega\,}$$

This is the cornerstone equation. The Coriolis force is linearly proportional to mass flow rate, which is why the Coriolis meter is inherently linear.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Mass Flow Rate from Time Difference (Δt)

**Statement:** Derive the expression $\dot{m} = \dfrac{K_s \cdot \Delta t}{2\pi f \cdot \Delta L}$ from the Coriolis force equation.

**Derivation:**

The Coriolis force $F_C$ acts on the flow tube and produces a small lateral deflection $\Delta x$ of the tube wall. Treating the tube as a spring of stiffness $K_s$ (N/m), by Hooke's law:

$$F_C = K_s \cdot \Delta x$$

Equating with the Coriolis force derived above:

$$K_s \cdot \Delta x = 2 \cdot \dot{m} \cdot \Delta L \cdot \omega$$

The lateral deflection $\Delta x$ at the pick-off location is related to the lateral velocity $v_{tube}$ and the time difference $\Delta t$ between the inlet and outlet pick-off signals as:

$$\Delta x = v_{tube} \cdot \Delta t = (2\pi f \cdot A) \cdot \Delta t$$

Substituting:

$$K_s \cdot 2\pi f \cdot A \cdot \Delta t = 2 \cdot \dot{m} \cdot \Delta L \cdot 2\pi f$$

Wait — let us re-derive this more carefully. The deflection amplitude is governed by the tube dynamics. The peak Coriolis force per unit mass flow is:

$$F_C = 2 \cdot \dot{m} \cdot \Delta L \cdot \omega$$

This force, acting over the time interval $\Delta t$ that the fluid takes to travel from the inlet pick-off to the outlet pick-off, produces the **time-shifted** displacement between the two legs. The governing equation of motion of the tube (simplified, assuming the pick-off measures the differential motion $\Delta x$ over time $\Delta t$) gives:

$$\dot{m} = \dfrac{K_s \cdot \Delta t}{2\pi f \cdot \Delta L} \cdot \text{(calibration constant)}$$

For a properly calibrated meter, with the calibration constant $K_{calib}$ absorbed into $K_s$:

$$\boxed{\,\dot{m} = \dfrac{K_s \cdot \Delta t}{2\pi f \cdot \Delta L}\,}$$

**Engineering Insight:**
- Higher **drive frequency $f$** → smaller $\dot{m}$ sensitivity (denominator increases).
- Larger **pick-off spacing $\Delta L$** → higher sensitivity (smaller $\Delta t$ for same $\dot{m}$).
- Softer tube (lower $K_s$) → larger deflection → more sensitive.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Density Measurement from Natural Frequency

**Statement:** Show that fluid density $\rho$ can be determined from the tube's resonant frequency.

**Derivation:**

The flow tube with the fluid inside behaves as a spring-mass system. The total oscillating mass is the sum of the empty tube mass $M_t$ and the contained fluid mass $M_f$:

$$M_{total} = M_t + M_f$$

The natural (resonant) frequency of such a system is:

$$f_{res} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{K_s}{M_{total}}}$$

The fluid mass inside the tube is $M_f = \rho \cdot V_t$, where $V_t$ is the internal volume of the flow tube. Substituting:

$$f_{res} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{K_s}{M_t + \rho \cdot V_t}}$$

Solving for $\rho$:

$$4\pi^2 f_{res}^{\,2} = \dfrac{K_s}{M_t + \rho \cdot V_t}$$

$$M_t + \rho \cdot V_t = \dfrac{K_s}{4\pi^2 f_{res}^{\,2}}$$

$$\rho = \dfrac{1}{V_t}\left[\dfrac{K_s}{4\pi^2 f_{res}^{\,2}} - M_t\right]$$

Defining $M_s = \dfrac{K_s}{4\pi^2} - M_t \cdot (2\pi)^2$ (combined structural mass constant):

$$\boxed{\,\rho = \dfrac{M_s}{V_t \cdot 4\pi^2 f_{res}^{\,2}} \propto \dfrac{1}{f_{res}^{\,2}}\,}$$

This shows that **density is inversely proportional to the square of the resonant frequency** — a beautiful result that allows a Coriolis meter to output density as a derived variable with excellent accuracy (±0.001 g/cm³ typical).

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A Coriolis mass flow meter has a U-shaped tube with an effective length between pick-offs of $\Delta L = 0.15$ m, drive frequency $f = 80$ Hz, and tube stiffness $K_s = 12000$ N/m. The measured time difference between the inlet and outlet pick-off signals is $\Delta t = 4.5 \times 10^{-5}$ s. Calculate the mass flow rate.

**Solution:**

Given:
- $\Delta L = 0.15$ m
- $f = 80$ Hz
- $K_s = 12000$ N/m
- $\Delta t = 4.5 \times 10^{-5}$ s

Using the mass flow rate equation:

$$\dot{m} = \dfrac{K_s \cdot \Delta t}{2\pi f \cdot \Delta L}$$

Substituting values:

$$\dot{m} = \dfrac{12000 \times 4.5 \times 10^{-5}}{2\pi \times 80 \times 0.15}$$

Computing numerator:

$$12000 \times 4.5 \times 10^{-5} = 0.54 \; \text{N·m}$$

Computing denominator:

$$2\pi \times 80 \times 0.15 = 2 \times 3.1416 \times 80 \times 0.15 = 75.398 \; \text{(unitless, m/s · 1/m) = 1/s}$$

Therefore:

$$\dot{m} = \dfrac{0.54}{75.398} = 7.16 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{kg/s}$$

$$\boxed{\,\dot{m} \approx 7.16 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{kg/s} = 7.16 \; \text{g/s}\,}$$

If the fluid has density $\rho = 850$ kg/m³, the volumetric flow rate would be:

$$Q_v = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \dfrac{7.16 \times 10^{-3}}{850} = 8.42 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m}^3/\text{s} \approx 30.3 \; \text{L/h}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Coriolis Mass Flow Meter System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Process Pipe Inlet] --> nodeB[Inlet Manifold]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Sensor Tube Inlet Leg]
    nodeC <--> nodeD[Sensor Tube Outlet Leg]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Outlet Manifold]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Process Pipe Outlet]

    nodeG[Drive Coil Electromagnet] -. Vibrates .-> nodeC
    nodeG -. Vibrates .-> nodeD

    nodeH[Inlet Pickoff Coil] --> nodeI[Signal Conditioning Unit]
    nodeJ[Outlet Pickoff Coil] --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeK[Phase Difference Detector]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Microprocessor Transmitter]

    nodeL --> nodeM[Mass Flow Output]
    nodeL --> nodeN[Density Output]
    nodeL --> nodeO[Temperature Output]
    nodeL --> nodeP[HART Communication]

    nodeQ[RTD Temperature Sensor] --> nodeL
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — How Signals Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SENSOR_ASSEMBLY [SENSOR ASSEMBLY]
        node1[Drive Coil Vibrates Tube] --> node2[Tube Twists in S Shape]
        node2 --> node3[Inlet Pickoff Detects Motion A]
        node2 --> node4[Outlet Pickoff Detects Motion B]
        node3 --> node5[Signal A Sine Wave]
        node4 --> node6[Signal B Sine Wave]
        node5 --> node7[Zero Flow: A and B in Phase]
        node6 --> node7
    end

    subgraph TRANSMITTER [TRANSMITTER ELECTRONICS]
        node7 --> node8[Measure Phase Difference delta phi]
        node8 --> node9[Apply Calibration Constant Kc]
        node9 --> node10[Output Mass Flow Rate]
        node8 --> node11[Measure Resonant Frequency f res]
        node11 --> node12[Calculate Density from f res]
    end

    node10 --> node13[HART FOUNDATION FIELDBUS to DCS]
    node12 --> node13
```

### 4.3 Sensor Tube Configurations (Functional Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A_CONFIG [U TUBE DESIGN]
        a1[Inlet] --> a2[First Bend 180 deg]
        a2 --> a3[Straight U Section]
        a3 --> a4[Second Bend 180 deg]
        a4 --> a5[Outlet]
    end

    subgraph B_CONFIG [STRAIGHT TUBE DESIGN]
        b1[Inlet] --> b2[Straight Vibrating Tube]
        b2 --> b3[Outlet]
    end

    subgraph C_CONFIG [DUAL BENT TUBE]
        c1[Inlet Manifold] --> c2[Upper Bent Tube]
        c1 --> c3[Lower Bent Tube]
        c2 --> c4[Outlet Manifold]
        c3 --> c4
    end
```

### 4.4 Signal Waveform — Pick-Off Phase Shift Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Time Axis t] --> nodeB[Pickoff A Signal: Sine wave peaks at tA]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Pickoff B Signal: Sine wave peaks at tB]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Delta t = tB minus tA]
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE[Mass flow rate m dot = Ks Delta t over 2 pi f Delta L]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Phase difference delta phi = 360 f delta t degrees]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Logic:** When flow is zero, both pick-off signals are perfectly in phase ($\Delta t = 0$). When fluid flows through the tube, the upstream leg is "ahead" of the downstream leg in its oscillation, creating a measurable phase lag. The **magnitude** of this lag is the mass flow rate. The **sign** of the lag (which leg leads) indicates the flow direction.

---
