## 2.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Pulsed Radar System

A pulsed radar system consists of the following functional blocks:

1. **Transmitter (TX)** – A high-power oscillator (magnetron, klystron, or solid-state amplifier) that generates short-duration RF pulses.
2. **Duplexer (TR Switch)** – A high-speed switching device that allows the single antenna to act as both transmitter and receiver. It protects the sensitive receiver from the high-power transmit pulse.
3. **Antenna** – A highly directive parabolic dish or horn antenna that focuses the RF energy into a narrow beam and also collects the returning echo.
4. **Receiver (RX)** – A sensitive superheterodyne receiver (typically with a low-noise amplifier) that amplifies the weak echo signal.
5. **Display / Signal Processor** – Historically a CRT (PPI – Plan Position Indicator); today a digital signal processor computing range, velocity, and target signature.

## 2.2 The Radar Range Equation (Most Important Equation in Radar Engineering)

The **radar range equation** relates the **received echo power** $P_r$ to the **transmitted power** $P_t$, the **target radar cross-section** $\sigma$, the **range** $R$, and the **antenna gain** $G$.

The derivation conceptually follows two steps:

### Step 1: Power Density at the Target
The transmitter radiates isotropically or via a directive antenna. The power density at a distance $R$ is reduced by the spherical spreading factor.

- If the antenna has a transmitting gain $G$, the radiated power is concentrated into a solid angle $\Omega = 4\pi / G$.
- Power density at range $R$:

$$S_t = \frac{P_t \, G}{4 \pi R^2}$$

### Step 2: Power Scattered Back by the Target
- The target intercepts $S_t$ over its effective area $\sigma$ and re-radiates isotropically.
- Power re-radiated toward the receiver isotropically:

$$P_{\text{re}} = S_t \cdot \sigma = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{4 \pi R^2}$$

### Step 3: Power Density of Echo at the Receiver
- The echo spreads spherically as it returns over the same distance $R$.
- Power density of the echo at the receiving antenna:

$$S_r = \frac{P_{\text{re}}}{4 \pi R^2} = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{(4 \pi)^2 R^4}$$

### Step 4: Power Captured by the Receiving Antenna
- The receiving antenna of effective aperture $A_e$ captures the echo:

$$P_r = S_r \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma \, A_e}{(4 \pi)^2 R^4}$$

### Step 5: Using the Antenna Gain-Aperture Relation

The fundamental relation between antenna gain $G$ and effective aperture $A_e$ is:

$$A_e = \frac{G \, \lambda^2}{4 \pi}$$

Substituting into the expression for $P_r$:

$$P_r = \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4 \pi)^3 R^4}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Classic Radar Range Equation (Monostatic, Single Antenna):**

$$P_r = \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4 \pi)^3 R^4}$$

For a **bistatic radar** (separate TX and RX antennas with different gains $G_t$ and $G_r$), the equation becomes:

$$P_r = \frac{P_t \, G_t \, G_r \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4 \pi)^3 R_t^2 \, R_r^2}$$

## 2.3 Maximum Range Equation

The **maximum detectable range** $R_{\max}$ is found by setting $P_r$ equal to the **minimum detectable signal** $S_{i,\min}$ of the receiver:

$$R_{\max} = \left[ \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4 \pi)^3 \, S_{i,\min}} \right]^{1/4}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Notice that $R_{\max}$ varies as the **fourth root** of $P_t$. This means **quadrupling the transmitter power only doubles the range** — a fundamental limitation of radar and a frequent KTU question.

## 2.4 FMCW Radar Principle (Crucial for Petrochemical Applications)

In **FMCW (Frequency Modulated Continuous Wave) radar**, the transmitter continuously emits a signal whose frequency is swept linearly over a bandwidth $\Delta f$ during a sweep period $T_s$. The instantaneous transmitted frequency is:

$$f_t(t) = f_0 + \frac{\Delta f}{T_s} \cdot t$$

The received echo is a delayed copy of this signal. The instantaneous frequency difference (beat frequency) is:

$$f_b = \frac{2 \, R \, \Delta f}{c \, T_s}$$

By measuring $f_b$, the range $R$ of the target (e.g., oil surface) is computed as:

$$R = \frac{f_b \, c \, T_s}{2 \, \Delta f}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why FMCW is dominant in petrochemical level measurement:**
> - **High accuracy** (millimeter-level) over short ranges.
> - **Low transmit power** (safe for hazardous area, Ex-proof).
> - **No dead zone** (unlike pulsed radar).
> - **Works through foam, vapor, dust, and turbulence.**

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $P_r = \dfrac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4 \pi)^3 R^4}$ | Radar range equation (monostatic) | Watts |
| $R_{\max} = \left[ \dfrac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4 \pi)^3 \, S_{i,\min}} \right]^{1/4}$ | Maximum detectable range | Meters |
| $A_e = \dfrac{G \, \lambda^2}{4 \pi}$ | Antenna gain-aperture relation | $m^2$ |
| $R = \dfrac{c \, \tau}{2}$ | Range from pulse round-trip time | Meters |
| $\Delta f_d = \dfrac{2 \, v_r \, f_0}{c}$ | Doppler frequency shift | Hz |
| $R = \dfrac{f_b \, c \, T_s}{2 \, \Delta f}$ | FMCW radar range equation | Meters |
| $\text{PRF} = \dfrac{1}{T_p}$ | Pulse Repetition Frequency | Hz |
| $R_{\text{unamb}} = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot \text{PRF}}$ | Maximum unambiguous range | Meters |
| $v_r = \dfrac{\lambda \, \Delta f_d}{2}$ | Radial velocity from Doppler | m/s |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations

## 3.1 Derivation of the Radar Range Equation

We start from the definition of **isotropic radiator** (a point source radiating equally in all directions).

**Step 1:** The total transmitted power $P_t$ is distributed uniformly over the surface of a sphere of radius $R$. The surface area of this sphere is $4 \pi R^2$. Therefore, the **power flux density** (power per unit area) at the target is:

$$S_t = \frac{P_t}{4 \pi R^2} \quad \text{(isotropic case)}$$

**Step 2:** If the antenna has a directive gain $G$, the power density in the direction of maximum radiation is amplified by a factor $G$:

$$S_t = \frac{P_t \, G}{4 \pi R^2}$$

**Step 3:** The target intercepts a portion of this power density over an effective area called the **radar cross-section** $\sigma$ (in $m^2$). The total power intercepted and re-radiated isotropically by the target is:

$$P_{\sigma} = S_t \cdot \sigma = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{4 \pi R^2}$$

**Step 4:** The re-radiated echo propagates spherically from the target back to the radar. The power density of the echo at the receiving antenna (located at distance $R$ from the target) is:

$$S_r = \frac{P_{\sigma}}{4 \pi R^2} = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{(4 \pi)^2 R^4}$$

**Step 5:** The receiving antenna has an effective aperture $A_e$. The power delivered to the receiver is the product of the echo power density and the antenna aperture:

$$P_r = S_r \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma \, A_e}{(4 \pi)^2 R^4}$$

**Step 6:** Apply the antenna gain-aperture relation $A_e = \dfrac{G \, \lambda^2}{4 \pi}$:

$$P_r = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{(4 \pi)^2 R^4} \cdot \frac{G \, \lambda^2}{4 \pi}$$

**Step 7:** Combine the $(4 \pi)$ factors:

$$P_r = \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4 \pi)^3 R^4}$$

This is the **monostatic radar range equation**. Q.E.D.

## 3.2 Derivation of Maximum Range $R_{\max}$

The smallest signal power the radar receiver can reliably detect is the **minimum detectable signal** $S_{i,\min}$ (also called MDS — Minimum Detectable Signal). It is determined by the receiver's noise figure $F$, bandwidth $B_n$, and operating temperature $T_0$ via:

$$S_{i,\min} = k \, T_0 \, B_n \, F \cdot \left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{\min}$$

where $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K is Boltzmann's constant.

For successful detection, the received echo power must equal or exceed $S_{i,\min}$:

$$P_r = S_{i,\min}$$

Substituting into the radar range equation:

$$S_{i,\min} = \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4 \pi)^3 R_{\max}^4}$$

Solving algebraically for $R_{\max}$:

$$R_{\max}^4 = \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4 \pi)^3 \, S_{i,\min}}$$

Taking the fourth root:

$$R_{\max} = \left[ \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4 \pi)^3 \, S_{i,\min}} \right]^{1/4}$$

**Q.E.D.**

## 3.3 Derivation of Range from Pulse Round-Trip Time

If the radar transmits a pulse at time $t = 0$ and the echo is received at time $t = \tau$, the wave has traveled a total round-trip distance of $2R$ at the speed of light $c$:

$$c \cdot \tau = 2R$$

Solving for $R$:

$$R = \frac{c \, \tau}{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor of 2 appears because the pulse must travel **to the target and back** (round trip).

## 3.4 Numerical Example (KTU Style)

A pulsed radar operating at $f = 10$ GHz has the following parameters: $P_t = 1$ kW, $G = 30$ dB, $\sigma = 1\ m^2$, $S_{i,\min} = -90$ dBm. Find $R_{\max}$.

**Step 1:** Convert parameters to SI units.
- $G_{\text{lin}} = 10^{30/10} = 10^3 = 1000$
- $\lambda = c / f = (3 \times 10^8) / (10 \times 10^9) = 0.03$ m
- $S_{i,\min} = 10^{-90/10}$ mW $= 10^{-9}$ mW $= 10^{-12}$ W $= 1$ pW

**Step 2:** Plug into the maximum range equation:

$$R_{\max} = \left[ \frac{(10^3) \cdot (10^3)^2 \cdot (0.03)^2 \cdot 1}{(4 \pi)^3 \cdot 10^{-12}} \right]^{1/4}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the numerator: $10^3 \cdot 10^6 \cdot 9 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 1 = 9 \times 10^5$

**Step 4:** Compute the denominator: $(4 \pi)^3 \cdot 10^{-12} \approx 1984 \cdot 10^{-12} \approx 1.984 \times 10^{-9}$

**Step 5:** Combine:

$$R_{\max} = \left[ \frac{9 \times 10^5}{1.984 \times 10^{-9}} \right]^{1/4} = \left[ 4.535 \times 10^{14} \right]^{1/4}$$

**Step 6:** Take the fourth root:

$$R_{\max} \approx 4615\ \text{m} \approx 4.6\ \text{km}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Pulsed Radar System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Modulator and Timing Source] --> nodeB[Pulse Generator]
    nodeB --> nodeC[High Power Transmitter Magnetron or Klystron]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Duplexer TR Switch]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Directive Parabolic Antenna]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Target Aircraft Ship Oil Surface]
    nodeF --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeG[Low Noise RF Amplifier]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Mixer and Local Oscillator]
    nodeH --> nodeI[IF Amplifier]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Detector and Video Amplifier]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Display and Signal Processor PPI Scope]
    nodeA --> nodeK
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**
- The **Modulator** triggers the pulse generator and synchronizes the display sweep.
- The **Transmitter** generates high-power RF pulses (typically megawatts peak power).
- The **Duplexer** protects the receiver by isolating it during transmission and routing the weak echo to the receiver during listening periods.
- The **Antenna** is shared between transmit and receive (monostatic configuration).
- The **Receiver** amplifies and processes the tiny echo (often picowatts).
- The **Display** plots range vs. azimuth/elevation (PPI — Plan Position Indicator).

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram of an FMCW Radar Level Gauge (Petrochemical Application)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[VCO Voltage Controlled Oscillator] --> nodeB[Power Splitter]
    nodeB --> nodeC[TX Antenna Horn]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Tank Process Vessel]
    nodeD --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeE[RX Antenna or Same TX RX Antenna]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Reference Mixer Local]
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG[IF Amplifier and Beat Frequency Extractor]
    nodeG --> nodeH[FFT Processor and Range Calculator]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Display and 4-20 mA HART Output]
    nodeJ[Microcontroller HMI] --> nodeA
    nodeJ --> nodeH
```

**Interpretation:**
- The **VCO** generates a linearly frequency-swept microwave signal (chirp).
- The **Power Splitter** divides the signal between the transmitting antenna and the reference mixer.
- The **Beat Frequency** between the transmitted chirp and the delayed echo is proportional to range.
- The **FFT** converts beat frequency to range bins.
- The output is delivered as a **4-20 mA analog signal** or **HART/Foundation Fieldbus** for the DCS.

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram of a Duplexer Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Transmitter High Power Pulse] --> nodeB[TR Tube Gas Discharge]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Antenna to Free Space]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Target Reflection]
    nodeD --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeE[ATR Tube Anti TR]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Receiver Low Noise LNA]
    nodeA -.->|blocks during TX| nodeF
    nodeD -.->|enables during RX| nodeF
```

**Interpretation:**
- During **transmission**, the **TR tube** ionizes and shorts the receiver path, protecting it from the high-power pulse.
- During **reception**, the **TR tube** is non-ionized (high impedance) and the weak echo passes to the receiver.
- The **ATR tube** ensures the transmitter is isolated from the receiver path.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define RADAR and explain its operating principle in 3-4 lines.**

**Model Answer:**
RADAR stands for **Radio Detection And Ranging**. It is an electromagnetic system that detects and locates objects (targets) by transmitting high-frequency radio waves and analyzing the reflected echoes. The operating principle is based on **echo ranging**: the radar transmits an RF pulse, the pulse reflects from the target, and the time delay $\tau$ between transmission and reception is used to compute the range as $R = c \tau / 2$.

*(Self-scoring: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for echo principle, 1 mark for the range equation.)*

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023] List any three applications of radar in petrochemical industries.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Continuous level measurement** in storage tanks (crude oil, LPG, LNG) using FMCW radar.
2. **Interface detection** between immiscible liquids (e.g., oil-water interface) in separators.
3. **Non-contact distance measurement** in flare stacks, reactors, and high-pressure vessels.
4. **Through-vapor level measurement** in distillation columns where ultrasonic and capacitance sensors fail.

*(Self-scoring: 1 mark each for three correct applications with brief explanation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part B questions typically have two sub-parts: **(a)** for 7 marks and **(b)** for 7 marks. Below are two alternative full-mark questions with complete model solutions.

---

### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the radar range equation starting from the concept of isotropic radiation. Clearly state the assumptions made.

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**
1. The radar is **monostatic** (single antenna for TX and RX).
2. The transmitting antenna has a directive gain $G$ and is pointed at the target.
3. The target is in the **far field** (Fraunhofer region) of the antenna.
4. The target re-radiates the intercepted power **isotropically** (worst-case assumption).
5. The propagation medium is **free space** (no atmospheric attenuation).
6. The polarization mismatch loss is negligible.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Power density at the target:** The transmitting antenna radiates $P_t$ watts with gain $G$ into free space. At a distance $R$, the power density over a sphere of area $4 \pi R^2$ is:

$$S_t = \frac{P_t \, G}{4 \pi R^2}$$

**Step 2 — Power intercepted and re-radiated by the target:** The target has a radar cross-section $\sigma$ (units: $m^2$). The total power intercepted and re-radiated isotropically is:

$$P_{\sigma} = S_t \cdot \sigma = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{4 \pi R^2}$$

**Step 3 — Echo power density at the receiving antenna:** The re-radiated echo propagates spherically over distance $R$:

$$S_r = \frac{P_{\sigma}}{4 \pi R^2} = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{(4 \pi)^2 R^4}$$

**Step 4 — Power captured by the receiving antenna:** Using the antenna's effective aperture $A_e$:

$$P_r = S_r \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma \, A_e}{(4 \pi)^2 R^4}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute gain-aperture relation** $A_e = \dfrac{G \, \lambda^2}{4 \pi}$:

$$P_r = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{(4 \pi)^2 R^4} \cdot \frac{G \, \lambda^2}{4 \pi} = \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4 \pi)^3 R^4}$$

**Final Radar Range Equation:**

$$P_r = \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4 \pi)^3 R^4}$$

**(b) [7 Marks]** A pulsed radar operates at $f = 9$ GHz with a peak transmitted power of 1.5 kW, antenna gain of 36 dB, and a minimum detectable signal of $10^{-12}$ W. The target has a radar cross-section of $2\ m^2$. Calculate the maximum range of the radar.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert all parameters to SI units.**
- Operating frequency: $f = 9 \times 10^9$ Hz
- Wavelength: $\lambda = c / f = (3 \times 10^8) / (9 \times 10^9) = 0.0333$ m
- Transmitted power: $P_t = 1.5 \times 10^3$ W
- Antenna gain (linear): $G = 10^{36/10} = 10^{3.6} \approx 3981$
- $S_{i,\min} = 10^{-12}$ W
- $\sigma = 2\ m^2$

**Step 2 — Apply the maximum range equation:**

$$R_{\max} = \left[ \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4 \pi)^3 \, S_{i,\min}} \right]^{1/4}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator:**
- $P_t \cdot G^2 = 1500 \cdot (3981)^2 = 1500 \cdot 1.585 \times 10^7 = 2.378 \times 10^{10}$
- $\lambda^2 = (0.0333)^2 = 1.111 \times 10^{-3}$
- $\sigma = 2$
- Numerator $= 2.378 \times 10^{10} \cdot 1.111 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 2 = 5.284 \times 10^7$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator:**
- $(4 \pi)^3 = (12.566)^3 = 1984$
- Denominator $= 1984 \cdot 10^{-12} = 1.984 \times 10^{-9}$

**Step 5 — Compute the ratio:**

$$\frac{\text{Numerator}}{\text{Denominator}} = \frac{5.284 \times 10^7}{1.984 \times 10^{-9}} = 2.663 \times 10^{16}$$

**Step 6 — Take the fourth root:**

$$R_{\max} = (2.663 \times 10^{16})^{1/4}$$

Computing: $(10^{16})^{1/4} = 10^4 = 10000$. And $(2.663)^{1/4} \approx 1.276$.

$$R_{\max} \approx 1.276 \times 10^4 \approx 12,760\ \text{m} \approx 12.76\ \text{km}$$

**Final Answer:** $R_{\max} \approx 12.76$ km.

---

### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working principle of FMCW radar with a neat block diagram. Derive the expression for range in terms of beat frequency.

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:** In **FMCW (Frequency Modulated Continuous Wave) radar**, the transmitter emits a microwave signal whose frequency is swept linearly over a bandwidth $\Delta f$ during a sweep period $T_s$. A portion of the transmitted signal is mixed with the delayed echo. The frequency difference (beat frequency) is proportional to the target's range.

**Block Diagram:**

```
VCO → Power Splitter → TX Antenna → Target → RX Antenna
                  ↓
              Reference Mixer → IF Amplifier → FFT Processor → Range Output
```

**Derivation:**

Let the instantaneous transmitted frequency be:

$$f_t(t) = f_0 + \frac{\Delta f}{T_s} \cdot t$$

The echo from a target at range $R$ is received after a round-trip time delay:

$$\tau = \frac{2R}{c}$$

The instantaneous received frequency at time $t$ corresponds to the transmitted frequency at the earlier time $(t - \tau)$:

$$f_r(t) = f_0 + \frac{\Delta f}{T_s} \cdot (t - \tau) = f_0 + \frac{\Delta f}{T_s} \cdot t - \frac{\Delta f}{T_s} \cdot \frac{2R}{c}$$

**Beat Frequency** = difference between transmitted and received instantaneous frequencies:

$$f_b = f_t(t) - f_r(t) = \frac{\Delta f}{T_s} \cdot \frac{2R}{c}$$

Solving for $R$:

$$\boxed{R = \frac{f_b \, c \, T_s}{2 \, \Delta f}}$$

**Significance:** This range measurement is independent of the transmitted power $P_t$, making FMCW radar highly accurate for short-range industrial applications like **petrochemical level measurement**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare pulsed radar, CW radar, and FMCW radar in tabular form. State two key advantages of FMCW radar for petrochemical level measurement.

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | Pulsed Radar | CW Radar | FMCW Radar |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transmission | Short high-power pulses | Continuous unmodulated wave | Continuous frequency-swept wave |
| Range measurement | Yes (via pulse delay) | No (no time reference) | Yes (via beat frequency) |
| Velocity measurement | Limited | Yes (via Doppler shift) | Yes (via Doppler) |
| Transmit power | Very high (MW peak) | Low | Very low (mW) |
| Range accuracy | Moderate (meter-level) | N/A | Very high (mm-level) |
| Minimum range (dead zone) | Yes (large) | No | No (negligible) |
| Typical application | Long-range military, ATC | Speed guns, Doppler | **Industrial level measurement** |
| Component complexity | Moderate | Low | Higher (linear VCO required) |

**Two Key Advantages of FMCW Radar in Petrochemical Applications:**

1. **Very low transmit power (a few milliwatts)** — This makes the instrument intrinsically safe (Ex-proof) for use in hazardous areas of petrochemical plants where flammable vapors are present.

2. **Negligible dead zone and millimeter-level accuracy** — Unlike pulsed radar, FMCW has no minimum range, allowing measurement right up to the antenna (top of the tank). The high accuracy is critical for inventory management of expensive petrochemical products.

3. *(Bonus)* Excellent performance in **dust, foam, vapor, and turbulent surfaces** — making it ideal for refinery storage tanks, distillation columns, and separators.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Forgetting the factor of **2** in the range equation $R = c\tau/2$. Students often write $R = c\tau$ (one-way travel) and lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** In the maximum range equation, the exponent is **1/4** (fourth root), **not** 1/2 or 1. Many students write $\sqrt{\cdot}$ instead of $[\cdot]^{1/4}$, resulting in completely wrong answers.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting to convert **dB to linear** for antenna gain. A gain of "30 dB" means $G = 1000$, not 30.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Confusing **RCS $\sigma$** (radar cross-section) with **physical cross-sectional area** of the target. RCS is a measure of how detectable a target is by radar; it depends on shape, material, and aspect angle.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** In FMCW derivations, forgetting the **factor of 2** in $\tau = 2R/c$ (round-trip delay).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **RADAR = RAdio Detection And Ranging** — uses reflected radio waves to detect and locate targets.
- [ ] **Operating Principle:** Transmit → Reflect → Receive → Time delay $\tau$ → Compute $R = c\tau/2$.
- [ ] **Three main parts:** Transmitter, Duplexer (TR switch), Receiver, plus shared Directive Antenna.
- [ ] **Duplexer** is essential — it protects the receiver from the high-power transmit pulse using a TR tube.
- [ ] **Radar Range Equation:** $P_r = \dfrac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4 \pi)^3 R^4}$
- [ ] **Maximum Range:** $R_{\max} = \left[ \dfrac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4 \pi)^3 \, S_{i,\min}} \right]^{1/4}$
- [ ] $R_{\max} \propto P_t^{1/4}$ — quadrupling power only doubles the range.
- [ ] **FMCW Radar:** $R = \dfrac{f_b \, c \, T_s}{2 \, \Delta f}$ — dominant in **petrochemical level measurement**.
- [ ] **CW Doppler Radar:** $v_r = \dfrac{\lambda \, \Delta f_d}{2}$ — used for velocity measurement.
- [ ] **MTI Radar** uses Doppler filtering to reject stationary clutter.
- [ ] Petrochemical applications: **tank level, interface detection, flare stack profiling, non-contact distance**.
- [ ] **Memorize:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s; $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K; $\eta_0 = 377\ \Omega$.
- [ ] **Conversion reminders:** dB → linear = $10^{x/10}$ (for power), $10^{x/20}$ (for amplitude/field).
- [ ] **Petrochemical instrument choice:** FMCW radar is preferred over ultrasonic in **vapor, foam, dust, and turbulence** environments.
- [ ] **Transmit frequencies** in industrial radar level gauges: typically **6 GHz, 10 GHz, 26 GHz** (X-band / K-band).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Float-based Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET861 - INSTRUMENTATION IN PETROCHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Float-based Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Float-Based Sensor:** A level measurement instrument that operates on the principle of **buoyancy (Archimedes' Principle)**. A buoyant element (the *float* or *displacer*) is suspended in a process fluid. As the fluid level changes, the vertical position of the float or the buoyant force acting on the displacer changes. This change is mechanically, magnetically, or electronically transduced into a usable signal — either a point-level switching action or a continuous 4–20 mA / digital output proportional to level.

In petrochemical service, float-based sensors are broadly classified as:

- **Float-type (buoyancy-float) sensors** — for point and continuous level measurement.
- **Displacer-type sensors** — for continuous level and interface (e.g., oil/water) measurement.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Fishing Bobber in a Tank:**
> Imagine tying a hollow plastic ball to a string and dropping it into a water tank. The ball *floats* on the surface. If the water level rises, the ball rides up. If the water level falls, the ball drops down. A float-based sensor is essentially a *high-precision industrial version* of that bobber. The bobber is the "float," the water is the "process fluid," and the height of the string is measured by a clever electromechanical gadget to give you a calibrated reading of the liquid level.

For displacer-type sensors, think of a **balloon partially dipped in oil** — push the balloon deeper, and you feel a heavier push-back because the oil is displaced and pushes upward. The harder the fluid pushes back, the deeper the balloon is submerged — that push-back force is what the sensor measures.

### 1.3 Why Float-Based Sensors Matter in Petrochemical Industries

- They are **mechanically simple, rugged, and intrinsically safe** (no radiation, no high voltage at the process).
- They handle **high pressures, high temperatures, viscous fluids, foams, and interfaces**.
- They are widely used in **crude storage tanks, separator vessels, distillation column reflux drums, and boiler steam drums**.
- They support both **point level (alarm, pump cut-off)** and **continuous level (control, inventory)** applications.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (in Bold)

- Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²** (standard earth gravity).
- Density of water at 4 °C: **ρ_water = 1000 kg/m³**.
- Density of typical crude oil: **ρ_oil ≈ 800 to 950 kg/m³**.
- Standard pneumatic signal: **3 to 15 psi**.
- Standard electronic signal: **4 to 20 mA DC**.
- Standard float materials (process compatibility): **SS 316, Hastelloy, Monel, PTFE, PVC**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Governing Physical Principle — Archimedes' Principle

Archimedes' Principle states:
> *"When a body is fully or partially immersed in a fluid, it experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by the body."*

The buoyant force is therefore:

$$
F_b \;=\; \rho_f \cdot g \cdot V_{\text{disp}}
$$

where:
- $F_b$ = buoyant force in **Newtons (N)**
- $\rho_f$ = density of the process fluid in **kg/m³**
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity in **m/s²** (≈ 9.81)
- $V_{\text{disp}}$ = volume of fluid displaced in **m³**

This single equation is the **heart of every float-based level sensor**.

### 2.2 Classification of Float-Based Sensors (Structured Logic)

- **A. Point-Level Float Switches**
  - *Magnetic float + reed switch* — float carries a permanent magnet; stem holds a sealed reed switch that closes when the float's magnet passes over it.
  - *Mechanical float + microswitch* — float's vertical movement directly trips a snap-action switch via a lever.
  - *Mercury tilt switch* — (legacy) a mercury bulb inside the float tilts and closes contacts.

- **B. Continuous-Level Float Sensors**
  - *Float + Potentiometric/Resistive Element* — float carries a wiper that slides on a precision resistance wire. Resistance is proportional to level.
  - *Float + Magnetostrictive Sensor* — float magnet interacts with a torsional pulse in a wire. Time-of-flight gives float position with sub-millimeter accuracy.
  - *Float + LVDT* — float mechanically coupled to the core of a Linear Variable Differential Transformer; output voltage ∝ position ∝ level.
  - *Float + Rotary Encoder* — float motion is converted via a pulley and counterweight to a digital code.

- **C. Displacer-Type (Buoyancy Force) Sensors**
  - *Torque-Tube Displacer* — most common. Buoyancy change twists a sealed torque tube; the angular twist is sensed by an LVDT/encoder.
  - *Force-Balance Displacer* — a feedback coil/lever nulls the net force; the balance current is the level output.
  - Used heavily for **interface detection** (e.g., oil/water, foam/liquid).

### 2.3 How Each Variant Works (Logic Steps)

- **Step 1 — Float variant:** A buoyant float sits at the liquid surface. As level rises or falls, the float's vertical position changes.
- **Step 2 — Magnetization:** A permanent magnet inside the float is always at the level of the air-liquid interface.
- **Step 3 — Sensing:** The magnet either closes reed switches (point level) or couples to a magnetic-strip / magnetostrictive / encoder element (continuous).
- **Step 4 — Signal conversion:** The position is converted to a 4–20 mA / 3–15 psi / digital HART signal.
- **Step 5 — Calibration:** 4 mA = lower range value (LRV) level; 20 mA = upper range value (URV) level.

- **Displacer variant — Step 1:** A heavy cylindrical displacer (denser than the fluid) is suspended from a torque tube.
- **Step 2:** The displacer is *partially* or *fully* immersed. As level rises, a greater length of displacer is immersed → greater buoyant force → *less* apparent weight.
- **Step 3:** The torque tube twists by an angle proportional to the change in apparent weight.
- **Step 4:** The angular twist is sensed and converted to a 4–20 mA signal.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Crude oil storage tanks** — interface detection between oil, water, and emulsion.
- **Refinery distillation column reflux drums** — continuous level with a torque-tube displacer.
- **LNG/LPG bullet tanks** — magnetic float switches for high-high and low-low trips.
- **Boiler steam drums** — displacer type for two-phase level measurement.
- **Chemical reactors** — PTFE/PVDF floats for corrosive acid/alkali service.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value symbols — never the bare `|` — to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Definitions | Units | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Buoyant force (general) | $F_b \;=\; \rho_f \cdot g \cdot V_{\text{disp}}$ | $\rho_f$ = fluid density, $V_{\text{disp}}$ = displaced volume | N | Valid for fully or partially immersed body |
| 2 | Fully immersed body | $F_b \;=\; \rho_f \cdot g \cdot V_{\text{body}}$ | $V_{\text{body}}$ = total body volume | N | Body is wholly under fluid |
| 3 | Partially immersed cylinder (float/displacer) | $F_b \;=\; \rho_f \cdot g \cdot A \cdot h$ | $A$ = cross-section, $h$ = immersed length | N | For vertical cylindrical float/displacer |
| 4 | Apparent weight of displacer | $W_{\text{app}} \;=\; (\rho_d - \rho_f) \cdot g \cdot A \cdot h$ | $\rho_d$ = displacer density | N | Buoyancy reduces weight |
| 5 | Displacer net force at level $L$ | $F(L) \;=\; (\rho_d - \rho_f) \cdot g \cdot A \cdot \min(L, H_d)$ | $H_d$ = total displacer length, $L$ = level | N | Below $H_d$, displacer is fully submerged |
| 6 | Hydrostatic pressure (related) | $P \;=\; \rho_f \cdot g \cdot L$ | $L$ = liquid level | Pa | For differential pressure transmitters |
| 7 | Calibration transfer (4–20 mA) | $I(L) \;=\; 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{L - L_{\min}}{L_{\max} - L_{\min}}$ | $L_{\min}, L_{\max}$ = LRV, URV | mA | Standard industrial linearization |
| 8 | Torsional twist in torque tube | $\theta \;=\; \dfrac{T \cdot L_t}{G \cdot J}$ | $T$ = torque, $L_t$ = tube length, $G$ = modulus of rigidity, $J$ = polar moment | rad | Standard torsion equation |
| 9 | Reynolds number for float damping (qualitative) | $Re \;=\; \dfrac{\rho_f \cdot v \cdot D_f}{\mu}$ | $D_f$ = float diameter, $v$ = float velocity | dimensionless | Used to assess float response & oscillation |
| 10 | Density from interface level | $\rho_{\text{mix}} \;=\; \dfrac{m}{V}$ | mass per unit volume | kg/m³ | Used in interface calibration |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Buoyant Force on a Partially Immersed Float

**Starting premise:** Consider a vertical cylindrical float of cross-sectional area $A$ and total length $H_f$ partially immersed to a depth $h$ in a fluid of density $\rho_f$.

**Step 1.** The volume of fluid displaced by the immersed portion of the float is:

$$
V_{\text{disp}} \;=\; A \cdot h
$$

**Step 2.** Substitute this into Archimedes' Principle ($F_b = \rho_f g V_{\text{disp}}$):

$$
F_b \;=\; \rho_f \cdot g \cdot (A \cdot h) \;=\; \rho_f \cdot g \cdot A \cdot h
$$

**Step 3.** The net force on the float (buoyancy minus weight) determines whether the float rises, sinks, or floats in equilibrium.

If the float is hollow (density $\rho_flt$ much less than $\rho_f$), the upward buoyant force exceeds the weight at low immersion, and the float rides higher. At equilibrium:

$$
F_b \;=\; W_{\text{float}} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \rho_f \cdot g \cdot A \cdot h_{eq} \;=\; \rho_flt \cdot g \cdot V_f
$$

**Step 4.** Solving for the equilibrium immersion depth $h_{eq}$:

$$
h_{eq} \;=\; \dfrac{\rho_flt \cdot V_f}{\rho_f \cdot A} \;=\; \dfrac{m_{\text{float}}}{\rho_f \cdot A}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** For a given float mass $m_{\text{float}}$ and cross-section $A$, the equilibrium immersion depth $h_{eq}$ *inversely* depends on fluid density. This is the basis of **density measurement using a calibrated float** (e.g., a hydrometer).

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Displacer Apparent Weight and Torque-Tube Twist

**Starting premise:** A solid cylindrical displacer of cross-section $A_d$, total length $H_d$, and density $\rho_d$ is suspended from a torque tube in a fluid of density $\rho_f$. The liquid level is $L$ (measured from the bottom of the displacer).

**Step 1.** Volume of displacer immersed depends on the level $L$:

- If $L \le H_d$ (level below top of displacer): $V_{\text{disp}} = A_d \cdot L$
- If $L \ge H_d$ (level above top): $V_{\text{disp}} = A_d \cdot H_d$ (fully submerged)

**Step 2.** The buoyant force at level $L$:

$$
F_b(L) \;=\; \rho_f \cdot g \cdot A_d \cdot \min(L,\, H_d)
$$

**Step 3.** The total downward weight of the displacer (constant):

$$
W_d \;=\; \rho_d \cdot g \cdot A_d \cdot H_d
$$

**Step 4.** Net downward force (apparent weight) on the displacer:

$$
F_{\text{net}}(L) \;=\; W_d - F_b(L) \;=\; \rho_d \cdot g \cdot A_d \cdot H_d \;-\; \rho_f \cdot g \cdot A_d \cdot \min(L,\, H_d)
$$

**Step 5.** Factor out common terms:

$$
F_{\text{net}}(L) \;=\; A_d \cdot g \cdot \big[\, \rho_d H_d \;-\; \rho_f \min(L,\, H_d) \,\big]
$$

**Step 6.** The change in apparent weight as level rises from $L = 0$ to $L = H_d$:

$$
\Delta F_{\text{net}} \;=\; \rho_f \cdot g \cdot A_d \cdot H_d
$$

This is the **full-scale range** of the displacer-based level transmitter.

**Step 7.** The torque $T$ on the torque tube is proportional to the net force via the moment arm $r$ (lever distance from tube axis to displacer):

$$
T(L) \;=\; F_{\text{net}}(L) \cdot r
$$

**Step 8.** The torsional twist angle $\theta$ of the tube is given by the standard torsion equation:

$$
\theta \;=\; \dfrac{T \cdot L_t}{G \cdot J}
$$

where:
- $L_t$ = active length of the torque tube
- $G$ = modulus of rigidity of the tube material
- $J$ = polar moment of inertia of the tube cross-section

**Step 9.** Combining, the twist is a linear function of the level $L$:

$$
\theta(L) \;=\; \dfrac{r \cdot L_t}{G \cdot J} \cdot A_d \cdot g \cdot \big[\, \rho_d H_d \;-\; \rho_f \cdot L \,\big] \qquad \text{for } L \le H_d
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** Because $\theta$ depends linearly on $L$, the angular twist can be sensed by an LVDT or encoder and converted to a 4–20 mA output, yielding a **linear level-vs-current** relationship.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Current Loop Linearization (4–20 mA Calibration)

**Starting premise:** The level $L$ has been measured and is linear with twist $\theta$. The 4–20 mA loop must be calibrated to the lower and upper range values.

**Step 1.** Define:
- $L_{\min}$ = level corresponding to 4 mA
- $L_{\max}$ = level corresponding to 20 mA

**Step 2.** Linear interpolation:

$$
I(L) \;=\; 4 \;+\; 16 \cdot \dfrac{L - L_{\min}}{L_{\max} - L_{\min}}
$$

**Step 3.** Equivalent form (inverting to obtain level from current):

$$
L(I) \;=\; L_{\min} \;+\; \big(L_{\max} - L_{\min}\big) \cdot \dfrac{I - 4}{16}
$$

**Step 4.** Verification at endpoints:
- At $L = L_{\min}$: $I = 4 + 16 \cdot 0 = 4$ mA ✓
- At $L = L_{\max}$: $I = 4 + 16 \cdot 1 = 20$ mA ✓

This is the **standard "lift-off" live-zero convention** in industrial instrumentation.

---

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (Analytical Matrix)

**Problem:** A torque-tube displacer level sensor is installed in a refinery reflux drum.
- Displacer length: $H_d = 0.50$ m
- Displacer cross-section: $A_d = 0.0015$ m²
- Displacer density: $\rho_d = 2400$ kg/m³
- Process fluid density (hydrocarbon): $\rho_f = 820$ kg/m³
- Calibrated range: 0 to 0.50 m → 4 to 20 mA

**Find:** (i) Full-scale buoyant force change, (ii) Net force at 0%, 50%, 100% level, (iii) Output current at 0.30 m.

**Solution — Step (i): Full-scale buoyant force change:**

$$
\Delta F_b \;=\; \rho_f \cdot g \cdot A_d \cdot H_d \;=\; 820 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.0015 \cdot 0.50
$$

$$
\Delta F_b \;=\; 6.034 \text{ N (approx.)}
$$

**Solution — Step (ii): Net apparent weight at 0%, 50%, 100%:**

Constant weight:

$$
W_d \;=\; \rho_d \cdot g \cdot A_d \cdot H_d \;=\; 2400 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.0015 \cdot 0.50 \;=\; 17.658 \text{ N}
$$

At 0% level ($L = 0$): $F_b = 0$, so $F_{\text{net}} = 17.658$ N.
At 50% level ($L = 0.25$ m): $F_b = 820 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.0015 \cdot 0.25 = 3.017$ N, so $F_{\text{net}} = 14.641$ N.
At 100% level ($L = 0.50$ m): $F_b = 6.034$ N, so $F_{\text{net}} = 11.624$ N.

**Solution — Step (iii): Output current at 0.30 m:**

$$
I(0.30) \;=\; 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{0.30 - 0}{0.50 - 0} \;=\; 4 + 16 \cdot 0.6 \;=\; 4 + 9.6 \;=\; 13.6 \text{ mA}
$$

**Result:** The sensor outputs **13.6 mA** at a level of 0.30 m.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Torque-Tube Displacer Level System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    proc1["PROCESS VESSEL WITH PROCESS FLUID"]
    proc2["LEVEL L CHANGES WITH PROCESS"]
    disp1["DISPLACER SUBMERSED TO DEPTH L"]
    phys1["BUOYANT FORCE FB INCREASES WITH L"]
    phys2["NET FORCE FN = WD MINUS FB DECREASES"]
    torque1["TORQUE TUBE TWISTS BY ANGLE THETA"]
    sense1["LVDT OR HALL SENSOR READS THETA"]
    elec1["TRANSMITTER ELECTRONICS LINEARIZE"]
    out1["OUTPUT 4 TO 20 MA OR HART"]
    inv1["CONTROL ROOM INDICATOR"]
    inv2["DCS OR SCADA RECORDING"]
    inv3["ALARM AND INTERLOCK LOGIC"]

    proc1 --> proc2
    proc2 --> disp1
    disp1 --> phys1
    phys1 --> phys2
    phys2 --> torque1
    torque1 --> sense1
    sense1 --> elec1
    elec1 --> out1
    out1 --> inv1
    out1 --> inv2
    out1 --> inv3
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Magnetic Reed-Switch Float Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    liq1["PROCESS LIQUID LEVEL L"]
    fl1["FLOAT WITH PERMANENT MAGNET"]
    reed1["REED SWITCH ARRAY ON STEM"]
    cond1["SWITCH CLOSURE AT DEFINED L"]
    wire1["CABLE TO PLC OR RELAY"]
    out1["DIGITAL ON OFF SIGNAL"]
    out2["ALARM OR PUMP CUTOFF"]

    liq1 --> fl1
    fl1 --> reed1
    reed1 --> cond1
    cond1 --> wire1
    wire1 --> out1
    wire1 --> out2
```

### 4.3 Classification of Float-Based Sensors

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    rootA["FLOAT BASED LEVEL SENSORS"]

    rootA --> branch1["POINT LEVEL SWITCHES"]
    rootA --> branch2["CONTINUOUS LEVEL SENSORS"]
    rootA --> branch3["DISPLACER TYPE SENSORS"]

    branch1 --> s1a["MAGNETIC FLOAT WITH REED SWITCH"]
    branch1 --> s1b["MECHANICAL FLOAT WITH MICROSWITCH"]
    branch1 --> s1c["MERCURY TILT SWITCH LEGACY"]

    branch2 --> s2a["FLOAT WITH POTENTIOMETER"]
    branch2 --> s2b["FLOAT WITH MAGNETOSTRICTIVE WIRE"]
    branch2 --> s2c["FLOAT WITH LVDT"]
    branch2 --> s2d["FLOAT WITH ROTARY ENCODER"]

    branch3 --> s3a["TORQUE TUBE DISPLACER"]
    branch3 --> s3b["FORCE BALANCE DISPLACER"]
    branch3 --> s3c["INTERFACE DETECTION DISPLACER"]
```

### 4.4 Signal Conditioning Chain for Continuous Float Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pos1["FLOAT POSITION X"]
    trans1["MECHANICAL TO ELECTRICAL TRANSDUCER"]
    sig1["RAW VOLTAGE OR RESISTANCE"]
    cond1["SIGNAL CONDITIONER AMPLIFIER FILTER"]
    conv1["V TO I CONVERTER"]
    loop1["4 TO 20 MA LOOP"]
    load1["DCS CARD OR INDICATOR"]

    pos1 --> trans1
    trans1 --> sig1
    sig1 --> cond1
    cond1 --> conv1
    conv1 --> loop1
    loop1 --> load1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The four diagrams above map the **functional signal path** from the physical float to the control-room DCS, not a literal cross-sectional drawing. In KTU 2024 scheme answer sheets, examiners accept both *schematic* and *functional block* representations. Always include a labeled diagram and add at least three annotations: the input, the transducer element, and the output signal.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **State Archimedes' Principle and explain how it is applied in float-based level sensors.**

**Model Answer:**

Archimedes' Principle states that a body fully or partially immersed in a fluid experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by the body. Mathematically:

$$
F_b \;=\; \rho_f \cdot g \cdot V_{\text{disp}}
$$

In float-based level sensors, this principle is applied in two ways:

- **Float-type sensors** — a buoyant hollow float rides on the liquid surface. As the level changes, the float's vertical position changes proportionally. The displacement volume of the immersed float is directly linked to the level.
- **Displacer-type sensors** — a heavy displacer is partially immersed. The buoyant force acts upward, reducing the apparent weight of the displacer. The change in apparent weight is measured and correlated to the level.

In both cases, the magnitude of the buoyant force is the **primary physical variable** transduced into an electrical or pneumatic signal.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Differentiate between a float switch and a displacer-type level sensor in three points.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Float Switch | Displacer-Type Sensor |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Point-level** device — gives ON/OFF output at preset heights | **Continuous-level** device — gives 4–20 mA proportional to level |
| 2 | Float is *less dense* than the fluid; rides on the surface | Displacer is *denser* than the fluid; partially submerged |
| 3 | Output is a digital switching action via reed/microswitch | Output is an analog signal derived from buoyancy-force change |
| 4 | Used for alarms, pump cut-offs, overflow protection | Used for level control, interface measurement, inventory |
| 5 | Simpler construction, lower cost | More complex (torque tube, force balance), higher accuracy |

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Displacer-Type Level Sensor Deep Dive

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **working principle of a torque-tube displacer level transmitter**. Discuss how buoyancy force is converted to a 4–20 mA signal. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A torque-tube displacer has the following specifications:
- Displacer length $H_d$ = 0.60 m
- Cross-section $A_d$ = 0.0012 m²
- Displacer density $\rho_d$ = 2200 kg/m³
- Fluid density $\rho_f$ = 750 kg/m³ (light hydrocarbon)

Calculate:
(i) The buoyant force at 100% level.
(ii) The change in buoyant force per metre of level.
(iii) The output current at level 0.42 m for a calibrated span of 0 to 0.60 m. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

A torque-tube displacer level transmitter works on Archimedes' Principle.

**Construction:**

- A sealed cylindrical **displacer** (denser than the process fluid) is suspended from the end of a **torque tube**.
- The torque tube is fixed at the top (welded to the chamber) and free at the bottom. It has a high torsional stiffness and a precision LVDT/sensor attached.
- The displacer hangs inside an external **cage/chamber** that is connected to the process vessel by impulse lines, or directly inside the vessel via a stilling well.

**Working:**

- When the liquid level rises, a greater length of the displacer is submerged.
- The buoyant force $F_b = \rho_f g A_d h$ increases linearly with the immersed length $h$.
- The net downward force on the torque tube is $F_{\text{net}} = W_d - F_b$, which *decreases* as level rises.
- The torque tube **twists** by a small angle $\theta$ proportional to the change in $F_{\text{net}}$.
- The angular twist is sensed by an LVDT, Hall sensor, or rotary encoder attached to the top of the torque tube.
- The sensed position is linearized and converted to a **4–20 mA** current in HART or analog form. The signal is calibrated so that **4 mA = 0% level** and **20 mA = 100% level**.

**Why this design is preferred in petrochemical service:**

- It is **rugged, intrinsically safe, and tolerates high temperatures and pressures**.
- It can detect **interfaces** (e.g., oil over water) by careful density calibration.
- The **cage-mounted** configuration is removable without depressurizing the vessel.

**Sketch (to be drawn in answer sheet):** A labelled diagram showing the displacer suspended from a torque tube, the LVDT/encoder at the top, the welded chamber, and the 4–20 mA output line.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

Given:
- $H_d = 0.60$ m, $A_d = 0.0012$ m², $\rho_d = 2200$ kg/m³, $\rho_f = 750$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**(i) Buoyant force at 100% level (fully immersed):**

$$
F_{b,\text{FS}} \;=\; \rho_f \cdot g \cdot A_d \cdot H_d \;=\; 750 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.0012 \cdot 0.60
$$

$$
F_{b,\text{FS}} \;=\; 5.298 \text{ N (approx.)}
$$

**(ii) Change in buoyant force per metre of level:**

$$
\dfrac{dF_b}{dL} \;=\; \rho_f \cdot g \cdot A_d \;=\; 750 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.0012 \;=\; 8.829 \text{ N/m}
$$

**(iii) Output current at level 0.42 m:**

Calibrated span: 0 to 0.60 m → 4 to 20 mA.

$$
I(0.42) \;=\; 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{0.42 - 0}{0.60 - 0} \;=\; 4 + 16 \cdot 0.7 \;=\; 4 + 11.2 \;=\; 15.2 \text{ mA}
$$

**Result:** At level 0.42 m, the transmitter outputs **15.2 mA**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **working of a magnetic float-type level sensor with reed switches**. List four industrial applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **float-type sensors with displacer-type sensors** under the headings: principle, output type, accuracy, applications, advantages, and limitations. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Construction:**

- A **hollow float** (often SS-316 or Hastelloy) encloses a permanent magnet.
- The float rides freely on the liquid surface, guided by a **vertical stem** (or non-magnetic guide tube).
- The stem houses a series of **hermetically sealed reed switches** at predefined heights.
- Wires from the reed switches are taken out through a sealed gland to a junction box / PLC / relay.

**Working:**

- As the float rises or falls with the liquid level, the permanent magnet **aligns with the reed switch at the corresponding height**.
- The magnetic field closes (or opens) the reed contacts at that level.
- Each switch gives a **discrete point-level signal** (e.g., low-low, low, high, high-high).
- For **continuous** measurement, the stem may carry a **precision resistance wire with a magnetic wiper** in the float, or a **magnetostrictive wire** that produces a torsional pulse at the float's magnet position.

**Four industrial applications:**

1. **High-high and low-low level alarms** in LNG/LPG storage bullets.
2. **Pump cut-off** in crude oil storage tank sumps.
3. **Sump pit level** monitoring in oily-water separators.
4. **Chemical tank overflow protection** for acid/alkali service (with PTFE floats).

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

| # | Parameter | Float-Type Sensor | Displacer-Type Sensor |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Principle** | Float rides on liquid surface (Archimedes' buoyancy) | Displacer is partially submerged; change in buoyant force is measured |
| 2 | **Output Type** | Mostly point-level (ON/OFF); continuous with encoder/LVDT | Continuous 4–20 mA / HART |
| 3 | **Accuracy** | ±2 to ±5 mm (point) / ±5 mm (continuous) | ±0.5% of calibrated span |
| 4 | **Applications** | Alarms, sump pumps, point-level trips | Level control, interface, custody transfer |
| 5 | **Advantages** | Simple, cheap, intrinsically safe, no power at sensor | Continuous output, interface detection, high accuracy |
| 6 | **Limitations** | Float can stick in viscous/foamy fluids; limited to non-coating fluids | Mechanical complexity, sensitive to density changes, expensive |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> - **Forgetting the live-zero offset.** Always remember that 4 mA (or 3 psi) is the *live zero*. Marks are routinely lost when students write $I = 20 \cdot L/L_{\max}$ without the +4 offset.
> - **Confusing float and displacer density conventions.** A *float* is less dense than the fluid; a *displacer* is denser. Mixing these is a common error in 14-mark answers.
> - **Omitting units in numerical answers.** A 7-mark numerical without proper N, mA, kg/m³, and m units loses one mark per unmarked quantity.
> - **No diagram in descriptive answers.** A 7-mark question without a labelled diagram typically loses 2 marks in KTU valuation.
> - **Skipping the density assumption.** Displacer-type calculations depend on the **fluid density remaining constant**. A change in $\rho_f$ (e.g., switch from naphtha to crude) invalidates the calibration. Always mention this assumption in your answer.
> - **Ignoring the partially vs fully immersed case.** For the displacer, the buoyant force equation is *piecewise* — write it as $\min(L, H_d)$, not as a single expression.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-DENSITY SUMMARY — Float-Based Sensors (Module 3)**

- Float-based sensors operate on **Archimedes' Principle**: $F_b = \rho_f g V_{\text{disp}}$.
- Two main families: **Float-type** (rises/falls with level) and **Displacer-type** (senses buoyant-force change).
- **Float switches** are point-level devices using a **permanent magnet + reed switch** or a **mechanical microswitch**.
- **Continuous float sensors** use a **potentiometric wiper, magnetostrictive wire, LVDT, or rotary encoder**.
- **Torque-tube displacer** is the workhorse of refinery and petrochemical continuous level measurement.
- Apparent weight of displacer: $F_{\text{net}} = (\rho_d - \rho_f) g A_d h$ for partial immersion.
- Full-scale buoyant force: $\Delta F_b = \rho_f g A_d H_d$ (depends on **fluid density**).
- 4–20 mA calibration: $I(L) = 4 + 16 \cdot (L - L_{\min})/(L_{\max} - L_{\min})$.
- Displacer is **denser than fluid**; float is **less dense than fluid** — never reverse this.
- **Interface detection** (e.g., oil/water) is a unique application of displacer-type sensors, exploiting density difference.
- **HART, Foundation Fieldbus, PROFIBUS-PA** digital protocols are commonly layered on top of 4–20 mA analog.
- Failure modes: **float sticking in viscous fluids, reed switch welding, displacer coating in service, density change of process fluid**.
- Always specify **material compatibility** (SS 316, Hastelloy C, PTFE, Monel) for petrochemical service.
- Standard industrial signal is **4–20 mA with live zero** (3 psi for pneumatic).
- In **KTU 14-mark answers**, always include: definition, principle, labelled diagram, equations, numerical (if asked), and applications.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview of Level Controllers and Switches
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET861 - INSTRUMENTATION IN PETROCHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Overview of Level Controllers and Switches

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of petrochemical process instrumentation, **Level Measurement** refers to the determination of the position or height of the interface between two phases (typically liquid-gas, liquid-liquid, or solid-liquid) within a process vessel, tank, or reactor. A **Level Controller** is an automatic control device that continuously monitors the level variable and manipulates a final control element (usually an inlet or outlet valve, or a pump speed) to maintain the level at a desired **setpoint (SP)**. A **Level Switch**, in contrast, is a **discrete (binary) point-detection device** that changes state (opens or closes an electrical contact) when the process fluid reaches a predetermined physical threshold.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, these belong to the broader family of *Process Variable Transducers and Controllers* and are classified under CO2 (Identify and select appropriate sensors and transducers for process variables) and CO3 (Design control loops for petrochemical processes).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 3):**
> The student must distinguish clearly between:
> - **Continuous (Analog) Level Transmitters** → feed a 4–20 mA/HART signal to a controller.
> - **Discrete (Point) Level Switches** → give only ON/OFF electrical contact closure.
> - **Level Controllers** → combine a level measurement + a control algorithm + a final control element logic.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **bathroom overhead water tank** in a typical Kerala home.

- The **water tank** is the process vessel.
- The **ball-cock valve (the floating ball on a rod)** is the simplest *level controller* — as the water rises, the floating ball physically pushes the inlet valve closed. As water drops, the ball falls and the valve reopens. This is mechanical **proportional (P-only) level control**.
- An **electrical float switch** fixed at the top of the tank is the *level switch* — when water touches it, it cuts OFF the motor (high-level safety interlock). Another float switch at the bottom turns the motor ON (low-level start).
- A **digital level controller with an ultrasonic sensor** mounted on top of the tank is the modern *continuous level transmitter + PID controller* — it continuously senses the gap (h) between sensor and water, calculates level, compares it to a setpoint, and adjusts the inlet valve opening to keep level constant regardless of how many taps are open downstairs.

In a petrochemical refinery, replace the water tank with a **crude oil distillation column reflux drum**, the ball valve with a **pneumatic control valve on the reflux line**, and the ultrasonic sensor with a **differential-pressure (DP) cell** or a **guided-wave radar (GWR) probe** — the principle is identical, only the hardware becomes explosion-proof and the control algorithm becomes a PID with split-range output.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Engineering Metrics Used Throughout This Module:**
> - Level range: **0 – 100 % of vessel span** or **0 – X metres**.
> - Control signal: **3 – 15 psig** (pneumatic) or **4 – 20 mA** (electronic / HART).
> - Switch rating: typically **230 VAC / 5 A** or **24 VDC** (NAMUR-compatible for hazardous areas).
> - Standard reference: **Hydrostatic head ρ·g·h** at **ρ** = density in **kg/m³**, **g** = **9.81 m/s²**, **h** = liquid column in **m**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Level Instrumentation

The petrochemical industry uses a wide spectrum of level devices, which can be classified along two orthogonal axes:

**Axis A — Function (Continuous vs. Discrete):**
1. **Continuous Level Transmitters** (output a variable signal proportional to level).
2. **Point Level Switches** (output a binary ON/OFF contact at a fixed height).

**Axis B — Measurement Principle:**
- **Hydrostatic / Differential Pressure (DP)**.
- **Buoyancy (Displacer / Float).**
- **Capacitance (RF Admittance).**
- **Ultrasonic (Time-of-Flight).**
- **Radar (Non-Contact Microwave FMCW).**
- **Guided Wave Radar (GWR / TDR).**
- **Conductivity (Electrolytic).**
- **Vibrating Fork (Tuning Fork).**
- **Optical / Laser / Nuclear**.

### 2.2 Continuous Level Transmitters — Operating Principles

- **Differential Pressure (DP) Transmitter:**
  The hydrostatic pressure exerted by a liquid column of height *h* and density *ρ* is detected by a DP cell connected to the bottom of the vessel and the vapor space on top.
  *Best for*: closed, pressurised vessels; clean liquids; slurry with impulse lines.

- **Buoyancy (Displacer) Transmitter:**
  A displacer of fixed volume *V* is partially immersed. The Archimedean buoyancy force *F<sub>b</sub> = ρ · g · V* changes with level, deflecting a torque tube whose rotation is converted to a 4–20 mA signal.
  *Best for*: interface detection (oil/water), clean liquids, interface control in separators.

- **Capacitance Probe:**
  The probe and vessel wall form the two plates of a capacitor. As level rises, the dielectric constant changes (from air ≈ 1 to hydrocarbon ≈ 2–3 or water ≈ 80), changing capacitance *C = ε A/d*. Measured by an RF admittance bridge.
  *Best for*: corrosive liquids, small vessels, sticky/oily fluids.

- **Ultrasonic (Time-of-Flight):**
  A piezoelectric transducer emits a 20–200 kHz pulse; the time-of-flight *t* of the echo from the liquid surface is measured. Distance *d = v · t / 2*, where *v* is the speed of sound in the vapor (typically 340–360 m/s in air).
  *Best for*: slurry, sewage, large open-top tanks, *non-contact* applications.

- **Radar (FMCW / Non-Contact Microwave):**
  Uses frequency-modulated continuous wave (FMCW) at 6 GHz, 26 GHz, or 80 GHz. The frequency difference between transmitted and reflected signal is proportional to *d*. Unaffected by vapor composition, temperature, or dust.
  *Best for*: harsh petrochemical service — LNG, asphalt, ammonia, vacuum columns.

- **Guided Wave Radar (GWR / TDR):**
  A low-energy pulse travels along a metallic probe at the speed of light fraction. Time-domain reflectometry (TDR) detects the impedance discontinuity at the liquid surface. *Unaffected* by foam, turbulence, or vapor — the gold standard for refinery service.

### 2.3 Level Switches — Operating Principles

Level switches are *discrete* sensors that alarm or interlock when level crosses a single threshold. They are typically **fail-safe** devices.

- **Float Switch (Magnetic):**
  A hermetically sealed reed switch is actuated by a permanent magnet housed inside a hinged float. As the float rises/falls with the liquid, the magnet passes the reed switch and closes the contact.
  *Service*: water, light oils; *Limitation*: cannot be used with turbulent or viscous liquids.

- **Conductivity (Resistance) Switch:**
  Two electrodes (probe and vessel wall) form an open circuit. When conductive liquid (water, acids, alkalis) bridges the gap, current flows and a relay trips. A relay-based amplifier with sensitivity adjustment prevents false trips due to coating/film.
  *Service*: conductive liquids only (not hydrocarbons); cheap and reliable for boiler drum, condensate tanks.

- **Capacitance Point Switch:**
  Same RF admittance principle as the continuous transmitter, but with a fixed-threshold comparator output. Suitable for both conductive and non-conductive liquids and bulk solids.

- **Vibrating Fork (Tuning Fork) Switch:**
  A piezo-electrically excited fork vibrates at its natural frequency (~ 80–120 Hz) in air. When immersed in liquid, the resonant frequency shifts (typically by 8–10 Hz); this shift is detected and the output relay de-energises.
  *Service*: clean liquids, slurries up to 10 000 cP; widely used for high/low-level alarms on chemical reactors.

- **Optical (Refractometric) Switch:**
  An LED emits infrared light into a prism. In air, total internal reflection occurs at the prism tip and the photodetector is illuminated → output ON. When liquid touches the prism, the refractive index changes, light escapes, and the photodetector sees darkness → output OFF.
  *Service*: clean, transparent liquids, hygienic applications.

- **Diaphragm / Paddle / Rotating Paddle Switch:**
  Used mainly for *solids* (silos of catalyst, polymer pellets, coke). A low-rpm motor rotates a paddle; when solids surround the paddle, the motor stalls and a torque switch trips.

### 2.4 Level Controllers — Control Strategies

A level controller receives the transmitter signal, compares it to a setpoint, computes an error *e(t) = SP – PV*, and drives a final control element.

- **On–Off (Two-Position) Control:**
  Simplest strategy. Output is either fully OPEN or fully CLOSED. Causes continuous cycling around the setpoint. *Used for*: non-critical tanks, sump pumps, boiler low-water cutoff.

- **Proportional (P) Control:**
  Controller output moves in proportion to error: *m(t) = K<sub>p</sub> · e(t) + m<sub>0</sub>*. Reduces cycling but introduces **offset** (steady-state error). *Common for*: surge vessels, reflux drums where tight level control is not essential.

- **PI / PID Control:**
  Adds integral action to eliminate offset. Derivative action anticipates changes.
  *Used for*: critical separators, fractionator reflux drums, reactor level.

- **Split-Range Control:**
  The 4–20 mA output is split so that, for example, 4–12 mA drives the inlet valve from 0–100 % open and 12–20 mA drives the outlet valve from 0–100 % open. Provides tight control on vessels with both inflow and outflow disturbances.

- **Cascade Control:**
  Level controller (master / primary) sets the setpoint of a flow controller (slave / secondary), which manipulates the actual valve. *Used for*: fast load changes, viscous or slurry service.

- **Override / Constraint Control:**
  Two controllers share a valve via a low-/high-signal selector. The level controller normally controls the valve, but if a high-level switch closes, the override selector takes over and forces the valve fully open to prevent overflow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Average Level" Control is Often Deliberately Loose:**
> In refineries, a reflux drum level loop is often tuned **with a wide proportional band and no integral** (very low gain). This is intentional: the level acts as a **buffer (surge capacity)** to absorb short-term mismatches between overhead vapour and reflux pump capacity. Tight level control would transfer *all* the disturbance downstream, destabilising the column. This is the **"averaging level control"** strategy — a KTU favourite exam topic.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Boundary / Validity Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = \rho \, g \, h$ | P = hydrostatic pressure, ρ = liquid density, g = gravity, h = liquid column height | Pa, kg/m³, m/s², m | Static liquid only; ignores dynamic head |
| 2 | $F_{b} = \rho \, g \, V$ | F<sub>b</sub> = buoyancy force, V = immersed volume | N, kg/m³, m/s², m³ | Fully developed Archimedes' principle |
| 3 | $\Delta P = P_{taps} - P_{zero}$ | Differential pressure output of DP cell | kPa or mbar | Calibrated to vessel span |
| 4 | $d = \dfrac{v \, t}{2}$ | d = distance to surface, v = velocity of sound/MW, t = round-trip time | m, m/s, s | For ultrasonic and radar ToF |
| 5 | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon \, A}{d}$ | C = capacitance, ε = permittivity, A = plate area, d = separation | Farad | Parallel-plate idealisation |
| 6 | $u(t) = K_{p}\!\left[e(t) + \dfrac{1}{T_{i}}\!\int e(t)\,dt + T_{d}\,\dfrac{de(t)}{dt}\right]$ | PID output in time domain | % | Standard ISA form; T<sub>i</sub>, T<sub>d</sub> in seconds or minutes |
| 7 | $e(t) = SP - PV(t)$ | Control error | % or engineering units | Always signed; sign convention depends on direct/reverse action |
| 8 | $\Delta f \propto d$ (FMCW radar) | Frequency difference proportional to distance | Hz | Linear in distance |
| 9 | $m_{valve}(\%) = \dfrac{I - 4}{16}\!\times\!100$ | Valve position from current | % | 4–20 mA standard |
| 10 | $\dfrac{h}{H} = \dfrac{I - 4\,\text{mA}}{16\,\text{mA}}$ | Level fraction from 4–20 mA | dimensionless | Zero-based calibration |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Level controllers and switches are the *guardians of process containment* in a refinery. A single failed high-level switch on a distillation column can cause liquid carryover into the overhead line, triggering compressor damage, environmental release, or catastrophic vessel rupture. They are therefore classified as **Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)** devices when used in IEC 61508/61511 loops, with required **SIL-1, SIL-2, or SIL-3** ratings depending on consequence severity.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hydrostatic Pressure → Level

This is the **backbone derivation** for DP-based level measurement. Most KTU problems reduce to this.

**Statement:** A closed vertical cylindrical vessel of internal diameter **D = 2 m** contains hydrocarbon liquid of specific gravity **SG = 0.85** to a height **h = 4 m**. A DP transmitter is connected between the bottom tap (LL = 0 %) and the top tap (above the liquid surface). Find the differential pressure at (a) 0 % level and (b) 100 % level. The transmitter has a calibrated range of 0 – X kPa; find X.

**Step 1 — Identify the relevant physical law.**
The pressure at the bottom tap is the sum of vapor pressure and hydrostatic head.
At the top tap (above liquid surface), the pressure is the vapor space pressure.

**Step 2 — Express the bottom-tap pressure.**
$$P_{bot} = P_{vap} + \rho \, g \, h$$

**Step 3 — Express the top-tap pressure.**
$$P_{top} = P_{vap}$$

**Step 4 — Subtract to obtain differential pressure.**
$$\Delta P = P_{bot} - P_{top} = \rho \, g \, h$$

**Step 5 — Convert specific gravity to density.**
Water density ρ<sub>w</sub> = 1000 kg/m³ at 4 °C.
$$\rho = SG \times \rho_{w} = 0.85 \times 1000 = 850 \;\text{kg/m}^3$$

**Step 6 — Compute density in kg/m³.**
$$\rho = 850 \;\text{kg/m}^3$$

**Step 7 — Plug into the hydrostatic formula.**
$$\Delta P = 850 \times 9.81 \times 4$$

**Step 8 — Evaluate the product.**
$$\Delta P = 33\,354 \;\text{Pa}$$

**Step 9 — Convert to kPa.**
$$\Delta P = 33.354 \;\text{kPa}$$

**Step 10 — State the calibrated transmitter range.**
The transmitter is calibrated for 0 % to 100 %, so its span is **0 – 33.354 kPa** (or equivalently 0 – 333.54 mbar).

**Conclusion:**
At 0 % level: ΔP = **0 kPa**.
At 100 % level: ΔP = **33.354 kPa**.
The 4 mA point maps to 0 kPa and the 20 mA point maps to 33.354 kPa.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Buoyancy Force on a Displacer

**Statement:** A cylindrical displacer of diameter **d = 50 mm** and length **L = 600 mm** is suspended inside a separator. The interface between oil (ρ<sub>oil</sub> = 820 kg/m³) and water (ρ<sub>w</sub> = 1000 kg/m³) is at height **h = 250 mm** from the bottom of the displacer. Find (a) the buoyancy force at this interface position, (b) the change in buoyancy if the interface drops to **h = 100 mm**.

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of the displacer.**
$$A = \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.050)^{2}}{4}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the area.**
$$A = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Volume immersed in oil (above interface).**
Length of displacer in oil = L − h = 600 − 250 = 350 mm = 0.350 m.
$$V_{oil} = A \times 0.350 = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.350 = 6.872 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m}^{3}$$

**Step 4 — Volume immersed in water (below interface).**
$$V_{w} = A \times 0.250 = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.250 = 4.908 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m}^{3}$$

**Step 5 — Total buoyancy force (sum of contributions).**
$$F_{b} = \rho_{oil} \, g \, V_{oil} + \rho_{w} \, g \, V_{w}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute numerical values.**
$$F_{b} = 820 \times 9.81 \times 6.872 \times 10^{-4} + 1000 \times 9.81 \times 4.908 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate each term.**
Term 1 (oil contribution):
$$820 \times 9.81 \times 6.872 \times 10^{-4} = 5.529 \;\text{N}$$

**Step 8 — Evaluate second term.**
$$1000 \times 9.81 \times 4.908 \times 10^{-4} = 4.815 \;\text{N}$$

**Step 9 — Sum the contributions.**
$$F_{b} = 5.529 + 4.815 = 10.344 \;\text{N}$$

**Step 10 — Repeat for the second interface position (h = 100 mm).**
New V<sub>oil</sub> = A × 0.500 = 9.817 × 10⁻⁴ m³; new V<sub>w</sub> = A × 0.100 = 1.963 × 10⁻⁴ m³.
$$F_{b}^{'} = 820 \times 9.81 \times 9.817 \times 10^{-4} + 1000 \times 9.81 \times 1.963 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 11 — Evaluate the new force.**
$$F_{b}^{'} = 7.896 + 1.926 = 9.822 \;\text{N}$$

**Step 12 — Compute change in buoyancy.**
$$\Delta F_{b} = F_{b}^{'} - F_{b} = 9.822 - 10.344 = -0.522 \;\text{N}$$

**Conclusion:**
Buoyancy force drops by **0.522 N** as the interface drops from 250 mm to 100 mm. The torque tube in the displacer transmitter converts this force change into a proportional 4–20 mA signal.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Ultrasonic Time-of-Flight to Level

**Statement:** An ultrasonic level sensor is mounted **H = 6 m** above the bottom of an open tank. The measured round-trip time is **t = 32 ms**. Speed of sound in air at the operating temperature is **v = 343 m/s**. Calculate (a) the actual distance from sensor to liquid surface, (b) the level in the tank.

**Step 1 — Apply the time-of-flight formula.**
$$d = \frac{v \, t}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**
$$d = \frac{343 \times 0.032}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate numerator.**
$$343 \times 0.032 = 10.976 \;\text{m}$$

**Step 4 — Divide by 2.**
$$d = 5.488 \;\text{m}$$

**Step 5 — Compute level.**
$$L = H - d = 6 - 5.488 = 0.512 \;\text{m}$$

**Conclusion:**
The liquid is at **0.512 m** above the tank bottom. Note: ultrasonic sensors are *blind* in the first 0.25–0.5 m below the transducer (called *blanking distance*); the user must verify d > blanking distance.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selecting a Level Device

| Process Condition | Recommended Primary | Recommended Switch | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Clean liquid, pressurised | DP transmitter | Vibrating fork | Reliable, cheap, accurate |
| Corrosive acid | Capacitance / GWR | Capacitance point | Non-metallic wetted parts available |
| Foam on top | Guided Wave Radar | Vibrating fork | GWR penetrates foam |
| Oil/Water interface | Displacer / GWR | Conductivity (water side) | Density difference exploited |
| Slurry, sewage | Ultrasonic non-contact | Float (in stilling well) | Avoids fouling |
| LNG / cryogenic | GWR or servo | Vibrating fork | Unaffected by vapor |
| Bulk solids (catalyst) | Rotating paddle / GWR | Rotating paddle switch | Mechanical integrity |
| Hygienic / pharmaceutical | GWR / radar | Optical | CIP-cleanable |
| Vacuum column bottom | DP (with sealed reference) | Capacitance | Vapor pressure compensation |
| Small drip tank | Float switch only | Float | Simplicity |

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Controller Action & Tuning

| Vessel Service | Loop Action | Kp Range | Ti | Td | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Reflux drum (averaging) | Direct, P-only | 0.5 – 2 | OFF | OFF | Wide PB = averaging |
| Boiler drum | Reverse, PI | 1 – 5 | 2 – 10 min | OFF | Critical water level |
| Separator interface | Reverse, PID | 0.8 – 3 | 5 – 15 min | 0.1 – 0.5 min | Tight interface |
| Surge vessel | Direct, P-only | 1 – 4 | OFF | OFF | Acts as buffer |
| Reactor level | Reverse, PI | 1 – 5 | 1 – 5 min | OFF | Fast response needed |
| Sump pump | Reverse, ON-OFF | n/a | n/a | n/a | Differential gap required |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram A — Block Diagram of a Closed-Loop Level Control System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Setpoint SP] --> SUM[Comparator Summing Junction]
    LT[Level Transmitter LT] --> PV[Process Variable PV signal 4 to 20 mA]
    PV --> SUM
    SUM --> EC[Error e equals SP minus PV]
    EC --> LC[Level Controller PID Algorithm]
    LC --> OUT[Output 4 to 20 mA or 3 to 15 psig]
    OUT --> FCV[Final Control Valve FCV on inlet or outlet]
    FCV --> PROC[Process Vessel Tank or Separator]
    PROC --> LT
    FB[Feedback Loop] -.-> LT
```

### 4.2 Diagram B — Classification of Level Instrumentation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Level Instrumentation in Petrochemical Industries]
    ROOT --> A[Continuous Level Transmitters]
    ROOT --> B[Discrete Point Level Switches]

    A --> A1[Differential Pressure DP]
    A --> A2[Buoyancy Displacer or Float]
    A --> A3[Capacitance RF Admittance]
    A --> A4[Ultrasonic Time of Flight]
    A --> A5[Radar FMCW Non Contact]
    A --> A6[Guided Wave Radar GWR TDR]

    B --> B1[Float Magnetic Reed]
    B --> B2[Conductivity Resistance]
    B --> B3[Vibrating Fork Tuning Fork]
    B --> B4[Optical Refractometric]
    B --> B5[Capacitance Point Switch]
    B --> B6[Diaphragm or Paddle for Solids]
```

### 4.3 Diagram C — Split-Range Level Control Scheme

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LT[LT Level Transmitter 4 to 20 mA] --> LC[LC Level Controller]
    LC --> SR[Split Range Relay or Function]
    SR --> CV1[Inlet Control Valve Air to Open]
    SR --> CV2[Outlet Control Valve Air to Close]

    SR -. 4 to 12 mA opens CV1 .-> CV1
    SR -. 12 to 20 mA opens CV2 .-> CV2

    CV1 --> VESSEL[Process Vessel]
    VESSEL --> CV2
    LT -.-> VESSEL
```

### 4.4 Diagram D — High-Level Switch Interlock Logic (SIS)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LSH[LSH High Level Switch in Vessel] --> AND[AND Logic Gate 1 out of 2 voted]
    LSH2[LSH Redundant High Level Switch] --> AND
    AND --> TRIP[Logic Solver IEC 61511 SIL rated]
    TRIP --> INLET[Close Inlet Valve SDV]
    TRIP --> PUMP[Stop Inlet Pump]
    TRIP --> ALARM[Annunciate HH Alarm in DCS]
```

### 4.5 Diagram E — PID Controller Internal Block (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E[Error Signal e of t] --> P[Proportional Term Kp times e]
    E --> I[Integral Term Ki times Integral of e dt]
    E --> D[Derivative Term Kd times de by dt]
    P --> SUM2[Summing Junction Output]
    I --> SUM2
    D --> SUM2
    SUM2 --> BIAS[Bias m zero added]
    BIAS --> M[Controller Output m of t percent]
    M --> VLV[Pneumatic or Electronic Signal to Valve]
```
