## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
>
> **Q1. Differentiate between a level transmitter and a level switch. Give one petrochemical application of each. (3 Marks)**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> A *level transmitter* is a **continuous** measurement device that outputs a signal **proportional** to the liquid height inside a vessel, typically a **4–20 mA** current superimposed with a **HART** digital protocol. It feeds a controller or a DCS for trending, recording, and closed-loop control. A *level switch*, in contrast, is a **discrete** point-detection device that changes its **electrical contact state** (open ↔ closed) at one fixed height. It does not measure level — it only *senses* that level has crossed a threshold.
> *Petrochemical application of transmitter*: differential-pressure transmitter on a crude distillation column reflux drum.
> *Petrochemical application of switch*: vibrating-fork high-level switch on a hydrocarbon storage tank interlocked to an SDV (Shutdown Valve).
> **(3 marks: 1 for definition difference, 1 for transmitter app, 1 for switch app.)**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
>
> **Q2. Explain why "averaging level control" is preferred for reflux drums in distillation columns. (3 Marks)**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> The reflux drum acts as a **surge buffer** between the overhead vapour flow (demand) and the reflux pump delivery (supply). Tight level control would transfer every transient mismatch instantly to the reflux flow, destabilising column composition and overhead temperature. By tuning the level loop with a **wide proportional band (low K<sub>p</sub>) and no integral action**, the level is allowed to *float* within a band — absorbing short-term disturbances by changing the stored volume. This is called **averaging level control** and it improves overall column stability.
> **(3 marks: 1 for buffer function, 1 for wide PB explanation, 1 for stability benefit.)**

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

#### Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**Part (a)** — 7 Marks
> *Explain with neat sketches the working principle, construction, and salient features of a Differential Pressure (DP) type level transmitter used in petrochemical industries. How is a wet leg used to compensate for high-temperature service?*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Construction (3 marks):**
A DP transmitter consists of a capacitive sensing cell with two chambers separated by a diaphragm. The high-pressure side is connected to the **lower tap** (submerged in liquid) via an **impulse line**. The low-pressure side is connected to the **upper tap** (vapor space) via another impulse line. The diaphragm deflects proportionally to the pressure difference. Capacitor plates on either side of the diaphragm detect this deflection. The capacitance change is converted to a **4–20 mA output** by an electronic circuit.

2. **Working principle (2 marks):**
The hydrostatic pressure $P = \rho g h$ is detected. At zero level, both taps see only vapor pressure → ΔP = 0. At maximum level h<sub>max</sub>, ΔP = ρ g h<sub>max</sub>. The 4 mA corresponds to 0 %, 20 mA to 100 %.

3. **Wet-leg compensation for high-temperature service (2 marks):**
When the process liquid is hot (e.g., above 200 °C), the impulse line must be filled with a **non-volatile, dense seal fluid** (e.g., glycol-water mixture or silicone oil) to prevent the process liquid from flashing into the impulse line. This filled section is called the **wet leg** or **chemical seal**. The wet-leg column height must be subtracted or compensated in the transmitter's zero/span configuration to obtain accurate level.

**Part (b)** — 7 Marks
> *A closed cylindrical storage tank of diameter 1.5 m contains benzene (ρ = 879 kg/m³) at 25 °C. A DP transmitter is calibrated for 0 – 100 % level over a hydrostatic range of 0 – 50 kPa. Calculate:*
> *(i) The maximum measurable level in metres.*
> *(ii) The current output (in mA) when the level is at 3.5 m.*
> *(iii) The new transmitter range (in kPa) if the same tank is used for a heavier hydrocarbon of ρ = 920 kg/m³.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the maximum DP at full level.**
The transmitter range is 0 – 50 kPa, so at 100 % level:
$$\Delta P_{max} = 50\,000 \;\text{Pa}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the hydrostatic formula to find h<sub>max</sub>.**
$$\Delta P_{max} = \rho \, g \, h_{max}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for h<sub>max</sub>.**
$$h_{max} = \frac{\Delta P_{max}}{\rho \, g} = \frac{50\,000}{879 \times 9.81}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate denominator.**
$$879 \times 9.81 = 8623.0 \;\text{N/m}^3$$

**Step 5 — Divide.**
$$h_{max} = \frac{50\,000}{8623.0} = 5.80 \;\text{m}$$

**Step 6 — Compute DP at h = 3.5 m for benzene.**
$$\Delta P_{3.5} = 879 \times 9.81 \times 3.5 = 30\,181 \;\text{Pa} = 30.18 \;\text{kPa}$$

**Step 7 — Compute % level.**
$$\%L = \frac{\Delta P_{3.5}}{\Delta P_{max}} \times 100 = \frac{30.18}{50} \times 100 = 60.36\,\%$$

**Step 8 — Convert % level to current.**
$$I = 4 + \frac{\%L}{100} \times 16 = 4 + 0.6036 \times 16 = 4 + 9.658 = 13.66 \;\text{mA}$$

**Step 9 — New range for ρ = 920 kg/m³.**
$$\Delta P_{max}^{new} = 920 \times 9.81 \times 5.80 = 52\,347 \;\text{Pa} = 52.35 \;\text{kPa}$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) h<sub>max</sub> = **5.80 m**
- (ii) I at 3.5 m = **13.66 mA**
- (iii) New range = **52.35 kPa** *(i.e., transmitter must be re-ranged)*

---

#### Question B — 14 Marks **[Alternative Internal Choice]**

**Part (a)** — 7 Marks
> *Describe the working principle of a guided-wave radar (GWR) level transmitter. Why is GWR preferred over ultrasonic level measurement in harsh petrochemical service?*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Working Principle of GWR (4 marks):**
A GWR (also called TDR — Time Domain Reflectometry) transmitter launches a low-energy electromagnetic pulse (~ 1 GHz bandwidth, 1 V amplitude) down a **metallic probe** (coaxial, rod, or flexible cable) immersed in the vessel. The pulse travels at a fraction of the speed of light inside the probe. When it encounters an **impedance discontinuity** at the liquid surface, part of the energy is reflected back. The round-trip time *t* is measured with picosecond resolution. Since the pulse velocity *v* along the probe is known and constant, the distance *d* to the surface is:
$$d = \frac{v \, t}{2}$$
The electronics convert *d* to a level value and output 4–20 mA + HART.

2. **Why GWR is preferred over ultrasonic in petrochemical service (3 marks):**
- **Ultrasonic waves** are *mechanical* and are strongly attenuated by foam, vapour, dust, and turbulence; they fail in vacuum service.
- **Electromagnetic pulses** in GWR are unaffected by foam, vapor composition, temperature, vacuum, or pressure changes — they "see" the actual liquid surface regardless of the atmosphere above it.
- GWR is immune to false echoes from vessel internals (using *echo curve mapping*).
- GWR provides ±2 mm accuracy versus ±0.5 % of span for ultrasonic.

**Part (b)** — 7 Marks
> *A reflux drum has a level setpoint of 50 % with a proportional-only level controller. The transmitter span is 0 – 100 %. If the controller output at 50 % is 12 mA (50 %) and at 60 % is 8 mA (25 %), determine:*
> *(i) Whether the controller action is direct or reverse.*
> *(ii) The controller gain K<sub>p</sub> in mA/%. *
> *(iii) The bias (output at setpoint).*
> *(iv) The level at which the controller output becomes 20 mA (valve fully open).*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the direction.**
At 50 % level the output is 12 mA; at 60 % level the output drops to 8 mA. Level up → output down → this is **reverse action** (used for outlet valves). *(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Compute K<sub>p</sub>.**
$$K_{p} = \frac{\Delta m}{\Delta e} = \frac{8\,\text{mA} - 12\,\text{mA}}{60\,\% - 50\,\%} = \frac{-4\,\text{mA}}{10\,\%} = -0.4 \;\text{mA/\%}$$

The magnitude **K<sub>p</sub> = 0.4 mA/%**. *(2 marks)*

**Step 3 — Compute bias.**
At setpoint (e = 0), output is 12 mA → **bias m<sub>0</sub> = 12 mA**. *(2 marks)*

**Step 4 — Solve for level when output = 20 mA.**
Using $m = K_{p} (SP - PV) + m_{0}$:
$$20 = -0.4 \times (50 - PV) + 12$$
$$20 - 12 = -0.4 \times (50 - PV)$$
$$8 = -0.4 \times (50 - PV)$$
$$50 - PV = -20$$
$$PV = 70\,\%$$

So when the level rises to **70 %**, the controller output saturates at 20 mA (valve fully closed/opened depending on action). *(2 marks)*

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Mixing up transmitter vs. switch:** Writing that a level switch gives a 4–20 mA output is a *fatal* error. Switches are binary.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in ultrasonic/radar ToF:** The pulse travels *down and back*. Many students forget the division by 2 and write $d = v \cdot t$.
> 3. **Wrong controller action:** Reflux drum level controller is almost always **reverse-acting** (level up → close outlet valve). Boiler drum is *direct-acting* (level down → open feedwater valve).
> 4. **Units mismatch in DP calculations:** Pressure in kPa but density in g/cm³ — always convert to SI.
> 5. **Sign convention in PID:** Writing $e = PV - SP$ instead of $e = SP - PV$ reverses the action sign. State convention explicitly.
> 6. **Ignoring wet-leg / dry-leg in hot service:** Always mention impulse-line compensation when vessel temperature exceeds 100 °C.
> 7. **Neglecting SIL rating in safety loops:** A high-level switch used as part of an interlock must carry an explicit IEC 61511 SIL-1/2/3 rating.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition triplet:** *Transmitter* (continuous, 4–20 mA) | *Switch* (discrete, ON/OFF) | *Controller* (algorithm + output).
- **Hydrostatic backbone equation:** $P = \rho g h$ — used everywhere.
- **Standard calibration:** 4 mA = 0 %, 20 mA = 100 %; linear span.
- **GWR is the modern gold standard** in refineries because it tolerates foam, vapor, and turbulence.
- **Ultrasonic is non-contact** but fails in vacuum, foam, and dust — use only in atmospheric service.
- **Capacitance works on dielectric change** — air ≈ 1, oil ≈ 2.2, water ≈ 80.
- **Vibrating fork switches** are the most common *discrete* choice for high/low alarms on clean liquids and slurries.
- **Averaging level control** uses a wide proportional band + no integral — used for reflux drums and surge vessels.
- **Split-range control** uses two valves driven from one controller output (4–12 mA / 12–20 mA).
- **Cascade level → flow** control for fast load changes on viscous or slurry service.
- **High-level switch + SDV** forms the typical IEC 61511 SIL-rated SIF.
- **Direct vs Reverse action:** Reverse action is used when the valve must close on rising level (outlet valve). Direct action is used when the valve must open on rising level (inlet valve to a tank you want to *fill*).
- **Wet-leg / chemical seal:** Mandatory when process temperature > 100 °C to prevent flashing in impulse lines.
- **Time-of-flight radar / ultrasonic** always carries a **blanking distance** (typically 0.25–0.5 m) below the transmitter.
- **KTU exam hot spots:** Numerical problems on DP level, ultrasonic ToF, and PID controller action / gain / bias.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications of Level Sensors (Storage Tanks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET861 - INSTRUMENTATION IN PETROCHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Applications of Level Sensors (Storage Tanks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
A **Level Sensor** is a process instrumentation device used to detect, measure, and continuously monitor the height, depth, or volume of a fluid (liquid or liquefied gas) contained within an enclosed or open vessel. In petrochemical storage tanks, level sensors form the primary sensing element in the tank gauging system, providing real-time data to Distributed Control Systems (DCS), Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS), and custody transfer systems.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, storage tanks in petrochemical plants span a wide process spectrum — from atmospheric **cone-roof tanks** storing crude oil to pressurized **spherical / Horton sphere vessels** holding LPG and LNG, and cryogenic **double-wall tanks** for liquefied gases. The level sensor must therefore satisfy stringent metrics:

- **Accuracy:** ±0.1 % of full scale (for custody transfer, e.g., API MPMS Ch. 3.1A).
- **Repeatability:** ±0.05 % of full scale.
- **Operating temperature range:** **-196 °C** (cryogenic LNG) to **+450 °C** (hot bitumen).
- **Operating pressure range:** Full vacuum to **40 bar(g)** and beyond.
- **Hazardous area classification:** ATEX/IECEx Zone 0, Zone 1, Zone 2.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU 2024 Module-3 evaluation, students are expected to (a) identify the correct level-sensing technology for a given storage application, (b) justify the choice based on fluid properties (dielectric constant, density, viscosity, foam, vapors, turbulence), and (c) explain installation best practices (stilling wells, bridles, calibration procedures).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy
Think of a level sensor as the **"smart water-float inside a giant petrol tank"** — exactly like the simple cork-float inside a domestic overhead water tank that rises and falls with the water level. The difference is that in a petrochemical plant:

- The float cannot touch the fluid (because the fluid may be corrosive, hot, cryogenic, or explosive).
- The reading must be digitized, transmitted, and integrated with safety interlocks.
- The measurement must be **legally traceable** (custody transfer) when oil is bought and sold across refinery gates.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Context:** In Indian refineries such as BPCL Kochi or IOCL Panipat, a single storage tank farm may hold over **100,000 m³** of crude. A 1 mm level error in a 20 m tank can mean **~50 m³ of crude ≈ ₹25–30 lakhs** discrepancy per tank. Hence the level sensor is not a mere accessory — it is a **fiscal instrument**.

### 1.3 Classification of Storage Tanks (Petrochemical Industry)
Before selecting a level sensor, one must know the tank. KTU 2024 commonly tests this taxonomy:

| Tank Type | Typical Service | Design Code | Pressure Class |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cone-Roof (CRT) | Crude oil, diesel, naphtha | API 650 | Atmospheric |
| External Floating-Roof (EFR) | Motor spirit, kerosene | API 650 | Atmospheric |
| Internal Floating-Roof (IFR) | Aviation fuel | API 650 | Atmospheric |
| Horton Sphere | LPG, butane, propane | ASME Sec. VIII Div. 1 | 6–18 bar(g) |
| Bullet / Cylindrical | LPG, propylene | ASME Sec. VIII | 15–25 bar(g) |
| Cryogenic Double-Wall | LNG, LOX, LIN | API 620 / EN 14620 | 0.3–4 bar(g) |
| Spherical | Ammonia, ethylene | ASME Sec. VIII | 10–30 bar(g) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Level Measurement Categories — Operational Logic
Level measurement is broadly classified into four physical principles. Each principle exploits a different physical property of the fluid.

#### 2.1.1 Direct / Mechanical Methods
- **Sight Glass (Gauge Glass):** A transparent column fitted externally on the tank, connected via top and bottom isolation valves. The fluid level inside the column equals the level inside the tank (communicating-vessel principle).
- **Float & Tape / Float & Pulley:** A buoyant float rides on the liquid surface. A counterweight, connected via a flexible tape/cable over a pulley, translates the float's vertical movement into a rotary position read by an encoder.
- **Dip Tape (Manual):** A calibrated brass tape with a conductive or magnetic bob. Used for verification and periodic calibration.

#### 2.1.2 Hydrostatic Pressure Methods
- The pressure at the bottom of a fluid column is directly proportional to the height of the column and the density of the fluid.
- **Bottom-mounted pressure transmitter** (d/p cell) measures hydrostatic head.
- **Differential Pressure (DP) Cell** measures the difference between bottom hydrostatic pressure and top vapor pressure — this compensates for vapor-space pressure fluctuations and is the **workhorse of pressurized vessels** (Horton spheres, bullet tanks).

#### 2.1.3 Electrical Methods
- **Capacitive Level Sensor:** The fluid acts as a dielectric. As the level rises, the capacitance between two electrodes (rod or coaxial) changes.
- **Conductivity / Resistance Level Sensor:** A low-voltage AC is applied between two electrodes; a conductive fluid (water, acids) closes the circuit. Used for **point-level (high/low) alarms** only.
- **Radar (FMCW & Pulsed):** Microwaves in the 6 GHz, 26 GHz, or 80 GHz band are transmitted toward the liquid surface. The time-of-flight is measured to compute distance.
- **Guided Wave Radar (GWR):** A low-energy pulse travels along a probe (coaxial, twin-rod, or single-rod) and reflects off the liquid surface. Immunity to foam, vapors, and turbulence is high.

#### 2.1.4 Non-Contact / Acoustic Methods
- **Ultrasonic Level Sensor:** Air-coupled ultrasound (typically 30–200 kHz) is emitted from a transducer mounted at the top of the tank. The time of flight to the surface and back is measured.
- **Laser Triangulation:** Used for small tanks and for verification only; sensitive to dust, foam, and vapors.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **syllabus-relevant for KTU 2024 Module-3**. Boundary conditions and standard units are explicitly noted.

| # | Quantity / Principle | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hydrostatic Pressure | $P = \rho \, g \, h$ | $\rho$ = fluid density **[kg/m³]**, $g$ = **9.81 m/s²**, $h$ = level [m], $P$ = pressure [Pa] |
| 2 | Differential Pressure (DP) for Level | $\Delta P = \rho \, g \, (h_2 - h_1)$ | $h_1, h_2$ = lower & upper tap heights [m] |
| 3 | Hydrostatic Level in Pressurized Vessel | $h = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho \, g} + h_{offset}$ | $h_{offset}$ = correction for tap elevation [m] |
| 4 | Capacitance of Coaxial Probe | $C = \dfrac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L}{\ln(b/a)}$ | $\varepsilon_0$ = **8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m**, $\varepsilon_r$ = relative permittivity, $L$ = immersed length [m] |
| 5 | Two-Phase Capacitance Model | $C = C_0 + (\varepsilon_r - 1) \cdot K \cdot h$ | $C_0$ = empty capacitance, $K$ = geometry constant |
| 6 | Ultrasonic Time of Flight | $h = \dfrac{c \, t}{2}$ | $c$ = speed of sound in air **[m/s]**, $t$ = echo time [s] |
| 7 | Radar Time of Flight | $h = \dfrac{c \, t}{2}$ | $c$ = speed of light **[3 × 10⁸ m/s]**, $t$ = round-trip time [s] |
| 8 | FMCW Radar Beat Frequency | $f_b = \dfrac{2 \cdot h \cdot \Delta f}{T \cdot c}$ | $\Delta f$ = sweep bandwidth [Hz], $T$ = sweep period [s] |
| 9 | Buoyancy on Float (Archimedes) | $F_b = \rho \, g \, V_{sub}$ | $V_{sub}$ = submerged volume of float [m³] |
| 10 | Sounding Volume from Level | $V = A \cdot h$ (cylindrical tank) | $A$ = cross-section area [m²] |
| 11 | Strapping Table (Non-linear Tank) | $V = \sum_{i} \left( \dfrac{A_i + A_{i+1}}{2} \right) \cdot \Delta h_i$ | Simpson / trapezoidal integration |
| 12 | Vapor Space Compensation | $P_{bottom} = P_{top} + \rho \, g \, h$ | Required for pressurized tank level |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL PITFALL:** When using radar or ultrasonic sensors, never confuse the **measured distance** with the **level**. The relationship is:
> $$\text{Level } (h) = H_{tank} - d_{measured}$$
> where $H_{tank}$ is the total tank height from sensor flange to the bottom, and $d_{measured}$ is the empty distance the wave traveled.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility
1. **Custody Transfer:** Approved radar / servo gauges (e.g., Endress+Hauser Proservo, Emerson Rosemount 5900S) provide API 3.1B / OIML R85 accuracy. Used in tanker loading terminals, refinery dispatch, and pipeline interconnections.
2. **Overfill Prevention (API 2350):** Mandatory redundant level switches (high-high, high, and continuous level) on Category 1, 2, and 3 tanks.
3. **Interface Detection (Oil-Water):** In desalters and produced-water separators, capacitance probes detect the water–oil interface and the emulsion band.
4. **Inventory Management (SCADA/DCS):** Real-time volume, mass (using density at multiple levels), and trend logs.
5. **Safety Instrumented Function (SIF):** Level transmitters form part of SIL-1 / SIL-2 loops for emergency shutdown, foam flooding, and inert gas blanketing control.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hydrostatic Pressure to Level Conversion
**Problem Context:** A pressurized LPG bullet tank (horizontal cylindrical, 12 m diameter) is fitted with a **differential pressure (DP) cell** with its high-pressure (HP) tap at the bottom and low-pressure (LP) tap at the top vapor space. Compute the level when the DP cell reads **8.40 kPa** and LPG density is **520 kg/m³**.

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation**

The fundamental hydrostatic equation for an incompressible fluid in a pressurized tank:

$$P_{bottom} = P_{vapor} + \rho \, g \, h$$

**Step 2 — Subtract vapor pressure to obtain the differential pressure**

The DP cell measures only the difference between the bottom and the top tap, removing vapor pressure as a common-mode signal:

$$\Delta P = P_{bottom} - P_{vapor} = \rho \, g \, h$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to solve for $h$ (the level)**

$$h = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho \, g}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values**

Given:
- $\Delta P = 8.40 \text{ kPa} = 8400 \text{ Pa}$
- $\rho = 520 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

$$h = \dfrac{8400}{520 \times 9.81}$$

**Step 5 — Perform the arithmetic**

$$h = \dfrac{8400}{5101.2}$$

$$h = 1.6467 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — Convert the level to a percentage of tank diameter for monitoring**

$$\% \text{ Fill} = \dfrac{h}{D} \times 100 = \dfrac{1.6467}{12} \times 100 \approx 13.72 \,\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In a **horizontal cylindrical** tank, the volume-to-level relationship is non-linear because the cross-sectional area varies with height. The volumetric mapping is given by:
> $$V(h) = L \left[ R^2 \cos^{-1}\left(\dfrac{R-h}{R}\right) - (R-h)\sqrt{2Rh - h^2} \right]$$
> where $L$ = tank length and $R$ = radius. This is implemented in DCS via **strapping tables**.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Ultrasonic Time-of-Flight Distance Calculation
**Problem Context:** An ultrasonic level sensor is mounted at the top of a 10 m cone-roof crude oil tank. The sensor emits a 50 kHz pulse, and the echo returns in **58.2 ms**. The ambient temperature above the oil is **35 °C**. Compute the distance from the sensor to the surface, and therefore the level.

**Step 1 — Determine the speed of sound in air at 35 °C**

The speed of sound in air varies with temperature as:

$$c = c_0 \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{T_0}}$$

where $c_0 = 331.3$ m/s at $T_0 = 273.15$ K, and $T = 35 + 273.15 = 308.15$ K.

**Step 2 — Evaluate $c$**

$$c = 331.3 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{308.15}{273.15}}$$

$$c = 331.3 \times \sqrt{1.1282}$$

$$c = 331.3 \times 1.0622 = 351.91 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the time-of-flight distance formula**

The ultrasonic pulse travels **to the surface and back**, so the distance is:

$$d = \dfrac{c \, t}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the values**

$$d = \dfrac{351.91 \times 58.2 \times 10^{-3}}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Perform the arithmetic**

$$d = \dfrac{20.481}{2} = 10.2405 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — Convert the measured distance to a level reading**

$$h = H_{tank} - d$$

Assuming the sensor flange is at the top of the 10 m tank and a small dead-zone offset of **0.30 m** is subtracted:

$$h = 10.00 - 10.2405 + 0.30 = 0.0595 \text{ m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget that the **measured distance $d$ already accounts for the round-trip**. Using $d = c \cdot t$ (without dividing by 2) gives double the actual distance — an immediate 7-mark loss in KTU valuation.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Capacitance Level Sensor Output for Two-Phase Liquid
**Problem Context:** A capacitance probe is used to measure the interface between oil and water in a separator vessel. The probe has a capacitance of **180 pF** when empty, **540 pF** when fully immersed in water, and **270 pF** when fully immersed in oil. Compute the percentage of water and oil levels for a measured capacitance of **360 pF**.

**Step 1 — Identify the linear mixing model**

For a coaxial capacitance probe, when two immiscible liquids (oil on top, water at the bottom) are present, the effective capacitance is a linear combination:

$$C = C_{air} + (\varepsilon_{oil} - 1) \cdot K \cdot h_{oil} + (\varepsilon_{water} - 1) \cdot K \cdot h_{water}$$

**Step 2 — Express in terms of fractions**

Let $x$ = fraction of water in the probe (by level). Then $h_{water} = x \cdot H$, and $h_{oil} = (1-x) H$. The simplified form becomes:

$$C = C_{air} + x \cdot (C_{water} - C_{air}) + (1 - x) \cdot (C_{oil} - C_{air})$$

**Step 3 — Substitute known values**

- $C_{air} = 180$ pF
- $C_{oil} = 270$ pF
- $C_{water} = 540$ pF
- $C = 360$ pF (measured)

$$360 = 180 + x(540 - 180) + (1 - x)(270 - 180)$$

**Step 4 — Expand and simplify**

$$360 = 180 + 360x + 90(1 - x)$$

$$360 = 180 + 360x + 90 - 90x$$

$$360 = 270 + 270x$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $x$**

$$270x = 90 \implies x = \dfrac{90}{270} = 0.3333$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the result**

- Water fills **33.33 %** of the probe length.
- Oil fills **66.67 %** of the probe length.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Technology Selection
| Application / Fluid | Best Level Sensor | Why? | Why Not Others? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Crude oil in cone-roof tank (large) | **Servo Level Gauge** / **Radar (26 GHz)** | Custody-grade accuracy, large measurement range | Float gauge requires maintenance; DP cell drifts |
| LNG cryogenic (-162 °C) | **Guided Wave Radar (cryogenic version)** | Works in dense vapor, low dielectric, low temperature | Ultrasonic: vapor absorbs sound; float: freezes |
| LPG in Horton sphere (pressurized) | **Differential Pressure (DP) cell** with diaphragm seals | Compensates for vapor pressure; safe in hazardous zone | Sight glass: explosion risk; radar: cost overkill |
| Interface (oil-water) in desalter | **Capacitance / Guided Wave Radar** | Detects dielectric change at interface | Float: tangles; conductivity: works only for water |
| Molten sulfur (140 °C) | **Radar with air-purge cooling** | Non-contact, no probe fouling | DP cell: high temperature damages seals |
| Foam-prone / turbulent chemical tank | **Guided Wave Radar (GWR)** | Probe-guided wave ignores foam and vapors | Ultrasonic: foam scatters; radar: still affected by foam |
| Boiling hydrocarbon (e.g., naphtha) | **Differential Pressure** | Reliable despite vapor | Float: damaged by turbulence; radar: vapor affects signal |
| Sludge / slurries (e.g., FCC slurry) | **Non-contact radar with purging** | No probe contact with abrasive media | Capacitance: probe fouls; float: jams |
| Small day tank (few m³) | **Float / Sight glass** | Cheap, simple, local indication | Radar: cost-prohibitive; DP: over-spec |
| Reactor with agitator and baffles | **Guided Wave Radar in stilling well** | Stilling well isolates from turbulence | Free-space radar sees agitator blades |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Tank Level Measurement Loop
The diagram below maps the complete signal flow from the physical tank level to the control room display, including all the supporting systems expected in a KTU Module-3 answer.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Tank Physical Level] --> B[Primary Sensor - Probe / Antenna / DP Cell]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioning - Linearization, Temperature Compensation]
    C --> D[Transmitter - 4-20 mA HART / Foundation Fieldbus]
    D --> E[Safety Barrier - Intrinsically Safe Barrier IS / Explosion Proof]
    E --> F1[DCS - Distributed Control System]
    E --> F2[SIS - Safety Instrumented System]
    E --> F3[Custody Transfer Computer - Flow Computer / Totalizer]
    F1 --> G1[Operator HMI - Mimic Display, Trends]
    F2 --> G2[Emergency Shutdown Logic - High High Level Trip]
    F3 --> G3[Bill of Lading - Legal Custody Report]
    B --> H[Local Indicator - Field Display / Sight Glass]
    F1 --> I[Alarm Management - High, Low, High-High, Low-Low]
    F2 --> J[Final Control Element - ESDV Shut-off Valve]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Comparative Working Principle of Major Level Sensors
The following block diagram compares the physical signal-transduction chain for the four most-examined technologies in KTU 2024 Module 3.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A1[Hydrostatic DP System]
        A1a[Liquid Column] --> A1b[DP Cell Diaphragm]
        A1b --> A1c[Capacitance Bridge to mV]
        A1c --> A1d[ADC to 4-20 mA]
    end
    subgraph A2[Ultrasonic System]
        A2a[Piezoelectric Burst 50 kHz] --> A2b[Air Path Transit]

    end
    subgraph A3[Free Space Radar]
        A3a[FMCW Sweep 26 GHz] --> A3b[Surface Reflection]

        A3c --> A3d[FFT to Distance]
    end
    subgraph A4[Guided Wave Radar]
        A4a[Pulser] --> A4b[Coaxial Probe]

    end
    A1d --> Z[Level Output to DCS]
    A2d --> Z
    A3d --> Z
    A4d --> Z
```

### 4.3 State Diagram — Installation & Calibration Lifecycle of a Level Sensor
```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SiteSurvey
    SiteSurvey --> Selection: Technology selected from matrix
    Selection --> MechanicalInstall: Nozzle, flange, stilling well
    MechanicalInstall --> ElectricalLoopCheck: Wiring, IS barrier
    ElectricalLoopCheck --> Calibration1: Zero and span at empty and full
    Calibration1 --> Commissioning: Loop test with DCS
    Commissioning --> Operation: Normal service
    Operation --> PeriodicVerification: API tank calibration
    PeriodicVerification --> Operation: Pass
    PeriodicVerification --> Maintenance: Fail
    Maintenance --> Calibration1: Recalibrate
    Operation --> [*]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State the working principle of a Differential Pressure (DP) type level sensor used in pressurized storage tanks. Mention why vapor pressure compensation is required.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A DP type level sensor operates on the **hydrostatic pressure principle**: $P = \rho g h$. It has two pressure taps — a high-pressure (HP) tap at the bottom of the tank and a low-pressure (LP) tap at the top vapor space. The DP cell measures the difference $\Delta P = \rho g h$, which directly corresponds to the level. **Vapor pressure compensation is required** because, in a pressurized tank, the bottom pressure $P_{bottom}$ includes the vapor space pressure $P_{vapor}$ plus the hydrostatic head. The DP cell automatically subtracts the common-mode $P_{vapor}$, eliminating error caused by pressure fluctuations and temperature-induced vapor pressure changes inside the tank. **(3 marks)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why is a Guided Wave Radar (GWR) preferred over a free-space (non-contact) radar in foaming hydrocarbon service?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A GWR uses a low-energy electromagnetic pulse that travels along a **metal probe (coaxial, twin-rod, or single-rod)** and reflects off the actual liquid surface below the foam. Because the wave is confined within the probe's electromagnetic field, it is **immune to foam, vapors, and turbulence**. In contrast, free-space radar beams must travel through the foam layer; the foam partially absorbs and scatters the microwave energy, leading to **signal loss, false echoes, and reduced accuracy**. Hence GWR is preferred in foaming hydrocarbon service. **(3 marks)**

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** A Horton sphere of diameter **20 m** is used to store LPG at **12 bar(g)** pressure. The tank is fitted with a **Differential Pressure (DP) cell** with a measuring range of **0–40 kPa**. The LPG density is **510 kg/m³**. Calculate the **level in metres** and **% fill** when the DP cell outputs **21.2 kPa**. Discuss the installation best practices for a DP cell in such a pressurized LPG storage tank. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Radar, Ultrasonic, Capacitance, and Float-based** level sensors with respect to: (i) contact vs non-contact, (ii) suitability for high-pressure tanks, (iii) accuracy, (iv) effect of foam and vapors, and (v) cost. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

Given: $\Delta P = 21.2 \text{ kPa} = 21200 \text{ Pa}$, $\rho = 510 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $D = 20 \text{ m}$.

**Step 1 — Apply the hydrostatic equation**

$$h = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho g}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute**

$$h = \dfrac{21200}{510 \times 9.81} = \dfrac{21200}{5003.1}$$

**Step 3 — Result**

$$h = 4.237 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute % fill**

$$\% \text{ Fill} = \dfrac{h}{D} \times 100 = \dfrac{4.237}{20} \times 100 = 21.18 \,\%$$

**Installation Best Practices (7 marks include 3 from calculation + 4 from discussion):**
- **Diaphragm seals** filled with inert silicone oil are used at both HP and LP taps to protect the DP cell from corrosive, sticky, or hot LPG.
- **Impulse lines** must be sloped downward toward the process so that no vapor pockets trap.
- **Purge connections** (nitrogen) at the LP tap prevent condensate accumulation.
- **Equalizing valves** (3-valve manifold) allow zero calibration of the DP cell in service.
- The DP cell must be **mounted below the HP tap** to avoid measurement errors due to liquid head in impulse lines.
- Use of **HART or Foundation Fieldbus** enables remote calibration from the DCS.
- **Flameproof / intrinsically safe** certification for Zone 1 area is mandatory per OISD-150.

**Model Solution for (b): Comparison Table**

| Parameter | Radar (Free-space) | Ultrasonic | Capacitance | Float-based |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Contact type | Non-contact | Non-contact | Contact | Contact |
| High-pressure suitability | Excellent | Poor (vapor dampens sound) | Good | Fair (mechanical wear) |
| Accuracy | ±1–3 mm | ±0.25 % FS | ±0.5 % FS | ±1 % FS |
| Foam / vapor effect | Foam attenuates; vapor slight effect | Vapor absorbs sound, foam scatters | Foam dielectric effect disturbs reading | Foam buoyancy may mislead float |
| Cost (relative) | High (5–10x) | Medium (2–3x) | Low–Medium (1–2x) | Low (1x) |

---

#### **Question B (14 marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Functional Principle of a Servo-Type Level Gauge** used in custody transfer applications. State its typical accuracy and the API standard it complies with. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An ultrasonic level sensor is mounted on top of a 15 m diesel storage tank. The echo return time is **87.6 ms** at an ambient temperature of **42 °C**. Compute: (i) the speed of sound at this temperature, (ii) the measured distance from the sensor to the diesel surface, and (iii) the level in metres, accounting for a 0.35 m dead-zone. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Working Principle:** A small **servo-driven** weight is suspended inside a measuring wire from the top of the tank. The wire is wound on a precision drum connected to a torque motor and a shaft encoder. The motor **lowers the weight** slowly. When the weight touches the liquid surface, the buoyant force on the disc is detected as a sudden **drop in the wire tension** (servo current). At that instant, the controller **reverses the motor**, raises the weight slightly, and hunts to find the exact surface position. The encoder reading is converted to a level reading, and the data is sent digitally (e.g., Enraf BPM protocol) to the DCS / flow computer.

**Key Features:**
- Reference: small weight with **known density and volume**, used for self-calibration.
- Temperature compensation via **multi-spot RTD / averaging Pt100** along the tank height (important for heated tanks).
- Density profiling using optional HIMS (Hydrometer Interface Measurement System) gives **mass measurement**, not just volume.

**Accuracy:** ±0.7 mm (e.g., Endress+Hauser Proservo NMS80 / NRF590).
**Standard compliance:** **API MPMS Chapter 3.1B** (Standard Practice for Level Measurement of Liquid Hydrocarbons in Storage Tanks by Automatic Tank Gauging) and **OIML R85**.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Speed of sound at 42 °C**

$$c = 331.3 \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{T_0}} = 331.3 \sqrt{\dfrac{315.15}{273.15}}$$

$$c = 331.3 \times \sqrt{1.1538} = 331.3 \times 1.0741 = 355.85 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Measured distance (round-trip division by 2)**

$$d = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2} = \dfrac{355.85 \times 87.6 \times 10^{-3}}{2}$$

$$d = \dfrac{31.176}{2} = 15.588 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Level computation**

$$h = H_{tank} - d + h_{deadzone} = 15 - 15.588 + 0.35 = -0.238 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation**

The negative value indicates that the **measured distance exceeds the tank height**, which is physically impossible. This suggests:
- The echo is not from the **diesel surface**, but from a **false echo** (e.g., tank bottom, internal heating coil, or floating roof).
- The dead-zone correction should be re-verified.
- Sensor should be inspected for **condensation on the diaphragm** or **misalignment**.

**Corrective action:** Adjust the time-window gating in the transmitter to ignore the spurious echo, or relocate the sensor.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to divide $t$ by 2** in ultrasonic / radar calculations (loses 2 marks per sub-part).
> 2. **Conflating distance with level** — many students write $h = d$ instead of $h = H - d$.
> 3. **Ignoring temperature compensation** for ultrasonic velocity (gives 5–10 % error in the answer).
> 4. **Using density of water** instead of the actual hydrocarbon in hydrostatic problems.
> 5. **Forgetting units and pressure units** — the DP cell reads in kPa, but the formula expects Pa; convert first.
> 6. **Drawing a float-based diagram** when the question specifies "radar" — the marks are awarded only for the asked technology.
> 7. **Omitting the API 2350 / API 3.1B standard** in custody-transfer answers — this is a typical 2-mark loss.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Level measurement is a fiscal, safety, and operational function** in petrochemical storage tanks — not a simple indicator.
- The **hydrostatic principle** ($P = \rho g h$) is the foundation of DP and bubbler-type level sensors.
- **Radar (FMCW and pulsed)** is the modern preferred technology for large crude and refined product tanks (non-contact, high accuracy).
- **Guided Wave Radar (GWR)** is best for foaming, turbulent, and interface applications.
- **DP cell** remains the dominant solution for **pressurized storage** (LPG, butane, ammonia) because it intrinsically compensates for vapor pressure.
- **Ultrasonic** is non-contact but vulnerable to vapor, dust, and temperature; suitable only for atmospheric, clean tanks.
- **Capacitance** is ideal for interface detection (oil-water, acid-base) in small to medium vessels.
- **Servo gauges** are the gold standard for **custody transfer** and comply with API MPMS 3.1B and OIML R85.
- **Sight glass and float** are local-indication methods; they are not preferred for modern DCS-integrated plants.
- Always convert **DP cell output from kPa to Pa** before substituting in the hydrostatic formula.
- Always **divide echo time by 2** in ultrasonic and radar distance equations.
- Always apply **temperature compensation** for ultrasonic velocity.
- For pressurized vessels, use the **DP cell** method — free-space radar may be cost-prohibitive.
- For LNG / cryogenic service, choose **cryogenic-rated GWR**; ultrasonic is unusable.
- **API 2350** governs overfill protection; **API 3.1B** governs custody transfer.
- Calibration is done at **empty and full** (zero and span) using either a calibrated reference or the tank strapping table.
- **Stilling wells** are recommended in turbulent tanks to stabilize the measurement surface.
- **HART, Foundation Fieldbus, and WirelessHART** are the standard digital protocols for transmitting level data to the DCS.
- The **strapping table** (volume vs level) must be implemented in the DCS for non-linear tanks (horizontal cylindrical, spherical).
- **Mass measurement** requires a level sensor **plus a density profile** (multi-spot densitometer or HIMS).
- **Redundancy:** Overfill prevention requires a **continuous level sensor (primary)** and at least **two independent point-level switches (high and high-high)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module-3 Closing Note:** KTU 2024 questions on this topic frequently combine **numerical problem + technology selection + installation advice**. A 14-mark answer that does not address all three typically caps at 10–11 marks. Always close your answer with a **selection-justification table** and a **standard compliance statement** (API 2350 / API 3.1B / OIML R85) to secure full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reactors and Separators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET861 - INSTRUMENTATION IN PETROCHEMICAL INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Reactors and Separators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Reactors in Petrochemical Industries

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A **Chemical Reactor** is a process vessel designed to contain and control chemical reactions, wherein reactants are converted into products under precisely regulated conditions of **temperature, pressure, residence time, and concentration**. In petrochemical industries, reactors form the *heart* of the process where feedstocks (naphtha, natural gas, ethylene, propylene) undergo transformation into valuable intermediates and finished products.

### 1.1.1 Types of Reactors (KTU Classification)

| Reactor Type | Description | Petrochemical Application |
|--------------|-------------|---------------------------|
| **Batch Reactor (BR)** | Charge → React → Discharge (discrete) | Specialty polymers, pharma-grade chemicals |
| **CSTR (Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor)** | Well-mixed, constant outflow | Polymerization, alkylation, emulsion processes |
| **PFR / Plug Flow Reactor** | Tubular, no back-mixing | Cracking furnaces, tubular reformers |
| **Fixed Bed Reactor** | Catalyst pellets stationary in tubes | Catalytic reforming, hydrodesulfurization (HDS) |
| **Fluidized Bed Reactor** | Catalyst suspended by gas/fluid | Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC), ethylene production |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Focus:** The syllabus emphasizes *instrumentation and control* rather than reaction kinetics alone. For every reactor, students must know the **critical control loops, sensor locations, and safety interlocks**.

### 1.1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Pressure Cooker:**
> Think of a petrochemical reactor like a *highly sophisticated pressure cooker*. In a pressure cooker, you seal the lid (pressure control), set the gas burner (temperature control), and cook food for a fixed duration (residence time). If the flame goes too high, the safety valve releases steam (Pressure Safety Valve / PSV). Similarly, an industrial reactor regulates temperature (exothermic/endothermic heat removal), maintains pressure (via control valves & PSVs), and ensures reactants stay long enough (residence time) for the desired reaction. The "stirrer" in a CSTR is the mechanical agitator; in an FCC unit, it's the fluidizing gas itself!

## 1.2 Separators in Petrochemical Industries

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A **Separator** (also called a *vessel*, *scrubber*, or *knockout drum*) is a static pressure vessel that uses **gravity, momentum, centrifugal force, and/or coalescing mechanisms** to separate a mixed fluid stream into its constituent phases (gas–liquid, liquid–liquid, or three-phase gas–oil–water). They are critical for downstream protection of pumps, compressors, and distillation columns.

### 1.2.1 Types of Separators

| Separator Type | Geometry | Typical Service |
|----------------|----------|-----------------|
| **Two-Phase Horizontal** | Cylindrical horizontal | Gas–liquid from wellhead, compressor scrubbers |
| **Two-Phase Vertical** | Cylindrical vertical | Slug-catching, low gas–liquid ratio streams |
| **Three-Phase Horizontal** | Horizontal with boot | Crude oil dehydrator, free-water knockouts |
| **Spherical Separator** | Spherical shell | High-pressure gas service, offshore platforms |
| **Filter / Coalescer Separator** | With internals | Final polishing of gas streams |

### 1.2.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Salad Dressing Bottle:**
> Shake a bottle of salad dressing (oil + vinegar + spices). When you set it on the table, after a few minutes, the oil floats on top and the vinegar settles at the bottom. The "boundary" between them is called the **interface level** — this is exactly what a three-phase separator does with crude oil (oil–water–gas), and a *level transmitter* watches that boundary. A *demister pad* inside the separator is like a fine mesh that catches tiny droplets — similar to how a kitchen sieve holds back tea leaves while letting water through.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Reactor Instrumentation — The Control Hierarchy

### 2.1.1 Why Instrumentation is Critical in Reactors

Petrochemical reactions are often:
- **Exothermic** → Runaway risk (e.g., polymerization, hydrogenation)
- **High-pressure** → Mechanical hazard (e.g., hydrocracking at 150 bar)
- **Catalytic** → Hot spots damage catalyst (e.g., reformer)
- **Side-reaction prone** → Selectivity loss (e.g., ethylene to methane)

Hence, **sensor placement** is not arbitrary — it follows the principles of:
1. Measure **representative** process variable
2. Place **redundant** transmitters for safety loops (SIL-rated)
3. Use **cascade control** for tight regulation

### 2.1.2 Key Reactor Control Loops

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Four Pillars of Reactor Control (KTU High-Yield):**
> 1. **Temperature Control** — via coolant flow or steam dump (most critical for exothermic)
> 2. **Pressure Control** — via vent valve or feed throttling
> 3. **Level / Inventory Control** — via product withdrawal valve
> 4. **Composition Control** — via feed ratio or recycle stream

### 2.1.3 Cascade Control for Reactor Temperature (KTU Favorite Topic)

**Why Cascade?**
A single-loop temperature controller on a jacket has poor response because the jacket water temperature disturbances propagate slowly. **Cascade control** uses a *primary* (master) controller on reactor temperature, whose output *resets the setpoint* of a *secondary* (slave) controller on jacket-outlet temperature.

$$\text{Output}_{TT} \rightarrow SP_{TIC_{jacket}} \rightarrow FCV_{coolant}$$

**Benefits:**
- Faster disturbance rejection
- Handles nonlinear jacket dynamics
- Foundation for **selective / override** control in runaway scenarios

## 2.2 Separator Instrumentation — Working Principles

### 2.2.1 Level Measurement Methods (KTU 2024)

| Method | Sensor Type | Application | Pros / Cons |
|--------|-------------|-------------|-------------|
| **Differential Pressure (DP)** | DP cell with impulse lines | Most separators | Robust, cheap — needs density compensation |
| **Displacer** | Buoyancy-based | Interface + total level | Mechanical, requires calibration |
| **Capacitance / RF** | Probe-based | Interface in harsh service | No moving parts, affected by coating |
| **Guided Wave Radar (GWR)** | TDR probe | All-weather separators | High accuracy, expensive |
| **Servo / Float** | Buoy + torque tube | Legacy systems | Accurate, mechanical wear |
| **Magnetostrictive** | Float + waveguide | Cryogenic separators | High precision |

### 2.2.2 Split-Range Level Control (KTU Frequent)

When **two valves** must operate from a single controller output (e.g., one for top-up, one for bottom-out):

$$0\% \leq LC_{output} \leq 50\% \rightarrow \text{Open Drain Valve (A)}$$
$$50\% \leq LC_{output} \leq 100\% \rightarrow \text{Open Fill Valve (B)}$$

This is achieved using a **valve positioner with split-range characterization** or two solenoids with bias springs.

### 2.2.3 Interface Level — The Three-Phase Problem

For a three-phase separator (oil/gas/water):
- **Top level** (gas–oil interface) measured by **displacer or GWR**
- **Bottom level** (oil–water interface) measured by **DP cell with dual-fill fluid** or **specialty capacitance probe**
- The difference is the **water-boot level**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse *total liquid level* with *interface level*. They are *different*! Total level = oil + water; interface level = oil/water boundary. Wrong choice of sensor leads to **water carry-over** or **oil loss to water treatment**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **mathematically verifiable** and aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme exam patterns.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\tau = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | **Residence Time** = Volume / Volumetric Flow | s or hr |
| 2 | $F = V\rho g$ | Hydrostatic force on level sensor | N |
| 3 | $h = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho g}$ | Level from DP measurement | m |
| 4 | $\Delta P = (\rho_{liq} - \rho_{vap}) \cdot g \cdot h$ | DP across two-phase separator | Pa |
| 5 | $P_{design} = P_{MAWP}$ + corrosion + surge | Vessel design pressure (per ASME) | bar |
| 6 | $t_{retention} = \dfrac{A_{liq} \cdot h_{liq}}{Q_{liq}}$ | Liquid retention time in separator | s |
| 7 | $v_{gas,allow} = K \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_{liq} - \rho_{gas}}{\rho_{gas}}}$ | **Souders-Brown** gas velocity (max superficial) | m/s |
| 8 | $D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4Q_{gas}}{\pi v_{gas,allow}}}$ | Minimum separator diameter | m |
| 9 | $L_{eff} = \dfrac{t_{retention} \cdot Q_{gas}}{A_{cross}}$ | Effective separator length | m |
| 10 | $K_p = \dfrac{1}{A}\cdot e^{-E/RT}$ | Arrhenius rate constant (reaction context) | 1/s |
| 11 | $r_{A} = -k \cdot C_{A}^{n}$ | Reaction rate for reactant A | kmol/(m³·s) |
| 12 | $\dot{Q}_{removed} = -\Delta H_{rxn} \cdot r_{A} \cdot V$ | Heat removal duty for exothermic reaction | kW |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constants to Remember:**
> - $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$ (gravity)
> - $R = 8.314 \, J/(mol \cdot K)$ (universal gas constant)
> - For Souders-Brown: $K \approx 0.107 \, m/s$ (vertical), $K \approx 0.152 \, m/s$ (horizontal with cross-flow)

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Reactor instrumentation** is directly applicable to: oil refineries, petrochemical complexes, polymer plants, ammonia/urea units, methanol plants.
- **Separator instrumentation** underpins: offshore oil & gas platforms, gas processing plants, LNG trains, refinery distillation pre-fractionators, gas-oil separation plants (GOSPs).
- Both are also foundational concepts for **process safety management (PSM)** and **IEC 61511 SIL** classification in modern DCS/ESD systems.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Residence Time for a CSTR

The **residence time (τ)** is the average time a fluid element spends inside the reactor. It is fundamental for sizing and conversion estimation.

### Given:
- $V$ = working volume of reactor (m³)
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate at operating conditions (m³/s)

### Derivation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Residence time, by definition} \quad &\tau = \frac{\text{Volume of reactor}}{\text{Volumetric flow rate through it}} \\[6pt]
&\tau = \frac{V}{Q} \quad \text{(units: m³ / (m³/s) = s)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Worked Example:
A CSTR has working volume $V = 50 \, m^3$ and feed flow $Q = 10 \, m^3/hr$. Find residence time.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau &= \frac{50 \, m^3}{10 \, m^3 / hr} \\[6pt]
\tau &= 5 \, hr = 18000 \, s
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering meaning:** A residence time of 5 hours means a fluid element entering now will exit, on average, 5 hours later — used in the *design equation* $X = \frac{k\tau}{1 + k\tau}$ for a first-order reaction in a CSTR.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Level Calculation from DP Transmitter in a Separator

A **Differential Pressure (DP) transmitter** is mounted on the side of a separator. The high-pressure leg is connected to the *bottom tap*; the low-pressure leg to the *top tap*.

### Step-by-step:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Pressure at bottom tap} \quad &P_{bottom} = P_{gas} + \rho_{liq} \cdot g \cdot h_{liq} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 2: Pressure at top tap} \quad &P_{top} = P_{gas} \quad \text{(wet leg assumed, but equal to gas pressure)} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 3: Differential pressure} \quad &\Delta P = P_{bottom} - P_{top} = \rho_{liq} \cdot g \cdot h_{liq} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 4: Solve for level} \quad &h_{liq} = \frac{\Delta P}{\rho_{liq} \cdot g}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Numerical:
A separator has $\Delta P = 25 \, kPa$ reading. Liquid (water) density $\rho = 1000 \, kg/m^3$. Find level.

$$
\begin{aligned}
h &= \frac{25000 \, Pa}{1000 \, kg/m^3 \times 9.81 \, m/s^2} \\[6pt]
h &= \frac{25000}{9810} \\[6pt]
h &= 2.548 \, m
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Note:** If the gas space is *not* vented to the low-pressure side, the *dry leg condition* changes the formula. Always check whether the impulse lines are *wet* or *dry*.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Souders-Brown Velocity & Minimum Separator Diameter

The **Souders-Brown correlation** sets the maximum allowable superficial gas velocity to prevent liquid re-entrainment into the gas outlet.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Souders-Brown equation} \quad &v_{gas,allow} = K \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho_{liq} - \rho_{gas}}{\rho_{gas}}} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 2: For natural gas / oil, } \rho_{gas} \ll \rho_{liq} \quad &v_{gas,allow} \approx K \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho_{liq}}{\rho_{gas}}} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 3: Continuity equation} \quad &Q_{gas} = v_{gas,allow} \cdot A_{cross} = v_{gas,allow} \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 4: Solve for minimum D} \quad &D_{min} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \cdot Q_{gas}}{\pi \cdot v_{gas,allow}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Numerical Worked Example:
- Gas flow $Q_{gas} = 0.5 \, m^3/s$
- Oil density $\rho_{liq} = 850 \, kg/m^3$
- Gas density $\rho_{gas} = 12 \, kg/m^3$ (at high pressure)
- $K = 0.107 \, m/s$ (vertical separator)

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{gas,allow} &= 0.107 \times \sqrt{\frac{850 - 12}{12}} \\[6pt]
v_{gas,allow} &= 0.107 \times \sqrt{69.83} \\[6pt]
v_{gas,allow} &= 0.107 \times 8.357 \\[6pt]
v_{gas,allow} &= 0.894 \, m/s \\[6pt]
D_{min} &= \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 0.5}{\pi \times 0.894}} \\[6pt]
D_{min} &= \sqrt{\frac{2.0}{2.808}} \\[6pt]
D_{min} &= \sqrt{0.7122} \\[6pt]
D_{min} &= 0.844 \, m
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Practical Sizing Note:** The chosen diameter is *always* the next standard pipe/vessel size up (e.g., 0.9 m or 1.0 m).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Heat Removal Duty for an Exothermic Reactor

For an exothermic reactor at steady state, the **heat generated by reaction** must equal the **heat removed** by coolant — otherwise, temperature rises (runaway).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{gen} &= (-\Delta H_{rxn}) \cdot r_{A} \cdot V_{reactor} \\[6pt]
\dot{Q}_{removed} &= \dot{m}_{coolant} \cdot C_{p,coolant} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in}) \\[6pt]
\text{At steady state} \quad \dot{Q}_{gen} &= \dot{Q}_{removed}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Worked Example:
- $\Delta H_{rxn} = -80 \, kJ/mol$ (exothermic)
- $r_{A} = 0.05 \, mol/(L \cdot s) = 50 \, mol/(m^3 \cdot s)$
- $V = 2 \, m^3$
- Coolant: water, $C_p = 4.18 \, kJ/(kg \cdot K)$
- $T_{in} = 25°C$, $T_{out} = 45°C$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{gen} &= 80 \times 50 \times 2 = 8000 \, kW \\[6pt]
\dot{m}_{coolant} &= \frac{\dot{Q}_{gen}}{C_p \cdot \Delta T} = \frac{8000}{4.18 \times 20} \\[6pt]
\dot{m}_{coolant} &= \frac{8000}{83.6} = 95.7 \, kg/s
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget the *negative sign* convention for exothermic reactions. The heat duty should always be reported as a **positive** number representing magnitude of removal required.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Reactor vs. Separator Instrumentation

| Aspect | Reactor | Separator |
|--------|---------|-----------|
| **Primary Controlled Variable** | Temperature (for exothermic) | Level (for inventory) |
| **Critical Safety Variable** | Temperature + Pressure | Level + Pressure (gas space) |
| **Common Sensors** | RTD / Thermocouple, PT, FT, LT, AT | LT (DP/displacer/GWR), PT, TT, FT |
| **Manipulated Variable** | Coolant flow, catalyst feed, pressure vent | Liquid dump valve, gas outlet valve |
| **Control Strategy** | Cascade (T → T_jacket), Selective, Split-range | Split-range, Cascade (L → L_interface) |
| **Alarm / Interlock** | High T → emergency cooling, depressurize | High-High L → ESD (shutdown downstream) |
| **Sample Point** | Online analyzer (GC) in recycle line | Inline BS&W meter, gas chromatograph |
| **Failure Consequence** | Runaway reaction → explosion | Gas carry-under → compressor damage, oil loss to water |
